I am studying lattices and reached distributive lattice.
While I understood the definition, I am struggling to understood what that definition means in terms of join and meet.
For example I can understand meet means where the two elements meet down below which is the glb of them.

Is there a sentence which describes the distributive property formula just like above?
answer:
First of all, in order to get an intuition for a property of objects, it's great to first see some examples where the property
fails
. The two simplest non-distributive lattices are
$M_3$
and
$N_5$
. In fact, and this is a good exercise, a lattice is nondistributive iff it contains a "copy" of either
$M_3$
or
$N_5$
. So these are in fact the best examples, in a precise sense.

OK, so what does distributivity
mean
exactly? For me, the key point is that
distributivity says exactly that the elements of the lattice can be thought of as sets
. Here's what I mean:

A lattice
$L$
is distributive iff there is some set
$X$
and some family of subsets
$A\subseteq\mathcal{P}(X)$
such that
$L$
is isomorphic to
$(A; \cup,\cap)$
.

A lattice
$L$
is distributive iff there is some set
$X$
and some family of subsets
$A\subseteq\mathcal{P}(X)$
such that
$L$
is isomorphic to
$(A; \cup,\cap)$
.

Call lattices of the form
$(A; \cup,\cap)$
with
$A\subseteq\mathcal{P}(X)$
for some set
$x$
"lattices-of-sets."

Note that a lattice-of-sets in this sense is different from merely a lattice whose elements are sets. Take for instance the lattice of subgroups of a given group
$G$
. This is a lattice whose elements are sets, and meet is intersection, but join is
not
union - it's the
closure of
the union under the group operation.
(See also the second bulletpoint below ...)

Note that a lattice-of-sets in this sense is different from merely a lattice whose elements are sets. Take for instance the lattice of subgroups of a given group
$G$
. This is a lattice whose elements are sets, and meet is intersection, but join is
not
union - it's the
closure of
the union under the group operation.
(See also the second bulletpoint below ...)

Anyways, back to the claim above, that distributive = isomorphic to a lattice of sets. One direction is immediate: if
$L$
embeds into
$(\mathcal{P}(X); \cup,\cap)$
then
$L$
is distributive simply because distributivity holds of union/interesection. The interesting direction is the converse, saying that every distributive lattice can be represented in this way. In the case of finite lattices this is quite nice (see Birkhoff's theorem); it's messier if we let
$L$
be infinite, but it's still true.

Given how frequently we see lattices appear as lattices-of-sets, it can feel a bit weird that set-representability has any content at all. To help motivate this, consider a couple examples of lattices which are
not
obviously isomorphic to lattices-of-sets:

The lattice of continuous functions
$\mathbb{R}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$
, with the lattice operations being pointwise min/max (e.g.
$(f\vee g)(x)=\max\{f(x),g(x)\}$
).
The lattice of subgroups of the group
$D_8$
.

The lattice of continuous functions
$\mathbb{R}\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$
, with the lattice operations being pointwise min/max (e.g.
$(f\vee g)(x)=\max\{f(x),g(x)\}$
).

The lattice of subgroups of the group
$D_8$
.

In fact, the first example is isomorphic to a lattice-of-sets, while the second isn't (since it's not distributive); both of these points are good exercises.

As a coda, let me say a bit about how this sort of characterization can lead to further questions and results.

If we accept that "distributive = (iso.-to-)lattice-of-sets" is a useful motif, then we're naturally led to the question of what happens if we weaken the right hand side a bit. For example, motivated by lattices of subgroups (and subrings and etc.), what if we look at lattices which are (isomorphic to) lattices of substructures of some algebraic structure? Here the elements of our lattice must be sets, the meet operation must be intersection, but the join operation is union
followed by
closure under some fixed family of operations.

Perhaps surprisingly, the resulting change is extreme: we don't even get
modularity
in general. Modularity is a technical property much weaker than distributivity. The easiest-to-understand definition of modularity (in my opinion) is that a lattice is modular iff it doesn't contain a copy of the specific lattice
$N_5$
, but that's hard to motivate; for a conceptual explanation, see
Andreas Blass' explanation
. One takeaway from this is to look for a dividing line amongst algebraic structures:

Question 1
: When does an algebraic structure
$A$
have the property that the lattice of substructures of
$A$
is modular?

Question 1
: When does an algebraic structure
$A$
have the property that the lattice of substructures of
$A$
is modular?

The fact that this fails even when
$A$
is a group suggests that this is a fairly stringent condition indeed. But there's another direction we can go, too: what if we replace "subgroup" with "
normal
subgroup"? In this case we
do
always get a modular lattice. We know that normal subgroups correspond to congruences (= group-operation-respecting equivalence relations; i.e., "stuff we can quotient by"), so we now have a second follow-up question:

Are lattices of
congruences of
(rather than
substructures of
) algebraic structures necessarily modular? If not, when does an algebraic structure have the property that its lattice-of-congruences is modular?

Are lattices of
congruences of
(rather than
substructures of
) algebraic structures necessarily modular? If not, when does an algebraic structure have the property that its lattice-of-congruences is modular?

This turns out to be more fruitful than the previous question, especially when we shift attention from a single structure to a class of structures (e.g. all groups versus a specific group). The term "congruence-modular variety" shows up here. But at this point I should
definitely
stop. The point of this coda is not to list facts/questions about modularity, but merely to indicate how the original idea of characterizing distributivity in terms of representability via sets can be mathematically fruitful beyond just building comfort with the original concept.

question:
Direct limit in $CRing$ is driving me mad these days.

I have difficulty verifying the proof of some theorems in $EGA1 new$.

How to construct the direct limit of commutative rings? Is there any standard reference?

$EGA1 new$ 0.6.1.1 Let $A=\varinjlim A_i =0$, then $\exists k$ s.t. $\forall i \ge k, A_i=0$.

$EGA1 new$ 0.6.3.3 Let $A=\varinjlim A_i =0$, $M$ be an $A$-module of finite presentation. Then $\exists i,\exists M_i$ be an $A_i$-module s.t. $M=A \otimes_{A_i}M_i$.

The proof in $EGA1 new$ is incomplete to me.
answer:
If $(R_i, \nu_{ij}:R_i\rightarrow R_j))_{i, j}$ is a directed system of commutative rings, consider the set
$$R=(\coprod_i R_i)/\sim\;, $$
where $\sim$ is the equivalence on $\coprod_i R_i$ given by $$r_i \sim r_j, \;\; r_i \in R_i,\; r_j \in R_j \text{  iff  } \nu_{ik}(r_i)=\nu_{jk}(r_j) \text{ for some }k\geq i, j $$
(note that here $\coprod$ denotes the disjoint union of sets, not coproduct of comm rings or anything else).

Define operations of $R$ as follows: Given $[r_i], [r_j] \in R,$ consider $k \geq i, j$ and put $[r_i]+[r_j]=[\nu_{ik}(r_i)+\nu_{jk}(r_j)]$ and $[r_i]\cdot[r_j]=[\nu_{ik}(r_i)\cdot \nu_{jk}(r_j)]$ (the correctness of this definition should be checked, but it works). Of course then the unit is given by the class $[1], \;1 \in R_i$ for arbitrary $i$, and define similarly $0=[0]$. The result, together with the co-cone of morphisms $R_i \rightarrow R, ; r_i \mapsto [r_i]$ gives the direct limit.

(
Remark:
although this description of direct limits seems kind of unnatural (i.e. does not come naturally as a coequalizer of a coproduct in the cat. of comm. rings), it has the advanage that this construction works in any variety (in the universal algebraic sense), i.e. one can describe in a similar manner direct limits of groups / modules / monoids/ lattices  ... abstractly, one could describe it by saying that the forgetful functor from a variety to sets preserves direct limits.)

This should take care of the statement 2. straightforwardly: If $R=0$, this means that $0=1$, which by the description of $\sim$ translates into $0=1$ in $R_k$ for some $k$. From this point on, of course $0=1$ in every $i \geq k$, since there is a homomorphism $R_k \rightarrow R_i$.

As for 3., if there is an exact sequence
$$R^m \stackrel{\varphi}{\rightarrow} R^n \rightarrow M \rightarrow 0,$$

the map $\varphi$ is essentially given by an $m \times n$ matrix with entries in $R$. Call the entries $s^{a, b}, (a, b) \in \{0, 1, \dots, m\}\times \{0, 1, \dots, n\}$. Then we can take the representatives: $s^{a, b}=[r_{i_{a, b}}]$ for some $r_{i_{a, b}} \in R_{i_{a, b}}$. Taking $k$ such that $k\geq i_{a, b}$ for all $a, b$, we have
$$s^{a, b}=[r_{i_{a, b}}]=[\nu_{i_{a, b}k}(r_{i_{a, b}})], \; \nu_{i_{a, b}k}(r_{i_{a, b}}) \in R_k,$$
which is a complicated way of saying that all the representatives can be taken from the single ring $R_k$. Now take the module $M_k$ to be the module given by the presentation
$$(R_k)^m \stackrel{\tilde{\varphi}}\rightarrow (R_k)^n \rightarrow M_k \rightarrow 0, $$
where $\tilde{\varphi}$ is given by the matrix of representatives. Now apply the right exact functor $R\otimes_{R_k}-$ and observe that you obtain the original exact sequence.

question:
Trying to prove this somewhat famous identity

Given
$f$
is a polynomial of degree less than
$n$
, where
$n \in
\mathbb{N}$
. Show that

$$\sum_{k=0}^{n} (-1)^{k} \binom{n}{k} f(k) = 0$$


I tried doing so with induction, I wonder what I need to do in order to proceed.


The base case is pretty simple. For
$n=1$
, this forces
$f$
to be constant function and the above identity gives
$f(0)-f(1)=0$
.


Now assume the identity is true for
$n$
. We need to show it is true for
$n+1$
.


Consider the
$n+1$
case, we have
$$\sum_{k=0}^{n+1} (-1)^{k} \binom{n+1}{k} f(k) = \sum_{k=0}^{n+1}(-1)^{k} \binom{n}{k} f(k) + \sum_{k=0}^{n+1}(-1)^{k}\ \binom{n}{k-1} f(k) \tag{1}$$


In which the degree of
$f$
is less than
$n+1$


I feel like I'm on the right path, but how do I continue with (1) ? I assume I have to consider the case when
$\deg f < n$
and
$n \le \deg f <n+1$
answer:
First of all, notice that your expansion of
$\binom{n+1}k$
only makes sense if you define
$\binom nk=0$
for
$k=-1,n+1$
. And so you get

$$\sum_{k=0}^{n+1} (-1)^{k} \binom{n+1}{k} f(k) = \sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k} \binom{n}{k} f(k) + \sum_{k=1}^{n+1}(-1)^{k}\ \binom{n}{k-1} f(k)$$
writing
$$f(x)=a_nx^n+g(x)$$
where
$\deg g< n$
, we get that the expression above equals
$$a_n\left(\underbrace{\sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k} \binom{n}{k} k^n + \sum_{k=1}^{n+1}(-1)^{k}\ \binom{n}{k-1} k^n}_{A}\right)+\underbrace{\sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k} \binom{n}{k} g(k) + \sum_{k=1}^{n+1}(-1)^{k}\ \binom{n}{k-1} g(k)}_B$$
but now, re-indexing the second sum
\begin{align}B&=\sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k} \binom{n}{k} g(k) + \sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k+1}\ \binom{n}{k} g(k+1)\\&=\sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k} \binom{n}{k} g(k) - \sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k}\ \binom{n}{k} g(k+1)\end{align}
and since both
$g(x)$
and
$g(x+1)$
have degreee less than
$n$
, by the induction hypothesis
$B=0$
. On the other hand, by re-indexing the second sum
\begin{align*}A&=\sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k} \binom{n}{k} k^n + \sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k+1}\ \binom{n}{k} (k+1)^n\\&=\sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k} \binom{n}{k} \left(k^n-(k+1)^n\right)\\&=\sum_{k=0}^{n}(-1)^{k} \binom{n}{k} h(k)\end{align*}
where
$h(x)=x^n-(x+1)^n$
. But,
$\deg h<n$
, since by the binomial theorem

$$h(x)=x^n-\sum_{j=0}^n\binom nj x^j=-\sum_{j=0}^{n-1}\binom njx^j$$
and so, by induction hypothesis
$A=0$
as well, and so
$$\sum_{k=0}^{n+1} (-1)^{k} \binom{n+1}{k} f(k)=a_nA+B=0$$
which completes the induction

question:
Let
$P(x)$
be a polynomial of degree
$n$
such that

$$P(k)=2^k\; \text{for}\; k=0,1,2,…,n.$$

Determine
$P(n+1)$
.


This is a well-known problem that has already been discussed many times on forums such as Art of Problem Solving and Math StackExchange.


According to the book
Mathematical Miniatures
(p. 48), the problem first appeared in the Pi Mu Epsilon Journal, 1964, Vol. 6, and was proposed by Murray S. Klamkin. However, Volume 6 of the journal does not correspond to the year 1964. I then wondered whether “6” referred instead to issue No. 6, but I could not find the problem there either.


I also tried searching JSTOR using keywords such as “
$P(n+1)$
”, “Klamkin”, and “polynomial”, and even considered the possibility of a typo in the citation (for example, a different year such as 1976 which corresponds to Vol. 6), but without success.

Can anyone help me?
answer:
You have already found the right keywords. The correct reference is

M. S. Klamkin, Problem 158, T
he Pi Mu Epsilon Journal, Vol. 3, No. 9
, p. 473, 1963

M. S. Klamkin, Problem 158, T
he Pi Mu Epsilon Journal, Vol. 3, No. 9
, p. 473, 1963

(There is a
$x-1$
missing from the power of
$2$
, must be a Typo)

question:
Why is
$\mathbf{Haus}^{\mathrm{op}}$
not
locally presentable
? Here,
$\mathbf{Haus}$
denotes the category of Hausdorff spaces with continuous maps. So the question is why
$\mathbf{Haus}$
is not locally copresentable. Since it is complete, the question equivalently asks why it is not coaccessible.

Here is a proof that
$\mathbf{Top}$
is not coaccessible (found by Yuto Kawase). Assume it is. Let
$p : S \to I$
be the identity map from the Sierpinski space to the two-element indiscrete space. Then, a topological space is discrete if and only if it is projective to the morphism
$p$
.

This implies that the full subcategory spanned by all discrete spaces, which is equivalent to
$\mathbf{Set}$
, is coaccessible by the dual of Prop. 4.7 in
Adamek-Rosicky
. However, since
$\mathbf{Set}$
is not coaccessible (say, by Thm. 1.64 in loc.cit.), this is a contradiction.

Maybe a similar reduction also works for
$\mathbf{Haus}$
, but I fail to see it. I have also tried to use directly the definition of
$\lambda$
-accessibility, but it is kind of hard to work with when you know nothing about the set of generators.

Maybe one can even show that
$\mathbf{Haus}^{\mathrm{op}}$
has no small colimit-dense subcategory? Notice that
$\mathbf{Haus}$
does not have a small colimit-dense subcategory by
MSE/4097315
.
answer:
The easiest way I could find to show
$\mathbf{Haus}^{\mathrm{op}}$
is not locally presentable is this.

For the sake of a contradiction, assume it is. Then there exists a regular cardinal
$\lambda$
such that
$\mathbf{Haus}^{\mathrm{op}}$
is locally presentable. Then by Theorem 1.20 in
Adamek-Rosicky
,
$\mathbf{Haus}^{\mathrm{op}}$
has a strong generator formed by
$\lambda$
-presentable objects. By remark 1.19 in loc. cit., this forms a small full subcategory. If we call it
$\mathcal A$
, then by duality,
$\mathcal C:=\mathcal A^{\mathrm{op}}$
is a small strong cogenerator for
$\mathbf{Haus}$
. In particular, it is a cogenerator and hence separates points.

Since
$\mathcal C$
is small,
$Y:=\prod_{C\in\mathcal C}C$
is a valid toplogical space, and since all
$C\in\mathbf{Haus}$
, so is
$Y$
. This implies
$Y$
is
$T_1$
. By
Herrlich's theorem
, there exists
$X\in\mathbf{Haus}$
with
$|X|\ge 2$
such that for all continuous maps
$X\to Y$
are constant.

So let
$x_1,x_2\in X$
with
$x_1\ne x_2$
. Since
$\mathcal C$
is a cogenerator, there must exist
$C^*\in\mathcal C$
and
$g:X\to C^*$
such that
$g(x_1)\ne g(x_2)$
. Let
$y_0=(y_C)_{C\in\mathcal C}$
be an arbitrary point in
$Y$
. Let us define
$h:X\to Y$
by
$\pi_{C^*}\circ h=g$
and
$\pi_C\circ h=\mathrm{const}_{y_C}$
for all
$C\in\mathcal C\setminus\{C^*\}$
. Because
$g(x_1)\ne g(x_2)$
, the points
$h(x_1)$
and
$h(x_2)$
differ in at least one coordinate. Therefore,
$h(x_1)\ne h(x_2)$
. Thus
$h$
is not-constant, which is a contradiction.

question:
I found the following two polynomial identities while experimenting with sums and differences of cubes.

Identity 1
(sum of two cubes = perfect square):

$$((x+y)(x+3y)(x^2+3y^2))^3+(−4xy(x^2+3xy+3y^2))^3=$$


$$((x^2−3y^2)(x^4+6x^3y+18x^2y^2+18xy^3+9y^4))^2$$


The key observation: using
$A^3+B^3=(A+B)(A^2−AB+B^2)$
, both factors are perfect squares simultaneously:


$A+B=(x^2−3y^2)^2$
,


$A^2−AB+B^2=(x^4+6x^3y+18x^2y^2+18xy^3+9y^4)^2$


Identity 2
(difference of two cubes = perfect square):


$$(3a^2+6ab−b^2)^3−(3a^2−6ab−b^2)^3=36ab(3a^2+b^2)^2$$


Substituting
$a = p^2$
,
$b = q^2$
gives a parametric family where a difference of two cubes equals a perfect square in integers:


$$(3p^4+6p^2q^2−q^4)^3−(3p^4−6p^2q^2−q^4)^3=(6pq(3p^4+q^4))^2$$


Questions:




Are these identities known in the literature?


Is there a unified framework that explains why both the sum
$A+B$
and the expression
$A^2-AB+B^2$
are simultaneously perfect squares in Identity 1?


More generally, what is the characterization of homogeneous polynomials

$A,B\in\mathbb Z[x,y]$
such that
$A^3\pm B^3$
is a perfect square in
$\mathbb Z[x,y]$
?
answer:
To answer your question 1;

I believe they are both known.

Identity
$2$
is a direct consequence of a well-known elementary algebraic identity:
$$(X+Y)^3 - (X-Y)^3 = 2Y(3X^2 + Y^2),$$
where
$X = 3a^2 - b^2$
and
$Y = 6ab$
.

Identity
$1$
I believe arises from the family of polynomial parameterizations for the surface
$A^3 + B^3 = C^2$
, where
$A$
and
$B$
are homogenous polynomials of degree 4.

To answer your question 2;

In any Unique Factorization Domain, if the product of two coprime factors results in a perfect square, then each factor must independently be a perfect square (up to a unit multiplier).

Through the
Euclidean algorithm
, we know that:
$$\gcd(A+B, A^2 - AB + B^2) = \gcd(A+B, (A+B)^2 - 3AB)$$
$$\gcd(A+B, A^2 - AB + B^2) = \gcd(A+B, 3AB)$$

If the polynomials
$A$
and
$B$
are constructed to be coprime, their GCD with
$A+B$
is
$1$
. Therefore, the GCD of
$(A+B)$
and
$(A^2 - AB + B^2)$
must divide
$3$
. Since your parameterization naturally avoids the factor of
$3$
being shared, the two factors are strictly coprime. Because their product
$C^2$
is a perfect square, algebraic necessity forces both
$(A+B)$
and
$(A^2 - AB + B^2)$
to be perfect squares simultaneously.

I am no expert but for question 3, I suspect it has to deal with the theory of elliptic curves.

question:
I found the following two polynomial identities while experimenting with sums and differences of cubes.

Identity 1
(sum of two cubes = perfect square):

$$((x+y)(x+3y)(x^2+3y^2))^3+(−4xy(x^2+3xy+3y^2))^3=$$


$$((x^2−3y^2)(x^4+6x^3y+18x^2y^2+18xy^3+9y^4))^2$$


The key observation: using
$A^3+B^3=(A+B)(A^2−AB+B^2)$
, both factors are perfect squares simultaneously:


$A+B=(x^2−3y^2)^2$
,


$A^2−AB+B^2=(x^4+6x^3y+18x^2y^2+18xy^3+9y^4)^2$


Identity 2
(difference of two cubes = perfect square):


$$(3a^2+6ab−b^2)^3−(3a^2−6ab−b^2)^3=36ab(3a^2+b^2)^2$$


Substituting
$a = p^2$
,
$b = q^2$
gives a parametric family where a difference of two cubes equals a perfect square in integers:


$$(3p^4+6p^2q^2−q^4)^3−(3p^4−6p^2q^2−q^4)^3=(6pq(3p^4+q^4))^2$$


Questions:




Are these identities known in the literature?


Is there a unified framework that explains why both the sum
$A+B$
and the expression
$A^2-AB+B^2$
are simultaneously perfect squares in Identity 1?


More generally, what is the characterization of homogeneous polynomials

$A,B\in\mathbb Z[x,y]$
such that
$A^3\pm B^3$
is a perfect square in
$\mathbb Z[x,y]$
?
answer:
Identity 2 is a well‑known parametric family for
$x^3+y^3=z^2$
— it’s essentially the one that appears in Cohen’s book and other references under the substitution
$a=p^2, b=q^2$
. Identity 1, on the other hand, looks new to me; I haven’t seen it in the literature.

The reason Identity 1 works is exactly your observation: both
$A+B$
and
$A^2-AB+B^2$
are forced to be perfect squares simultaneously. That’s a standard trick to generate parametric solutions to
$A^3+B^3=C^2$
: if you can find polynomials where
$A+B=P^2$
and
$A^2-AB+B^2=Q^2$
, their product is automatically
$(PQ)^2$
. Your example is a particularly neat realization of that idea.

For your third question: all coprime polynomial solutions (over
$\mathbb{Z}[x,y]$
) to
$A^3\pm B^3=\square$
can in principle be obtained from the known integer parametrizations by letting the parameters be polynomials. For the sum case, a complete rational parametrization is
$$
\begin{aligned}
x &= -3s^4+6s^2t^2+t^4,\\
y &= 3s^4+6s^2t^2-t^4,\\
z &= 6st(3s^4+t^4),
\end{aligned}
$$
and the difference case follows by flipping a sign. This doesn’t give a simple “characterization” of homogeneous
$A,B$
directly, but it does tell you that all solutions come from such families with
$s,t\in\mathbb{Z}[x,y]$
.

answer:
Bruin, N. (2000). On Powers as Sums of Two Cubes. In: Bosma, W. (eds) Algorithmic Number Theory. ANTS 2000. Lecture Notes in Computer Science, vol 1838. Springer, Berlin, Heidelberg.
https://doi.org/10.1007/10722028_9
suggests that a lot is known about square sums of two cubes. The paper is behind a paywall, only the first page is visible at that site, so I don't know more than that.

question:
Let
$X$
be a preorder and let
$\bf 2$
be the lattice with underlying set
$\{0,1\}$
and the usual lattice operations.

Preliminary definitions.

Denote by
$\mathrm{Up}(X)$
the set of upward closed subsets in
$X$
. For any
$x\in X$
, set
$x_\uparrow:=\{x'\in X:x\leq x'\}$
.

A map
$\omega:\mathrm{Up}(X)\to \bf 2$
is an
prime filter
when it satisfies these properties:

$\omega(U\cap V)=\omega(U)\wedge \omega(V)$
;

$\omega(U\cup V)=\omega(U) \vee \omega(V)$
;

$\omega(X)=1$
and
$\omega(\emptyset)=0$
;

for all
$U,V\in \mathrm{Up}(X)$
.

The prime filter
$\omega$
is called
principal
when there is
$x\in X$
satisfying this property:
$\omega(U)=1$
if and only if
$x\in U$
, for all
$U\in\mathrm{Up}(X)$
.

Question.

I am trying to prove that, if the preorder
$X$
is finite, any prime filter on
$X$
is principal.
I had made a bit of progress, but then I got stuck as usual.

So, assume that
$X$
is finite (say
$|X|=n$
) and let
$\omega:\mathrm{Up}(X)\to \bf 2$
be a prime filter.

First step.
There is some
$x\in X$
such that
$\omega(x_\uparrow)=1$
: in fact
$$\bigcup_{i=1}^n{x_i}_\uparrow=X,$$
implying that
$$\sum_{i=1}^n\omega({x_i}_\uparrow)=1,$$
so it is not possible that
$\omega({x_i}_\uparrow)=0$
for all
$i$
.


Second step.
For all
$U\in \mathrm{Up}(X)$
, if
$x\in U$
then
$\omega(U)=1$
: in fact
$$U=x_\uparrow \cup U,$$
implying that
$$\omega(U)=1+\omega(U)=1.$$


Third step.
I should prove that, for all
$U\in \mathrm{Up}(X)$
, if
$\omega(U)=1$
then
$x\in U$
. Analogously to the
First step
, I could argue that there is
$y\in U$
such that
$\omega(y_\uparrow)=1$
. However it seems to me that, from our choice of
$x$
, it is not necessarily true that
$x\in y_\uparrow$
, or in general that
$x\in U$
. Am I missing something here in the
Third step
or should I make more assumptions in the
First step
when I choose
$x$
? Maybe something on maximality? Thank in advance for any suggestion


Edit.


If
$X$
is a finite preorder then
$\mathrm{Up}(X)$
is a finite poset. Let
$\omega:\mathrm{Up}(X)\to \bf 2$
be a prime filter; we said that there is some
$x\in X$
such that
$\omega (x_\uparrow)=1$
. Since
$\mathrm{Up}(X)$
is a finite poset we can choose
$x$
with the property (
$*$
) that, if for
$x'\in X$
one has
$\omega(x'_\uparrow)=1$
and
$x'_\uparrow\subset x_\uparrow$
, then
$x'_\uparrow=x_\uparrow$
. Suppose that
$y\in X$
is another element with
$\omega (y_\uparrow)=1$
, having the property (
$*$
). Then there is
$z\in x_\uparrow\cap y_\uparrow$
such that
$\omega(z_\uparrow)=1$
, since
$\omega(x_\uparrow\cap y_\uparrow)=1$
. By the property (
$*$
) follows
$x_\uparrow= y_\uparrow$
. But then any
$U\in \mathrm{Up}(X)$
satisfies
$\omega(U)=1$
iff there is some
$x'\in U$
such that
$\omega (x'_\uparrow)=1$
iff there is some
$x'\in U$
such that
$\omega (x'_\uparrow)=1$
and
$x'$
has the property (
$*$
) iff
$x_\uparrow\subset U$
.
answer:
Suppose
$\omega$
is a prime filter. Let
$V$
be the intersection of all upward-closed sets
$U$
such that
$\omega(U)=1$
. Since
$\mathrm{Up}(X)$
is finite, this is a finite intersection, so
$\omega(V)=1$
.

Since
$V$
is upward-closed,
$V=\bigcup_{x\in V} x_\uparrow$
. Since
$X$
is finite, this is a finite union, so there is some
$x\in V$
such that
$\omega(x_\uparrow)=1$
.

Now for any upward-closed set
$U$
, if
$x\in U$
, then
$x_\uparrow\subseteq U$
, so
$\omega(U)=1$
. Conversely, if
$\omega(U)=1$
, then
$x\in x_\uparrow\subseteq V\subseteq U$
, so
$x\in U$
.

question:
Let
$X$
be a preorder and let
$\bf 2$
be the lattice with underlying set
$\{0,1\}$
and the usual lattice operations.

Preliminary definitions.

Denote by
$\mathrm{Up}(X)$
the set of upward closed subsets in
$X$
. For any
$x\in X$
, set
$x_\uparrow:=\{x'\in X:x\leq x'\}$
.

A map
$\omega:\mathrm{Up}(X)\to \bf 2$
is an
prime filter
when it satisfies these properties:

$\omega(U\cap V)=\omega(U)\wedge \omega(V)$
;

$\omega(U\cup V)=\omega(U) \vee \omega(V)$
;

$\omega(X)=1$
and
$\omega(\emptyset)=0$
;

for all
$U,V\in \mathrm{Up}(X)$
.

The prime filter
$\omega$
is called
principal
when there is
$x\in X$
satisfying this property:
$\omega(U)=1$
if and only if
$x\in U$
, for all
$U\in\mathrm{Up}(X)$
.

Question.

I am trying to prove that, if the preorder
$X$
is finite, any prime filter on
$X$
is principal.
I had made a bit of progress, but then I got stuck as usual.

So, assume that
$X$
is finite (say
$|X|=n$
) and let
$\omega:\mathrm{Up}(X)\to \bf 2$
be a prime filter.

First step.
There is some
$x\in X$
such that
$\omega(x_\uparrow)=1$
: in fact
$$\bigcup_{i=1}^n{x_i}_\uparrow=X,$$
implying that
$$\sum_{i=1}^n\omega({x_i}_\uparrow)=1,$$
so it is not possible that
$\omega({x_i}_\uparrow)=0$
for all
$i$
.


Second step.
For all
$U\in \mathrm{Up}(X)$
, if
$x\in U$
then
$\omega(U)=1$
: in fact
$$U=x_\uparrow \cup U,$$
implying that
$$\omega(U)=1+\omega(U)=1.$$


Third step.
I should prove that, for all
$U\in \mathrm{Up}(X)$
, if
$\omega(U)=1$
then
$x\in U$
. Analogously to the
First step
, I could argue that there is
$y\in U$
such that
$\omega(y_\uparrow)=1$
. However it seems to me that, from our choice of
$x$
, it is not necessarily true that
$x\in y_\uparrow$
, or in general that
$x\in U$
. Am I missing something here in the
Third step
or should I make more assumptions in the
First step
when I choose
$x$
? Maybe something on maximality? Thank in advance for any suggestion


Edit.


If
$X$
is a finite preorder then
$\mathrm{Up}(X)$
is a finite poset. Let
$\omega:\mathrm{Up}(X)\to \bf 2$
be a prime filter; we said that there is some
$x\in X$
such that
$\omega (x_\uparrow)=1$
. Since
$\mathrm{Up}(X)$
is a finite poset we can choose
$x$
with the property (
$*$
) that, if for
$x'\in X$
one has
$\omega(x'_\uparrow)=1$
and
$x'_\uparrow\subset x_\uparrow$
, then
$x'_\uparrow=x_\uparrow$
. Suppose that
$y\in X$
is another element with
$\omega (y_\uparrow)=1$
, having the property (
$*$
). Then there is
$z\in x_\uparrow\cap y_\uparrow$
such that
$\omega(z_\uparrow)=1$
, since
$\omega(x_\uparrow\cap y_\uparrow)=1$
. By the property (
$*$
) follows
$x_\uparrow= y_\uparrow$
. But then any
$U\in \mathrm{Up}(X)$
satisfies
$\omega(U)=1$
iff there is some
$x'\in U$
such that
$\omega (x'_\uparrow)=1$
iff there is some
$x'\in U$
such that
$\omega (x'_\uparrow)=1$
and
$x'$
has the property (
$*$
) iff
$x_\uparrow\subset U$
.
answer:
I think that in the first step you should take the minimal possible
$x$
such that
$\omega(x_{\uparrow}) = 1$
. It always exists because X is finite.

Then if
$\omega(U) = 1$
, we also have
$\omega(x_{\uparrow}\cup U) = 1$
.
Consider the set
$x_{\uparrow}\cup U$
. Since
$X$
is finite, there must be a finite number of elements
$y_1, \dots, y_n$
such that
$U = \{y_1, ..., y_n\}_{\uparrow}$
. This means that
$\omega(x\cup y_1 \cup ... \cup y_n) = 1$
. Now using the minimality of
$x$
we conclude that
$y_1 = ... = y_n = x$
. Which means that
$U \subseteq x_{\uparrow}$
.

question:
It seems to me that the formulation of
Schinzel's hypothesis H

is related to Pólya's results on
integer-valued polynomials
(1) and my question is if this conjecture can be generalized (revised) as below?

Given non-constant irreducible polynomials
$f_1,\dots,f_k$
with the fixed divisors

$m_1,\dots,m_k$
, such that there are no integer
$N$
that divides
$F(n) = \prod_{i=1}^k \frac{f_i(n)}{m_i}$
for all
$n>0$
, then there are infinitely many positive integers
$n$

such that all of
${\displaystyle f_{1}(n)/m_1,f_{2}(n)/m_2,\dots,f_{k}(n)/m_k}$
are simultaneously prime numbers.

(1) Paul-Jean Cahen & Jean-Luc Chabert (2016),
What You Should Know About Integer-Valued Polynomials
, The American Mathematical Monthly, 123:4, 311-337
answer:
I don't think this is a generalization of Schinzel's hypothesis H.

Let's try to look at the example (copied from wikipedia) with
$f_1=x+4$
and
$f_2=x+7$
.

If you require that all the
$m_i >1$
, then this example wouldn't be covered by your hypothesis. In fact you couldn't include any linear polynomials. So this wouldn't generalize Schinzel's hypothesis.

If you allow
$m_i=1$
, then just picking
$m_1=m_2=1$
gives a counter example to your conjecture.

question:
Reading Axler as I never felt like I learned linear algebra well, and I just got to the section on the definition of adjoints of linear maps. The definition they gave is that for linear map
$T \in L(V,W)$
, the adjoint
$T^*$
is the map such that

$$\forall v \in V, \forall w \in W, \  \left\langle Tv, w\right\rangle = \left\langle v, T^*w\right\rangle$$

After reading this, I was confused by the extreme similarity to the dual linear map
$T'$
. Axler defines dual elements as linear functionals, but in my head there's essentially some bilinear map
$D: V \times V' \rightarrow \mathbb F$
that describes the action of dual elements on vectors, and the dual map definition

$$T'(\phi) = \phi \circ T$$

becomes

$$\forall v, \ T'(\phi)v = \phi(T(v)) \iff \forall v, \ D(T(v), \phi) = D(v, T'(\phi))$$

which kinda looks like the same thing.


My main question is a bit open-ended, but: how related are these concepts? What can you prove about adjoints given the properties of dual maps? I looked at some related articles and saw that the Riesz representation theorem gives you

$$T^* = I_V^{-1} T' I_W$$

where
$I_X: X \rightarrow X'$
is the canonical map defined by
$I_X(x)(y) = \left\langle y, x\right\rangle$
. It seems like you can use this to prove linearity of
$T^*$
given linearity of
$T'$
(since
$I_V$
and
$I_W$
are both antilinear) -- what else can you do / why aren't these taught as being as similar as they are?


(Side note: inner product spaces and dual spaces already felt pretty similar before this, so the relationship between these would also be helpful -- my current understanding is that the main difference is that while dual bases are similar to orthonormal bases, there is no canonical dual basis, but the inner product specifies which bases are orthonormal. Also one is bilinear and the other sesquilinear.)


EDIT: probably should've read a little further before posting, I found this paragraph in the book quite helpful, as it lists many of the common relationships:


"The Riesz representation theorem as stated in 6.58 provides an identification of
$V$
with its dual space
$V'$
defined in 3.110. Under this identification, the orthogonal complement
$U^\perp$
of a subset
$U \subseteq V$
corresponds to the annihilator
$U^0$
of
$U$
. If
$U$
is a subspace of
$V$
, then the formulas for the dimensions of
$U^\perp$
and
$U^0$
become identical under this identification—see 3.125 and 6.51. Suppose
$T: V \rightarrow W$
is a linear map. Under the identification of
$V$
with
$V'$
and the identification of
$W$
with
$W'$
, the adjoint map
$T^*: W \rightarrow V$
corresponds to the dual map
$T': W' \rightarrow V'$
defined in 3.118, as Exercise 32 asks you to verify. Under this identification, the formulas for
$\text{null} \ T^*$
and
$\text{range} \ T^*$
[7.6(a) and (b)] then become identical to the formulas for
$\text{null} \ T'$
and
$\text{range} \ T'$
[3.128(a) and 3.130(b)]. Furthermore, the theorem about the matrix of
$T^*$
(7.9) is analogous to the theorem about the matrix of
$T'$
(3.132)."
answer:
They are the same thing: Axler is defining adjoints for inner product spaces rather than general vector spaces. In the latter context, if
$T\colon V \to W$
is a linear map, then its dual is the linear map
$T' \colon W^*\to V^*$
given by
$T'(f) = f\circ T$
. If
$V$
and
$W$
are just vector spaces that is all you have.

As the OP points out in the question, you can think of
$V$
and
$V^*$
more symmetrically (so for
$V$
finite-dimensional this is natural) by noting that there is a canonical bilinear map
$e\colon V\times V^* \to \mathbb C$
, where
$e(v,f)= f(v)$
. The fact that
$V^*$
is dual to
$V$
is then reflected in the fact that, given
$f \in V^*$
, the operation of pairing with it under
$e$
gives an element of
$V^*$
. While that is completely tautological, notice that pairing with
$v\in V$
under
$e$
gives a functional on
$V^*$
, and this is the natural transformation from
$V$
to
$V^{**}$
.

Now if
$V$
and
$W$
are inner product spaces, then the inner products give of course sesquilinear maps
$V\times V \to \mathbb C$
which I can think of as bilinear maps
$\bar{V}\times V \to \mathbb C$
if I define
$\bar{V}$
to be the
$\mathbb C$
-vector space which is the same abelian group as
$V$
but for which the action of scalars is twisted by complex conjugation, that is, scalar multiplication in
$\bar{V}$
is defined to be
$\lambda\star v = \bar{\lambda}.v$
. This way a Hermitian form on
$V$
becomes a bilinear form on
$\langle -,- \rangle\colon \bar{V}\times V$
since
$$
\lambda.\langle v_1,v_2\rangle = \langle v_1,\lambda.v_2\rangle = \langle \bar{\lambda}.v_1,v_2\rangle = \langle \lambda\star v_1,v_2\rangle
$$

Pairing against a vector in
$\bar{V}$
under the inner product then gives a functional on
$V$
, so the inner product gives (linear) map
$\theta_V\colon \bar{V}\to V^*$
. The conjugate symmetry of the inner products means the map
$\vartheta\colon V \to (\bar{V})^*$
sending a vector in
$v$
to the linear functional you get by pairing against it  under the inner product is just
$c\circ\theta$
where
$c\colon \mathbb C\to \mathbb C$
is complex conjugation and because the inner product is positive definite,
$\theta_V$
is an isomorphism.

If
$W$
is another inner product space then you similarly get
$\theta_W\colon \bar{W}\to W^*$
. Now if
$T\colon V\to W$
then we have

$$
\require{AMScd}
\begin{CD}
\bar{W} @>{\theta_W}>> W^*\\
@VV{T^*}V @VV{T'}V\\
\bar{V} @>{\theta_V}>> V^*
\end{CD}
$$

and this diagram commutes, that is
$T^* = \theta_V^{-1}T'\theta_W$
. Indeed this is equivalent to
$\theta_V T^* = T'\theta_W$
since
$\theta_V$
is an isomorphism, and
$$
\theta_V( T^*(w))(v) = \langle T^*(w),v\rangle
$$
while
$$
T'\theta_W(w)(v) = \theta_W(w)\circ T(v) = \langle w,T(v)\rangle,
$$
so that the condition
$\theta_VT^* = T'\theta_W$
is just the standard definition of
$T^*$
, that is,
$\langle T^*(w),v\rangle = \langle w,T(v)\rangle$
for all
$v \in V$
,
$w \in W$
.

One last point -- the map defined by
$\theta_V^{-1}T'\theta_W$
is a linear map from
$\bar{W}$
to
$\bar{V}$
and not
$W$
to
$V$
. But if you twist by complex conjugation on
$V$
and
$W$
, then the condition that a map is linear is the same, that is
$\text{Hom}(W,V) = \text{Hom}(\bar{W},\bar{V})$
, so
$T^*$
really is a linear map from
$W$
to
$V$
. (You see the complex conjugation appearing when you compute the matrix of
$T^*$
-- it is the conjugate transpose, not the transpose, and the conjugation is the result of how
$T^*$
appears as a map from
$\bar{W}$
to
$\bar{V}$
.)

$T \in L(V,W)$

corresponds to the annihilator

become identical under this identification—see 3.125 and 6.51. Suppose

corresponds to the dual map

answer:
The key is the concept of a
dual system
.

A dual system consists of a pair of vector spaces
$(V,W)$
and a non-degenerate bilinear form
$b : V \times W \to F$
(= ground field). The latter means that

If
$x \in V$
has the property
$b(x,y) = 0$
for all
$y$
, then
$x = 0$
.
If
$y \in W$
has the property
$b(x,y) = 0$
for all
$x$
, then
$y = 0$
.

Now observe

Each inner product on
$V$
gives the dual system
$(V,V, \langle -, - \rangle)$
.
For each
$V$
we obtain the dual system
$(V, V', e)$
with
$e(v,\phi) = \phi(v)$
.

Each inner product on
$V$
gives the dual system
$(V,V, \langle -, - \rangle)$
.

For each
$V$
we obtain the dual system
$(V, V', e)$
with
$e(v,\phi) = \phi(v)$
.

For finite-dimensional spaces one can easily show that all dual systems
$(V,W,b)$
and
$(V, \hat W, \hat b)$
with the same
$V$
are isomorphic. This means that there exists an isomorphism
$f : W \to \hat W$
such that
$b(x,y) = \hat b(x,f(y))$
for all
$x, y$
.

For the dual systems in 1. and 2. this is the Riesz represenation theorem: Each linear form
$\psi : V \to F$
has the form
$\psi =  \langle x, - \rangle$
for a unique
$x \in V$
.

question:
Let
$X$
be a vector space and let
$d$
be a metric on
$X$
. We know that if the following two properties are satisfied, then
$d$
induces a norm.

$1)$

$d(x+a,y+a)=d(x,y)$
for any
$x,y,a \in X$
.

$2)$

$d(\alpha x, \alpha y) = |\alpha| d(x,y) $
for any
$\alpha \in \mathbb R$
.

I am wondering if we can replace these two conditions as below:

Let
$d$
satisfy the following properties:

$1)$

$d(x+a,y+a)=d(x,y)$
for any
$x,y,a \in X$
.

$2)$

$d(\alpha x, \alpha y) \leq \alpha d(x,y) $
for any
$\alpha \in (0,1)$
.

In this case, does
$d$
induce a norm on
$X$
?

I tried to prove the homogeneity condition by using these properties, but I couldn't.I also tried to find a counterexample, but I found that for all the metrics I know that do not induce a norm, the second property is not satisfied.
answer:
Yes, the second group of properties are equivalent to the first one.

For any
$\alpha\in (0, 1)$
, let
$\beta:=1-\alpha$
, then by the second property, we
$$d(\alpha \cdot x, \alpha \cdot 0)+d(\beta\cdot x, \beta\cdot 0)\le \alpha d(x, 0)+\beta d(x, 0)=d(x, 0)$$

On the other hand, by the first property and the triangle inequality,
$$d(\alpha x, 0)+d(\beta x, 0)= d((\alpha+\beta)x, \beta x)+d(\beta x, 0)\ge d((\alpha+\beta)x, 0)=d(x,0)$$

And the above two combined imply all the inequalities must be actually equalities, thus
$$d(\alpha x, 0)=\alpha d(x, 0)$$

For arbitrary
$r>1$
, we have
$d(x, 0)=d(\frac{1}{r} (rx), 0)=\frac{1}{r} d(rx, 0)$
, hence
$d(rx, 0)=r d(x, 0)$
for arbitrary positive
$r$
. Also note that by the first property and the symmetry of
$d$
,
$$d(-x, 0)=(-x+x, 0+x)=d(0, x)=d(x, 0)$$
which can be used to show
$d(rx, 0)=|r| d(x, 0)$
holds for all negative
$r$
too.

Finally for all
$r, x, y$
,
$$d(rx, ry)=d(rx-ry, 0)=|r|d(x-y, 0)=|r|d(x,y)$$

Note that the second property cannot be replaced by
$d(\alpha x, \alpha y)\ge \alpha d(x,y)$
for
$\alpha\in (0, 1)$
, due to the counterexample on the real line
$\mathbb R$
with metric
$d(x,y)=\frac{|x-y|}{1+|x-y|}$
.

question:
Let
$X$
be a vector space and let
$d$
be a metric on
$X$
. We know that if the following two properties are satisfied, then
$d$
induces a norm.

$1)$

$d(x+a,y+a)=d(x,y)$
for any
$x,y,a \in X$
.

$2)$

$d(\alpha x, \alpha y) = |\alpha| d(x,y) $
for any
$\alpha \in \mathbb R$
.

I am wondering if we can replace these two conditions as below:

Let
$d$
satisfy the following properties:

$1)$

$d(x+a,y+a)=d(x,y)$
for any
$x,y,a \in X$
.

$2)$

$d(\alpha x, \alpha y) \leq \alpha d(x,y) $
for any
$\alpha \in (0,1)$
.

In this case, does
$d$
induce a norm on
$X$
?

I tried to prove the homogeneity condition by using these properties, but I couldn't.I also tried to find a counterexample, but I found that for all the metrics I know that do not induce a norm, the second property is not satisfied.
answer:
Claim
:Let
$X$
be a
$\mathbb{R}-$
vector space such that
$d$
is a metrix on
$X$
which satisfies the following properties:
$(1) d(x+a,y+a)=d(x,y) \text{ for any }x,y,a∈X$
,
$(2) d(αx,αy)≤αd(x,y)\text{ for any }α∈(0,1),$
then
$d$
induces a norm on
$X$
.

Proof
: It suffices to show that
$d(\beta x,\beta y)=|\beta|d(x,y) \text{ for each } x,y\in X, \beta\in \mathbb{R}.$
By
$(1)$
,
$d(x,y)=d(x-x-y,y-x-y)=d(-y,-x)=d(-x,-y)$
.
So, it's enough to prove that
$d(\beta x,\beta y)=\beta d(x,y)$
for each
$x,y\in X, \beta >0$
.
First we extend
$(2)$
to all of
$\alpha\in [0,\infty)$
.
For
$\beta\in \mathbb{R}^{+}$
by
$(1)$
,we get
$d(\beta x,\beta y)=d(\beta x-\frac{\beta x}{2}-\frac{\beta y}{2},\beta y-\frac{\beta x}{2}-\frac{\beta y}{2} )=d(\frac{\beta x}{2}-\frac{\beta y}{2},\frac{\beta y}{2}-\frac{\beta x}{2})$
By triangle inequality , we have
$d(\frac{\beta x}{2}-\frac{\beta y}{2},\frac{\beta y}{2}-\frac{\beta x}{2})\leq d(\frac{\beta x}{2}-\frac{\beta y}{2},0)+d(0,\frac{\beta y}{2}-\frac{\beta x}{2})$
By
$(1)$
, we have
$d(\frac{\beta x}{2}-\frac{\beta y}{2},0)+d(0,\frac{\beta y}{2}-\frac{\beta x}{2})=2d(\frac{\beta x}{2},\frac{\beta y}{2})$
So,
$d(\beta x, \beta y)\leq  2d(\frac{\beta x}{2}, \frac{\beta y } {2})$
.
Combining it with
$(2)$
, we get
$d(\beta x, \beta y)\leq 2d(\frac{\beta x}{2}, \frac{\beta y } {2})\leq 2\frac12d(\beta x,\beta y)=d(\beta x, \beta y).$
So,
$d(\beta x,\beta y)=2d(\frac{\beta x}{2}, \frac{\beta y } {2})$
.
Now, choose
$N\in \mathbb{N}$
large enough so that
$\beta <2^N$
.
So,
$d(\beta x,\beta y)=2^Nd(\frac{\beta x}{2^N},\frac{\beta y}{2^N})\leq 2^N\frac{\beta}{2^N}d( x, y)=\beta d(x,y)$
.Thus,
$(2)$
is now extended to whole
$[0,\infty)$
.
For
$\beta>1$
,
$\frac{1}{\beta }<1$
, so , we get
$d(x,y)=d(\beta\frac{1}{\beta}x,\beta \frac{1}{\beta}y)\leq \frac{1}{\beta}d(\beta x,\beta y)$
.
So, we get the inequality
$d(\beta x,\beta y)=\beta d(x,y)$
for each
$x,y\in X, \beta >1$
.
For
$\beta \in (0,1)$
,
$\frac{1}{\beta}>1$
, so we get
$d(x,y)=d(\beta \frac{x}{\beta },\beta\frac{1}{\beta}y)=\frac{1}{\beta}d(\beta x,\beta y)$
, we are done .

Circle Finding Using Non-Iterative Least Squares

This is a method I developed
a while ago
for fitting a circle
to a set of points
using linear least squares.
I hope it may be useful
to others.

Prologue

In the early 1990's,
I developed a method of fitting
a circle
to a set of data points
using linear least squares.
This is the document I wrote
describing my method.

I am entering it here
in the hope that
this may be useful to others.

Note:
this can be readily generalized
to fitting d-dimensional points
to a d-dimensional ball.

Note 2:
this was converted by hand
from the original LaTex
to MathJax,
so there may be some errors.

Prologue

In the early 1990's,
I developed a method of fitting
a circle
to a set of data points
using linear least squares.
This is the document I wrote
describing my method.

I am entering it here
in the hope that
this may be useful to others.

Note:
this can be readily generalized
to fitting d-dimensional points
to a d-dimensional ball.

Note 2:
this was converted by hand
from the original LaTex
to MathJax,
so there may be some errors.

Introduction

The problem of fitting a circle to a set of points arose
from attempts to fit extended lines.
The technique involved 3 steps:
(1) Fit short segments over small regions;
(2) group together segments that are close;
(3) combine grouped segments
to produce extended lines.
We observed that segments that were grouped together by step (2)
often formed piecewise linear fits to curves.
In these cases,
step (3) produced a number of extended lines
which collectively fit the curve fairly well.
However, since the actual object is curved,
we want to fit a curve to the data.

The segments found in step (1)
are found using the method of least squares.
By keeping the underlying statistics,
this allows step (3) to also be done
using least squares.
It is thus natural to try to use least squares
to fit a curve.
Our hope is that only a few additional statistics
will need to be gathered.

We considered the circle first.
Its equation is the simplest,
it is invariant under rotation
(the equation of other conics become much more complicated when
they are rotated out of standard position),
and is bounded.

The standard application of the method of least squares goes
like this:
The distance between the point
$(x,y)$
and the circle with center at
$(a,b)$
and radius
$r$
is
$$\sqrt{\x}-r.$$
Given a set of points
$(x_i,\,y_i)_{i=1}^n$
,
define the mean squared error
$D$
by
$$D~=~\snqt{\sqrt{\xyi}-r}.$$
The values of
$a$
,
$b$
, and
$r$
are obtained by solving the
equations
$$\DD{a}~=~0,\q
\DD{b}~=~0,\q
{\rm and\q}
\DD{r}~=~0.
\tag{1}\label{1}
$$

However, if you actually evaluate the partial derivatives,
you get a complex, disheartening mess.
There is clearly no simple way
of directly solving the resulting equations.

We therefore tried a simpler expression for
$D$
:
$$D~=~\snqt{\xyi-r^2}.$$
This metric,
instead of minimizing the mean square
distance
from the circle to the data points,
minimizes the mean square
area
between the
candidate circle
(center at
$(a,b)$
, radius
$r$
)
and the circles with center (again) at
$(a,b)$
passing through the data points.

With this simpler metric,
though the solution to (1) might involve nonlinear equations for
$a$
,
$b$
, and
$r$
, at least the square roots would be gone.
As it turns out, while the solution to (1) does involve
nonlinear terms, they occur in such a way that they can be
eliminated.

It is interesting to note that,
since
$$\sqrt{\x}-r~=~{ {\x - r^2} \over {\sqrt{\x}+r} },$$
if the data points actually lie close to a circle,
the different expressions for
$D$
give equivalent results.
To verify this, note that, if
$(x,y)$
is close to the circle with
center
$(a,b)$
and radius
$r$
, then
$$\sqrt{\x} \approx r,$$
so that
$$\sqrt{\x}+r \approx 2r.$$
The expressions for
$D$
will thus differ by a factor of
$4r^2$
.

Historical note:
A literature search has turned up
``A Circle Fitting Procedure
and Its Error Analysis''
by I. Kasa
in IEEE Transactions on Instrumentation
and Measurement,
March 1976, pages 8-14,
which utilizes this same
area-based metric.
This article has further references
that have not been checked,
one dating back to 1950.

Defining notation

We want to find the circle with center at
$(a,b)$
and radius
$r$
that best approximates the data points
$(x_i,y_i)_{i=1}^n$
.

We use the following notation:
For any expression
$f$
,
the
mean value
of
$f$
is written
$\mean{f}$
and is defined by
$$\mean{f}~=~\snm{f_i}.$$

For example,
$$\begin{array}{rl}
\mean{(x-a)^2} ~&=~ \snm{(x_i-a)^2}\\
~&=~ \snm{(x_i^2 - 2 a x_i + a^2)}\\
~&=~ \snm{x_i^2} - 2 a \snm{x_i} + a^2\\
~&=~ \mean{x^2} - 2 a \mean{x} + a^2.
\end{array}
$$

We define the covariance
$\cov{p}{q}$
between two expressions
$p$
and
$q$
by
$$\cov{p}{q}~=~\mean{pq} - \mean{p} \mean{q}.$$
For example,
$$\cov{x}{x}~=~\mean{x^2}-\mean{x}^2$$
and
$$\cov{x}{y}~=~\mean{xy}-\mean{x}\mean{y}.$$

We write the equation of the circle as
$$x^2 - 2ax + y^2 - 2by - c~=~0.$$
This equation is
linear
in its parameters
(
$a$
,
$b$
, and
$c$
)
and, if
$$c~=~r^2-a^2-b^2,$$
is equivalent to
$$(x-a)^2 + (y-b)^2 ~=~ r^2.$$
Once the least squares fit has been done,
yielding values for
$a$
,
$b$
, and
$c$
,
we can recover the radius
$r$
by
$$r~=~\sqrt{a^2 + b^2 + c}.$$

We define
$$Q_i~=~x_i^2 + y_i^2
\hq({\rm or,\hq equivalently\hq}
Q~=~x^2+y^2),$$
$$d_i~=~x_i^2 - 2ax_i + y_i^2 - 2by_i - c
~=~ Q_i - 2ax_i - 2by_i - c,$$
$$({\rm or}\hq
d~=~x^2-2ax+y^2-2by-c
~=~Q-2ax-2by-c)$$
and
$$D~=~\mean{d^2}~=~\snm{d_i^2}.$$

Our goal is to
find the best values
for
$a$
,
$b$
, and
$c$
in the least squares sense
by minimizing
$D$
.
We do this
by solving the equations
$$\DD{a}~=~0,\q
\DD{b}~=~0,\q
{\rm and\q}
\DD{c}~=~0.$$

Setting up the equations

For any variable
$v$
(one of
$a$
,
$b$
, or
$c$
),
$$\ddt{v}~=~2 d \d{v}$$
and
$$\DD{v}~=~\mean{\ddt{v}}
~=~ 2 \mean{d \d{v}}.$$

From the definition of
$d$
,
$$\d{c}~=~-1,\q
\d{a}~=~-2(x-a),\q
{\rm and\q}
\d{b}~=~-2(y-b).$$

We thus have
$$\DD{c}~=~- 2 \mean{d},\q
\DD{a}~=~-4 \mean{(x-a)d},\q
{\rm and\q}
\DD{b}~=~-4 \mean{(y-b)d},$$
so that the equations we need to solve are
$$\mean{d}~=~0,\hq
\mean{(x-a)d}~=~0,\hq
{\rm and\hq}
\mean{(y-b)d}~=~0.$$

Solving the equations

The first equation implies
$$\begin{array}{rl}
0 ~&=~ \mean{d}\\
~&=~ \mean{x^2 - 2ax + y^2 - 2by - c}\\
~&=~ \mean{x^2+y^2} - 2a\mean{x} - 2b\mean{y} - c\\
~&=~ \mean{Q} - 2a\mean{x} - 2b\mean{y} - c.\\
& ({\rm since}\hq Q~=~x^2+y^2)
\end{array}$$

The second equation implies
$\mean{xd}~=~a\mean{d}$
,
so that
$\mean{xd}~=~0$
.
Thus,
$$\begin{array}{rl}
0 ~&=~ \mean{xd}\\
~&=~ \mean{x(x^2-2ax+y^2-2by-c)}\\
~&=~ \mean{x(x^2+y^2)} - 2a\mean{x^2} - 2b\mean{xy} - c\mean{x}\\
~&=~ \mean{xQ} - 2a\mean{x^2} - 2b\mean{xy} - c\mean{x}.
\end{array}$$

Similarly,
the third equation implies
$\mean{yd}~=~b\mean{d}$
,
so that
$\mean{yd}~=~0$
.
Thus,
$$\begin{array}{rl}
0 ~&=~ \mean{yd}\\
~&=~ \mean{y(x^2-2ax+y^2-2by-c)}\\
~&=~ \mean{y(x^2+y^2)} - 2a\mean{xy} - 2b\mean{y^2} - c\mean{y}\\
~&=~ \mean{yQ} - 2a\mean{xy} - 2b\mean{y^2} - c\mean{y}.
\end{array}$$

We thus get the following system of equations
for
$a$
,
$b$
, and
$c$
:
$$\begin{array}{ccccccc}
2a\mean{x}\hfill  &+ &2b\mean{y}\hfill  &+ &c\hfill &=~ &\hfill \mean{Q}\\
2a\mean{x^2}\hfill  &+ &2b\mean{xy}\hfill  &+ &c\mean{x}\hfill &=~ &\hfill \mean{xQ}\\
2a\mean{xy}\hfill  &+ &2b\mean{y^2}\hfill  &+ &c\mean{y}\hfill &=~ &\hfill \mean{yQ}
\end{array}
\tag{2}\label{2}
$$

$c$
can be eliminated
from these equations
by subtracting
$\mean{x}$
times the first equation
from the second
and
$\mean{y}$
times the first equation
from the third.
Writing the resulting equations
in terms of the covariance
(
$\cov{p}{q}~=~\mean{pq} - \mean{p} \mean{q}$
)
we get these equations:

$$\begin{array}{ccccc}\\
a\cov{x}{x} + b\cov{x}{y}
&= \frac12 \cov{x}{Q}\\
a\cov{x}{y} + b\cov{y}{y}
&= \frac12 \cov{y}{Q}\\
\end{array}
$$

These equations
can be explicitly solved
by determinants to get
$$a~=~\Delta_a / \Delta,
\q{\rm and}\q
b~=~\Delta_b / \Delta
\tag{4a}\label{4a}$$
where
$$\begin{array}{rlr}
\Delta~&=~\cov{x}{x}\cov{y}{y} - \cov{x}{y}^2,\\
\Delta_a~&=~\cov{x}{Q}\cov{y}{y} - \cov{y}{Q}\cov{x}{y} / 2,\\
\Delta_b~&=~\cov{y,Q}\cov{x}{x} - \cov{x}{Q}\cov{x}{y} / 2.
\tag{4b}\label{(4b}
\end{array}$$
Once these equations
have been solved
for
$a$
and
$b$
,
we can find
$c$
from the first equation of (2)
by
$$c~=~\mean{Q} - 2a\mean{x} - 2b\mean{y}
\tag{4c}\label{(4c}$$
and then find
$r$
by
$$r^2~=~a^2+b^2+c.
\tag{4d}\label{(4d}$$

Since
$\mean{d}~=~0$
and
$d~=~(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2-r^2$
,
we get the following
interesting result:
$$r^2~=~\mean{(x-a)^2+(y-b)^2}.$$

Computing the error

The mean squared error
$D$
can be computed as follows:
$$\begin{array}{rl}
D&~=~ \mean{d^2}\\
&~=~ \mean{d(x^2 - 2ax + y^2 - 2by - c)}\\
&~=~ \mean{d(x^2+y^2)}\\
&({\rm since\ } \mean{d}~=~\mean{xd}~=~\mean{yd}~=~0)\\
&~=~ \mean{dQ}\\
&~=~ \mean{(x^2 - 2ax + y^2 - 2by - c)Q}\\
&~=~ \mean{Q^2} - 2a\mean{xQ} - 2b\mean{yQ} - c\mean{Q}\\
&~=~ \mean{Q^2} - 2a\mean{xQ} - 2b\mean{yQ}
- (\mean{Q}-2a\mean{x}-2b\mean{y})\mean{Q}\\
&~=~ (\mean{Q^2}-\mean{Q}^2)
-2a(\mean{xQ}-\mean{x}\mean{Q})
-2b(\mean{yQ}-\mean{y}\mean{Q})\\
&~=~ \cov{Q}{Q} - 2a\cov{x}{Q} - 2b\cov{y}{Q}.
\end{array}$$

$D$
can thus be computed
either from mean values
and
$a$
,
$b$
, and
$c$
by
$$D~=~ \mean{Q^2} - 2a\mean{xQ} - 2b\mean{yQ} - c\mean{Q}
\tag{5a}\label{5a}$$
or from covariances
and just
$a$
and
$b$
by
$$D~=~ \cov{Q}{Q} - 2a\cov{x}{Q} - 2b\cov{y}{Q}.\tag{5b}\label(5b)$$

Note that the only additional statistics
needed beyond those used for a linear fit
(which occur on the left side of
(2))
are
$\mean{Q}$
,
$\mean{xQ}$
,
$\mean{yQ}$
, and
$\mean{Q^2}$
.
Since
$\mean{Q}~=~\mean{x^2} + \mean{y^2}$
,
only
$\mean{xQ}$
and
$\mean{yQ}$
need to be gathered
to actually {\it do}
the least squares fit,
with
$\mean{Q^2}$
being needed
only if the mean squared error is wanted.

The expressions for
$D$
in (5a) and (5b)
are quite useful.
Remember, however,
that
$$D~=~\,\mean{(\x - r^2)^2}.$$
This is fine by itself,
but may be hard to use
if other types of curves
(such as a straight line)
are to be fit to the data points
and the best fitting curve chosen.
In this case,
we would like to use
$$D^*~=~\,\mean{(\qx - r)^2}.$$

$D^*$
cannot be directly computed
from the statistics --
it can only be computed
from the original data points.
If they are not available,
$D^*$
can be estimated
as follows:
$$\begin{array}{rl}
D&=\,\mean{(\x - r^2)^2}\\
&=\,\mean{(\qx-r)^2 (\qx+r)^2}.
\end{array}$$
If the data points
are close to the
estimated circle,
$$\x \approx r^2$$
so that
$$\qx + r \approx 2r.$$
Substituting this
in the expression above for
$D$
,
$$\begin{array}{rl}
D&\approx\,\mean{4r^2 (\qx-r)^2}\\
&=\,4r^2 D^*,
\end{array}$$
so that
$$D^* \approx D / 4r^2.\tag{6}\label{6}$$

If the original data points
are available,
and
$D^*$
is the desired
expression for the error
that is to be minimized,
the values for
$a$
,
$b$
, and
$r$
computed in (4)
can be used
as the initial values
in a non-linear
least squares routine
to find the
minimum value of
$D^*$
.

Computational experience and analysis

The simplicity of these results is surprising,
so we implemented them
to test their validity.
Since we derived equations (2) first,
they were tested first
and found to give
good values for
$a$
,
$b$
, and
$r$
.

The equations (2) are set up in double precision
and solved using Gaussian elimination
with partial pivoting.
(The actual algorithm is that in
algorithms 5 and 6 in
J.~C.~Nash,
{\it Compact Numerical Methods for Computers},
Halsted Press,
1979.)

Equations (2) are simple enough
that they can be solved symbolically,
and an explicit solution found.
This was done, and the two solutions compared.

We had expected that the explicit solution
would be more accurate
than the solution via elimination
(after all,
it was the {\it explicit} solution).
To our surprise, this did not hold.
In fact,
when
$r$
was small compared with
$a$
and
$b$
,
the explicit solution could be
many orders of magnitude
{\it worse}
than the solution obtained via elimination.

To show why this happens,
consider the case when the data points
are many and lie equally spaced
on the circle.
For this case,
$$x_i ~=~ a + r \cos \theta_i
{\rm\hq and\hq}
y_i ~=~ b + r \sin \theta_i$$
where
$$\theta_i ~=~ 2\pi (i-1)/n.$$

As the number of points grows large,
$$\mean{x^j y^k} \approx
\imean{(a+r\cos\theta)^j (b+r\sin\theta)^k}.$$
This can be evaluated explicitly
for the terms in (2), (3), and (5),
using the recurrance
(equation 4.3.127
in {\it Handbook of Mathematical Functions},
Abramowitz and Stegun,
Dover Publications,
New York, 1965)
$$\begin{array}{rl}
\int \sin^m z \cos^n z\,dz
&= {\sin^{m+1}z \cos^{n-1}z \over m+n}
+ {n-1 \over m+n}
\int \sin^m z \cos^{n-2}z\,dz\\
&= -{\sin^{m-1}z \cos^{n+1}z \over m+n}
+ {m-1 \over m+n}
\int \sin^{m-2}z \cos^nz\,dz
\end{array}$$
which implies that
$$\begin{array}{rl}
\int_0^{2\pi} \sin^m \theta \cos^n \theta\,d\theta
&= {n-1 \over m+n} \int_0^{2\pi}
\sin^m \theta \cos^{n-2}\theta\,d\theta\\
&= {m-1 \over m+n} \int_0^{2\pi}
\sin^{m-2}\theta \cos^n\theta\,d\theta.
\end{array}$$

The results are:
$$\mean{x}~=~a,\q
\mean{y}~=~b,\q
\mean{xy}~=~ab,$$
$$\mean{x^2}~=~a^2 + r^2/2,\q
\mean{y^2}~=~b^2 + r^2/2,$$
$$\mean{x^3}~=~a^3 + 3 a r^2 / 2,\q
\mean{y^3}~=~b^3 + 3 b r^2 / 2,$$
$$\mean{x^2y}~=~a^2b + b r^2 /2,\q
\mean{xy^2}~=~ab^2 + a r^2 / 2,$$
$$\mean{x^4}~=~a^4 + 3a^2r^2 + 3r^4/8,\q
\mean{y^4}~=~b^4 + 3b^2r^2 + 3r^4/8,$$
$$\mean{x^2y^2}~=~a^2b^2 + r^2(a^2+b^2)/2 + r^4/8,$$
$$\mean{Q}~=~a^2 + b^2 + r^2,\q
\mean{Q^2}~=~(a^2+b^2)^2 + 4r^2(a^2+b^2) + r^2,$$
$$\mean{xQ}~=~a^3 + ab^2 + 2ar^2,\q
\mean{yQ}~=~b^3 + a^2b + 2br^2,$$
$$\cov{x}{x}~=~r^2/2,\q
\cov{y}{y}~=~r^2/2,\q
\cov{x}{y}~=~0,$$
$$\cov{x}{Q}~=~ar^2,\q
\cov{y}{Q}~=~br^2,\q
\cov{Q}{Q}~=~2r^2(a^2+b^2).$$

It is interesting to note that
the covariances are much simpler
than the mean values.

When these values
are substituted into equations (2) and (3),
we get the following systems of equations,
where we have written
$A$
,
$B$
, and
$C$
for the
values of
$a$
,
$b$
, and
$c$
to be found:
$$\begin{array}{ccccccc}
2aA &+ & 2bB &+ & C ~&=~ &a^2 + b^2 + r^2\\
2(a^2+r^2/2)A &+ & 2abB &+ & aC ~&=~ &a^3 + ab^2 + 2ar^2\\
2abA &+ & 2(b^2+r^2/2)B &+ & bC ~&=~ &b^3 + a^2b + 2br^2
\end{array}
\tag{2'}\label{2'}
$$
and
$$\begin{array}{ccccc}
Ar^2 &+ &0 ~&=~ &ar^2\\
0 &+ &Br^2 ~&=~ &br^2
\end{array}
\tag{3'}\label{3'} $$

Note that (
$3'$
)
is somewhat easier to solve
than (
$2'$
).

As a check on the algebra,
using (5),
$$\begin{array}{rl}
D~&=~ \cov{Q}{Q} - 2a\cov{x}{Q} - 2b\cov{y}{Q}\\
&=~2r^2(a^2+b^2) -2a(ar^2) - 2b(br^2)\\
&=~0
\end{array}$$
as it should be,
since all the points
lie {\it on} the circle.

Solving (
$2'$
)
by determinants
will not work well
if
$r$
is small compared with
$a$
and
$b$
.
The determinant of (
$2'$
) is
$r^4$
,
while individual terms are typically
$4a^2b^2$
.
The loss of precision
is thus of order
$a^2b^2/r^4$
.
If
$r=1$
and
$a=b=1000$
,
12 significant figures will be lost,
so that even double precision computation
will give only 2 reliable figures
out of 14 figures available.

Even if (
$2'$
) is explicitly solved
by expansion by minors,
similar problems occur,
though the cancellation
is not as extreme.
After some simplification,
the computations that take place
for finding the determinant,
$a$
,
$b$
,
and
$c$
, respectively are
$$det~=~r^2(2a^2+r^2) - 2a^2r^2,$$
$$A~=~{ ar^2(a^2+b^2+2r^2) - ar^2(a^2+b^2+r^2) \over det },$$
$$B~=~{ br^2(a^2+b^2+2r^2) - br^2(a^2+b^2+r^2) \over det },$$
and
$$C~=~{ r^2(a^2+b^2+r^2)(2(a^2+b^2)+r^2)
- 2a^2r^2(a^2+b^2+2r^2)
- 2b^2r^2(a^2+b^2+2r^2)
\over det }.$$
If
$a \approx b$
and
$a$
is large compared with
$r$
,
the loss of precision
in each of these expressions is
of the order
$a^2/r^2$
.
If
$a \approx 1000$
and
$r \approx 1$
,
6 significant figures will be lost.

In contrast,
(
$3'$
) is a {\it diagonal} system,
and is immediately solvable
with no loss of accuracy,
giving the exact values for
$A$
and
$B$
.

Another indication
of why problems can occur
when
$r$
is small compared with
$a$
and
$b$
is that
$r^2~=~a^2+b^2+c$
.
This implies that,
in this case,
$c$
must be close to
$-(a^2+b^2)$
,
and thus must be computed
very accurately
for the value of
$r$
to be correct.

Summary of circle fitting algorithm

The technique for fitting a circle
to a set of data points is thus:

(1)Gather the needed mean values:

$\mean{x}$
,
$\mean{y}$
,
$\mean{xy}$
,
$\mean{x^2}$
,
$\mean{y^2}$
,
$\mean{xQ}$
,
$\mean{yQ}$
,
and
$\mean{Q^2}$
,
where
$Q~=~x^2+y^2$
.

(2)
Compute the covariances

$\cov{x}{x}$
,
$\cov{y}{y}$
,
$\cov{x}{y}$
,
$\cov{x}{Q}$
, and
$\cov{y}{Q}$
and solve these equations for
$a$
and
$b$
:
$$\begin{array}{ccccc}
a\cov{x}{x} &+ &b\cov{x}{y} &=~ &\cov{x}{Q} \big/ 2\\
a\cov{x}{y} &+ &b\cov{y}{y} &=~ &\cov{y}{Q} \big/ 2
\end{array}
$$
The center of the circle
is at
$(a,~b)$
.

(3)
Get
$c$
by
$$c~=~\mean{Q} - 2a\mean{x} - 2b\mean{y}$$
and the radius of the circle
$r$
by
$$r~=~\sqrt{a^2+b^2+c}.$$

(4)
Compute the mean squared error
$D$
by
$$D~=~ \cov{Q}{Q} - 2a\cov{x}{Q} - 2b\cov{y}{Q}.$$
If
$$D^*~=~\,\mean{(\qx - r)^2}$$
is wanted,
it can be estimated by
$$D^* \approx D / 4r^2.$$

question:
This post is based on an observation, and request vetting:

Euler gave a faulty formula:

$E(n) = n^2 -n +41, n\in \mathbb{Z+},$
though it was obvious that for
$n=41$
would have the quadratic equation:

$n^2 -n +n \implies n^2.$

Let, us take a constant integer value
$m=41.$

Coming to the next integer value for
$n=42,$
get:

$(m+1)^2 -(m+1) +m \implies m(m+2).$

However, it can be seen that for
$n=43,$
we get a prime
$1847,$
as the discriminant of the quadratic equation is not a perfect square.

I.e.,
$(m+2)^2 -(m+2) +m\implies m^2+4m+2$
with discriminant
$=8,$
which is not a perfect square. It leads to two roots in the real number space.

Similarly, for all values of
$n \leq 40,$
have a negative discriminant, leading to roots in the complex number space.

Say, for
$n=40,$
:

$(m-1)^2 -(m+1) +m\implies m^2-2m+2;$
have negative discriminant.

Also, this occurs for all values of
$n$
till
$n=1.$
answer:
So, you start with
$n^2-n+41$
, set
$m=41$
to get
$n^2-n+m$
, and substitute
$n=m+k$
to get
$m^2+2km+k^2-k$
, is that right? Then you try to factorise this, by asking what the discriminant is, yes?

There's nothing wrong with this approach. It will tell you some
$E(n)$
that are not prime, but you can't conclude that the ones it misses are prime.

When I work it out, I get a discriminant of
$4k$
. This will be a perfect square whenever
$k=a^2$
, and in fact:

$(m+1)^2 - (m+1) + m=m(m+1)$
, as you observed, reflecting the fact that
$E(42)$
is not prime.
$(m+4)^2 - (m+4) + m=(m+2)(m+6)$
, so
$E(45)$
is not prime
$(m+9)^2 - (m+9) + m=(m+6)(m+12)$
, so
$E(50)$
is not prime,
$(m+16)^2 - (m+16) + m=(m+12)(m+20)$
, so
$E(57)$
is not prime,

and so on.

There are other
$k$
for which
$E(41+k)$
is not prime, even though the discriminant is not a square: for example,
$k=41, 42, 44, 47$
yield polynomials
$p(m)$
that don't factorise, but
$p(41)$
is not prime (the corresponding
$E(n)$
are
$41\times163$
,
$41\times167$
,
$43\times167$
and
$43\times179$
)

question:
There are many, many connections between the trigonometric and hyperbolic functions, some of which are listed here. It is probably too optimistic to expect that a single insight could explain all of these connections, but is there a holistic way of seeing the parallels between
$\sin$
and
$\sinh$
,
$\cos$
and
$\cosh$
? Can all of these seemingly disparate connections be shown to be essentially the same, or at least very similar?

Geometric connections

Sine and cosine parameterise the unit circle
$x^2+y^2=1$
, just as hyperbolic sine and cosine parameterise the 'unit hyperbola'
$x^2-y^2=1$
. Both circles and hyperbolas are
conic sections
.

The sector of the circle connecting the points
$(0,0)$
,
$(1,0)$
, and
$(\cos t,\sin t)$
has an area of
$t/2$
. The region of the hyperbola connecting the points
$(0,0)$
,
$(1,0)$
, and
$(\cosh t,\sinh t)$
has an area of
$t/2$
. This can even be used to define the hyperbolic functions
geometrically
, and many authors do the same with the trigonometric functions.

Sine and hyperbolic sine are odd, whereas cosine and hyperbolic cosine are even.
But
sine and cosine are periodic functions, unlike the hyperbolic counterparts.

The analogue of the identity
$\cos^2x+\sin^2x \equiv 1$
is
$\cosh^2x-\sinh^2x \equiv 1$
. The compound angle formulae are almost identical to their hyperbolic counterparts, save for a pesky minus sign:

\begin{align}
\sin(x+y) &= \sin(x)\cos(y)+\cos(x)\sin(y) \\
\sinh(x+y) &= \sinh(x)\cosh(y) + \cosh(x)\sinh(y) \\[4pt]
\cos(x+y) &= \cos(x)\cos(y) \color{red}{-} \sin(x)\sin(y) \\
\cosh(x+y) &= \cosh(x)\cosh(y) \color{blue}{+} \sinh(x)\sinh(y) \, .
\end{align}

In general, given a trigonometric identity, it is (usually) possible to find a corresponding hyperbolic identity using Osborn's rule: replace every occurrence of
$\cos$
with
$\cosh$
; replace every occurrence of
$\sin$
by
$\sinh$
,
but
negate the product of two
$\sinh$
terms.

Analytic connections

$\sin$
is the unique solution to the initial value problem

\begin{align}
f''(x) &= \color{red}{-}f(x) \\
f'(0) &= 1 \\
f(0) &= 0 \, ,
\end{align}

and the corresponding initial value problem for
$\sinh$
is the same, except
$f''(x) = \color{blue}{+}f(x)$
.

Likewise, the initial value problem for
$\cos$
is

\begin{align}
f''(x) &= \color{red}{-}f(x) \\
f'(0) &= 0 \\
f(0) &= 1 \, ,
\end{align}

and again we see a mysterious sign change for
$\cosh$
:
$f''(x) = \color{blue}{+}f(x)$
.

It follows that the higher-order derivatives of
$\sin$
and
$\sinh$
form periodic sequences.

If we solve the initial value problems shown above, we obtain the exponential forms of all
$4$
functions:

\begin{align}
\sin x &= \frac{e^{\color{green}{i}x}-e^{-\color{green}{i}x}}{2\color{green}{i}} \quad{} \cos x = \frac{e^{\color{\green}{i}x}+ e^{-\color{green}{i}x}}{2} \\[3pt]
\sinh x &= \frac{e^{x}-e^{-x}}{2} \quad{} \cosh x = \frac{e^x + e^{-x}}{2} \, .
\end{align}

All
$4$
functions are analytic, and their Taylor series bear a striking resemblance to each other:

\begin{align}
\sin x &= x \color{red}{-} \frac{x^3}{3!} + \frac{x^5}{5!} \color{red}{-} \frac{x^7}{7!} + \ldots \\[4pt]
\sinh x &= x \color{blue}{+} \frac{x^3}{3!} + \frac{x^5}{5!} \color{blue}{+} \frac{x^7}{7!} + \ldots \\[4pt]
\cos x &= 1 \color{red}{-} \frac{x^2}{2!} + \frac{x^4}{4!} \color{red}{-} \frac{x^6}{6!} + \ldots \\[4pt]
\cosh x &= 1 \color{blue}{+} \frac{x^2}{2!} + \frac{x^4}{4!} \color{blue}{+} \frac{x^6}{6!} + \ldots
\end{align}

And Euler's formula

$$
e^{ix} = \cos x + i \sin x
$$

is replaced by the underwhelming

$$
e^x = \cosh x + \sinh x \, .
$$
answer:
I my opinion I think that when you know

$$\sin(x) = \frac{e^{ix}-e^{-ix}}{2i} ~~~~~~~~~~~~~ \cos(x) = \frac{e^{ix} + e^{-ix}}{2}$$

you can derive all the circular trigonometric identities.

If you add the "Wick" transformation
$ x \to ix$
then you will step into the hyperbolic world, with all the consequent identities.

$$\sin(ix) = \frac{e^{-x} - e^{x}}{2i} = i\frac{e^x - e^{-x}}{2} = i\sinh(x) ~~~~~ \longrightarrow ~~~~~ \sinh(x) = \frac{e^x + e^{-x}}{2}$$

And similarly for
$\cosh(x)$
.

\begin{align}

\end{align}

All
$4$

\begin{align}
\sin x &= x \color{red}{-} \frac{x^3}{3!} + \frac{x^5}{5!} \color{red}{-} \frac{x^7}{7!} + \ldots \\[4pt]
\sinh x &= x \color{blue}{+} \frac{x^3}{3!} + \frac{x^5}{5!} \color{blue}{+} \frac{x^7}{7!} + \ldots \\[4pt]
\cos x &= 1 \color{red}{-} \frac{x^2}{2!} + \frac{x^4}{4!} \color{red}{-} \frac{x^6}{6!} + \ldots \\[4pt]
\cosh x &= 1 \color{blue}{+} \frac{x^2}{2!} + \frac{x^4}{4!} \color{blue}{+} \frac{x^6}{6!} + \ldots
\end{align}

And Euler's formula

$$

$$

answer:
Yes; in fact they can all be traced to the same point, and it is a point that you brought up in your question. One important thing to note is that in Math there can be many equivalent definitions of concepts that can lead to each other, for instance one can define
$\pi$
as the ratio of circumference to diameter; or area to radius squared. Both are equivalent.

Similarly we can define these functions geometrically,

And from that obtain differential equations that describe the functions, and from that obtain their exponential definitions; this can be used to unify all of the points you brought up, the Pythagorean Identity, sum of angles, Taylor Series, and exponential definition.

Alternatively we could have defined these functions in terms of their exponential definitions (As @Turing suggests), and from that obtain the Pythagorean Identity, sum of angles, Taylor Series, and geometric definition.

The latter is much easier, but the former is how we came to understanding this family of functions historically.

.

is

and again we see a mysterious sign change for

:

answer:
The trigonometric functions are related to the imaginary exponential
$e^{iv}=\cos v+i\sin v$
.

The hyperbolic functions are related to the real exponential
$e^{u}=\cosh u+\sinh u$
.

They both are special cases of the complex exponential
$e^z=e^{u+iv}$
.

The associated conics are obtained by

$$1=e^{iv}e^{-iv}=(\cos v+i\sin v)(\cos v-i\sin v)=\cos^2u-i^2\sin^2u,$$

$$1=e^{v}e^{-v}=(\cosh v+\sinh v)(\cosh v-\sinh v)=\cosh^2u-\sinh^2u.$$

A unifying ODE is

$$z''''=z$$
giving the characteristic polynomial
$$\omega^4=1,\\\omega=\pm1,\pm i$$

and the solution

$$z=a\cos t+b\sin t+c\cosh t+d\sinh t.$$

Just think

$$\text{real}\leftrightarrow\text{imaginary}.$$

question:
There are many, many connections between the trigonometric and hyperbolic functions, some of which are listed here. It is probably too optimistic to expect that a single insight could explain all of these connections, but is there a holistic way of seeing the parallels between
$\sin$
and
$\sinh$
,
$\cos$
and
$\cosh$
? Can all of these seemingly disparate connections be shown to be essentially the same, or at least very similar?

Geometric connections

Sine and cosine parameterise the unit circle
$x^2+y^2=1$
, just as hyperbolic sine and cosine parameterise the 'unit hyperbola'
$x^2-y^2=1$
. Both circles and hyperbolas are
conic sections
.

The sector of the circle connecting the points
$(0,0)$
,
$(1,0)$
, and
$(\cos t,\sin t)$
has an area of
$t/2$
. The region of the hyperbola connecting the points
$(0,0)$
,
$(1,0)$
, and
$(\cosh t,\sinh t)$
has an area of
$t/2$
. This can even be used to define the hyperbolic functions
geometrically
, and many authors do the same with the trigonometric functions.

Sine and hyperbolic sine are odd, whereas cosine and hyperbolic cosine are even.
But
sine and cosine are periodic functions, unlike the hyperbolic counterparts.

The analogue of the identity
$\cos^2x+\sin^2x \equiv 1$
is
$\cosh^2x-\sinh^2x \equiv 1$
. The compound angle formulae are almost identical to their hyperbolic counterparts, save for a pesky minus sign:

\begin{align}
\sin(x+y) &= \sin(x)\cos(y)+\cos(x)\sin(y) \\
\sinh(x+y) &= \sinh(x)\cosh(y) + \cosh(x)\sinh(y) \\[4pt]
\cos(x+y) &= \cos(x)\cos(y) \color{red}{-} \sin(x)\sin(y) \\
\cosh(x+y) &= \cosh(x)\cosh(y) \color{blue}{+} \sinh(x)\sinh(y) \, .
\end{align}

In general, given a trigonometric identity, it is (usually) possible to find a corresponding hyperbolic identity using Osborn's rule: replace every occurrence of
$\cos$
with
$\cosh$
; replace every occurrence of
$\sin$
by
$\sinh$
,
but
negate the product of two
$\sinh$
terms.

Analytic connections

$\sin$
is the unique solution to the initial value problem

\begin{align}
f''(x) &= \color{red}{-}f(x) \\
f'(0) &= 1 \\
f(0) &= 0 \, ,
\end{align}

and the corresponding initial value problem for
$\sinh$
is the same, except
$f''(x) = \color{blue}{+}f(x)$
.

Likewise, the initial value problem for
$\cos$
is

\begin{align}
f''(x) &= \color{red}{-}f(x) \\
f'(0) &= 0 \\
f(0) &= 1 \, ,
\end{align}

and again we see a mysterious sign change for
$\cosh$
:
$f''(x) = \color{blue}{+}f(x)$
.

It follows that the higher-order derivatives of
$\sin$
and
$\sinh$
form periodic sequences.

If we solve the initial value problems shown above, we obtain the exponential forms of all
$4$
functions:

\begin{align}
\sin x &= \frac{e^{\color{green}{i}x}-e^{-\color{green}{i}x}}{2\color{green}{i}} \quad{} \cos x = \frac{e^{\color{\green}{i}x}+ e^{-\color{green}{i}x}}{2} \\[3pt]
\sinh x &= \frac{e^{x}-e^{-x}}{2} \quad{} \cosh x = \frac{e^x + e^{-x}}{2} \, .
\end{align}

All
$4$
functions are analytic, and their Taylor series bear a striking resemblance to each other:

\begin{align}
\sin x &= x \color{red}{-} \frac{x^3}{3!} + \frac{x^5}{5!} \color{red}{-} \frac{x^7}{7!} + \ldots \\[4pt]
\sinh x &= x \color{blue}{+} \frac{x^3}{3!} + \frac{x^5}{5!} \color{blue}{+} \frac{x^7}{7!} + \ldots \\[4pt]
\cos x &= 1 \color{red}{-} \frac{x^2}{2!} + \frac{x^4}{4!} \color{red}{-} \frac{x^6}{6!} + \ldots \\[4pt]
\cosh x &= 1 \color{blue}{+} \frac{x^2}{2!} + \frac{x^4}{4!} \color{blue}{+} \frac{x^6}{6!} + \ldots
\end{align}

And Euler's formula

$$
e^{ix} = \cos x + i \sin x
$$

is replaced by the underwhelming

$$
e^x = \cosh x + \sinh x \, .
$$
answer:
The connection between the trigonometric and hyperbolic functions becomes more intimate when one introduces
split-complex numbers
: the numbers of the form
$a+bj$
, where
$j^2=1$
and
$j\ne \pm 1$
.

Using Taylor series you can easily find that
$$
e^{jx} = \cosh x + j\sinh x , \quad
\sinh x = \frac{e^{jx}-e^{-jx}}{2j}, \quad \cosh x = \frac{e^{jx} + e^{-jx}}{2}.
$$

One can understand
$\cos x$
and
$\sin x$
as the real and imaginary parts of the complex exponential
$e^{ix}$
. Likewise,
$\cosh x$
and
$\sinh x$
are the real and 'imaginary' parts of the split-complex exponential
$e^{jx}$
.

The analogy can be summarized as follows:
$$
\begin{align}
& &&\textbf{Complex numbers} && \textbf{Split-complex numbers} \\
&\text{Imaginary unit:} &&  i^2 = -1, &&    j^2 = 1, \\
&\text{Numbers:}  && z = a+bi,  &&    z = a+bj,  \\
&\text{Real part:}  && \mathfrak{R}(a+bi)=a,  &&   \mathfrak{R}(a+bj)=a,  \\
&\text{Conjugation:}  && z=a+bi\mapsto \bar{z}=a-bi,  &&    z=a+bj\mapsto \bar{z}=a-bj,  \\
&\text{Sesquilinear form:}  && \langle z|w\rangle= z\bar{w},  && \langle z|w\rangle= z\bar{w}  ,  \\
&\text{Real bilinear form:}  && \mathfrak{R}(z\bar{w})\quad \text{(positive definite)},  && \mathfrak{R}(z\bar{w}) \quad\text{(of indefinite signature)}  ,  \\
&\text{Other real bilinear form:}  && \mathfrak{R}(zw)\quad \text{(of indefinite signature)},  && \mathfrak{R}(zw) \quad\text{(positive definite)}  ,  \\
&\text{Squared modulus:}  && |z|^2=\langle z|z\rangle = a^2+b^2, && |z|^2=\langle z|z\rangle = a^2-b^2, \\
&\text{Euler's formula:}  &&  e^{ix} = \cos x + i \sin x, &&   e^{jx} = \cosh x + j \sinh x, \\
&\text{Pythagorean theorem:} &&  |e^{ix}| = \cos^2 x + \sin^2 x =1,  &&   |e^{jx}| = \cosh^2 x - \sinh^2 x = 1,  \\
&\text{Geometric meaning:} &&  \text{Unit circle,}  &&   \text{Unit hyperbola (right branch)}.  \\
\end{align}
$$

The connection is in the correspondence:
$$
\begin{pmatrix} \text{complex} \; i \\ \sin, \; \cos  \end{pmatrix} \leftrightarrow \begin{pmatrix} \text{split-complex} \; j \\ \sinh, \; \cosh  \end{pmatrix}.
$$

One can use imagination to say that
$\sin$
,
$\cos$
and complex numbers come from
the elliptic world
, while
$\sinh$
,
$\cosh$
and split-complex numbers are their counterparts from
the hyperbolic world
.

answer:
Closed relation of the trigonometrical and hyperbolic functions can be demonstrated on the example of the Chebyshev Polyhomials of the First Kind
$$T_n(x) =
\begin{cases}
\cos(n\arccos x),\;\text{if}\;|x|\le 1\\
\cosh(n\text{ arccosh } x),\;\text{otherwize}.
\end{cases}
$$
This example shows that hyperbolic functions can be suitable addition to the trigonometrical ones in the real analysis.

answer:
The
Jacobi elliptic functions
,
in particular the sine and cosine of the amplitude are also connected to
the hyperbolic trigonometric functions using the
Gudermannian function
which relates the circular functions and hyperbolic functions without
explicitly using complex numbers.
More precisely, if the parameter
$\,m=0\,$
then
$$ \text{sn}(x,0) = \sin(x)\quad \text{ and }
\quad \text{cn}(x,0) = \cos(x) $$
while if
$\,m=1\,$
then
$$ \text{sn}(x,1) = \tanh(x)\quad \text{ and }
\quad \text{cn}(x,1) = \text{sech}(x). $$
The Gudermannian function links the two cases with the identity
$$ \sin(\text{gd}(x)) = \tanh(x) \quad \text{ and }
\quad \tan(\text{gd}(x)) = \sinh(x) $$
and the related identity
$$ \cos(\text{gd}(x)) = \text{sech}(x) \quad \text{ and }
\quad \sec(\text{gd}(x)) = \cosh(x). $$
In some sense, the Jacobi functions generalize the circular
and hyperbolic trigonometric functions. Note the fundamental identity
$$ \text{sn}(x,m)^2 + \text{cn}(x,m)^2 = 1. $$
When
$\,m=0\,$
this becomes
$$ \sin(x)^2+\cos(x)^2=1 $$
and
when
$\,m=1\,$
this becomes
$$ \tanh(x)^2+\text{sech}(x)^2=1. $$
In the
$\,m=1\,$
case replace with equivalent functions to get
$$ \frac{\sinh(x)^2}{\cosh(x)^2} + \frac{1}{\cosh(x)^2} = 1  $$
which is algebraically equivalent to
$$\cosh(x)^2-\sinh(x)^2=1. $$

question:
Consider the average distance between two points drawn from a concave distribution on
$[0,1]$
. This is a one dimensional version of
this

question
. For the uniform distribution, it is well
known
to be equal
$\frac{1}{3}$
. More explicitly:

$$\int_0^1 \int_0^1 |x - y| \rho(x) \rho(y) dx dy \le \frac{1}{3}$$
where
$\rho$
is nonnegative, sums to
$1$
and concave. I can't help but feel there should be an integral inequality which solves it in one fell swoop.


Without concavity assumption this was studied by Björck (G. Björck, “Distributions of positive mass, which maximize a certain generalized energy integral,” Arkiv för Matematik 3, 255–269) which I stumbled upon in
this MathOverflow question
but I couldn't find any work discussing this case.


(Bonus question: given
$p \ge 0$
(
$p \le 0$
) then average of the distance to power of
$p$
is maximized (minimized) by the uniform distribution among all concave distributions,
$p=2$
is
known
, Bonus bonus question: replace concave distributions with unimodal distributions)
answer:
I found this
paper
and in Section 4.5 under they solve a similar problem except the support is over
$[-1,1]$
and weakened concavity of
$\rho$
to concavity of
$\rho^{1/(d-1)}$
.

question:
This question already has answers here
:

Show that $\underset {x \in [0,1]} {\sup} f_n(x) \rightarrow \underset {x \in [0,1]} {\sup} f(x)$ as $n \rightarrow \infty$.

(4 answers)

Closed
3 days ago
.

Let
$(f_n)$
be a sequence of functions on
$D \subset \Re$
with
$f_n$
converging uniformly to
$f^*$
,i.e.
$f_n \rightarrow f^*$
.
$f_n$
is bounded for all
$n$
. We would like to show that
$$\sup f_n(x) \rightarrow \sup f^*(x)$$


This is my attempt. We prove by contradiction. Let
$\sup f_n(x) \not\rightarrow \sup f^*(x)$
then
$\sup f_n(x) > \sup f^*(x)$
or
$\sup f_n(x) < \sup f^*(x)$
. First, let
$\sup f_n(x) > \sup f^*(x)$
and
$|f_n(x) - f^*(x)| < \varepsilon$
.


There exists a
$\delta > 0$
such that
$\sup f_n(x) - \delta > \sup f^*(x)$
. By definition of supremum we have that there exists an
$\epsilon > 0$
such that
$f_n(x) > \sup f_n(x) - \epsilon$
. Now, let
$\delta = \epsilon = \varepsilon$
, then
$f_n(x) > \sup f_n(x) - \varepsilon > \sup f^*(x) \geq f^*(x)$
and so
$f_n(x) - \varepsilon > f^*(x)$
which means
$|f_n(x) - f^*(x)| > \varepsilon$
, a contradiction. And so
$\sup f_n(x) \ngtr \sup f^*(x)$
. Similarly we can show that
$\sup f_n(x) \nless \sup f^*(x)$
.

In other problems I was able to prove directly, i.e. not by contradiction. I'm new to real analysis so I'm not sure the reasoning is fully sound. Are there any holes? And is there a more straightforward way to prove the statement?
answer:
This was too long for a comment, so I will post it as an outline towards an answer so as to not spoil the fun for you...

There are two issues with your argument.

First, the negation of
$\sup_D f_n \to \sup_D f^*$
is not simply that
$\sup_D f_n > \sup_D f^*$
or
$\sup_D f_n < \sup_D f^*$
. Those inequalities say nothing about
how far apart
the suprema are; convergence fails only if there is some fixed
$\varepsilon_0 > 0$
such that, for arbitrarily large
$n$
,
$$
\left|\sup_D f_n - \sup_D f^*\right| \ge \varepsilon_0.
$$

Second, watch the bookkeeping on
$\varepsilon$
. In your chain
$$
f_n(x) > \sup_D f_n - \varepsilon > \sup_D f^* \ge f^*(x),
$$
the final inequality only yields
$f_n(x) > f^*(x)$
, not
$f_n(x) - f^*(x) > \varepsilon$
. The margin
$\varepsilon$
has already been used in approximating
$\sup_D f_n$
by a value of
$f_n(x)$
, so no gap of size
$\varepsilon$
remains.

A more direct route uses uniform convergence as a sup-norm bound. Let
$$
a_n = \sup_{x \in D} |f_n(x) - f^*(x)|,
$$
so
$a_n \to 0$
by hypothesis. Try to prove
$$
\left|\sup_D f_n - \sup_D f^*\right| \le a_n
$$
by comparing
$f_n(x)$
and
$f^*(x)$
for arbitrary
$x \in D$
and then taking suprema. (This is really a continuity property of the supremum functional. Such statements usually come out more cleanly as direct estimates than as contradictions.)
