9 | 1 2 3 4 6 9 15 33 ∞
10 | 1 2 3 4 5 8 12 19 42 ∞

question:
I'm reading about the motivation behind the infinitesimal rotation generator for
$\mathbb{R}^2$
while doing some self-study about Lie Algebras. I've noticed this jump in logic, and I'm curious as to whether there is more rigorous reasoning behind what is going on.

Assuming
$R_h$
is a rotation by a small angle
$h$
, we say that
$R_h = \begin{bmatrix} \cos h & -\sin h \\ \sin h & \cos h \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -h \\ h & 1 \end{bmatrix}$
.

Roughly speaking, I get that when we are doing a tiny rotation, the
$\cos h \approx 1$
which results in the diagonal entries of our matrix. However, by that logic, should we not also have
$\sin h \approx 0$
, reducing our rotation to just
$I$
? I get that for later down the road, it makes sense to keep the
$h$
as is to use as a generator, but keeping
$h$
as two entries exactly while approximating the other two entries to just
$1$
s feels dishonest and somewhat hand-wavey to me.

Can anyone give me a more rigorous reason as to why it is perfectly ok to approximate these two diagonal entries differently?
answer:
The terms in that matrix are the closest
$1$
st order approximations. Let's write out the closest
$3$
rd order approximations of
$\cos(h)$
and
$\sin(h)$
(which is a bit of overkill):
$$\cos(h) \approx 1 + 0 \, h + \frac{1}{2} \, h^2 + 0 h^3 + \frac{1}{24} \, h^3 $$
$$\sin(h) \approx 0 + h + 0 h^2 +  \frac{1}{6} h^3 + 0 h^4
$$
Now drop all terms of
$h^2$
and higher to get the closest first order approximations:
$$\cos(h) \approx 1 \qquad \sin(h) \approx h
$$
In other words, the entries of the
$R_h$
matrix are the best 1st order approximations of the terms in the rotation matrix, where "best" is interpreted rigorously using Taylor's theorem.

But keep in mind: that
$R_h$
matrix is
not
an element of the Lie algebra. If you want a first order approximation of the rotation matrix itself then sure, keep the
$h$
around. But if you want, instead, the "infinitesimal rotation", i.e. if you want the corresponding element of the Lie algebra, then you take the limit as
$h \to 0$
, which in this situation is accomplished by simply substituting
$h=0$
into the formula for
$R_h$
.

In full rigor, the element of the Lie algebra corresponding to the matrix
$$M_h = \begin{bmatrix} \cos h & -\sin h \\ \sin h & \cos h \end{bmatrix} \approx \begin{bmatrix} 1 & -h \\ h & 1 \end{bmatrix}
$$
is just the term by term derivative of
$M_h$
at
$h=0$
.

One reason for focusing on the first order approximations is that you get an element of a vector space, known affectionately as the "tangent space of
$O(2)$
at the identity element". On top of that, when you apply the derivative operator on both sides of the function
$(g,h) \mapsto g^{-1} h^{-1} g h$
then you get a very interesting binary operator on the tangent space which defines the Lie algebra structure, the "Lie bracket" operator. Well, in this abelian situation, that's not so interesting. But for nonabelian Lie groups a beautiful structure appears.

question:
Suppose
$\mathscr{A}$
and
$\mathscr{B}$
are
$\mathbb{Z}_2$
-graded algebras. Firstly, I wish to verify if this means that:

$1.\ \mathscr{A}=\mathscr{A}^0\oplus\mathscr{A}^1$

$2. \ \mathscr{A}^i\cdot\mathscr{A}^j\subseteq \mathscr{A}^{i+j \pmod 2}$

and the same for
$\mathscr{B}$
.

If that is what we mean by
$\mathbb{Z}_2$
-graded algebra, then consider a general element
$a\otimes b\in \mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}$
. Then it must be written as
$(a^0+a^1)\otimes (b^0+b^1)=(a^0\otimes b^0+a^1\otimes b^1)+(a^0\otimes b^1+a^1\otimes b^0)$
.

If I wish to turn
$\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}$
into a
$\mathbb{Z}_2$
-graded algebra, then I try to "naively" define
$(a\otimes b)\cdot (a'\otimes b'):=aa'\otimes bb'$
.

Under this definition, I observe that if I define
$(\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^0:=\mathscr{A}^0\otimes \mathscr{B}^0+\mathscr{A}^1\otimes \mathscr{B}^1$
and
$(\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^1:=\mathscr{A}^0\otimes \mathscr{B}^1+\mathscr{A}^1\otimes \mathscr{B}^0$
, then firstly from looking at the general element of
$\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}$
, I have the decomposition
$\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}=(\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^0\oplus (\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^1$
. Then from my "naive" product, I have
$(\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^i\cdot (\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^j=(\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^{i+j \pmod 2}$
. Thus I have turned
$\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}$
into a
$\mathbb{Z}_2$
-graded algebra.

But the text I am using defines the prduct as
$(a\otimes b)\cdot (a'\otimes b'):=(-1)^{\deg(b)\deg(a')}aa'\otimes bb'$
, and I was wondering why is that when the naive product already gives the grading.

This question was asked here
, and the resolution was to inherit some properties from the algebras
$\mathscr{A}$
and
$\mathscr{B}$
. For example, this definition of the product inherits anticommutativeness from these algebras, but in the text, we are not given that these algebras are anticommutative. The motivation for defining graded tensor product in the text I am referring to is to prove the following:

Suppose
$V$
is a vector space over some commutative field
$k$
(another question I had here was, aren't fields by defintion commutative division rings? If yes, then why do we need to specify commutativity of the field here?) and suppose
$q$
is a quadratic form over
$V$
. If
$V$
admits a
$q$
-orthogonal decomposition
$V_1\oplus V_2$
, then we have an isomorphism between
$Cl(V,q)$
and
$Cl(V_1,q_1)\hat{\otimes}Cl(V_2,q_2)$
. where
$q_i$
is restriction of
$q$
to
$V_i$
.

Now I was wondering that
$Cl(V,q)$
is not exactly anticommutative. We have
$v\cdot w+w\cdot v=-q(v,w)1$
. The anticommutativity would hold for the
$q$
-orthonormal basis of
$V$
. So why such a definition of the graded tensor product?
answer:
This is a short answer, but a long story, trying to give some orientation. As it is stated (now), the question goes through three different areas, and assumes there is one and only one way to look at the things.

Fields are usually commutative. This happens in the first years of study for didactic reasons. Most mathematical basic constructions start with commutative fields. Usually the authors of book mention the commutativity somewhere as part of the axioms or of the convention, some of them mention that later structures without the commutativity of the multiplication may occur, they will be called fields, too. Each text finds a place to make clear this convention. As an example of a non-commutative field we have the field of quaternions.

The best way to answer the question on the "canonical" choice of a tensor product is by saying something about categories. It is hard to do it here, but i will try to give at least the hints. Mathematicians, each one for herself or himself, decide sooner or later to work in one (or a few) categories. They are specified by:

their objects,
the morpisms.

For instance, fix a (commutative) field
$K$
. One of
$\Bbb Q,\Bbb R,\Bbb C$
is good to fix ideas. There is a category
$\mathcal V$
of vector spaces over this field -- say with finite dimension, to make things easy. Sometimes is makes sense to also make a choice of a basis. The new category needs a new name, for instance
$(\mathcal V,\text{basis})$
. So objects in the new category are of the shape
$(V,B)$
,
$V$
vector space,
$B$
is a basis of
$V$
. This kind of "adding data" may be useful (or not). But the process or enriching the structure continues below. For instance we add a grading by a monoid like
$\Bbb N$
,
$\Bbb Z$
,
$\Bbb Z/2$
. And then maybe also a product structure. (And then maybe a coproduct structure. And so on.)

We have universal constructions from linear algebra on
$\mathcal V$
. They are
$\oplus$
(direct sum), the star (taking dual),
$\otimes$
(tensor product). Morphisms are linear morphisms. Some constructions work in the world of "diagrams", for instance consider an arrow
$V\to W$
, this is a "diagram", and immediately we can associate its kernel, and its cokernel.

One of the question is about choices of a product structure on the
$\otimes$
-objects. Let us answer this question from a more general perspective.

Now (some) mathematicians get excited about categories and "constructions" inside them. They see only categories everywhere. There is a category of vector spaces, one of groups (objects are groups, morphisms are morphisms of groups), a category of fieds (morphisms are extensions), a category of topological spaces (with continuous maps), a category of manifolds (of a specific class), a category of simplicial spaces, of cubic spaces, of algebras, of graded algebras, of
$\Bbb Z_2$
-graded algebras and so on. So they start
isolating
the notion of a category and try to do "proper" things inside it. Sometimes they make a topos out of it, whatever it is, they have the maps and look at "coverings". Not our business here. Sometimes they look at categories of specific diagrams in a category, and ask for universal objects. Not our case.

Our case is a category
$\mathcal C$
, abstractly written, with operations,
$\oplus$
,
$\otimes$
, maybe also taking dual, that behave like those written in the same manner for vector spaces. (And maybe more.) Axioms are now written down, so that
$\oplus$
,
$\otimes$
, ... in
$\mathcal C$
behaves like the same "constructions" in vector spaces.

Now we have a big human choice when working with
graded
(by
$\Bbb Z$
or
$\Bbb Z/2$
) algebras.
We may want them commutative.

But more often we want them anti-commutative.
E.g. from the perspective of a person living inside physics, or differential geometry, or (co)homology theories, or ... and this person may see the whole day only differential forms (or similar matter). Like
$dx$
or
$dy$
or
$dz$
or
$dx\wedge dy = -dy\wedge dx$
or ... often multiplied by functions, often constrained to be cycles and taken modulo "boundaries". (Note that two such objects satisfy by definition the relation
$\xi\otimes \eta + \eta\otimes \xi=0$
. Such a relation is
$\Bbb Z$
-homogeneous, so taking the corresponding quotient the
$\Bbb Z$
-grading is preserved.) Such a person "sees" for instance that starting from manifolds
$X,Y$
, points
$x,y$
on them, the tangent spaces
$T_xX$
and
$T_yY$
(which are suddenly in
$\mathcal V$
) gives rise to an "understanding" of the tangent space
$T_{(x,y)}X\times Y$
as
$T_xX\oplus T_yY$
, and want to study what is the "extension" of this understanding for differential forms, starting from
$\Omega^\cdot_xX = \wedge^\cdot T_xX$
and
$\Omega^\cdot_yY = \wedge^\cdot T_yY$
. These are graded
anticommutative
algebras. It turns out that the natural object on
$X\times Y$
, the differential forms
$\Omega^\cdot_{(x,y)}X\times Y$
of the tangent space
$T_{(x,y)}X\times Y$
is also
anticommutative
.

We isolate the situation, and see two anticommutative
$\Bbb N$
-graded algebras. There is a tensor product of them. Which is the right product on it that also brings anticommutativity?

So we need a construction in the world of anticommutative algebras that makes us "happy" when we apply it. (For differential forms for instance.) Is there an isolated, abstract choice which takes two anticommutative algebras as input and delivers on their
$\otimes$
-join also and anticommutative algebra. Yes. This works if we implements a sign by the cocycle
$(i, j)\to (-1)^{ij}$
, where
$i,j$
are grades (of the grading).

(And people living (only) inside category theory have now a good occasion to introduce associativity constraints for "tensor" categories, and study them.)

The Clifford algebra is a completely different construction. We start with an object
$(V, q)$
, a vector space over
$K$
with a quadratic form on it. We take again repeated tensor products,
$K=V^{\otimes 0}$
,
$V=V^{\otimes 1}$
,
$V\otimes V=V^{\otimes2}$
,
$V\otimes V\otimes V=V^{\otimes3}$
, ... ,
$V\otimes V\otimes\dots\otimes V=V^{\otimes n}$
, ... and may be unhappy with the relation
$\xi\otimes \eta + \eta\otimes \xi=0$
to be introduced on
$\sum_{n\ge 0}=V^{\otimes n}$
. Not me, but this was nevertheless successfully done for the purpose of quantum modelling, people wanted to write papers, and somebody saw a possibility to introduce the quantum relation
$xy-yx=i\bar h$
with a different sign. The different sign may make mathematicians argue that a different world appears, but in physics it is the same world, they knew in advance about bosons and fermions, signs are there to be changed. Mathematics is there to make computations still work. In mathematics the people are declared either symplectic or orthogonal (or ignorants of this aspect), but in quantum physics they must accept both. That
$i\bar h$
is a constant, so we may norm it to one. But for different
$x,y$
values it may change. So let us use that enriched structure
$(V,q)$
, and the
$q$
part will take care on the constant. The Clifford algebra relations are now in the tensor algebra of the shape
$\xi\otimes \eta + \eta\otimes \xi=Q(\xi,\eta)\cdot 1$
with the induced bilinear form
$Q$
from
$q$
. This relation is
$\Bbb Z/2$
-homogeneous (but not
$\Bbb Z$
-homogeneous), so building the quotient w.r.t. these relations still keeps the
$\Bbb Z/2$
-grading. So the Clifford algebra comes with such an algebra-grading. But the commutativity or anticommutativity is lost. As wanted.

In differential geometry there is a good modelling strategy. We start with a space
$X$
and consider bundles
$E$
over
$X$
with some structure. So the fiber
$E_x$
in
$X$
is an object of the linear algebra, and we may perform
universal
constructions in the fiber, which for the universality reason glue together to deliver corresponding constructions for bundles. (The
$\sum$
of the linear spaces in the fiber glue together to deliver a
$\sum$
for the bundles. Same for
$\otimes$
, for building quotients, ... so we have the direct sum of bundles, tensor products, tensor algebra, tensor algebra modulo quotients, ... for bundles.) In physics, the space is the given geometric reality where
$1D$
-ants or
$2D$
-ants or
$3D$
-ants or ... live in, and bundles come to model values of measurements of physical properties of matter like impulse, velocity and electromagnetic forces. Some of the matter commutes, bosons are perfect to work in the "naive" (commutative) algebra, some of the matter anticommutes, fermions are such particles. This is the "signed" world. Things that commute or anticommute (correspondingly) do not interract (in a bosonic and/or fermionic world). But some of them do interract, and we must model this by using something like the Clifford algebra.

The choice of the sign, is the "choice of the algebra"(ic manipulations).

It turns out that the mathematical choice of the "algebra" (i.e. ring) structure has meanings in both mathematics and physics. Examples: Mathematically, the category of bundles (with enriched structure like metric, associated connection, ...) on some space
$X$
tells a lot (gives invariants) for the space
$X$
itself. In physics, extensions of the Schrödinger equation needs the Clifford algebra formalism to condensate it in a proper, supple calculus.

The question <<... why do we implement
$\pm 1$
(or
$(-1)^{ij}$
for degrees
$i,j\in\Bbb Z/2$
) instead of the simple and "naive"
$+1$
...?>> may be answered as "i do it because i want so, let me do it as long as algebraically the computations make sense, this is my choice" or "i do it because someone does and wants it so...", or "someone does it because someone wants it so..." but maybe the good strategy is to look at both (and further) choices and question the richness of the implied constructions.

question:
Suppose
$\mathscr{A}$
and
$\mathscr{B}$
are
$\mathbb{Z}_2$
-graded algebras. Firstly, I wish to verify if this means that:

$1.\ \mathscr{A}=\mathscr{A}^0\oplus\mathscr{A}^1$

$2. \ \mathscr{A}^i\cdot\mathscr{A}^j\subseteq \mathscr{A}^{i+j \pmod 2}$

and the same for
$\mathscr{B}$
.

If that is what we mean by
$\mathbb{Z}_2$
-graded algebra, then consider a general element
$a\otimes b\in \mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}$
. Then it must be written as
$(a^0+a^1)\otimes (b^0+b^1)=(a^0\otimes b^0+a^1\otimes b^1)+(a^0\otimes b^1+a^1\otimes b^0)$
.

If I wish to turn
$\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}$
into a
$\mathbb{Z}_2$
-graded algebra, then I try to "naively" define
$(a\otimes b)\cdot (a'\otimes b'):=aa'\otimes bb'$
.

Under this definition, I observe that if I define
$(\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^0:=\mathscr{A}^0\otimes \mathscr{B}^0+\mathscr{A}^1\otimes \mathscr{B}^1$
and
$(\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^1:=\mathscr{A}^0\otimes \mathscr{B}^1+\mathscr{A}^1\otimes \mathscr{B}^0$
, then firstly from looking at the general element of
$\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}$
, I have the decomposition
$\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}=(\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^0\oplus (\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^1$
. Then from my "naive" product, I have
$(\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^i\cdot (\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^j=(\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B})^{i+j \pmod 2}$
. Thus I have turned
$\mathscr{A}\otimes \mathscr{B}$
into a
$\mathbb{Z}_2$
-graded algebra.

But the text I am using defines the prduct as
$(a\otimes b)\cdot (a'\otimes b'):=(-1)^{\deg(b)\deg(a')}aa'\otimes bb'$
, and I was wondering why is that when the naive product already gives the grading.

This question was asked here
, and the resolution was to inherit some properties from the algebras
$\mathscr{A}$
and
$\mathscr{B}$
. For example, this definition of the product inherits anticommutativeness from these algebras, but in the text, we are not given that these algebras are anticommutative. The motivation for defining graded tensor product in the text I am referring to is to prove the following:

Suppose
$V$
is a vector space over some commutative field
$k$
(another question I had here was, aren't fields by defintion commutative division rings? If yes, then why do we need to specify commutativity of the field here?) and suppose
$q$
is a quadratic form over
$V$
. If
$V$
admits a
$q$
-orthogonal decomposition
$V_1\oplus V_2$
, then we have an isomorphism between
$Cl(V,q)$
and
$Cl(V_1,q_1)\hat{\otimes}Cl(V_2,q_2)$
. where
$q_i$
is restriction of
$q$
to
$V_i$
.

Now I was wondering that
$Cl(V,q)$
is not exactly anticommutative. We have
$v\cdot w+w\cdot v=-q(v,w)1$
. The anticommutativity would hold for the
$q$
-orthonormal basis of
$V$
. So why such a definition of the graded tensor product?
answer:
These are all the standard definitions, yes.

To reiterate what Nicholas Todoroff has said in the comments, the last part of your question doesn't really ask anything meaningful. The theorem is true with the graded-commutative product only, and that
$\mathrm{Cl}(V, q)$
is "not exactly anticommutative" doesn't really bear on this.

So here the context tells you that you want graded commutativity. This is generally true: You never talk about definitions in a vacuum, and whatever you want to do with your ring will tell you which sign rule is best. ...and if you're not out doing mathematics on your own, authors will generally tell you what sign rules they use. Less so because it would be hard to discern whether they want graded or strict commutativity and more so because there are about as many sign conventions for maps, products, differentials, etc. out there as there are authors, and you run the risk of utterly confusing your readers if you don't tell them which one you use. If the author doesn't do this, then they're probably writing in a field that has some fixed conventions, e.g. rings in algebraic topology are basically always graded-commutative, as opposed to, say, algebraic geometry.

I warmly recommend worrying less about things like this in advance. There is plenty of time to worry if this ever arises as a concrete problem, emphasis on the
if
.

question:
I found this problem in a textbook of abstract algebra:

Let $H$ be a subgroup of $G$. Prove that $$\{x\in G:xHx^{-1}\subseteq H\}$$ is a subgroup of $G$.




It's easy to prove that the set is closed under multiplication, but I'm stuck on proving that it is closed under inverses.


If $H$ is finite, say $H=\{a_1,\ldots,a_n\}$, suppose $x$ is an element of the set. Then $xa_1x^{-1},\ldots,xa_nx^{-1}$ are all distinct, hence they are exactly $a_1,\ldots,a_n$, in some order. Therefore any element $b\in H$ can be written as $xcx^{-1}$ for some $c\in H$, and hence $x^{-1}bx=x^{-1}(xcx^{-1})x=c$ is also in $H$. So $x^{-1}$ is also an element of the set.


However, the above method does not work if $H$ is infinite. The main idea is to prove that $x^{-1}ax\in H$ for every $a\in H$, given that $xax^{-1}\in H$ for every $a\in H$. I was trying to do some substitutions of $a$ to get the required result, but I can't seem to get the $x^{-1}$ to the left.

Any help would be appreciated. It may be worth mentioning that I just started learning this group theory thing for a few days, so please adjust your explanation accordingly.

Thanks in advance.
answer:
The reason you are having trouble proving it is that it is not true as stated.

For a heavy-handed example, let $G$ be the free group on $x$ and $y$, and let $H$ be the subgroup generated by all elements of the form $x^nyx^{-n}$ with $n\gt 0$.

Then for any $a\in H$ we have $xax^{-1}\in H$. However, $x^{-1}yx\notin H$, because any element of $H$ is a word that starts with a nonnegative power of $x$.

To fix the problem, you would need to require $xHx^{-1}=H$, rather than $xHx^{-1}\subseteq H$. Then your argument would go through in the infinite case as well.

Okay, here's an example you can get your hands on (courtesy my Math 257 notes with T.Y. Lam, Spring 97):

Let $G$ be the group of all invertible $2\times 2$ matrices with coefficients in $\mathbb{Q}$, $G=\mathrm{GL}_2(\mathbb{Q})$.

Let $H$ be the subgroup given by
$$ H = \left\{\left.\left(\begin{array}{cc}
1 & m\\
0 & 1\end{array}\right)\in G\ \right|\ m\in\mathbb{Z}\right\}.$$
Let
$$x = \left(\begin{array}{cc}
2 & 0\\
0 & 1\end{array}\right), \qquad x^{-1} = \left(\begin{array}{cc}
\textstyle\frac{1}{2} & 0 \\
0 & 1
\end{array}\right).$$
Then for every $m\in\mathbb{Z}$ we have:
$$\begin{align*}
\left(\begin{array}{cc}
2 & 0\\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{cc}1 & m\\0 & 1\end{array}\right) \left(\begin{array}{cc}\textstyle\frac{1}{2}&0\\0&1\end{array}\right)
&= \left(\begin{array}{cc}
2 & 2m\\0 & 1\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{cc}
\textstyle\frac{1}{2}&0\\0 & 1\end{array}\right)\\
&= \left(\begin{array}{cc}
1 & 2m\\
0 & 1\end{array}\right)\in H.
\end{align*}$$
So $xHx^{-1}\subseteq H$. However, even though
$$ \left(\begin{array}{cc}1&1\\0&1\end{array}\right)\in H,$$
we have
$$\begin{align*}
x^{-1}\left(\begin{array}{cc}1&1\\0&1\end{array}\right)x &= \left(\begin{array}{cc}
\textstyle\frac{1}{2} & 0\\0 &1\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{cc}1 & 1\\ 0 & 1\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{cc}2 & 0\\0&1\end{array}\right)\\
&= \left(\begin{array}{cc}
\textstyle\frac{1}{2}&\textstyle\frac{1}{2}\\0&1\end{array}\right)\left(\begin{array}{cc}2&0\\0&1
\end{array}\right)\\
&= \left(\begin{array}{cc}
1 & \textstyle\frac{1}{2}\\0&1\end{array}\right)\notin H.
\end{align*}$$
So $x\in \{g\in G\mid ghg^{-1}\in H\text{ for all }h\in H\}$, but $x^{-1}\notin\{g\in G\mid ghg^{-1}\in H\text{ for all }h\in H\}$. So the set need not be closed under inverses.

question:
Let

$$I=:I_1+I_2$$


where


$$I_1=:\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{x}{(\cos x+1)\sin x}dx$$


$$I_2=:\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{(2-\cos x)\sin x-x}{\sin^3 x}dx$$




Show that
$$I=1+\frac{\pi}{4}$$




Help from Wolfram


Wolfram claims
that
$I_1=C-\frac12+\frac{\pi}{4}$
where
$C$
is
Catalan's constant
.
$I_1$
has a
messy antiderivative
.


Wolfram does not give a closed form
for
$I_2$
, but
with some coaxing suggests that

$I_2=\frac32-C$
.
$I_2$
has a
messy antiderivative
.


Context


The integral
$I=:I_1+I_2$
came up when I was trying to answer this question (which I made up): "Inside a sphere with centre
$O$
and diameter
$AB$
, uniformly random point
$P$
is chosen.
$AP$
and
$BP$
intersect the sphere again at
$A'$
and
$B'$
, respectively. What is
$\mathbb{E}(\angle A'OB')$
?
$(0\le\angle A'OB'\le\pi)$
" It turns out that if
$I=1+\frac{\pi}{4}$
then
$\mathbb{E}(\angle A'OB')=\frac{\pi}{4}$
.
answer:
The other current answers seem to be going for a direct attack for
$I_1$
and
$I_2$
individually, but the manner the problem is posed led me to think that evaluating
$I_1 + I_2$
as a whole would yield a simplification, as indeed it does:

$$I_1 + I_2 = \int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{x}{\sin x(1+\cos x)} + \frac{(2-\cos x)\sin x - x}{\sin^3 x}\mathrm dx$$
$$= \int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{x\sin^2 x + (2-\cos x)(1+\cos x)\sin x - x(1+\cos x)}{\sin^3 x(1+\cos x)}\mathrm dx$$
$$ = \int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{x\sin^2 x + 2\sin x + \cos x\sin x-\cos^2 x\sin x - x-x\cos x}{\sin^3 x(1+\cos x)}\mathrm dx$$
$$=\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{x(\sin^2 x - 1 - \cos x)-2\sin x + \cos x\sin x-\cos^2 x\sin x}{\sin^3 x(1+\cos x)}\mathrm dx$$
$$=\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{-x\cos x(1 + \cos x) + \sin x(\cos x-\cos^2 x + 2)}{\sin^3 x(1+\cos x)}\mathrm dx$$
$$=\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{-x\cos x}{\sin^3 x}\mathrm dx + \int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{(\cos x-\cos^2 x + 2)}{\sin^2 x(1+\cos x)}\mathrm dx$$

Now
$\cos x-\cos^2 x + 2 = (1 + \cos x) + (1 - \cos^2 x) = (1 + \cos x) + \sin^2 x$
and so the integral
$I$
becomes
$$I = \int_0^{\pi/2}-x\cot x \csc^2 x\mathrm dx + \int_0^{\pi/2}\bigg(\csc^2 x + \frac{1}{1+\cos x}\bigg)\mathrm dx$$
which should be much easier to integrate.

Update

As pointed out by the OP, the integrals at the end diverge by themselves. So we combine them, find their antiderivative and then take the limit at the end.

For the first integral we use integration by parts:
$$\int-x\cot x \csc^2 x\mathrm dx = x\frac{\cot^2 x}{2} - \int\frac{\cot^2 x}{2}\mathrm dx = x\frac{\cot^2 x}{2} - \int\frac{\csc^2 x- 1}{2}\mathrm dx$$
$$= x\frac{\cot^2 x}{2} + \frac x2 + \frac{\cot x}{2}$$
and
$$\int\bigg(\csc^2 x + \frac{1}{1+\cos x}\bigg)\mathrm dx = -\cot x  + \int \frac{1-\cos x}{1-\cos^2 x}\mathrm dx = -2\cot x + \csc x $$

Combine them to get
$$f(x) = \frac x2(1 + \cot^2 x) + \csc x - \frac32 \cot x = \frac x2\csc^2 x + \csc x - \frac32 \cot x$$

At
$x = \pi/2,\quad f(\pi/2) = 1 + \frac{\pi}{4}$
. So we essentially need to prove that
$\displaystyle \lim_{x\to 0}f(x) = 0$
.

$$\lim_{x\to 0}f(x) = \lim_{x\to 0}\frac{x + 2\sin x - 3\cos x\sin x}{\sin^2 x}$$
which is equal to
$0$
, as can be seen easily after two iterations of L'Hopital's rule.

Thus,
$$I=I_1+I_2 = \boxed{1+\frac\pi 4}$$

question:
Let

$$I=:I_1+I_2$$


where


$$I_1=:\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{x}{(\cos x+1)\sin x}dx$$


$$I_2=:\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{(2-\cos x)\sin x-x}{\sin^3 x}dx$$




Show that
$$I=1+\frac{\pi}{4}$$




Help from Wolfram


Wolfram claims
that
$I_1=C-\frac12+\frac{\pi}{4}$
where
$C$
is
Catalan's constant
.
$I_1$
has a
messy antiderivative
.


Wolfram does not give a closed form
for
$I_2$
, but
with some coaxing suggests that

$I_2=\frac32-C$
.
$I_2$
has a
messy antiderivative
.


Context


The integral
$I=:I_1+I_2$
came up when I was trying to answer this question (which I made up): "Inside a sphere with centre
$O$
and diameter
$AB$
, uniformly random point
$P$
is chosen.
$AP$
and
$BP$
intersect the sphere again at
$A'$
and
$B'$
, respectively. What is
$\mathbb{E}(\angle A'OB')$
?
$(0\le\angle A'OB'\le\pi)$
" It turns out that if
$I=1+\frac{\pi}{4}$
then
$\mathbb{E}(\angle A'OB')=\frac{\pi}{4}$
.
answer:
Expand the integrand of
$I_2$
by rewriting
$\sin^3x=\left(1-\cos^2x\right)\sin x$
and decomposing into partial fractions, so that in combination with
$I_1$
we have

$$\begin{align*}
I_1+I_2 &= \frac12 \int_0^\tfrac\pi2 \left(\frac{(1+\cos x)\sin x-2x\cos x}{\sin^3x} + \frac3{1+\cos x}\right) \, dx \\
&= \frac12 \int_0^1 \left(u+3u^3-\left(1-u^4\right)\arctan u\right)\frac{du}{u^3} & u=\tan\frac x2
\end{align*}$$

The second line follows from

$$\int_0^\tfrac\pi2 \frac3{1+\cos x}\,dx \stackrel{x\to2x}= \frac32 \int_0^\tfrac\pi4 \sec^2x\,dx = \frac32$$

and this can be replaced into the first integral by using
$\displaystyle\int_0^1du=1$
.

Integrate by parts to finish:

$$I_1+I_2 = -1 + \frac12 \int_0^1 \left(5+2u\arctan u\right)\,du = \boxed{1+\frac\pi4}$$

question:
Let

$$I=:I_1+I_2$$


where


$$I_1=:\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{x}{(\cos x+1)\sin x}dx$$


$$I_2=:\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{(2-\cos x)\sin x-x}{\sin^3 x}dx$$




Show that
$$I=1+\frac{\pi}{4}$$




Help from Wolfram


Wolfram claims
that
$I_1=C-\frac12+\frac{\pi}{4}$
where
$C$
is
Catalan's constant
.
$I_1$
has a
messy antiderivative
.


Wolfram does not give a closed form
for
$I_2$
, but
with some coaxing suggests that

$I_2=\frac32-C$
.
$I_2$
has a
messy antiderivative
.


Context


The integral
$I=:I_1+I_2$
came up when I was trying to answer this question (which I made up): "Inside a sphere with centre
$O$
and diameter
$AB$
, uniformly random point
$P$
is chosen.
$AP$
and
$BP$
intersect the sphere again at
$A'$
and
$B'$
, respectively. What is
$\mathbb{E}(\angle A'OB')$
?
$(0\le\angle A'OB'\le\pi)$
" It turns out that if
$I=1+\frac{\pi}{4}$
then
$\mathbb{E}(\angle A'OB')=\frac{\pi}{4}$
.
answer:
Using the standard half-angle substitution
$t = \tan\frac{x}{2}$
so the first integral becomes

$$I_1 = \int_0^1 \frac{(1+t^2)\arctan t}{t}\,dt = \int_0^1 \frac{\arctan t}{t}\,dt + \int_0^1 t\arctan t\,dt$$
Integration by parts:
$$\int_0^1 t\arctan t\,dt = \left[\frac{t^2}{2}\arctan t\right]_0^1 - \frac{1}{2}\int_0^1 \frac{t^2}{1+t^2}\,dt=\frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{1}{2}$$
And the second integral becomes
$$I_2 = \int_0^1 \left(\frac{3}{2} + \frac{1}{2t^2} - \frac{\arctan t}{2t^3} - \frac{\arctan t}{t} - \frac{t\arctan t}{2}\right)\,dt$$

We can use the following:
$$\left(-\frac{1}{2t} + \frac{\arctan t}{2t^2} + \frac{\arctan t}{2}\right)' = \frac{1}{t^2} - \frac{\arctan t}{t^3}$$
Therefore:
$$\int_0^1 \left(\frac{1}{t^2} - \frac{\arctan t}{t^3}\right)\,dt = \left[-\frac{1}{2t} + \frac{\arctan t}{2t^2} + \frac{\arctan t}{2}\right]_0^1= \frac{\pi}{4} - \frac{1}{2}$$

And combine the pieces together:
$$I = I_1 + I_2 = \left(C + \frac{\pi}{4}- \frac{1}{2} \right) + \left(\frac{3}{2} - C\right) = 1 + \frac{\pi}{4}$$

$I_2=\frac32-C$
.
$I_2$
has a
messy antiderivative
.


Context


The integral
$I=:I_1+I_2$
came up when I was trying to answer this question (which I made up): "Inside a sphere with centre
$O$
and diameter
$AB$
, uniformly random point
$P$
is chosen.
$AP$
and
$BP$
intersect the sphere again at
$A'$
and
$B'$
, respectively. What is
$\mathbb{E}(\angle A'OB')$
?
$(0\le\angle A'OB'\le\pi)$
" It turns out that if
$I=1+\frac{\pi}{4}$
then
$\mathbb{E}(\angle A'OB')=\frac{\pi}{4}$
.
answer:
There are probably several trig-based substitutions or plans of attack that work. To me,
$I_1$
seems to be calling for the half-angle identities:
$\cos x + 1 = 2\cos^2(x/2)$
and
$\sin x = 2\sin(x/2)\cos(x/2)$
, so the denominator becomes
$4\sin(x/2)\cos^3(x/2).$

The substitution
$u = x/2$
then gives
$$I_1 = \int_0^{\pi/4} \frac{u}{\sin u\,\cos^3 u}\,du.$$
We will handle this by integration by parts. In preparation for that, first compute the antiderivative of the trigonometric factor alone. Writing
$1 = \sin^2 v + \cos^2 v$
in the numerator of
$1/(\sin v\,\cos^3 v)$
,
$$\frac{1}{\sin v\,\cos^3 v} = \frac{\sin^2 v + \cos^2 v}{\sin v\,\cos^3 v} = \frac{\sin v}{\cos^3 v} + \frac{1}{\sin v\,\cos v},$$
and each piece integrates to give
$$\int \frac{dv}{\sin v\,\cos^3 v} = \tfrac{1}{2}\sec^2 v + \ln|\tan v|.$$

Now apply integration by parts to
$I_1$
with
$f = u$
and
$dg = du/(\sin u\,\cos^3 u)$
, using the antiderivative just computed:

$$I_1 = \Bigl[u\cdot\bigl(\tfrac{1}{2}\sec^2 u + \ln(\tan u)\bigr)\Bigr]_0^{\pi/4} - \int_0^{\pi/4} \Bigl(\tfrac{1}{2}\sec^2 u + \ln(\tan u)\Bigr)\,du.$$

At
$u = \pi/4$
the bracket equals
$\pi/4$
while at
$u = 0$
the bracket vanishes. The remaining integrals are
$$\int_0^{\pi/4} \tfrac{1}{2}\sec^2 u\,du = \tfrac{1}{2}, \qquad \int_0^{\pi/4} \ln(\tan u)\,du.$$

Now, for the second integral, substitute
$t = \tan u$
so
$du = dt/(1+t^2)$
to get
$$\int_0^{\pi/4} \ln(\tan u)\,du = \int_0^1 \frac{\ln t}{1+t^2}\,dt.$$
Write
$$\frac{1}{1+t^2} = \sum_{n=0}^\infty (-1)^n t^{2n}$$
and integrate termwise:

$$\int_0^1 \frac{\ln t}{1+t^2}\,dt = \sum_{n=0}^\infty (-1)^n \int_0^1 t^{2n}\ln t\,dt = \sum_{n=0}^\infty (-1)^n \cdot \frac{-1}{(2n+1)^2}=-G,$$
where
$$G = \sum_{n=0}^\infty \frac{(-1)^n}{(2n+1)^2}$$
is Catalan's constant. (Here, use parts to get
$\int_0^1 t^{2n}\ln t\,dt = -1/(2n+1)^2$
with
$f = \ln t$
,
$dg = t^{2n}\,dt$
.)

Thus,
$\boxed{I_1=\frac{\pi}{4}-\frac{1}{2}+G}$
.

For
$I_2$
, the integrand is singular at
$0$
but integrable there, so we work on
$[\varepsilon,\pi/2]$
and let
$\varepsilon\to 0^+$
at the end. Split
$$
I_2(\varepsilon)
=
\int_\varepsilon^{\pi/2}\frac{2-\cos x}{\sin^2 x}\,dx
-
\int_\varepsilon^{\pi/2}\frac{x}{\sin^3 x}\,dx.
$$
Each piece diverges as
$\varepsilon\to 0^+$
, but the divergences will cancel. Direct differentiation verifies
$$
\int\frac{2-\cos x}{\sin^2 x}\,dx=\frac{1-2\cos x}{\sin x}+C,
\qquad
\csc^3 x=\frac{d}{dx}\!\left(\frac{-\cos x}{2\sin^2 x}\right)+\frac{1}{2\sin x}.
$$
Integration by parts on the second integral with
$f=x$
then gives
$$
\int \frac{x}{\sin^3 x}\,dx
=
-\frac{x\cos x}{2\sin^2 x}-\frac{1}{2\sin x}+\frac{1}{2}\int\frac{x}{\sin x}\,dx,
$$
so
$$
I_2(\varepsilon)
=
\left[\frac{3-4\cos x}{2\sin x}+\frac{x\cos x}{2\sin^2 x}\right]_\varepsilon^{\pi/2}
-\frac{1}{2}\int_\varepsilon^{\pi/2}\frac{x}{\sin x}\,dx.
$$
The bracket equals
$\tfrac32$
at
$x=\pi/2$
and tends to
$0$
as
$x\to 0^+$
(after some work, the Taylor expansions show the
$1/x$
poles cancel), so letting
$\varepsilon\to 0^+$
,
$$
I_2=\frac{3}{2}-\frac{1}{2}\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{x}{\sin x}\,dx.
$$

For the remaining integral, take
$f=x$
,
$dg=\csc x\,dx$
. Then
$$
\int_\varepsilon^{\pi/2}\frac{x}{\sin x}\,dx
=
\Bigl[x\ln(\tan(x/2))\Bigr]_\varepsilon^{\pi/2}
-\int_\varepsilon^{\pi/2}\ln(\tan(x/2))\,dx.
$$

At
$x=\pi/2$
,
$\ln(\tan(\pi/4))=0$
. At
$x=\varepsilon\to 0^+$
,
$\tan(\varepsilon/2)\sim \varepsilon/2$
, so
$\varepsilon\ln(\tan(\varepsilon/2))\sim \varepsilon\ln(\varepsilon/2)\to 0$
. Thus,
$$
\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{x}{\sin x}\,dx
=-\int_0^{\pi/2}\ln(\tan(x/2))\,dx.
$$
Substituting
$u=x/2$
in the right-hand integral and using
$\int_0^{\pi/4}\ln(\tan u)\,du=-G$
(which we computed when finding
$I_1$
), we get
$$
-\int_0^{\pi/2}\ln(\tan(x/2))\,dx
=-2\int_0^{\pi/4}\ln(\tan u)\,du
=2G.
$$

Therefore,
$
\boxed{I_2=\frac32-G},
$
and hence
$$
\boxed{I=
1+\frac{\pi}{4}.}
$$

question:
Let

$$I=:I_1+I_2$$


where


$$I_1=:\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{x}{(\cos x+1)\sin x}dx$$


$$I_2=:\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{(2-\cos x)\sin x-x}{\sin^3 x}dx$$




Show that
$$I=1+\frac{\pi}{4}$$




Help from Wolfram


Wolfram claims
that
$I_1=C-\frac12+\frac{\pi}{4}$
where
$C$
is
Catalan's constant
.
$I_1$
has a
messy antiderivative
.


Wolfram does not give a closed form
for
$I_2$
, but
with some coaxing suggests that

$I_2=\frac32-C$
.
$I_2$
has a
messy antiderivative
.


Context


The integral
$I=:I_1+I_2$
came up when I was trying to answer this question (which I made up): "Inside a sphere with centre
$O$
and diameter
$AB$
, uniformly random point
$P$
is chosen.
$AP$
and
$BP$
intersect the sphere again at
$A'$
and
$B'$
, respectively. What is
$\mathbb{E}(\angle A'OB')$
?
$(0\le\angle A'OB'\le\pi)$
" It turns out that if
$I=1+\frac{\pi}{4}$
then
$\mathbb{E}(\angle A'OB')=\frac{\pi}{4}$
.
answer:
$$I=\int\left(\frac{x}{(\cos (x)+1)\sin (x)}+\frac{(2-\cos (x))\sin (x)-x}{\sin^3 x}\right)\,dx$$

Uisng the tangent half-angle substitution
$$I=\int \Big(\frac{3}{2} +\frac{1}{2 t^2}+\frac{\left(t^4-1\right)\, \tan
^{-1}(t)}{2 t^3} \Big)\,dt$$
$$I=\frac{t \left(5 t^2-1\right)+\left(t^2+1\right)^2 \tan ^{-1}(t)}{4 t^2}$$
and then the result for the definite integral.

Back to
$x$
$$I=\frac{1}{2} x \csc ^2(x)+\csc (x)-\frac{3 }{2}\cot (x)$$

question:
So I just learned (I always regret not taking more number theory back in university) about the sum

$$
\sum_{0 < t < T} \cos(t x)
$$

where the
$t$
s are the imaginary parts of the non-trivial zeroes of the Riemann
$\zeta$
function. This sum exhibits spikes at exactly the natural logarithms of prime powers (with smaller spikes at larger powers, and the largest spikes at the primes themselves). For instance, with the first 200 zeroes, the spikes are relatively clearly visible up to somewhere between
$x = \ln 150$
and
$x = \ln 200$
. Here is a graph, with a few of the spikes labeled for reference:




If the Riemann hypothesis is false, would a non-trivial zero of
$\zeta$
outside of the critical line introduce an exponential term to this sum? Is it the Riemann hypothesis that keeps this sum bounded for any finite
$T$
?
answer:
Assuming
$\rho=\alpha+i \gamma$
is a non-trivial zeta-zero in the upper-half plane, the Riemann hypothesis (where
$\alpha=\frac{1}{2}$
) implies

$$\underset{T\to\infty}{\text{lim}}\left(\sum\limits_{0<\gamma<T} \cos\left(\gamma\, x\right)\right)=\frac{1-\psi'(e^x)+\frac{1}{e^x-e^{3 x}}}{2}\, e^{x/2}\,,\quad x>0\tag{1}$$

which follows from formula (4) in
this answer
I posted to a related question on Math StackExchange.

If the Riemann hypothesis is false and
$\rho=\alpha+i \gamma$
is a non-trivial zeta-zero off the critical line, then so is
$1-\rho=1-\alpha-i \gamma$
and likewise their complex conjugates
$\rho^*=\alpha-i \gamma$
and
$(1-\rho)^*=1-\alpha+i \gamma$
.

If the Riemann hypothese is false, then formula (1) above becomes

$$\underset{T\to\infty}{\text{lim}}\left(\sum\limits_{0<\gamma<T} e^{\left(\alpha-\frac{1}{2}\right) x} \cos\left(\gamma\, x\right)\right)=\frac{1-\psi'(e^x)+\frac{1}{e^x-e^{3 x}}}{2}\, e^{x/2}\,,\quad x>0\tag{2}$$

which is based on the pairing of each non-trivial zeta-zero
$\rho=\alpha+i \gamma$
in the upper-half plane with its complex-conjugate
$\rho^*=\alpha-i \gamma$
in the relationship

$$e^{(\alpha-1+i\, \gamma) x}+e^{(\alpha-1-i\, \gamma) x}=2 e^{(\alpha-1) x} \cos(\gamma x)\tag{3}.$$

The Riemann hypothesis does not bound formula (1) above at
$T=\infty$
.

question:
Let
$G$
be a finite group and
$G'$
be the commutator subgroup of
$G$
. In the paper "
SOME SUPERSOLVABILITY CONDITIONS FOR FINITE GROUPS
" (
https://www.jstor.org/stable/40656943?read-now=1&seq=1#page_scan_tab_contents
) by Barry et al., mentioned a Lemma (Lemma-3.9, Page-167) which is, if
$G' \cap Z(G)=\lbrace e \rbrace$
then
$$|G/Z(G)|\leq |G'|.|\text{Aut}(G')|$$

For this lemma they cite "
G.M. Ratay, 1974 On the relationship between the commutator and central factor groups, unpublished PhD thesis, Adelphi University, New York
".


But I am not clear on how the proof goes and also unable to find the unpublished thesis.


My Attempt :
For finite abelian groups the result is trivial. So we are going to assume
$G$
is a finite non-abelian group. Consider the homomorphism
$\phi : G \xrightarrow{} \text{Aut}(G')$
as
$\phi(g)=\phi_g$
where
$\phi_g \in \text{Aut}(G')$
and defined as
$\phi_g(x)=gxg^{-1}$
, for all
$g \in G \text{ and } x \in G'$
. Then
$$N=Ker(\phi)=\lbrace g\in G : \phi(g)=I \rbrace =\lbrace g \in G : gx=xg \text{ , } \forall x \in G' \rbrace = C_G(G')$$
where
$I=$
trivial automorphism on
$G'$
. Then by First Isomorphism Theorem,
$$G/C_G(G') \cong \phi(G) \leq \text{Aut}(G') \implies |G| \leq|C_G(G')|.|\text{Aut}(G')|$$

Since we have
$G' \cap Z(G) = \lbrace e \rbrace$
, my plan is to show
$C_G(G') \subset G'.Z(G)$
. If this holds then directly we can have
$|C_G(G')|\leq |G'| \times |Z(G)|$
and our claim holds.

But I am stuck at this point. Any help and comments are welcome.
answer:
Let
$D=G'$
,
$Z=Z(G)$
, and
$C=C_G(D)$
. Your reduction is right! In fact,
$C\leq DZ$
is true.

First observe that
$C$
is abelian. Indeed
$C\lhd G$
, since
$D$
is characteristic. Hence:

$$
[C,G]\leq C\cap G'=C\cap D.
$$

Also
$[C,D]=1$
, by definition of
$C$
. By the three-subgroups lemma:

$$
[[C,C],G]=1,
$$

because both
$[[G,C],C]$
and
$[[C,G],C]$
are trivial. Thus
$[C,C]\leq Z$
. But also
$[C,C]\leq G'=D$
, so:

$$
[C,C]\leq D\cap Z=1.
$$

Therefore
$C$
is abelian.

Now let the abelian group
$G/D$
act on the finite abelian group
$C$
by conjugation. This is well-defined, because
$D$
centralises
$C$
. The fixed-point subgroup is:

$$
C^{G/D}=Z(G)=Z.
$$

Let's write:

$$
N=[C,G].
$$

Then
$N\leq C\cap D$
. Also:

$$
N^{G/D}=N\cap Z(G)\leq D\cap Z(G)=1.
$$

We shall make use of the following elementary lemma.

Lemma.
Let an abelian group
$A$
act on a finite abelian group
$M$
. Put
$N=[M,A]$
. If
$N^A=1$
, then:

$$
M=M^A N.
$$

Applying this with
$A=G/D$
and
$M=C$
, we get:

$$
C=ZN.
$$

Since
$N\leq D$
, this gives exactly:

$$
C\leq ZD.
$$

Thus:

$$
|C/Z|\leq |D|.
$$

Finally, conjugation on
$D=G'$
defines a homomorphism:

$$
G\longrightarrow \operatorname{Aut}(D)
$$

with kernel
$C=C_G(D)$
. Hence:

$$
|G/C|\leq |\operatorname{Aut}(D)|.
$$

Therefore:

$$
|G/Z|
=
|G/C|\,|C/Z|
\leq
|\operatorname{Aut}(D)|\,|D|.
$$

Since
$D=G'$
, this is:

$$
\boxed{|G/Z(G)|\leq |G'|\,|\operatorname{Aut}(G')|.}
$$

Just for completeness sake here is how I proved the lemma. Use additive notation! Let
$A$
act on
$M$
, and let
$N=[M,A]$
. For
$m\in M$
, the map:

$$
\delta_m(a)=a m-m
$$

is a derivation
$A\to N$
. If
$N^A=0$
, then every such derivation is principal, i.e.
$\delta_m(a)=a n-n$
for some
$n\in N$
. Then
$m-n\in M^A$
, so
$m\in M^A+N$
. Hence
$M=M^A+N$
, as required.

Usually to show every derivation
$A\to N$
is principal when
$A$
is abelian and
$N^A=0$
you would use induction on
$|N|$
: take a minimal nonzero
$A$
-invariant subgroup
$L\leq N$
. Since
$L^A=0$
, some
$a\in A$
has
$a-1$
invertible on
$L$
. Then every derivation
$A\to L$
is principal by using commutativity of
$A$
. Passing to
$N/L$
completes the induction. Happy to go into more detail if required.

question:
Let me call a category
mono-regular
when every monomorphism is a regular monomorphism, i.e. an equalizer of a parallel pair. The category
$\mathbf{Rel}$
has sets as objects and relations as morphisms.

Question.
Is the category
$\mathbf{Rel}$
mono-regular?

A characterization of monomorphisms in
$\mathbf{Rel}$
can be found in
MSE/350716
: A relation
$R : A \dashrightarrow B$
is a monomorphism iff the map

$$R_* : P(A) \to P(B),\quad T \mapsto \{b \in B : \exists a \in T \,((a,b) \in R) \}$$

is injective.


Unfortunately,
$\mathbf{Rel}$
does not have equalizers in general. In fact,
$\mathbf{Rel}$
is not Cauchy complete by
MSE/1931577
. Still, the question if every monomorphism can be written as an equalizer can be asked.


A similar question is if every monomorphism in
$\mathbf{Rel}$
is normal, i.e. the kernel of some morphism; kernels are well-defined since
$\mathbf{Rel}$
has a zero object, the empty set. But this is not the case: The kernel of
$R : A \dashrightarrow B$
always exists and is given by the set
$A \setminus \operatorname{dom}(R)$
with the inclusion map to
$A$
. Since by
MSE/350716
there are monomorphisms which are no functions, there are monomorphisms which are no kernels.
But
this does not rule out the possibility that they are equalizers because we are not working in a preadditive category (it is only semiadditive).


Since
$\mathbf{Rel}$
is self-dual, the question is equivalent to if
$\mathbf{Rel}$
is epi-regular.


It is also worth mentioning that every mono-regular category is
balanced
. This prevents
many
categories from being mono-regular. But
$\mathbf{Rel}$
turns out to be balanced by
MSE/5030393
.

Context.
As of writing this, this is the last property that has not been decided (in the sense of: recorded) yet in
CatDat
for
Rel
.
answer:
I am surprised that CatDat says mono-regularity of
$\mathbf{Rel}$
is not yet decided (edit: recorded, thanks Martin for the clarification). I once did this as an exercise, here's the solution. If correct, we can check this off in CatDat.

Let
$R : A \dashrightarrow B$
be a monomorphism in
$\mathbf{Rel}$
. This means the map
$R_* : P(A) \to P(B)$
given by
$R_*(T) = \{b \in B : \exists a \in T \,((a,b) \in R)\}$
is injective. Because
$R_*$
is injective, for any
$a \in A$
, we must have
$R_*(A \setminus \{a\}) \neq R_*(A)$
. This allows us to define the set of "unique witnesses" for each
$a \in A$
:
$$W(a) = R(a) \setminus R_*(A \setminus \{a\})$$
By definition,
$W(a)$
is non-empty for every
$a \in A$
. Furthermore, if
$w \in W(a)$
, then
$w \in R(a)$
but
$w \notin R(a')$
for any
$a' \neq a$
. Thus,
$W(a) \cap W(a') = \emptyset$
whenever
$a \neq a'$
.

Let
$\mathcal{I} = \operatorname{Im}(R_*) = \{R_*(X) : X \subseteq A\}$
be the family of all subsets of
$B$
that are in the image of
$R_*$
. For any arbitrary subset
$Y \subseteq B$
, we can "guess" which subset
$X \subseteq A$
it could have come from by checking the witnesses.  Define

$$X_Y = \{a \in A : W(a) \cap Y \neq \emptyset\}$$

If
$Y \in \mathcal{I}$
, then
$Y = R_*(U)$
for some
$U \subseteq A$
. Since elements of
$W(a)$
exclusively appear in
$R(a)$
, we have
$W(a) \cap R_*(U) \neq \emptyset \iff a \in U$
. Therefore, if
$Y \in \mathcal{I}$
, it is strictly necessary that
$Y = R_*(X_Y)$
. Conversely, if
$Y \notin \mathcal{I}$
, then
$Y \neq R_*(X_Y)$
. This means either
$Y \setminus R_*(X_Y) \neq \emptyset$
or
$R_*(X_Y) \setminus Y \neq \emptyset$
.

Now we want to construct an object
$C$
and a parallel pair
$S, T : B \rightrightarrows C$
such that
$R$
is their equalizer. This is conceptually equivalent to designing
$S$
and
$T$
such that
$S_*(Z) = T_*(Z) \iff Z \in \mathcal{I}$
. Let
$C = P(B) \setminus \mathcal{I}$
(the set of all subsets of
$B$
that are not in the image of
$R_*$
). For every
$Y \in C$
, we will define testing subsets
$S_Y, T_Y \subseteq B$
separated into two exhaustive cases (they are not exclusive, but when both hold, either choice of construction works, the verifications later apply to whichever case is chosen):

$Y \setminus R_*(X_Y) \neq \emptyset$
. Choose an element
$b_Y \in Y \setminus R_*(X_Y)$
. Let
$A_{b_Y} = \{a \in A : b_Y \in R(a)\}$
. We define
$S_Y = \{b_Y\}$
and
$T_Y = \bigcup_{a \in A_{b_Y}} W(a)$
.
$R_*(X_Y) \setminus Y \neq \emptyset$
. Choose an element
$b_Y \in R_*(X_Y) \setminus Y$
. Since
$b_Y \in R_*(X_Y)$
, there exists some
$a_Y \in X_Y$
such that
$b_Y \in R(a_Y)$
. Because
$a_Y \in X_Y$
, we know
$W(a_Y) \cap Y \neq \emptyset$
. We can pick an element
$w_Y \in W(a_Y) \cap Y$
. We define
$S_Y = \{w_Y, b_Y\}$
and
$T_Y = \{b_Y\}$
.

$Y \setminus R_*(X_Y) \neq \emptyset$
. Choose an element
$b_Y \in Y \setminus R_*(X_Y)$
. Let
$A_{b_Y} = \{a \in A : b_Y \in R(a)\}$
. We define
$S_Y = \{b_Y\}$
and
$T_Y = \bigcup_{a \in A_{b_Y}} W(a)$
.

$R_*(X_Y) \setminus Y \neq \emptyset$
. Choose an element
$b_Y \in R_*(X_Y) \setminus Y$
. Since
$b_Y \in R_*(X_Y)$
, there exists some
$a_Y \in X_Y$
such that
$b_Y \in R(a_Y)$
. Because
$a_Y \in X_Y$
, we know
$W(a_Y) \cap Y \neq \emptyset$
. We can pick an element
$w_Y \in W(a_Y) \cap Y$
. We define
$S_Y = \{w_Y, b_Y\}$
and
$T_Y = \{b_Y\}$
.

Now, construct the relations
$S, T : B \dashrightarrow C$
by setting

$$(b, Y) \in S \iff b \in S_Y \quad \text{and} \quad (b, Y) \in T \iff b \in T_Y$$

We need to now verify the equalizer. Let's evaluate
$S_*(Z)$
and
$T_*(Z)$
for any
$Z \subseteq B$
. Note that

$$Y \in S_*(Z) \iff Z \cap S_Y \neq \emptyset$$

and identically for
$T_*$
.

If
$Z \in \mathcal{I}$
(i.e.,
$Z = R_*(U)$
for some
$U \subseteq A$
):

In Case 1:
$$Z \cap S_Y \neq \emptyset \iff b_Y \in Z \iff U \cap A_{b_Y} \neq \emptyset$$
On the other hand,
$$Z \cap T_Y \neq \emptyset \iff \exists a \in A_{b_Y} (W(a) \cap Z \neq \emptyset) \iff \exists a \in A_{b_Y} (a \in U) \iff U \cap A_{b_Y} \neq \emptyset$$
Thus,
$Z \cap S_Y \neq \emptyset \iff Z \cap T_Y \neq \emptyset$
.
In Case 2:
$$Z \cap S_Y \neq \emptyset \iff w_Y \in Z \lor b_Y \in Z$$
However, if
$w_Y \in Z$
, since
$w_Y \in W(a_Y)$
, we must have
$a_Y \in U$
. Consequently,
$R(a_Y) \subseteq Z$
, and since
$b_Y \in R(a_Y)$
, we inherently get
$b_Y \in Z$
. Therefore, the condition collapses to
$$w_Y \in Z \lor b_Y \in Z \iff b_Y \in Z \iff Z \cap T_Y \neq \emptyset$$

In Case 1:
$$Z \cap S_Y \neq \emptyset \iff b_Y \in Z \iff U \cap A_{b_Y} \neq \emptyset$$
On the other hand,
$$Z \cap T_Y \neq \emptyset \iff \exists a \in A_{b_Y} (W(a) \cap Z \neq \emptyset) \iff \exists a \in A_{b_Y} (a \in U) \iff U \cap A_{b_Y} \neq \emptyset$$
Thus,
$Z \cap S_Y \neq \emptyset \iff Z \cap T_Y \neq \emptyset$
.

In Case 2:
$$Z \cap S_Y \neq \emptyset \iff w_Y \in Z \lor b_Y \in Z$$
However, if
$w_Y \in Z$
, since
$w_Y \in W(a_Y)$
, we must have
$a_Y \in U$
. Consequently,
$R(a_Y) \subseteq Z$
, and since
$b_Y \in R(a_Y)$
, we inherently get
$b_Y \in Z$
. Therefore, the condition collapses to
$$w_Y \in Z \lor b_Y \in Z \iff b_Y \in Z \iff Z \cap T_Y \neq \emptyset$$

In both cases, we find that for all
$Z \in \mathcal{I}$
,
$S_*(Z) = T_*(Z)$
. Because
$\mathcal{I}$
contains the image of
$R_*$
, it immediately follows that
$S \circ R = T \circ R$
.

If
$Z \notin \mathcal{I}$
, then
$Z \in C$
. Let us evaluate the sets at the specific coordinate
$Y = Z$
.

In Case 1:
$Y \cap S_Y = \{b_Y\}$
, which is non-empty. But
$$Y \cap T_Y \neq \emptyset \iff \exists a \in A_{b_Y} (a \in X_Y) \iff b_Y \in R_*(X_Y)$$
Since we explicitly picked
$b_Y \notin R_*(X_Y)$
,
$Y \cap T_Y = \emptyset$
. They disagree.
In Case 2:
$Y \cap S_Y$
contains
$w_Y \in Y$
, so it is non-empty. But
$Y \cap T_Y = Y \cap \{b_Y\} = \emptyset$
because we strictly chose
$b_Y \notin Y$
. They disagree.

Thus,
$S_*(Z) = T_*(Z) \iff Z \in \mathcal{I}$
.

We now show the universal property. Suppose we have an object
$E$
and a morphism
$Q : E \dashrightarrow B$
such that
$S \circ Q = T \circ Q$
. This equation implies that for every element
$e \in E$
, we have
$S_*(Q(e)) = T_*(Q(e))$
. By our separation result above, this means
$Q(e) \in \mathcal{I}$
for all
$e$
. Because
$Q(e)$
belongs to the image of the injective function
$R_*$
, there is a unique subset
$U_e \subseteq A$
such that
$R_*(U_e) = Q(e)$
. We can thus uniquely define a relation
$U : E \dashrightarrow A$
by assigning
$U(e) = U_e$
for all
$e \in E$
. By construction,
$R \circ U = Q$
, and the uniqueness of
$U_e$
guarantees the uniqueness of the relation
$U$
. This confirms that
$R$
fits the universal property of an equalizer for
$S$
and
$T$
.

Because every monomorphism in
$\mathbf{Rel}$
satisfies this construction,
$\mathbf{Rel}$
is mono-regular. This finishes the proof.

Using the axiom of choice is really a bummer (personally for me), but I really don't see a way to avoid it if I am being honest. Maybe there is a better proof out there that chooses canonical elements.

Using
Martin's suggestion
, we can get rid of AC.

For Case 1, the idea is that instead of picking a single
$b_Y \in Y \setminus R_*(X_Y)$
, we take the entire difference as our test set. Let
$S_Y = Y \setminus R_*(X_Y)$
and
$A_{S_Y} = \{a \in A : S_Y \cap R(a) \neq \emptyset\}$
. Define
$T_Y = \bigcup_{a \in A_{S_Y}} W(a)$
.

Then if
$Z \in \mathcal{I}$
, we have

$$Z \cap S_Y \neq \emptyset \iff \exists b \in Z (b \in S_Y) \iff U \cap A_{S_Y} \neq \emptyset$$

Again

$$Z \cap T_Y \neq \emptyset \iff \exists a \in A_{S_Y} (W(a) \cap Z \neq \emptyset) \iff \exists a \in A_{S_Y} (a \in U) \iff U \cap A_{S_Y} \neq \emptyset$$

Thus they agree.

If
$Y = Z \notin \mathcal{I}$
, then
$Y \cap S_Y = Y \cap (Y \setminus R_*(X_Y)) = Y \setminus R_*(X_Y)$
, which is non-empty by the premise of Case 1. Again

$$Y \cap T_Y \neq \emptyset \iff \exists a \in A_{S_Y} (a \in X_Y) \iff S_Y \cap R_*(X_Y) \neq \emptyset$$

But
$S_Y$
is specifically defined as elements not in
$R_*(X_Y)$
, so this intersection is empty, and thus they disagree.

For Case 2, let
$B_Y = R_*(X_Y) \setminus Y$
and
$A_{B_Y} = \{a \in X_Y : R(a) \cap B_Y \neq \emptyset\}$
be the set of all elements in
$A$
responsible for mapping into this difference. Let
$W_Y = \bigcup_{a \in A_{B_Y}} (W(a) \cap Y)$
be the collection of all available witnesses in
$Y$
. Define
$S_Y = W_Y \cup B_Y$
and
$T_Y = B_Y$
.

Now for
$Z \in \mathcal{I}$
, we have

$$Z \cap S_Y \neq \emptyset \iff (W_Y \cap Z \neq \emptyset) \lor (B_Y \cap Z \neq \emptyset)$$

If
$W_Y \cap Z \neq \emptyset$
, there is some witness
$w \in W(a) \cap Y \cap Z$
for some
$a \in A_{B_Y}$
. Since
$w \in W(a)$
and
$w \in Z$
, it forces
$a \in U$
. If
$a \in U$
, then
$R(a) \subseteq Z$
. By definition of
$A_{B_Y}$
, we know
$R(a)$
intersects
$B_Y$
, meaning at least one element of
$B_Y$
is in
$Z$
. Thus, the condition collapses to
$B_Y \cap Z \neq \emptyset \iff Z \cap T_Y \neq \emptyset$
and hence they agree.

If
$Y = Z \notin \mathcal{I}$
, then
$Y \cap S_Y = (Y \cap W_Y) \cup (Y \cap B_Y)$
. Since
$B_Y$
contains elements explicitly not in
$Y$
,
$Y \cap B_Y = \emptyset$
. This leaves
$Y \cap W_Y = W_Y$
, which is non-empty. Also
$Y \cap T_Y = Y \cap B_Y = \emptyset$
, and hence they disagree.

The rest of the proof stays the same.

question:
Closed.
This question does not meet
Mathematics Stack Exchange guidelines
. It is not currently accepting answers.

This question is not about mathematics, within the scope defined in the
help center
.

Closed
4 days ago
.

Improve this question

Can Aristotle’s notions of potency and act be applied to mathematics?

I ask because I think it can for consider the following. Suppose we had a circle. Then that circle is potentially a line because I can cut the circle at some point on the circumference and deform it into a line.

Suppose we had an infinite line and that infinite line had negative infinity and positive infinity as end points. Then that infinite line is potentially a circle because I can glue the end points together to form a circle.
answer:
That depends on your philosophy of mathematics. Aristotle's theory of act and potency is a theory of change. If your philosophy is that mathematics consists of timeless unchanging truths, then it is difficult to see either act or potency.

Your geometry example hints at something else -
Klein's Erlangen program
. Klein asks us to first specify what changes we are allowed to make, and then study only those features that remain invariant under these changes. This means that Euclidean geometry can study circles because all the rigid motions that are allowed in that geometry preserve circles as circles. Affine geometry does not preserve distances, and so we cannot study circles, but only ellipses in general as affine geometry can transform any circle or ellipse into any other circle or ellipse. Projective geometry does not allow that also as it can send a circle into an ellipse, parabola or hyperbola. We can talk only of conic sections.

Your specific example belongs to inversive geometry which does not distinguish between circles and lines. These
generalized circles
are often called clines or circlines.

Many mathematicians when studying circlines for example would not say that a circle is potentially a line or vice versa, but would say that circles and lines are really the same thing in inversive geometry. This is in line with the philosophy of unchanging mathematical truths that I mentioned at the outset.

But you could have a different philosophy of mathematics that makes potency and act relevant. I would encourage you to consider other examples. Is
$7$
potentially
$2$
because if you divide
$7$
by
$5$
and take the remainder, the result is
$2$
? (In modulo
$5$
arithmetic, we would regard
$7$
and
$2$
as being the same, and in modulo
$3$
arithmetic, we would regard
$7$
and
$1$
as being the same.)

question:
I am trying to understand the distinction between the relations

$$
\to_\beta
\qquad\text{and}\qquad
\twoheadrightarrow_\beta
$$


in lambda calculus notation.


By
$M \to_\beta N$
, I mean that
$N$
is obtained from
$M$
by exactly one
$\beta$
-reduction step.


By
$M \twoheadrightarrow_\beta N$
, I mean that
$N$
is obtained from
$M$
by zero or more
$\beta$
-reduction steps.


I am looking for an explicit example of a lambda term
$M$
such that


$$
M \twoheadrightarrow_\beta M
$$


via a nontrivial reduction sequence.


More precisely, I want a sequence


$$
M \to_\beta M_1 \to_\beta \cdots \to_\beta M
$$


with at least two
$\beta$
-reduction steps and such that at least one intermediate term
$M_i$
is syntactically different from
$M$
.


I do not want the trivial repetition


$$
\Omega \to_\beta \Omega \to_\beta \Omega
$$


where every term in the sequence is identical.


Does such an example exist in pure
$\beta$
-reduction?
answer:
Take Turing's
fixpoint combinator
$\Theta = \big(\lambda x.\lambda y.y(xxy)\big) \lambda x.\lambda y.y(xxy)$
.
Note that, for every term
$M$
, we have
\begin{align}
\Theta M &= \Big(\big(\lambda x.\lambda y'\!.y'(xxy')\big) \lambda x.\lambda y.y(xxy) \Big) M
\\
&\to_\beta \Big(\lambda y'\!.y' \big((\lambda x.\lambda y.y(xxy) ) \lambda x.\lambda y.y(xxy) \big)y' \Big) M
\\
&\to_\beta M \Big(\big((\lambda x.\lambda y.y(xxy)) \lambda x.\lambda y.y(xxy) \big) M \Big) = M (\Theta M)
\end{align}
that is,
$\Theta M$
$\beta$
-reduces to
$M (\Theta M)$
in two
$\beta$
-steps, for every term
$M$
.

In particular, for
$M = \lambda z.z$
, we have (I omit the intermediate term after the first
$\beta$
-step)
$$
\Theta \, \lambda z.z \to_\beta \cdot \to_\beta (\lambda z.z) (\Theta \, \lambda z.z)  \to_\beta \Theta \, \lambda z.z
$$
which answers positively your question.

question:
Recently I've been investigating the constant
$\omega=\int_{0}^{\infty}{W(x)e^{-x}dx}$
, where
$W$
is the principal branch of the Lambert W function. My main goal was to find a series or limit that could quickly approximate the constant. I had the idea to take advantage of a trick used to show the integral
$\int_{0}^{\infty}{\ln(x)e^{-x}dx}=-\gamma$
where one replaces the
$e^{-x}$
in the integrand with
$\lim_{n\to\infty}{\xi_{(0,n]}(x)(1-\frac{x}{n})^{n}}$
and then uses the dominated convergence theorem to justify a swap of the limit and the integral. While this did yield a closed form expression for
$\omega$
, the form converges rather slowly. To be more explicit one has that,
$$\int_{0}^{\infty}{W(x)e^{-x}dx}=\int_{0}^{\infty}{W(x)\lim_{n\to\infty}{\xi_{[0,n]}(x)(1-\frac{x}{n})^{n}}dx}=\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_{0}^{\infty}{W(x){\xi_{[0,n]}(x)(1-\frac{x}{n})^{n}}dx}=\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_{0}^{n}{W(x)(1-\frac{x}{n})^{n}}dx=\lim_{n\to\infty}\int_{0}^{n}{W(x)\sum_{k=0}^{n}{\binom nk}(-1)^{n-k}\big(\frac{x}{n}\big)^k}dx=\lim_{n\to\infty}\sum_{k=0}^{n}\frac{{\binom nk}(-1)^{n-k}}{n^k}\int_{0}^{n}{W(x)x^k}dx.$$
The integral in the summand on the right above can be put into exponential form with the substitution
$x=ue^u$
and then finally expressed either in terms of the incomplete gamma functions or a very messy sum. After cleaning up the final expression for
$\omega$
I obtained the following,
$$\omega=\lim_{n\to\infty}\sum_{k=0}^{n}{\frac{\binom nk\Omega_n(k)}{n^k}},$$
where
$$\Omega_{n}{(k)}=\frac{1}{(k+1)^{k+2}}\bigg[e^{(k+1)W(n)}\bigg((k+1)^{k+1}(-W(n))^{k+2}+\sum_{j=0}^{k+1}{\frac{k!(-(k+1)W(n))^j}{j!}}\bigg)-k!\bigg].$$
Although this limit does converge to
$\omega$
, it does so fairly slowly. For instance if
$n=30000$
we have
$0.48182147...$
when
$\omega=0.48185437.... $
My question is what other representations exist for
$\omega$
that converge faster. I suppose the best way to approximate
$\omega$
would be through numerical integration however I'd still like to know other expressions. In fact any other information on this integral would be appreciated.
answer:
Noting the alternate representation

$$\omega=\int\limits_0^1 x^{\frac{1}{x}-1} \, dx\tag{1}$$

mentioned by
Eli Bartlett
in a comment on the question above is equivalent to
$\frac{1}{2}$
times the inverse Fourier cos transform

$$f(t)=2 \int\limits_0^\infty F(x)\, \cos(2 \pi t x) \, dx\tag{2}$$

evaluated at
$t=0$
where

$$F(x)=\left\{\begin{array}{cc}
0 & x=0 \\
x^{\frac{1}{x}-1} & 0<x<1 \\
\frac{1}{2} & x=1 \\
0 & x>1 \\
\end{array}\right.\tag{3}$$

leads to the formula

$$\omega=\lim\limits_{N\to\infty} \left(\frac{1}{2} \sum\limits_{n=1}^{N} \frac{\mu(2 n-1)}{2 n-1}\\ \left(\sum\limits_{k=1}^{2 (2 n-1)} (-1)^k\, F\left(\frac{k}{4 n-2}\right) \cos\left(\frac{\pi k}{2 n-1}\right)-\frac{1}{4} \sum\limits_{k=1}^{4 (2 n-1)} (-1)^k\, F\left(\frac{k}{8 n-4}\right)\right)\right)\tag{4}$$

where
$\mu(n)$
is the
Möbius function
and
$F(x)$
is defined in formula (3) above.

When evaluated at
$N=10$
formula (4) above gives the result
$\omega=0.48185437562720135$
using Mathematica's default precision.

Formula (4) above is a simplification of formula (11) in my
related Math StackOverflow question
where the simplification takes into account
$F(x)=0$
both when
$x=0$
and also when
$x>1$
.

WorkingPrecision -> 260, AccuracyGoal -> 220, PrecisionGoal -> 220,
Method -> "GlobalAdaptive", MaxRecursion -> 200];

I got the following from "N[I100, 200]" :

$0.48185437942079727269048102349957905797810242109514911079173234982611\\ 45432449576633992215274184787220163598652177968547753352692405120919486\\4712476461309131931094278740788924517235800945153948326916843$

This should be enough digits.

answer:
we have :
$$\int_{0}^{\infty}{W(x)e^{-x}dx} =\int_{1}^{\infty} x^{-x-1} dx $$

using this
paper
we find

$$\int_{0}^{\infty}{W(x)e^{-x}dx} =\sum_{n \ge 0} (-1)^n (n+1)^n $$

question:
The case
$\sqrt{-7}$

By analogy to the Eisenstein integers which involve
$\sqrt{-3}$
, we can consider
primitive
representations in
three
ways to integers
$N$
,

\begin{align}
N &=\, 7a^4+50a^2b^2+7b^4\\
&=(a^2+7b^2)(7a^2+b^2) = (c^2+7d^2)(7c^2+d^2) = (e^2+7f^2)(7e^2+f^2)\end{align}

with
$\text{GCD}(a,b,c,d,e,f)=1$
. Surprisingly, within a very limited range of terms
$<1500$
, I already found
five

$N$
,

\begin{align}
N_1 = 712966144;\; (a,b;\;c,d;\;e,f) &= (16, 96;\; 37, 79;\; 38, 78)\\
N_2 = 3951643869184;\; (a,b;\;c,d;\;e,f) &= (43, 863;\; 117, 839;\; 372, 628)\\
N_3 = 11415746535424;\; (a,b;\;c,d;\;e,f) &= (327, 973;\; 378, 926;\; 614, 686)\\
N_4 = 13156119691264;\; (a,b;\;c,d;\;e,f) &= (436, 916;\; 438, 914;\; 587, 761)\\
N_5 = 25085359366144;\; (a,b;\;c,d;\;e,f) &= (139, 1351;\; 469, 1119;\; 566, 1022)
\end{align}

Question
: Are there more primitive solutions
$(a,b,c,d,e,f)$
? Or, more generally, are there
infinitely
many, like in the case
$a^4-b^4$
below?

(
Start context
.)

I. Complex and Split-complex numbers

Consider the
imaginary unit

$i$
and
hyperbolic unit

$j$
such that
$i^2=-1$
and
$j^2=1$
where
$j\neq1$
. Then,

$$N=a^4-b^4=(a+bi)(a-bi)(a+bj)(a-bj)\quad$$


and there are
infinitely
many
$N$
with
primitive
representations in
three
ways, like in
this post
,


$$N=335084^4-296668^4 =265076^4-93436^4 = 264047^4-1169^4$$




II. Eisenstein integers


Like the norm of the complex number
$a+bi\,$
is
$a^2+b^2$
, the norm of the Eisenstein integer
$a+b\omega\,$
is
$a^2-ab+b^2$
. But there is only
one
known
$N_1=346084535811$
to,


\begin{align}
N_1 &= a^4+a^2b^2+b^4 = (a^2+ab+b^2)(a^2-ab+b^2)\\
&= a^4+a^2b^2+b^4 = c^4+c^2d^2+d^4 = e^4+e^2f^2+f^4\end{align}


with a primitive representation in three ways (found by "Aleksandr" in
this post
),


$$(a,b;\;c,d;\;e,f) = (1,767;\;187,755;\;485,659)$$


Equivalently, like the analogy to
$\sqrt{-7}$
,


\begin{align}
N_1 &=3a^4+10a^2b^2+3b^4\\
& = (a^2+3b^2)(3a^2+b^2) = (c^2+3d^2)(3c^2+d^2) = (e^2+3f^2)(3e^2+f^2)\end{align}


$$(a,b;\;c,d;\;e,f)=(87, 572;\; 284, 471;\; 383, 384)$$


which uses slightly smaller integers.




III. Golden integers


Given the golden ratio
$\phi$
, the field norm of the
golden integer

$a+b\phi\,$
is
$a^2+ab-b^2$
. But there is also only
one
known
$M=116589605$
to,


\begin{align}M &= a^4-3a^2b^2+b^4 = (a^2+ab-b^2)(a^2-ab-b^2)\\
&= a^4-3a^2b^2+b^4 = c^4-3c^2d^2+d^4 = e^4-3e^2f^2+f^4\end{align}


with a primitive representation in three ways (found by "T. Piezas")


$$(a,b;\;c,d;\;e,f) = (17, 106;\; 94, 167;\; 134, 223)$$

(
End context. Question is the first section.
)

The case
$\sqrt{-7}$

By analogy to the Eisenstein integers which involve
$\sqrt{-3}$
, we can consider
primitive
representations in
three
ways to integers
$N$
,

\begin{align}
N &=\, 7a^4+50a^2b^2+7b^4\\
&=(a^2+7b^2)(7a^2+b^2) = (c^2+7d^2)(7c^2+d^2) = (e^2+7f^2)(7e^2+f^2)\end{align}

with
$\text{GCD}(a,b,c,d,e,f)=1$
. Surprisingly, within a very limited range of terms
$<1500$
, I already found
five

$N$
,

\begin{align}
N_1 = 712966144;\; (a,b;\;c,d;\;e,f) &= (16, 96;\; 37, 79;\; 38, 78)\\
N_2 = 3951643869184;\; (a,b;\;c,d;\;e,f) &= (43, 863;\; 117, 839;\; 372, 628)\\

: Are there more primitive solutions
$(a,b,c,d,e,f)$
? Or, more generally, are there
infinitely
many, like in the case
$a^4-b^4$
below?

Start context
.)

I. Complex and Split-complex numbers

Consider the
imaginary unit

$i$
and
hyperbolic unit

$j$
such that
$i^2=-1$
and
$j^2=1$
where
$j\neq1$
. Then,

and there are
infinitely
many
$N$
with
primitive
representations in
three
ways, like in
this post
,


$$N=335084^4-296668^4 =265076^4-93436^4 = 264047^4-1169^4$$

Like the norm of the complex number
$a+bi\,$
is
$a^2+b^2$
, the norm of the Eisenstein integer
$a+b\omega\,$
is
$a^2-ab+b^2$
. But there is only
one
known
$N_1=346084535811$
to,


\begin{align}
N_1 &= a^4+a^2b^2+b^4 = (a^2+ab+b^2)(a^2-ab+b^2)\\
&= a^4+a^2b^2+b^4 = c^4+c^2d^2+d^4 = e^4+e^2f^2+f^4\end{align}


with a primitive representation in three ways (found by "Aleksandr" in

$$(a,b;\;c,d;\;e,f)=(87, 572;\; 284, 471;\; 383, 384)$$


which uses slightly smaller integers.

$\phi$

golden integer

$a+b\phi\,$
is

. But there is also only
one
known
$M=116589605$
to,


\begin{align}M &= a^4-3a^2b^2+b^4 = (a^2+ab-b^2)(a^2-ab-b^2)\\
&= a^4-3a^2b^2+b^4 = c^4-3c^2d^2+d^4 = e^4-3e^2f^2+f^4\end{align}

answer:
I've searched
$N=(a^2+7b^2)(7a^2+b^2) < 7\times392000^4 \approx 1.6 \times 10^{23}$
and found 4 more solutions to the original 5:

$$  N_6 =     150573624914944 ;  ( 2134,  154 ;  2038,  378 ;  1617 ,  869) $$
$$  N_7 =   48039544529895424 ;  ( 7428, 3076 ;  6722, 3782 ;  6649 , 3853) $$
$$  N_8 =  161054532645766144 ;  (11258, 2758 ; 10507, 3673 ;  8034 , 6146) $$
$$  N_9 = 3030188339610101824 ;  (25277, 2323 ; 24311, 4439 ; 19881 , 9737) $$

Tomita has already reported
$N_8$
in his answer. One more solution with search extended to
$N < 7\times598200^4 \approx 8.9 \times 10^{23}$
:

$$ N_{10} = 634285026602406972457984 ;  (545407, 31613 ; 433659, 199849; 423602, 209906) $$

Note 1
: For
$N=(a^2+7b^2)(7a^2+b^2)\, < 7\times 1376800^4 \approx 2.5 \times 10^{25}$
, no more solutions.

Note 2
: For
$N=(a^4+a^2b^2+b^4)\, < 2534100^4 \approx 4.1 \times 10^{25}$
, only
one
primitive solution.

Note 3
: For
$N= (a^4 - 3a^2b^2 + b^4)$
, the algorithm doesn't work because it is not a monotone function.

P.S.
The search algorithms are based on D. J. Bernstein, "Enumerating solutions to
$p(a)+q(b)=r(c)+s(d)$
", Math. Comp. Vol. 70 (2001), pp. 389-394.

$$\boxed{A^4-3A^2B^2+B^4=C^4-3C^2D^2+D^4}$$
$$\begin{cases} A=(-p^2+3ps+2s^2)m^2+(-3p^2-6ps+3s^2)mn+(2p^2-3ps-s^2)n^2 \\ B=(2p^2-3ps-s^2)m^2 +(3p^2+6ps-3s^2)mn+(-p^2+3ps+2s^2)n^2 \\ C=(2p^2-s^2+3ps)m^2+(-3p^2+6ps+3s^2)mn+(-p^2-3ps+2s^2)n^2 \\ D=(-p^2-3ps+2s^2)m^2+(3p^2-6ps-3s^2)mn+(2p^2+3ps-s^2)n^2 \end{cases}$$

$$86^4-3×81^2×151^2+151^4=103^4-3×103^2×38^2+38^4$$

answer:
We claim that there are not infinitely many integer solutions (primitive or not) which answer a question posted by the O.P. By the way, remark that
$$(ax^2+y^2)(ay^2+x^2)=a(x^2+y^2)^2+((a-1)xy)^2\hspace2cm(1)$$
which show a kind of stability under multiplication, maybe useful for the O.P.'s search.

Now, for
$a$
given, all
$(x,y)$
determines an integer
$N_a$
and looking for other similar representations of
$N_a$
we have to studied the plane curve
$a(x^2+y^2)^2+((a-1)xy)^2=N_a$
(or, equivalently,
$(ax^2+y^2)(ay^2+x^2)=N_a$
).

This fact shows easily what we claim because the curve is bounded and symmetric, completely defined in the first quadrant (all solution yields three other trivially distinct).

Looking for example the case
$a=7$
for which the O.P. gives
$3$
solutions
$$(x,y)=(16,96),(37,79),(38,78)$$
With the first one we define the curve
$$7(x^2+y^2)^2+36x^2y^2=712966144\hspace1cm(2)$$
and the searched representations is given by the total number of integer solutions of
$(2)$
. As we said above the curve cannot have infinitely many integer solutions.
And remains open the problem of knowing if there are more of three integer points
(
this can be finish with a computer proving with
$0\le x\le 100$
).

question:
I've heard that
$ω<ω+1$
, which implies that
$ω-1<ω$
, but if
$ω-1$
isn't finite, wouldn't that mean
$ω-1$
is infinite, making it the new smallest infinite ordinal? Furthermore, we can continue this sequence to
$ω-2$
, then
$ω-3$
, and so on, so why is
$ω$
the smallest infinite ordinal?
answer:
It is correct that
$\omega < \omega + 1$
. However, you cannot deduce
$\omega - 1 < \omega$
from that.

If
$\alpha,\beta$
are ordinals satisfying
$\alpha \leq \beta$
, then
$\beta - \alpha$
is defined
$^1$
to be the unique ordinal satisfying
$$\alpha + (\beta - \alpha) = \beta. \tag{1}$$
It is important to write this equation in this form. The equation
$(\beta - \alpha) + \alpha = \beta$
would be different since ordinal addition is not commutative. Also, ordinal addition is only left cancellative, not right cancellative, so if we demand
$(\beta - \alpha) + \alpha = \beta$
(even though it looks more natural), neither existence nor uniqueness would be guaranteed. To make
$(1)$
look better, maybe the notation
$(-\alpha + \beta)$
would be more appropriate:
$\alpha + (-\alpha + \beta) = \beta$
.

Now, we have
$1 + \omega = \omega$
, because the left hand is defined as the supremum of all
$1 + n$
with
$n < \omega$
, which is equal to
$\omega$
. More generally then, we have
$n + \omega = \omega$
for all
$n < \omega$
. By definition of the subtraction above, this means that
$$\omega - n = \omega$$
for all
$n < \omega$
.

$^1$
See
MSE/1339957
or
Proof Wiki
for the proof that this ordinal exists and is unique. The latter article references Def. 8.14 in Takeuti-Zaring's
Introduction to Axiomatic Set Theory
, where the the notation is slightly different: there is a dot underneath the minus sign (
Screenshot
).
You may use a different definition, but then you need to provide it. Notice that there is no ordinal
$\delta$
satisfying
$\delta + 1 = \omega$
, so the "other" definition of
$\omega - 1$
does not make any sense. My answer here refers to the only possible definition of ordinal subtraction inside of the collection of ordinals.
Another definition can be found in the context of
surreal numbers
, see Conway's
On Numbers and Games
, Chapter 0. Here,
$\omega - 1 = \{0,1,2,\dotsc \mid \omega\}$
(p. 13), but this is not an ordinal anymore. For surreal numbers, all sorts of expressions like
$1/(\pi \omega^2 + \omega)$
are defined.

question:
Given object in world coordinate system (north east up), I would like to convert them into my target coordinate system (east north up). I managed to compute the rotation matrix for this transformation. However, I am not able to extract roll pitch yaw out from the rotation matrix, since its determinant is
$-1$
.

Since this involves the conversion from left-handed coordinate system to right-handed coordinate, and swapping
$x$
and
$y$
axes means reflection, which makes the determinant of the rotation matrix to be
$-1$
. In this case, I have no clue on the next step in how to extract roll pitch yaw from this rotation matrix.

The steps that I have tried:

compute the extrinsic matrix from the object's roll pitch yaw in north east up coordinate system using scipy, this is to create the projection matrix to project bbox from its local frame to the world frame. This forms
$T$
.

compute the extrinsic matrix from the world (north east up) to the target coordinate frame (east north up). first i create the extrinsic matrix by taking into account the roll pitch yaw of the target coordinate frame. Then, I swapped the
$x$
and
$y$
axes to change the coordinate frame from north east up to east north up. This forms
$T_{\text{target}_\text{world}}$
.

$T_\text{target} = T_{\text{target}_\text{world}} @ T$
, but when I want to extract roll pitch yaw from this, I realised the determinant of the rotation matrix is
$-1$
, and I can't extract roll pitch yaw out from this matrix.

Appendix:

import numpy as np
from scipy.spatial.transform import Rotation

def compute_extrinsic_matrix(rotation_deg: list, position: list, R_frame: np.ndarray) -> np.ndarray:
"""4×4 world-to-camera extrinsic matrix given rotation (degrees), position (world), and frame transform."""
R_world = Rotation.from_euler("xyz", np.deg2rad(rotation_deg)).as_matrix()
R = R_frame @ R_world
t = -R @ np.array(position)
T = np.eye(4)
T[:3, :3] = R
T[:3, 3] = t
return T

def compute_corners_extrinsic(centroid, rotation_deg):
R = Rotation.from_euler("xyz", [
-np.deg2rad(rotation_deg[0]),
-np.deg2rad(rotation_deg[1]),
np.deg2rad(rotation_deg[2]),
]).as_matrix()

T = np.eye(4)
T[:3, :3] = R
T[:3, 3] = centroid
return T

# source coordinate system: north east up
# final coordinate system: east north up
target_pos = [1899.179375, -874.0764843750001, 6.1342230224609375]
target_rot = [-8.445300102233887, 2.3178999423980713, 151.41549682617188]
R_mmdet_target = np.zeros((3, 3))
# swapping x and y axes
R_mmdet_target[0, 1] = 1
R_mmdet_target[1, 0] = 1
R_mmdet_target[2, 2] = 1
T_target_world = compute_extrinsic_matrix(target_rot, target_pos, R_mmdet_target)

# coordinate system: north east up
obj_centroid = [-111.3290625, -923.026640625, 17.872371826171875]
obj_rotation = [-0.09640000015497208, -0.2476000040769577, 157.31590270996094] # roll, pitch, yaw (deg)
obj_size = [319.59347656250003, 42.5987353515625, 63.4886259765625]

T = compute_corners_extrinsic(obj_centroid, obj_rotation)
T_target = T_target_world @ T
print("T_target:\n", T_target[:3,:3])

roll, pitch, yaw = Rotation.from_matrix(T_target[:3, :3]).as_euler("xyz", degrees=True)

Error:
ValueError: Non-positive determinant (left-handed or null coordinate frame) in rotation matrix 0:
[[-0.77666852 0.61946678 -0.11422312] 0.62946924 0.77003759 -0.10397399] [-0.02354766 0.15265327 0.98799923]]
.
answer:
The decomposition into roll, pitch, and yaw rotations is only defined for rotations. Your transformation is not a rotation but a reflection, and its matrix is not a rotation matrix (rotation matrix always has determinant
$+1$
).

So roll, pitch, and yaw are not well defined in your case, and if you need something similar, you need to properly define what exactly do you want to compute.

One variant is that a reflection or an improper rotation can be presented as a composition of a rotation and the inversion transformation (the transformation with matrix
$P = \begin{bmatrix} -1 & 0 & 0 \\ 0 & -1 & 0 \\ 0 & 0 & -1\end{bmatrix}$
that reverses all axes, transforming a right coordinate system into a left coordinate system and vice versa), so you can represent your transformation as a composition of some roll, pitch, yaw, and inversion. If this is fine for your goals, you can compute the angles by first multiplying you reflection matrix by
$P$
.

question:
Let
$f:M\to \mathbb R$
be a smooth function on a Riemannian manifold
$M$
. Define the following notion of constrained equilibrium with respect to
$f$
:

$1.$
A constrained equilibrium with respect to
$f$
and
$\alpha>0$
is a smooth map
$\Phi:M\times \mathbb [0,\infty)\to \mathbb R$
such that
$\partial_t\Phi(x,t)=\Delta \Phi^\alpha(x,t)$
, for all
$t$
and
$\Phi(x,0)=f(x)$
, for all
$x\in M$
, and there exists a
$T>0$
and a corresponding
$c\in \mathbb R$
such that
$\Phi(x,T)=c$
, for all
$x\in M$
. Since
$T$
depends on
$\Phi$
, so we write
$T_{\Phi}$
.

$2.$
A minimal-time equilibrium to
$f$
and
$\alpha$
is an equilibrium
$\Phi$
, such that for every equilibrium
$\Psi$
with respect to
$f$
,
$T_{\Phi}\leq T_{\Psi}$
.

My question is whether there exists a constrained minimal-time equilibrium for any given
$f$
and
$\alpha>0$
, and if there exists such an equilibrium, is it stable, i.e. does it also satisfy
$\Phi(x,t)=c$
, for all
$t\geq T_{\Phi}$
.

Can anyone suggest me some directions to start with this problem?
answer:
As written in the comments by @AlexJones, this seems to be a generalization of the standard heat equation on a manifold which corresponds to
$\alpha=1$
in your notation. If we assume
$M$
to be compact, one can show that the map
$\Phi$
satisfying
$\partial_t\Phi(x,t)=\Delta \Phi(x,t)$
, for all
$t$
and
$\Phi(x,0)=f(x)$
, for all
$x\in M$
exists and is unique.

This map will converge to the constant map for
$t$
large, however unless
$f$
is already the constant map, one will not have
$\Phi(x,T)=c$
for any finite
$T$
.

question:
The
bounty
expires
in 6 days
. Answers to this question are eligible for a
+50
reputation bounty.

Kiarash Koopal
wants to
draw more attention
to this question:

This problem deserves more attention.

Let a finite rectangular board be divided into unit squares. Along the grid lines of the board, an invisible simple closed path (that is, a non-self-intersecting closed polygonal line) is drawn.
In each cell, a nonnegative integer not exceeding
$4$
is written; this number equals the number of sides of the cell that belong to the path.
Prove that the length of the path can always be determined from the numbers written on the board alone.

Some (trivial) observations:

There are several useful identities that can be obtained by double-counting. For example, if
$S$
denotes the sum of the numbers written in the cells inside the path, then the length of the path equals
$S$
.

Another identity is the following: if we add all numbers written on the board and also add the number of unit segments of the path lying on the boundary of the board, then the result is twice the length of the path.

Thus, knowing any one of the following quantities would immediately solve the problem:

. the number of path segments lying on the boundary of the board;

. the sum of the numbers written in the cells inside the path;

. the sum of the numbers written in the cells outside the path.

However, none of these quantities seems easy to recover directly from the given data. So there must be some way to exploit the arrangement of the numbers on the board itself.

I tried beginning the analysis from the boundary cells, but the casework quickly became messy, and I do not see a clear path forward.

A naive idea is to construct a graph from the board; let vertices be the cells and two of them be connected iff they share a side which belongs to the path.
answer:
As I read it the
'grid lines of the board'
exclude the boundary lines or the path needs to stay on the inside of the grid. Then the length of the closed path is indeed and obviously the sum of cell entries divided by 2.

A proof can start from a closed path around one cell and then extend to more cells...

If you include the boundary lines, you can't read off the length of the cycle without additional information...
