details or clarity
. It is not currently accepting answers.

Want to improve this question?
As written, this question is lacking some of the information it needs to be answered. If the author adds details in comments, consider
editing them into the question
. Once there's sufficient detail to answer, vote to reopen the question.

Closed
3 days ago
.

Improve this question

What are solutions of system

$\begin{cases} 1+a | b^2+c^2 \\ 1+b | a^2+c^2 \\ 1+c | a^2+b^2 \end{cases}$

so
$1+a$
divides
$b^2+c^2$
,
$1+b$
divides
$a^2+c^2$
and
$1+c$
divides
$a^2+b^2$

(
$a,b,c$
are integers)

Are there infinite set of solutions (in natural numbers) and how to constuct them ? Some examples are quite easy to observe , this are
$(0, n, n^2-1)$
or (-3,-3,-3);
is that good idea to take
$c=a^2+b^2-1$
; example
$(2,3,12)$
?!

So the question is how to generate all solutions ?

Another related problem is to prove that if
$b, c >1$
then system

$1+b |c^2+1 $

$1+c |b^2+1 $

with
$b+c$
even has no sulutions
( that generate
$a=1$
)

However similar system

$b |c^2+1 $

$c |b^2+1 $

has infinitely many solutions (constructed for example with Fibonacci numbers).
answer:
We will show
$$
(a,b,c)=\left(n,\ n^2-1,\ n^2(n^2-1)\right), \ n \geq 2
$$
always works
(assume
$0 \not \in \mathbb{N}$
).

For verification, simply check the three conditions.

Divisibility Condition 1:
$a+1 \mid b^2+c^2$
Note
$$
a+1 = n+1,\quad b = n^2-1 = (n-1)(n+1),
$$
so
$a+1 \mid b$
. Further,
$a+1\mid c$
, since
$$
c = n^2(n^2-1).
$$
Hence,
$$
a+1 \mid b^2 + c^2. \quad \text{QED.}
$$

Divisibility Condition 2:
$b+1 \mid a^2+c^2$
Note
$$
b+1 = n^2.
$$
Since
$$
a^2 = n^2, \quad c = n^2(n^2-1),
$$
and both
$a^2, c^2$
are divisible by
$n^2$
,
$$
b+1 \mid a^2+c^2. \quad \text{QED.}
$$

Divisibility Condition 3:
$c+1 \mid a^2+b^2$
Note
$$
c+1 = n^2(n^2-1) + 1 = n^4-n^2+1
$$
and
$$
a^2+b^2=n^2+(n^2-1)^2
=n^4-n^2+1.
$$
Hence,
$$
c+1=a^2+b^2, \implies c+1\mid a^2+b^2.
$$

Therefore
$$
\boxed{(n,\ n^2-1,\ n^2(n^2-1))}
$$
Is always a solution given
$n \geq 2$
.

Hope this helps!

question:
Prove that no order can be defined in the complex field that turns it into an ordered field.

My question is what exactly does it mean to "define an order?". Here is my proof, which I think is incorrect: Suppose the complex field is an ordered field defined by the order by
$i>0$
. Since
$i^2=-1<0$
, this is impossible. If we instead define
$i<0$
, we have a similar contradiction. Finally, if we define
$i=0$
, then
$-1=i^2=0^2=0$
, another contradiction. Thus we cannot define an order on the complex field which makes it an ordered field.

I just don't know if the way I defined the order makes sense.
answer:
Rudin defines an order (def 1.5) as a relation "
$<$
" that obeys the following two conditions

i) for any
$x,y \in S$
exactly one of the following is true
$x< y, x=y, y < x$

ii) for
$x,y,z \in S$
if
$x<y$
and
$y < z$
then
$x < z$
always follows.

So to "define an order" means simply to come up with such a relationship.

It's actually pretty easy to "define some orders" on
$\mathbb C$
that obey those properties.

If we view
$z = a+bi$
(
$a,b\in \mathbb R$
) as simply the ordered pair
$z=(a,b)$
. We can define a "dictionary order" that
$(a,b) < (c,d)$
if either
$a < c$
or if
$a=c$
then
$b < d$
.

More abstractly so you don't get biased to your idea of what "less" and "greater" mean intuitively....

Each ordered pair
$(a,b)$
has a distance from
$0$
that is
$\sqrt{a^2 +b^2}$
and we can define an order so that
$(a,b) < (c,d)$
if the distance from
$(a,b)$
to
$0$
is
greater
than the distance of
$(c,d)$
from
$0$
. (Why did I say if it was
greater
rather than less? Simply to screw with your preconceptions). But what do we do if the distance of
$(a,b)$
is equal to the distance from
$(c,d)$
, that is what ife
$\sqrt{a^2+b^2} =\sqrt{c^2 + d^2}$
? The we need some way to distiguish those. As each order pair
$(a,b)$
has an argument angle we can define
$(a,b) < (c,d)$
if either
$\sqrt{a^2 +b^2} > \sqrt {c^2 + d^2}$
; or if
$\sqrt{a^2+b^2}=\sqrt{c^2+d^2}$
but
$\arg(a,b)> \arg(c,d)$

Those are valid orders on the set. But notice they have
nothing
to do with arithmetic of the complex numbers.

......

The thing is these orders, do not make an ordered
field
. An ordered field (on Def. 1.17.) must
also
obey these two conditions.

If
$y < z$
then
$x +y < x+z$
for all
$x$
. (This fails in both my examples)

And if
$x>0, y> 0$
then
$xy > 0$
. (Fails on my first order but does actually work on my second as
$0 << w$
never happens).

.....

So the goal here is not to define an order on
$\mathbb C$
(there are infinitely many) but to show it is
not
possible to have an order on
$C$
that makes
$\mathbb C$
an ordered field. That is to say, those to conditions must fail.

"Since
$i^2 = -1 < 0$
this is impossible"

"Since
$i^2 = -1 < 0$
this is impossible"

What if
$i^2 =-1 > 0$
? We can define
$>$
so that
$-1> 0$
because
$>$
is a
new
order we can make up any way we want..

.......

You are
very
close to having a correct proof.

Rudin has proposition 1.18 that includes 1.18 d) In an ordered field then if
$x\ne 0$
then
$x^2 >0$
and in particular,
$1 = 1^2 > 0$
.

So you do not need to consider
$i > 0$
and
$i< 0$
separately, we know by 1.18d that
$i^2 = -1 > 0$
.

But there's nothing really wrong with that. If this is a new order then
$-1 > 0$
might be just fine.

But we also have by 1.18d that
$1^2 = 1 > 0$
so we have both
$-1>0$
and
$1> 0$
. Is that possible?

Hint Consider our original condition:

If
$y <z$
then
$x+y<x+z$
for all
$x$
.

If
$y <z$
then
$x+y<x+z$
for all
$x$
.

And try to deal with
$y=0; z=-1$
and
$0<-1$
and
$x = 1$
so
$1+0 < 1+(-1)$
.

....OR....

Proposition 1.18a). If
$x > 0$
then
$-x < 0$
. So if
$1> 0$
(Prop. 1.18d) then
$-1 < 0$
.

So now
$i^2 =-1 > 0$
is
a contradiction!

question:
Prove that no order can be defined in the complex field that turns it into an ordered field.

My question is what exactly does it mean to "define an order?". Here is my proof, which I think is incorrect: Suppose the complex field is an ordered field defined by the order by
$i>0$
. Since
$i^2=-1<0$
, this is impossible. If we instead define
$i<0$
, we have a similar contradiction. Finally, if we define
$i=0$
, then
$-1=i^2=0^2=0$
, another contradiction. Thus we cannot define an order on the complex field which makes it an ordered field.

I just don't know if the way I defined the order makes sense.
answer:
Your proof is fine basically fine, but yes "define an order" is awkward phrasing.

An ordered field is a field with a total ordering (that is compatible with the field operations), i.e. a field where for any elements
$a,b,$
we must have
$a = b$
,
$a < b$
, or
$a > b$
. With that in mind, here is how I would rewrite your proof.

Suppose the complex field is an ordered field. If
$i>0$
, then we find that
$i^2=-1<0$
, which is impossible. If
$i<0$
, we have a similar contradiction. Finally, if
$i=0$
, then
$-1=i^2=0^2=0$
, another contradiction.
So,
$i < 0$
,
$i > 0$
, and
$i = 0$
do not hold, contradicting our supposition that the complex field is ordered.

Suppose the complex field is an ordered field. If
$i>0$
, then we find that
$i^2=-1<0$
, which is impossible. If
$i<0$
, we have a similar contradiction. Finally, if
$i=0$
, then
$-1=i^2=0^2=0$
, another contradiction.

So,
$i < 0$
,
$i > 0$
, and
$i = 0$
do not hold, contradicting our supposition that the complex field is ordered.

question:
How to prove that the transport equation with smooth coefficient

$u_t + (a(x)u)_x = 0$

admits a unique smooth solution for smooth initial data?

More precisely, let

$u(x,0)=u_0(x),$

where (u_0\in C^1(\mathbb{R})), and assume

$a \in C^1(\mathbb{R}).$

When
$a(x)\equiv a$
is constant, existence and uniqueness follow from the standard method of characteristics argument:

$u(x,t)=u_0(x-at).$

My question is:

Can the method of characteristics be extended to the variable coefficient case?

How does one prove rigorously that the associated flow map is globally invertible?

Is there a clean proof that smooth initial data remains smooth for all time?

I understand formally that the characteristics solve

$\dot X(t)=a(X(t)),$

but I am looking for a careful argument showing: bijectivity/diffeomorphism property of the flow map,

A precise reference (PDE/ODE text, lecture notes, etc.) or a self-contained proof would be greatly appreciated.

I could not find this discussion in the standard conservation laws books, which mostly focus on nonlinear equations and weak solutions.

Thanks in advance.
answer:
$$u_t+\left(a(x)u \right)_x=0$$
$$u_t+a(x)u_x=-a'(x)u$$
Charpit-Lagrange characteristic ODEs :
$$\begin{cases}
\frac{dt}{1}=\frac{dx}{a(x)}=\frac{du}{-a'(x)u}\quad\text{ if }a(x)\: \text{not constant}\\
\frac{dt}{1}=\frac{dx}{a(x)}=\frac{du}{0}\quad\text{ if }a(x)=a=\text{constant}
\end{cases}$$
A first characteristic equation comes from solving
$\frac{dt}{1}=\frac{dx}{a(x)}$
$$\int \frac{dx}{a(x)}-t=c_1$$
A second characteristic equation comes from solving
$\begin{cases}
\frac{dx}{a(x)}=\frac{du}{-a'(x)u}\quad\text{ if }a(x)\: \text{not constant}\\
\frac{dx}{a(x)}=\frac{du}{0}\quad\text{ if }a(x)=a=\text{constant}
\end{cases}$
$$\begin{cases}
a(x)u=c_2\quad\text{ if }a(x)\: \text{not constant}\\
u=c'_2\quad\text{ if }a(x)=a=\text{constant}
\end{cases}$$
The general solution of the PDE on implicit form
$c_2=F(c_1)$
with arbitrary function
$F$
is :
$$\boxed{\begin{cases}
a(x)\:u(x,t)=F\left(\int \frac{dx}{a(x)}-t\right)\quad\text{ if }a(x)\: \text{not constant}\\
u(x,t)=F\left(\frac{x}{a}-t\right)\quad\text{ if }a(x)=a=\text{constant}
\end{cases}}$$
In the two equations the functions
$F$
are not the same since both are arbitrary. They depends on the initial condition.

Solution in case of initial condition
$u(x,0)=u_0(x)$
:
$$\begin{cases}
a(x)\:u_0(x)=F\left(\int \frac{dx}{a(x)}\right)\quad\text{ if }a(x)\: \text{not constant}\\
u_0(x)=F\left(\frac{x}{a}\right)\quad\text{ if }a(x)=a=\text{constant}
\end{cases}$$
One have to determine the function
$F$
in both cases.

Case
$a(x)=a=$
constant :

Let
$X=\frac{x}{a}$
then
$x=a\:X$

$u_0(aX)=F\left(X\right)$

The function
$F$
is determined. We put it into the above general solution
$u(x,t)=F\left(X\right)$
where
$X=\frac{x}{a}-t$

$u(x,t)=u_0\Big( a \left(\frac{x}{a}-t\right)\Big)$
$$\boxed{u(x,t)=u_0\left(x-at\right)}$$

Case
$a(x)$
not constant :

The above general solution is
$u(x,t)=\frac{1}{a(x)}F\left(\int \frac{dx}{a(x)}-t\right)$

Let
$X=\int \frac{dx}{a(x)}$

If it is possible to express the inverse function
$x=\Phi(X)$
one can write the general solution on the form:

$u(\Phi(X),t)=\frac{1}{a(\Phi(X))}F(X-t)$

At
$t=0$
:

$u_0(\Phi(X))=\frac{1}{a(\Phi(X))}F(X)$

$F(X)=a(\Phi(X))\:u_0(\Phi(X))$

The function
$F(X)$
is determined. We put it into the above general solution where
$X=\int \frac{dx}{a(x)}-t$
:

$u(x,t)=\frac{1}{a(x)}F\Big(\int \frac{dx}{a(x)}-t\Big)$

$$\boxed{u(x,t)=\frac{1}{a(x)}a\left(\Phi\left(\int \frac{dx}{a(x)}-t\right)\right)\:u_0\left(\Phi\left(\int \frac{dx}{a(x)}-t\right)\right)}$$

One cannot go further whitout knowing what kind of function
$a(x)$
is. One cannot say if the function
$\Phi$
is one-to-one or not and even if it can be expressed on closed form.

question:
Motivation: I want to classify all fields
$K$
which satisfy the property 1. (below). I also want to classify all fields
$K$
which satisfy the property 2. (below). Alternatively, I would be happy with showing that there are interesting fields satisfying 1. or 2. (apart from real closed fields, algebraically closed fields, and subfields of the algebraic closure of finite fields):

If
$\alpha$
is algebraic over
$K$
, then for every
$n \in \mathbb{N}_+$
there is
some
root of unity
$\zeta \in K(\alpha)$
and an element
$\beta \in K(\alpha)$
such that
$\zeta \alpha = \beta^n$
.

Stronger: For every finite field extension
$K \subseteq L$
, the quotient of
$L^\times$
by its maximal divisible subgroup is a torsion group.

My Galois theory is a bit rusty. Here are my thoughts: This feels somewhat related to the Artin-Schreier theorem:

If
$K \subseteq \overline{K}$
is a finite field extension, where
$\overline{K}$
is the algebraic closure of
$K$
, then either
$K = \overline{K}$
or
$K$
is real closed (in particular, not algebraically closed, of characteristic
$0$
and
$\overline{K} = K(\sqrt{-1})$
).

If it is possible to show the following:

Assume
$K \subseteq \overline{K}$
is generated by roots of unity (possibly infinitely many), then
$K$
is real closed, algebraically closed, or the subfield of the algebraic closure of a finite field.

Then in characteristic
$0$
, I think, it would follow that 1.
$\iff$
2 is precisely satisfied for the real closed fields and the algebraically closed fields using Problem 2(ii) in
https://www.math.uni.wroc.pl/~kkrup/stable_groups_list7.pdf
(which I'm also currently unable to solve):

If for all finite field extensions
$K \subseteq L$
and all
$n \in \mathbb{N}_+$
the map(s)
$X \mapsto X^n$
(and
$X \mapsto X^p -X$
in characteristic
$p$
) are surjective maps
$L^\times \to L^\times$
, then
$K$
is already algebraically closed.

I would appreciate anything related to 1., 2., 3. or 4.
answer:
I think I have a partial answer to 1 and full answer to 4:

Lemma 1
. Assume a field
$K$
has property 1., then so does every algebraic extension of it.

Proof
: Let
$K \subseteq L$
be algebraic,
$n \in \mathbb{N}_+$
, and
$\alpha$
algebraic over
$L$
. Then
$\alpha$
is algebraic over
$K$
and so,
$K(\alpha)$
contains
$\beta$
and a root of unity
$\zeta$
such that
$\zeta \alpha = \beta^n$
. But
$K(\alpha) \subset L(\alpha)$
.
$\qquad\square$

Lemma 2
. 4. holds.

Proof
: Take an extension
$K \subseteq E$
, we show
$K = E$
. By assumption the field is perfect
$K$
(every element has a
$p$
th root), so we assume
$E$
is Galois (by replacing it with its normal closure if necessary). But then
$\mathrm{Gal}(E/K)$
is finite so, if its non-trivial, it has a subgroup
$H$
of prime index. Then
$L = E^H \subsetneq L$
is Galois and of prime degree. So either an Artin--Schreier extension or a Kummer extension. This is impossible by the assumption on
$L$
. So,
$E = L$
and thus,
$K = E$
.
$\qquad \square$

Partial result 3
. Every algebraic element of
$K$
is obtained by iterated adjoining a root of unity and Kummer extensions.

Proof
: Take the field
$L$
of all elements which can be obtained from
$K$
by adjoining roots of unity and Kummer extensions. Then the maps
$L^\times \to L^\times$
with
$X \mapsto X^n$
(
$n \in \mathbb{N}_+$
) or
$X \mapsto X^p - X$
(in characteristic
$p$
) are surjective. For the first map this follows from Lemma 1, assumption on
$K$
and the fact that
$L$
contains all roots of unity. For the second map this follows because every element
$l \in L$
is hit by some element
$\rho$
in the algebraic closure of
$L$
and as
$l$
lies in some subextension obtained by adjoining finitely many roots of unity and Kummer extensions, so is
$\rho$
. It follows from Lemma 2 that
$L$
is algebraically closed.
$\qquad \square$

question:
Let
$\mathbb{H}_{\mathbb{Q}}$
denote the division algebra of rational quaternions, and let
$\mathcal{H}$
be the
Hurwitz order
:

$$\mathcal{H} = \left\{ a + bi + cj + dk \;\middle|\; a, b, c, d \in \mathbb{Z} \text{ or } a, b, c, d \in \mathbb{Z} + \frac{1}{2} \right\}$$


I am trying to prove




Every integral element
$\alpha \in \mathbb{H}_{\mathbb{Q}}$
lies in a conjugate of
$\mathcal{H}$
. That is, there exists some
$\epsilon \in \mathbb{H}_{\mathbb{Q}}^{\times}$
such that
$\alpha \in \epsilon \mathcal{H} \epsilon^{-1}$
.




If
$\alpha$
has a minimal polynomial
$X^2 - tX + n$
over
$\mathbb{Z}$
, this embeds the quadratic integer ring
$\mathbb{Z}[\alpha]$
into
$\mathbb{H}_{\mathbb{Q}}$
. The claim reduces to the fact that any embedding of a quadratic order into
$\mathbb{H}_{\mathbb{Q}}$
can be conjugated into the maximal order
$\mathcal{H}$
.


For example, the element
$\frac{1}{3}i + \frac{2}{3}j + \frac{2}{3}k\notin\cal H$
has minimal polynomial
$X^2+1$
, so it is integral over
$\Bbb Z$
. If we pick
$a = 2i + j + k \in \mathbb{H}_{\mathbb{Q}}$
. Conjugating
$i\in\cal H$
by
$a$
yields:

WolframAlpha


$$a \cdot i \cdot a^{-1} = \frac{1}{3}i + \frac{2}{3}j + \frac{2}{3}k$$

To see that there are infinitely many such integral elements outside of
$\mathcal{H}$
that each require distinct conjugations, we can generalize the
$X^2 + 1 = 0$
example.
For each
$n \in \mathbb{N}$
, we define the pure quaternion:
$$q(n) = \frac{1}{2n^2+1}i + \frac{2n}{2n^2+1}j + \frac{2n^2}{2n^2+1}k$$
Because of the algebraic identity:
$$1^2 + (2n)^2 + (2n^2)^2 = (2n^2+1)^2$$
each
$q(n)$
has a reduced norm of exactly
$1$
. Since they are pure quaternions, their trace is
$0$
, meaning they all satisfy the minimal polynomial
$X^2 + 1 = 0$
and are therefore integral over
$\mathbb{Z}$
.


However, for all
$n \ge 1$
:
$q(n)$
is not a Hurwitz quaternion because its
$i$
-coordinate,
$\frac{1}{2n^2+1}$
, is neither an integer nor a half-integer.


To generalize the element
$a = 2i + j + k$
to all
$n \ge 1$
such that conjugating
$i$
by
$a(n)$
yields
$q(n)$
, we can solve the quaternion equation:
$$a(n) \cdot i \cdot a(n)^{-1} = q(n) \implies a(n) \cdot i = q(n) \cdot a(n)$$
By finding the nullspace of this linear system, we obtain:
$$a(n) = (n^2 + 1)i + nj + n^2 k$$
We show that
$\{q(n):n\ge1\}$
cannot all live in the same few conjugates of
$\mathcal{H}$
. Let
$\mathcal{O}(n) = a(n)\mathcal{H}a(n)^{-1}$
be the maximal order containing
$q(n)$
, where
$q(n) = a(n)ia(n)^{-1}$
. We will prove that if
$\mathcal{O}(n) = \mathcal{O}(m)$
for
$n, m \ge 1$
, then
$n = m$
.


Suppose
$q(n) \in \mathcal{O}(m)$
. By definition, this means
$a(m)^{-1} q(n) a(m) \in \mathcal{H}$
.


Because conjugation preserves both the trace and the reduced norm,
$a(m)^{-1} q(n) a(m)$
must be a pure Hurwitz quaternion of norm
$1$
. The only such elements in
$\mathcal{H}$
are
$\pm i, \pm j, \pm k$
.




Case 1 (
$\pm i$
):
If
$a(m)^{-1} q(n) a(m) = \pm i$
, then conjugating both sides by
$a(m)$
gives
$q(n) = \pm q(m)$
. Since the
$i$
-coefficient of
$q(x)$
is
$\frac{1}{2x^2+1} > 0$
for all
$x$
, the minus sign is impossible. Thus
$q(n) = q(m)$
, which implies
$n = m$
by the injectivity of
$q$
.


Case 2 (
$\pm j, \pm k$
):
Notice that
$i$
anticommutes with both
$j$
and
$k$
(i.e.,
$ij + ji = 0$
and
$ik + ki = 0$
). Since quaternion conjugation is a ring automorphism:




$$(a \cdot x \cdot a^{-1})(a \cdot y \cdot a^{-1}) + (a \cdot y \cdot a^{-1})(a \cdot x \cdot a^{-1}) = a(xy + yx)a^{-1}$$


conjugation preserves this anticommutativity relation. If
$a(m)^{-1} q(n) a(m) = \pm j$
or
$\pm k$
, then
$q(n)$
must anticommute with
$q(m) = a(m)ia(m)^{-1}$
.


However, for any two pure quaternions, anticommutativity requires their scalar dot product to be zero. A direct calculation of the dot product of
$q(n)$
and
$q(m)$
yields:


$$q(n) \cdot q(m) = \frac{1 + 4nm + 4n^2m^2}{(2n^2+1)(2m^2+1)} = \frac{(1+2nm)^2}{(2n^2+1)(2m^2+1)}$$


For
$n, m \ge 1$
, this dot product is strictly positive, meaning
$q(n)$
and
$q(m)$

never
anticommute. This rules out the
$\pm j$
and
$\pm k$
cases entirely.
Thus,
$q(n) \in \mathcal{O}(m) \implies n = m$
. It follows that the map
$n \mapsto \mathcal{O}(n)$
is injective, yielding infinitely many distinct maximal orders of integral rational quaternions.

Theorem 1: Conjugation of Pure Quaternions

Let
$v, w \in \mathbb{H}(\mathbb{Q})$
be two pure quaternions with the same reduced norm, i.e.,
$N(v) = N(w)$
. Then there exists a nonzero quaternion
$u \in \mathbb{H}(\mathbb{Q})^\times$
such that
$u v u^{-1} = w$
, or equivalently:
$$uv = wu$$

Proof:
We proceed by cases based on the relationship between
$v$
and
$w$
.

Case 1 (
$w \neq -v$
):
Let
$u = v + w$
. Since
$w \neq -v$
,
$u \neq 0$
. For any pure quaternions, the identity
$x^2 = -N(x)$
holds. Expanding the products:
$$uv = (v + w)v = v^2 + wv = -N(v) + wv$$
$$wu = w(v + w) = wv + w^2 = wv - N(w)$$
Since
$N(v) = N(w)$
, we have
$-N(v) = -N(w)$
, which immediately implies
$uv = wu$
.
Case 2 (
$w = -v$
):
Since
$v \neq 0$
, we seek a nonzero pure quaternion
$u$
that anticommutes with
$v$
, which satisfies
$uv = -vu = wv$
. For pure quaternions, anticommutativity is equivalent to orthogonality under the standard dot product (
$\langle v, u \rangle = 0$
). Letting
$v = v_i i + v_j j + v_k k$
, we construct a nonzero orthogonal
$u$
directly:
If
$v_i = v_j = 0$
:
Then
$v = v_k k$
(
$v_k \neq 0$
). Choose
$u = i$
.
Otherwise:
Choose
$u = -v_j i + v_i j$
.
In both cases,
$\langle v, u \rangle = 0$
, which yields
$uv + vu = 0 \implies uv = -vu = wv$
.
$\blacksquare$

If
$v_i = v_j = 0$
:
Then
$v = v_k k$
(
$v_k \neq 0$
). Choose
$u = i$
.
Otherwise:
Choose
$u = -v_j i + v_i j$
.
In both cases,
$\langle v, u \rangle = 0$
, which yields
$uv + vu = 0 \implies uv = -vu = wv$
.
$\blacksquare$

Main Theorem: Embedding Integral Quaternions into the Hurwitz Order

Let
$q \in \mathbb{H}(\mathbb{Q})$
be an integral quaternion, meaning its reduced trace
$\text{Tr}(q) = 2a$
and reduced norm
$N(q) = a^2 + N(v)$
are integers, where
$q = a + v$
and
$v$
is a pure quaternion. We show there exists
$\epsilon \in \mathbb{H}(\mathbb{Q})^\times$
such that
$\epsilon^{-1} q \epsilon \in \mathcal{H}$
.

Since conjugation preserves the scalar part
$a$
, it suffices to show that the pure part
$v$
can be conjugated into an element
$w$
such that
$a + w \in \mathcal{H}$
. We split this into three distinct cases based on the parity of the trace:

1. Central Case (
$v = 0$
)

If
$v = 0$
, then
$q = a$
. Because
$\text{Tr}(q) = 2a \in \mathbb{Z}$
and
$N(q) = a^2 \in \mathbb{Z}$
,
$a$
must be an integer (
$a \in \mathbb{Z}$
). Thus,
$q \in \mathbb{Z} \subset \mathcal{H}$
, and any choice of
$\epsilon$
works.

2. Even Trace Case (
$2a \in 2\mathbb{Z}$
)

If the trace is even, then
$a \in \mathbb{Z}$
. Since
$N(q) = a^2 + N(v) \in \mathbb{Z}$
, it follows that
$N(v) \in \mathbb{Z}$
.

By definition,
$v = v_1 i + v_2 j + v_3 k$
for
$v_m \in \mathbb{Q}$
, meaning
$N(v) = v_1^2 + v_2^2 + v_3^2$
is a sum of three rational squares. Thus, by
Cassels' Lemma
,
$N(v)$
can be written as a sum of three
integer
squares:
$$x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = N(v) \quad (x, y, z \in \mathbb{Z})$$
As detailed in
Keith Conrad's expository paper on Sums of Squares in
$\mathbb{Q}$
and
$F(T)$
, Cassels' lemma is proved by strong induction on the denominator
$D$
, where
$A^2 + B^2 + C^2 = nD^2$
. Given such a representation with
$D > 1$
, we construct centered remainders
$A_0 \equiv A$
,
$B_0 \equiv B$
,
$C_0 \equiv C \pmod D$
with
$|A_0|, |B_0|, |C_0| \le D/2$
, then perform a geometric reflection in
$\mathbb{Q}^3$
to produce a new representation
$A'^2 + B'^2 + C'^2 = nQ^2$
where
$Q < D$
.

Define the pure integral quaternion
$w = xi + yj + zk \in \mathcal{H}$
. By construction,
$N(w) = N(v)$
. By
Theorem 1
, there exists an
$\epsilon \in \mathbb{H}(\mathbb{Q})^\times$
such that
$\epsilon^{-1} v \epsilon = w$
. Therefore:
$$\epsilon^{-1} q \epsilon = a + w \in \mathcal{H}$$

3. Odd Trace Case (
$2a \in 2\mathbb{Z} + 1$
)

If the trace is odd, then
$a \in \mathbb{Z} + \frac{1}{2}$
. We can write
$a = k + \frac{1}{2}$
for some
$k \in \mathbb{Z}$
, which means:
$$a^2 = k^2 + k + \frac{1}{4} = k(k+1) + \frac{1}{4}$$

Since the product of two consecutive integers
$k(k+1)$
is always even, let
$k(k+1) = 2m$
. Then
$a^2 = 2m + \frac{1}{4}$
. Substituting this into our integer norm equation gives:
$$N(v) = N(q) - a^2 = N(q) - 2m - \frac{1}{4}$$

Let
$M = 4N(v)$
. Clearing denominators yields:
$$M = 4N(q) - 8m - 1 = 4(N(q) - 2m) - 1 \implies M \equiv 3 \pmod 4$$

Since
$v$
is a pure rational quaternion,
$M = 4N(v) = (2v_1)^2 + (2v_2)^2 + (2v_3)^2$
is an integer that is a sum of three rational squares. Thus, by
Cassels' Lemma
,
$M$
can be written as a sum of three
integer
squares:
$$x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = M \quad (x, y, z \in \mathbb{Z})$$

Because
$M \equiv 3 \pmod 4$
, looking at perfect squares modulo 4 (
$0^2, 2^2 \equiv 0$
and
$1^2, 3^2 \equiv 1$
), the only way for three squares to sum to
$3 \pmod 4$
is if
$x^2 \equiv y^2 \equiv z^2 \equiv 1 \pmod 4$
. Thus,
$x, y, z$
are all
odd integers
.

Now, define the pure quaternion:
$$w = \frac{x}{2}i + \frac{y}{2}j + \frac{z}{2}k$$

Since
$x, y, z$
are odd, the coordinates of
$w$
are strictly half-integers. Furthermore,
$N(w) = \frac{x^2+y^2+z^2}{4} = \frac{M}{4} = N(v)$
. By
Theorem 1
, there exists
$\epsilon \in \mathbb{H}(\mathbb{Q})^\times$
such that
$\epsilon^{-1} v \epsilon = w$
.

Since
$a \in \mathbb{Z} + \frac{1}{2}$
and the components of
$w$
are half-integers, all four components of
$\epsilon^{-1} q \epsilon = a + w$
are half-integers. Thus,
$\epsilon^{-1} q \epsilon \in \mathcal{H}$
.
$\blacksquare$

from collections import Counter
from fractions import Fraction
import itertools
import math

def get_prime_factors(n):
factors = {}
if n < 0:
n = -n
d = 2
while d * d <= n:
while (n % d) == 0:
factors[d] = factors.get(d, 0) + 1
n //= d
d += 1
if n > 1:
factors[n] = factors.get(n, 0) + 1
return factors

def get_fraction_powers(frac):
num_factors = get_prime_factors(frac.numerator)
den_factors = get_prime_factors(frac.denominator)

all_primes = set(num_factors.keys()).union(set(den_factors.keys()))
return {p: num_factors.get(p, 0) - den_factors.get(p, 0) for p in all_primes}

def find_solutions(max_num=5, max_den=3):
fraction_pool = set()
for num in range(1, max_num + 1):
for den in range(1, max_den + 1):
fraction_pool.add(Fraction(num, den))

fraction_list = sorted(list(fraction_pool))

print(f"Search {len(fraction_list)} integer solutions...")
solutions_count = 0

precomputed_factors = {frac: get_fraction_powers(frac) for frac in fraction_list}

for a, b, c, d, f in itertools.product(fraction_list, repeat=5):
k = 1 - a - b - c + d + f

if k == 0:
continue

counts = Counter([a, b, c, d, f, k])
if any(v > 3 for v in counts.values()):
continue

g_k_powers = {}

all_primes = set()
for x in (a, b, c, d, f):
all_primes.update(precomputed_factors[x].keys())

possible_g = True
for p in all_primes:
power_p = (
a * precomputed_factors[a].get(p, 0)
+ b * precomputed_factors[b].get(p, 0)
+ c * precomputed_factors[c].get(p, 0)
- d * precomputed_factors[d].get(p, 0)
- f * precomputed_factors[f].get(p, 0)
)

g_power_p = power_p / k

if g_power_p.denominator != 1:
possible_g = False
break

g_k_powers[p] = int(g_power_p)

if not possible_g:
continue

valid_solution = True
for var in (a, b, c, d, f):
var_factors = precomputed_factors[var]
for p in set(g_k_powers.keys()).union(set(var_factors.keys())):
total_power = g_k_powers.get(p, 0) + var_factors.get(p, 0)
if total_power < 0:
valid_solution = False
break
if not valid_solution:
break

if valid_solution:
solutions_count += 1

g_val = Fraction(1, 1)
for p, pwr in g_k_powers.items():
if pwr >= 0:
g_val *= p**pwr
else:
g_val /= p**-pwr

print(f"Solution #{solutions_count}:")
print(f" a={a}, b={b}, c={c}, d={d}, f={f} -> k={k}")
print(f" g = {g_val}")
print(
f" ga={int(g_val*a)}, gb={int(g_val*b)}, gc={int(g_val*c)}, gd={int(g_val*d)}, gf={int(g_val*f)}"
)
print("-" * 40)

if __name__ == "__main__":
#Search settings: numerator up to 6, denominator up to 2.
find_solutions(max_num=6, max_den=3)
```

question:
Evaluate this definite integral
$$I=\int_{0}^{2}\frac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{x}-1\right)}{\sqrt{2x-x^2}}dx$$




My attempt:


I used the substitution
$2x-x^2=t^2$
,


$1-(1-x)^2=t^2\implies x=1-\sqrt{1-t^2}\ (0<x<1)$


$1-(x-1)^2=t^2\implies x=1+\sqrt{1-t^2}\ (1<x<2)$


$\frac{d}{dx}(2x-x^2)=\frac{d}{dx}t$


$2(1-x)dx=dt$


$$I=\int_{0}^{2}\frac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{x}-1\right)}{\sqrt{2x-x^2}}dx$$

splitting the limit,

$$I=\int_{0}^{1}\frac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{x}-1\right)}{\sqrt{2x-x^2}}dx+\int_{1}^{2}\frac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{x}-1\right)}{\sqrt{2x-x^2}}dx$$


$$I=\int_{0}^{1}\frac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{1-\sqrt{1-t^2}}-1\right)}{t}\frac{dt}{2\sqrt{1-t^2}}+\int_{1}^{0}\frac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{1+\sqrt{1-t^2}}-1\right)}{t}\frac{dt}{2\sqrt{1-t^2}}$$


$$I=\int_{0}^{1}\frac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\sqrt{1-t^2}}{1-\sqrt{1-t^2}}\right)}{2t\sqrt{1-t^2}}dt-\int_{0}^{1}\frac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1-\sqrt{1-t^2}}{1+\sqrt{1-t^2}}\right)}{2t\sqrt{1-t^2}}dt$$


$$I=\int_{0}^{1}\frac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\sqrt{1-t^2}}{1-\sqrt{1-t^2}}\right)-\cot^{-1}\left(\frac{1+\sqrt{1-t^2}}{1-\sqrt{1-t^2}}\right)}{2t\sqrt{1-t^2}}dt$$

I got stuck here, and looks to me that the substitution does not work. Please help me solve this.
answer:
Recall the property of definite integral:
$\int_a^bf(x)\,dx=\int_a^bf(a+b-x)\,dx$
$$I=\int_0^2\dfrac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{x}-1\right)}{\sqrt{2x-x^2}}\, dx=\int_0^2\dfrac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2-x}{x}\right)}{\sqrt{2x-x^2}}\, dx\tag 1$$
$$I=\int_0^2\dfrac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2-(2-x)}{2-x}\right)}{\sqrt{2(2-x)-(2-x)^2}}\, dx$$
$$I=\int_0^2\dfrac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{x}{2-x}\right)}{\sqrt{2x-x^2}}\, dx=\int_0^2\dfrac{\cot^{-1}\left(\frac{2-x}{x}\right)}{\sqrt{2x-x^2}}\, dx\tag 2$$

Adding (1) and (2),
$$I+I=\int_0^2\dfrac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2-x}{x}\right)+\cot^{-1}\left(\frac{2-x}{x}\right)}{\sqrt{2x-x^2}}\, dx$$
$$2I=\int_0^2\dfrac{\frac{\pi}{2}}{\sqrt{2x-x^2}}\, dx$$
$$I=\dfrac {\pi}{4}\int_0^2\dfrac{1}{\sqrt{1-(x-1)^2}}\, dx$$
$$=\dfrac {\pi}{4}\left[\sin^{-1}\left(x-1\right)\right]_0^2$$
$$=\dfrac{\pi}{4}\left(\dfrac{\pi}{2}+\dfrac{\pi}{2}\right)$$
$$=\dfrac{\pi^2}{4}$$

answer:
\begin{align}
I&=\int_{0}^{2}\frac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{x}-1\right)}{\sqrt{2x-x^2}}\ dx\\
&=\int_{0}^{2}\frac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{x}-1\right)}{\sqrt{1-(x-1)^2}}\ dx\\
&\overset{x-1=\sin t}=\int_{-\frac\pi2}^{\frac\pi2}\tan^{-1}\bigg(\frac{1-\sin t}{1+\sin t}\bigg)dt.\tag1\\
&\overset{t\to-t}=\int_{-\frac\pi2}^{\frac\pi2}\tan^{-1}\bigg(\frac{1+\sin t}{1-\sin t}\bigg)dt.\tag2\\
\end{align}
So (1)+(2) gives
$$ 2I=\int_{-\frac\pi2}^{\frac\pi2}\bigg[\tan^{-1}\bigg(\frac{1-\sin t}{1+\sin t}\bigg)+\tan^{-1}\bigg(\frac{1+\sin t}{1-\sin t}\bigg)\bigg]dt=\int_{-\frac\pi2}^{\frac\pi2}\frac\pi2dt=\frac{\pi^2}{2} $$
and hence
$$ I=\frac{\pi^2}{4}. $$

answer:
Via two substitutions, we have
$$
I\stackrel{x\mapsto 1-x}{=}  \int_{-1}^1 \frac{\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1-x}{1+x}\right)}{\sqrt{(1+x)(1-x)}} d y \stackrel{x\mapsto -x}{=} \int_{-1}^1 \frac{\tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{1+x}{1-x}\right)}{\sqrt{(1-x)(1+x)}} d x
$$
Averaging them with the identity
$\tan y+\tan\left(\frac 1y \right)=\frac \pi 2$
gives
$$
\begin{aligned}
I & =\frac{1}{2} \int_{-1}^1 \frac{\frac{\pi}{2}}{\sqrt{1-x^2}} d x \\
& =\frac{\pi}{4}\left[\sin ^{-1} x\right]_{-1}^1 \\
& =\frac{\pi^2}{4}
\end{aligned}
$$

I got stuck here, and looks to me that the substitution does not work. Please help me solve this.
answer:
Since

$$\frac{2}{x}-1=\frac{2-x}{x},$$

we may write

$$I=\int_{0}^{2}\frac{\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2-x}{x}\right)}{\sqrt{x(2-x)}}\,dx .$$

Now replace
$x$
by
$2-x$
. The denominator does not change, so

$$I=\int_{0}^{2}\frac{\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{x}{2-x}\right)}{\sqrt{x(2-x)}}\,dx .$$

Adding these two equal expressions gives

$$2I=\int_{0}^{2}\frac{\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{2-x}{x}\right)+\tan^{-1}\!\left(\frac{x}{2-x}\right)}{\sqrt{x(2-x)}}\,dx .$$

For
$0<x<2$
, both fractions are positive reciprocals. Hence

$$\tan^{-1} u+\tan^{-1}\frac1u=\frac{\pi}{2}, \qquad u>0.$$

Therefore

$$2I=\frac{\pi}{2}\int_{0}^{2}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{x(2-x)}}.$$

Finally, put
$x=1-\cos\theta$
. Then

$$dx=\sin\theta\,d\theta, \qquad \sqrt{x(2-x)}=\sin\theta,$$

and the remaining integral becomes

$$\int_{0}^{2}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{x(2-x)}}=\int_{0}^{\pi}d\theta=\pi.$$

Thus

$$2I=\frac{\pi^{2}}{2},$$

so

$$\boxed{I=\frac{\pi^{2}}{4}}.$$

answer:
$$\begin{align*}
I &= \int_0^2 \frac{\arctan\left(\frac2x-1\right)}{\sqrt x\sqrt{2-x}} \, dx \\
&= \int_0^2 \int_0^\tfrac{2-x}x \frac1{\sqrt x\sqrt{2-x}} \cdot\frac1{1+y^2} \, dy \,dx \\
&= \int_0^\infty \int_0^\tfrac2{1+y} \frac1{\sqrt x\sqrt{2-x}} \cdot\frac1{1+y^2} \, dx \, dy \\
&= \int_0^\infty \int_0^\tfrac1{\sqrt y} \frac2{1+z^2}\cdot\frac1{1+y^2} \, dz \, dy & \sqrt x = \frac{\sqrt2\,z}{\sqrt{1+z^2}} \\
&= \int_0^\infty \int_0^1 \frac{2\sqrt y}{y+z^2} \cdot \frac1{1+y^2} \, dz \, dy & z\to\frac z{\sqrt y} \\
&= \int_0^\infty \int_0^1 \frac{2y}{y^2+z^2} \cdot \frac1{1+y^2} \, dz \, dy & y\to y^2 \\
I' &= \int_0^\infty \int_1^\infty \frac{2y}{z^2+y^2} \cdot \frac1{1+y^2} \, dz \, dy & (y,z)\to\left(\frac1y,\frac1z\right) \\
I = \frac{I+I'}2 &= \int_0^\infty \int_0^\infty \frac y{y^2+z^2}\cdot\frac1{1+y^2} \, dz \, dy \\
&= \int_0^\infty \int_0^\infty \frac1{1+z^2}\cdot\frac1{1+y^2} \, dz \, dy & z\to yz \\
&= \left(\int_0^\infty \frac{dy}{1+y^2}\right)^2 = \boxed{\frac{\pi^2}4}
\end{align*}$$

answer:
For the definite integral
$$I=\int_{0}^{2}\frac{\tan^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{x}-1\right)}{\sqrt{2x-x^2}}\,dx$$
$$x=\frac{2}{t+1} \quad \implies \quad I=\int_0^\infty\frac{\tan ^{-1}(t)}{(t+1)\sqrt{t} } \,dt$$

Using Feynman's trick
$$J(a)=\int_0^\infty\frac{\tan ^{-1}(at)}{(t+1)\sqrt{t} } \,dt$$
$$J'(a)=\int_0^\infty\frac{\sqrt{t}}{(t+1) \left(a^2 t^2+1\right)}\,dt$$

Using
$$\frac{1}{(t+1) \left(a^2 t^2+1\right)}=\frac{1}{\left(a^2+1\right) (t+1)}+\frac {ia}2 \Bigg(\frac{1}{(a-i) (a t+i)} -\frac{1}{(a+i) (a t-i)} \Bigg)$$
this makes simple antiderivatives and integrals. After simplifications
$$J'(a)=\Bigg(\frac{\sqrt{a}}{\sqrt{2} \left(a^2+1\right)}-\frac{1}{a^2+1}+\frac{1}{\sqrt{2} \left(a^2+1\right) \sqrt{a}}  \Bigg)\,\pi$$
$$I=\int_0^1 J'(a)\,da=\frac{\pi}{2} \left(-\coth ^{-1}\left(\sqrt{2}\right)+(1-i) \left(\tan
^{-1}\left(\sqrt[4]{-1}\right)+\tanh ^{-1}\left(\sqrt[4]{-1}\right)\right)\right)$$
Using the logarithmic represention of the arc tangent and hyperbolic arc tangent functions,
$$I=\frac {\pi^2}4$$

question:
Let
$H$
be a complex Hilbert space and
$A:D(A)\subset H\to H$
a skew-adjoint operator (densely defined and closed) that is
$A=-A^*$
. We can immediately see that
$A$
is a normal operator that is
$A A^* =A^*A$
. Recall that
$A$
is skew-adjoint if and only
if
$iA$
is self-adjoint
$($
see
$[3$
, Lemma
$1.6.2])$
.

Denote by
$\rho(A)$
the resolvent set of
$A$
and
$\sigma(A)$
by the spectrum of
$A$
. See
here
for more details.

By Stone's theorem
$([3$
, Theorem
$3.9.1])$
,
$A$
generates a
$C_0$
-unitary group
$\{U(t): H \to H \; ; \; t\in \mathbb{R}\}$
. Consider the
$C_0$
-semigroup of contractions
$\{T(t):=U(t): H \to H \; ; \; t\geq 0\}$
.

How to prove that
$\{T(t): H \to H \; ; \; t\geq 0\}$
is an analytic semigroup?

I am looking for a proof via resolvent estimates (sectorial-type estimates), or a clear reference to a theorem that directly covers this classical situation. For instance, since
$A$
is normal, by
$[1$
,
$(3.31)$
, page
$277]$
, we have

\begin{equation}\label{resolventnorm}
\|R(\lambda;A)\|_{\mathcal{B}(H)}= \frac{1}{\operatorname{dist}(\lambda, \sigma(A))}, \qquad \lambda \in \rho(A)
\end{equation}

where
$R(\lambda;A)=(A-\lambda I)^{-1}$
for
$\lambda \in \rho(A)$
, then I tried to use
$[2$
, Theorem
$5.2]$
by assuming that
$\sigma(A)\subset i\mathbb{R}$
,
$(0, \infty) \subset \rho(A)$
and
$0 \in \rho(A)$
. Also, there are classic results, such as in
$[3$
, Corollary
$7.1.1. ]$
, which gives sufficient conditions for a
self-adjoint
infinitesimal generator to generate an analytic
$C_0$
-semigroup. However, here
$A$
é
skew-adjoint
.

The restriction of a unitary
$C_0$
-group generated by a skew-adjoint operator to
$t\geq 0$
is analytic? If it's true, I would like to understand the details of the argument.

Any indications of specific sections, theorems, or exercise numbers in classical references would be extremely helpful.

$[1]$
Kato T., Perturbation theory for linear operators, Springer-Verlag, Berlin, 1995.

$[2]$
Pazy, A., Semigroups of Linear Operators and Applications to Partial Differential Equations. New York: Springer-Verlag New York, Inc.,
$1983$
.

$[3]$
Vrabie, I.,
$C_0$
-Semigroups and Applications, Elsevier, Amsterdam,
$2003$
.
answer:
Are you sure, that this result is true? I think that a generator of an analytic Semigroup has to be (after subtraction of a positive constant) a sectorial Operator, e.g.

the sector
$\{z\in\mathbb{C}: arg(z)\in(-\delta-\frac{π}{2},\delta+\frac{π}{2})\}\setminus\varnothing$
for some
$\delta>0$
has to be contained in the resolvent Set of
$A$
.
(And on each smaller sector the resolvent has to be bounded in some sense)

However this semms to be impossible for any (unbounded) Skew-Adjoined Operator since there we have
$\sigma(A)\subseteq i\mathbb{R}$

My reference for this is One-Parameter Semigroups for Linear Evolution Equations by Engel and Nagel (2000) [Chapter II.4]

question:
See that
$(n, m) = (1, 1)$
is a valid positive integer solution to the Diophantine equation:

$$2^n + 1 = 3m^3$$


I am interested in proving that this is the
only
solution for
$n, m \in \mathbb{Z}^+$
. This equation is related to
Catalan's conjecture and Pillai's conjecture
. I have run a brute-force script to check for small solutions.


If we split the exponent
$n$
into its residue classes modulo
$3$
(writing
$n = 3\mu + j$
for
$j \in \{0, 1, 2\}$
), the problem naturally transforms into finding points on:




For
$n \equiv 0 \pmod 3 \implies 3X^3 - Y^3 = 1$


For
$n \equiv 1 \pmod 3 \implies 3X^3 - 2Y^3 = 1$


For
$n \equiv 2 \pmod 3 \implies 3X^3 - 4Y^3 = 1$




Where
$X = m$
and
$Y = 2^\mu$
.


Questions




Is there an obstruction for any of the branches?


Is there a way to establish that
$(1,1)$
is unique?
answer:
$2^n + 1 = 3m^3$

Suppose we had a solution for
$n \geqslant 3$
. We have
$3m^3 \equiv 1 \pmod{8}$
, or
$m \equiv 3 \pmod{8}$
. As mentioned by others, taking the equation mod
$3$
gives
$n$
odd.

Adding
$3$
to both sides,
$$
\begin{align*}
2^n + 4 &= 3 \left( m^3 + 1 \right) \\
2 \left( 2^{n-1} + 2 \right) &= 3 (m + 1) \left( m^2 - m + 1 \right)
\end{align*}
$$
Since
$m^2 - m + 1 \equiv 1 - 3 + 1 \equiv 7 \pmod{8}$
, there exists a prime factor
$p \equiv 5 \text{ or } 7 \pmod{8}$
. (if all the primes dividing
$m^2 - m + 1$
were
$1 \text{ or } 3 \pmod{8}$
then the product would be
$1 \text{ or } 3 \pmod{8}$
, a contradiction.)

Choose such
$p$
. Then letting
$n = 2k + 1$
,
$$
\begin{align*}
2^{n-1} + 2 &\equiv 0 &\pmod{p} \\
2^{2k} &\equiv -2 &\pmod{p} \\
\left( 2^k \right)^2 &\equiv -2 &\pmod{p}
\end{align*}
$$

But for
$p$
an odd prime,
$-2$
is a
quadratic residue
mod
$p$
if and only if
$p \equiv 1 \text{ or } 3 \pmod{8}$
, a contradiction.

question:
See that
$(n, m) = (1, 1)$
is a valid positive integer solution to the Diophantine equation:

$$2^n + 1 = 3m^3$$


I am interested in proving that this is the
only
solution for

. This equation is related to
Catalan's conjecture and Pillai's conjecture
. I have run a brute-force script to check for small solutions.


If we split the exponent
$n$

for

For
$n \equiv 0 \pmod 3 \implies 3X^3 - Y^3 = 1$


For

For

Where

Questions




Is there an obstruction for any of the branches?


Is there a way to establish that
$(1,1)$
is unique?
answer:
Let
$m$
and
$n$
be positive integers such that
$$2^n+1=3m^3.$$
Clearly
$m$
is odd, and reducing mod
$3$
shows that
$n$
is odd, say
$n=2k+1$
for some nonnegative integer
$k$
. Then in
$\Bbb{Z}[\sqrt{-2}]$
we have
$$3m^3=(1+2^k\sqrt{-2})(1-2^k\sqrt{-2}),$$
where the two factors on the right hand side are coprime, because their sum is
$2$
and their product is odd. Because
$\Bbb{Z}[\sqrt{-2}]$
is a unique factorization domain in which
$$3=(1+\sqrt{-2})(1-\sqrt{-2}),$$
and both units in
$\Bbb{Z}[\sqrt{-2}]$
are cubes, without loss of generality
$$1+2^k\sqrt{-2}=(1+\sqrt{-2})(a+b\sqrt{-2})^3,$$
for some coprime integers
$a$
and
$b$
. Expanding the right hand side yields
\begin{eqnarray}
1&=&a^3-6ba^2-6b^2a+4b^3\\
2^k&=&a^3+3ba^2-6b^2a-2b^3.
\end{eqnarray}
These are two Thue equations, for which effective methods exist to find all finitely many integral solutions. With the help of PARI/GP we find that
$(a,b)=(1,0)$
is the only integral solution to the first equation. And this corresponds precisely to the solution
$(m,n)=(1,1)$
to the original equation.

answer:
As mentioned in now deleted answer, evaluating equation mod
$7$
gives you that
$n\equiv 1\pmod 3$
(your second case), so you need to focus only on that one. You have already transformed that to
$$3X^3 - 2Y^3 = 1.$$

This is a
Thue equation
, and by Thue's theorem it has only finitely many solutions. In fact, those solutions can be effectively bounded and there is algorithm implemented in most CAS systems that will find all solutions, some of them are listed in the linked article. For example in PARI/GP:

thue(thueinit(3*X^3-2),1)

You will get
$X=1,Y=1$
as the only solution, which after substitution back corresponds to
$n=1,m=1$
.

The algorithm itself was described in
On the practical solution of the Thue equation
.

question:
See that
$(n, m) = (1, 1)$
is a valid positive integer solution to the Diophantine equation:

$$2^n + 1 = 3m^3$$


I am interested in proving that this is the
only
solution for
$n, m \in \mathbb{Z}^+$
. This equation is related to
Catalan's conjecture and Pillai's conjecture
. I have run a brute-force script to check for small solutions.


If we split the exponent
$n$
into its residue classes modulo
$3$
(writing
$n = 3\mu + j$
for
$j \in \{0, 1, 2\}$
), the problem naturally transforms into finding points on:




For
$n \equiv 0 \pmod 3 \implies 3X^3 - Y^3 = 1$


For
$n \equiv 1 \pmod 3 \implies 3X^3 - 2Y^3 = 1$


For
$n \equiv 2 \pmod 3 \implies 3X^3 - 4Y^3 = 1$




Where
$X = m$
and
$Y = 2^\mu$
.


Questions




Is there an obstruction for any of the branches?


Is there a way to establish that
$(1,1)$
is unique?

See that
$(n, m) = (1, 1)$
is a valid positive integer solution to the Diophantine equation:

$$2^n + 1 = 3m^3$$


I am interested in proving that this is the
only
solution for
$n, m \in \mathbb{Z}^+$
. This equation is related to
Catalan's conjecture and Pillai's conjecture
. I have run a brute-force script to check for small solutions.


If we split the exponent
$n$
into its residue classes modulo
$3$
(writing
$n = 3\mu + j$
for
$j \in \{0, 1, 2\}$
), the problem naturally transforms into finding points on:




For
$n \equiv 0 \pmod 3 \implies 3X^3 - Y^3 = 1$


For
$n \equiv 1 \pmod 3 \implies 3X^3 - 2Y^3 = 1$


For
$n \equiv 2 \pmod 3 \implies 3X^3 - 4Y^3 = 1$




Where
$X = m$
and
$Y = 2^\mu$
.


Questions




Is there an obstruction for any of the branches?


Is there a way to establish that
$(1,1)$
is unique?
answer:
This kind of equations associated with Catalan's conjecture, rarely can be solved by elementary means (see Mihäilesku's proof). Here I give a partial answer, namely with
$3x^3-y^3=1$
, the first of the three ones given by the O.P.
The equivalence
$$a+b=c\iff(9a^2b+(b-a)^3)^3+(9ab^2-(b-a)^3)^3=abc27(a^2+ab+b^2)^3\hspace1cm(*)$$
leads to the three equations
$X^3+Y^3=AZ^3$
for
$A=3,6,12$
and it is known that the above mentioned first equation  has no integers solutions with
$XYZ\ne0$
which proves the impossibility of
$3x^3-y^3=1$
.

On the other hand both equations for
$A=6,12$
have infinitely many solutions and we cannot finish the proof so esily as for
$A=3$
. Nevertheless I wonder that It might be possible that by replacing the corresponding values ​​of the two equations in the RHS of (*), one could perhaps obtain something.....

question:
An equilateral triangle of area
$1$
is contained inside a convex heptagon of area
$1.0000001$
. Prove that at least one interior angle of the heptagon is greater than
$139^{\circ}$
. (Leningrad MO 1972)

My intuition is that the area difference between the heptagon and the triangle is extremely small, so most of the vertices of the heptagon must lie very close to the boundary of the triangle. Since at least four vertices of the heptagon cannot coincide with vertices of the triangle, at least two vertices must lie very close to the same side of the triangle, which should force a very large interior angle and perhaps yield the desired bound.

However, I do not see how to make this rigorous. In particular, the numerical value
$139^{\circ}$
seems difficult to obtain cleanly from such heuristic considerations.

I would appreciate either a complete solution or suggestions on how to formalize this intuition.
answer:
We can develop some intuition if we think about a (convex) hexagon containing the triangle, rather than a heptagon. If we assign two hexagon vertices to be placed near each triangle vertex, we can imagine an equiangular configuration of the hexagon vertices such that the difference in area can be made arbitrarily small (but not zero). Specifically, suppose without loss of generality that our triangle's vertices in the complex plane are
$$\{\rho, \rho e^{2\pi i/3}, \rho e^{-2\pi i/3}\}, \quad \rho = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3\sqrt{3}}}.$$
Then we may construct a family of such hexagons as
$$\{\rho \pm \epsilon e^{i \pi/2}, \rho e^{2\pi i/3} \pm \epsilon e^{i \pi/6}, \rho e^{-2\pi i/3} \pm \epsilon e^{-i \pi/6}\}.$$
As
$\epsilon \to 0$
, these vertices obviously approach those of the triangle; yet we can also show that the interior angles of the hexagon always remain
$2\pi/3 = 120^\circ$
.  So this type of configuration is suggestive of a pigeonhole-type argument in the heptagonal case.

To this end, we eliminate all configurations in which the heptagon's vertices are situated "far away" from the triangular vertices, since in such a case, any heptagon vertex not approaching a triangular vertex would have an interior angle larger than
$139^\circ$
.

By the pigeonhole principle, there must be at least one triangle vertex for which there are at least
$3$
heptagon vertices clustered around it.

Now, draw a picture of this cluster.  What is the optimal way to place them such that their interior angles are minimized?  Since the sum total of a convex heptagon's interior angles must be
$(7-2)\pi = 5\pi$
, and the four vertices that are clustered in pairs would have interior angles approaching
$2\pi/3$
, this means we need to distribute
$$5\pi - 4(2\pi/3) = 7\pi/3 = 420^\circ$$
among the remaining three vertices.  And now we can see where the
$139^\circ$
lower bound comes from.

question:
Let
$L_0$
be an axiomatic proof system for propositional logic which has the following axiom schema:

A1.

$(\alpha\to(\beta\to\alpha))$

A2.

$((\alpha\to(\beta\to\gamma))\to((\alpha\to\beta)\to(\alpha\to\gamma)))$

A3.

$((\neg\beta\to\neg\alpha)\to(\alpha\to\beta))$

as well as modus ponens (MP). Take it as read that
$L_0$
enjoys basic metatheoretic results (Deduction Theorem, Cut, Weakening...)

We construct an alternative schema for
A3
,
A3'
:

A3'.

$((\neg\beta\to\alpha)\to(\neg\alpha\to\beta))$

Let
$L'_0$
be just like
$L_0$
except we replace
A3
for
A3'
. The ultimate goal is to prove that these proof systems are equivalent (in the sense that
$\Gamma\vdash_{L_0}\varphi$
holds iff
$\Gamma\vdash_{L_0'}\varphi$
); along the way we should try and prove that we have a proof schema of
A3
in
$L_0'$
, and this is what I am struggling with.

Question:
how can one (schematically) prove
$\vdash_{L'_0}((\neg\beta\to\neg\alpha)\to(\alpha\to\beta))$
, if one can at all (see remark later).

What I've tried.
By the Deduction Theorem (twice) we may equivalently show
$\{\alpha,(\neg\beta\to\neg\alpha)\}\vdash_{L'_0}\beta$
. By
$\mathbf{A3'}[\neg\alpha,\beta]=((\neg\beta\to\neg\alpha)\to(\neg\neg\alpha\to\beta))$
, we see that it suffices to show double-negation introduction (DNI), i.e.
$\alpha\vdash_{L'_0}\neg\neg\alpha$
.

It's not obvious to me how to prove DNI in
$L'_0$
, particularly because somehow the negation symbols in
A3'
are in the "wrong places at the wrong times".

Remark.
I think I believe these systems are equivalent, but there is a possibility that they're not. This comes from a past paper question on an introductory mathematical logic course, and apparently the solutions for this question (which I don't have access to) derive DNI incorrectly!

[P.S This is my first MSE post; I have tried to abide by the etiquette but apologies if I miss the mark in any way!]
answer:
The systems are
not
equivalent, as your
$L'_0$
is sound with respect to a modified propositional semantics where all formulas of the form
$\neg\alpha$
have value
$0$
under all assignments. Thus,
$L'_0$
cannot prove e.g. the
$L_0$
-tautology
$p\to\neg\neg p$
, where
$p$
is a propositional atom. (On the other hand, the systems do become equivalent if you add
$\alpha\to\neg\neg\alpha$
as an axiom schema.)

This post was edited and submitted for review
4 days ago
and failed to reopen the post:

Original close reason(s) were not resolved

Improve this question

If we have a sequence say
$(a_n) \notin \ell^p$
then

$$\sup \Bigl\{\Bigl|\sum_{n=1}^\infty b_n a_n\Bigr| : (b_n) \in \ell^q , \|(b_n)\|_{\ell^q} \leq 1\Bigr\} = \infty.
$$


Q1.
Is this true as written?


Q2
. How we could write
$\sum_{n=1}^\infty b_n a_n$
safely inside the absolute inside the supremum?
To write this series we must make sure before that
$\lim_{n \rightarrow \infty} \sum_{k=1}^n b_k a_k$
exists?


Also this definition means for every positive integer
$k$
there is a corresponding sequence
$(\alpha_n^{(k)})_{n=1}^\infty$
such that




$\sum_{n=1}^\infty \lvert\alpha_n^{(k)}\rvert^q \leq 1.$


$\lvert\sum_{n=1}^\infty \alpha_n^{(k)} a_n\rvert > k.$




Q3.
So my question is can we find
$N_k$
such that
$|\sum_{n=1}^{N_k} \alpha_n^{(k)} a_n| > k.$


Is
$N_k$
guaranteed to exist?
answer:
A linear operator
$A: \ell^q \to \mathbb{R}$
has the
Operator norm
$$
\|A\|_{\text{op}(\ell^q)}
= \sup_{b\in \ell^q} \frac{|A b|}{\|b\|_{\ell^q}}
= \sup\bigl\{|Ab| : \|b\|_{\ell^q} \le 1\bigr\}
$$
Notice that this is your definition if you take
$A=a^*$
with
$a=(a_n)_{n\in \mathbb N}$
and
$$
(a)^*b := \sum_{n=1}^\infty a_n b_n.
$$
Clearly the above is linear in
$b$
so the dual
$a^*$
of
$a$
is indeed a linear operator. As it turns out

Lemma (Norm duality).
If
$\frac1p + \frac1q = 1$
, then the
$\ell^p$
norm is dual to the
$\ell^q$
norm, that is
$$
\|a^*\|_{\mathrm{op}(\ell^q)} = \|a\|_{\ell^p} = \Bigl(\sum_{n=1}^\infty |a_n|^p\Bigr)^{\frac1p}
$$

That is if
$a\notin \ell^p$
, then
$\infty = \|a\|_{\ell^p} = \|a^*\|_{\mathrm{op}(\ell^q)}$
, which is exactly what you want/claim (
Q1
).
Q2
is answered since the supremum will take
$b_n$
that have the same sign as
$a_n$
, this is a bit difficult to define properly but you get the idea.

Proof.
Hölder's inequality
states that
$$
\tag{Hölder}
\sum_{n=1}^\infty |a_nb_n| \le \|a\|_{\ell^p}\|b\|_{\ell^q}.
$$
Consequently we have
$$
\|a^*\|_{\mathrm{op}(\ell^q)}
= \sup_{\|b\|_{\ell^q}=1}\Bigl|\sum_{n=1}^\infty a_n b_n\Bigr|
\le \sup_{\|b\|_{\ell^q}=1}\sum_{n=1}^\infty |a_n b_n|
\overset{\text{Hölder}} \le \|a\|_{\ell^p}.
$$
Equality for
$a\in \ell^p$
:
we are going to construct a
$b=(b_n)$
that achieves this upper bound. For this we select
$$
b_n = \begin{cases}
\|a\|_{\ell^p}^{1-p}\frac{|a_n|^p}{a_n} & a_n \neq 0
\\
0 & a_n = 0.
\end{cases}
$$
We now need to check that
$b \in \ell^q$
with
$\|b\|_{\ell^q}=1$
. To see this observe that
$\frac1p + \frac1q$
implies
$q= \frac{p}{p-1}$
and therefore
$$
\|b\|_{\ell^q}^q = \sum_{n=1}^\infty |b_n|^q = \sum_{n=1}^\infty \|a\|_{\ell^p}^{(1-p)q} |a_n|^{(p-1)q}
= \|a\|_{\ell^p}^{-p}\sum_{n=1}^\infty |a_n|^{p}
= \|a\|_{\ell^p}^{-p}\|a\|_{\ell^p}^{p}
=1.
$$
And by definition
$$
(a)^* b = \sum_{n=1}^\infty a_n b_n
= \sum_{n=1}^\infty\|a\|_{\ell^p}^{1-p}|a_n|^p
=\|a\|_{\ell^p}^{1-p}\sum_{n=1}^\infty|a_n|^p
=\|a\|_{\ell^p}^{1-p}\|a\|_{\ell^p}^{p}
=\|a\|_{\ell^p}
$$
This implies
$$
\|a^*\|_{\mathrm{op}(\ell^q)}
= \sup_{\|b\|_{\ell^q}=1}\Bigl|\sum_{n=1}^\infty a_n b_n\Bigr|
= \|a\|_{\ell^p}.
$$
Now we have only proven that
$\|a\|_{\ell^p} = \|a^*\|_{\mathrm{op}(\ell^q)}$
for
$a\in \ell^p$
but we made no statement about
$a\notin \ell^p$
.

Equality for
$a\notin \ell^p$
:
To see that
$\|a^*\|_{\mathrm{op}(\ell^q)}$
must be infinite for any
$a \notin \ell^p$
, you simply define
$a^{N} = (a^N_n)$
with
$$
a^N_n := \begin{cases}
a_n & n\le N\\
0 & n>N
\end{cases}
$$
Then clearly
$a^N \in \ell^p$
with
$$\begin{aligned}
\infty
= \lim_{N\to \infty}\|a^N\|_{\ell^p}
&= \lim_{N\to \infty} \|a^N\|_{\mathrm{op}(\ell^q)}
\\
&= \lim_{N\to \infty} \sup \{\sum_{n=1}^N a_n b_n : \|b\|_{\ell^q}\le 1\}
\\
&= \lim_{N\to \infty} \sup \{\sum_{n=1}^N a_n b_n : \|b\|_{\ell^q}\le 1\}
\\
&= \sup_N \sup \{\sum_{n=1}^N a_n b_n : \|b\|_{\ell^q}\le 1\}
\\
&= \sup \{\sum_{n=1}^\infty a_n b_n : \|b\|_{\ell^q}\le 1\}
\end{aligned}
$$
We have thus proven in both cases
$a\in \ell^p$
and
$a\notin \ell^p$
that we have equality. QED

Re
Q3
you can construct the
$a^{(k)}$
using the construction of
$b=(b_n)$
for
$a^N$
with
$N$
big enough. In that case
$b$
will do as it will similarly be zero after some finite index.

Let
$D\subset\mathbb R^n$
be a compact domain, and let
$f:D\to\mathbb R$
to be continuous on
$D$
. Let
$\partial D$
denote the boundary of
$D$
, and let
$S\subset D^\circ$
be the set of interior local maximas of
$f$
. If
$S\ne\varnothing$
, then
$$\max_{x\in\partial D}f(x)>\max_{x\in S\backslash\partial D}f(x)\implies\max_{x\in\partial D}f(x)=\max_{x\in S}f(x)$$
answer:
Wouldn't a counterexample be
$f(x) = x + 0.1 \cdot \sin(4 \pi x)$
on
$D = [0,1]$
? Then
$f(0) = 0$
,
$f(1) = 1$
and
$f(x) < 1$
for
$x \in (0,1)$
. So the assumption is satisfied but the implication false.

question:
Assume that
$a, b, c$
are integers, find the greatest common divisor of all integers in the form
$\left(2^{a^2} 19^{b^2} 53^{c^2}+8\right)^{16}-1$
.

Attempt: By FLT, it is clear that 17 can divide all these numbers. In fact, if we assume 17 divides
$2^{a^2} 19^{b^2} 53^{c^2}+8$
, this leads to
$2^{a^2＋b^2+c^2}≡9$
(mod 17), this implies
$a^2＋b^2+c^2≡7$
(mod 8), which is impossible.
answer:
The OP has already shown that
$17\mid (2^{a^2}19^{b^2}53^{c^2}+8)^{16}-1$
for every triple of integers
$(a,b,c)$
. I'll show that there doesn't exist any other prime number like that.

Suppose that there exists a prime
$p\ne 17$
, such that

$$ (2^{a^2}19^{b^2}53^{c^2}+8)^{16}\equiv 1\pmod{p} \quad \forall(a,b,c)\in\mathbb{N^3}.$$

Since this modular equation is of degree 16 (and all solutions are coprime to
$p$
), there exists
$\le 16$
solutions to it modulo
$p$
. Therefore, the expression
$2^{a^2}19^{b^2}53^{c^2}+8$
, and therefore
$2^{a^2}19^{b^2}53^{c^2}$
can yield no more than 16 different residues modulo
$p$
.

Note, that if
$p\not\in\{17,2,3\}$
(and
$p=2, p=3$
obviously don't work), then
$2\not\equiv 19, 2\not\equiv53, 19\not\equiv 53 \bmod p$
. Therefore, if all of
$2^{a^2}, 19^{b^2}, 53^{c^2}$
yield at least three distinct remainders modulo
$p$
, the number of all possible remainders of
$2^{a^2}19^{b^2}53^{c^2}$
modulo
$p$
is at least
$3^3=27>16$
, therefore, that
$p$
won't work.

So, at least one of the expressions
$(2^{a^2}, 19^{b^2}, 53^{c^2})$
yields at most two distinct remainders modulo
$p$
. Note than
$p\in\{2,19,53\}$
don't work. So, let
$ q\in\{2,19,53\}$
be such that
$q^{n^2}$
yields at most two distinct remainders modulo
$p$
.

Let's fix other exponents to 0, so the full expression also transforms into simple
$q^{n^2}$
. Since
$n=0$
and
$n=1$
give different remainders (
$1$
and
$q$
, respectively), we conclude that either
$q^4\equiv q^0$
, either
$q^4\equiv q^1 \bmod p$
, implying that
$\text{ord}_q(p)\le 4$
. This implies that
$p$
divides at least one of the integers
$2^4-1, 19^4-1, 53^4-1, 2^3-1, 19^3-1, 53^3-1$
, check all the possible values of
$p$
(which is feasible as we can easily factorise numbers of form
$A^B-1$
) only to find that there are no primes
$p$
satisfying our conditions (to simplify our work, we can check if
$p \mid 9^{16}-1$
and
$p\mid 10^{16}-1$
).

Finally, note that

$$ 17^2\nmid (1+8)^{16}-1,$$

meaning that
$17^2$
also does not work and therefore the desired
$\gcd$
is equal to
$\boxed{\bf 17}$
.

question:
Let
$p$
be a prime and
$\chi:(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\times\to\mathbb{C}^\times$
be a nontrivial Dirichlet charcater of order
$d$
modulo
$p$
, with
$d\mid p-1$
. Fix
$\zeta_p$
a nontrivial
$p$
-th root of unity. Let
$\tau(\chi)$
be the Gauss sum of
$\chi$
. What is the field

$$
K:=\mathbb{Q}(\tau(\chi))\subset\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d,\zeta_p)?
$$

Let
$\mathfrak{p}$
be a prime of
$\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d)}$
lying above
$p$
, and
$\mathfrak{P}$
be a prime of
$\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d)K}$
lying above
$\mathfrak{p}$
, then
$(\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d)K)_\mathfrak{P}/\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d)_\mathfrak{p}\subset\mathbb{Q}_p(\zeta_p)/\mathbb{Q}_p$
is a totally ramified extension. Let
$\chi_0:(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\times\to\mathbb{Z}_p^\times$
be the character satisfying
$\chi_0(a)\equiv a\bmod{p}$
. If
$\chi=\chi_0^a$
,
$1\le a\le p-2$
in
$L_\mathfrak{P}$
, then

$$
v_p(\tau(\chi))=1-\dfrac{a}{p-1}
$$

(see page 6 of
this link
). So we have

$$
e(\mathfrak{P}/\mathfrak{p})=d,\quad f(\mathfrak{P}/\mathfrak{p})=1.
$$

Comparing the degrees we have

$$
\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d)K=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d)\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_p)^G,
$$

where
$G$
is the unique index-
$d$
subgroup of
$(\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\times$
.


Question.
Do we actually have
$K=\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d)\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_p)^G$
, i.e.,
$\zeta_d\in K$
?


I have no idea on how to get
$\zeta_d$
by manipulating with
$\tau(\chi)$
, so I think I need some help here. Thank you in advance.
answer:
Yes, that result holds! But honestly, I don't easily see a way to get
$\zeta_d$
by just manipulating
$\tau(\chi)$
. One can sidestep this issue by proving that every automorphism fixing
$\tau(\chi)$
also fixes
$\zeta_d$
, and this can be shown using local arithmetic. The idea is to use the Teichmuller character to "see" the Galois action; it turns the Galois action on the character into an arithmetic operation on the exponent, which the valuation can detect and thus allow us to "see" the twist purely from the valuation.

Let
$L = \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d, \zeta_p)$
. The Galois group of
$L$
over
$\mathbb{Q}$
is

$$\Gamma = \text{Gal}(L/\mathbb{Q}) \cong (\mathbb{Z}/d\mathbb{Z})^\times \times (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\times$$

An element
$\gamma = (u, v) \in \Gamma$
acts on the roots of unity by
$\gamma(\zeta_d) = \zeta_d^u$
and
$\gamma(\zeta_p) = \zeta_p^v$
. Because
$\chi(x)$
is a
$d$
-th root of unity (and thus lies in
$\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d)$
),
$\gamma$
acts on the character by
$\gamma(\chi(x)) = \chi(x)^u = \chi^u(x)$
.

Let's see how
$\gamma$
acts on the Gauss sum
$\tau(\chi)$

$$\gamma(\tau(\chi)) = \sum_{x=1}^{p-1} \gamma(\chi(x)) \gamma(\zeta_p^x) = \sum_{x=1}^{p-1} \chi^u(x) \zeta_p^{vx}$$

Substituting
$y \equiv vx \pmod p$
, which means
$x \equiv yv^{-1} \pmod p$
we get

$$\gamma(\tau(\chi)) = \sum_{y=1}^{p-1} \chi^u(yv^{-1}) \zeta_p^y = \chi^u(v^{-1}) \sum_{y=1}^{p-1} \chi^u(y) \zeta_p^y = \chi^{-u}(v) \tau(\chi^u)$$

We want to find the stabilizer subgroup
$H = \{ \gamma \in \Gamma \mid \gamma(\tau(\chi)) = \tau(\chi) \}$
. Once we know
$H$
, Galois theory tells us that
$K = L^H$
.

To that end suppose
$\gamma = (u, v) \in H$
. Then
$\tau(\chi) = \chi^{-u}(v) \tau(\chi^u)$
. This is an equality of two elements in
$L$
. If we embed
$L$
into the local field
$L_\mathfrak{P} = \mathbb{Q}_p(\zeta_p)$
, their
$p$
-adic valuations must be exactly the same (this holds because
$d \mid p-1$
implies
$p$
splits completely in
$\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d)$
, so each completion
$\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d)_\mathfrak{p} \cong \mathbb{Q}_p$
). Because
$\chi^{-u}(v)$
is a root of unity, it is a unit in the valuation ring, so
$v_p(\chi^{-u}(v)) = 0$
. Therefore
$$v_p(\tau(\chi)) = v_p(\tau(\chi^u))$$

In the completion
$L_\mathfrak{P}$
, the character
$\chi$
is identified with
$\chi_0^a$
, where
$\chi_0$
is the Teichmuller character. Since
$\chi$
has order exactly
$d$
, the exponent
$a$
must be of the form
$a = c \cdot \frac{p-1}{d}$
for some integer
$c$
where
$1 \le c < d$
and
$\gcd(c, d) = 1$
. By the formula you provided in the post, the valuation of
$\tau(\chi)$
is
$$v_p(\tau(\chi)) = 1 - \frac{a}{p-1} = 1 - \frac{c}{d}$$
Now, what is the valuation of
$\tau(\chi^u)$
? The character
$\chi^u$
is identified with
$\chi_0^{au}$
. To apply your formula, the exponent needs to be reduced modulo
$p-1$
. We have
$$au = cu \frac{p-1}{d} \equiv (cu \bmod d) \frac{p-1}{d} \pmod{p-1}$$
Applying your formula to this reduced exponent gives
$$v_p(\tau(\chi^u)) = 1 - \frac{(cu \bmod d)\frac{p-1}{d}}{p-1} = 1 - \frac{cu \bmod d}{d}$$

Since the two valuations must be equal, we have
$1 - \frac{c}{d} = 1 - \frac{cu \bmod d}{d}$
and because
$c$
is coprime to
$d$
, we can solve this to find that
$u \equiv 1 \pmod d$
. Since
$u \in (\mathbb{Z}/d\mathbb{Z})^\times$
, this forces
$u = 1$
. Substituting
$u=1$
back into our stabilizer equation
$\gamma(\tau(\chi)) = \tau(\chi)$
gives
$$\tau(\chi) = \chi^{-1}(v) \tau(\chi) \implies \chi(v) = 1$$
The set of elements
$v \in (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\times$
such that
$\chi(v) = 1$
is exactly the kernel of
$\chi$
, which is the unique index-
$d$
subgroup
$G \subset (\mathbb{Z}/p\mathbb{Z})^\times$
.

Thus we have shown that the stabilizer
$H$
of
$\tau(\chi)$
consists precisely of the elements
$(1, v)$
where
$v \in G$
. Therefore,
$H = \{1\} \times G$
. By the Fundamental Theorem of Galois Theory,
$K = \mathbb{Q}(\tau(\chi))$
is the fixed field
$L^H$
. More importantly, the first component is
$1$
, meaning it fixes everything in
$\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d)$
while the second component is
$G$
, which fixes exactly the subfield
$\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_p)^G$
inside
$\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_p)$
.

The field fixed by
$H$
is the compositum of these two fixed fields
$K = \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d) \mathbb{Q}(\zeta_p)^G$
. Because
$\mathbb{Q}(\zeta_d)$
is trivially a subfield of this compositum,
$\zeta_d$
is indeed contained in
$K$
.

question:
Let
$X$
be a CW complex. Denote by
$X^{(k)}$
its
$k$
-skeleton.

The cellular chain complex
$(C_*(X),\partial_*)$
is defined by

$ C_n(X) = H_n\bigl(X^{(n)}, X^{(n-1)}\bigr) $

and the boundary map
$\partial_n : C_n(X) \to C_{n-1}(X)$
is the composition

$$ \partial_n : C_n(X) = H_n\bigl(X^{(n)}, X^{(n-1)}\bigr)
\xrightarrow{\;\Delta_n\;} H_{n-1}\bigl(X^{(n-1)}\bigr)
\xrightarrow{\;j_{n-1}\;} H_{n-1}\bigl(X^{(n-1)}, X^{(n-2)}\bigr) = C_{n-1}(X) $$

where
$\Delta_n$
is the connecting homomorphism of the pair
$(X^{(n)}, X^{(n-1)})$
and
$j_{n-1}$
is induced by the inclusion
$(X^{(n-1)},\varnothing)\hookrightarrow (X^{(n-1)},X^{(n-2)})$
.


Equivalently, in terms of cells, for an
$n$
-cell
$e^n_\alpha$
with attaching map
$\varphi_\alpha : S^{n-1} \to X^{(n-1)}$
, the incidence number
$[e^n_\alpha : e^{n-1}_\beta]$
is defined by

$[e^n_\alpha : e^{n-1}_\beta] = \deg\bigl( q_\beta \circ \varphi_\alpha \bigr)$
,
where
$q_\beta : X^{(n-1)} \to S^{n-1}$
collapses the complement of the
$(n-1)$
-cell
$e^{n-1}_\beta$
to a point. Then define

$\partial_n(e^n_\alpha) = \sum_{\beta} [e^n_\alpha : e^{n-1}_\beta] \; e^{n-1}_\beta.$


Prove that
$\partial_{n-1}\circ\partial_n = 0$
for all
$n$
, i.e. the cellular boundary maps satisfy
$\partial^2 = 0$
by homotopy theoretic methods without using homology.


This is from
tom Dieck
's
Algebraic Topology
which is left as an exercise.


$$
\partial_n(e^n_\alpha) = \sum_{\beta} [e^n_\alpha : e^{n-1}_\beta] \; e^{n-1}_\beta,
\qquad
\partial_{n-1}(e^{n-1}_\beta) = \sum_{\gamma} [e^{n-1}_\beta : e^{n-2}_\gamma] \; e^{n-2}_\gamma.
$$


$$
\partial_{n-1}\partial_n(e^n_\alpha)
= \partial_{n-1}\!\left( \sum_{\beta} [e^n_\alpha : e^{n-1}_\beta] \, e^{n-1}_\beta \right)
= \sum_{\beta} [e^n_\alpha : e^{n-1}_\beta] \sum_{\gamma} [e^{n-1}_\beta : e^{n-2}_\gamma] \, e^{n-2}_\gamma
= \sum_{\gamma} \left( \sum_{\beta} [e^n_\alpha : e^{n-1}_\beta] [e^{n-1}_\beta : e^{n-2}_\gamma] \right) e^{n-2}_\gamma.
$$

I want to construct a map
$S^{n-1} \to S^{n-1}$
with the degree of
$\sum_{\beta} [e^n_\alpha : e^{n-1}_\beta] [e^{n-1}_\beta : e^{n-2}_\gamma]$
and show it is null homotopic.But I don't know how.
answer:
Collating the hints into a solution, this is how it looks. Hopefully you can fill in the details.

Recall that the
$(n-1)$
-skeleton
$X^{(n-1)}$
is obtained from
$X^{(n-2)}$
by attaching the
$(n-1)$
-cells via their attaching maps
$\psi_\beta : S^{n-2} \to X^{(n-2)}$
. Let
$\psi : \coprod_\beta S^{n-2}_\beta \to X^{(n-2)}$
be the combined attaching map. Topologically,
$X^{(n-1)}$
is the mapping cone of
$\psi$
, written as
$C_\psi$
. The standard cofibration sequence (the Puppe sequence) associated with this attachment is

$$ \coprod_\beta S^{n-2}_\beta \xrightarrow{\;\psi\;} X^{(n-2)} \xrightarrow{\;i\;} X^{(n-1)} \xrightarrow{\;q\;} \bigvee_\beta S^{n-1}_\beta \xrightarrow{\;\Sigma\psi\;} \Sigma X^{(n-2)} $$

where

$q$
collapses
$X^{(n-2)}$
to a point, identifying
$X^{(n-1)}/X^{(n-2)}$
with the wedge of spheres
$\bigvee_\beta S^{n-1}_\beta$
.
$\Sigma\psi$
is the suspension of the attaching map
$\psi$
.

Now, the map
$(\Sigma\psi) \circ q : X^{(n-1)} \to \Sigma X^{(n-2)}$
is null-homotopic (this is where you would invoke
$h$
-coexactness as you mention in the draft). We can show this directly actually, by explicitly factoring it through a contractible space. Consider the cone over the
$(n-2)$
-skeleton,
$C(X^{(n-2)})$
. There is a map
$X^{(n-1)} \to C(X^{(n-2)})$
that maps
$X^{(n-2)}$
to the base of the cone and extends linearly across the attached cells. Because
$C(X^{(n-2)})$
is contractible, this map is null-homotopic. If we follow this map by the natural projection

$$C(X^{(n-2)}) \to C(X^{(n-2)})/X^{(n-2)} = \Sigma X^{(n-2)}$$

the composition is exactly
$(\Sigma\psi) \circ q$
.

The
$n$
-cell
$e^n_\alpha$
is attached to
$X^{(n-1)}$
via the map
$\varphi_\alpha : S^{n-1} \to X^{(n-1)}$
. Since
$(\Sigma\psi) \circ q \simeq 0$
, if we pre-compose it with
$\varphi_\alpha$
, the resulting map is also null-homotopic

$$ (\Sigma\psi) \circ q \circ \varphi_\alpha : S^{n-1} \to \Sigma X^{(n-2)} \simeq 0 $$

To isolate the coefficient for a specific
$(n-2)$
-cell
$e^{n-2}_\gamma$
, let
$p_\gamma : X^{(n-2)} \to S^{n-2}$
be the quotient map that collapses everything outside of
$e^{n-2}_\gamma$
to a point.Taking the suspension of
$p_\gamma$
gives us a map

$$\Sigma p_\gamma : \Sigma X^{(n-2)} \to \Sigma S^{n-2} \cong S^{n-1}$$
Now, post-compose our null-homotopic map with
$\Sigma p_\gamma$
to create a map
$H: S^{n-1} \to S^{n-1}$
, namely

$$ H = (\Sigma p_\gamma) \circ (\Sigma\psi) \circ (q \circ \varphi_\alpha) $$

Because it contains a null-homotopic factor,
$H$
is null-homotopic, which means
$\deg(H) = 0$
. Let's break the map
$H$
into two parts,
$H = A \circ B$
, and compute the degree.

The map
$B = q \circ \varphi_\alpha : S^{n-1} \to \bigvee_\beta S^{n-1}_\beta$
.

The degree of
$B$
onto the
$\beta$
-th summand of the wedge is, by definition, the incidence number
$d_{\alpha\beta} = [e^n_\alpha : e^{n-1}_\beta]$
. In the homotopy group
$\pi_{n-1}(\bigvee_\beta S^{n-1}_\beta)$
, the map
$B$
represents the sum
$ [B] = \sum_\beta d_{\alpha\beta} \cdot \iota_\beta $
where
$\iota_\beta$
is the inclusion of the
$\beta$
-th sphere.

The map
$A = (\Sigma p_\gamma) \circ (\Sigma\psi) : \bigvee_\beta S^{n-1}_\beta \to S^{n-1}$
.

Let's evaluate
$A$
restricted to the
$\beta$
-th summand. This restriction is exactly
$\Sigma (p_\gamma \circ \psi_\beta)$
. Since suspension preserves degrees, we have
$\deg(\Sigma f) = \deg(f)$
. Therefore, the degree of this restricted map is exactly the degree of
$p_\gamma \circ \psi_\beta : S^{n-2} \to S^{n-2}$
, which is by definition
$d_{\beta\gamma} = [e^{n-1}_\beta : e^{n-2}_\gamma]$
.

The composition
$H = A \circ B$
.

When we compose
$B$
(which maps
$S^{n-1}$
into the wedge sum with degrees
$d_{\alpha\beta}$
) with
$A$
(which maps each
$\beta$
-th wedge summand to
$S^{n-1}$
with degree
$d_{\beta\gamma}$
), the total degree is the sum of the products of the degrees along each branch

$$ \deg(H) = \sum_\beta d_{\alpha\beta} \cdot d_{\beta\gamma} = \sum_\beta [e^n_\alpha : e^{n-1}_\beta] [e^{n-1}_\beta : e^{n-2}_\gamma] $$

Since we proved topologically in Step 3 that
$H$
is null-homotopic, we can conclude that

$$ \deg(H) = 0 \implies \sum_\beta [e^n_\alpha : e^{n-1}_\beta] [e^{n-1}_\beta : e^{n-2}_\gamma] = 0 $$

Because this holds for every
$\alpha$
and
$\gamma$
, the composition of the cellular boundary maps
$\partial_{n-1} \circ \partial_n = 0$
.

question:
I am reading
Calculus - Several Variables -
(in Japanese) by Shoshichi Kobayashi.

On p.12:

Theorem 1

Let
$f(x,y)$
be a function defined on an open set
$R$
.

If
$f_x(x,y)$
and
$f_y(x,y)$
exist on
$R$
and
$f_x(x,y)$
or
$f_y(x,y)$
is bounded on
$R$
, then
$f(x,y)$
is continuous on
$R$
.

Proof

Suppose
$|f_x(x,y)|\leq M$
for all
$(x,y)\in R$
.

\begin{align*}f(a+\Delta x,b+\Delta y)-f(a,b)&=f(a+\Delta x,b+\Delta y)-f(a,b+\Delta y)\\+f(a,b+\Delta y)-f(a,b)\\&=f_x(a,b+\Delta y)\Delta x+\varepsilon_1\Delta x+f_y(a,b)\Delta y+\varepsilon_2\Delta y,\end{align*}
where
$\varepsilon_1\to 0$
when
$\Delta x\to 0$
and
$\varepsilon_2\to 0$
when
$\Delta y\to 0$
.

$|f(a+\Delta x,b+\Delta y)-f(a,b)|\leq |M+\varepsilon_1||\Delta x|+|f_y(a,b)+\varepsilon_2||\Delta y|$
.

So, if
$\Delta x\to 0$
and
$\Delta y\to 0$
,
$f(a+\Delta x,b+\Delta y)\to f(a,b)$
.

$\varepsilon_1$
is a function of
$\Delta x$
and
$\Delta y$
.

I think Kobayashi thinks the following holds in general in his proof of Theorem 1:

When
$\Delta x\to 0$
and
$\Delta y\to 0$
, then
$\varepsilon_1\to 0$
.

But I think in general, this is not true.

Am I right?

Since I could not understand this proof, I rewrite the proof as follows:

\begin{align*}f(a+\Delta x,b+\Delta y)-f(a,b)
&=
f(a+\Delta x,b+\Delta y)-f(a,b+\Delta y)\\+f(a,b+\Delta y)-f(a,b)\\
&=
f_x(a+\theta\Delta x,b+\Delta y)\Delta x+f(a,b+\Delta y)-f(a,b).\end{align*}

\begin{align*}|f(a+\Delta x,b+\Delta y)-f(a,b)|
&\leq
|f_x(a+\theta\Delta x,b+\Delta y)||\Delta x|\\+|f(a,b+\Delta y)-f(a,b)|\\
&\leq M|\Delta x|+|f(a,b+\Delta y)-f(a,b)|.
\end{align*}

When
$\Delta x\to 0$
,
$M|\Delta x|\to 0$
.

When
$\Delta y\to 0$
,
$|f(a,b+\Delta y)-f(a,b)|\to 0$
because
$f_y(a,b)$
exists.
answer:
You are right, Kobayashi's proof tacitly assumes that
$\epsilon_1$
(which depends on both
$\Delta x$
and
$\Delta y$
) goes to
$0$
as
$(\Delta x, \Delta y) \to (0,0)$
. It would also be sufficient to know that
$\epsilon_1$
is bounded in a neighborhood of
$(a,b)$
, but that requires a proof (which was not given by Kobayashi).

Therefore your proof is the correct and elegant approach.

answer:

When
$\Delta x\to 0$
and
$\Delta y\to 0$
, then
$\varepsilon_1\to 0$
.

When
$\Delta x\to 0$
and
$\Delta y\to 0$
, then
$\varepsilon_1\to 0$
.

But I think in general, this is not true.

I found the following example in the book.

Let
$f(x,y):=\frac{2xy}{x^2+y^2}$
if
$(x,y)\neq (0,0)$
and
$f(0,0):=0$
.

Let
$f(x,y):=\frac{2xy}{x^2+y^2}$
if
$(x,y)\neq (0,0)$
and
$f(0,0):=0$
.

Kobayashi proved that
$f(x,y)$
is not continuous at
$(0,0)$
.
Kobayashi proved that
$f_x(x,y)$
and
$f_y(x,y)$
exist for all
$(x,y)$
.
Kobayashi proved that
$f_x(x,y)$
is not bounded.
Kobayashi proved that
$f_y(x,y)$
is not bounded.

$f(0+\Delta x,0+\Delta y)-f(0,0+\Delta y)=f_x(0,0+\Delta y)\Delta x+\varepsilon_1\Delta x$
.
$\varepsilon_1:=0$
if
$\Delta x=0$
.
$\varepsilon_1=0$
if
$\Delta x\neq 0$
and
$\Delta y=0$
.
$\varepsilon_1=\frac{\frac{2\Delta x\Delta y}{\Delta x^2+\Delta y^2}-\frac{2\Delta x}{\Delta y}}{\Delta x}=-\frac{2\Delta x^2}{\Delta y(\Delta x^2+\Delta y^2)}$
if
$\Delta x\neq 0$
and
$\Delta y\neq 0$
.
Let
$\Delta x=\Delta y=t\neq 0$
.
Then,
$\varepsilon_1\to -\infty$
when
$t\to 0$
.
So,

When
$\Delta x\to 0$
and
$\Delta y\to 0$
, then
$\varepsilon_1\to 0$

When
$\Delta x\to 0$
and
$\Delta y\to 0$
, then
$\varepsilon_1\to 0$

is not true in general.

Yes, the claim of the author looks correct. To illustrate with different notation and just a function of a single variabe:
\begin{align}
F(x+h) - F(x) &= h \times \frac{F(x+h)-F(x)}{h} \\
&= h\, F'(x) + h \times \biggl(\frac{F(x+h)-F(x)}{h} - F'(x) \biggr) \\
&= h\, F'(x) + h\, \epsilon_1
```
