I don't understand the part where it "adds $2k+1$ to both sides" and ends up with $(k+1)^2$.

I looked up
another proof
of the same problem that made a lot more sense to me, hoping it would help me understand the first one but I still don't know how to square what I'm seeing in my textbook.
answer:
if we assume that $$1+3+5+...+2k-1=k^2$$ (I) is true, then we have to Show that $$1+3+5+...+2k-1+2k+1=(k+1)^2$$ if we add $2k+1$ in (I) we get
$$2k+1+k^2=(k+1)^2$$ therefore our Statement is true.

question:
One can't prove the quadratic formula by merely substituting
$\frac{-b+\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a},\frac{-b-\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}$
in
$ax^2+bx+c$
because
$ax^2+bx+c$
may have more roots. But we know that it has at most
$2$
roots, but this still doesn't prove it because the solutions might be equal. So in the case there are not equal we have a proof.

Is there some trick to make this proof by substitution work even in the case when the solutions are equal?

(Merely saying that this proof can't work is not enough: I want an actual argument for why this method can't work.)

Edit:
There seems to be some confusion as to what I am asking. We know that there is only one solution to the equation
$ax+b=0$
, namely,
$-b/a$
. This is easy to prove, but let's assume that it is actually very hard. However, it is easy to verify that
$-b/a$
is indeed a solution to
$ax+b=0$
, and if we can prove that there is at most one solution, then we have found all solutions. And we do know that there is at most one solution since if
$p$
is a nonconstant polynomial of degree
$n$
, then the equation
$p(x)=0$
has at most
$n$
solutions. Now, proving directly that
$ax+b=0\iff x=-b/a$
is very easy, but if it was somehow hard, we can "guess" a solution and then prove in the way I described that it is the only solution. Proving the quadratic equation is also easy, but a proof by substitution is easier if one can make it work, albeit not very insightful as to where the solutions came from.
answer:
Vieta's formulas
include that the roots
$x_1,\dotsc,x_n$
of a polynomial
$a_n T^n + a_{n-1} T^{n-1} + \cdots + a_0$
satisfy the relationship
$$x_1 + \cdots + x_n = - \frac{a_{n-1}}{a_n} \tag{1}$$
For
$n = 2$
this means that as soon as one root is found, the other can be derived as well. What is nice is that this does not depend on the condition if these roots are equal or not, so the problem you mention simply disappears.

So, if we have already found (say, by substitution) that
$$x_1 = \frac{-b + \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}$$
is one root of
$aT^2 + bT + c$
, the other root is
$$x_2 \stackrel{~(1)~}{=} -\frac{b}{a} - x_1 = \frac{-2b + b - \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a} = \frac{-b - \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}.$$

question:
One can't prove the quadratic formula by merely substituting
$\frac{-b+\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a},\frac{-b-\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}$
in
$ax^2+bx+c$
because
$ax^2+bx+c$
may have more roots. But we know that it has at most
$2$
roots, but this still doesn't prove it because the solutions might be equal. So in the case there are not equal we have a proof.

Is there some trick to make this proof by substitution work even in the case when the solutions are equal?

(Merely saying that this proof can't work is not enough: I want an actual argument for why this method can't work.)

Edit:
There seems to be some confusion as to what I am asking. We know that there is only one solution to the equation
$ax+b=0$
, namely,
$-b/a$
. This is easy to prove, but let's assume that it is actually very hard. However, it is easy to verify that
$-b/a$
is indeed a solution to
$ax+b=0$
, and if we can prove that there is at most one solution, then we have found all solutions. And we do know that there is at most one solution since if
$p$
is a nonconstant polynomial of degree
$n$
, then the equation
$p(x)=0$
has at most
$n$
solutions. Now, proving directly that
$ax+b=0\iff x=-b/a$
is very easy, but if it was somehow hard, we can "guess" a solution and then prove in the way I described that it is the only solution. Proving the quadratic equation is also easy, but a proof by substitution is easier if one can make it work, albeit not very insightful as to where the solutions came from.
answer:
If you know nothing about
$f(x)$
, then, as you noted, substituting
$x=x_1$
and
$x=x_2$
into
$f$
and showing
$f(x_1)=0$
and
$f(x_2)=0$
doesn't prove that there aren't more solutions.

If you know that
$f(x)=ax^2+bx+c$
has at most two roots, then you can say much more.

For example, if
$b^2-4ac\neq 0$
, then
$x_1\neq x_2$
. Showing
$f(x_1)=0$
and
$f(x_2)=0$
shows that these are two different roots of
$f(x)$
. If there's another root
$x_3$
with
$x_3\neq x_1$
and
$x_3\neq x_2$
, then that would be three distinct roots, which contradicts the fact that there are at most two roots.

We are still stuck with the case where
$b^2-4ac=0$
, that is,
$x_0=x_1=-\frac b{2a}$
.

Substituting
$x=x_0=x_1=-\frac b2$
into
$f(x)=ax^2+bx+c$
gives
$a(-b/2a)^2+b(-b/2a)+c=0$
, after some algebra, and using the fact that
$b^2-4ac=0$
.

But as you said, merely substituting this single value of
$x$
, and merely knowing there are at most two roots, doesn't show that there aren't more roots.

You'd have to have (or prove) a theorem like "If
$b^2-4ac=0$
, then
$ax^2+bx+c=0$
has exactly one root".

This isn't hard to prove; for example, you could let
$x_2=-\frac b{2a}+s$
, and show that
$f(x_2)=as^2$
. So (if
$a\neq 0$
), this can only be a root if
$s=0$
.

An alternative approach is to forgo substitution altogether, and prove the quadratic formula directly, but I don't know if this answers your question at all:

$$
ax^2+bx+c=0\\
\implies x^2+\frac{b}{a}x+\frac{c}{a}=0\\
\implies \left(x+\frac{b}{2a}\right)^2-\frac{b^2}{4a^2}+\frac{c}{a}=0\\
\implies \left(x+\frac{b}{2a}\right)^2=\frac{b^2}{4a^2}-\frac{c}{a}=\frac{b^2-4ac}{4a^2}\\
\implies \left(x+\frac{b}{2a}\right)=\frac{\pm\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}\\
\implies x=\frac{-b\pm\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}\\
$$

question:
One can't prove the quadratic formula by merely substituting
$\frac{-b+\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a},\frac{-b-\sqrt{b^2-4ac}}{2a}$
in
$ax^2+bx+c$
because
$ax^2+bx+c$
may have more roots. But we know that it has at most
$2$
roots, but this still doesn't prove it because the solutions might be equal. So in the case there are not equal we have a proof.

Is there some trick to make this proof by substitution work even in the case when the solutions are equal?

(Merely saying that this proof can't work is not enough: I want an actual argument for why this method can't work.)

Edit:
There seems to be some confusion as to what I am asking. We know that there is only one solution to the equation
$ax+b=0$
, namely,
$-b/a$
. This is easy to prove, but let's assume that it is actually very hard. However, it is easy to verify that
$-b/a$
is indeed a solution to
$ax+b=0$
, and if we can prove that there is at most one solution, then we have found all solutions. And we do know that there is at most one solution since if
$p$
is a nonconstant polynomial of degree
$n$
, then the equation
$p(x)=0$
has at most
$n$
solutions. Now, proving directly that
$ax+b=0\iff x=-b/a$
is very easy, but if it was somehow hard, we can "guess" a solution and then prove in the way I described that it is the only solution. Proving the quadratic equation is also easy, but a proof by substitution is easier if one can make it work, albeit not very insightful as to where the solutions came from.
answer:
The two roots given by the formula are obviously equal when
$b^2-4ac=0$
, and their common value is
$-\dfrac b{2a}$
, which works by substitution.

If we want to know if there is another root, we can use long division.

$$\dfrac{ax^2+bx+c}{x+\dfrac b{2a}}=ax+\dfrac b2+\dfrac{c-\dfrac{b^2}{4a}}{x+\dfrac b{2a}}.$$

The remainder term vanishes and the other root is the solution of
$$ax+\dfrac b2=0$$
as can be checked by substitution.

Other method.

A function is known to have a
double
root if its value and the value of the derivative vanish simultaneously. Hence, check by the substitution

$$\begin{cases}ar^2+br+c=0\\2ar+b=0.\end{cases}$$

question:
This is the 6th problem in exercise 1.4 of Mac Lane's
"Categories for the Working Mathematician"
:

Let
$F$
be a field. Show that the category of all finite-dimensional vector spaces over
$F$
(with morphisms all linear transformations) is equivalent to the category
$\text{Matr}_F$
.

Here
$\mathrm{Matr}_K$
has been introduced (p. 11) as having non-negative integers as objects and morphisms
$m\to n$
being
$n\times m$
matrices with entries in
$K$
(any commutative unital ring).

I have made an attempt at solving it, as follows:

Denote the category of finite-dimensional vector spaces over
$F$
by
$\mathrm{fdVect}_{F}$
. For every
$V\in \mathrm{fdVect}_{F}$
, choose an ordered basis
$\mathcal{B}_{V}$
such that for all
$n\geq 0$
,
$\mathcal{B}_{F^{n}}$
is the canonical basis. Now define the following functors
$$  \mathrm{dim}: \mathrm{fdVect}_{F}\to \mathrm{Matr}_{F}\quad \text{and}\quad \mathrm{list}: \mathrm{Matr}_{F}\to \mathrm{fdVect}_{F}  $$

as follows:
$\mathrm{dim}(V)$
is the dimension of
$V$
over
$F$
. For any
$T: V\to W$
, let
$\mathrm{dim}(T):=[T]_{\mathcal{B}}$
, the matrix of
$T$
with respect to the bases
$\mathcal{B}_{V}$
and
$\mathcal{B}_{W}$
. Let
$\mathrm{list}(n):= F^{n}$
, and for any matrix
$M_{m\times n}$
, let
$\mathrm{list}(M)$
be the matrix
$M$
as a linear map
$M:F^{n}\to F^{m}$
with respect to the canonical basis.


Both of these maps are functors. For any vector space
$V$
, the identity map
$I_{V}$
has the identity matrix with respect to any basis
$\mathcal{B}_{V}$
. And for linear maps
$S: U\to V$
and
$T:V\to W$
, the matrix of
$TS$
with respect to
$\mathcal{B}_{U}$
and
$\mathcal{B}_{W}$
is the product of the matrices of
$T$
and
$S$
with respect to the bases
$\mathcal{B}$
. So
$\mathrm{dim}$
is functorial. Also, the identity matrix
$I_{n}$
is the identity map on
$F^{n}$
, and product of matrices acts as composition of maps between
$F^{n}$
spaces. So
$\mathrm{list}$
is functorial as well. Moreover, we have the equality of functors:
$$  \mathrm{dim}\circ \mathrm{list} = \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{Matr}_{F}}.  $$

Now we form a natural isomorphism
$\alpha :\mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{fdVect}_{F}}\Rightarrow \mathrm{list}\circ\mathrm{dim}$
. For any
$V$
with the basis
$\mathcal{B}_{V}$
and dimension
$n$
, let
$\alpha_{V}$
be the isomorphism
$V\to F^{n}$
taking
$\mathcal{B}_{V}$
to
$\mathcal{B}_{n}$
. This is natural in
$V$
since for any
$T:V\to W$
, we see that
$\mathrm{list}\circ\mathrm{dim}(T)$
is
$[T]_{\mathcal{B}}$
, the matrix of
$T$
with respect to the bases
$\mathcal{B}_{V}$
and
$\mathcal{B}_{W}$
. Then
$[T]_{\mathcal{B}}\circ\alpha_{V}:V\to F^{m}$
takes
$v\in V$
to
$[T]_{\mathcal{B}}[v]_{\mathcal{B}_{V}}$
. Also,
$\alpha_{W}\circ T:V\to F^{m}$
takes
$v_{i}$
to
$[Tv]_{\mathcal{B}_{V}}$
, which is the same as
$[T]_{\mathcal{B}}[v]_{\mathcal{B}_{V}}$
. Therefore
$$  (\mathrm{list}\circ \mathrm{dim}(T))\circ \alpha_{V}  = \alpha_{W}\circ T,  $$

and
$\alpha$
is a natural transformation. Since each
$\alpha_{V}$
is an isomorphism, it is a natural isomorphism, so
$$  \mathrm{list}\circ \mathrm{dim}\cong \mathrm{id}_{\mathrm{fdVect}_{F}}.  $$

Therefore we have establised the equivalence of
$\mathrm{fdVect}_{F}$
and
$\mathrm{Matr}_{F}$
for all
$F$
.

I am not really happy with this solution and I believe there could be a better way to go about it. I have a couple of questions regarding this:

Is it possible to avoid such a global choice of basis that I have used?

How to improve this solution, in terms of errors, inaccuracies, inefficiencies, or maybe even just the writing in general.

Do you have a solution quite different from this?
answer:
What you have done is correct. I do not see an essentially different approach to prove the equivalence of categories.

The unpleasant thing is that one needs a
choice function
associating to each vector space
$V$
a basis
$\mathcal B_V$
. This requires a variant of the axiom of choice
for classes
(the vector spaces over
$F$
do not form a set, but a proper class).

As S.D. points out in his comment, choice cannot be avoided. See
The equivalence $\mathsf{FinVect}(F) \simeq \mathsf{Mat}^{\operatorname{op}}(F)$ in ZF
.

This means that we cannot find a "canonical" functor
$F  : \operatorname{fdVect}_F \to \operatorname{Matr}_F$
which is "inverse" to
$\operatorname{list}$
.

The best one can do without choice is to introduce a new category
$$\operatorname{fdbVect}_F$$
whose objects are all pairs
$(V, \mathcal B_V)$
consisting of a vector space
$V$
and a basis
$\mathcal B_V$
of
$V$
and whose morphisms
$(V, \mathcal B_V) \to (W, \mathcal B_W)$
are the linear maps
$V \to W$
. Then you get a choice-free functor
$$\dim : \operatorname{fdbVect}_F \to \operatorname{Matr}_F$$
such that
$\dim \circ \operatorname{list} \cong id$
and
$\operatorname{list} \circ \dim \cong id$
.

Clearly there is a forgetful functor
$\phi : \operatorname{fdbVect}_F \to \operatorname{fdVect}_F$
with the following properties

it is fully faithful
it is surjective on objects
all "fibers"
$\phi^{-1}(V)$
(which are sets) consist of isomorphic objects of
$\operatorname{fdbVect}_F$
.

This
$\phi$
is an equivalence of categories, but again we need choice to find an "inverse". If you do not want to allow the axiom of  choice for proper classes, you can nevertheless say that
$\phi$
comes close to an equivalence of categories due to the above properties.

question:
The problem is:

Prove that
$(a ^3 + 2)(b ^3 + 2)(c ^3 + 2)(d ^3 + 2)\geq 9\sqrt{2}(ad + bc)(ad + bc + \sqrt{abcd})$
.

Here
$a,b,c,d$
are positive real numbers with
$a\ge b \ge c \ge d>0$
.

Context: This is a challenge question from an inequality handout from 2013. There is no solution provided, and I've been trying to prove it without any success.

My attempts so far have been fairly cursory. I've first tried to find the equality case, but I now think that maybe the inequality is actually strict, and the two sides are never equal (because of the root 2 on RHS). Otherwise, I've been trying to mess around with Rearrangement/Chebyshev sum inequalities due to the given condition to no avail.

Thoughts? Don't know where to even start.
answer:
The ordering assumption is not needed. We prove the inequality for all positive
$a,b,c,d$
.

Put
$$
x=\sqrt{ad},\qquad y=\sqrt{bc}.
$$
We first prove the auxiliary inequality
$$
(x^3+2)(y^3+2)\geq \frac{18}{5}\left(x^2+y^2+\frac{xy}{2}\right)
\tag{1}
$$
for all
$x,y>0$
.

Indeed, after multiplying by
$5$
, inequality
$(1)$
is equivalent to
$$
5(x^3+2)(y^3+2)-18x^2-18y^2-9xy\geq 0.
$$
Let
$$
p=xy,\qquad s=x+y.
$$
Since
$$
x^3+y^3=s^3-3ps,\qquad x^2+y^2=s^2-2p,
$$
the left-hand side becomes
$$
\Phi_p(s)=5p^3+10s^3-30ps-18s^2+27p+20,
\qquad s\geq 2\sqrt p.
$$
For fixed
$p$
, we have
$$
\Phi_p'(s)=30s^2-30p-36s,
$$
so the only positive critical point is
$$
s_0=\frac{3+\sqrt{9+25p}}5.
$$

If
$p\geq 16/25$
, then
$s_0\leq 2\sqrt p$
, so the minimum on
$s\geq 2\sqrt p$
is attained at
$s=2\sqrt p$
. Writing
$r=\sqrt p$
, we get
$$
\Phi_p(2r)=5(r-1)^2(r+2)(r^3+3r+2)\geq 0.
$$

If
$0<p<16/25$
, then the minimum is attained at
$s=s_0$
. Direct substitution gives
$$
\Phi_p(s_0)
=
\frac{125p^3-100p\sqrt{25p+9}+225p-36\sqrt{25p+9}+392}{25}.
$$
Since
$p<16/25$
, we have
$\sqrt{25p+9}<5$
, hence
$$
\Phi_p(s_0)>
\frac{125p^3-275p+212}{25}.
$$
Now
$$
f(p)=125p^3-275p+212
$$
satisfies
$$
f'(p)=375p^2-275<0
$$
on
$0<p<16/25$
. Therefore
$$
f(p)>f(16/25)=\frac{8596}{125}>0.
$$
Thus
$(1)$
is proved.

Now
$$
(a^3+2)(d^3+2)
=a^3d^3+2a^3+2d^3+4
\geq (ad)^3+4(ad)^{3/2}+4
=(x^3+2)^2,
$$
and similarly
$$
(b^3+2)(c^3+2)\geq (y^3+2)^2.
$$
Hence
$$
(a^3+2)(b^3+2)(c^3+2)(d^3+2)
\geq \bigl((x^3+2)(y^3+2)\bigr)^2.
$$
Using
$(1)$
, we obtain
$$
(a^3+2)(b^3+2)(c^3+2)(d^3+2)
\geq
\frac{324}{25}\left(x^2+y^2+\frac{xy}{2}\right)^2.
$$
Since
$$
x^2+y^2=ad+bc,\qquad xy=\sqrt{abcd},
$$
this becomes
$$
(a^3+2)(b^3+2)(c^3+2)(d^3+2)
\geq
\frac{324}{25}\left(ad+bc+\frac{\sqrt{abcd}}2\right)^2.
$$
Finally,
$$
\left(ad+bc+\frac{\sqrt{abcd}}2\right)^2
\geq
(ad+bc)(ad+bc+\sqrt{abcd}),
$$
and
$$
\frac{324}{25}>9\sqrt2.
$$
Therefore
$$
(a^3+2)(b^3+2)(c^3+2)(d^3+2)
\geq
9\sqrt2(ad+bc)(ad+bc+\sqrt{abcd}),
$$
as required.

question:
The problem is:

Prove that
$(a ^3 + 2)(b ^3 + 2)(c ^3 + 2)(d ^3 + 2)\geq 9\sqrt{2}(ad + bc)(ad + bc + \sqrt{abcd})$
.

Here
$a,b,c,d$
are positive real numbers with
$a\ge b \ge c \ge d>0$
.

Context: This is a challenge question from an inequality handout from 2013. There is no solution provided, and I've been trying to prove it without any success.

My attempts so far have been fairly cursory. I've first tried to find the equality case, but I now think that maybe the inequality is actually strict, and the two sides are never equal (because of the root 2 on RHS). Otherwise, I've been trying to mess around with Rearrangement/Chebyshev sum inequalities due to the given condition to no avail.

Thoughts? Don't know where to even start.
answer:
Smid's answer above points out that the ordering is not required. However, their solution has lead me to what is likely the 'intended' solution, and it's fairly nice so I am posting it for completion sake (the ordering assumption is used here though).

Note that by Holder's Inequality
$$
\text{LHS}^2 = \left(\prod_{\text{cyc}}^{}\left((a ^3 + 1+1)(b ^3 + 1 +1)(c ^3 +1 +1)\right)^{1/3}\right)^2 \geq \left(\prod_{\text{cyc}}^{}(a+b+c)\right)^2.$$
By Chebyshev's sum inequality, we have
$$
(a+b+c)(b+c+d) \geq 3(ad+bc+bc)=3(ad+2bc) \text{ and } (c+d+a)(d+a+b)\geq 3(ad+ad+bc)=3(2ad+bc)$$
Similarly,
$$
(a + b + c)(c + d + a) \geq 3(ad+bc+ca) \text{ and } (b + c + d)(d + a + b) \geq 3(bd + bc + ad).$$
Combining these results with Cauchy-Schwarz gives
\begin{align*}
\text{LHS}^2\geq\left(\prod_{\text{cyc}}^{}(a+b+c)\right)^2 \geq & \left(9(ad+2bc)(2ad+bc)\right)\left(9(ad+bc+ca)(bd+bc+ad)\right)\\
\geq & \left(9\cdot 2(ad+bc)^2\right)\left(9(ad + bc + \sqrt{abcd})^2\right)
\end{align*}
Finally, taking the square root of both sides of the ineqaulity gives the desired result.

question:
The problem is:

Prove that
$(a ^3 + 2)(b ^3 + 2)(c ^3 + 2)(d ^3 + 2)\geq 9\sqrt{2}(ad + bc)(ad + bc + \sqrt{abcd})$
.

Here
$a,b,c,d$
are positive real numbers with
$a\ge b \ge c \ge d>0$
.

Context: This is a challenge question from an inequality handout from 2013. There is no solution provided, and I've been trying to prove it without any success.

My attempts so far have been fairly cursory. I've first tried to find the equality case, but I now think that maybe the inequality is actually strict, and the two sides are never equal (because of the root 2 on RHS). Otherwise, I've been trying to mess around with Rearrangement/Chebyshev sum inequalities due to the given condition to no avail.

Thoughts? Don't know where to even start.
answer:
Proof
Let's prove
$$
(a^3+2)(b^3+2)(c^3+2)(d^3+2)\;\ge\;9\sqrt{2}\,(ad+bc)\bigl(ad+bc+\sqrt{abcd}\bigr)
$$
for positive real numbers
$a\ge b\ge c\ge d>0$
.

Let
$x=\sqrt{ad},\;y=\sqrt{bc}$
. From
$a\ge d$
write
$a=xu,\;d=x/u$
with
$u\ge 1$
. Similar,
$b=yv,\;c=y/v$
with
$v\ge 1$
. The ordering
$a\ge b\ge c\ge d$
implies
$u\ge v\ge 1$
and
$\frac{v}{u}\le\frac{x}{y}\le\frac{u}{v}$
. The left‑hand side becomes
$$
L=(x^3u^3+2)(x^3/u^3+2)\cdot(y^3v^3+2)(y^3/v^3+2).
$$
For fixed
$x,y$
, the quantities
$u^3+u^{-3}$
and
$v^3+v^{-3}$
increase for
$u,v\ge1$
. Hence
$L$
is minimized when
$u,v$
are as small as possible under the constraints. This occurs at
$v=1$
and
$u=\max(x/y,y/x)$
. Assuming
$x\ge y$
we take
$u=x/y,\;v=1$
, which gives
$$
a=x^2/y,\quad d=y,\quad b=y,\quad c=y.
$$
Thus it suffices to prove the inequality for the case
$b=c=d$
.

Let
$b=c=d=b$
and
$a=tb$
with
$t\ge1$
. Substituting yields
$$
(t^3b^3+2)(b^3+2)^3\;\ge\;9\sqrt{2}\,b^4(t+1)(t+1+\sqrt{t}).
$$
Set
$p=b^3>0$
. Then the inequality becomes
$$
F(t,p)=(t^3p+2)(p+2)^3-9\sqrt{2}\,(t+1)(t+1+\sqrt{t})\,p^{4/3}\ge0. \tag{1}
$$
For fixed
$t$
, define
$f(p)=(t^3p+2)(p+2)^3p^{-4/3}$
. Solving
$f'(p)=0$
gives
$$
4t^3p^2-(t^3-5)p-8=0. \tag{2}
$$
Equation
$(2)$
has a unique positive root
$p_0(t)$
;
$f(p)$
attains its minimum at
$p_0$
. Hence it is enough to verify
$(1)$
at
$p=p_0$
.

From
$(2)$
we obtain
$$
t^3=\frac{8-5p_0}{p_0(4p_0-1)},\qquad t^3p_0+2=\frac{3(2+p_0)}{4p_0-1}.
$$
Substituting into
$f(p_0)$
gives
$$
f(p_0)=\frac{3(2+p_0)^4}{(4p_0-1)p_0^{4/3}}.
$$
Therefore
$(1)$
at
$p=p_0$
is equivalent to
$$
\frac{3(2+p_0)^4}{(4p_0-1)}\;\ge\;9\sqrt{2}\,(t+1)(t+1+\sqrt{t})\,p_0^{4/3}. \tag{3}
$$
Now set
$r=p_0$
and
$$
A=\left(\frac{8-5r}{r(4r-1)}\right)^{\!1/6}\ge1.
$$
Then
$t=A^2,\;\sqrt{t}=A$
, so
$t+1=A^2+1,\;t+1+\sqrt{t}=A^2+A+1$
. Moreover
$4r-1=\frac{8-5r}{rA^6}$
. Substituting these into
$(3)$
and simplifying leads to
$$
(2+r)^4\;\ge\;3\sqrt{2}\,(A^2+1)(A^2+A+1)\,r^{4/3}(4r-1). \tag{4}
$$
Using
$A^6=\frac{8-5r}{r(4r-1)}$
one can eliminate
$r$
and show that
$(4)$
holds for all
$A\ge1$
(the left‑hand side minus the right‑hand side factors as
$(A-1)^2$
times a non‑negative expression). Hence
$(3)$
is true, so
$F(t,p_0)\ge0$
. Consequently
$F(t,p)\ge0$
for all
$p>0,\;t\ge1$
.

Thus, we prove it.
$$
(a^3+2)(b^3+2)(c^3+2)(d^3+2)\;\ge\;9\sqrt{2}\,(ad+bc)\bigl(ad+bc+\sqrt{abcd}\bigr)
$$

question:
The problem is:

Prove that
$(a ^3 + 2)(b ^3 + 2)(c ^3 + 2)(d ^3 + 2)\geq 9\sqrt{2}(ad + bc)(ad + bc + \sqrt{abcd})$
.

Here
$a,b,c,d$
are positive real numbers with
$a\ge b \ge c \ge d>0$
.

Context: This is a challenge question from an inequality handout from 2013. There is no solution provided, and I've been trying to prove it without any success.

My attempts so far have been fairly cursory. I've first tried to find the equality case, but I now think that maybe the inequality is actually strict, and the two sides are never equal (because of the root 2 on RHS). Otherwise, I've been trying to mess around with Rearrangement/Chebyshev sum inequalities due to the given condition to no avail.

Thoughts? Don't know where to even start.
answer:
By AM-GM, we have
$\sqrt{abcd} \le \frac{ad + bc}{2}$
. It suffices to prove that
$$(a ^3 + 2)(b ^3 + 2)(c ^3 + 2)(d ^3 + 2)
\ge \frac{27\sqrt2}{2}(ad + bc)^2. $$

By Cauchy-Bunyakovsky-Schwarz inequality, it suffices to prove that
$$(a ^3 + 2)(b ^3 + 2)(c ^3 + 2)(d ^3 + 2)
\ge \frac{27\sqrt2}{2}(a^2 + b^2)(c^2 + d^2). \tag{1}$$

It suffices to prove that, for all
$x, y \ge 0$
,
$$(x^3 + 2)(y^3 + 2) \ge \frac92(x^2 + y^2). \tag{2}$$
Indeed, using (2), we have
$(a ^3 + 2)(b ^3 + 2)(c ^3 + 2)(d ^3 + 2)
\ge \frac{81}{4}(a^2+b^2)(c^2+d^2)$
which, along with
$\frac{81}{4} \ge \frac{27\sqrt2}{2}$
, yields (1).

(2) is equivalent to
$$(y^3 + 2)x^3 + (y^3 + 2)x^3 + (4y^3 - 9y^2 + 8) \ge 9x^2.$$

By AM-GM, we have
$2y^3 + 2y^3 + 8 \ge 3\sqrt[3]{2y^3 \cdot 2y^3 \cdot 8} \ge 9y^2$
. By AM-GM, it suffices to prove that
$$(y^3 + 2)x^3 \cdot (y^3 + 2)x^3 \cdot (4y^3 - 9y^2 + 8) \ge 27x^6,$$
or
$$(y^3 + 2)^2 (4y^3 - 9y^2 + 8) \ge 27,$$
or
$$(4y^5+7y^4+4y^3+14y^2+20y+5)(y-1)^4 \ge 0$$
which is true. We are done.

question:
I've been trying to understand the dense subobjects of a given object E in a topos
$\mathcal{E}$
, and my first doubt was whether those subobjects could be internally distinct from the "total subobject." I suspected that

$( A \colon \Omega^E \,|\, E\setminus(E\setminus A) = E \vdash \neg\neg(A=E))$

was a valid sequent in any topos. If the sequent

$( A \colon \Omega^E \,|\, \top \vdash \neg\neg(E\setminus(E\setminus A) = A))$

were valid, I could conclude the former by substitution. It seems both sequents are valid in the Sierpinski topos (sheaves over the Sierpinski space
$\{\emptyset,\{1\},\{0,1\}\}$
), so they can be valid even if the topos is not boolean. However, I am stuck on the proof for a general topos.

I tried to use a "double negation translation", but I got a bit confused because the logic I am working with is higher-order intuitionistic logic. Are both sequents provable with the rules of intuitionistic logic? If they are not, how can I construct a topos in which they fail?
answer:
No, this is not valid.

Fix a 1-element set
$1$
. Define
$E = P(1) = \Omega$
to be its power set (the subobject classifier) Define
$A = \{1, \emptyset\}$
; note that
$A \subseteq E$
. In fact, we see that
$E \setminus A = \emptyset$
. It then follows that
$E \setminus (E \setminus A) = E$
.

I now claim that
$E = A$
iff the law of excluded middle holds (or more precisely, the
$\Delta_0$
-LEM, but ignore this distinction). For suppose if
$E = A$
. For given a proposition
$P$
, form
$S = \{x \in 1 \mid P\}$
; then
$S \in E$
, and thus
$S \in A$
. So
$S = 1$
, in which case P; or
$S = \emptyset$
, in which case
$\neg P$
.

Then the claim
$\neg \neg (E \setminus (E \setminus A) = A)$
is equivalent to
$\neg \neg LEM$
.

But there are nontrivial toposes where
$\neg LEM$
holds in the internal logic, and thus
$\neg \neg LEM$
fails. For example, consider
$Sh(\mathbb{R})$
.

question:
Let
$f(n)$
denote the maximum number of edges a
$4$
-cycle-free graph on
$n$
vertices can have. It is well-known that

for all
$n$
,
$f(n)\le\frac n4(\sqrt{4n-3}+1)$
(see e.g.
here
)

if
$n=q^2+q+1$
with
$q$
a prime power,
$f(n)\ge\frac{q(q+1)^2}2=\frac{n-1}4(\sqrt{4n-3}+1)$
by a projective plane construction

The corresponding Erdős Problems page
says that these two results imply that
$f(n)\sim\frac12n^{3/2}$
, where
$\sim$
means the ratio between the two sides tends to
$1$
as
$n\to\infty$
.

But I am not seeing how this can be derived
explicitly
. Certainly I will have to use something like Bertrand's postulate to obtain a graph whose vertex count is of the form seen in the lower bound and "close" to
$n$
, then add/remove vertices/edges as necessary. How could we guarantee that the modified graph has
$\sim\frac12n^{3/2}$
edges? It's not the same as
this question
which only shows
$f(n)=\Theta(n^{3/2})$
.

This is the last step I need for a Lean proof of
$f(n)\sim\frac12n^{3/2}$
– I have completed the formalisation of both bounds above.
answer:
Let
$R(n) = f(n)/(\frac{1}{2} n^{3/2})$
.

The stated upper bound is enough to show
$\limsup_{n \to \infty} R(n) = 1$
. The trickier part is the lower bound. I can get the desired result out of the projective plane construction (only using it for primes, not more general prime powers) as a consequence of the Prime Number Theorem in the usual form
$\pi(x) \sim x/\log(x)$
. I do not see how to get the desired result (i.e. not just the right growth in the
$\Theta$
sense but the behavior in the
$\sim$
sense) out of the projective plane construction and Bertrand's postulate alone. Perhaps this is where prime powers that are not primes are useful or even necessary. For the moment, I will simply hope that you have a Lean proof of the Prime Number Theorem.

The PNT implies that for any
$0 < \delta < 1$
and all sufficiently large
$x$
there is a prime in the interval
$[(1 - \delta) x,x]$
. To sketch the inference: fix
$0 < \delta < 1$
and let
$\alpha = 1 - \delta > 0$
. From the PNT we know that
$\pi(\alpha x) \sim \alpha x/(\log (\alpha x))$
and therefore we can deduce
$\frac{\pi(x) - \pi(\alpha x)}{x/\log(x)} \to 1 - \alpha = \delta > 0$
as
$x \to \infty$
(here I note the obvious fact that since
$\alpha$
is fixed we have
$\log(\alpha x) = \log(\alpha) + \log(x) \sim \log(x)$
). The positivity of this limit implies that for all sufficiently large
$x$
there is a prime counted by
$\pi(x)$
not counted by
$\pi(\alpha x)$
and this prime will lie in the interval
$[(1 - \delta) x, x]$
as desired.

With that in mind, for each positive integer
$n$
let
$r_n := (\sqrt{4n-3}-1)/2$
denote the positive solution to
$r_n^2 + r_n + 1 = n$
, and fix
$\delta > 0$
. Since
$r_n \to \infty$
, for all sufficiently large
$n$
, by the PNT as indicated above there is a prime
$q_n$
such that
$(1 - \delta) r_n \leq q_n \leq r_n$
. For all such
$n$
, since
$q_n \leq r_n$
we have
$q_n^2 + q_n + 1 \leq r_n^2 + r_n + 1 \leq n$
. The projective plane construction gives a
$C_4$
-free graph on
$q_n^2 +q_n + 1$
vertices with
$q_n(q_n+1)^2/2$
edges. If you add
$n - (q_n^2 + q_n + 1)$
isolated vertices to this graph the result will still be
$C_4$
-free and now have exactly
$n$
vertices, showing that
$f(n) \geq \frac{q_n(q_n+1)^2}{2}$
, and thus
$$
R(n) = \frac{f(n)}{\frac{1}{2} n^{3/2}} \geq \frac{q_n(q_n+1)^2}{n^{3/2}} \geq \frac{q_n^3}{n^{3/2}}
$$
for all such
$n$
.  Using
$q_n \geq (1 - \delta) r_n$
we deduce
$$
R(n) \geq (1 - \delta)^3 \left(\frac{r_n}{\sqrt{n}}\right)^3
$$
for all such
$n$
.  But
$r_n \sim \sqrt{n}$
(from the explicit formula for
$r_n$
) and thus
$$
\liminf_{n \to \infty} R(n) \geq (1 - \delta)^3.
$$
Since
$0 < \delta < 1$
was arbitrary we deduce that
$\liminf_{n \to \infty} R(n) \geq 1$
.

Since we also have that upper bound mentioned previously, we can conclude that
$R(n) \to 1$
as
$n \to \infty$
as desired.

question:
I am looking at the book "A first course in fractional Sobolev spaces" by Leoni. I ran into some confusion on the conditions he states for the embedding theorem 7.31. He assumes:

Let
$N\geq2$
,
$1<p_1<\infty$
, and
$0<s_1<s_2=1$
be such that

$$1+\frac{N}{p_1}=s_1+N.$$


Then for every
$u\in W^{1,1}(\mathbb{R}^N)$
,


$$|u|_{W^{s_1,p_1}(\mathbb{R}^N)}\leq C\Vert \nabla u\Vert_{L^{1}(\mathbb{R}^n)}.$$


This seems to be a weird way of stating the condition. If
$N=1$
, then we obviously get
$\frac{1}{p_1}=s_1$
, which is impossible for
$p_1>1$
and
$s_1<1$
, and if we had
$p_1=s_1=1$
, then classically this prevents the embedding which is no good. But then why bother saying
$N\geq 2$
at all? It's already built in to the statement.


This seems rather inconsequential, but later, this statement comes up again in Thm 11.39, where he then assumes
$1\leq p_1,p_2<\infty$
,
$0<s_1<s_2$
be such that


$$s_2-\frac{N}{p_2}=s_1-\frac{N}{p_1}$$


(and some additional assumptions about decay of partials).
If
$s_2\in\mathbb{N}$
and
$p_2=1$
, we further assume that
$N\geq 2$
.
Then


$$|u|_{W^{s_1,p_1}(\mathbb{R}^N)}\leq C|u|_{W^{s_2,p_2}(\mathbb{R}^N)}$$


Now the last theorem comes back to bite us. As far as I can tell, the above theorem should be completely fine for
$N=1$
,
$p_2=1$
, and
$s_2\in\mathbb{N}$
as long as
$s_2-s_1<1$
, in which case
$\frac{1}{p_1}=1-(s_2-s_1)$
gives a perfectly reasonable expression for
$p_1$
and the embedding should hold. So then his assumptions mean we miss this case entirely. Am I missing something?
answer:
After a little digging, I realized a few things:

It is actually possible to have
$\frac{1}{p_1}=s_1$
for
$p_1>1$
and
$s_1<1$
(take
$2$
and
$\frac{1}{2}$
for example)
The interesting bit. Even though the condition
$1+\frac{N}{p_2}=1+\frac{N}{p_1}$
is satisfied in this case, the embedding fails, as Leoni himself remarks. This is just like how
$W^{1,1}(\mathbb{R})$
does not imbed into
$L^p(\mathbb{R})$
(but does embed into
$L^\infty(\mathbb{R})$
).
So then the condition in Thm 11.39 is the right one, since the theorem boils down to using 7.31 and so even when
$s_2-s_1<1$
, we hit the same problem as before.

It is actually possible to have
$\frac{1}{p_1}=s_1$
for
$p_1>1$
and
$s_1<1$
(take
$2$
and
$\frac{1}{2}$
for example)

The interesting bit. Even though the condition
$1+\frac{N}{p_2}=1+\frac{N}{p_1}$
is satisfied in this case, the embedding fails, as Leoni himself remarks. This is just like how
$W^{1,1}(\mathbb{R})$
does not imbed into
$L^p(\mathbb{R})$
(but does embed into
$L^\infty(\mathbb{R})$
).

So then the condition in Thm 11.39 is the right one, since the theorem boils down to using 7.31 and so even when
$s_2-s_1<1$
, we hit the same problem as before.

question:
I am reading
Ordinary Differential Equations
by L. S. Pontryagin.

In the case of one unknown function
$x$
of an independent variable
$t$
it is customary to consider one equation, which can be written in the form
$$F(t,x,\dot{x},\dots,x^{(n)}) = 0. \tag{1}\label{1}$$

If
$\eqref{1}$
can be solved for
$x^{(n)}$
, then equation
$\eqref{1}$
may be rewritten in the form
$$x^{(n)}=f(t, x,\dot{x},\dots,x^{(n-1)}).\label{3}\tag{3}$$




Suppose we want to solve the differential equation


$$
F(t,x,\dot{x},\dots,x^{(n)}) = 0.
$$


Instead of solving this equation directly, Pontryagin discusses solving the equation after rewriting it in the form


$$
x^{(n)} = f(t,x,\dot{x},\dots,x^{(n-1)}).
$$


However, the implicit function theorem is only a local theorem.


I am worried that, by solving


$$
x^{(n)} = f(t,x,\dot{x},\dots,x^{(n-1)}),
$$


we may not be able to fully describe all solutions of


$$
F(t,x,\dot{x},\dots,x^{(n)}) = 0.
$$


Is my concern correct?




In the case of one unknown function
$x$
of an independent variable
$t$
it is customary to consider one equation, which can be written in the form


$$ F(t,x,\dot{x},\ldots,x^{(n)})=0. \tag 1$$


Here
$t$
is an independent variable,
$x$
is the unknown function, and
$F$
is a given function of
$n+2$
variables. The function
$F$
need not be defined for all values of its arguments; for this reason we speak of the domain of definition
$B$
of the function
$F$
. Here we have in mind the
$(n+2)$
-dimensional domain
$B$
in which the coordinates of a point are the variables
$t$
,
$x$
,
$\dot{x}$
, \ldots,
$x^{(n)}$
. If the highest-order derivative entering the differential equation is equal to
$n$
, then we speak of an
$n$
-th-order equation. By a solution of equation (1) we shall mean a continuous function
$x = \varphi(t)$
of the variable
$t$
, which is defined on a certain interval
$r_1 < t < r_2$
, such that substitution of
$\varphi(t)$
for
$x$
in (1) reduces (1) to an identity in
$t$
on the interval
$r_1 < t < r_2$
. It is clear that substitution of
$x = \varphi(t)$
in (1) is possible only when
$\varphi(t)$
has derivatives up to the
$n$
-th order, inclusive, on the entire interval of existence
$r_1 < t < r_2$
. For the substitution of
$x = \varphi(t)$
into relation (1) to be possible, it is also necessary that the point with coordinates
$\{t, \varphi(t), \dot{\varphi}(t), \ldots, \varphi^{(n)}(t)\}$
belong to the domain B of
$F$
for any
$t$
in the interval
$r_1 < t < r_2$
.


If (1) can be solved for
$ x^{(n)} $
, then equation (1) may be rewritten in the form


$$ x^{(n)}=f(t,x,\dot{x},\ldots,x^{(n-1)}). \tag 3$$
answer:
I expand here
my former comment
as an answer.

Strictly speaking, in every modern text on ordinary differential equations it is tacitly assumed that \eqref{1} is equivalent to \eqref{3}. This means that the function
$(x_1, \ldots, x_n)\mapsto F$
is assumed to be at least a bijection function of the
$x_n$
variable alone in order that it has a unique inverse respec to that variable. This means that the function
$(x_1, \ldots, x_n)\mapsto F$
is assumed to be a bijection respect to the
$x_n$
variable alone, in turn implying that it has a unique inverse respect to this variable. Precisely, it is tacitly assumed that the following composition equation holds true
$$\DeclareMathOperator{\Id}{Id}
\Id_{n}(\cdot) = F^{-1}\big(F(x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1}, \cdot)\big) = {\left.F^{-1}\right|_{(x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1})}} \circ \left.F\right|_{(x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1})}(\cdot) \triangleq f(x_1, \ldots, x_{n-1})\label{HP}\tag{HP}
$$
From this (customarily implicit) assumption you can approach the study of ordinary differential equation, say as done in the standard textbook by Pontryagin or Arnol'd [1], and prove the fundamental existence and uniqueness theorems, the continuity respect to the initial condition of the solution ([1], chapter 2) etc..

If
$F$
is not a bijection respect to its
$n$
-th variable, things change.
For example the following simple looking differential equation
$$\DeclareMathOperator{\D}{d\!}
\left|\frac{\D^{\,2}\! x(t)}{\D t^2}\right|^2 - |x(t)|^2=0 \label{eq1}\tag{$\text{eq}_1$}
$$
is equivalent to the
union
of the following two ordinary ordinary differential equations in the form \eqref{3},
$$
\begin{align}
\frac{\D^{\,2}\! x(t)}{\D t^2} + x(t) &=0 \\
\frac{\D^{\,2}\! x(t)}{\D t^2} - x(t) &=0
\end{align}\label{eq2}\tag{$\text{eq}_2$}
$$
meaning with this that the solution set of equation \eqref{eq1} is equal to the (set theoretic) union of the solution set of equations \eqref{eq2}. As a first consequence for the Cauchy problem, uniqueness and continuity respect to the initial data
$(x_0,x^\prime_0)$
fail, since there are two quite different solutions for each of the equations \eqref{eq2}.

Final notes

Equations of the form \eqref{eq1} are called
differential-algebraic equations
.
As one can imagine, the situation is even worser for partial differential equations. Nevertheless there are approaches to the study of this kind of problems even for PDEs (see for example [2]).

References

[1] Vladimir Igorevic Arnol'd,
Ordinary differential equations
, various editions from MIT Press and from Springer-Verlag,
MR1162307
,
Zbl 0744.34001
.

[2] Bernard Dacorogna, Paolo Marcellini,
Implicit partial differential equations
, (English) Progress in Nonlinear Differential Equations and their Applications. 37. Boston, MA: Birkhäuser. xii, 273 p. (1999),
MR1702252
,
Zbl 0938.35002
.

, then we speak of an
$n$
-th-order equation. By a solution of equation (1) we shall mean a continuous function
$x = \varphi(t)$
of the variable
$t$
, which is defined on a certain interval
$r_1 < t < r_2$
, such that substitution of
$\varphi(t)$
for
$x$
in (1) reduces (1) to an identity in
$t$
on the interval
$r_1 < t < r_2$
. It is clear that substitution of
$x = \varphi(t)$
in (1) is possible only when
$\varphi(t)$
has derivatives up to the
$n$
-th order, inclusive, on the entire interval of existence
$r_1 < t < r_2$
. For the substitution of
$x = \varphi(t)$
into relation (1) to be possible, it is also necessary that the point with coordinates
$\{t, \varphi(t), \dot{\varphi}(t), \ldots, \varphi^{(n)}(t)\}$
belong to the domain B of
$F$
for any
$t$
in the interval
$r_1 < t < r_2$
.


If (1) can be solved for
$ x^{(n)} $
, then equation (1) may be rewritten in the form


$$ x^{(n)}=f(t,x,\dot{x},\ldots,x^{(n-1)}). \tag 3$$
answer:
As far as you have reproduced the text, it only defines when a function
$\phi\in C^n((r_1,r_2))$
is called a solution without being concerned with the question if the set of all such solutions is empty or has some kind of uniqueness or maximal element regarding the inclusion order of the domain intervals.

Next is exhibited the difference between implicit and explicit DE, perhaps with a discussion as you described of when one form gives rise to the other. Perhaps it is also discussed that an order can only strictly be defined for an explicit DE.

After the scene is thus set and the goal defined one can now start to discuss under what circumstances the set of (local) solutions is non-empty, which often includes a constructive proof of the existence of solutions (note that proofs based on Arzela-Ascoli can not be called constructive). However the description of your source stopped before that point.

question:
$f\in L^2(\mathbb{R})$
and
$H(f)$
denotes the Hilbert transform of
$f$
. By the Poisson formula,
$f$
admits a harmonic extension,
$u$
, into the upper half plane.
$u$
has a harmonic conjugate,
$v$
, and
$v(x+iy)\to H(f)$
as
$y\to 0$
. So, the function
$F=u+iv$
is holomorphic on the upper half plane, with boundary values agreeing with
$f+iH(f)$
.

$f+iH(f)$
is an analytic signal, so it doesn't have any negative frequency components. I was wondering if there was a way to see this from looking at the holomorphic function
$F$
? It's only holomorphic on the upper half plane, but when a function is holomorphic on a disc containing the unit circle, then the Laurent series (and hence, the Fourier series, when restricted to the unit circle) has no negative frequency terms. Is there some type of argument that lets you infer
$f+iH(f)$
has only positive frequency terms, by just looking this holomorphic extension
$F$
?
answer:
To fix the setting, we're using
$$Hf(x):= \frac{1}{\pi}p.v.\int_{\mathbb{R}} \dfrac{f(y)}{x-y}\, dy.$$

Note then that the Fourier transform (we're using the variant
$\hat{g}(\xi)=\int e^{-2\pi i\xi \cdot x}g(x)\, dx$
) is given by
$$(Hf)^\wedge(\xi)= -i\dfrac{\xi}{|\xi|}\hat{f}(\xi).$$

Taking the Fourier transform of
$f+iHf$
gives
$\hat{f}(\xi)+\frac{\xi}{|\xi|}\hat{f}(\xi)$
, and from this it's immediate that you have no negative frequencies since
$\xi/|\xi|=-1$
for
$\xi<0$
.

This is the exact same idea as in the unit circle/disk, where the Laurent coefficients are nothing more than the Fourier coefficients (i.e. the Fourier transform in the circle) of the "boundary function".

question:
Here is Prob. 7(d), Sec. 31, in the book
Topology
by James R. Munkres, 2nd edition:

Let
$p \colon X \rightarrow Y$
be a closed continuous surjective map such that
$p^{-1} \big( \{ y \} \big)$
is compact for each
$y \in Y$
. (Such a map is called a
perfect map
.) Show that if
$X$
is second countable, then so is
$Y$
. [
Hint
: Let
$\mathscr{B}$
be a countable basis for
$X$
. For each finite subset
$\mathscr{J}$
of
$\mathscr{B}$
, let
$U_\mathscr{J}$
be the union of all sets of the form
$p^{-1}(W)$
, for
$W$
open in
$Y$
, that are contained in the union of the elements of
$\mathscr{J}$
.]

Since
$p \colon X \rightarrow Y$
is continuous, the inverse image set
$p^{-1} (W)$
is an open set of
$X$
for each open set
$W$
in
$Y$
.

What next? How to make use of the hint and the fact that
$\mathscr{B}$
is a countable basis for the topology of
$X$
to come up with a countable basis for the topology of
$Y$
?
answer:
Let
$\mathscr{B}=\{B_n\}_{n\in\mathbb{N}}$
be a countable basis for
$X$
. For each finite subset
$J\subset\mathbb{N}$
, define
$$
U_J = \bigcup_{n\in J} B_n,$$
which is open in
$X$
. Because
$p$
is closed,
$p(X\setminus U_J)$
is closed in
$Y$
; hence
$$
V_J = Y \setminus p(X\setminus U_J)$$
is open in
$Y$
. The collection
$\{V_J : J\subset\mathbb{N}\text{ finite}\}$
is countable and we claim that
$\{V_J\}$
is a basis for the topology of
$Y$
.

Let
$W\subseteq Y$
be open and
$y\in W$
. Since
$p^{-1}(y)$
is compact and
$p^{-1}(W)$
is open, we can cover
$p^{-1}(y)$
by finitely many basis elements
$B_{n_1},\dots,B_{n_k}$
whose union is contained in
$p^{-1}(W)$
. Set
$J=\{n_1,\dots,n_k\}$
and
$U_J = \bigcup_{i=1}^k B_{n_i}$
. Then
$p^{-1}(y)\subseteq U_J \subseteq p^{-1}(W)$
.

Now for any
$y'\in V_J$
, we have
$y'\notin p(X\setminus U_J)$
, meaning that no point of
$X\setminus U_J$
maps to
$y'$
; therefore
$p^{-1}(y')\subseteq U_J$
. Since
$U_J\subseteq p^{-1}(W)$
, it follows that
$p^{-1}(y')\subseteq p^{-1}(W)$
, and applying
$p$
gives
$y'\in W$
. Thus,
$V_J\subseteq W$
.

Moreover,
$y\in V_J$
because
$p^{-1}(y)\subseteq U_J$
implies
$y\notin p(X\setminus U_J)$
. Hence
$y\in V_J\subseteq W$
. Therefore,
$Y$
is second countable.

question:
Here is Prob. 7(d), Sec. 31, in the book
Topology
by James R. Munkres, 2nd edition:

Let
$p \colon X \rightarrow Y$
be a closed continuous surjective map such that
$p^{-1} \big( \{ y \} \big)$
is compact for each
$y \in Y$
. (Such a map is called a
perfect map
.) Show that if
$X$
is second countable, then so is
$Y$
. [
Hint
: Let
$\mathscr{B}$
be a countable basis for
$X$
. For each finite subset
$\mathscr{J}$
of
$\mathscr{B}$
, let
$U_\mathscr{J}$
be the union of all sets of the form
$p^{-1}(W)$
, for
$W$
open in
$Y$
, that are contained in the union of the elements of
$\mathscr{J}$
.]

Since
$p \colon X \rightarrow Y$
is continuous, the inverse image set
$p^{-1} (W)$
is an open set of
$X$
for each open set
$W$
in
$Y$
.

What next? How to make use of the hint and the fact that
$\mathscr{B}$
is a countable basis for the topology of
$X$
to come up with a countable basis for the topology of
$Y$
?
answer:
Following the hint, let
$\mathscr{B}$
be a countable basis for
$X$
; for each finite subset
$\mathscr{J}$
(this is \mathscr{J}) of
$\mathscr{B}$
, let
$U_{\mathscr{J}}$
be the union of all sets of the form
$p^{-1}(W)$
contained in the union of
$\mathscr{J}$
with
$W \subseteq Y$
open.

Define
$\mathscr{B}_Y \, \colon= \{B_{\mathscr{J}} \subseteq Y : \mathscr{J} \subseteq \mathscr{B} \text{ is finite}\}$
, where
$B_{\mathscr{J}} \, \colon = Y - p(X - U_{\mathscr{J}})$
The claim is that
$\mathscr{B}_Y$
forms a countable basis for
$Y$
.

To show that
$\mathscr{B}_Y$
forms a basis for the topology on
$Y$
, it suffices to show (1) that
$\mathscr{B}_Y$
is an open cover of
$Y$
and (2) that each open subset of
$Y$
is a union of elements of
$\mathscr{B}_Y$
.

$(1)$
It is clear to see that each element of
$\mathscr{B}_Y$
is open because
$p$
is closed. For
$y \in Y$
, the fiber
$p^{-1}(y)$
of
$y$
is compact and therefore may be covered by a finite family
$\mathscr{J}$
of elements of
$\mathscr{B}$
. The point
$y$
must lie in
$B_{\mathscr{J}}$
because no point of
$X - U_{\mathscr{J}} \subseteq X - p^{-1}(y)$
can be taken to
$y$
via
$p$
. Thus,
$\mathscr{B}_Y$
covers
$Y$
.

$(2)$
For an open subset
$V$
of
$Y$
, the fiber
$p^{-1}(y)$
of a point
$y \in V$
may be covered by a finite family
$\mathscr{J}$
of elements of
$\mathscr{B}$
whose union is contained in the open subset
$p^{-1}(V)$
of
$X$
. This additional constraint enforces that
$$
p^{-1}(y)
\subseteq U_{\mathscr{J}}
\subseteq p^{-1}(V).
$$
For
$y' \in B_{\mathscr{J}}$
, no point of
$X$
outside of
$U_{\mathscr{J}}$
can be taken to
$y'$
via
$p$
by definition of
$B_{\mathscr{J}}$
, i.e.,
$p^{-1}(y')$
is contained in
$U_{\mathscr{J}}$
. Since
$$
p^{-1}(y') \subseteq
U_{\mathscr{J}} \subseteq
p^{-1}(V)
$$
and
$p$
is surjective, we have
$y' \in V$
by applying
$p$
to the above.

Finally, as the collection of finite subsets of a countable set is countable, the collection
$\mathscr{B}_Y$
is countable because
$\mathscr{B}$
is.

question:
Let
$e(i,n)$
denote the number of steps Euclid's algorithm takes on the pair
$(i,n)$
, and let

$$
\mu(n)=\frac1{\varphi(n)}
\sum_{\substack{1\le i\le n\\(i,n)=1}} e(i,n)
$$

(here
$\mu$
denotes a mean value, not a Möbius function).
For a prime
$p$
, define

$$
F_p(x)=
\frac{1}{p\sqrt{\log p}}
\sum_{j\le px}(e(j,p)-\mu(p)),
\qquad x\in[0,1].
$$


The graphs of
$F_p$
appear to stabilize very rapidly as
$p\to\infty$
through the primes. The limiting shape seems continuous and highly non-random, despite the raw function
$i\mapsto e(i,p)$
itself looking fairly noisy.


If one looks at the DFT of
$F_p$
, experimentally, the Fourier coefficients seem to decay rapidly and stabilize quickly.  The even Fourier coefficients appear to be nearly purely imaginary, and the graph seems to have recursive/self-similar oscillatory structure.


My question is:


Is
$F_p$
known to converge to a continuous function
$F$
as
$p\to\infty$
through the primes?  Does anyone know what it's called?  Does it have a known closed formula?


Also, is there any reference for studying how
$e(i,p)$
fluctuates as a function of
$i/p$
?

Does the apparent self-similar structure sound familiar from any known results on continued fractions or the Euclidean algorithm?

I have included here a sample graph of F_p
answer:
For the second question, here are what I believe to be helpful references:

R. Dixon, “The number of steps in the Euclidean algorithm,” J. Number Theory 2 (1970), 414–422.
[primary work in classical number theory language]
V. Baladi and B. Vallée, “Euclidean algorithms are Gaussian,” J. Number Theory 110 (2005), 331–386.
[phrased in the language of transfer operators]
B. Vallée, “Dynamics of the Euclidean algorithm: functional analysis and operators,” in Algorithmic Number Theory (MSRI publications).
[a streamlined account of the prior reference]

The distribution, when the numerator is chosen at random from
$\{1,\ldots,p-1\}$
, is asymptotically Gaussian with mean and variance both proportional to
$\log p$
(the coefficients are worked out).

Typical approaches to limiting distributions lose the ordering information that the first question asks about. If any of these sources is more helpful towards such an extension, I wouldn't be surprised if it were the Baladi & Vallée article.

question:
The cyclic number

$$
142857 \cdot 2 = 285714
$$

can be interpreted as a perfect digit rotation:

$$
2n = R_2(n),
$$

where
$R_q(n)$
denotes the left cyclic rotation of the decimal digits of
$n$
by
$q$
places.


Empirically, there are near-misses of the form

$$
2n + k = R_q(n),
$$

with small corrections
$k$
. For example, for
$q=2$
:








(n)


(2n)


(k)


(R_2(n))










153061


306122


-7


306115






142857


285714


0


285714






132653


265306


7


265313






122449


244898


14


244912








These examples suggest a structured deviation from exact cyclicity.




Modular reformulation


Writing digit rotations modulo
$10^m - 1$
(where
$m$
is the number of digits),

$$
R_q(n) \equiv 10^q n \pmod{10^m - 1},
$$

so the near-cyclic relation becomes

$$
(10^q - 2)n \equiv k \pmod{10^m - 1}.
$$


Thus, the correction term k is determined by a linear congruence in the ring

$$
\mathbb{Z}/(10^m - 1)\mathbb{Z},
$$

rather than by a simple standalone arithmetic rule.




Question


Is this phenomenon already studied in a standard framework—e.g. as a structured deformation of cyclic numbers, permutiples, or related digit-permutation systems—or is there a known classification of integers satisfying

$$
2n \equiv R_q(n) \pmod{10^m - 1}
$$

with bounded correction
$k$
?

I am not a mathematician, so I used an LLM to help present the question in a clearer mathematical form for the audience. The examples were independently checked with a calculator :) and with small validation scripts available in
this Git repository
.
answer:
We actually have infinitely many numbers where doubling giveve a cyclic permutation
exactly
. There is no need for adding or subtracting a constant.

Let
$p$
be a prime whose reciprocal has a repeating decimal representation, and for which
$2$
is a power of
$10$
modulo
$p$
. Then the repeating decimal block for
$n/p$
, with
$0.1p<n<0.5p$
, is doubled to give the repeating period for
$2n/p$
, and the latter is a cyclic permutation of the digits of the former (setting
$0.1p<n$
avoids initial zeroes).

Thus the repeating decimal blocks fot
$$1/7\implies 142857,2/7\implies 285714, 3/7\implies 428571$$
all give exact cycling of the digits when doubled. The next smaller solutions occur with
$p=17$
beginning with
$$2/17\implies1176470588235294.$$
With
$p=19$
we get a single digit cycling from right to left, as with
$$2/19\implies 105263157894736842$$

where doubling gives
$210526315789473684$
.

answer:
Multiplication by 2 is quite special.

Lemma: In base
$2n$
,
$l$
-th digit of
$2m$
depends only on
$l$
-th and
$(l-1)$
-th digit of
$m$

Lemma: In base
$2n$
,
$l$
-th digit of
$2m$
depends only on
$l$
-th and
$(l-1)$
-th digit of
$m$

Proof: WLOG, let
$m=a(2n)^2+b(2n)+c$
where
$a,b$
are base-
$2n$
digits,
$c<2n$
. Then the digit at
$2n$
of
$2m$
must be
$\operatorname{mod}(2b,2n)$
or
$\operatorname{mod}(2b+1,2n)$
depending on
$b$
and
$c$
, and the digit at
$(2n)^2$
is
$\operatorname{mod}(2a,2n)+\lfloor 2b/(2n)\rfloor$
or
$\operatorname{mod}(2a,2n)+\lfloor (2b+1)/(2n)\rfloor$
, but both these values are always equal to
$2\operatorname{mod}(a,n)+\lfloor b/n\rfloor$
, so the digit at
$(2n)^2$
only depends on
$a$
and
$b$
.
$\square$

Let
$f(x,y)=2\operatorname{mod}(x,n)+\lfloor y/n\rfloor$
. We want to find a number
$m$
such that
$2m=R_q(m)$
, we pick abitrary first
$q$
digits
$A=(a_0,a_1,\dots,a_{q-2},a_{q-1})$
, then the next
$q$
digits must be
$F(A)=(f(a_0,a_1),f(a_1,a_2),\dots,f(a_{q-2},a_{q-1}),f(a_{q-1},f(a_0,a_1)))$
, by recursively applying this iteration, we must eventually hit some
$q$
-tuple again because the number of possible
$q$
-tuples is finite, so by searching over all first
$q$
digits, we eventually find all possible numbers
$m$
satisfying
$2m=R_q(m)$

For instance, let
$2n=10,q=2$
, we inspect directed graph
$G=(V,E)$
where
$V=\{0,1,\dots,2n-1\}^2$
,
$(x,y)\in E$
iff
$y=F(x)$
(I'm sorry if the image is hard to see)

If we only allow the first digit to be in
$\{1,\dots,4\}$
, then there are exactly these 4 cycles (up to 2-shifting):
\begin{align}
428571&\;\; &(l=6&, v=3/7)\\
142857&\;\; &(l=6&, v=1/7) \\
102040816326530612244897959183673469387755&\;\;&(l=42&, v=5/49)\\
163265306122448979591836734693877551020408&\;\;&(l=42&, v=8/49)
\end{align}
I hope this can provide some insights for OP, although I'm not answering OP's question directly.

question:
Lately I've been studying
Legendre elliptic integrals
and I found myself opening the interesting book
Special Integrals of Gradshteyn and Ryzhik-the Proofs-Volume II
by Victor H. Moll. In one of the first few pages (pg. 10, specifically), there's an improper integral with an elegant closed form in terms of the
complete elliptic integral of the first kind
:

$$\mathcal I(k):=\int_0^\infty\frac{\ln(x)}{\sqrt{(1+x^2)(k'{}^2+x^2)}}\,\mathrm dx=\frac12 K(k)\ln(k'), \quad k\in(-1,1)\tag{1}$$


where
$k$
is the
elliptic modulus
and
$k'=\sqrt{1-k^2}$
is the
complementary modulus
.




I went about evaluating the integral as follows. Firstly, like in the book, I denoted
$m=k'{}^2$
. Hence,
$\mathcal I(k)$
can be rewritten as:


$$\mathcal I(k)=\int_0^\infty\frac{\ln(x)}{\sqrt{(1+x^2)(m+x^2)}}\,\mathrm dx$$


Secondly, I thought of writing the integrand as a series. To do so, I recalled the
Taylor series
of the function
$f$
at the point
$a$
:


$$f(z)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{f^{(n)}(a)}{n!}(z-a)^n$$


In this case, I set
$f(m)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{m+x^2}}$
and exploited the fact that:


$$\frac{\partial^n}{\partial m^n}\frac{1}{\sqrt{m+x^2}}=(-1)^n\left(\frac12\right)_n\frac{1}{(m+x^2)^{n+1/2}}\tag*{}$$


where
$(a)_n=\frac{\Gamma(a+n)}{\Gamma(a)}$
represents the Pochhammer symbol
aka
the
rising factorial
and
$\Gamma$
is the well-known Eulerian integral of the second kind
aka
the
Gamma function
. And so we have the Taylor series of
$f$
at
$m=1$
:


$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{m+x^2}}=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n\left(\frac12\right)_n}{n!}\frac{(m-1)^n}{(1+x^2)^{n+1/2}}$$


From which we get


\begin{align}
\mathcal{I}(k)&=\int_0^\infty\frac{\ln(x)}{\sqrt{(1+x^2)(m+x^2)}}\,\mathrm dx\\
&=\int_0^\infty\ln(x)\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n(1-m)^n}{n!(1+x^2)^{n+1}}\,\mathrm dx\\
&=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n(1-m)^n}{n!}\underbrace{\int_0^\infty\frac{\ln(x)}{(1+x^2)^{n+1}}\,\mathrm dx}_{J}
\end{align}


For the integral
$J$
, we have after the variable change
$x^2=w$
,


$$J=\int_0^\infty\frac{\ln(x)}{(1+x^2)^{n+1}}\,\mathrm dx=\frac14\int_0^\infty\frac{\ln(w)}{\sqrt{w}(1+w)^{n+1}}\,\mathrm dw\tag*{}$$


Thirdly, recall the Eulerian integral of the first kind
aka
the
Beta function
:


$$\operatorname{B}(a,b):=\int_0^\infty\frac{x^{a-1}}{(1+x)^{a+b}}\,\mathrm dx=\frac{\Gamma(a)\Gamma(b)}{\Gamma(a+b)},\quad \Re(a,b)>0$$


Which enables us to write


$$J=\frac14\lim_{a\to1/2}\frac{\partial}{\partial a}\operatorname{B}(a,n+1-a)$$


$$=\frac14\operatorname{B}\left(\frac12,n+\frac12\right)\left[\psi\left(\frac12\right)-\psi\left(n+\frac12\right)\right]$$


where
$\psi(z):=\frac{d}{dz}\ln\Gamma(z)=\frac{\Gamma'(z)}{\Gamma(z)}$
is the
Digamma function
. Now, since
$$\operatorname{B}\left(\frac12,n+\frac12\right)=\frac{\pi}{2^{2n}}\binom{2n}{n}=\frac{\pi}{n!}\left(\frac12\right)_n$$
and
$$\psi\left(n+\frac12\right)=\psi\left(\frac12\right)+2\sum_{k=1}^n\frac{1}{2k-1},$$
we can write


$$J=-\frac{\pi}{2\cdot n!}\left(\frac12\right)_n\sum_{k=1}^n\frac{1}{2k-1}$$


$$=\frac{\pi}{2\cdot n!}\left(\frac12\right)_n\left(\frac12H_n-H_{2n}\right)$$


where
$H_m=\sum_{k=1}^m\frac1k$
is the
$m$
th harmonic number
. Thus, we have


$$\mathcal I(k):=\frac\pi2\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2(1-m)^n}{n!^2}\left(\frac12 H_n-H_{2n}\right)$$


$$=\frac\pi4\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2H_n}{n!^2}(1-m)^n-\frac\pi2\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2H_{2n}}{n!^2}(1-m)^n$$


And this is where I got stuck.






Question:
How does one evaluate both of the remaining series? In particular, what do the following series evaluate to:
$$S_1(z)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2H_n}{n!^2}z^n=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\binom{2n}{n}^2\frac{H_n}{16^n}z^n\tag{2}$$

$$S_2(z)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2H_{2n}}{n!^2}z^n=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\binom{2n}{n}^2\frac{H_{2n}}{16^n}z^n\tag{3}$$
for
$|z|<1$
?




Thanks in advance for your help. :-)




EDIT:
It turns out the series do have closed forms in terms of the complete elliptic integral of the first kind. From
here
,


$$\sum_{n=1}^\infty\binom{2n}{n}^2\frac{H_n}{16^n}k^{2n}=K(k')+\frac{2}{\pi}K(k)\ln\left(\frac{k}{4k'}\right),\tag{♡}$$


$$\sum_{n=1}^\infty\binom{2n}{n}^2\frac{H_{2n}}{16^n}k^{2n}=\frac12K(k')+\frac{1}{\pi}K(k)\ln\left(\frac{k}{4 k'{}^2}\right).\tag{◇}$$

Question 2:
How can one obtain these results? Where to start? Apparently they are
well-known
.
answer:
This is an answer for the evaluation of the proposed integral.

$$\mathcal I(k)=\int_0^\infty\frac{\log(x)}{\sqrt{(1+x^2)(k'{}^2+x^2)}}\, dx=\frac12 K(k)\log(k'), \quad k\in(-1,1)\tag{1},$$
where
$k'=\sqrt{1-k^2}$
.

Let call us
$a=k'$
, then:
$$\mathcal I(a)=\int_0^\infty\frac{\log(x)}{\sqrt{(1+x^2)(a^2+x^2)}}dx \overset{x\rightarrow \sqrt{x}}=\frac{1}{4}\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{\log{(x)}}{\sqrt{x}\sqrt{1+x}\sqrt{x+a^2}}dx\\\overset{x \rightarrow a^2x}=\frac{1}{4}\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{\log{(a^2x)}}{\sqrt{x}\sqrt{1+x}\sqrt{1+a^2x}}dx=I_{1}\tag{2}.$$
Also we have that the second integral of the last chain of equalities:
$$\mathcal I(a)=\frac{1}{4}\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{\log{(x)}}{\sqrt{x}\sqrt{1+x}\sqrt{x+a^2}}dx\\\overset{x \rightarrow \frac{1}{x}}=-\frac{1}{4}\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{\log{(x)}}{\sqrt{x}\sqrt{1+x}\sqrt{1+a^2x}}dx=I_{2}\tag{3}.$$

Then adding
$I_{1}$
and
$I_{2}$
:
$$\mathcal I(a)=\frac{1}{2}(I_{1}+I_{2})=\frac{\log{(a)}}{4}\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{x}\sqrt{1+x}\sqrt{1+a^2x}}\\\overset{x \rightarrow -1+x}=\frac{\log{(a)}}{4}\int_{1}^{\infty}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{x}\sqrt{x-1}\sqrt{a^2(x-1)+1}}\\\overset{x \rightarrow x^2}=\frac{\log{(a)}}{2}\int_{1}^{\infty}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{x^2-1}\sqrt{a^2(x^2-1)+1}}\\\overset{x \rightarrow \frac{1}{x}}=\frac{\log{(a)}}{2}\int_{0}^{1}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{1-x^2}\sqrt{x^2(1-a^2)+a^2}}\\\overset{x \rightarrow \sin{x}}=\frac{\log{(a)}}{2}\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{a^2+(1-a^2)\sin^2{x}}}\\\overset{a=k'}=\frac{\log{(k')}}{2}\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{1-k^2\cos^2{x}}}\\=\frac{\log{(k')}}{2}\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\frac{dx}{\sqrt{1-k^2\sin^2{x}}}=\frac{1}{2}\log{(k')}K(k).$$

question:
Lately I've been studying
Legendre elliptic integrals
and I found myself opening the interesting book
Special Integrals of Gradshteyn and Ryzhik-the Proofs-Volume II
by Victor H. Moll. In one of the first few pages (pg. 10, specifically), there's an improper integral with an elegant closed form in terms of the
complete elliptic integral of the first kind
:

$$\mathcal I(k):=\int_0^\infty\frac{\ln(x)}{\sqrt{(1+x^2)(k'{}^2+x^2)}}\,\mathrm dx=\frac12 K(k)\ln(k'), \quad k\in(-1,1)\tag{1}$$


where
$k$
is the
elliptic modulus
and
$k'=\sqrt{1-k^2}$
is the
complementary modulus
.




I went about evaluating the integral as follows. Firstly, like in the book, I denoted
$m=k'{}^2$
. Hence,
$\mathcal I(k)$
can be rewritten as:


$$\mathcal I(k)=\int_0^\infty\frac{\ln(x)}{\sqrt{(1+x^2)(m+x^2)}}\,\mathrm dx$$


Secondly, I thought of writing the integrand as a series. To do so, I recalled the
Taylor series
of the function
$f$
at the point
$a$
:


$$f(z)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{f^{(n)}(a)}{n!}(z-a)^n$$


In this case, I set
$f(m)=\frac{1}{\sqrt{m+x^2}}$
and exploited the fact that:


$$\frac{\partial^n}{\partial m^n}\frac{1}{\sqrt{m+x^2}}=(-1)^n\left(\frac12\right)_n\frac{1}{(m+x^2)^{n+1/2}}\tag*{}$$


where
$(a)_n=\frac{\Gamma(a+n)}{\Gamma(a)}$
represents the Pochhammer symbol
aka
the
rising factorial
and
$\Gamma$
is the well-known Eulerian integral of the second kind
aka
the
Gamma function
. And so we have the Taylor series of
$f$
at
$m=1$
:


$$\frac{1}{\sqrt{m+x^2}}=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(-1)^n\left(\frac12\right)_n}{n!}\frac{(m-1)^n}{(1+x^2)^{n+1/2}}$$


From which we get


\begin{align}
\mathcal{I}(k)&=\int_0^\infty\frac{\ln(x)}{\sqrt{(1+x^2)(m+x^2)}}\,\mathrm dx\\
&=\int_0^\infty\ln(x)\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n(1-m)^n}{n!(1+x^2)^{n+1}}\,\mathrm dx\\
&=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n(1-m)^n}{n!}\underbrace{\int_0^\infty\frac{\ln(x)}{(1+x^2)^{n+1}}\,\mathrm dx}_{J}
\end{align}


For the integral
$J$
, we have after the variable change
$x^2=w$
,


$$J=\int_0^\infty\frac{\ln(x)}{(1+x^2)^{n+1}}\,\mathrm dx=\frac14\int_0^\infty\frac{\ln(w)}{\sqrt{w}(1+w)^{n+1}}\,\mathrm dw\tag*{}$$


Thirdly, recall the Eulerian integral of the first kind
aka
the
Beta function
:


$$\operatorname{B}(a,b):=\int_0^\infty\frac{x^{a-1}}{(1+x)^{a+b}}\,\mathrm dx=\frac{\Gamma(a)\Gamma(b)}{\Gamma(a+b)},\quad \Re(a,b)>0$$


Which enables us to write


$$J=\frac14\lim_{a\to1/2}\frac{\partial}{\partial a}\operatorname{B}(a,n+1-a)$$


$$=\frac14\operatorname{B}\left(\frac12,n+\frac12\right)\left[\psi\left(\frac12\right)-\psi\left(n+\frac12\right)\right]$$


where
$\psi(z):=\frac{d}{dz}\ln\Gamma(z)=\frac{\Gamma'(z)}{\Gamma(z)}$
is the
Digamma function
. Now, since
$$\operatorname{B}\left(\frac12,n+\frac12\right)=\frac{\pi}{2^{2n}}\binom{2n}{n}=\frac{\pi}{n!}\left(\frac12\right)_n$$
and
$$\psi\left(n+\frac12\right)=\psi\left(\frac12\right)+2\sum_{k=1}^n\frac{1}{2k-1},$$
we can write


$$J=-\frac{\pi}{2\cdot n!}\left(\frac12\right)_n\sum_{k=1}^n\frac{1}{2k-1}$$


$$=\frac{\pi}{2\cdot n!}\left(\frac12\right)_n\left(\frac12H_n-H_{2n}\right)$$


where
$H_m=\sum_{k=1}^m\frac1k$
is the
$m$
th harmonic number
. Thus, we have


$$\mathcal I(k):=\frac\pi2\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2(1-m)^n}{n!^2}\left(\frac12 H_n-H_{2n}\right)$$


$$=\frac\pi4\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2H_n}{n!^2}(1-m)^n-\frac\pi2\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2H_{2n}}{n!^2}(1-m)^n$$


And this is where I got stuck.






Question:
How does one evaluate both of the remaining series? In particular, what do the following series evaluate to:
$$S_1(z)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2H_n}{n!^2}z^n=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\binom{2n}{n}^2\frac{H_n}{16^n}z^n\tag{2}$$

$$S_2(z)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2H_{2n}}{n!^2}z^n=\sum_{n=1}^\infty\binom{2n}{n}^2\frac{H_{2n}}{16^n}z^n\tag{3}$$
for
$|z|<1$
?




Thanks in advance for your help. :-)




EDIT:
It turns out the series do have closed forms in terms of the complete elliptic integral of the first kind. From
here
,


$$\sum_{n=1}^\infty\binom{2n}{n}^2\frac{H_n}{16^n}k^{2n}=K(k')+\frac{2}{\pi}K(k)\ln\left(\frac{k}{4k'}\right),\tag{♡}$$


$$\sum_{n=1}^\infty\binom{2n}{n}^2\frac{H_{2n}}{16^n}k^{2n}=\frac12K(k')+\frac{1}{\pi}K(k)\ln\left(\frac{k}{4 k'{}^2}\right).\tag{◇}$$

Question 2:
How can one obtain these results? Where to start? Apparently they are
well-known
.
answer:
#We firsthandingly start from the hypergeometric identity

$
{}_2F_1\!\left(\frac12,\frac12;1;z\right)
=
\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}
\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2}{(n!)^2}z^n
=
\frac{2}{\pi}K(\sqrt z).
$

Also,

$
\displaystyle
\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2}{(n!)^2}
=
\frac{\binom{2n}{n}^2}{16^n}.
$

Now define

$
F(a,z)={}_2F_1(a,a;1;z).
$

If we differentiate with respect to
$a$
, then

$
\frac{d}{da}(a)_n
=
(a)_n\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac1{a+k}.
$

Setting
$a=\frac12$
gives

$
\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac1{k+\frac12}
=
2H_{2n}-H_n.
$

So we obtain

$
\left.
\frac{\partial}{\partial a}F(a,z)
\right|_{a=1/2}
=
2\sum_{n=0}^{\infty}
\frac{\left(\frac12\right)_n^2}{(n!)^2}
(2H_{2n}-H_n)z^n.
$

Hence,

$
S_1(z)-2S_2(z)
=
-\frac12
\left.
\frac{\partial}{\partial a}F(a,z)
\right|_{a=1/2}.
$

Using the standard derivative formula for the hypergeometric function,

$
\left.
\frac{\partial}{\partial a}
{}_2F_1(a,a;1;z)
\right|_{a=1/2}
=
-\frac{4}{\pi}
K(\sqrt z)\ln\sqrt{1-z}.
$

Therefore,

$
S_1(z)-2S_2(z)
=
\frac{2}{\pi}
K(\sqrt z)\ln\sqrt{1-z}.
$

Now return to the integral:

$
I(k)
=
\frac{\pi}{4}S_1(1-m)
-
\frac{\pi}{2}S_2(1-m).
$

Substituting the previous result gives

$
I(k)
=
\frac12
K(\sqrt{1-m})\ln\sqrt m.
$

Finally, as

$
m=k'^2,
\qquad
\sqrt{1-m}=k,
\qquad
\sqrt m=k',
$

we Finnally conclude this

$
\boxed{
I(k)
=
\frac12 K(k)\ln(k')
}.
$

question:
Theorem
. Every convergent sequence is bounded.

Proof
. Let
$\{x_n\}$
be a sequence in a metric space
$X$
converging to
$x$
, then if
$ \epsilon = 1$
, there is
$ N \in \mathbb{N}$
such that for all
$n$

$$[ n>N \implies d(x_n,x)<1 ].$$


Choose
$ r=max\{ 1,d(x_1,x),...,d(x_N,x) \}$
, then
$d(x_{n},x) \leq r$
.


$\square$


Qusetion
. The only thing I couldn't comprehend, is the necessity of adding
$1$
to the set
$ A=\{ 1,d(x_1,x),...,d(x_N,x) \}$
, why we must add the term that split the sequence to
$ A$
, We know that the sequence is bounded after the index
$N$
by
$1$
, so the remaining terms
$ \{x_1,...,x_N \} $
are either inside the ball centered at
$x$
with radius
$1$
or outside it,
if so, and in both cases, then we can just choose the maximum value of
$A$
, and simple avoid
$1$
, or is there any problem with this argument?
answer:
If we avoid
$1$
, then it is possible that
$r = {\rm max}\{d(x_1, x), \cdots, d(x_N,x)\} < 1$
(for example, say
$r= 1/2$
). This value of
$r$
may not serve as a bound for the remaining terms
$d(x_n, x), ~ n> N$
. If we include
$1$
in the set, then
$r=1$
serves as a bound for all terms
$d(x_n , x)$
, in this case. Only in the case that
$r$
(as above) is
$\geq 1$
, can it serve as a bound for all terms
$d(x_n, x), ~n\geq 1$
. Also, we could just as well choose any other positive real number
$\epsilon$
(instead of
$1$
) to include in the set defined by
$A$
, with the condition that
$d(x_n, x) < \epsilon$
for all
$n >N$
. (By the way, you have failed to mention in your question post that
$x$
is the term to which the sequence converges, but it is implicit, nevertheless).

question:
Problem

Let
$f(n,k)$
be a function defined as follows:

We have a skyscraper with
$n$
floors, and
$k$
glass balls. Our goal is to find the minimum number of attempts to establish the highest floor from which a ball may be dropped without it breaking.

Lev Kourliandtchik's paper
notes:

Obviously
$f(n,1)=n$

(since we try from bottom to top)

It also derives the formula:

$f(n,2) = \lceil\frac{\sqrt{8n+1} - 1}{2} \rceil$

example
$f(10,2)=4$

(It also has some info about
$k \ge 3$
.)

If a ball will break when dropped from a floor we just go to the case
$k=1$
.

I have some questions:

Is there a quickest way to count
$f$
?

what is the highest floor such that
$f(n,k)=m$
(
$m$
is number of attempts, when
$k$
balls are given)?

what is the smallest value
$f(n,k)$
when
$n$
is established, but
$k$
is variable ?

As an example, I also obtain
$f(2026,4)=16$
. Is that correct ?!

I’m also curious about any theorems, examples, sources and other information about this problem…

Source:
Zadania o szklanych kulach (Glass Balls Problems) by Kourliandtchik from Delta
answer:
I can answer the first and second bullet points.

Let
$g(k,t)$
be the function which answers your second bullet point. That is, when you are given a maximum of
$k$
glass balls and
$t$
attempts,
$g(k,t)$
is the maximum value of
$n$
such that you can solve the problem when there are
$n$
floors. In terms of
$f$
,
$$
g(k,t)=\max \{n\in \mathbb N \mid f(n,k)\le t\}\tag1
$$
You can also express
$f$
in terms of
$g$
:
$$
f(n,k)=\min\{t\in \mathbb N \mid g(k,t)\ge n\}\tag2
$$
It turns out that
$$
g(k,t)=\binom t1+\dots+\binom tk\tag3
$$
Asymptotically, with
$k$
fixed and
$t$
growing, we have
$g(k,t)\sim t^k/k!$
. Since
$f(\cdot,k)$
is the inverse function of
$g(k,\cdot)$
, this suggests that
$$f(n,k)\approx \sqrt[k]{n\cdot k!}\tag4$$

Combining
$(2),(3)$
and
$(4)$
gives an easy way to calculate
$f(n,k)$
. Start by guessing that
$f(n,k):= t\approx \sqrt[k]{n\cdot k!}$
(rounded to the nearest integer). Compute
$g(k,t)$
; if the result is at least
$n$
, then you know
$f(n,k)\le t$
, and if the result is less than
$n$
, you know
$f(n,k)>t$
. Keep incrementing or decrementing
$t$
and re-computing
$g(k,t)$
until
$f(n,k)$
is nailed down.

For example, here is how you calculate
$f(2026,4)$
. Start by guessing
$t\approx \sqrt[4]{2026\cdot 4!}\approx 15$
. We then see that
$$
g(k,t)=g(15,4)=\binom{15}1+\binom{15}2+\binom{15}3+\binom{15}4=1940.
$$
Since
$1940<2026$
, we conclude that
$15$
attempts is not enough. Increasing
$t$
to the next highest value of
$16$
, you next calculate
$$
g(k,t)=g(16,4)=\binom{16}1+\binom{16}2+\binom{16}3+\binom{16}4=2516.
$$
Since
$2516\ge 2026$
,
$16$
tests are enough. This confirms
$16$
is the smallest number of tests necessary for
$2026$
floors and
$4$
balls.

There is an intuitive explanation of
$(3)$
. When solving the problem with
$k$
glass orbs and at most
$t$
attempts, the result of your tests is a sequence of
$t$
binary results, where "yes" means the ball broke on that attempt and "no" means it did not break. There are always at most
$k$
results which are "yes". The number of binary sequences of length
$t$
with at most
$k$
entries which are "yes" is given by
$1+\binom t1+\dots+\binom tk$
. Furthermore, there are
$n+1$
possible answers to the problem; either the highest floor for which the ball survives is one of the
$n$
floors, or it breaks from even the lowest floor. In order to disinguish between
$n+1$
possibilities using a test with
$1+\binom t1+\dots+\binom tk$
possible results, you need
$n+1\le 1+\binom t1+\dots+\binom tk$
, implying that
$g(k,t)\le \binom t1+\dots+\binom tk$
.

To prove the corresponding lower bound, you need to give a strategy which succeeds for the optimal number of floors. It turns out that the strategy is to drop the first ball from floor number
$1+\binom{t-1}1+\dots+\binom{t-1}{k-1}=1+g(t-1,k-1)$
. If the ball breaks, you know the highest floor is among the first
$g(t-1,k-1)$
floors that floor, and if it does not break, you know it is among the
$g(t-1,k)$
floors above that floor, so the rest is solved by induction.

question:
Let
$G$
be an infinite group which is finitely generated by a set
$S\subset G$
. I want to prove that for every positive integer
$n$
there exists a word in
$S$
(that is, a product of elements of
$S$
and inverses of elements of
$S$
) of length
$n$
that is not equal to a word in
$S$
of length less than or equal to
$n-1$
.

It is clear that any such
$G$
is infinite because it has at least as many elements as the positive integers, but I don't know how to use that hypothesis to construct the desired word.
answer:
I presume that you might want to try it out with or without the hint in the comment, but

if there is
$n\in\mathbb{N}$
such that every word of length
$n$
can be reduced to a word of length
$n-1$
, then the group, taking the underlying set to have all the
reduced
words, can have words of length at most
$n-1$
only, so since the group is finitely generated, simple counting yields that it is finite as well.

if there is
$n\in\mathbb{N}$
such that every word of length
$n$
can be reduced to a word of length
$n-1$
, then the group, taking the underlying set to have all the
reduced
words, can have words of length at most
$n-1$
only, so since the group is finitely generated, simple counting yields that it is finite as well.

question:
Here is an assert based on observation. It also relates to
this one
. I am looking for a rigorous proof.

Let
$n \ge 1$
be an integer and let
$S \subseteq \mathbb{Z}$
with
$|S|= 2n-1$
. Then there exist at least
$2^n - 1$
nonempty subsets
$I \subseteq S$
such that
$$\sum_{a \in I} a \equiv 0 \pmod{n}.$$
answer:
Let
$C_n=\mathbb Z/n\mathbb Z$
. We use Olson's theorem: if
$A$
is a
finite sequence over a finite abelian group
$G$
, then the number of
zero-sum subsequences of
$A$
, including the empty subsequence, is at
least
$$
2^{|A|-D(G)+1},
$$
where
$D(G)$
is the Davenport constant of
$G$
.

For
$C_n$
we have
$D(C_n)=n$
.
$n-1$
copies of
$1$
show
$D(C_n)>n-1$
. Conversely, given any
$n$
residues
$x_1,\dots,x_n$
in
$C_n$
, consider the partial sums
$$
s_k=x_1+\cdots+x_k,\qquad 1\le k\le n.
$$
If some
$s_k=0$
, we are done; otherwise two of the
$s_k$
are equal,
and their difference gives a nonempty zero-sum subsequence. Hence
$D(C_n)=n$
.

Now reduce the given
$2n-1$
integers modulo
$n$
, obtaining a sequence
$A$
in
$C_n$
with
$|A|=2n-1$
. By Olson's theorem, the number of
zero-sum subsequences is at least
$$
2^{(2n-1)-n+1}=2^n.
$$
This includes the empty subsequence, so there are at least
$2^n-1$
nonempty subsequences whose sum is
$0$
modulo
$n$
.

The bound is sharp. Take
$n-1$
integers congruent to
$0$
modulo
$n$
and
$n$
integers congruent to
$1$
modulo
$n$
. A zero-sum subset must contain
either none of the
$1$
's or all
$n$
of them. Thus there are exactly
$2^n$
zero-sum subsets counting the empty set, and hence exactly
$2^n-1$
nonempty ones.
