Improve this question

I encountered this claim in a paper and cannot get it: "Assuming CH, every maximal Hardy field is isomorphic to
$(\bf{No}(\omega_1), \partial_{\omega_1})$
"

I cannot get how it could be true.
This paper
is cited for this result.

As I understand it, any germ(growth rate) of a real function at infinity is mapped to countable surreals ad vice versa.

There are no countable surreals beyond those germs.

But what if we allow a generalization of the Hardy fields to include functions with countably-infinite derivatives or polynomials with coefficients in countable surreals? Would not they be greater than the germs of all real functions, yet will be countable surreals themselves?

I was also thinking about the theory of numerosity. It is a measure of subsets of reals satisfying the inclusion-exclusion principle, also known as Euclid's principle and surreal-valued.

Any surreal-valued element of a Hardy field can be mapped to a sequence of reals by dividing the derivative of the corresponding germ into blocks of area 1 and putting
$a_k$
into the center of mass of that segment, thus the germ becomes a smoothed counting function for that sequence.

But then what about a numerosity of a dense set, such as rationals or dyadic rationals? According to the Euclid's principle they are greater than any Hardy field, but at the same time smaller than the numerosity of reals. This contradicts the claim that there are no countabvle surreals that are greater than all elements of a maximal Hardy field.

The surreals are said to be a maximal ordered field, so the numerosity of rationals should belong there. But if it is greater than any Hardy field element, so not countable, what it should be?... Uncountable? Numerosity of a countable set is uncountable? This makes no sense.

P.S. A more clear re-formulation of the central argument by AI:

The Hardy Field Equivalence:
By the theorem, the maximal Hardy field
$\mathcal{H}$
is isomorphic to
$\mathbf{No}(\omega_1)$
. This means every growth rate (germ) in
$\mathcal{H}$
corresponds to a "countable surreal" (a surreal born before stage
$\omega_1$
).

Your Construction:
For any given germ
$f \in \mathcal{H}$
(which corresponds to some countable surreal
$s$
), we can find a discrete subset
$S \subset \mathbb{Q}$
whose counting function outgrows
$f$
. Because
$\mathbb{Q}$
is dense, we can pack as many points of
$S$
as we want into any interval to make its counting function arbitrarily large, while
$S$
remains discrete.

Euclid's Principle:
According to the theory of numerosity, if
$S \subset \mathbb{Q}$
, then
$\mathfrak{n}(S) < \mathfrak{n}(\mathbb{Q})$
.

The Conclusion:
Since we can construct a discrete
$S \subset \mathbb{Q}$
to beat
any
given germ in the Hardy field,
$\mathfrak{n}(\mathbb{Q})$
must be strictly greater than the numerosity of
every
such sequence. Therefore,
$\mathfrak{n}(\mathbb{Q})$
is strictly greater than every element in
$\mathbf{No}(\omega_1)$
.

PP.S. The crux of this issue has been clarified in the
discussion with the AI here
(the first few replies are the most important), where it comes to a conclusion that the numerosity of rationals is, indeed, uncountable, this would make a huge break of numerosity theory from cardinality (it is more natural to assume that sets with countable numerosity have countable cardinality).
answer:
The AI has led you down a weird rabbit hole, which is ultimately unrelated to the issue and so I'm going to ignore it
(hm, if only there were a general lesson here ...)
. Consider the following much simpler version of this confusion:

"
Somebody
has said that every countable dense linear order is isomorphic to
$\mathbb{Q}$
. But how can that be true? Consider the countable dense linear order
$I=(0,1)\cap\mathbb{Q}$
; there is a rational bigger than every element of
$I$
, so how can
$I$
be isomorphic to
$\mathbb{Q}$
?"

The fact that there is a rational bigger than every element of
$I$
just tells you that
the inclusion map
$I\hookrightarrow\mathbb{Q}$
is not an isomorphism of linear orderings. It does not tell you that there is
no isomorphism whatsoever
. Indeed, there is (more than) one, although it's not quite trivial to describe in a snappy way.

Exactly the same thing is going on here. Numerosity provides
one example of a map between
certain fields, which indeed is not always an isomorphism. However, this doesn't have any bearing on the truth of the (correct) statement that you are concerned about (whose proof, incidentally, is similar to that of the simpler claim I've brought up above).

question:
Let
$\{f_n\}_{n=1}^\infty$
be a sequence of random variables on the probability space
$(X,\mathscr{F},P)$
. The tail
$\sigma$
-algebra is defined as
$$\mathscr{G}=\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty\sigma(f_n,f_{n+1},\cdots)=\bigcap_{n=1}^\infty\sigma\left(\bigcup_{k=n}^\infty f_k^{-1}(\mathscr{B}_\mathbb{R})\right),$$
where
$\mathscr{B}_\mathbb{R}$
is the Borel set. The Kolmogorov's zero-one law says that if all of the random variables are independent, then the probability of any event in
$\mathscr{G}$
is either
$0$
or
$1$
.


But what's the intuition behind the definition of this tail
$\sigma$
-algebra? How did Kolmogorov find the expression for
$\mathscr{G}$
? This mathematical formula feels like it comes out of nowhere to me. Roughly speaking, I understand that Kolmogorov's zero-one law wants to state a fact that in a stochastic system governed by an infinite sequence of independent factors, those characteristics that depend solely on long-term aggregate behavior and remain unaffected by any finite number of local elements are, in essence, not random at all. This implies that the expression for
$\mathscr{G}$
should not depend on any finite number of random variables, so I think it would make sense that the expression should look like the following
$$\lim_{n\to\infty}h(f_n,f_{n+1},\cdots),$$
where
$h(f_n,f_{n+1},\cdots)$
represents an expression that depends only on random variables after
$n$
. But why must
$$h(f_n,f_{n+1},\cdots)=\sigma\left(\bigcup_{k=n}^\infty f_k^{-1}(\mathscr{B}_\mathbb{R})\right)?$$
Why shouldn't it be something like a ring generated by
$\displaystyle\bigcup_{k=n}^\infty f_k^{-1}(\mathscr{B}_\mathbb{R})$
, a
$\lambda$
system generated by
$\displaystyle\bigcap_{k=n}^\infty f_k^{-1}(\mathscr{B}_\mathbb{R})$
, or something crazier and more complicated? On the other hand, it seems hard for me to imagine what kind of elements should be in this tail
$\sigma$
-algebra, i.e., I don't understand what this formula is trying to describe. I believe there should be some derivations for this formula, or some intuitive explanations, or at least some examples that implies this expression. Any help will be appreciated.
answer:
As you suggest, we are looking at events that are determined by the random variables
$f_1, f_2, f_3, \dots$
, but which are unaffected by changing any finite number of them. Such an event is in
$\sigma(f_1, f_2, f_3, \dots)$
. But since we may ignore
$f_1$
, it is also in
$\sigma(f_2, f_3, f_4, \dots)$
. Similarly it is in
$\sigma(f_3, f_4, f_5, \dots)$
. In fact, it is in
$\sigma(f_n, f_{n+1}, f_{n+2},\dots)$
for every
$n$
. In other words, it is in
$\mathcal{G}:=\bigcap_{n\geq 1} \sigma(f_n, f_{n+1}, f_{n+2}, \dots)$
. And that is the definition of the tail sigma-algebra.

Examples of events that are in the tail sigma-algebra:

the event that the series
$f_1+f_2+f_3+\dots$
converges;
the event that the sequence
$f_1, f_2, f_3, \dots$
contains infinitely many terms which are equal to
$0$
;
the event that
$f_n<f_{n+1}$
for all sufficiently large
$n$
.

Example of events that are not in the tail sigma-algebra:

the event that the series
$f_1+f_2+f_3+\dots$
converges to
$1$
;
the event that the sequence
$f_1, f_2, f_3, \dots$
contains infinitely many terms which are equal to
$f_1$
;
the event that
$f_n<f_{n+1}$
for all
$n$
.

question:
This is the problem in question from Mac Lane's
"Categories for the Working Mathematician"
, chapter 2, section 4, exercise 1:

For
$R$
a ring, describe
$R\mbox{-}\mathbf{Mod}$
as a full subcategory of the functor category
$\mathbf{Ab}^R$
.

My question is,
what is this functor category
$\mathbf{Ab}^R$
?

So far in the book I do not think a ring has been defined or interpreted as a category in itself, so how can one have functors from
$R$
to
$\mathbf{Ab}$
? The only thing I can currently think of in this direction is interpreting
$R$
as a monoid in two ways (the abelian group and the multiplication monoid, since Mac Lane requires rings to be unital and homomoprhisms to preserve identities, viz. chapter 1, end of section 2, p.12), so then we can require any functor from
$R$
to be a functor both from the abelian group
$R$
and the monoid
$R$
. The issue with this (possibly questionable) interpretation is that it is quite non-trivial and far-fetched.

So what could Mac Lane be meaning by this?
answer:
Usually, a monoid is interpreted as a one object category where the endomorphisms of that one object are that monoid itself, with the composition rule being given by the multiplication of the monoid.

Now, a preadditive category is a category where the morphisms between any two objects are enriched with an abelian group structure, in a way that composition is bilinear.

Maybe you can see where this is going. Using this, you can view a ring
$R$
as a preadditive category with one object, so you can not only compose (multiply) endomorphisms but also add them. Call this category
$R$
as well for convenience.

It is easy to define a reasonable preadditive structure on
$\mathbf{Ab}$
, you add your homomorphisms pointwise.

Now, for the full subcategory. A (left)
$R$
-module structure on an abelian group
$M$
is normally defined by giving a ring homomorphism
$R\to \operatorname{End}_\mathbb{Z}(M)$
. If you simply take a random functor from
$R$
to
$\mathbf{Ab}$
, it will only preserve the multiplicative structure. So, you restrict yourself to the functors
$F$
which also preserve the addition, i.e.,
$F(f+g)=F(f)+F(g)$
. In other words, functors such that the induced maps
$\operatorname{End}_R(*)\to \operatorname{End}_{\mathbf{Ab}}(M)$
are group homomorphisms.

These are more generally known as additive functors, and if you restrict to the (full) subcategory of additive functors in
$\mathbf{Ab}^R$
, you will precisely recover your modules.

question:
This is the problem in question from Mac Lane's
"Categories for the Working Mathematician"
, chapter 2, section 4, exercise 1:

For
$R$
a ring, describe
$R\mbox{-}\mathbf{Mod}$
as a full subcategory of the functor category
$\mathbf{Ab}^R$
.

My question is,
what is this functor category
$\mathbf{Ab}^R$
?

So far in the book I do not think a ring has been defined or interpreted as a category in itself, so how can one have functors from
$R$
to
$\mathbf{Ab}$
? The only thing I can currently think of in this direction is interpreting
$R$
as a monoid in two ways (the abelian group and the multiplication monoid, since Mac Lane requires rings to be unital and homomoprhisms to preserve identities, viz. chapter 1, end of section 2, p.12), so then we can require any functor from
$R$
to be a functor both from the abelian group
$R$
and the monoid
$R$
. The issue with this (possibly questionable) interpretation is that it is quite non-trivial and far-fetched.

So what could Mac Lane be meaning by this?
answer:
Mac Lane is not explicit how to regard a ring a category, but the only reasonable interpretation is to take
$R$
as the multiplication monoid (see p.11 how to regard it as a category).

Actually each functor
$A : R \to \mathbf{Ab}$
is determined by an abelian group
$A= A(*)$
where
$*$
is the single object of the category
$R$
and homomorphisms
$A(r) : A \to A$
for each
$r \in R$
. Now define a multiplication on
$A$
by
$$r \cdot a = A(r)(a) .$$

It is easy to verfiy that
$A$
becomes an
$R$
-module with this multiplication
provided
$A(r_1 + r_2) = A(r_1) + A(r_2)$
for all
$r_1, r_2 \in R$
.

Conversely, given an
$R$
-module
$A$
, we can define a functor
$T : R \to \mathbf{Ab}$
via
$$T(*) = A \text{ regarded as an abelian group}, \\
T(r)(a) = r \cdot a .$$
We then have
$T(r_1 + r_2) = T(r_1) + T(r_2)$
for all
$r_1, r_2 \in R$
.

question:
This is the problem in question from Mac Lane's
"Categories for the Working Mathematician"
, chapter 2, section 4, exercise 1:

For
$R$
a ring, describe
$R\mbox{-}\mathbf{Mod}$
as a full subcategory of the functor category
$\mathbf{Ab}^R$
.

My question is,
what is this functor category
$\mathbf{Ab}^R$
?

So far in the book I do not think a ring has been defined or interpreted as a category in itself, so how can one have functors from
$R$
to
$\mathbf{Ab}$
? The only thing I can currently think of in this direction is interpreting
$R$
as a monoid in two ways (the abelian group and the multiplication monoid, since Mac Lane requires rings to be unital and homomoprhisms to preserve identities, viz. chapter 1, end of section 2, p.12), so then we can require any functor from
$R$
to be a functor both from the abelian group
$R$
and the monoid
$R$
. The issue with this (possibly questionable) interpretation is that it is quite non-trivial and far-fetched.

So what could Mac Lane be meaning by this?
answer:
Based on an educated guess as to the intended solution, the answer would almost certainly have to be to use the multiplicative monoid of
$R$
. The additive monoid of
$R$
will not work nearly as nicely for this problem, if at all. Similarly, another option not mentioned of using the discrete category on the underlying set of
$R$
will also not work as nicely, if at all.

question:
This is the problem in question from Mac Lane's
"Categories for the Working Mathematician"
, chapter 2, section 4, exercise 1:

For
$R$
a ring, describe
$R\mbox{-}\mathbf{Mod}$
as a full subcategory of the functor category
$\mathbf{Ab}^R$
.

My question is,
what is this functor category
$\mathbf{Ab}^R$
?

So far in the book I do not think a ring has been defined or interpreted as a category in itself, so how can one have functors from
$R$
to
$\mathbf{Ab}$
? The only thing I can currently think of in this direction is interpreting
$R$
as a monoid in two ways (the abelian group and the multiplication monoid, since Mac Lane requires rings to be unital and homomoprhisms to preserve identities, viz. chapter 1, end of section 2, p.12), so then we can require any functor from
$R$
to be a functor both from the abelian group
$R$
and the monoid
$R$
. The issue with this (possibly questionable) interpretation is that it is quite non-trivial and far-fetched.

So what could Mac Lane be meaning by this?
answer:
I recently did this exercise myself.

Recall that
$\mathbf{Ab}^R = \operatorname{Fun}(R, \mathbf{Ab})$
is the functor category from
$R$
to
$\mathbf{Ab}$
, so both of these ought to be categories. So this question is just as much about category theory as it is how to view a ring categorically.

We view
$R$
as an
enriched
category (we put more structure on the category).

First we deal with the ring multiplication. If (as a category)
$\operatorname{Ob}(R) = \{*\}$
is a singleton set, we see that

$$r, s \in \operatorname{Hom}(*, *) \implies rs \in \operatorname{Hom}(*, *).$$

This means, as a category,
$R$
is a one-object category with
$\operatorname{Hom}(*, *)$
taken to be ring elements.

Thus ring multiplication is closed and ensures
$(rs)t = r(st)$
.

Now for the enrichment part: put an abelian group structure on
$\operatorname{Hom}(*, *)$
, so that

$$r + s \in \operatorname{Hom}(*, *)$$

and composition bilinear

$$
\begin{cases}
t(r + s) = tr + ts, \\
(r + s)t = rt + st, \\
1r = r1 = r, \\
1 := \operatorname{id}_*.
\end{cases}
$$

Thus
$R$
(as a category) models a unitary ring.

A functor
$F \colon R \to \mathbf{Ab}$
will then pick out an abelian group
$G$
and an automorphism of
$G$
by

$$
\begin{align*}
F(*) &= G \in \operatorname{Ob}(\mathbf{Ab}) \\
F(r \colon * \to *) & \in \operatorname{Aut}(G)
\end{align*}
$$

satisfying
$F(r \circ s) = F(r) \circ F(s)$
.

We then see that
$F(1) = \operatorname{id}_{F(*)}$
, quite trivially.

The endomorphism
$F(r \colon * \to *) \in \operatorname{End}\left(F(*)\right)$
of
$G$
has to be invertible because
$r$
is invertible.

Suppose now we have a natural transformation

$$\mu \colon F_1 \to F_2, \quad \begin{cases} F_1(*) = G, \\ F_2(*) = H. \end{cases}$$

From the commutative diagram

we have

$$
\begin{align*}
F_2(r) \circ \mu_* &= \mu_* \circ F_1(r), \\
(r \mu_*(g))_H =& \left( \mu_*\left((rg)_G\right) \right)_H,
\end{align*}
$$

or simply
$r \mu_*(g) = \mu_*(rg)$
.

(The subscript
$_G$
and
$_H$
indicates that
$rg$
is a multiplication in
$G$
, while the multiplication
$r \mu_*$
is taken in
$H$
.)

Note that if
$F(r + s) = F(r) + F(s)$
, then
$F(r)$
is an additive and multiplicative endomorphism

$$F(r) \colon F(*) \to F(*).$$

Thus

$$\operatorname{eval}_g(F(r)) = F(r)g \in F(*).$$

So
$g \mapsto F(r)g$
is a right-action and we simply define

$$rg := F(r)g.$$

We check the ring axioms:

$(r+s)g = F(r+s)g = \left(F(r) + F(s) \right)g = F(r)g + F(s)g = rg + sg$
.
$r(x+y) = F(r)(x+y) = F(r)x + F(r)y = rx + ry$
.
$(rs)g = F(rs)g = F(r) F(s) g = F(r) \left( F(s) g \right) = r(sg)$
.
$1g = F(1)g = \operatorname{id}_{F(*)} g = \operatorname{id}_G(g) = g$
.

$(r+s)g = F(r+s)g = \left(F(r) + F(s) \right)g = F(r)g + F(s)g = rg + sg$
.

$r(x+y) = F(r)(x+y) = F(r)x + F(r)y = rx + ry$
.

$(rs)g = F(rs)g = F(r) F(s) g = F(r) \left( F(s) g \right) = r(sg)$
.

$1g = F(1)g = \operatorname{id}_{F(*)} g = \operatorname{id}_G(g) = g$
.

Conversely, any
$R$
-module
$G$
defines an additive functor
$R \to \mathbf{Ab}$
by

$$F(r)(g) = rg, \quad G = F(*).$$

Then

$$
\begin{align*}
F(r+s)(g) &= (r+s)g = rg + sg \\
&= F(r)g + F(s)(g),
\end{align*}
$$

$$
\begin{align*}
F(rs)(g) &= (rs)g = r(sg) = F(r)(sg) \\
&= F(r) \left( F(s) g \right) \\
&= \left( F(r) F(s) g \right).
\end{align*}
$$

Finally, the identity axiom is checked:

$$F(1)g = 1g = g,$$

implying that
$F(1) = \operatorname{id}_G$
.

So we have proven that:

$$\text{additive functors } R \to \mathbf{Ab} \iff \text{a right-action}.$$

question:
Vopěnka's principle says that for any class
$C$
of models, there is a model
$A$
that elementarily embeds in model
$B$
.

A cardinal
$\kappa$
is 1-extendible if it is the critical point of a non-trivial elementary embedding
$j:V_{\kappa + 1} \to V_{j(\kappa)+1}$
.

I know that Vopěnka's principle implies there is an extendible cardinal (Jech p. 380) so it follows that a 1-extendible cardinal exists. But, I just want a nice little proof to show it directly.

So far I have, let
$C$
be the class of
$\langle V_{\alpha + 1}, \in \rangle$
for
$\alpha \in ORD$
. By Vopěnka's principle there exist
$\alpha < \beta$
and
$j$
such that

$$ j:V_{\alpha + 1} \to V_{\beta + 1}$$

is an elementary embedding. This
$j$
has a critical point, say
$\kappa$
since
$j(\alpha) = \beta$
so it is non-trivial. If
$\kappa = \alpha$
we are done. If not, then
$\kappa < \alpha$
. Consider the restricted embedding
$j \upharpoonright V_{\kappa + 1}$
.


Can I prove the restricted embedding is an elementary embedding with induction? Is there a nicer way like in the Jech proof where I can restrict to certain
$\alpha$
? This is for a post I am writing about Vopěnka's principle and I want to give an example like this showing how VP implies other large cardinals. Thanks for any help.
answer:
Note that the definition of
$1$
-extendible also requires
$\kappa=\mathit{crit}(j)$
, which is missing from the OP. This makes things a bit trickier; if all we require is an elementary embedding
$V_{\kappa+1}\rightarrow V_{j(\kappa)+1}$
, then any elementary embedding between successor levels of
$V$
will do the job since such an embedding will necessarily take the greatest ordinal of the source model to the greatest ordinal of the target model.

With this in mind, let me slightly rephrase the question as follows:

Given
$f: V_{\theta+1}\rightarrow V_{\rho+1}$
elementary with
$\theta<\rho$
, for which
$\mu<\theta$
is the restriction
$$f_\mu: V_{\mu+1}\rightarrow V_{f(\mu)+1}: x\mapsto f(x)$$
also elementary?

Given
$f: V_{\theta+1}\rightarrow V_{\rho+1}$
elementary with
$\theta<\rho$
, for which
$\mu<\theta$
is the restriction
$$f_\mu: V_{\mu+1}\rightarrow V_{f(\mu)+1}: x\mapsto f(x)$$
also elementary?

The answer is
always
- in particular, "1-extendible" is the same as "critical point of some elementary embedding between distinct successor levels of the
$V$
-heirarchy." We can see this via the Tarski-Vaught test: suppose
$\overline{a}\in V_{\mu+1}$
and
$\varphi$
is a formula with
$V_{f(\mu)+1}\models\exists x\varphi(x,\overline{a})$
. We can pull this up to
$V_{\rho+1}$
by tweaking the formula slightly and including
$f(\mu)$
as a parameter. By elementarity of
$f$
, the same sentence holds in
$V_{\theta+1}$
with
$f(\mu)$
replaced by
$\mu$
. But now this gives
$V_{\mu+1}\models\exists x\varphi(x,\overline{a})$
as desired.

In fact, there's a more general principle here.

Suppose
$f:\mathcal{M}\rightarrow\mathcal{N}$
is an elementary embedding,
$\overline{m}\in\mathcal{M}$
is a (tuple of) element(s) of
$\mathcal{M}$
, and
$\varphi(x,\overline{y})$
is a formula. Then
$f$
restricts to an elementary embedding
$\varphi(-,\overline{m})^\mathcal{M}\rightarrow \varphi(-,f(\overline{m}))^\mathcal{N}$
. Basically, if
$A$
and
$B$
are definable-with-parameters "in the same way" (= via the same formula and with
$f$
-connected parameters), then
$f$
's restriction to
$A$
gives an elementary embedding from
$A$
to
$B$
.

(In the above, the relevant
$m$
and
$n$
are
$\mu$
and
$f(\mu)$
respectively.)

question:
In maths/logic we use quantifiers, for example the most common
$\forall$
and
$\exists$
. In some countries these symbols are also used:
$\bigwedge$
and
$\bigvee$
. Now, what would be the most common notation for the quantifiers? Wikipedia states many (
https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quantifier_(logic)#Notation
), but what about something like this,

$$
\underset{n \in \mathbb{N}}{\forall}
$$

or

$$
\underset{\epsilon > 0}{\exists}
$$

which is ultimately less cramped? Frankly, I did not find this notation anywhere, but in display-style equations or formulas it would perhaps be quite readable. Many thanks for comments if such a notation is used anywhere in the literature. And if not, then why?
answer:
Quantifiers are syntactic binders, not indexed algebraic operators. Expressions such as
$\forall x\,P(x)$
and
$\exists x\,P(x)$
belong to the syntax of first-order logic, where the quantifier binds a variable in a formula, much like
$\lambda x.\,f(x)$
in lambda calculus. By contrast,
$\sum_{i\in I} a_i$
and
$\prod_{i\in I} a_i$
represent algebraic aggregation over indexed families.

Moreover,
$\forall x\in X\,P(x)$
is shorthand for
$\forall x(x\in X \implies P(x))$
, while
$\exists x\in X\,P(x)$
abbreviates
$\exists x(x\in X \land P(x))$
.

Because quantifiers are logical binders rather than aggregation operators, mathematical notation conventionally keeps them inline with the formula rather than using large-operator display notation.

question:
I'm reading
the Handbook
by Borceux.

Let
$\mathcal A$
be a bicategory, an arrow
$f:A\to B$
is said left adjoint to
$g:B\to A$
if there are unit and counit 2-cells, satisfying triangular identities (involving associativity isomorphisms and unit isomorphisms).

I feel not quite comfortable with this definition since I prefer isomorphism of hom sets, which allows me to perform some formal deductions like co/end calculus (if possible).

So the question is can we characterize this definition of adjunction, by an equivalence of hom categories
$[X,B](f\circ {-}, -)\simeq [X,A](-,g\circ -)$
. Or can we characterize if by saying certain Kan extensions exist (and is absolute) like in the ordinary case?

If not, how about 2-category cases? (by bi- I mean weak 2-). I'm new to bicategory so I'm not familiar with these weak definitions.
answer:
Let
$f : A \to B$
be a morphism in a 2-category or bicategory.
The following are equivalent:

$f$
has a right adjoint, i.e. there exist a morphism
$g : B \to A$
and 2-morphisms
$\eta : \textrm{id}_A \Rightarrow g \circ f$
and
$\epsilon : f \circ g \Rightarrow \textrm{id}_B$
satisfying the triangle identities.
The right lift of
$\textrm{id} : B \to B$
through
$f : A \to B$
exists and is absolute.
The left extension of
$\textrm{id} : A \to A$
along
$f : A \to B$
exists and is absolute.

$f$
has a right adjoint, i.e. there exist a morphism
$g : B \to A$
and 2-morphisms
$\eta : \textrm{id}_A \Rightarrow g \circ f$
and
$\epsilon : f \circ g \Rightarrow \textrm{id}_B$
satisfying the triangle identities.

The right lift of
$\textrm{id} : B \to B$
through
$f : A \to B$
exists and is absolute.

The left extension of
$\textrm{id} : A \to A$
along
$f : A \to B$
exists and is absolute.

Note that the absoluteness condition is actually a coherence condition like naturality.
If we allow talking about naturality, then we also get these equivalents:

For every object
$X$
, the functor
$f \circ {-} : [X, A] \to [X, B]$
has a right adjoint, pseudonaturally in
$X$
.
For every object
$Y$
, the functor
${-} \circ f : [B, Y] \to [A, Y]$
has a left adjoint, pseudonaturally in
$Y$
.

For every object
$X$
, the functor
$f \circ {-} : [X, A] \to [X, B]$
has a right adjoint, pseudonaturally in
$X$
.

For every object
$Y$
, the functor
${-} \circ f : [B, Y] \to [A, Y]$
has a left adjoint, pseudonaturally in
$Y$
.

The reason for requiring pseudonaturality becomes apparent if you actually try to prove the claim.

Indeed, if we know that
$f \circ {-} : [X, A] \to [X, B]$
has a right adjoint for every
$X$
, then in particular
$[B, B](f \circ {-}, \textrm{id}_B)$
is a representable presheaf on
$[B, A]$
.
Suppose it is represented by
$g : B \to A$
and
$\epsilon : f \circ g \Rightarrow \textrm{id}_B$
.
Being a representation means, for every
$g' : B \to A$
and every
$\epsilon' : f \circ g' \Rightarrow \textrm{id}_B$
, there is a unique
$\beta : g' \Rightarrow g$
such that
$\epsilon \bullet (f \circ \beta) = \epsilon'$
.
In other words,
$(g, \epsilon)$
is a right lift of
$\textrm{id}_B$
through
$f$
.

However, without pseudonaturality, it is unclear whether the lift is absolute.
It is not even clear whether the right adjoint of
$f \circ {-} : [B, A] \to [B, B]$
is
$g \circ {-} : [B, B] \to [B, A]$
– all we have assumed is that there is a functor
$G : [B, B] \to [B, A]$
and natural transformations
$\textrm{id}_{[B, A]} \Rightarrow G (f \circ {-})$
and
$f \circ G ({-}) \Rightarrow \textrm{id}_{[B, B]}$
satisfying the triangle identities;
$g$
is obtained as
$G (\textrm{id}_B)$
and
$\epsilon$
is the component of the counit at
$\textrm{id}_B$
.

If we assume strict 2-naturality, then the Yoneda lemma tells us immediately that
$G$
is indeed
$g \circ {-} : [B, B] \to [B, A]$
.
If we assume pseudonaturality, we have
$G$
isomorphic
to
$g \circ {-}$
.
Either way, the component of the unit of the adjunction at
$X = A$
gives us
$\eta : \textrm{id}_A \Rightarrow g \circ f$
.
Applying naturality again gives the triangle identities.

We might hope that pseudonaturality is automatic, but this is not so.
Suppose
$G_X : [X, B] \to [X, A]$
is a right adjoint for
$f \circ {-} : [X, A] \to [X, B]$
.
For each
$x : X' \to X$
, we have an induced natural transformation
$G_X ({-}) \circ x \Rightarrow G_{X'} ({-} \circ x)$
, namely the composite:
$$G_X ({-}) \circ x \Rightarrow G_{X'} (f \circ G_X ({-}) \circ x) \Rightarrow G_{X'} ({-} \circ x)$$
(This is the mate of the identity natural transformation of
$f \circ {-} \circ x$
.)
However, for (strict) 2-naturality we would need
$G_X ({-}) \circ x = G_{X'} ({-} \circ x)$
and the above to be the identity natural transformation.
Even for pseudonaturality we would need the above to be invertible.
Instead, what we have is an oplax natural transformation.

Improve this question

Is there a counterexample for the claim in the question subject, that a sum of two closed sets in $\mathbb R$ is closed? If not, how can we prove it?

(By sum of sets $X+Y$ I mean the set of all sums $x+y$ where $x$ is in $X$ and $y$ is in $Y$)

Thanks!
answer: Consider the sets $A=\{ n\mid n=1,2,\ldots\}$ and $B=\{- n+{1\over n}\mid n= 2,3,\ldots\}$. Note that $0$ is not in the sum, but $1\over n$ is for each $n\ge2$.

answer:
It's worth mentioning that :

if one is closed + bounded, another one is closed,then the addition is closed

Since closedness can be charaterized by sequence in
$\Bbb{R}^n$
,if
$(x_n) \in A+B$
we need to show limit of the convergence sequence still lies in it.assume
$A$
is compact
$B $
is closed.

Since
$x_n= a_n +b_n \to x$
,compactness implies sequential compactness hence
$a_{n_k} \to a\in A$
for some subsequence. now
$x_{n_k} \to x$
which means subsequence
$b_{n_k}\to x-a$
converge,since
$B$
is closed,
$x-a \in B$
,hence
$x = a+b \in A+B$
,which means the sum is closed.

Improve this question

Is there a counterexample for the claim in the question subject, that a sum of two closed sets in $\mathbb R$ is closed? If not, how can we prove it?

(By sum of sets $X+Y$ I mean the set of all sums $x+y$ where $x$ is in $X$ and $y$ is in $Y$)

Thanks!
answer: Take $A=\{(a,0):a\in\mathbb{R}$ and $B=\{(b,\frac{1}{b}):b\in \mathbb{R}-\{0\}\}$. Then both $A,B\subset \mathbb{R}^2$ are closed. But $A+B=\{(a+b,\frac{1}{b}):a\in \mathbb{R},b\in \mathbb{R}-\{0\}\}.$The sequence $\{(0,\frac{1}{n})\}=\{(n-n,\frac{1}{n})\}\subset A+B$ but the limit $(0,0)$ which is not in the sum.

Improve this question

Is there a counterexample for the claim in the question subject, that a sum of two closed sets in $\mathbb R$ is closed? If not, how can we prove it?

(By sum of sets $X+Y$ I mean the set of all sums $x+y$ where $x$ is in $X$ and $y$ is in $Y$)

Thanks!
answer:
The sum $E +F$ may fail to be closed even if $E$ and $F$ are closed. For instance, set
$E = \{(x, y) \in \mathbb R^2 : y > 1/x\text{ and }x > 0\}$ and $F = \{(x, y) \in\mathbb R^2 : y > -1/x\text{ and }x < 0\}$

Then $E$ and $F$ are closed, but
$$E + F = \{(x, y) \in \mathbb R^2 : y > 0\}$$
is not closed.

question:
In what follows,
$F$
is a field and "
$F$
-algebra" means "associative unital
$F$
-algebra".

Let
$A$
be an
$F$
-algebra. If
$L/F$
is a Galois extension (not necessarily of finite degree),
then any
$\sigma\in G_L:=Gal(L/F)$
yields an automorphism
$Id_A\otimes L$
of
$A\otimes_F L$
. This yields a continuous action by
$F$
-algebra automorphisms on
$(A\otimes_FL)^\times$
, so we may talk about of the pointed set
$H^1(G_L,(A\otimes_F L)^\times)$
.

We say that
Hilbert 90 holds for
$A$
if for every Galois extension
$L/F$
, we have
$H^1(G_L,(A\otimes_F L)^\times)=1$
.

I am looking for a counterexample to Hilbert 90 for a while now, but it is not that easy since Hilbert 90 holds for finite-dimensional algebras, as
pointed out as an exercise in Serre's local fields (see also
https://mathoverflow.net/questions/28469/hilbert-90-for-algebras
). So we really have to go to the case of infinite dimensional
$F$
-algebras...

We can make first few reductions, though.

One may show that
$H^1(G_L,(A\otimes_F L)^\times)=1$
is trivial if and only if the same holds on every finite subextension of
$L$
.
Using this, one sees that, if Hilbert 90 holds for finite Galois extensions and finitely generated
$F$
-algebras, it holds in full generality: if
$G_L$
is finite, and
$\alpha: G_L\to (A\otimes_F L)^\times$
, then for each
$\sigma\in G_L$
, only finitely many elements of
$A$
are used to write
$\alpha_\sigma$
and
$\alpha_\sigma^{-1}$
, and the image of
$\alpha$
lies in
$(A_0\otimes_F L)^\times$
, where
$A_0$
is the subalgebra generated by these values.
It readily follows that if
$H^1(G_L,(A_0\otimes_F L)^\times)$
is trivial, so is
$H^1(G_L,(A\otimes_F L)^\times)$

So, the main question is:

Question.
Can we find a finite Galois extension
$L/F$
and a finitely generated
$F$
-algebra
$A$
such that
$H^1(G_L,(A\otimes_F L)^\times)\neq 1$
? Or does Hilbert 90 hold in full generality ?

For the moment, i have tried: polynomial algebras, Weyl algebras,
$F[X,X^{-1}]$
. For the two first cases,
$(A\otimes_F L)^\times=L^\times$
, so Hilbert 90 holds. For the third one, we have an isomorphism of
$G_L$
-modules
$(A\otimes_FL)^\times\simeq L^\times\times\mathbb{Z}$
, where the action on
$\mathbb{Z}$
is trivial. Hence
$H^1(G_L,(A\otimes_FL)^\times)=H^1(G_F, L^\times)\times H^1(G_L,\mathbb{Z})$
, which is trivial as well.

The next natural case case should be the case of a local finitely generated commutative algebra
$A$
, for which I have no idea if Hilbert 90 holds or not.
answer:
A counterexample can be obtained from
$F=\mathbb R$
,
$L=\mathbb C$
, and
$A=\mathbb R[x,y]/(x^2+y^2-1)$
, since after base change
$A\otimes_{\mathbb R}\mathbb C\cong\mathbb C[z,z^{-1}]$
with conjugation sending
$z$
to
$z^{-1}$
, and the cocycle
$\alpha_\sigma=z$
is not a coboundary because coboundaries have even
$z$
-exponent.

Here are more details.

Take
$F=\mathbb R$
,
$L=\mathbb C$
, and
$$
A=\mathbb R[x,y]/(x^2+y^2-1).
$$
Then
$$
A\otimes_{\mathbb R}\mathbb C\cong \mathbb C[z,z^{-1}],
\qquad z=x+iy.
$$
If
$\sigma$
is complex conjugation, then
$$
\sigma(z)=x-iy=z^{-1}.
$$
Define
$\alpha_e=1$
and
$\alpha_\sigma=z$
. Then
$$
\alpha_\sigma\sigma(\alpha_\sigma)=zz^{-1}=1,
$$
so
$\alpha$
is a
$1$
-cocycle.

It is not a coboundary. Every unit of
$\mathbb C[z,z^{-1}]$
has the form
$cz^n$
, with
$c\in\mathbb C^\times$
and
$n\in\mathbb Z$
, and
$$
(cz^n)^{-1}\sigma(cz^n)=\frac{\bar c}{c}z^{-2n}.
$$
Thus every coboundary has even exponent of
$z$
, while
$\alpha_\sigma=z$
has exponent
$1$
.

Hence
$$
H^1\left(\operatorname{Gal}(\mathbb C/\mathbb R),
(A\otimes_{\mathbb R}\mathbb C)^\times\right)\neq 1.
$$

question:
As I accepted an answer to the second part of this question, I reposted the first part
here
.

Let
$G$
be a finitely generated nilpotent group, with nilpotency class
$c$
. Is there some value
$n(c)$
depending only on
$c$
and a commutative ring
$R$
such that
$G$
is isomorphic to a subgroup of the group
$H_{n(c)}(R)$
of upper triangular matrices over
$R$
with ones in the diagonal? It would be nice if the optimal value
$n=c+1$
worked.

If
$G$
is torsion free, we know that it imbeds into
$H_{n}(\mathbb{Z})$
for some
$n$
, but just based on the rank of the group, the value of
$n$
could be arbitrarily large; I am not sure if changing the ring may fix this (and also fix the problem of torsion).

I would also accept a positive answer to the following (at first glance weaker) statement: Is there some upper bound
$n$
depending only on
$c$
such that, for all
$r\in\mathbb{N}$
, we can imbed the free nilpotent group on
$r$
generators and nilpotency class
$c$
in
$H_n(R)$
for some commutative ring
$R$
?
answer:
Let
$$N_{r,c}=F_r/\gamma_{c+1}(F_r)$$
be the free nilpotent group on
$r$
generators
$F_r=\langle a_1,\cdots,a_r\rangle$
of class
$c$
.  Define
$$R=\mathbb{Z}[x_{i,j}\mid 1\le i\le r, 1\le j\le c].$$
$R$
is a commutative polynomial ring with identity over the integers
$\mathbb{Z}$
.  For each generator
$a_i$
, set
$$\rho(a_i)=I+x_{i,1}E_{1,2}+x_{i,2}E_{2,3}+\cdots+x_{i,c}E_{c,c+1}\in H_{c+1}(R)$$
where
$E_i,j$
are
$(c+1)\times(c+1)$
matrices with
$1$
in the
$i,j$
position and
$0$
elsewhere.  It is clear that each
$\rho(a_i)$
is unipotent, so each
$\rho(a_i)$
are invertible, and
$\rho$
extends to a homomorphism
$\rho:F_r\to H_{c+1}(R)$
.

$H_{c+1}(R)$
has nilpotency class at most
$c$
, since commutators move strictly upward along superdiagonals.  Therefore,
$\gamma_{c+1}(F_r)\subseteq\ker\rho$
.

Now, use the Magnus expansion
$M:F_r\to\langle\!\langle X_1,\cdots,X_r\rangle\!\rangle^\times$
defined by
$$M(a_i)=I+X_i, \quad M(a_i^{-1})=1-X_i+X_i^2-X_i^3+\cdots$$
Magnus’ Theorem on the Lower Central Series of a Free Group ensures that
$$g\in\gamma_m(F_r)\iff M(g)=1+I^{\hat m}$$
where
$I^{\hat m}$
is some series of homogeneous terms of degree at least
$m$
.  Consequently,
$$g\notin\gamma_{c+1}(F_r)\iff M(g)=1+P_k+\cdots$$
for some
$1\le k\le c$
where
$P_k\ne 0$
is homogeneous of degree
$k$
.

Compare
$\rho$
with the Magnus expansion.  In
$\rho(a_i)$
, the variable
$x_{i,j}$
records placing
$X_i$
in the
$j$
-th position.  Thus, a noncommutative monomial
$X_{i_1}X_{i_2}\cdots X_{i_k}$
contributes to the
$(1,k+1)$
-entry of
$\rho(g)$
as
$x_{i_1,1}x_{i_2,2}\cdots x_{i_k,k}$
.  Different words
$X_{i_1}\cdots X_{i_k}$
give different commutative monomials because the second index records the position.  Therefore, the map
$X_{i_1}\cdots X_{i_k}\mapsto x_{i_1,1}\cdots x_{i_k,k}$
is injective on degree
$k$
words.

Since
$P_k\ne0$
, the corresponding degree
$k$
contribution to the
$(1,k+1)$
-entry of
$\rho(g)$
is nonzero, and
$\rho(g)\ne I$
.  Thus,
$g\notin\ker\rho$
, and
$\ker\rho\subseteq \gamma_{c+1}(F_r)$
.  Therefore,
$\ker\rho=\gamma_{c+1}(F_r)$
, so
$\rho$
descends to an embedding
$$N_{r,c}\hookrightarrow H_{c+1}(R).$$

$H_n(R)$
has nilpotency class at most
$n-1$
, while the free nilpotent group of class
$c$
has class
$c$
, so
$n-1\ge c,$
and
$n\ge c+1$
.  For free nilpotent groups,
$n=c+1$
works and is optimal.

Improve this question

This question was asked in masters entrance exam of a university and I am not able to make significant progress on it.

Question: How many elements does the set {
$z \in \mathbb{C}^{*} |z^{60} =-1 ,z^k\neq -1 $
for
$0<k <60$
} have?

Attempt:
$z^{60} \neq 1 $
implies that
$z^{120}\neq 1$
but

Which result can I use to calculate the number of such elements
$z$
which when raised to the power of
$120$
does not gives
$1$
? Which result in Algebra or number theory to use?

Can you please tell me?
answer:
Let
$$
S=\{z\in \mathbb C^*\mid z^{60}=-1,\ z^k\neq -1 \text{ for }0<k<60\}.
$$

If
$$
z^{60}=-1,
$$
then
$$
z^{120}=1.
$$
Hence
$z$
is a
$120$
th root of unity.

The condition
$$
z^k\neq -1 \quad (0<k<60)
$$
means that
$60$
is the smallest positive integer
$n$
such that
$$
z^n=-1.
$$
Equivalently,
$z$
must be a primitive
$120$
th root of unity.

Therefore the number of such
$z$
is
$$
\varphi(120),
$$
where
$\varphi$
is Euler's totient function.

Since
$$
120=2^3\cdot 3\cdot 5,
$$
we get
$$
\varphi(120)
=120\left(1-\frac12\right)\left(1-\frac13\right)\left(1-\frac15\right).
$$

Hence
$$
\varphi(120)
=120\cdot\frac12\cdot\frac23\cdot\frac45
=32.
$$

Therefore the number of elements in the set is
$$
\boxed{32}.
$$

question:
Suppose that a right (circular) cylinder is slanted by angle
$\theta$
to become an oblique (circular) cylinder:

The red and green planes above are perpendicular. The red plane is perpendicular to the circular bases, but its parallelogram cross section is not a rectangle. The green plane is not perpendicular to the circular bases (since it is not perpendicular to every diameter of each base), but its cross section is a rectangle.

My questions are:

Is the entire oblique cylinder considered to have the same tilt, namely,
$\theta$
?

If the tilt varies depending on where one is measuring from, which cross section would be considered to have the maximum tilt applied by the cylinder and which would be considered to have the minimum tilt applied by the cylinder? Is it measured by the cross section itself, or the plane that contains it? Or,

Am I looking at this the wrong way altogether? If so, what is the correct way to look at it?
answer:
The case you are considering in physics is known as
shear deformation
, which is “
a change in the shape of a material (rather than its volume) caused by forces acting along opposite faces in different directions. It causes internal parallel surfaces to slide past one another, transforming an originally rectangular element into a parallelogram without significantly altering its dimensions
”.

Shear deformation is measured by the
shear strain
, defined as the ratio between the horizontal displacement and the height:

$$\gamma = \tan \theta = \frac{\Delta x}{l}$$

This deformation is uniform throughout the element. In fact, if we isolate a smaller rectangular portion, the shear strain remains equal to
$\tan \theta$
. When the deformation is very small, we can use the approximation
$\tan \theta \approx \theta$
, so that the shear strain becomes approximately equal to the angular distortion itself, which is what you are denoting by "
tilt
".

question:
Recently, I opened for myself a book by Elisha Scott Loomis
that contains 300+ proofs of the Pythagorean theorem. I really liked the idea of presenting multiple proofs of one statement in one book. What other books of the same kind do you know?

It's not necessarily that a book should be just about one statement; it is ok to have dozens of statements but with multiple proofs of them.

I know about the website Cut-the-Knot, but I would like a paper book, and not necessarily in geometry. Any mathematical subject is welcome.
answer:
The book
"99 Variations on a Proof"
by Philip Ording is an example of what you want. It solves
the cubic equation
$$ x^3 - 6x^2 + 11x - 6 = 2x - 2 $$
in 99 different ways.
As you can imagine, the author came up with many
creative ways to solve such a simple equation.
It is a one of a kind book.

question:
Recently, I opened for myself a book by Elisha Scott Loomis
that contains 300+ proofs of the Pythagorean theorem. I really liked the idea of presenting multiple proofs of one statement in one book. What other books of the same kind do you know?

It's not necessarily that a book should be just about one statement; it is ok to have dozens of statements but with multiple proofs of them.

I know about the website Cut-the-Knot, but I would like a paper book, and not necessarily in geometry. Any mathematical subject is welcome.
answer:
Proofs from THE BOOK
, by Aigner and Ziegler (2014, sixth edition), presents:

Six proofs
of the infinity of primes.
Five proofs
for the Turán’s graph theorem.
Four proofs
for (i) the Cayley’s formula for the number of trees and (ii) the Euler theorem on the series for
$\pi^2/6$
.
Three proofs
for (i) a theorem on tiling rectangles and (ii) the fact that every prime of the form
$p=4m+1$
is a sum of two squares.
Two proofs
for (i) the law of quadratic reciprocity and (ii) the fact that a graph on
$n$
vertices without triangles has at most
$n^2/4$
edges (with a condition for the equality).

answer:
J. Michael Steele,
The Cauchy--Schwarz Master Class
, Cambridge University Press (2013).

This book was very important for me during my student years. I recommend it warmly. As the title implies it contains a plethora of proofs of the Cauchy-Schwarz inequality and related topics.

question:
Recently, I opened for myself a book by Elisha Scott Loomis
that contains 300+ proofs of the Pythagorean theorem. I really liked the idea of presenting multiple proofs of one statement in one book. What other books of the same kind do you know?

It's not necessarily that a book should be just about one statement; it is ok to have dozens of statements but with multiple proofs of them.

I know about the website Cut-the-Knot, but I would like a paper book, and not necessarily in geometry. Any mathematical subject is welcome.
answer:
Probably
[1]
,
[2]
,
[3]
qualify for what you want. Maybe
[4]
&
[5]
, if we extend “theorem” to “versions of a fundamental principal”. If we further extend to ways of answering a certain mathematical question, then probably many dozens of books exist, such as
[6]
(ways of solving a certain well known transcendental equation) and
[7]
(ways of proving there exists a nowhere differentiable continuous function).

[1]
Arie [Arik] Hinkis,
Proofs of the Cantor-Bernstein Theorem. A Mathematical Excursion
, Science Networks / Historical Studies #45, Birkhäuser, 2013, xxiv + 429 pages.

[2]
Craig Alan Smorynski,
A Treatise on the Binomial Theorem
, Texts in Mathematics #5, College Publications, 2012, xii + 343 pages.

[3]
Antonie [Anton] Frans Monna,
Dirichlet's principle. A Mathematical Comedy of Errors and its Influence on the Development of Analysis
, Oosthoek, Scheltema & Holkema (Utrecht, The Netherlands), 1975, viii + 138 pages.

[4]
Gregory Harvey Moore ,
Zermelo’s Axiom of Choice. Its Origins, Development, and Influence
, Studies in the History of Mathematics and Physical Sciences #8, Springer-Verlag, 1982, xiv + 410 pages.
Reprinted by Dover Publications in 2013.

[5]
David Shane Gunderson,
Handbook of Mathematical Induction. Theory and Applications
, Discrete Mathematics and Its Applications, CRC Press, 2011, xxviii + 893 pages.

[6]
Peter Colwell,
Solving Kepler’s Equation Over Three Centuries
, Willmann-Bell (Richmond, Virginia), 1993, x + 202 pages.

[7]
Marek Jarnicki and Rolf Peter Pflug,
Continuous Nowhere Differentiable Functions. The Monsters of Analysis
, Springer Monographs in Mathematics, Springer, 2015, xii + 299 pages.

that contains 300+ proofs of the Pythagorean theorem. I really liked the idea of presenting multiple proofs of one statement in one book. What other books of the same kind do you know?

It's not necessarily that a book should be just about one statement; it is ok to have dozens of statements but with multiple proofs of them.

I know about the website Cut-the-Knot, but I would like a paper book, and not necessarily in geometry. Any mathematical subject is welcome.
answer:
Not a book, but a paper that won Wagon the 1988 Lester R. Ford award:

Wagon, S. (1987). Fourteen Proofs of a Result About Tiling a Rectangle. The American Mathematical Monthly, 94(7), 601–617.
https://doi.org/10.1080/00029890.1987.12000693

question:
Recently, I opened for myself a book by Elisha Scott Loomis
that contains 300+ proofs of the Pythagorean theorem. I really liked the idea of presenting multiple proofs of one statement in one book. What other books of the same kind do you know?

It's not necessarily that a book should be just about one statement; it is ok to have dozens of statements but with multiple proofs of them.

I know about the website Cut-the-Knot, but I would like a paper book, and not necessarily in geometry. Any mathematical subject is welcome.
answer:
Barry Simon's book
Loewner's Theorem on Monotone Matrix Functions
contains a dozen proofs of said theorem. Loewner's theorem is not only a lovely piece of mathematics on its own, the proofs also involve a variety of ideas and techniques from analysis and neighboring fields that are very useful to know.

While it may be a bit beyond the usual undergraduate curriculum, some sections of this book detail work of Otte Heinävaara done during his undergrad, so I guess this still counts.

answer:
The book by Rademacher and Grosswald, Dedekind Sums, has four proofs of the reciprocity theorem for Dedekind sums – and the third of the four proofs has three variants.
https://www.cambridge.org/core/books/abs/dedekind-sums/frontmatter/0A345D97A2863053454EE348BC2353D0

question:
I am looking to solve the convection-diffusion equation for a scalar
$c(r, z, t)$
which shows the concentration of molecules in a cylindrical geometry. The system involves a Newtonian power-law fluid flowing through a cylindrical medium.The velocity profile
$\mathbf{u}$
for a power-law fluid in a pipe of radius
$R$
is given by:

$$u_z(r) = u_{\max.} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{r}{R} \right)^{2} \right]$$


The diffusion-convection equation that I'm working on in cylindrical coordinates assuming angular symmetry
$\frac{\partial c}{\partial \theta} = 0$
is given by:

$$\frac{\partial c}{\partial t} + u_z(r) \frac{\partial c}{\partial z} = D \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial c}{\partial r} \right) + \frac{\partial^2 c}{\partial z^2} \right]$$


Assume fully developed axial flow type i.e
$u_r = 0$
and
$u_z = u_z(r)$


Boundary Conditions:




Reflecting Surface (No-flux): At the cylinder wall (
$r = R$
), there is reflecting    boundary:

$$\left. \frac{\partial c}{\partial r} \right|_{r=R} = 0$$




Assume the point source initial condition i.e

$$c(r, z, t_0) = \frac{1}{r} \delta(r - r_0)  \delta(z - z_0)$$

For background, I encountered the above problem when solving for concentration of Information Molecules being transmitted by a disc transmitter in the channel of blood vessel to study how the concentration changes with time and the detection probabilities in case of a disc receiver at a certain distance from the transmitter for further analysis. The assumption of Newtonian fluid is to solve for a simpler case.
answer:
Without loss of generality, consider
$t_0=0$
. Define the function

$$\Omega(r,k,s)=\int_0^\infty ds~e^{-st}\int_{-\infty}^\infty dk~e^{-ikz}c(r,z,t)$$
Here, the z-direction is Fourier transformed under the assumption that the cylinder is of infinite length.
Then, you can show that it satisfies the second order linear ODE

$$D\left(\frac{\partial^2\Omega}{\partial r^2}+\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial \Omega}{\partial r}\right)+(-Dk^2-s+ik u_z(r))\Omega=-\frac{e^{-ikz_0}\delta(r-r_0)}{r},~~~\frac{\partial \Omega}{\partial r}\Bigg|_{r=R}=0$$

Since the coefficients of this ODE are polynomial, a finite recurrence can be derived for the coefficients of the Frobenius ansatz. Once two independent solutions have been found, the method of undetermined coefficients can be used to find a particular solution, which must be adjusted to satisfy the boundary condition.

I am looking to solve the convection-diffusion equation for a scalar
$c(r, z, t)$
which shows the concentration of molecules in a cylindrical geometry. The system involves a Newtonian power-law fluid flowing through a cylindrical medium.The velocity profile
$\mathbf{u}$
for a power-law fluid in a pipe of radius
$R$
is given by:

$$u_z(r) = u_{\max.} \left[ 1 - \left( \frac{r}{R} \right)^{2} \right]$$


The diffusion-convection equation that I'm working on in cylindrical coordinates assuming angular symmetry
$\frac{\partial c}{\partial \theta} = 0$
is given by:

$$\frac{\partial c}{\partial t} + u_z(r) \frac{\partial c}{\partial z} = D \left[ \frac{1}{r} \frac{\partial}{\partial r} \left( r \frac{\partial c}{\partial r} \right) + \frac{\partial^2 c}{\partial z^2} \right]$$


Assume fully developed axial flow type i.e
$u_r = 0$
and
$u_z = u_z(r)$


Boundary Conditions:




Reflecting Surface (No-flux): At the cylinder wall (

), there is reflecting    boundary:

$$\left. \frac{\partial c}{\partial r} \right|_{r=R} = 0$$




Assume the point source initial condition i.e

$$c(r, z, t_0) = \frac{1}{r} \delta(r - r_0)  \delta(z - z_0)$$

For background, I encountered the above problem when solving for concentration of Information Molecules being transmitted by a disc transmitter in the channel of blood vessel to study how the concentration changes with time and the detection probabilities in case of a disc receiver at a certain distance from the transmitter for further analysis. answer:
I could not find your exact equation in the
Handbook of Linear Partial Differential Equations for Engineers and Scientists
by Polyanin & Nazaikinskii 2nd ed, but I did find the time-independent version:
\begin{align}
\frac{\partial^2w}{\partial r^2}+\frac 1r\frac{\partial w}{\partial r}+\frac{\partial^2w}{\partial z^2}=\mathbf{Pe}(1-r^2)\frac{\partial w}{\partial z},
\end{align}

which differs meaningfully only in the missing time dependence (I do not know what this may correspond to physically). Take
$r\rightarrow Rr$
and
$z\rightarrow Rz$
to match the rest;
$\mathbf{Pe}=u_{max}R/D$
. I will shamelessly copy the quoted qualities from the text for the case that you, Hachiman, do not have access to it and do not know these things:

This equation governs steady-state heat exchange in a laminar fluid flow with parabolic (Poiseuille’s) velocity profile in a circular tube. The equation is written in terms of the dimensionless cylindrical coordinates
$x$
,
$y$
related to the tube radius
$R$
;
$\mathbf{Pe} = U R/a$
is the Peclet number and
$U$
is the fluid velocity at the tube axis (at
$r = 0$
). The walls of the tube correspond to
$r = 1$
.

This equation governs steady-state heat exchange in a laminar fluid flow with parabolic (Poiseuille’s) velocity profile in a circular tube. The equation is written in terms of the dimensionless cylindrical coordinates
$x$
,
$y$
related to the tube radius
$R$
;
$\mathbf{Pe} = U R/a$
is the Peclet number and
$U$
is the fluid velocity at the tube axis (at
$r = 0$
). The walls of the tube correspond to
$r = 1$
.

$1^{\circ}$
. Particular solutions:
\begin{align}
w(r)&=A+B\log r,\\
w(r,z)&=16Az+A\mathbf{Pe}(4r^2-r^4)+B,\\
w(r,z)&=\sum_{n=1}^mA_n\exp\left(-\frac{\lambda_n^2}{\mathbf{Pe}}z\right)f_n(r).
\end{align}
Here,
$A$
,
$B$
,
$A_n$
, and
$\lambda_n$
are arbitrary constants, and the functions
$f_n$
are defined by
\begin{align}
f_n(r)=\exp\left(-\frac12\lambda_n r^2\right)\Phi\left(\alpha_n,1;\lambda_nr^2\right),\quad \alpha_n=\frac12-\frac14\lambda_n-\frac14\lambda_n^3\mathbf{Pe}^{-2},\qquad(1)
\end{align}
where
$\Phi(\alpha,\beta;\xi)$
is the degenerate hypergeometric function.

$1^{\circ}$
. Particular solutions:
\begin{align}
w(r)&=A+B\log r,\\
w(r,z)&=16Az+A\mathbf{Pe}(4r^2-r^4)+B,\\
w(r,z)&=\sum_{n=1}^mA_n\exp\left(-\frac{\lambda_n^2}{\mathbf{Pe}}z\right)f_n(r).
\end{align}
Here,
$A$
,
$B$
,
$A_n$
, and
$\lambda_n$
are arbitrary constants, and the functions
$f_n$
are defined by
\begin{align}
f_n(r)=\exp\left(-\frac12\lambda_n r^2\right)\Phi\left(\alpha_n,1;\lambda_nr^2\right),\quad \alpha_n=\frac12-\frac14\lambda_n-\frac14\lambda_n^3\mathbf{Pe}^{-2},\qquad(1)
\end{align}
where
$\Phi(\alpha,\beta;\xi)$
is the degenerate hypergeometric function.

$2^\circ$
. Let the tube wall be maintained at a constant temperature such that
$w = 0$
for
$z < 0$
and
$w = w_0$
for
$z > 0$
. The boundary conditions are written as
\begin{align}
&r=0,\quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial r}=0;\quad r=1,\quad w=\begin{cases}0\quad \mathrm{for}\ \ z<0,\\ w_0\quad\mathrm{for}\ \ z>0;\end{cases}\\
&z\rightarrow -\infty,\quad w\rightarrow0;\quad z\rightarrow\infty,\quad w\rightarrow w_0.
\end{align}
The solution of the original equation under these boundary conditions is sought in the form
\begin{align}
w(r,z)&=w_0\sum_{n\geq1}B_n\exp\left(\frac{\mu_n^2}{\mathbf{Pe}}z\right)g_n(r)\qquad \mathrm{for}\ \ z<0,\\
w(r,z)&=w_0\left[1-\sum_{n\geq0}A_n\exp\left(\frac{\lambda_n^2}{\mathbf{Pe}}z\right)f_n(r)\right]\qquad \mathrm{for}\ \ z>0.
\end{align}
The series coefficients must satisfy the matching conditions at the boundary,
\begin{align}
w(r,z)|_{z\rightarrow0,z<0}-w(r,z)|_{z\rightarrow0,z>0}&=0,\\
\partial_z w(r,z)|_{z\rightarrow0,z<0}-\partial_zw(r,z)|_{z\rightarrow0,z>0}&=0.
\end{align}
For
$z > 0$
, the functions
$f_n(r)$
are defined by relations (1), where the eigenvalues
$λ_n$
are roots of the transcendental equation
\begin{align}
\Phi(\alpha_n,1;\lambda_n)=0,\quad \mathrm{where}\quad \alpha_n=\frac12-\frac14\lambda_n-\frac14\lambda_n^3\mathrm{Pe}^{-2}.
\end{align}
For
$\mathbf{Pe}\rightarrow\infty$
, it is convenient to use the following approximate relation to identify the
$λ_n$
:
\begin{align}
\lambda_n=4(n-1)+2.7\quad (n=1,2,3,\ldots),
\end{align}
whose maximum error is 0.5%. No results for
$z < 0$
are given here, since they are of secondary importance in applications.

$2^\circ$
. Let the tube wall be maintained at a constant temperature such that
$w = 0$
for
$z < 0$
and
$w = w_0$
for
$z > 0$
. The boundary conditions are written as
\begin{align}
&r=0,\quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial r}=0;\quad r=1,\quad w=\begin{cases}0\quad \mathrm{for}\ \ z<0,\\ w_0\quad\mathrm{for}\ \ z>0;\end{cases}\\
&z\rightarrow -\infty,\quad w\rightarrow0;\quad z\rightarrow\infty,\quad w\rightarrow w_0.
\end{align}
The solution of the original equation under these boundary conditions is sought in the form
\begin{align}
w(r,z)&=w_0\sum_{n\geq1}B_n\exp\left(\frac{\mu_n^2}{\mathbf{Pe}}z\right)g_n(r)\qquad \mathrm{for}\ \ z<0,\\
w(r,z)&=w_0\left[1-\sum_{n\geq0}A_n\exp\left(\frac{\lambda_n^2}{\mathbf{Pe}}z\right)f_n(r)\right]\qquad \mathrm{for}\ \ z>0.
\end{align}
The series coefficients must satisfy the matching conditions at the boundary,
\begin{align}
w(r,z)|_{z\rightarrow0,z<0}-w(r,z)|_{z\rightarrow0,z>0}&=0,\\
\partial_z w(r,z)|_{z\rightarrow0,z<0}-\partial_zw(r,z)|_{z\rightarrow0,z>0}&=0.
\end{align}
For
$z > 0$
, the functions
$f_n(r)$
are defined by relations (1), where the eigenvalues
$λ_n$
are roots of the transcendental equation
\begin{align}
\Phi(\alpha_n,1;\lambda_n)=0,\quad \mathrm{where}\quad \alpha_n=\frac12-\frac14\lambda_n-\frac14\lambda_n^3\mathrm{Pe}^{-2}.
\end{align}
For
$\mathbf{Pe}\rightarrow\infty$
, it is convenient to use the following approximate relation to identify the
$λ_n$
:
\begin{align}
\lambda_n=4(n-1)+2.7\quad (n=1,2,3,\ldots),
\end{align}
whose maximum error is 0.5%. No results for
$z < 0$
are given here, since they are of secondary importance in applications.

$3^\circ$
. Let a constant thermal flux be prescribed at the wall for
$z > 0$
and let, for
$z < 0$
, the tube surface be insulated from heat and the temperature vanishes as
$z\rightarrow-\infty$
. Then the boundary conditions have the form
\begin{align}
r=0,\quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial r}=0;\quad r=1,\quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial r}=\begin{cases}0\quad \mathrm{for}\ \ z<0,\\ q\quad \mathrm{for}\ \ z>0;\end{cases} \quad z\rightarrow-\infty,\quad w\rightarrow0.
\end{align}
In the domain of thermal stabilization, the asymptotic behavior of the solution (as
$z\rightarrow\infty$
) is as follows:
\begin{align}
w(r,z)=q\left(4\frac{z}{\mathbf{Pe}}+r^2-\frac14 r^4+\frac{8}{\mathbf{Pe}^2}-\frac{7}{24}\right).
\end{align}
$\circledcirc$
Literature: C. A. Deavours (1974), A. D. Polyanin, A. M. Kutepov, A. V. Vyazmin, and D. A. Kazenin
(2002).

$3^\circ$
. Let a constant thermal flux be prescribed at the wall for
$z > 0$
and let, for
$z < 0$
, the tube surface be insulated from heat and the temperature vanishes as
$z\rightarrow-\infty$
. Then the boundary conditions have the form
\begin{align}
r=0,\quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial r}=0;\quad r=1,\quad \frac{\partial w}{\partial r}=\begin{cases}0\quad \mathrm{for}\ \ z<0,\\ q\quad \mathrm{for}\ \ z>0;\end{cases} \quad z\rightarrow-\infty,\quad w\rightarrow0.
\end{align}
In the domain of thermal stabilization, the asymptotic behavior of the solution (as
$z\rightarrow\infty$
) is as follows:
\begin{align}
w(r,z)=q\left(4\frac{z}{\mathbf{Pe}}+r^2-\frac14 r^4+\frac{8}{\mathbf{Pe}^2}-\frac{7}{24}\right).
\end{align}
$\circledcirc$
Literature: C. A. Deavours (1974), A. D. Polyanin, A. M. Kutepov, A. V. Vyazmin, and D. A. Kazenin
(2002).

I welcome you to look into the quoted literature, perhaps you may glean something I did not, for I did not find more worth sharing.

question:
Consider the following packing of
$n=12$
equilateral triangles with sides equal to
$1$
inside of a square with sides
$s$
. The goal is to express the length of the square
$s$
in terms of
$\theta$
the angle between the bottom of the bottom left triangle and the bottom of the square. Notice that also the angle between the bottom of the bottom right triangle and the bottom of the square is
$\theta$
. Furthermore, the figure is
$90$
-degrees rotation symmetric.
(Despite the image, the corner of the triangles on the right and top touch the sides of the square.)

Draw a line between the upper corner of the bottom left triangle and the bottom of the square, such that the angle with the bottom of the square is
$90$
degrees, call the length of this line
$x$
.
Furhtermore, draw a line between the upper corner of the bottom left triangle and the upper corner of the bottom right triangle, call its length
$y$
.
Using the law of sines, it holds that

$$x = \sin\left(\theta + \frac\pi3\right) \quad\text{and}\quad y= \frac{\sin\left(\frac\pi3\right)}{\sin\left(\frac23\pi-\theta\right)}.$$


An upper bound on
$s$
is
$$s\leq 2x+y.$$

What is the exact value of
$s$
in terms of
$\theta$
? Moreover, what is the maximum value of
$\theta$
such that the triangles do not overlap.
answer:
Take the origin at
$A$
and the axes parallel to the sides of the square (see figure below). We have then:
$$
B=(\cos\theta,\sin\theta),\quad
C=(\cos(60°+\theta),\sin(60°+\theta))
$$
and from the sine rule applied to triangle
$AEB$
:
$$
E=\left({\sin60°\over\sin(60°+\theta)},0\right).
$$
In addition:
$$
F=E+B,\quad G=E+C,\quad
C'=G+(F-G)x,
$$
where
$x=GC'$
is our second free parameter.
We can then find the midpoint
$M$
of
$CC'$
and the rotation center
$O$
, which can be obtained by rotating
$C$
about
$M$
by
$90°$
clockwise:
$$
M={C+C'\over2},\quad O=M+R(C-M),\quad\text{where}\quad
R=\pmatrix{0&1\\-1&0}.
$$
Finally, the side
$s$
of the square is just twice the
$y$
coordinate of
$O$
. The computation gives:
$$
s= 2 x\big(\sin \theta+\cos \theta
-\sin (30°-\theta)-\cos (30°-\theta)\big)
+\sqrt{3} \sec (30°-\theta)+2\cos(30°-\theta).
$$

EDIT.

The above formula can be rewritten as follows:
$$
s= 4 x(\cos15°-\sin15°)\sin(\theta-15°)
+\sqrt{3} \sec (30°-\theta)+2\cos(30°-\theta).
$$
It is then clear that
$s$
is an increasing function of
$x$
for
$\theta>15°$
, while it is decreasing for
$\theta<15°$
.

question:
Consider the following packing of
$n=12$
equilateral triangles with sides equal to
$1$
inside of a square with sides
$s$
. The goal is to express the length of the square
$s$
in terms of
$\theta$
the angle between the bottom of the bottom left triangle and the bottom of the square. Notice that also the angle between the bottom of the bottom right triangle and the bottom of the square is
$\theta$
. Furthermore, the figure is
$90$
-degrees rotation symmetric.
(Despite the image, the corner of the triangles on the right and top touch the sides of the square.)

Draw a line between the upper corner of the bottom left triangle and the bottom of the square, such that the angle with the bottom of the square is
$90$
degrees, call the length of this line
$x$
.
Furhtermore, draw a line between the upper corner of the bottom left triangle and the upper corner of the bottom right triangle, call its length
$y$
.
Using the law of sines, it holds that

$$x = \sin\left(\theta + \frac\pi3\right) \quad\text{and}\quad y= \frac{\sin\left(\frac\pi3\right)}{\sin\left(\frac23\pi-\theta\right)}.$$


An upper bound on
$s$
is
$$s\leq 2x+y.$$

What is the exact value of
$s$
in terms of
$\theta$
? Moreover, what is the maximum value of
$\theta$
such that the triangles do not overlap.
answer:
If
$\theta\lt15^\circ$
, the square is minimised by sliding the left three triangles up and the right three down until each group of three has a corner in the square's corner.

If
$\theta\gt15^\circ$
, slide them the other way until they collide in the middle and form a central square.

The changeover point is where the rightmost edge of the lower three triangles has slope
$45^\circ$
. When left and right triads move a distance
$d$
, the width changes by
$d(\cos(60^\circ-\theta)-\sin(60^\circ-\theta))$
.

In both cases, you get an extra equation.

question:
Problem:

Let
$C_1$
and
$C_2$
be two curves passing through the origin as indicated in Figure 5.2. A curve
$C$
is said to “bisect in area” the region between
$C_1$
and
$C_2$
if, for each point
$P$
of
$C$
, the two shaded regions
$A$
and
$B$
shown in the figure have equal areas. Determine the upper curve
$C_2$
, given that the bisecting curve
$C$
has the equation
$y = x^2$
and that the lower curve
$C_1$
has the equation
$y = \frac{1}{2} x^2$
.

The solutions manual at the back of the book says its
$y = \frac{16}{9} x^2$
.

I dont see how that can be the case when you have:

$$\int C - C_1 = \int C_2 - C \implies C - C_1 = C_2 - C$$

(via fundamental theorem of calculus) and


$$2C - C_1 = C_2$$

(forgot what this is called but I consider it an axiom)


$$2C = 2 x^2$$

(as per the problem statement)


$$C_1 = \frac{1}{2} x^2$$

(as per the problem statement)


$$2C - C_1 = C_2 = \frac{3}{2} x^2$$

Can anyone let me know if my logic is wrong, or is the books answer wrong?

I don't see how what the book is saying could possibly be correct.
answer:
You are given
$$C_1(x) = \frac{1}{2}x^2, \quad C(x) = x^2,$$
and wish to identify
$C_2(x)$
such that for all
$(x_0, y_0)$
satisfying
$0 \le x_0 = \sqrt{y_0}$

$$A(x_0) = \int_{x=0}^{x_0} x^2 - \frac{1}{2} x^2 \, dx = B(y_0) = \int_{y=0}^{y_0} \sqrt{y} - C_2^{-1}(y) \, dy. \tag{1}$$

Note that I have done a few things here:  first, I identified the point
$P = (x_0, y_0)$
on the parabola
$C$
with the relationship
$x = \sqrt{y_0}$
, or
$y_0 = x_0^2$
.  Second, the area relationship for the region
$A$
, as a function of this point on the parabola, is expressed as a straightforward integral with respect to
$x$
; however, the region
$B$
is expressed as an integral with respect to the vertical axis
$y$
of the
inverse functions
$C^{-1}(y) = \sqrt{y}$
and
$C_2^{-1}(y)$
, the latter of which is the quantity that we wish to solve for.

I set it up this way because the diagram suggests this is a natural way to compute the area of
$B$
as a function of
$x_0$
(and in turn,
$y_0 = x_0^2$
).  If we perform the computation for
$(1)$
, we find
$$K(y_0) - K(0) = \frac{1}{2} x_0^3, \tag{2}$$
where
$K$
is a function satisfying
$K'(y) = C_2^{-1}(y)$
; that is,
$K$
is an antiderivative of the inverse function of
$C_2$
.  Hence
$$\frac{d}{dx}[K(y_0) - K(0)] = \frac{d}{dx}[K(x_0^2)] = 2x_0 K'(x_0^2) = 2x_0 C_2^{-1}(x_0^2) = \frac{3}{2}x_0^2, \tag{3}$$
from which it follows that
$$C_2^{-1}(x_0^2) = \frac{3}{4}x_0. \tag{4}$$
To recover
$C_2$
, all that remains is to write
$$x_0^2 = C_2(\tfrac{3}{4}x_0),$$
hence
$$C_2(x_0) = \left(\tfrac{4}{3}x_0\right)^2.$$

The missing computations are left as an exercise for the reader.

Of course, we are not
required
to perform the evaluation this way.  We could compute the area of
$B$
as the piecewise sum of two integrals, e.g.
$$B(x_0) = \int_{x=0}^? C_2(x_0) - x^2 \, dx + \int_{x=?}^{x_0} x_0^2 - x^2 \, dx.$$
But it is not immediately clear what we should write for the boundary between the two regions to be integrated, which I have written as "
$?$
" above. How would you identify this point? This is ultimately why I chose the inverse function approach.

The key learning objective of this exercise is to test your understanding of the relationship between areas between curves and integrals of functions; the relationship between functions and their inverses, and how these relate to the fundamental theorem of calculus.

question:
Preemptive Prefatory Note: This is
not
an "opinion-based" question in the sense that I am not asking which philosophy of set theory is the "right" one. Rather, I am seeking to know what exactly is the argument underlying the "monist" view.

As I understand it, the "monist" or "universist" philosophy of set theory is the view that, although there are many possible models of set theory, there is only one (up to isomorphism) "true" or "best" set-theoretic universe (see
here
and
here
). An implication of this view is presumably that there is a single "true" mapping of sentences in the language of set theory to truth values (the mapping that is implied by the one "true" universe).

But it seems that this "monist" concept runs into some clear difficulties. There are of course the well-known independence results (e.g.
$\mathsf{CH}$
vs.
$\mathsf{\neg CH}$
). Furthermore
$\mathsf{NFU}$
set theory with the Axiom of Small Ordinals (call it
$\mathsf{NFU+SO}$
)
seems
capable of formalizing all the mathematics that can be formalized in
$\mathsf{ZFC}$
(see comment
here
by user MJD), yet the Axiom of Foundation does not hold in
$\mathsf{NFU+SO}$
. Thus (assuming consistency of
$\mathsf{NFU+SO}$
is not significantly more in doubt than that of
$\mathsf{ZFC}$
) it seems not at all clear by what criteria we could possibly even adjudicate whether the one "true" universe of sets should obey Foundation or instead some kind of Anti-Foundation axiom.

The axioms of
$\mathsf{NFU+SO}$
seem fairly intuitive, nothing there that seems open to the charge that it "morally" should be false
(edit: I say this in regards to the
finite
axiomatization of NFU described
here
, which is not dependent on syntactic logic concepts like "stratified formula"; see also
here
)
.

While
$\mathsf{ZFC}$
may be more "elegant" (e.g. cumulative hierarchy) or convenient in some respects, the perception of elegance or convenience by human mathematicians hardly seems the same as "truth". (to argue that a "fully legitimate" set universe must be arranged as a well-ordered collection of "stages" seems more like an aesthetic judgement than anything like mathematical "truth")

So my question is basically: What exactly would be an argument for the "monist" view of set theory (which can successfully deal with counter-examples like the ones just given, so as to be able to plausibly argue that there is indeed a single "true" universe of sets)?

I realize this maybe be a complicated thing to spell out in a Stack Exchange post, so a reference to a book or paper would totally suffice for me.

It seems there are lots of resources online advocating various forms of set-theoretic pluralism (Joel Hamkins well-known multiverse paper comes to mind). But not much I've seen really defending the "monist" view.

Edit (to add a perhaps helpful analogy)
: With geometry (as far as I know) the generally agreed-upon understanding of it is a "pluralist" one, in that there are various kinds of geometry (Euclidean, hyperbolic, elliptic, etc., and these can be further sub-categorized) but it is not claimed that there is a
mathematical
reason (as opposed to a physics reason) for considering one particular geometry to be the one "true" or "best" geometry. Set-theoretic monism (as I understand it) takes the view that set theory is
not
like geometry, that there in fact
is
a single "true" or "best" universe of sets. But it's unclear to me what sort of argument could be invoked to support this view. And absent such an argument, it seems like we have every reason to at least suspect that set theory actually
is
like geometry in that there exist a plurality of "equally good" universes.
answer:
This is a bit of a tangent, but I actually don't find
$\mathsf{NFU}$
and its variants as natural as you suggest. A big reason for this - and I'm not claiming that other people care about it, just me - is that
$\mathsf{ZFC}$
can be viewed as an
instance
of a general idea which has a version,
$\mathsf{ZFC}_\mathcal{L}$
, in every logic
$\mathcal{L}$
: keep the axioms the same except for the Separation/Replacement schemes, and have those range over
$\mathcal{L}$
-formulas. The same is true of Peano arithmetic, with the
induction scheme
being the "logic-dependent" part.

(Note that a major technical difference between the two is that "strong-logic-versions" of
$\mathsf{ZFC}$
aren't actually that far from
$\mathsf{ZFC}$
itself even when we use different axiomatizations of
$\mathsf{ZFC}$
(
1
,
2
) whereas it's hard
not
to strengthen
$\mathsf{PA}$
without becoming categorical (
3
,
4
). But that's not really relevant for this conversation.)

So what? Well, I personally find the idea that "logic precedes theory" to be not fully convincing. A really good theory, good for
foundational
purposes I mean, is one which represents a coherent idea, and that idea should be flexible: allowing a richer language (= stronger logic) should never be negative.
$\mathsf{NFU}$
is so tightly grounded in the particular syntax of first-order logic that I don't see any principled way to extend it to an arbitrary logic
$\mathcal{L}$
. The same is true of theories like
$\mathsf{GPK}_\infty^+$
(which I
do like
, at least as an object of mathematical investigation if not foundation): notions like "stratified formula" and "positive formula" are necessarily syntactic ones, and they don't seem to reflect a "pre-logical" intuition.

Preemptive Prefatory Note: This is
not
an "opinion-based" question in the sense that I am not asking which philosophy of set theory is the "right" one. Rather, I am seeking to know what exactly is the argument underlying the "monist" view.

As I understand it, the "monist" or "universist" philosophy of set theory is the view that, although there are many possible models of set theory, there is only one (up to isomorphism) "true" or "best" set-theoretic universe (see
here
and
here
). An implication of this view is presumably that there is a single "true" mapping of sentences in the language of set theory to truth values (the mapping that is implied by the one "true" universe).

mathematical

not
like geometry, that there in fact
is

answer:
The "monist" point of view is traditionally a consequence of the "realist" or "platonist" point of view, as in the
SEP article
.

The realist viewpoint can be (poorly) summarized as the principle that natural language phrases like "all ordinals", "all real numbers", and "all natural numbers" actually refer to some well-defined collection of things that exist in an abstract sense. Some authors add a qualifier that these collections exist up to an appropriate isomorphism (look up "structuralism").

From that viewpoint, we arrive at "truth value realism": phrases like "the continuum hypothesis is true" have some objective truth value even if we don't know what that truth value is.

In set theory, this is related to the folklore motto "set theory is the study of sets, not the study of models of set theory", which a number of set theorists have espoused.

So the right place to start in the literature, with regard to "monism", is with platonism in general and "
plenitudinous platonism
" in particular.

The issue with ZFC vs. NFU brings in a separate issue: these theories refer to different notions of "set". The notion of set in ZFC is about the cumulative hierarchy, while in NFU it is not (e.g., because of the urelements).

If we look at the Peano axioms for
$\mathbb{N}$
and the axioms of a complete ordered field for
$\mathbb{R}$
, we would say these are about different concepts of "number", not that they conflict with each other. Similarly, if we talk about "sets" we need to be clear which collection we are interested in before we could possibly expect to have a uniquely defined collection of sets.

One of the standard counter-arguments to platonism, as you mentioned, is: how do we gain knowledge about these platonically existing objects, if they are abstract and not directly observable? That subject also has an extensive treatment from all angles in the literature.

answer:
I think the issue here is a conflation of a few different questions.

Is there a unique/canonical/best (set-theoretical) foundation for mathematics?
Is there a unique/canonical/best concept of what sets are?
Is there a unique/canonical/best mathematical object that is what we are attempting to axiomatize with ZFC? (Similar to how the natural numbers are what we're trying to axiomatize with PA.)

The universalist view of set theory is really only about question 3, but many of your objections are about 1 and 2.

Most set theoretical universalists would also believe that there is a canonical mathematical object that AFA axiomatizes, or that ZFA + "there is a countable set of atoms" axiomatizes. Of course this isn't some generic thing that applies to all theories, it's just that these two correspond to conceptions similar in spirit to the cumulative hierarchy, though a bit different in structure.

I don't know enough about NFU + SO to say if something similar would hold there, but it's at the very least clear that this is represents a significantly different conception of what sets are than the cumulative hierarchy, just like AFA, and to a lesser extent ZFA. Thus arguing that any of these theories are equally valid choices as ZFC is really an argument about question 2, not question 3, and thus not really relevant to the universalist/multiversalist question. Similarly, arguing that they're also adequate for mathematics is about question 1, not question 3.

The analogy with geometry is more on the topic of question 3. The field of set theory has evolved a lot in pursuit of answers to questions of independence, and we generally think about this in terms of the discovery of many different models of ZFC, though it's debatable if that's what's "really" happened. When we have all these different, seemingly equally interesting models, what reason is there to single out one? A counterpoint would be that if there isn't some objective mathematical background, where are all these different models in the first place?

The natural numbers start with
$0$
, then you keep adding
$1$
and going to the next one. You do that "forever". Most people think, as you seem to, that despite the possible vagueness of "forever", this is actually a really sharp concept referencing a unique mathematical object, and thus that PA has one "intended model".

The cumulative hierarchy starts with the empty set, then you keep taking the power set, and continue on "forever", only this time "forever" is a little longer, as we continue transfinitely through the ordinals. Set theoretical universalists think that, despite the possible vagueness of the power set and of (transfinite) "forever", that this is actually a really sharp concept referencing a unique mathematical object, and thus that ZFC has one "intended model".

The plausibility of this is significantly less than the former case with the natural numbers, so set theoretical universalism has fewer adherents than natural number universalism. But it's the same kind of thing, and is defensible, and some other answers have given links to defenses.
