answer:
The "universalist" view has a long tradition in mathematics (in general) and in the field of logic (in a wider sense) specifically.

See well-known
Gödel’s Realism
, for example discussing
Russell's mathematical logic (1944)
, p.449:

What strikes one as surprising in this field is Russell's pronouncedly
realistic attitude, which manifests itself in many passages of his writings.
"Logic is concerned with the real world just as truly as zoology, though
with its more abstract and general features," he says, e.g., in his
Introduction
to Mathematical Philosophy
(edition of 1920, p. 169). It is true,
however, that this attitude has been gradually decreasing in the course of
time and also that it always was stronger in theory than in practice.

What strikes one as surprising in this field is Russell's pronouncedly
realistic attitude, which manifests itself in many passages of his writings.
"Logic is concerned with the real world just as truly as zoology, though
with its more abstract and general features," he says, e.g., in his
Introduction
to Mathematical Philosophy
(edition of 1920, p. 169). It is true,
however, that this attitude has been gradually decreasing in the course of
time and also that it always was stronger in theory than in practice.

Not the place to discuss Russell's views, but the situation is similar: one "true logic" (
Logical Monism
) vs the
plurality of modern logics
.

But still the issue stands: is there a "favored" logic, at least for us humans: some sort of
firmware
embedded into our brain/language?

This is quite similar to geometry: there are many alternative geometries applicable to different contexts, but maybe one of them fits with our universe.

The same path has been followed recently with so-called
set-theoretic pluralism
.

The issue is the same that we have with geometry: assuming that there is a plurality of
possible
universes with different universes of sets, the issue about our "mental universe" still stands: do we have a favored concept of set that is part of our brain/language machinery?

The issue is more relevant for philosopher considering that the concept of
set
is linked to (but not identical with: Gödel was one of them) our view about
concepts
. And also with the proponents of
Realism about mathematics
. See also Maddy's
Believing the axioms
.

You say:

The axioms of
$\mathsf{NFU+SO}$
seem fairly intuitive [...] While
$\mathsf{ZFC}$
may be more "elegant"...

The axioms of
$\mathsf{NFU+SO}$
seem fairly intuitive [...] While
$\mathsf{ZFC}$
may be more "elegant"...

But behind "intuitive" and "elegant" there is an aspect that we cannot avoid:
set theory
is today the language of mathematics and
language
means meaning and communication. See part II:
Truth and Deducibility
, of Yuri Manin,
A Course in Mathematical Logic for Mathematicians (Springer, 2010)
, with its focus on
language
:

[p.4] “Reality” for the languages of mathematics consists of certain classes of
(mathematical) arguments or certain computational processes using (abstract)
automata. [...] As a means of communication, discovery, and codification, no formal
language can compete with the mixture of mathematical argot and formulas
that is common to every working mathematician. [p.9] On several occasions we have said that a certain object (a sign on paper, an element of an alphabet as an abstract set, etc.) is a notation for, or denotes, another element. A convenient general term for this relationship is naming. [...] A successfully chosen name is a bridge between scientific knowledge and common sense, between new experience and old habits. The conceptual foundation of any science consists of a complicated network of names of things, names of ideas, and names of names. It evolves itself, and its projection on reality changes.

[p.4] “Reality” for the languages of mathematics consists of certain classes of
(mathematical) arguments or certain computational processes using (abstract)
automata. [...] As a means of communication, discovery, and codification, no formal
language can compete with the mixture of mathematical argot and formulas
that is common to every working mathematician. [p.9] On several occasions we have said that a certain object (a sign on paper, an element of an alphabet as an abstract set, etc.) is a notation for, or denotes, another element. A convenient general term for this relationship is naming. [...] A successfully chosen name is a bridge between scientific knowledge and common sense, between new experience and old habits. The conceptual foundation of any science consists of a complicated network of names of things, names of ideas, and names of names. It evolves itself, and its projection on reality changes.

Here we have the key-point: language has
names
and names are "names of things, names of ideas, and names of names." Since the beginning, the study of
formal language
cannot elude the issues about
meaning
.

In conclusion
: language means
syntax
(that may account for "elegance") and also
communication
(that is linked to "intuition"), and thus there are also
cognitive
and
social
aspects: we understand and share results (
proofs
). But this is *meaning and in the end there is
truth
.

Can we really avoid the issue about
What We Talk About When We Talk About Sets
?

(
But this is simply the
Benacerraf Problem
).

question:
Let
$z\in \mathbb{C}$
satisfy
$|z^2+1|\le 2$
. I am interested in sharp inequalities of the form
$$|z^3+az+b|\le c$$

where
$a,b,c\in\mathbb Z$
, and where
$c$
is the best possible constant (The inequality is sharp). I conjecture that

$$|z^2+1|\le 2 \implies |z^3+az+b|\le a+b+1, \quad |b| \ge \operatorname{ceil}\left(\frac{a^{2}}{6}+ \frac{1}{2}\right) - 1$$

and that the constant
$a+b+1$
is sharp with equality at
$z=1$
(I think a proof will exist along the lines of
SOS
). There is also the reflected family

$$|z^2+1|\le 2\implies|z^3+az-b|\le a+b+1,$$

with equality at
$z=-1$
. My questions are (1) Is the above family correct? (2) Are there other infinite families of integer triples
$(a,b,c)$
for which

$|z^2+1|\le 2 \implies |z^3+az+b|\le c$
holds sharply? I think the correct approach would be SOS for this problem, as ordinary triangle inequality estimates seem far too weak to give a sharp bound.


Edit:
Here
is a cool visualization if it helps. (Thanks to @mathlove, if you zoom in the graph for
$a=2$
,
$b=1$
, you will see the inequality doesn't hold.)
answer:
Regarding the question (1),
$$(a,b,z)=\bigg(2,1,\frac{999-302i}{1000}\bigg)$$
is a counterexample to your conjecture
$$|z^2+1|\le 2 \implies |z^3+az+b|\le a+b+1, \quad |b| \ge \operatorname{ceil}\left(\frac{a^{2}}{6}+ \frac{1}{2}\right) - 1$$
since we have
$$|z^2+1|=\sqrt{4-\frac{1538719}{4\times 10^{10}}}\lt 2$$
(see
here
) and
$$|z^3+2z+1|=\sqrt{16+\frac{344783802074321}{8\times 10^{15}}}\gt 4$$
(see
here
).

Also,
$$(a,b,z)=\bigg(2,1,\frac{-997-400i}{1000}\bigg)$$
is a counterexample to the reflected family
$$|z^2+1|\le 2\implies|z^3+az-b|\le a+b+1$$
since we have
$$|z^2+1|=\sqrt{4-\frac{245227919}{10^{12}}}\lt 2$$
(see
here
) and
$$|z^3+2z-1|=\sqrt{16+\frac{15621734936422729}{10^{18}}}\gt 4$$
(see
here
).

Regarding the question (2), I'll use
Alex Ravsky
's nice
result
which says that
$$|z^3+az+b|\le \max_{-1\le w\le 1}\sqrt{M(w)-(a+1)^2+b^2}\tag1$$
where
$$\frac{M(w)}{4}=\frac {b(a+3)w^5+(a^2+3)w^4+2b(a+5)w^3+2(a^2+6a-3)w^2+b(a-9)w+a^2-4a+7}{(1+w^2)^3}$$

Using
$(1)$
, I think we can prove the following three claims.

In the following,
$a,b$
are
real numbers
.

Claim 1
: If
$a,b$
are real numbers such that
$a\ge 2$
and
$b\ge\frac{a^2+3}{6}$
, then
$$|z^2+1|\le 2\implies |z^3+az+b|\le a+b+1$$
where the equality holds for
$z=1$
.

Claim 2
: If
$a,b$
are real numbers such that
$a\ge 2$
and
$b\le -\frac{a^2+3}{6}$
, then
$$|z^2+1|\le 2\implies |z^3+az+b|\le a-b+1$$
where the equality holds for
$z=-1$
.

Claim 3
: If
$a,b$
are real numbers such that
$a\in (-\infty,12-3\sqrt{13}]\cup [12+3\sqrt{13},\infty)$
and
$b=0$
, then
$$|z^2+1|\le 2\implies |z^3+az+b|\le \sqrt 3\ |a-3|$$
where the equality holds for
$z=\sqrt 3\ i$
.

In the following, let us prove the three claims.

Claim 1
: If
$a,b$
are real numbers such that
$a\ge 2$
and
$b\ge\frac{a^2+3}{6}$
, then
$$|z^2+1|\le 2\implies |z^3+az+b|\le a+b+1$$
where the equality holds for
$z=1$
.

Proof of Claim 1
:

Since
$z=1$
corresponds to
$w=1$
, all we need is to prove that for
$-1\le w\le 1$
,
$M(1)\ge M(w)$
holds.

Let us introduce a non-negative real number
$k$
such that
$b=k+\frac{a^2+3}{6}$
to eliminate
$b$
. Then, we can write
$\frac 12(1+w^2)^3(M(1)-M(w))$
as
$P(w)+kQ(w)$
where
$P(w),Q(w)$
are independent from
$k$
.

$P(w)$
is non-negative for
$-1\le w\le 1$
since we can write
$P(w)$
as
$$P(w) = \frac{1}{6}(w-1)^2 \bigg( (w^2+1)^2(a-2)^3 +(\underbrace{13w^4+8w^3+30w^2+8w+1}_{A})(a-2)^2+(\underbrace{55w^4+56w^3+174w^2+152w+55}_{B})(a-2)+\underbrace{75w^4+80w^3+226w^2+176w+39}_{C}\bigg)$$
where
$A,B,C$
are non-negative:
$$\begin{align}A&=4w^2(w+1)^2+9w^4+10w^2+(4w+1)^2
\\B&=55w^4+56w^2(w+1)+118 \bigg(w + \frac{38}{59}\bigg)^2 + \frac{357}{59}
\\C&=75w^2\bigg(w + \frac{8}{15}\bigg)^2 + \frac{2}{3}w^2+204\bigg(w + \frac{22}{51}\bigg)^2 + \frac{53}{51}\end{align}$$

Also,
$Q(w)$
is non-negative since
$$Q(w)= (a-2)(w-1)^2(1+w^2)^2+(1-w)(\underbrace{-3 w^5 + 7 w^4 - 2 w^3 + 26 w^2 + 17 w + 3}_{D})$$
where
$D$
is non-negative:
$$D=3w^4(1-w)+2w^2(1-w)+\bigg(2w^2-\frac{1}{10}\bigg)^2+\frac{122}{5}\bigg(w + \frac{85}{244}\bigg)^2 + \frac{353}{12200}$$

Since we have proved that
$M(1)\ge M(w)$
, it follows from
$(1)$
that
$$|z^3+az+b|\le \sqrt{M(1)-(a+1)^2+b^2}=a+b+1.\ \blacksquare$$

Claim 2
: If
$a,b$
are real numbers such that
$a\ge 2$
and
$b\le -\frac{a^2+3}{6}$
, then
$$|z^2+1|\le 2\implies |z^3+az+b|\le a-b+1$$
where the equality holds for
$z=-1$
.

Proof of Claim 2
:

Setting
$(a,b,z)=(a',-b',-z')$
in Claim 1, we see that Claim 2 follows.
$\ \blacksquare$

Claim 3
: If
$a,b$
are real numbers such that
$a\in (-\infty,12-3\sqrt{13}]\cup [12+3\sqrt{13},\infty)$
and
$b=0$
, then
$$|z^2+1|\le 2\implies |z^3+az+b|\le \sqrt 3\ |a-3|$$
where the equality holds for
$z=\sqrt 3\ i$
.

Proof of Claim 3
:

Since
$z=\sqrt 3\ i$
corresponds to
$w=0$
, all we need is to prove that for
$-1\le w\le 1$
,
$M(0)\ge M(w)$
holds.

We can write
$\frac 14(1+w^2)^3(M(0)-M(w))$
as
$$(a-2)^2 w^6 +3w^6 + 2(a-3)^2w^4  +(\underbrace{a^2 - 24 a + 27}_{\text{non-negative}})w^2$$
which is non-negative.

Since we have proved that
$M(0)\ge M(w)$
, it follows from
$(1)$
that
$$|z^3+az+b|\le \sqrt{M(0)-(a+1)^2}=\sqrt 3\ |a-3|.\ \blacksquare$$

question:
Let
$z\in \mathbb{C}$
satisfy
$|z^2+1|\le 2$
. I am interested in sharp inequalities of the form
$$|z^3+az+b|\le c$$

where
$a,b,c\in\mathbb Z$
, and where
$c$
is the best possible constant (The inequality is sharp). I conjecture that

$$|z^2+1|\le 2 \implies |z^3+az+b|\le a+b+1, \quad |b| \ge \operatorname{ceil}\left(\frac{a^{2}}{6}+ \frac{1}{2}\right) - 1$$

and that the constant
$a+b+1$
is sharp with equality at
$z=1$
(I think a proof will exist along the lines of
SOS
). There is also the reflected family

$$|z^2+1|\le 2\implies|z^3+az-b|\le a+b+1,$$

with equality at
$z=-1$
. My questions are (1) Is the above family correct? (2) Are there other infinite families of integer triples
$(a,b,c)$
for which

$|z^2+1|\le 2 \implies |z^3+az+b|\le c$
holds sharply? I think the correct approach would be SOS for this problem, as ordinary triangle inequality estimates seem far too weak to give a sharp bound.


Edit:
Here
is a cool visualization if it helps. (Thanks to @mathlove, if you zoom in the graph for
$a=2$
,
$b=1$
, you will see the inequality doesn't hold.)
answer:
Here I provide some preliminary work, which, as I hope, can help to solve the general case, when
$a,b\in\mathbb R$
.

Abdullah's
answer
,
suggests that by the maximum principle, it suffices to find the maximum of
$|z^3+az+b|$
on the boundary
$|z^2+1|=2$
.

The main problem which I encountered is to find a good parametrization.
After several tries, the most concise is the following.

Let
$z=x+iy$
,
$x,y\in\mathbb R$
.

Then

$$2^2=|z^2+1|^2=|(x+iy)^2+1|^2=|x^2-y^2+1+2xyi|^2=(x^2-y^2+1)^2+4x^2y^2=$$
$$x^4+y^4+1+2x^2y^2+2x^2-2y^2=(x^2+y^2+1)^2-4y^2,$$
so
$x^2+y^2+1=2\sqrt{1+y^2}$
and
$x^2+(\sqrt{1+y^2}-1)^2=1$
.

Let
$t=\sqrt{1+y^2}\ge 1$
. Then
$y^2=t^2-1$
and
$x^2=2t-t^2$
, so
$t\le 2$
.

Mathcad yields

$$|z^3+az+b|^2=$$
$$y^6+(3x^2-2a)y^4+(3x^4-6bx+a^2)y^2+x^6+2ax^4+2bx^3+a^2x^2+2abx+b^2=$$
$$2b(2t+a-4t^2+3)x+(8-8a)t^3+(12a-12)t^2+(2a^2+6)t-a^2-2a-1+b^2.$$

So we have to maximize

$$M=2b(2t+a-4t^2+3)x+(8-8a)t^3+(12a-12)t^2+(2a^2+6)t$$

Since
$x^2+(t-1)^2=1$
, we can put
$x=\sin\alpha$
and
$t=1+\cos\alpha$
,
$-\frac{\pi}2\le\alpha\le\frac{\pi}2$
.
Let
$w=\tan\frac\alpha{2}$
, so
$-1\le w\le 1$
.
Then
$x=\frac{2w}{1+w^2}$
and
$t=1+\cos\psi=1+\frac{1-w^2}{1+w^2}=\frac 2{1+w^2}$
.
Mathcad yields that
$\frac M4$
is equal to
$$\frac {(ab+3b)w^5+(a^2+3)w^4+(2ab+10b)w^3+(2a^2+12a-6)w^2+(ab-9b)w+a^2-4a+7}{(1+w^2)^3}.$$

question:
Below is a problem I did and the answer matches the back of the book. Therefore, I believe it is right. I would like feed back on my style. Also, did I make an error like, I used
$Y_1$
but I should have used
$y_1$
.

Problem:

Let
$Y_1$
and
$Y_2$
be independent and uniformly distributed over the interval
$[0,1]$
. Find the

(a)
$U_1 = \min( Y_1, Y_2 )$

(b)
$U_2 = \max( Y_1, Y_2 )$

Answer: (a)

Let
$U_1 = \min( Y_1, Y_2)$
and let
$u_1$
be a possible value of
$U_1$
. Let
$F_1$
be the
distribution function for
$U_1$
. First we will consider the case where
$0 \leq u_1 \leq 1$
. We
have:

\begin{align*}
P( U_1 \leq u_1 ) &=
P ( Y_1 \leq u_1 ) + P(Y_2 \leq u_1 ) - P ( Y_1 \leq u_1 )P ( Y_2 \leq u_1 ) \\
P( Y_1 \leq u_1 ) &= u_1 \\
P(Y_2 \leq u_1 ) &= u_1 \\
P( U_1 \leq u_1 ) &= u_1 + u_1 - u_1 u_1 = 2u_1 - u_1^2
\end{align*}

Hence the distribution function we are looking for is:

$$ F_{1} (u_1) = \begin{cases}
0					& u_1 \leq 0 \\
2u_1 - u_1^2		& \text{ for } 0 \leq u_1 \leq 1 \\
1					& u_1 > 1
\end{cases} $$

Now to find the density function, we differentiate the distribution function. This gives
us:

$$ f_{1} (u_1) = \begin{cases}
2( 1 - u_1)		& \text{ for } 0 \leq u_1 \leq 1 \\
0				&  \text{ otherwise }
\end{cases} $$


Part (b):


Let
$u_2 = \max( y_1, y_2)$
. Let
$F_2$
be the distribution function for
$U_2$
. First we will
consider the case where
$0 \leq u_2 \leq 1$
. We have:

\begin{align*}
P( U_2 \leq u_2 ) &= P ( Y_1 \leq u_2 ) P(Y_2 \leq u_2 ) \\
P ( Y_1 \leq u_2 ) &= u_2 \\
P( Y_2 \leq u_2 ) &= u_2 \\
P( U_2 \leq u_2 ) &= u_2 u_2 = u_2 ^ 2
\end{align*}

Hence the distribution function we are looking for is:

$$ F_{2} (u_2) = \begin{cases}
0					& u_2 \leq 0 \\
u_2^2				& \text{ for } 0 \leq u_2 \leq 1 \\
1					& u_2 > 1
\end{cases} $$

Now to find the density function, we differentiate the distribution function. This gives
us:

$$ f_{2} (u_2) = \begin{cases}
2u_2				& \text{ for } 0 \leq u_2 \leq 1 \\
0					& \text{ otherwise }
\end{cases} $$
answer:
I think your solution is fine, with no major issues. One small, stylistic detail is that you say that you'll handle the case
$0 \le u_1 \le 1$
first, then sort of tack on
$F_1(u_1) = 1$
for
$u_1 > 1$
almost as an afterthought. It's okay because we know that the joint support of
$Y_1, Y_2$
is on the unit square
$[0,1]^2$
, so the support of
$U_1$
and
$U_2$
are also similarly restricted. But in terms of presentation, it could benefit from at least a minor note.

Personally, my style of solution would be to consider
$$F_{U_1}(u_1) = \Pr[U_1 \le u_1] = 1 - \Pr[U_1 > u_1] = 1 - \Pr[Y_1 > u_1]\Pr[Y_2 > u_1] = 1 - (1-u_1)^2,$$
only because I like symmetry:  looking at
$\min(Y_1, Y_2)$
is like looking at
$\max(1-Y_1, 1-Y_2)$
in the uniformly distributed case.

question:
My background
(from Hatcher - Algebraic topology): A spectrum
$K=\{K_n\}$
is a sequence of CW-complexes with maps
$\Sigma K_n \to K_{n+1}$
. A spectrum induces a reduced homology theory as
$\tilde{h}_i(X):=\text{dirlim}_{n}\  \pi_{i+n}(X \wedge K_n)$
.

An
$\Omega$
-spectrum
$K'=\{K'_n\}$
is a sequence of CW-complexes with homotopy equivalences
$K'_n\simeq \Omega K'_{n+1}$
. An
$\Omega$
-spectrum induces an unreduced cohomology theory as
$h^n(X):=[X,K'_n]$
where
$[-,-]$
here means continuous maps up to homotopy.

A spectrum
$K=\{K_n\}$
induces an
$\Omega$
-spectrum
$K'=\{K'_n\}$
defined as
$K'_n:=\text{dirlim}_i \ \Omega^i K_{n+i}$
, and so a unreduced cohomology theory
$h^i(-)$
.

Given a spectrum
$K=\{K_n\}$
, we define
$\pi_n(K):=\text{dirlim}_i \ \pi_{i+n}(K_{i})$
.

Question:
Let
$K=\{K_n\}$
be a spectrum and let
$h^i(-)$
be the cohomology theory given by the induced
$\Omega$
-spectrum
$K'=\{K'_n\}$
. Is it true that
$h^n(*)=\pi_{-n}(K)$
?

My attempt:

$h^n(*):=[*,K'_n] = \langle S^0, K'_n\rangle$
where
$\langle {-}, {-} \rangle$
are pointed continuous maps up to homotopy, and this is
$\pi_0(K'_n)=\pi_0(\text{dirlim}_i(\Omega^i K_{n+i}))$
. Now in
this question
they proved that in general
$\pi_0(-)$
and
$\text{dirlim}$
don't commute, one needs extra hypotheses, for example that the transition maps are embeddings. But if that is the case, then we would have
$\text{dirlim}_i(\pi_0(\Omega^i K_{n+i}))=\text{dirlim}_i(\pi_i(K_{n+i}))$
. Now call
$j=n+i$
and we get
$\text{dirlim}_i(\pi_i(K_{n+i}))= \text{dirlim}_{j}(\pi_{j-n}(K_j))=:\pi_{-n}(K)$
.

Assuming therefore that
$\pi_0(-)$
and
$\text{dirlim}$
commute here seems sufficient to prove this result. Do they commute here? Is this argument the standard one, or are there different approaches that don't need this commutativity property?

In particular I'm interested in the Morava
$K$
-theory case. If I'm not mistaken, Morava
$k$
-theory
$K(n)$
at
$p$
is a spectrum
$K(n)$
, and in this case in literature people often already assume that
$K(n)^q(*)=\pi_{-q}(K(n))$
.
answer:
I don't know where you picked up that
$K'_n$
should be
$\operatorname{colim}_i \Omega^i K_{n + i}$
(I cannot bring myself to write
$\operatorname{dirlim}$
, forgive me), but this isn't morally the right thing: A directed colimit need not be invariant under homotopy, but you're clearly trying to do something homotopy invariant.

The solution is to replace the
$\operatorname{colim}$
by a
$\operatorname{hocolim}$
, that is, a sequential homotopy colimit. Then your headache goes away:
$\pi_n$
commutes with sequential homotopy colimits. In fact, you can model this homotopy colimit either as a mapping telescope or by replacing sufficiently many maps in your diagram by cofibrations and then taking the ordinary colimit.

...which, if the maps
$K_n \to \Omega K_{n + 1}$
turn out to be cofibrations to begin with, then you're already good. But they don't have to be: A cofibration is in particular injective, but these maps may not be (and being injective is not enough, nor is being an embedding, although
$\pi_*$
happens to commute with, say, colimits over closed embeddings of Hausdorff spaces even when they are not cofibrations).

Generally, if you're aiming to read about stable homotopy theory, then Hatcher does not prepare you well. If your background is roughly textbook-level algebraic topology, then part 3 of Adams'
Blue Book
is
the
standard introduction to the subject. It is quite old, but also quite elementary, and it covers all of the bedrock theory.

...and yeah, this is bedrock trivial fact on the level of stable homotopy theory, and nobody outside an introductory text would even think of justifying it.

As a final piece of (admittedly unasked-for) advice, even for stable homotopy theory the Morava
$K$
-theories are not exactly elementary fare. It's never wrong to read a little about something that interests you, of course, but it might be a good idea to postpone any serious study until you're comfortable with the basic language of stable homotopy theory.

question:
In Chapter 1 of Waclaw Sierpinski's
Elementary Theory of Numbers
, among the very first theorems are basic properties of the divisibility relation
$\mid$
on
$\mathbb{Z}$
. One of them is closure under integer linear combinations:

$$
d \mid a \ \text{and} \ d \mid b \ \Longrightarrow\ d \mid (ax+by) \quad \forall\,x,y\in\mathbb{Z}.
$$


Let
$R$
be a binary relation on
$\mathbb{Z}$
satisfying the same condition:


$$
d R a \ \text{and} \ d R b \ \Longrightarrow\ d R (ax+by) \quad \forall\,x,y\in\mathbb{Z}. \tag{1}
$$


Without further axioms,
$R$
need not be ordinary divisibility - the total relation (all pairs) and the empty relation satisfy (1) trivially.


Consider the following properties, all of which hold for
$\mid$
:




Reflexivity:

$a R a$
for all
$a$
.


Unit property:

$1 R a$
for all
$a$
.


Zero property:

$a R 0$
for all
$a$
.




I was able to show these three together with (1) do not force
$R$
to be
$\mid$
.


Argument
: the total relation (
$d R a$
for every
$d,a$
) still satisfies (1) and all three properties, so the axioms so far are not enough.


The standard boundedness property from Sierpinski's theorem states:


$$
\text{if } d \mid a \text{ and } a \neq 0, \text{ then } |d| \le |a|.
$$


Let us add its analogue for
$R$
:

$$
d R a \ \text{and} \ a \neq 0 \ \Longrightarrow\ |d| \le |a|. \tag{2}
$$

We also need to fix the behaviour at
$0$
, because
$\mid$
satisfies
$0 \mid a \iff a = 0$
. Therefore impose:


$$
0 R a \ \Longleftrightarrow\ a = 0. \tag{3}
$$


Claim.

If a binary relation
$R$
on
$\mathbb{Z}$
satisfies
(1),
reflexivity
, (2)
and
(3),
then
$R$
coincides exactly with ordinary divisibility
$\mid$
.


Proof (sketch):
From (1) and
reflexivity
(which ensures
$d \in A_d$
, so
$A_d \neq \emptyset$
), the set
$A_d = \{\,a \in \mathbb{Z} \mid d R a\,\}$
is an ideal of
$\mathbb{Z}$
. Hence
$A_d = (k_d)$
for a unique non‑negative integer
$k_d$
(where
$k_d=0$
explicitly yields the zero ideal
$A_d = \{0\}$
), meaning
$d R a \iff k_d \mid a$
.


Reflexivity forces
$d \in A_d$
, so
$k_d \mid d$
. If
$d \neq 0$
, then
$k_d \neq 0$
and
$|k_d| \le |d|$
. Picking
$a = k_d \in A_d \setminus \{0\}$
, property (2) yields
$|d| \le |k_d|$
. Hence
$|k_d| = |d|$
, so
$k_d = |d|$
and
$A_d = (|d|) = (d)$
. For
$d = 0$
, property (3) directly gives
$A_0 = \{0\} = (0)$
. Thus, in all cases,
$d R a \iff d \mid a$
.
$\square$


Notice that the unit property and the zero property are now consequences of the other axioms, so they need not be assumed separately.


Is the axiom set
$\{(1), \text{reflexivity}, (2), (3)\}$
minimal to force
$R = \mid$
? Can any of these be weakened, or can (2) be replaced by a weaker principle (for instance, one that still forces
$k_d$
to equal
$|d|$
but without explicitly invoking the ordering of absolute values)?


Are there other known characterizations of divisibility on
$\mathbb{Z}$
via closure properties and order-like conditions that are even more concise?

All references or explicit counterexamples showing the independence of the axioms are welcome as well.
answer:
Even if we re-include the unit and zero properties as axioms, I don't believe any of (1), reflexivity, (2), or (3) can be weakened at all without admitting other relations besides divisibility.

Note crucially that the relation
$R$
is completely determined by the sets
$A_d$
for
$d\in\Bbb Z$
(that’s obvious) but moreover that
the sets
$A_d$
can be chosen completely independently of one another
, since none of the axioms or properties constrain one
$A_d$
based on any of the others. This makes constructing “counterexamples” of modified axiom sets much easier to contemplate.

For example, suppose there is a single violation of (1), by which I mean a single integer
$n$
that is of the form
$ax+by$
, yet
$dRa$
and
$dRb$
and
$\lnot dRn$
. (And suppose this violation still obeys all the other axioms.) Then we can take
$R$
to be the usual divisibility relation except that
$A_d$
is replaced by
$A_d\setminus\{n\}$
.

From now on, we don’t alter (1), which with the zero property implies (as in the OP) that
$A_d$
must always be the set of multiples of a fixed integer (depending on
$d$
).

If there is a single failure of reflexivity, say
$\lnot a_0Ra_0$
, then we take
$R$
to be usual divisibility except that
$A_{a_0}$
is chosen to be
$\{0\}$
.
If there is a single
$d_0$
for which (2) fails, then we take
$R$
to be divisibility except that
$A_{d_0}$
is chosen to be
$\Bbb Z$
.
If there is a single failure
$0Ra_0$
of (3) with
$a_0\ne0$
, then we take
$R$
to be divisibility except that
$A_{a_0}$
is chosen to be all multiples of
$a_0$
.

question:
Problem:
Let
$X_1,X_2,\dots$
be independent random variables. Let
$\sum X_n^2$
converges almost surely (a.s.), and
$\sum\mathbb{E}[X_n \mathbf{1}_{|X_n|\le 1}]$
converges. It is to prove that series
$\sum X_n$
converges a.s.

Solution:
Since
$\sum X_n^2<\infty$
a.s., then
$$\sum\mathbb{E}X_n^2<\infty,\quad\sum\mathbb{P}(|X_n|>1)<\infty,\quad\sum\mathbb{E}[X_n^2\bf{1}_{|X_n|\le 1}]<\infty.\qquad (1)$$

For all
$n\ge 1$
, using Jensen inequality, one gets

$$\sum_{j=1}^n\mathbb{D}[X_j\mathbf{1}_{|X_j|\le 1}] = \sum_{j=1}^n\mathbb{E}[X_j^2\mathbf{1}_{|X_j|\le 1}] - \sum_{j=1}^n(\mathbb{E}[X_j\mathbf{1}_{|X_j|\le 1}])^2 \le
\sum_{j=1}^n\mathbb{E}[X_j^2\mathbb{1}_{|X_j|\le 1}] - \frac{1}{n}\Bigl(\sum_{j=1}^n\mathbb{E}[X_j\mathbf{1}_{|X_j|\le 1}]\Bigr)^2.\qquad (2)$$

Since
$\sum\mathbb{E}[X_n\mathbf{1}_{|\xi_n|\le 1}]<\infty$
, in limits as
$n\to\infty$
one gets

$$\sum_{j=1}^\infty\mathbb{D}X_j\mathbf{1}_{|X_j|\le 1} \le \sum_{j=1}^\infty\mathbb{E}X_j^2\mathbf{1}_{|X_j|\le 1} < \infty.\qquad (3)$$

Thus, all three series
$\sum\mathbb{D}[X_n\mathbf{1}_{|X_n|\le 1}], \sum\mathbb{E}[X_n\mathbf{1}_{|X_n|\le 1}]$
and
$\sum\mathbb{P}(|X_n|>1)$
converge. Therefore series
$\sum X_n$
converges a.s.

My Question:
Is it possible to solve the problem without using the Jensen inequality?
answer:
A better look at
Kolmogorov's three series theorem
would tell you that
$\sum X_{n}$
converges if and only if for some
$A>0$
, we have

i.
$\sum P(|X_{n}|\geq A)<\infty$

ii.
$\sum E(X_{n}\mathbf{1}_{|X_{n}|\leq A})$
converges

iii.
$\sum \text{Var}(X_{n}\mathbf{1}_{|X_{n}|\leq A})<\infty$

and if such a conclusion holds for atleast one
$A>0$
, it must hold for all
$A>0$
.

So infact, as you are given that
$\sum X_{n}^{2}$
converges, we can say that for
$A=1$
, the following holds:

(i).
$\sum P(|X_{n}|^{2}\geq 1)=\sum P(|X_{n}|\geq 1)< \infty$

(ii).
$\sum E(X_{n}^{2}\mathbf{1}_{|X_{n}|\leq 1})<\infty$

(iii).
$\sum\text{Var}(X_{n}^{2}\mathbf{1}_{|X_{n}|\leq 1})<\infty$

But now, (ii) gives you
$\sum\text{Var}(X_{n}\mathbf{1}_{|X_{n}|\leq 1})\leq \sum E(X_{n}^{2}\mathbf{1}_{|X_{n}|\leq 1})<\infty$

You are already given that
$\sum E(X_{n}\mathbf{1}_{|X_{n}|\leq 1})$
converges.

Thus, combining these facts, we get that
$\sum X_{n}$
converges almost surely as the conditions of Kolmogorov's three series theorem are satisfied for
$A=1$
.

question:
What exactly is a 2D normal vector function? Why does it form a circle if it's the normal vector function an ellipse?

Please excuse me if I'm missing something obvious - I can't find anything about it and my math education ended in multivariable calculus. I was playing around with ellipses and normal vector functions (Context):

For a 2D ellipse centered at
$(0,0)$
defined as
$\frac{x^2}{A^2} + \frac{y^2}{B^2}=1$
where A and B determine its horizontal/vertical stretch:
Parameterize as a vector function
$\overrightarrow{r(t)} = \begin{bmatrix} A \cos(t) \\ B \sin(t)\end{bmatrix}, t \in[0, 2\pi]$

Find the derivative of the vector function
$\overrightarrow{r(t)}$
:
$$\overrightarrow{r'(t)}= \begin{bmatrix} -Asin(t) \\ Bcos(t)\end{bmatrix},t\in[0,2\pi]$$
Find the normal vector function
$\overrightarrow{n(t)}$
:
$$ \overrightarrow{n(t)}= \begin{bmatrix}
\frac{y'(t)}{||\overrightarrow{r'(t)}||} \\
-\frac{x'(t)}{||\overrightarrow{r'(t)}||} \end{bmatrix}= \begin{bmatrix} \frac{B\cos(t)}{\sqrt{(-A\sin(t))^2+(B\cos(t))^2} } \\ -\frac{-A\sin(t)}{\sqrt{(-A\sin(t))^2+(B\cos(t))^2} } \end{bmatrix} , t\in[0,2\pi] $$




So what I noticed is that for



$t\in[0,2\pi]$



$A\in(-\infty,0)\cup(0,\infty)$



$B\in(-\infty,0)\cup(0,\infty)$



The following is true:



$\overrightarrow{n(t)}$
forms a closed continuous curve which perfectly traces a circle with radius 1, described by
$x^2+y^2=1$
.



Why? This is true about the normal vector function for any ellipse, so why? Does this apply to the normal vector functions of other 2D polygons? My thought here is that an ellipse is basically just a transformed (through being stretched) circle, so the normal vector function represents a normalization to the pre-transformed form... is this true of other shapes? (Questions)






To further demonstrate what I mean with the normal vector function forming a circle, here is an image of what I mean. Note that the arbitrary values used here are
$A=3, B=2$
so the ellipse used is
$\frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1$
. (Also note that the ellipse is green, the circle formed by the normal vector function is blue (as are the normal vectors starting at (0,0,0) ), and the normal vectors starting at points on the ellipse are red):
answer:
The normal vectors are all one unit long. Because the ellipse is a smooth curve that makes a complete turn, it has normal vectors in every direction in the plane.

The unit circle is a circle of radius
$1$
centered at the origin, which means it consists of points one unit from the origin in all directions.

Therefore the normals of an ellipse are precisely the vectors you need if you want to produce the unit circle.

Note that you can get the same result from the normals of any figure that makes a complete turn in a continuous manner around a loop.
But a polygon makes discontinuous jumps in direction and has only a finite number of distinct normals so its normals do not produce a circle.
A curve that changes direction continuously, but that does not make a full (
$360$
-degree) turn left or right, might have normals that generate a circular arc rather than a complete circle. For example, running along a figure-
$8$
path, you turn over
$180$
degrees in one direction and then turn back. The normal vectors swing through the same angles. And of course an open curve such as a parabola could have normals that generate a circular arc. (A parabola generates a semicircle.)

question:
What exactly is a 2D normal vector function? Why does it form a circle if it's the normal vector function an ellipse?

Please excuse me if I'm missing something obvious - I can't find anything about it and my math education ended in multivariable calculus. I was playing around with ellipses and normal vector functions (Context):

For a 2D ellipse centered at
$(0,0)$
defined as
$\frac{x^2}{A^2} + \frac{y^2}{B^2}=1$
where A and B determine its horizontal/vertical stretch:
Parameterize as a vector function
$\overrightarrow{r(t)} = \begin{bmatrix} A \cos(t) \\ B \sin(t)\end{bmatrix}, t \in[0, 2\pi]$

Find the derivative of the vector function
$\overrightarrow{r(t)}$
:
$$\overrightarrow{r'(t)}= \begin{bmatrix} -Asin(t) \\ Bcos(t)\end{bmatrix},t\in[0,2\pi]$$
Find the normal vector function
$\overrightarrow{n(t)}$
:
$$ \overrightarrow{n(t)}= \begin{bmatrix}
\frac{y'(t)}{||\overrightarrow{r'(t)}||} \\
-\frac{x'(t)}{||\overrightarrow{r'(t)}||} \end{bmatrix}= \begin{bmatrix} \frac{B\cos(t)}{\sqrt{(-A\sin(t))^2+(B\cos(t))^2} } \\ -\frac{-A\sin(t)}{\sqrt{(-A\sin(t))^2+(B\cos(t))^2} } \end{bmatrix} , t\in[0,2\pi] $$




So what I noticed is that for



$t\in[0,2\pi]$



$A\in(-\infty,0)\cup(0,\infty)$



$B\in(-\infty,0)\cup(0,\infty)$



The following is true:



$\overrightarrow{n(t)}$
forms a closed continuous curve which perfectly traces a circle with radius 1, described by
$x^2+y^2=1$
.



Why? This is true about the normal vector function for any ellipse, so why? Does this apply to the normal vector functions of other 2D polygons? My thought here is that an ellipse is basically just a transformed (through being stretched) circle, so the normal vector function represents a normalization to the pre-transformed form... is this true of other shapes? (Questions)






To further demonstrate what I mean with the normal vector function forming a circle, here is an image of what I mean. Note that the arbitrary values used here are
$A=3, B=2$
so the ellipse used is
$\frac{x^2}{9}+\frac{y^2}{4}=1$
. (Also note that the ellipse is green, the circle formed by the normal vector function is blue (as are the normal vectors starting at (0,0,0) ), and the normal vectors starting at points on the ellipse are red):
answer:
the vector
$\vec r(t) = (x(t), y(t))$
, when drawn from the origin, places the terminal point on the ellipse, so serves as a parametrization of that ellipse. I think this much you can immediately see.

The vector
$\vec r'(t)$
is a tangent vector to the ellipse. Geometrically, if we draw this vector with its initial point
on
the ellipse at the terminal point of
$\vec r(t)$
, then its direction will be on the tangent line through
$\vec r(t)$
. Since a line points in two directions, which way does the vector
$\vec r'(t)$
point? It goes in the direction of increasing
$t$
along the curve. What does the
length
of
$\vec r'(t)$
represent? Its magnitude in a sense represents the instantaneous rate at which the terminal point of vector
$r(t)$
is "moving" or "changing" as
$t$
increases. These properties are directly inherited from the familiar differential calculus of a single variable function.

But what if we put the initial point of
$\vec r'(t)$
at the
origin
? Then the terminal point of
$\vec r'(t)$
will trace out a curve in the plane that might or might not look related to the "original" curve that is traced out by
$\vec r(t)$
. You are encouraged to do some sample plots of
$\vec r'(t)$
for various choices of
$\vec r(t)$
in this manner, as well as in the "usual" manner where we place the initial point of
$\vec r'$
at the terminal point of
$\vec r$
.

Now, where it comes to the
normal
vector
$\vec n(t)$
, a similar phenomenon applies. We can either choose to place its initial point at the origin (as you have done), or you can place it at the terminal point of
$\vec r(t)$
.

To construct
$\vec n(t)$
, it's very natural to simply compute the perpendicular vector to the tangent vector
$\vec r'(t)$
. The only thing that your formula has additionally done (that is different than the tangent vector
$\vec r'(t)$
) , is that it is a
unit
normal vector, where its length has been modified so as to be
$1$
.

So in this context, it is easy to see why when you plot
$\vec n(t)$
such that its initial point is at the origin, you will get a parametrization of the unit circle. And if you plot
$\vec n(t)$
with its initial point at the terminal point
$\vec r(t)$
, then you get the analogous geometric interpretation to
$\vec r'(t)$
where
$\vec n(t)$
points in a perpendicular direction to the curve.

Now, does this mean that
$\vec n(t)$
always travels in a continuous counterclockwise direction around the unit circle? No; you can easily think of some constructions such that
$\vec n(t)$
might even reverse direction as it travels around the unit circle.

question:
I found a rather unique number theory question in an
old thread
, but need some help in answering it.

Let
$p(x)\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$
such that
$\forall n\in\mathbb{Z}$
, the sum of the decimal digits of
$\lvert p(n) \rvert$
is not a Fibonacci number. Must
$p(x)$
be constant?

I believe the answer is yes, but struggling to prove it. My initial thought was to investigate the Fibonaccis modulo 9 since the digit sum of an integer is congruent to the integer (mod
$9$
). We can see that the Fibonacci numbers are surjective modulo 9

$$\begin{align}
F_1=1\equiv1&\mod9 \\
F_3=2\equiv2&\mod9 \\
F_4=3\equiv3&\mod9 \\
F_5=5\equiv5&\mod9 \\
F_6=8\equiv8&\mod9 \\
F_7=13\equiv4&\mod9 \\
F_9=34\equiv7&\mod9 \\
F_{12}=144\equiv0&\mod9 \\
F_{16}=987\equiv6&\mod9 \\
\end{align}$$

Since every residue class modulo 9 is hit and the Fibonacci numbers are periodic for every residue class modulo 9, there exists infinitely many Fibonacci numbers in each residue class. The periodicity claim is a pretty standard result and it is true for any fixed modulus. MathWorld calls it "
Pisano Period
."

This line of reasoning seems very relevant to the problem, but from here I am not sure how to force a contradiction for a nonconstant polynomial. How can one proceed?
answer:
Thanks to the comment from anankElpis, I found the original source and official solution for this problem:
Problem 6 of 2019 US TSTST
, proposed by Nikolai Beluhov.

I was missing a second lemma,

If
$p(x)\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$
is nonconstant with positive leading coefficient, then there exists an integer polynomial
$F(x)$
such that all coefficients of
$P\circ F$
are positive except for the second one, which is negative.

If
$p(x)\in\mathbb{Z}[x]$
is nonconstant with positive leading coefficient, then there exists an integer polynomial
$F(x)$
such that all coefficients of
$P\circ F$
are positive except for the second one, which is negative.

The official solution linked above shows how to construct such an
$F$
for any
$P$
. Then we arrive at
$$S(P(F(10^e)))=9e+k,$$
for some constant
$k$
where
$S$
denotes the sum of decimal digits. This must eventually hit a Fibonacci number from the claim laid out in my question, and we are done. I've brushed over the details, but I hope this is a decent sketch of the remaining proof. Namely there are some subtleties regarding the number of negative coefficients, but the link above explains everything much better than I ever could.

I thought it was interesting that the question specified decimal digits, and it turns out the base is actually relevant.
$p(x)$
need not be constant in base
$b$
if surjectivity modulo
$b-1$
does not hold.

This paper provides conditions for such
$b$
https://arxiv.org/abs/1304.2892v3
, and Ritwin at AoPS
provided
the first few bases where this holds
$$2,3,4,5,6,7,8,10,11,15,16,21,26,28,\dots.$$

question:
Recently I've come across this problem:

Show that in any set of
$n$
integers, there is a nonempty subset whose sum is divisible by
$n$
.
Here

We can prove it by introducing
$$S_k = \sum_{i=1}^{k} a_i, \qquad k = 0, 1, \dots, n$$


Suppose no one is divisible by
$n$
, then there must exist
$0 \le i < j \le n$
such that
$S_i \equiv S_j \pmod{n}$
. So
$S_j - S_i = a_{i+1} + a_{i+2} + \cdots + a_j \equiv 0 \pmod{n}$
. Otherwise there exists
$S_k$
such that
$n \mid S_k$
.


Later, I came up with another idea:




In any set of
$(n+1)$
integers, there is at least
$3$
nonempty subset whose sum is divisible by
$n$
.




For example, when
$n=3$
, Let
$A=\{0,1,4,7\}$
, we can find that
$\{0,1,4,7\}$
,
$\{1,4,7\}$
,
$\{0\}$
are divisible by
$3$
.


I want to know if this is true and how to prove this.
I also wonder how to generalize it into
$(n+k)$
integers.
answer:
Here's a quick proof of the stated problem:

Let
$S$
be your set with
$n+1$
elements. Remove one element,
$s\in S$
, chosen at random. Let
$S_1=S-\{s\}$
, so
$|S_1|=n$
. By the prior theorem, we can find a non empty subset
$X_1\subseteq S_1$
which sums to
$0\pmod n$
.

Now let
$x\in X_1$
be a randomly chosen element of
$X_1$
and let
$S_2=S-\{x\}$
. Again we have
$|S_2|=n$
so the prior result gives us a non-empty subset
$X_2\subseteq S_2$
which sums to
$0$
. Note that
$X_1$
can't equal
$X_2$
as the first contains
$x_1$
and the second does not.

Thus we have two distinct non-empty subsets of
$S$
which sum to
$0\pmod n$
.

To get a third, consider the intersection
$X_1\cap X_2$
. If it is empty, then
$X_1\cup X_2$
is a good third set. If
$y\in X_1\cap X_2$
then we can define
$S_y=S-\{y\}$
and find a good third set
$X_3$
in that. Clearly it can not coincide with either
$X_1$
or
$X_2$
as they both contain
$y$
and
$X_3$
does not.

Many thanks to commenter @Andre who pointed out a blunder in the first version of this proof and then promptly supplied the final argument needed (the construction of
$X_3$
).

question:
I am currently studying the space of ends of a non-compact surface
$S$
. I am familiar with the classical topological approach (often associated with Freudenthal), which defines ends using equivalence classes of suitable nested sequences of open subsets (or connected components of complements of compact sets).

However, I understand that the space of ends can also be studied through equivalence classes of proper rays. In this context, two proper rays
$\gamma_1, \gamma_2 \colon [0, \infty) \to S$
define the same end if and only if for any compact set
$K \subset S$
, there exists
$N > 0$
such that the image
$\gamma_1([N, \infty))$
and
$\gamma_2([N, \infty))$
are contained in the same connected component of
$S \setminus K$
.

I want to delve deeper into this specific point of view. My questions are:

What are some good references that systematically develop the topology of the space of ends of a non-compact surface strictly using classes of proper rays?

Are there texts that explicitly establish the relationship between this approach and the classical nested-sequences approach, detailing how the homeomorphism between the two resulting spaces of ends is constructed?
answer:
Take a look at
this paper of Mihalik
. Section 1 contains a (terse) history of the "ray" point of view on ends; and Section 2 contains the basic definitions. The point of view is rather general: in place of
$S$
, you can use any space which is a locally finite, connected CW complex.

One can be somewhat more general, using any space which is a locally compact, locally path connected, and connected, which is exactly the category of spaces in which Freudenthal's theorem behaves best. From those hypotheses, it follows that the whole space is path connected; it also follows that for every open subset, the components and the path components of that subset are the same thing. With that in mind, I would say that it is an exercise to prove the equivalence between Freudenthal ends and ends defined using rays as in your post (and in Mihalik's paper).

question:
I’ve been working through Waclaw Sierpinski's
Elementary Theory of Numbers
. Right at the start (Chapter 1, Section 1 one meets the fundamental boundedness property for integers:

If
$d \mid a$
and
$a \neq 0$
, then
$|d| \le |a|$
.

Formally this guarantees that the set of divisors of any non‑zero integer is finite. Computationally it gives us a deterministic search space for trial division. Of course, we immediately optimize that space from size
$|a|$
down to
$\sqrt{|a|}$
because
$a = d \cdot k$
and the absolute value is multiplicative:

$$
|a| = |d|\,|k| \quad\Longrightarrow\quad \min(|d|,|k|) \le \sqrt{|a|}.
$$


This observation leads me to a structural question.


Let
$R$
be an integral domain (or, more generally, a ring with some notion of 'size') equipped with a function
$f : R \setminus \{0\} \to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$
that is multiplicative, i.e.
$f(xy) = f(x)f(y)$
, and satisfies
$f(x) = 0 \iff x = 0$
. From multiplicativity, we obtain the same kind of divisor bound:


$$
d \mid a,\; a \neq 0 \;\Longrightarrow\; f(d) \le f(a),
$$


and one is tempted to reduce the search for divisors to
$\{d \mid f(d) \le \sqrt{f(a)}\}$
.


I am looking for some concrete, non‑contrived examples (e.g., from algebraic number theory or other familiar rings) where either the square‑root reduction fails (so the obvious bound
$f(d) \le f(a)$
does not shrink to
$\sqrt{f(a)}$
), or the reduction is formally valid but the search space remains intractable for a non‑obvious structural reason (e.g., infinitely many associate divisors with the same
$f$
‑value, so sieving up to
$\sqrt{f(a)}$
still leaves a class‑group or unit‑group problem that cannot be tamed by size alone).


Note:
"By 'intractable' in the context of generalized rings, I formally mean that either the fiber of the size function under a given bound is infinite (e.g., due to a rank
$\ge 1$
unit group via Dirichlet's Unit Theorem), or the cardinality of the search space scales exponentially with respect to the bit-length of the element's norm, preventing any polynomial-time deterministic search.


I have thought about a few candidates, as listed:




$\mathbb{Z}[i]$
(Gaussian integers) with the usual norm
$N(a+bi) = a^2+b^2$
: for these, multiplicativity holds, the square‑root bound works, and trial division up to
$\sqrt{N}$
is still possible, so it does not illustrate the kind of obstacle I have in mind.


Polynomial rings
$F[x]$
: the degree function acts as a multiplicative size, and again one can restrict to divisors of degree at most half of the degree of the dividend, which is very analogous to
$\mathbb{Z}$
.


Rings of integers in number fields with infinite unit groups: For a factorization
$a = d \cdot k$
, multiplicativity gives
$N(a) = N(d)N(k)$
, so at least one of the factors has norm
$\le \sqrt{N(a)}$
. Consequently, searching for elements of norm
$\le \sqrt{N(a)}$
is sufficient to detect a non‑trivial factor (if one exists). However, by Dirichlet's Unit Theorem, the unit group can be infinite (e.g., in real quadratic fields like
$\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{2}]$
). This means a given divisor
$d$
yields infinitely many associates
$u \cdot d$
with the exact same norm. Thus, even though we only need to examine norms up to
$\sqrt{N(a)}$
, the set of elements of bounded norm is infinite, so we cannot simply list them and test divisibility without extra machinery. Is this the standard textbook example where the square‑root bound holds but fails to provide a finite constructive search space without invoking the geometry of numbers?




I would be grateful for precise references or explicit constructions that clarify where the naive intuition from
$\mathbb{Z}$
breaks and how algebraic number theory or ring theory handles it.
answer:
This is more like a remark, hence CW. The "square‑root reduction" as you name it always holds for trivial reasons, and you can even relax multiplicativity to submultiplicativity
$f(xy) \leq f(x) f(y)$
.

Proof: If
$a=xy$
we get
$$f(a) \geq f(x) f(y) \geq \min(f(x),f(y)) \cdot \min(f(x),f(y)) = \min(f(x),f(y))^2$$
and hence
$\sqrt{f(a)} \geq \min(f(x),f(y))$
.

I will not answer "if the search space remains intractable" since this has no precise definition.

question:
Let
$p \in (0,1)$
,
$c > 0$
, and let
$(R_n)_n$
be a sequence of
nonnegative random variables. Define
$(V_n)_n$
recursively by

$$ V_{n+1} = p \min(c, V_n) + R_{n+1}, \qquad V_0 \ge 0 $$


Question:
Does there exist an example such that




$(R_n)_{n \ge 1}$
is a Markov chain,


For each
$n$
,
$R_n$
is a mixture of a Dirac mass at
$0$
and an absolutely continuous distribution supported on
$[0,\infty)$
,




for which the process
$(V_n)_n$
is
not
a Markov chain?


More generaly, do we know when does
$X_{n+1}=f(X_n,M_{n+1})$
inherit the Markov property from
$(M_n)_n$
?
answer:
There definitely is an example. Suppose the
$R_n$
sequence alternates between
$0$
and i.i.d. samples from some distribution. Then the conditional distribution of
$(V_{n + 1} \mid V_n = v, R_n = 0)$
looks very different from
$(V_{n + 1} \mid V_n = v, R_n \neq 0)$
. So, in particular,
$(V_{n + 1} \mid V_n = v, V_{n - 1} = v)$
looks different from
$(V_{n + 1} \mid V_n = v, V_{n - 1} \neq v)$
(for large enough
$v$
for the minimum to not matter), which violates the Markov property.

If you want an example where the
$R_n$
chain isn't periodic—the alternation gives it period
$2$
—then instead of alternating, have it switch between
$0$
and the i.i.d. samples with, say, probability
$1/100$
. That is, let the conditional distribution of
$(R_{n + 1} \mid R_n = 0)$
be "
$0$
with probability
$99/100$
, i.i.d. sample with probability
$1/100$
", and vice versa if
$R_n \neq 0$
.

question:
I am studying set theory right now, and my intuition is that the Axiom of Replacement is used to generate all sorts of sets starting from the Inductive set (from the Axiom of Infinity). We use the Axiom of Power Set to generate larger cardinalities (which also uses Replacement for other sets of similar size). However, for me, the Axiom of Infinity is weird, because it creates a specific type of infinity.

Are there constructions (possibly in other theories) that have infinities "non-isomorphic" to every repeated application of power set to the inductive set? I'm thinking maybe something outside the Von Neumann universe. I'm also unfamiliar with the Aleph-Omega things.
answer:
In
$\mathsf{ZF}$
- so we don't even need the axiom of choice! - every set is (in bijection with) a subset of some repeated powerset of
$\mathbb{N}$
. That said, there are three subtleties here.

First, the word "repeated" here needs to be clarified: we need to allow
arbitrary ordinal length applications
. Precisely, for
$\alpha$
an arbitrary ordinal we define
$\mathcal{P}^\alpha(\mathbb{N})$
by recursion as follows:

$\mathcal{P}^0(\mathbb{N})=\mathbb{N}$
.
$\mathcal{P}^{\alpha+1}(\mathbb{N})=\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{P}^\alpha(\mathbb{N}))$
.
If
$\lambda$
is a nonzero limit ordinal (e.g.
$\lambda=\omega$
) then
$\mathcal{P}^\lambda(\mathbb{N})=\bigcup_{\alpha<\lambda}\mathcal{P}^\alpha(\mathbb{N})$
.

$\mathcal{P}^0(\mathbb{N})=\mathbb{N}$
.

$\mathcal{P}^{\alpha+1}(\mathbb{N})=\mathcal{P}(\mathcal{P}^\alpha(\mathbb{N}))$
.

If
$\lambda$
is a nonzero limit ordinal (e.g.
$\lambda=\omega$
) then
$\mathcal{P}^\lambda(\mathbb{N})=\bigcup_{\alpha<\lambda}\mathcal{P}^\alpha(\mathbb{N})$
.

If we only allow
finite
iterates of the powerset, then we're obviously going to fall short of things like
$\mathcal{P}^\omega(\mathbb{N})$
. While tricky to visualize, this is a perfectly well-defined set.

The second subtlety is the role of the axiom of
foundation
(also called
regularity
). In the absence of this axiom, things can indeed be trickier: it's consistent with
$\mathsf{ZFC-Foundation}$
that there is a set onto which no
$\mathcal{P}^\alpha(\mathbb{N})$
surjects.

Finally, and to my mind most interestingly, the word "subset" in the first sentence is absolutely crucial even assuming choice. Let
$\beth_\alpha=\vert\mathcal{P}^\alpha(\mathbb{N})\vert$
. While it's easy to show that
$\aleph_\alpha\le\beth_\alpha$
, the converse is
a bit trickier
. For example, it's entirely possible that there is a set whose cardinality is
strictly between
that of
$\mathbb{N}$
and
$\mathcal{P}(\mathbb{N})$
. Such a set needs to be rather complicated - the subject of
descriptive set theory
in particular shows that there is no "easy-to-describe" set of real numbers with such intermediate cardinality - but they can exist. So there's real tension between the
title
of your question (which uses the word "larger") and the
body
(which merely says "non-isomorphic"). If we focus on the latter question we see fascinating possibilities even within
$\mathsf{ZFC}$
itself.
