Improve this question

I often see it recommended for learners to use multiple math textbooks to understand a topic. I've done this while self studying, and agree that it works. However, I've also noticed that many textbooks define concepts / construct objects differently, and it takes a while to read up on all the prerequisite material in the new textbook, before I can finally reach the section that covers the material I found confusing in the original textbook.

For example, I've been trying to understand the formal proof of the Change of Variables Theorem from Spivak's Calculus on Manifolds. I've switched to Munkres' Analysis on Manifolds, and found that I had to go back several chapters to understand Munkres' version of the proof, because he defines things differently to Spivak. This is fine, considering that I'm self studying, but I wonder if there's a more efficient way to go about it if I had to learn this for a university course. Surely there has to be a more efficient way to use a secondary textbook for clarification than reviewing multiple chapters of material, just to get to an explanation of a single confusing theorem?
answer:
This is really a matter of opinion, but one of the essential skills in mathematics is to learn how to "see" various concepts presented in different ways, not merely as a matter of understanding the subject, but also because it is a reflection of how mathematics is communicated in practice.

To provide an analogy, within a language, there are often distinct dialects of that language, in which some vocabulary, pronunciation, or other linguistic features may be different. The extent of mutual intelligibility may be broad or narrow. The communication of mathematics is similar in that way; and so, in a practical sense, it is important for mathematicians or those interested in mathematical applications, to have some flexibility with respect to the ability to learn and adapt to different mathematical styles and presentations of a topic.

That said, a very popular approach to learning a particular topic in mathematics is to first pick a presentation or treatment by a specific author, and once some familiarity is gained, then broaden one's understanding by reading other authors in that area, for the reason of efficiency as you raised in your question. This is why, for instance, some learners gravitate toward certain texts and their choices of notation, because these are presented particularly clearly, or are "broken down" more. But not always--some learners might prefer a very terse presentation.

Concurrent readings on a topic by multiple authors can be counterproductive because it may take additional mental energy to go back and forth between their presentation styles, hindering the primary task of learning the concepts. But sometimes it is necessary, especially at a research level, which is why developing that flexibility is important.

My suggestion is to adopt the popular approach--pick a specific presentation and stick with that one until you gain some familiarity. Then go back and learn how others go about teaching it.

question:
I was playing mini-golf, and I started wondering about this geometry-styled question.

Given any putting green (smooth and continuous), any initial putting point, and any cup location, is it always possible that a putt could be made?

I’ve tried to imagine cases where this wouldn’t work, but I can’t convince myself that I’m not simply missing solutions. There is one exception to this question I have thought of - if the putting green forms a ramp where the golf ball would always fly over the cup is a case where no putt would be possible, but other than this I still wonder.

I’d appreciate any answers. I’m also very curious how this could be proved one way or another. Thanks!
answer:
Take this curve and extend perpendicular to the plane you're seeing it in:

Hitting the ball hard enough from the tee to get it over the "mountaintop" and roll down the far side will launch it off the 'ledge' and over the cup, after which it'll continue to roll/bounce to the right forever.

question:
Let
$G$
be a locally compact abelian group (or just discrete if it helps). Let
$I$
be a closed ideal in the Banach algebra
$L^1(G)$
and suppose that the Gelfand spectrum of
$L^1(G)/I$
is just a singleton. Is
$L^1(G)/I$
finite-dimensional? I suppose that yes and that it should be very standard.
Is there either an easy argument or an explicit reference?
answer:
This is a very concise answer that aims to give you the necessary keywords for your own research.

The spectrum of
$L^1(G)/I$
is
$\sigma(L^1(G)/I) = hull(I) = \{\gamma\in\sigma(L^1(G)): I\subseteq\ker\gamma\}$
. You can find this in, for example, Kaniuth's book on commutative Banach algebras.

Second, if
$E\subseteq\sigma(L^1(G))$
a closed subset, there exist two ideals
$$k(E) = \{f\in L^1(G): supp(\hat{f})\cap E=\emptyset \}$$
$$j(E) = \{f\in k(E): supp(\hat{f}) \textrm{ is compact}\}$$

where
$\hat{f}$
is the Fourier transform, such that if a closed ideal
$I$
satisfies
$hull(I) = E$
, then
$j(E)\subseteq I\subseteq k(E)$
. This is
Theorem 25D
in Loomis' book.

A closed set
$E\subseteq\sigma(L^1(G))$
is called a
set of synthesis
if
$\overline{j(E)} = k(E)$
. Every 1-element subset of
$\sigma(L^1(G))$
is a set of synthesis.

Combining these, if
$E=\{\gamma\}=hull(I)$
is a singleton, then
$I=k(E)=\ker\gamma$
, which is a maximal ideal. Thus,
$L^1(G)/I \simeq \mathbb{C}$
is 1 dimensional.

question:
Motivation

I was reading "Heat Kernels and Dirac Operators" by Berline,Getzler,and Vergne.
In this book,to show some properties about generalized Laplacian, it is necessary to define by yourself "L^2 norm" on the space of (not necessarily continuous) sections of E
$ \otimes$
|
$\varLambda$
|^(1/2) and to prove the completeness of the space w.r.t. the norm.
$  $
(E is a vector bundle on a compact manifold M,and |
$\varLambda$
|^(1/2)$is a half-density bundle.)
I proved that by defining trivial "L^2 inner product",taking local trivializations and reduced the problem to Euclidian case,but it is a coordinate-depending way. If such a measure as the title exists,(i.e.,if there is a pair of sigma-algebra and a measure which can define the same integration as normal one),the coordinate-depending discussion can be skipped.

Question

Are there such measure and sigma-algebra?
answer:
Any manifold
$M$
comes with a Borel
$\sigma$
-algebra
$\mathcal B(M)$
(the one generated by opens) and one can define a Borel measure
$\mu\colon \mathcal B(M)\to [0,\infty]$
to be
smooth
, if in any local coordinate system it looks like
$f(x)dx$
for a smooth function
$f$
, where
$dx$
is the Lebesgue measure in coordinates
$x=(x_1,\dots, x_d)$
. This notion is coordinate invariant (a change of coordinates changes
$f$
by a term of the form
$|\det D\psi(x)|$
, so it does not affect smoothness. By the same token, asking
$f(x)$
to be positive in all coordinate systems gives an invariant notion, let's then call
$\mu$
a
positive smooth measure
.

In general, there is no way to pin down such a positive smooth measure
$\mu$
uniquely. Just think about
$\mathbb R^d$
itself: the characterisation of Lebesgue measure requires in particular that it is translation invariant and gives the box
$[0,1]^d$
the measure
$1$
, so without specifying further structure we cannot really distinguish between different smooth positive measures.

Sometimes, one does not need to pin down a particular
$\mu$
: for example, all smooth positive measures are mutually absolutely continuous (more or less by definition), and so have the same null-sets. Moreover, if
$M$
happens to be compact, then e.g. the
$L^p$
-spaces for
$\mu$
do not depend on the choice (careful: the norm itself might depend on
$\mu$
, but a different choice leads to an equivalent norm).

Sometimes there is more structure and that fixes
$\mu$
: for example, if
$M$
has a Riemannian metric, there is the Riemannian volume measure
$\mu_g$
(in local coordinates it looks like
$\det (g_{ij} )^{1/2} dx$
) and if
$M$
is a Lie group, you can ask
$\mu$
to be invariant and obtain a Haar measure (this is the analogue of translation invariant--to completely fix it, you still have to declare what the measure of some open subset ought to be).

Instead of smooth positive measures, it is more common to speak of smooth positive densities (which naturally induce such a measure) - these are sections of the so-called density bundle on
$M$
. If
$M$
is oriented, it is further common to instead consider
volume forms
. But the comments about uniqueness etc. also hold with this rephrasing.

Edit: I just noticed that the question already mentions
$1/2$
-densities. These guys carry with them enough information to integrate and so no measure is needed. Given two
$1/2$
-densities
$f,g$
on
$M$
the product
$\mu=fg$
is a
$1$
-density (or simply density) and hence
$\int_M \mu = \int_M fg $
makes sense sense (if convergent).

question:
Let
$a,b$
be integers larger than
$0$
.

Are there any solutions to the following diophantine equation:

$$a^2 + 1 = b^5$$


??


This is one of the simplest diophantine equations because it only has
$1$
addition, no big numbers and
$2$
integer variables.


But its degree is
$5$
and I do not know how to solve it.


Maybe there is some
$\bmod n$
for which we can show it has no solutions.


I tried substitions and factoring, but without succes.


Factoring
$1$
over other rings usually does not work since
$1$
is a unit.
I can factor
$b^5 -1$
, but that did not help me.


Maybe factoring over a ring that are roots of unity ?




We can show that
$a$
can not be prime because of the factorization of
$b^5 -1$
.


So we get:


$$ a^2 c^2 + 1 = b^5 $$


if that may help.


Likewise:


$$a^2 c^2 + i$$


cannot be a prime over the gaussian integers.




edit


Two important notices :


I know this is a special case of Catalan conjecture.


But I want to look at this specificly.


the proof of the generalization; the Catalan conjecture
$a^x - b^y = 1$
by Preda V. Mihăilescu is a much more general conjecture and a very complicated proof.


Therefore I would like to see how to show there are no solutions more easily.


Secondly I wonder if a simple
$\mod n$
argument can solve this.
answer:
We can indeed work over the Gaussian integers. Write
$$(a+i)(a-i)=b^5$$
By considering
$\pmod{4}$
you can see that
$a$
must be even. If
$p$
is a prime of
$\mathbb{Z}[i]$
which divides both
$a+i$
and
$a-i$
then it would divide the difference, that is
$2i$
. Then it would divide
$2$
, hence it would divide
$a$
, therefore also
$a+i-a=i$
and we have a contradiction. Thus the factors are coprime and by unique factorization
$$(a+i)=u(x+iy)^5$$
for a unit
$u$
and rational integers
$x, y$
. Since each unit is also a perfect fifth power, we may assume
$u=1$
. Taking the imaginary part we get
$$1=5x^4y-10x^2y^3+y^5=y(5x^4-10x^2y^2+y^4)$$
which would imply
$y=1$
or
$y=-1$
, but you can check that
$5x^4-10x^2+1$
cannot be a unit and we are done. You may like to extend this to other exponents (different from
$5$
). The same approach works with some minor modifications.

$\textbf{Edit}$
regarding the
$\pmod{N}$
question:
I don't expect such an approach to work here, although if someone can prove me wrong I would be very interested. First, note that we always have the solution
$(0, 1)$
, so we would need an infinite family of natural numbers
$N$
such that
$(0, 1)$
or
$(u, 0)$
are the only solutions
$\pmod{N}$
. Note that taking
$\pmod{p^k}$
can't increase the exponent of
$p$
dividing
$a$
because generally for a
$p$
-adic integer
$a$
with
$\lvert a \rvert_p<1$
the "binomial theorem" holds (actually, we get a homomorphism in the sense that
$(a+1)^x(a+1)^y=(a+1)^{x+y}$
when everything converges):
$$b=(a^2+1)^{\frac 15}=\sum_{j\ge 0}\binom{\frac{1}{5}}{j}a^{2j}$$
which converges to a
$p$
-adic number. Taking
$\pmod{p^k}$
will yield solutions.
Also, for primes which are not
$1\pmod{5}$
$x^5$
is surjective
$\pmod{p}$
, while for the others nontrivial solutions seem to exist: we try small solutions. The following is in
$\mathbb{F_p}$
. If
$b=-1$
does not work, then
$-2$
is a quadratic non-residue. If
$b=3$
does not work, then
$242$
is a quadratic non-residue, so
$2$
is a quadratic non-residue, so
$-1$
is a quadratic residue. If
$b=2$
does not work, then
$31$
is a quadratic non-residue. If
$b=-2$
does not work, then
$-33$
is a quadratic non-residue, so
$33$
is a quadratic non-residue. But then
$31\cdot 33=4^5-1$
is a quadratic residue and we find a solution with
$b\equiv 4\pmod{p}$
. I think you generally get lots of solutions
$\pmod{p}$
, search the Hasse-Weil bound. I disclose that I know next to nothing about arithmetic geometry (though I plan to study it) or about the proof of that theorem, but it might apply.

question:
Part I.
This is the other half of the
previous post
. To recall, the square of the
triangular number

$T_n = \dfrac{n(n+1)}2$
appears in a sum of consecutive cubes,

$$\sum_{k=1}^n k^3 = 1^3+2^3+3^3+\dots+n^3 = \left(\frac{n(n+1)}2\right)^2$$


In 1961, Sierpienski proved that
infinitely
many Pythagorean triples may have
two
terms as triangular numbers. I don't know how he did it, but we showed in the previous post that,


$$x^2+\left(\frac{a(a+1)}2\right)^2=\left(\frac{b(b+1)}2\right)^2$$


can be given infinitely many polynomial solutions by the
Pell equation
,


$$p^2-(\color{red}{1+4n^4})q^2=-1$$




Part II.
It turns out its complementary equation,


$$\left(\frac{a(a+1)}2\right)^2+\left(\frac{b(b+1)}2\right)^2 = c^2$$


Equivalently,


$$\sum_{k=1}^a k^3 + \sum_{k=1}^b k^3 = c^2$$


can
also
be given infinitely many polynomial solutions to its
$(a,b,c)$
by the similar Pell equation,


$$p^2-(\color{red}{1+n^4})q^2=\mp1$$


Proof:
For some unknowns
$(u,v)$
let,


\begin{align}a &=n^2u-nu-1\\
b &=n(n^2u-nu-1)\\
c &=\frac{(n-1)bv}2\end{align}


where
$n\neq1$
to avoid trivial
$a=b=-1$
, then the complementary triple reduces to making a quadratic into a square,


$$1-2n^2u+(1+n^4)u^2=v^2=(p/qu+1)^2$$


This has solution,


$$u =\frac{{-2}(p+n^2q)q\quad}{p^2-(1+n^4)q^2}$$


For
$u$
to be an integer, then one must solve the Pell equation
$p^2-(1+n^4)q^2 = \mp1$
. Initial solutions are,


$$(p_1,q_1) = (n^2,\;1)$$


$$(p_2,q_2) = (2n^4+1,\;2n^2)$$


$$(p_3,q_3) = (4n^6+3n^2,\;4n^4+1)$$


and so on.
Note
: For
$(p_k,q_k)$
with even
$k$
, then
$(a,b)$
are negative. But it doesn't matter since one can always transform it to appropriate form. For example,


$$\small\left(\frac{-2081(-2081+1)}2\right)^2+\left(\frac{-4162(-4162+1)}2\right)^2 = \left(\frac{2080(2080+1)}2\right)^2+\left(\frac{4161(4161+1)}2\right)^2 = 8925409^2$$




Part III.
However, there is a non-polynomial solution with
$(a,b,c) = (p-1,\, p,\, pq)$
, where
$p^2-2q^2=-1$
with small
$(p_1,q_1)=(7,5)$
and
$(p_2,q_2)=(41,29)$
yielding,


$$ \left(\frac{6(6+1)}2\right)^2+\left(\frac{7(7+1)}2\right)^2 = (7\times5)^2$$


$$ \left(\frac{40(40+1)}2\right)^2+\left(\frac{41(41+1)}2\right)^2 = (41\times29)^2$$


and for all
$(p_k,q_k)$
.




Question:
Similar to the other post, are there polynomial solutions to
$\left(\frac{a(a+1)}2\right)^2+\left(\frac{b(b+1)}2\right)^2 = c^2$
that don't belong to the Pell family above? (In the previous post, there
were
.)
answer:
(I. Possible answer)
. Although the parametric representation I obtained may not be very useful since it covers rational solutions, we can try to find polynomials for
$[p;q;t]$
which will result in the integer values
$[a;b;c]$
.
It can be transformed into a straight line when replacing
$a=X$
,
$b=ka$
.
After that, it remains to replace
$k=\dfrac{p^2-q^2}{2pq}$
and we can find an infinite number of rational points. Given,
$$(1+k^4)X^2+2(1+k^3)X+(1+k^2)=Y^2$$

Then a rational solution
$X$
is,

$$X = \frac{-4 p q (e - 4 p^4 q^2 t - 4 p^2 q^4 t)}{f - 16 p^4 q^4 t^2}$$

where
$$e = (p^2-q^2)^3+(2pq)^3$$
$$f = (p^2-q^2)^4+(2pq)^4$$

and a solution to,

$$\left(\frac{a(a+1)}2\right)^2+\left(\frac{b(b+1)}2\right)^2 = c^2$$

\begin{align}
a &=X =\frac{-4 p q (e - 4 p^4 q^2 t - 4 p^2 q^4 t)}{f - 16 p^4 q^4 t^2}\\
b &= \frac{-2(p^2 - q^2)(e - 4 p^4 q^2 t - 4 p^2 q^4 t)}{f - 16 p^4 q^4 t^2}\\
c &=\frac{aY}{2}\end{align}

It was confirmed in Wolfram Mathematica. Example: If
$p=1, q=2, t=1,$
$$a=\frac{344}{81},\; b=-\frac{86}{27},\; c=\frac{76583}{6561}$$

II. OP's note
: We can convert Aleksandr's answer to one using
Pell equations
.

$$\left(\frac{a(a+1)}2\right)^2+\left(\frac{b(b+1)}2\right)^2 = \big((p^2+q^2)^2-4ez+4fz^2)\times z^2$$

and the RHS is a square if,

\begin{align}
a &= - 4pqz\\
b &= -2(p^2-q^2)z\\
z &= \frac{-(p^2+q^2)uv-ev^2}{u^2-fv^2}\end{align}

So
$(a,b,z)$
are
integers
if
$(u,v)$
are choosen such that,

$$u^2-fv^2=\pm1$$

with
$(e,f),$

$$e = (p^2-q^2)^3+(2pq)^3$$
$$f = (p^2-q^2)^4+(2pq)^4$$

as defined in the first section.

question:
$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\arctan x}{x^2+x+1}dx$$

The only idea I have is to formulate the denominator, get the derivative form of
$\arctan$
and then perform the segmentation, but this doesn't seem to work.

How to use calculus to calculate this integral?

Any help will be appreciated
answer:
Set
$\dfrac1x=y$

$$I=\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\arctan x}{x^2+x+1}dx=\int_\infty^0\dfrac{\arctan\dfrac1y}{\dfrac1{y^2}+\dfrac1y+1}\left(-\dfrac1{y^2}\right)=\int_0^\infty\dfrac{\dfrac\pi2}{y^2+y+1}-I$$

using
Are $\mathrm{arccot}(x)$ and $\arctan(1/x)$ the same function?
and
$\arctan x+\text{arccot}x=\dfrac\pi2$

and
$$\int_a^bf(x)\ dx=-\int_b^af(x)\ dx$$

question:
$$\int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\arctan x}{x^2+x+1}dx$$

The only idea I have is to formulate the denominator, get the derivative form of
$\arctan$
and then perform the segmentation, but this doesn't seem to work.

How to use calculus to calculate this integral?

Any help will be appreciated
answer:
Indeed, we may evaluate
$$f(a)=\int_0^\infty\frac{\arctan x}{x^2+2ax+1}dx\qquad |a|<1.$$
Using
$x=1/t$
,
$$f(a)=\int_0^\infty \frac{\pi/2-\arctan t}{\frac1{t^2}+\frac{2a}{t}+1}\frac{dt}{t^2}=\frac\pi2\int_0^\infty\frac{dt}{t^2+2a+1}-f(a).$$
Thus
$$f(a)=\frac\pi4\int_0^\infty\frac{dx}{x^2+2ax+1}.$$
We complete the square in the denominator,
$$f(a)=\frac\pi4\int_0^\infty\frac{dx}{(x+a)^2+1-a^2}$$
and note that
$|a|<1$
ensures that
$1-a^2>0$
so that we may set
$x+a=\sqrt{1-a^2}\tan t$
,
$$f(a)=\frac{\pi}{4\sqrt{1-a^2}}\int_{\phi(a)}^{\pi/2}\frac{\sec^2 t\ dt}{1+\tan^2 t}=\frac\pi{4\sqrt{1-a^2}}\left(\frac\pi2-\phi(a)\right)$$
where
$$\phi(a)=\arctan\frac{a}{\sqrt{1-a^2}}.$$
For the case
$a=1/2$
, which is your integral,
$$f(\tfrac12)=\frac{\pi^2}{6\sqrt3}.$$

question:
I'd like to learn some differential geometry & vector calculus. Problem is, I've not studied any calculus past Jacobians and path integrals, and even that's very rusty. Since studying those, I've specialised more in algebra, and have an early-graduate level understanding of linear algebra, representation theory, reasonable proficiency in category theory, and a morsel of homological algebra.

I was therefore wondering if there are any (reasonably introductory) multivariable-calculus textbooks aimed at explaining the subject to those with an algebraic background (e.g. using category-theoretic terminology, making links to linear algebra / algebraic topology). Especially something that dips its toes into slightly higher-level material, like differential geometry and applications in physics. And especially something that uses results as motivations for inquiries in homological algebra, topology, etc (i.e. the more 'big picture' the better).

Thanks a lot!
answer:
I don't think there is a multivariable-calculus text written specifically in category-theoretic language, but given your algebra background, I would suggest John Lee’s Introduction to Smooth Manifolds. It is not a "Calc III" textbook, but it gives the conceptual framework behind vector calculus: tangent spaces, cotangent spaces, differential forms, pullbacks, orientations, integration, and Stokes’ theorem.
For a free companion, Eugene Lerman at UIUC has posted lecture notes for a course using this material:
https://lerman.web.illinois.edu/518/f23/518f23.html
This may fit your background better than a standard multivariable calculus book, with ordinary Calc III references used only when analytic details need review.

question:
I came up with the following integral while exploring compositions of hyperbolic, logarithmic and inverse–trigonometric functions:

$$
\mathcal{W}=\int\limits_{0}^{\infty}\left(\operatorname{sech}x-2\operatorname{sech}^{3}x\right)\ln(\cosh x)\,\arctan(\sinh x)\;dx.
$$


A quick numerical check gives


$$
\mathcal{W}\approx 1.1168502750680849136771556874922594459571\ldots
$$


which matches the closed form


$$
\boxed{\displaystyle \mathcal{W}=\frac{\pi^{2}+8}{16}}
$$


What I have checked.




The integral converges absolutely: near
$x=0$
the integrand behaves like
$-\tfrac12x^{3}$
, and as
$x\to\infty$
it decays like
$\pi x e^{-x}$
.


Feeding the integrand to
Mathematica
and
Maple
(2026) returns it unevaluated; the mixture of
$\arctan(\sinh x)$
(the Gudermannian
$\operatorname{gd}x$
),
$\ln(\cosh x)$
and the hyperbolic–secant power bracket seems to break the standard Risch / Meijer-
$G$
heuristics.


I do have a handwritten proof that arrives at the boxed value via the substitution
$u=\sinh x$
followed by two careful integrations by parts, but I am intentionally
not
posting it yet because I would like to see whether the community can find a cleaner or more illuminating route.




Question.
Can you evaluate
$\mathcal{W}$
and reach
$\dfrac{\pi^{2}+8}{16}$
? Alternative methods, generalizations, or insights into why this combination collapses so neatly are especially welcome.
answer:
Yes! Let's start to evaluate
$$
\mathcal{W}=\int_{0}^{\infty}\bigl(\operatorname{sech}x-2\operatorname{sech}^{3}x\bigr)\ln(\cosh x)\,\arctan(\sinh x)\ \mathrm dx.$$

First, let
$t = \sinh x$
. Then
$\mathrm dx = \mathrm dt/\sqrt{1+t^{2}}$
,
$\cosh x = \sqrt{1+t^{2}}$
,
$\operatorname{sech}x = 1/\sqrt{1+t^{2}}$
,
$\ln(\cosh x)=\frac12\ln(1+t^{2})$
, and
$$
\operatorname{sech}x-2\operatorname{sech}^{3}x = \frac{1}{\sqrt{1+t^{2}}}-\frac{2}{(1+t^{2})^{3/2}} = \frac{t^{2}-1}{(1+t^{2})^{3/2}}.$$
Therefore,
$$
\mathcal{W}=\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{t^{2}-1}{(1+t^{2})^{3/2}}\cdot\frac12\ln(1+t^{2})\cdot\arctan t\cdot\frac{\mathrm dt}{\sqrt{1+t^{2}}}
= \frac12\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{t^{2}-1}{(1+t^{2})^{2}}\ln(1+t^{2})\arctan t\,\mathrm dt.$$

Next, let
$t=\tan\theta$
, where
$\theta\in[0,\pi/2)$
. Then
$\mathrm dt=\sec^{2}\theta\,\mathrm d\theta$
,
$1+t^{2}=\sec^{2}\theta$
,
$\ln(1+t^{2})=2\ln\sec\theta=-2\ln\cos\theta$
,
$\arctan t=\theta$
, and
$$
\frac{t^{2}-1}{(1+t^{2})^{2}} = \frac{\tan^{2}\theta-1}{\sec^{4}\theta}= (\tan^{2}\theta-1)\cos^{4}\theta = -\cos2\theta\cos^{2}\theta.
$$
Hence,
$$
\mathcal{W}= \frac12\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\bigl(-\cos2\theta\cos^{2}\theta\bigr)\bigl(-2\ln\cos\theta\bigr)\,\theta\,\sec^{2}\theta\,\mathrm d\theta
= \int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\cos2\theta\ln(\cos\theta)\,\mathrm d\theta.$$

Let
$u=\theta\ln(\cos\theta)$
,
$\mathrm dv=\cos2\theta\,\mathrm d\theta$
. Then
$\mathrm du=\bigl(\ln(\cos\theta)-\theta\tan\theta\bigr)\mathrm d\theta$
,
$v=\frac12\sin2\theta$
.
The boundary terms vanish, so
$$
\mathcal{W}= -\frac12\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\sin2\theta\ln(\cos\theta)\,\mathrm d\theta +\frac12\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\sin2\theta\tan\theta\,\mathrm d\theta.$$

Next, we evaluate the two integrals separately.

For the first, set
$u=\cos\theta$
,
$\mathrm du=-\sin\theta\ \mathrm d\theta$
,
$\sin2\theta=2\sin\theta\cos\theta=2u\sin\theta$
:
$$
\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\sin2\theta\ln(\cos\theta)\,\mathrm d\theta = \int_{1}^{0}2u\ln u\,(-\mathrm du)=2\int_{0}^{1}u\ln u\, \mathrm du = -\frac12.
$$
For the second,
$\sin2\theta\tan\theta = 2\sin^{2}\theta$
, so
$$
\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\sin2\theta\tan\theta\,\mathrm d\theta = 2\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\sin^{2}\theta\,\mathrm d\theta.
$$
Thus,
$$
\mathcal{W}= -\frac12\left(-\frac12\right) + \frac12\cdot2\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\sin^{2}\theta\,\mathrm d\theta = \frac14 + \int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\sin^{2}\theta\,\mathrm d\theta.$$

For the first, set
$u=\cos\theta$
,
$\mathrm du=-\sin\theta\ \mathrm d\theta$
,
$\sin2\theta=2\sin\theta\cos\theta=2u\sin\theta$
:
$$
\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\sin2\theta\ln(\cos\theta)\,\mathrm d\theta = \int_{1}^{0}2u\ln u\,(-\mathrm du)=2\int_{0}^{1}u\ln u\, \mathrm du = -\frac12.
$$

For the second,
$\sin2\theta\tan\theta = 2\sin^{2}\theta$
, so
$$
\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\sin2\theta\tan\theta\,\mathrm d\theta = 2\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\sin^{2}\theta\,\mathrm d\theta.
$$
Thus,
$$
\mathcal{W}= -\frac12\left(-\frac12\right) + \frac12\cdot2\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\sin^{2}\theta\,\mathrm d\theta = \frac14 + \int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\sin^{2}\theta\,\mathrm d\theta.$$

Using
$\sin^{2}\theta = \frac{1-\cos2\theta}{2}$
:
$$
\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\sin^{2}\theta\,\mathrm d\theta = \frac12\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\,\mathrm d\theta - \frac12\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\cos2\theta\,\mathrm d\theta.
$$
The first term is
$\frac12\cdot\frac{\pi^{2}}{8} = \frac{\pi^{2}}{16}$
.

For the second, integrate by parts:
$\int\theta\cos2\theta\,\mathrm d\theta = \frac{\theta}{2}\sin2\theta+\frac14\cos2\theta$
; evaluating from
$0$
to
$\pi/2$
gives
$-\frac12$
. Hence
$$
\int_{0}^{\pi/2}\theta\sin^{2}\theta\,\mathrm d\theta = \frac{\pi^{2}}{16} - \frac12\left(-\frac12\right) = \frac{\pi^{2}}{16} + \frac14.
$$

Finally,
$$
\mathcal{W}= \frac14 + \left(\frac{\pi^{2}}{16}+\frac14\right) = \frac{\pi^{2}}{16} + \frac12 = \frac{\pi^{2}+8}{16}.$$

question:
I came up with the following integral while exploring compositions of hyperbolic, logarithmic and inverse–trigonometric functions:

$$
\mathcal{W}=\int\limits_{0}^{\infty}\left(\operatorname{sech}x-2\operatorname{sech}^{3}x\right)\ln(\cosh x)\,\arctan(\sinh x)\;dx.
$$


A quick numerical check gives


$$
\mathcal{W}\approx 1.1168502750680849136771556874922594459571\ldots
$$


which matches the closed form


$$
\boxed{\displaystyle \mathcal{W}=\frac{\pi^{2}+8}{16}}
$$


What I have checked.




The integral converges absolutely: near
$x=0$
the integrand behaves like
$-\tfrac12x^{3}$
, and as
$x\to\infty$
it decays like
$\pi x e^{-x}$
.


Feeding the integrand to
Mathematica
and
Maple
(2026) returns it unevaluated; the mixture of
$\arctan(\sinh x)$
(the Gudermannian
$\operatorname{gd}x$
),
$\ln(\cosh x)$
and the hyperbolic–secant power bracket seems to break the standard Risch / Meijer-
$G$
heuristics.


I do have a handwritten proof that arrives at the boxed value via the substitution
$u=\sinh x$
followed by two careful integrations by parts, but I am intentionally
not
posting it yet because I would like to see whether the community can find a cleaner or more illuminating route.




Question.
Can you evaluate
$\mathcal{W}$
and reach
$\dfrac{\pi^{2}+8}{16}$
? Alternative methods, generalizations, or insights into why this combination collapses so neatly are especially welcome.
answer:
More than inspired by @Bowei Tang's answer, using
$x=\sinh ^{-1}(t)$
and not repeating the calculation, the antiderivative is
$$-\frac{\log \left(t^2+1\right)-\left(\left(t^2+1\right) \tan ^{-1}(t)^2\right)+2 t
\left(\log \left(t^2+1\right)+1\right) \tan ^{-1}(t)+2}{4 \left(t^2+1\right)}$$

Expanded for large
$t$
$$I=\frac{\pi ^2+8}{16} -\frac{\pi  (\log (t)+1)}{2 t}+\frac{2 \log (t)+1}{4 t^2}+\frac{\pi  (6 \log (t)+1)}{12 t^3}+O\left(\frac{\log (t)}{t^4}\right)$$
which is a very good approximation for
$x \geq e$
where the relative error is
$0.125$
%.

question:
I aim to compute the Laplace-Beltrami operator of a geodesic distance.
I find a book ("Riemannian Geometry" by S. Gallot, D. Hulin and J. Lafontaine ; Springer) that gives me this formula (page 213) for a distance function r:

$$\Delta r = - \frac{n-1}{r}- \frac{J'(u;r)}{J(u;r)}$$

The minus  sign comes from the definition of the Laplacian (here it is -div(grad(.)) ). J(u,r) is the density volume form in normal coordinates.


My question is : Is n the dimension of the space where the Riemannian manifold is modeled ? or n is the dimension of the manifold ?


For example for a sphere of
$\mathbb{R}^2$
embedded in
$\mathbb{R}^3$
: is
$n=2$
or
$n=3$
?
answer:
In the book ("Riemannian Geometry" by S. Gallot, D. Hulin and J. Lafontaine ; Springer),
$n$
denotes the dimension of the Riemannian manifold
$M$
itself (the intrinsic dimension), not the dimension of any ambient space. So for example
$S^2 \subset \mathbb{R}^3$
,
$H^2 \subset \mathbb{R}^3$
we have
$n = 2$
.

This is the standard convention used throughout the book:
$n$
is fixed from the outset as the dimension of the Riemannian manifold
$(M,g)$
. The Laplace–Beltrami operator is defined purely from the metric tensor
$g$
on
$M$
, no ambient space enters its definition, so any
$n$
that appears in a general formula for
$\Delta_g$
must refer to
$\dim M$
.

question:
I aim to compute the Laplace-Beltrami operator of a geodesic distance.
I find a book ("Riemannian Geometry" by S. Gallot, D. Hulin and J. Lafontaine ; Springer) that gives me this formula (page 213) for a distance function r:

$$\Delta r = - \frac{n-1}{r}- \frac{J'(u;r)}{J(u;r)}$$

The minus  sign comes from the definition of the Laplacian (here it is -div(grad(.)) ). J(u,r) is the density volume form in normal coordinates.


My question is : Is n the dimension of the space where the Riemannian manifold is modeled ? or n is the dimension of the manifold ?


For example for a sphere of
$\mathbb{R}^2$
embedded in
$\mathbb{R}^3$
: is
$n=2$
or
$n=3$
?
answer:
The sphere is a smooth 2-dimensional manifold (more precisely, a smooth surface embedded in
$\mathbb{R}^3$
). The induced surface is locally diffeomorphic to
$\mathbb{R}^2$
, so it is a 2D manifold. This means that n=2.
The ordinary sphere in three-dimensional space,
\begin{equation}
S^2 = \left\{ (x, y, z) \in \mathbb{R}^3 \;\middle|\; x^2 + y^2 + z^2 = R^2 \right\},
\end{equation}
is called a 2-dimensional smooth manifold. This means that even though the sphere is embedded in the ambient space
$\mathbb{R}^3$
, every sufficiently small neighborhood on the sphere behaves like an open subset of
$\mathbb{R}^2$
.
The key idea is the notion of a local diffeomorphism. A manifold is locally Euclidean: around every point, there exists a smooth coordinate map that transforms a small patch of the surface into ordinary planar coordinates.
For the sphere, consider the upper hemisphere:
\begin{equation}
z = +\sqrt{R^2 - x^2 - y^2}.
\end{equation}
Every point with
$z > 0$
can be uniquely described by the two coordinates
$(x, y)$
. Hence, the map
\begin{equation}
\phi : (x, y, z) \mapsto (x, y)
\end{equation}
provides local coordinates on the sphere. Its inverse map is
\begin{equation}
\phi^{-1}(x, y)
=
\left(
x,\,
y,\,
+\sqrt{R^2 - x^2 - y^2}
\right),
\end{equation}
which is smooth whenever
$x^2 + y^2 < R^2$
. Therefore, a neighborhood of the sphere is smoothly equivalent (diffeomorphic) to an open region of
$\mathbb{R}^2$
. This is why the sphere is called a \emph{2-dimensional manifold}.
Geometrically, one can imagine zooming in on the sphere near a point. At sufficiently small scales, the curved surface becomes almost indistinguishable from a flat plane. The local coordinates
$(x, y)$
play the same role as Cartesian coordinates in
$\mathbb{R}^2$
.
Notice that the sphere is not globally diffeomorphic to
$\mathbb{R}^2$
. A single coordinate chart cannot cover the entire sphere without singularities. For example, the projection above fails at the equator where
$z = 0$
. To describe the whole sphere, one needs several overlapping coordinate charts, forming what is called an atlas.

question:
I am writing tabulated Hilbert-style derivations, using a proof checker which
I wrote in Python.

See also:
https://math.stackexchange.com/a/5135207/1755256

The book I am studying is Introduction to Mathematical Logic, 4th ed. by Mendelson, Elliott.

Each line of the proof has:

a line number,

a formula or sequent,

a justification referencing earlier lines,

and sometimes a human-readable label.

I am dealing with longer proofs now, and when I perform proof steps which
depend on previous steps, the bookkeeping is becomming very difficult.

Specifically, in my proof I reference previous lines which are labelled when instantiating new lines, or doing whatever else. When I do this, just looking at the line number is bad bookkeeping. But so would be repeating the same label, because then how do I easily find the original?

Hence my question;

When writing large formal derivations, what are the usual conventions for referencing earlier proof steps?

More generally, I am trying to understand how people usually balance precise dependency tracking, readability, and maintainability when presenting formal proofs.

This is the current output of my Python code. I am specifically having trouble referencing previous lines, as they become painfully difficult to find in longer proofs. (That's actually why there isn't more, is because I made a mistake along the way, and couldn't easily recover the process after it without checking each line number individually.)

Proof sequence.

Each line records one step in the derivation:
- Line gives the step number.
- Logic gives the statement proved at that step. This may be a bare proposition,
a sequent such as A, B ⊢ C, or an if-then style metatheorem.
- Reasoning names the rule, substitution, or earlier lines used to justify it.
- Label gives an optional human-readable tag with no proof effect.

Rule labels:
- axiom: take this as given, with no assumptions.
- hypothesis: assume this line for the current proof.
- meta hypothesis: assume this proof pattern for the metaproof.
- X.[A:=B, ...]: substitute throughout line X.
- IE[X, Y]: use X and Y to infer the next implication step.
- IE_abst[X, Y]: the same move, but on abstract proof variables.
- Cut[X, Y]: use Y to remove one assumption from X.
- reorganize_assumptions[X]: rewrite the assumptions on X without changing them.
- induct[X, Y]: use base case X and step case Y to form the induction rule.
- apply_inference[X, Y]: use theorem X on Y to discharge an assumption.

answer:
A
derivation
is a formalized proof, the formal counterpart of a mathematical proof according to the language and rules of a
formal system
.:

See e.g. Herbert Enderton,
A Mathematical Introduction to Logic
(2nd ed - 2001), page 111 for the definition (but Mendelson's textbook is not difefrent):

DEFINITION
. A
deduction of
$\varphi$
from
$\Gamma$
is a finite sequence
$\langle α_0,\ldots,α_n \rangle$
of formulas such that
$α_n$
is
$\varphi$
and for each
$k ≤ n$
, either

DEFINITION
. A
deduction of
$\varphi$
from
$\Gamma$
is a finite sequence
$\langle α_0,\ldots,α_n \rangle$
of formulas such that
$α_n$
is
$\varphi$
and for each
$k ≤ n$
, either

(a)
$α_k$
is in
$\Gamma \cup \Lambda$
[where
$\Lambda$
is the set of
logical axioms
], or

(a)
$α_k$
is in
$\Gamma \cup \Lambda$
[where
$\Lambda$
is the set of
logical axioms
], or

(a)
$α_k$
is in
$\Gamma \cup \Lambda$
[where
$\Lambda$
is the set of
logical axioms
], or

(b)
$α_k$
is obtained by
modus ponens
[the
rule of inference
of the
calculus
, or
proof system
] from two earlier formulas in the sequence; that is, for some
$i,j < k$
,
$α_j$
is
$α_i \to α_k$
.

(b)
$α_k$
is obtained by
modus ponens
[the
rule of inference
of the
calculus
, or
proof system
] from two earlier formulas in the sequence; that is, for some
$i,j < k$
,
$α_j$
is
$α_i \to α_k$
.

(b)
$α_k$
is obtained by
modus ponens
[the
rule of inference
of the
calculus
, or
proof system
] from two earlier formulas in the sequence; that is, for some
$i,j < k$
,
$α_j$
is
$α_i \to α_k$
.

If such a deduction exists, we say that
$\varphi$
is
deducible from
$\Gamma$
, or that
$\varphi$
is a
theorem
of
$\Gamma$
, and we write
$\Gamma \vdash \varphi$
.

If such a deduction exists, we say that
$\varphi$
is
deducible from
$\Gamma$
, or that
$\varphi$
is a
theorem
of
$\Gamma$
, and we write
$\Gamma \vdash \varphi$
.

Thus, in a nutshell, the "reference system" is quite simple: every line has a unique number
$k$
and a reference to its source: either
$\text {Ax}$
or the previous lines
$i,j$
(if Modus Ponens is the only rule; if also Gen is used, the previous
$i$
line).

In addition, as per your example, you can have a
comemnt
field, to be used to highlight the process: Substitution etc.

If your proof has a clear hierarchical structure, you might want to put your proof in outline format, e.g.

etc.

This also makes your proof more modular, as you can work on one subsection without having to renumber the entire proof.

question:
Find

$$\lim_{n \to \infty} (1+\sqrt{13})^n \left(  \frac{1+\sqrt{13}}{2}-\underbrace{\sqrt{3+\sqrt{3+\sqrt{3 + \dots}}}}_{\text{$n$ radicals}}\right).$$


I thought of this question when I considered the limit


$$\lim_{n \to \infty} 4^n \left( 2-\underbrace{\sqrt{2+\sqrt{2+\sqrt{2 + \dots}}}}_{\text{$n$ radicals}}\right)=\frac{\pi^2}{4}$$

which is solved by the common trick that

$$\underbrace{\sqrt{2+\sqrt{2+\sqrt{2 + \dots}}}}_{\text{$n$ radicals}}=2\cos\left(\frac{\pi}{2^{n+1}}\right).$$

However, I do not think a similar trigonometric trick works for the above.


If we replace
$3$
by general
$m$
, is there a closed form for any other
$m$
? i.e.,

$$\left(1+\sqrt{1+4m}\right)^n \left(  \frac{1+\sqrt{1+4m}}{2}-\underbrace{\sqrt{m+\sqrt{m+\sqrt{m + \dots}}}}_{\text{$n$ radicals}}\right). $$


The scaling term
$(1+\sqrt{1+4m})^n$
comes from finding the rate of convergence of the nested radical to its limit.


Edit:
Perhaps, if there is no closed form, maybe we can analyse the asymptotics of this limit with respect to
$m$
?

Edit:
It is easy to show the limit converges. (In fact, I came out with the scaling term by this method). What I'm hopeful for is the closed-form expression of this limit, or any asymptotic bounds.
answer:
A comment which would not fit in the appropriate section:

Let us denote our series of interest as
\begin{align}
T_n=(2R)^n(R-R_n)\implies T_n=\frac{2R}{R+R_n}T_{n-1}\implies T_n=2R(R-\sqrt m)\prod_{k=2}^n\frac{2R}{R+R_k},
\end{align}
where
$R_n=\sqrt{m+R_{n-1}}$
,
$R=\sqrt{m+R}$
, and
$T=\lim_{n\rightarrow\infty}T_n$
is our limit of interest.

By using the series of
$\sqrt{1+x}$
at
$x=0$
and
$x=\infty$
we can arrive at asymptotes for small and large
$m$
:
\begin{align}
T&\sim-\log m+\mathcal O(\sqrt m\log m),\\
T&\sim \sqrt m+\frac34+\frac{3}{8\sqrt m}+\frac{5}{64m}+\mathcal O(m^{-3/2}).
\end{align}

We know that for
$m=2$
,
$T=\pi^2/4\approx2.4674$
. Our large
$m$
approximation yields
$T\approx 2.4684$
. Here is a log-log-plot of the two asymptotes (where I've dropped two terms):

Something of note is the minimum around
$10^{-0.5}$
, it might be interesting to see if we can prove where that is!

question:
Let
$p(t)$
be the mouse population in the month of
$t$
. In the presence of owls who prey on the mice the growth rate per month of the mouse population is
$p'(t) = 0.5 p(t) -450$
. For the initial mouse population
$p(0) = 850$
the solution to the differential equation is
$p(t) = 900 -50 e^(t/2)$
(Boyce and DiPirma, 2012). The mouse population for the current month
$p(t)$
can also be computed directly using the mouse population for the previous month
$p(t-1)$
plus the growth rate per month
$p'(t-1):$

$$p(t) = p(t-1) + p'(t-1)$$


$$p(t) = p(t-1) + 0.5 p(t-1) -450$$


$$p(t) = 1.5 p(t-1) -450$$


Thus the mouse population for the current month can be computed based on the mouse population of the previous month:
$$p(t) = 1.5 p(t-1) -450$$
. Figure 2 lists the mouse population for the initial month
$(t= 0)$
plus the next
$15$
months
$(t=1, 2, ... 15)$
using
$p(t) = 1.5 p(t-1) -450$
and
$p(t) = 900 -50 e^(t/2)$
.


t      p(t) = 1.5p(t-1) -450      p(t) = 900 -50 e^(t/2)
0      850                        850
1      825                        817.5639364649936
2      787.5                      764.0859085770478
3      731.25                     675.9155464830967
4      646.875                    530.5471950534675
5      520.3125                   290.87530196482646
6      330.46875                  -104.27684615938324
7      45.703125                  -755.7725979346153
8      -381.4453125               -1829.9075016572115
9      -1022.16796875             -3600.85656502609
10     -1983.251953125            -6520.657955128829
11     -3424.8779296875           -11334.596613211017
12     -5587.31689453125          -19271.43967463675
13     -8830.975341796875         -32357.081652218076
14     -13696.463012695312        -53931.65792142291
15     -20994.69451904297         -89502.12072280313



Figure
$2$
Mouse Population for
$16$
months
$(t=0, 1, 2, ... 15)$


Why is there a difference between mouse populations computed using "
$p(t+1) = 1.5p(t) -450$
" and "
$p(t) = 900 -50 e^{t/2}$
"? What is the correct mouse population for the months
$t=1, 2, 3, ... 15$
?


Reference


Boyce, W. E. and DiPrima, R. C. (
$2012$
). Example
$1$
Field Mice and Owls (pp.
$10 -12$
). In Elementary Differential Equations and Boundary Value Problems.
$10^{\text{th}}$
Edition. Hoboken, NJ: John Wiley & Sons.


Chionglo, J. F. (
$2026$
). Verifying the Solution to
$p'(t) = 0.5p(t) - 450$
where
$p(0) = 850$
. DOI: 10.13140/RG.2.2.29479.92329.
https://www.researchgate.net/publication/404709069_Verifying_the_Solution_to_p't_05pt_-450_where_p0_850
answer:
The formula
$$p(t)=900-50e^{t/2}$$
describes a
continuous
evolution, for every instant. With that formula, you are constantly monitoring the evolution of the population. Mathematicians would say, this is the most correct model, at least if
$t\in[0,5]$
(because after that the population becomes negative).

The recurrence formula
$$p(t)=1.5p(t−1)−450$$
is a
discrete
model. You are checking the population at single moments, like once every month. Discrete models are often used in biology.

This discrete formula is actually derived using
Euler's method
: a first-order numerical procedure for solving ordinary differential equations with a given initial value. It uses that for small values
$h$
we have
$$\begin{aligned}
p'(t)\approx \frac{p(t+h)−p(t)}{h} & \implies 0.5p(t)-450​ \approx \frac{p(t+h)−p(t)}{h}\\
&\implies p(t+h)\approx p(t)+h(0.5p(t)−450)\\
\end{aligned}$$
And for
$h=1$
, we find
$$p(t+1)\approx p(t)+0.5p(t)−450 = 1.5p(t)-450,$$
exactly your recurrence formula.

Nevertheless, choosing a step size of
$h=1$
for a population in such rapid decline is a very poor approximation strategy.
