answer:
The mouse population for the current month
$p(t)$
can also be computed directly using the mouse population for the previous month
$p(t-1)$
plus the growth rate per month
$p'(t-1):$
$$p(t) = p(t-1) + p'(t-1)$$

The mouse population for the current month
$p(t)$
can also be computed directly using the mouse population for the previous month
$p(t-1)$
plus the growth rate per month
$p'(t-1):$

$$p(t) = p(t-1) + p'(t-1)$$

This is not true. The function
$p'$
represents the
instantaneous
growth rate of
$p$
at time
$t$
. This is different from the
average
growth rate over the last
$t$
-interval of length
$1$
.

For example, consider
$p'(0) = 0$
and
$p'(t) > 0$
for
$t>0$
. Your equation claims that
$p(1) = p(0)$
, i.e. that
$p$
doesn't change for the whole interval
$t\in[0,1]$
, despite that it has a positive growth rate for all
$t\in(0,1]$
.

The correct version of the statement is that
$p(t)$
can be computed directly using
$p(t-1)$
plus the
integral of
the growth rate
over the last
month:

$$p(t) = p(t-1) + \int_{t-1}^t p'(s)\ \text ds$$

which is just a special case of FTC.

question:
According to the hairy ball theorem, a continuous vector field on
$S²$
necessarily has at least one zero. Here is a
video
by 3Blue1Brown with a proof and which shows an example with only one zero. However, that example seems a bit like "cheating" (don't take this literally) because that zero looks like a 2nd order zero. Kind of like if you put two zeros together, either a source and a sink or two vortices rotating in opposite direction. That got me thinking, is more than one zero guaranteed if we impose these extra conditions:

The vector field should be continuously differentiable everywhere.

Its Jacobian should be non-singular at all of the zeros.

My intuition would say that in this case you need to have at least two zeros. A source/sink needs to be matched with a sink/source and a vortex needs to be matched with another one rotating in the opposite direction. However, I can't think of a proof for this nor can I think of a counterexample. Is my intuition right here?
answer:
The short answer is: Yes. I'd challenge you to take another stab at proving this before you click on the spoiler:

You are intuiting the
Poincare-Hopf theorem

You are intuiting the
Poincare-Hopf theorem

Here's a guide to your instinct. You are doing two things:

Intuiting that what
really
matters is the zeroes of the vector fields and how they cancel each other out
Thinking about how those zeroes might aggregate into a single number that describes the whole sphere

Hint: What do you associate with spheres and the number two?

You're trying to capture the space in a single number - this is topological data. So the trick is bridging the gap between the local, analytic information in the vector field aggregating it into global topological information.

Here's an idea you might play with. Given a vector field, perhaps you can construct a triangulation or a cell complex for the sphere (vertices, edges, faces) and compute some kind of topological information based on that decomposition.

question:
I am working on a character table for a research problem (
$\mathrm{UI}_2^{(2)}$
from
here
).

As part of the character table, sums of the following form have shown up:
$$\sum_{t\in \mathbb{F}_q} \theta(t \beta b)\theta(t^2\alpha a) $$
for  fixed
$\alpha,\beta,b \in \mathbb{F}_q$
and fixed
$a \in \mathbb{F}_q^\times,$
and a fixed
$\theta$
a nontrivial homomorphism from
$\mathbb{F}_q^+\to \mathbb{C}^\times.$


As a starting point, what can be said about the following simplified sum?
$$\sum_{t\in \mathbb{F}_q} \theta(t^2)$$
And further, can the big sum be simplified in general?
answer:
Many thanks to @JyrkiLahtonen for setting me in the right direction, and partly solving the problem.

For the following proof, let
$\newcommand{\FF}{\mathbb{F}}
\newcommand{\CC}{\mathbb{C}}  \theta:\FF_q^+\to \CC^\times$
be a fixed non trivial group homomorphism, and
$\theta_a(x)=\theta(ax).$
Assume
$\mathrm{char}(q)>2$
and that
$a_2=\alpha a\neq 0$
as otherwise the problem is trivial, and let
$a_1=\beta b$
.

$\newcommand{\FF}{\mathbb{F}}
\newcommand{\CC}{\mathbb{C}} \begin{align}\sum_{t\in\FF_q}\theta(a_2t^2+a_1t)
&=\sum_{t\in\FF_q}\theta_{a_2}\left(t^2+\frac{a_1}{a_2}t\right)
\\&=\sum_{t\in\FF_q}\theta_{a_2}\left(t^2+\frac{a_1}{a_2}t+\frac{a_1^2}{4a_2^2}-\frac{a_1^2}{4a_2^2}\right)
\\&=\sum_{t\in\FF_q}\theta_{a_2}\left(\left(t+\frac{a_1}{2a_2}\right)^2-\frac{a_1^2}{4a_2^2}\right)
\\&=\theta_{a_2}\left(\frac{a_1^2}{4a_2^2}\right)\sum_{t\in\FF_q}\theta_{a_2}\left(\left(t+\frac{a_1}{2a_2}\right)^2\right)
\\&=\theta_{a_2}\left(\frac{a_1^2}{4a_2^2}\right)\sum_{t\in\FF_q}\theta_{a_2}\left(t^2\right)
\\&=\begin{cases}
\displaystyle{\theta\left(\frac{a_1^2}{4a_2}\right)\cdot \left(\left(\frac{a_2}{p}\right)\right)^n (-1)^{n+1}q^{\frac{1}{2}}} & p \equiv 1 \mod{4} \\
\displaystyle{\theta\left(\frac{a_1^2}{4a_2}\right)\cdot \left(\left(\frac{a_2}{p}\right)\right)^n(i)^{3n-1}q^{\frac{1}{2}}} & p \equiv 3 \mod{4}
\end{cases} & \end{align}$

Hasse-Davenport Lifting Relation

Gauss Sum

question:
What are the asymptotics of the function

$$S_c(z) \equiv \sum_{n=0}^\infty \left( n + 1/2 \right)^{-1/4} \frac{z^n}{n!} \exp \left( -\frac{c}{|z|^2}(n+1/2)^2 \right) \quad \text{as } |z| \to \infty$$

for
$c \geq 0$
? If a motivation is needed: this sum appears in quantum mechanics when considering quantum tunneling from an oscillating initial state. I would be happy with
$\sim$
asymptotics, where the ratio goes to 1. The interesting part of the problem is what happens when
$z$
crosses into the left half-plane.




$c = 0$
case




For
$c = 0$
I
think
I managed to get the asymptotics:

\begin{align}
S_0(z) \sim \left\{
\begin{array}{ll}
e^z \, z^{-1/4} & \mbox{when } -\pi/2 < \arg z < \pi/2 \\
\displaystyle\frac{\sqrt{\pi}/\Gamma(1/4)}{\sqrt{-z} \, [\log(-2z)]^{3/4}} & \mbox{elsewhere,}
\end{array}
\right. \label{finalanswerSofz}
\end{align}

and there is a sharp transition at
$|\arg z| = \pi/2$
. I just sketch what I did to get this, because it's not quite rigorous and maybe not the best way: first we rewrite
$S_0(z)$
exactly as

\begin{equation} \label{Sofzintegral}
S_0(z) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(1/4)} \int_0^\infty \mathrm{d}s \, e^{f(s)} \,, \quad f(s) = z \, e^{-s} - \frac{s}{2} - \frac{3}{4} \log s \,,
\end{equation}

where we used

\begin{equation}
\lambda^{-\alpha} = \frac{1}{\Gamma(\alpha)} \int_0^\infty \mathrm{d} s \, s^{\alpha-1} e^{-\lambda s}
\end{equation}

for the
$(n+1/2)^{-1/4}$
factor, then exchanged the sum and integral and performed the sum. Now as a first step we look for saddle points of
$f$
. I find there are 2 relevant ones: one at
$s_1 \approx -3/4z$
at large
$z$
and the other at
$s_2 \approx \log(-2z)$
. By plotting the steepest ascent and descent contours associated with these saddles I convinced myself that
$s: [0,\infty)$
could be deformed onto: (1) a piece going from
$s = 0$
to
$s_1$
following the steepest ascent contour of
$s_1$
in the downwards direction, (2) from
$s_1$
to
$-\infty + i \, \text{Im} \, s_2$
following a steepest descent line emanating from
$s_1$
, and (3) from
$-\infty + i \, \text{Im} \, s_2$
to
$\infty + i \, \text{Im} \, s_2$
, approximately along the steepest descent line associated with
$s_2$
. The vanilla saddle point method where one truncates the exponent to quadratic order doesn't work, but still one can expand
$f$
around
$s = 0$
and
$s = s_2$
to extract the contributions from the integrals close to
$s_1$
and
$s_2$
(I will not go into details about that).
$s_1$
gives the dominant contribution for
$-\pi/2 < \arg z < \pi/2$
while
$s_2$
gives the dominant contribution elsewhere (although both saddles are always relevant).




$c > 0$
proposal




I
think
the answer is


\begin{align}
S_0(z) \sim \left\{
\begin{array}{ll}
e^z \, z^{-1/4} \, \exp \left( - c \, e^{2i \arg z} \right) & \mbox{when } -\pi/2 < \arg z < \pi/2 \\
\displaystyle\frac{\sqrt{\pi}/\Gamma(1/4)}{\sqrt{-z} \, [\log(-2z)]^{3/4}} & \mbox{elsewhere.}
\end{array}
\right.
\end{align}

So the
$\sim$
asymptotics in the left half-plane is not affected by
$c$
. In a similar non-rigorous fashion I arrived at this by using the same trick for
$(n+1/2)^{-1/4}$
, and the for
$c$
-factor I used

\begin{equation}
e^{-q \lambda^2} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{4\pi q}} \int_\mathbb{R} \mathrm{d} t \, e^{-t^2/4q - i \lambda t} \,.
\end{equation}

I then went through a similar analysis, finding two saddles of the exponent which is now

\begin{equation}
f_c(s,t) = z \, e^{-(s+it)} - \frac{s+it}{2} - \frac{|z|^2}{4c} t^2 - \frac{3}{4} \log s \,.
\end{equation}
answer:
This is to investigate what happens when
$z$
moves from right to left haft of the complex plane. We will take
$c=0$
in our consideration, because in this case we can make a rigorous evaluation (the factor
$e^{-\frac c{|z^2|}(n+\frac12)^2}$
does not change the picture).

Choosing a purely imaginary
$z=ia,\,a>0$
$$S_0(ia)=\sum_{n=0}^\infty\Big(n+\frac12\Big)^{-1/4}\frac{(ia)^n}{n!}=\frac1{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)}\int_0^\infty\left(\sum_{n=0}^\infty\frac{(e^{-s}ia)^n}{n!}\right)e^{-s/2-3/4\ln s}\,ds$$
$$\overset{e^{-s}=t}{=}\frac1{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)}\int_0^1e^{iat-3/4\ln(-\ln t)}\frac{dt}{\sqrt t}=\frac1{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)}\int_0^1e^{iat}\frac{dt}{\sqrt t\,(-\ln t)^{3/4}}$$
Now let's move to the complex plane and consider a rectangular contour
$C:\, r\to 1\to 1+i\to i\to ir$
, where we added a small arch of the radius
$r\to 0$
around
$t=0$

$$\frac1{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)}\oint_C e^{iat}\frac{dt}{\sqrt t\,(-\ln t)^{3/4}}=I_r+I_1+I_2+I_3+I_4=0$$
because we do not have singularities inside our contour. We also note that the integral
$I_r$
along a small arch tends to zero at
$r\to 0$
, and the intgral 3 contains exponentially small factor
$e^{-a}$
and, therefore, can be dropped. We are left with
$$S_0(ia)+\frac1{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)}\int_1^{1+i}e^{iat}\frac{dt}{\sqrt t\,(-\ln t)^{3/4}}+\frac1{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)}\int_i^0e^{iat}\frac{dt}{\sqrt t\,(-\ln t)^{3/4}}=O(e^{-a})$$
$$S_0(ia)=\frac{e^{\frac{\pi i}4}}{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)}\int_0^1\frac{e^{-ax}}{\sqrt x}\frac{dx}{(-\ln x-\frac{\pi i}2)^{3/4}}-\frac {i\,e^{ia}}{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)}\int_0^1\frac{e^{-ax}}{\sqrt {1+ix}}\frac{dx}{\big(-\ln (1+ix)\big)^{3/4}}+O\big(e^{-a}\big)$$
$$=\frac{e^{\frac{\pi i}4}}{\sqrt a\,\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)}\int_0^a\frac{e^{-x}}{\sqrt x}\frac{dx}{(\ln a-\ln x-\frac{\pi i}2)^{3/4}}-\frac {i\,e^{ia}}{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)}\int_0^1\frac{e^{-ax}}{\sqrt {1+ix}}\frac{dx}{\big(-\ln (1+ix)\big)^{3/4}}+O\big(e^{-a}\big)$$
$$=J_1+J_2+O\big(e^{-a}\big)$$
where
$$J_1=\frac{e^{\frac{\pi i}4}}{\sqrt a\,\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)}\int_0^a\frac{e^{-x}}{\sqrt x}\frac{dx}{(\ln a-\ln x-\frac{\pi i}2)^{3/4}}\sim\frac{e^{\frac{\pi i}4}}{\sqrt a\,\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)(\ln a)^{3/4}}\int_0^a\frac{e^{-x}}{\sqrt x}dx$$
$$J_1\sim\frac{\sqrt\pi\,e^{\frac{\pi i}4}}{\sqrt a\,\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)(\ln a)^{3/4}}=\frac{\sqrt\pi}{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)\,\sqrt{-ia}\,(\ln a)^{3/4}}=\frac{\sqrt\pi}{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)\,\sqrt{-z}\,(\ln |z|)^{3/4}}\,\bigg|_{z=ia}\tag{1}$$
Regarding
$J_2$
: only
$x$
near
$x=0$
contrubute - due to exponential suppresion. Hence, decomposing the denominator near zero and integrating, we get the leading asymptotic term:
$$J_2=-\frac {i\,e^{ia}}{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)}\int_0^1\frac{e^{-ax}}{\sqrt {1+ix}}\frac{dx}{\big(-\ln (1+ix)\big)^{3/4}}\sim\frac {-i\,e^{ia}}{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)}\int_0^1e^{-ax}\frac{dx}{\big(-ix\big)^{3/4}}$$
$$J_2\sim\frac{e^{ia}}{\big(ia\big)^{1/4}}=\frac{e^z}{z^{1/4}}\bigg|_{z=ia}\tag{2}$$
Taking (1) and (2) together, we see that for purely imaginary
$z$
$$S_0(z)\sim\frac{e^z}{z^{1/4}}+\frac{\sqrt\pi}{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)\,\sqrt{-z}\,(\ln |z|)^{3/4}}$$
Now we understand how the general asymptotics works:

at
$\Re \,z>0\,$
we must keep only exponentially big term, i.e.
$\,\displaystyle \frac{e^z}{z^{1/4}}$
at
$\Re \,z<0\,$
we must drop exponentially small term and keep the second term, i.e.
$\,\displaystyle \frac{\sqrt\pi}{\Gamma\big(\frac14\big)\,\sqrt{-z}\,(\ln |z|)^{3/4}}$
at
$\Re \,z=0\,$
the leading (greatest in absolute value) term is
$\,\displaystyle \frac{e^z}{z^{1/4}}$
- highly ocsillating at
$|z|\gg1$
. Which term to keep in this case depends on our future actions. For example, if we avarage the asymptotics, the second term can bring more contribution than the first one.

question:
I am currently working through Spanier's text on Algebraic Topology.

From the beginning, Spanier emphasises the notion of the topological pair. He defines it as a tuple
$(X, A)$
consisting of a space
$X$
and a subspace
$A$
. I am trying to understand why this relative viewpoint is necessary.

Of course, when we compute the homology groups of spheres
$S^n$
, we look at the sphere as quotient of a closed disc
$\mathbb{D}^{n}$
along its boundary sphere of a lower dimension and the long exact sequence captures how "Information about homology of the disc" + "Information about the homology of the boundary sphere
$S^{n-1}$
being quotiented" can give information about the homology of
$S^n$
. I have over-simplified it, by skipping over the use of the long exact sequence and excision, but in essence I believe that we wanted a way to describe homologies of quotients using only information from about the space upstairs and the subspace being crushed. We might have already suspected that the information should come in this exact sequence recipe, but we were not quite right: The homology quotient does not always fit into this exact sequence recipe, but something very similar does, and that is the relative homology, and we are fortunate that in most nice cases, the two are the same. Is this a reasonable way of looking at it for homology and homotopy groups?

Now, I also saw in a course on homology that for any pair
$(X, A)$
, the relative homology
$H_{n}(X, A)$
is the same as
$\tilde{H}_{n}(C)$
where
$C$
is the (unbased) mapping cone of the inclusion
$i: A \rightarrow X$
. This led me to suspect that homology can be generalised to be seen as a property of a continuous map between spaces, rather than that of an individual space. Would it make sense to define the relative homology of an arbitrary map
$f: A \rightarrow X$
to be the reduced homology of the mapping cone
$C_{f}$
? If so, why do we not see this treatment in standard texts on algebraic topology?

In addition, after playing around with the Puppe fibre and cofibre sequences, we see that the relative homotopy group
$\pi_{n+1}(X, A)$
of a pair of pointed spaces is the absolute homotopy group
$\pi_{n}(F)$
where
$F$
is the mapping fibre of the inclusion
$i: A \rightarrow X$
. In this context, would it be reasonable to make a similar generalisation to homotopy groups and homotopy sets in general? If so, why do cofibres work for relative homology while we require fibres for relative homotopy? Is there a deeper reason behind this? More generally, I am getting the feeling that we do not have to stop here, and might wish to describe the homology/homotopy of "an arbitrary diagram
$D$
" of topological spaces. If this is correct, then what is the purpose of trying to describe such groups. How are they of value to us?
answer:
I'll address your concerns out of order.

Would it make sense to define the relative homology of an arbitrary map
$f\colon A \rightarrow X$
to be the reduced homology of the mapping cone
$C_{f}$
?

Would it make sense to define the relative homology of an arbitrary map
$f\colon A \rightarrow X$
to be the reduced homology of the mapping cone
$C_{f}$
?

Yes.

If so, why do we not see this treatment in standard texts on algebraic topology?

If so, why do we not see this treatment in standard texts on algebraic topology?

You can definitely find notation like
$H_n(f)$
for what you describe in the literature (similarly for homotopy groups), if not in standard textbooks (my brain is telling me that Hatcher uses notation like that somewhere, but I may well be wrong). There is a good reason this notation isn't more common:
$H_n(f)$
is already commonly used for the induced map
$H_n(X) \to H_n(Y)$
. As for the homology of spaces vs. homology of maps viewpoint, usually the thing you care about first and foremost is the homology of a space,
especially
historically. And, really, what benefits do you gain from a "homology of maps"-type approach? I think the burden of proof (that this has some notable merit over the standard approach and that therefore there are good reasons to adopt it) is on you here. :-)

The homology quotient does not always fit into this exact sequence recipe, but something very similar does, and that is the relative homology, and we are fortunate that in most nice cases, the two are the same. Is this a reasonable way of looking at it for homology and homotopy groups?

The homology quotient does not always fit into this exact sequence recipe, but something very similar does, and that is the relative homology, and we are fortunate that in most nice cases, the two are the same. Is this a reasonable way of looking at it for homology and homotopy groups?

Kind of, yes, but also not really. These issues are ultimately homotopy theoretic in nature: There is a basic issue in homotopy theory that if you have a pair
$(X, A)$
, then the quotient
$X / A$
is not invariant under homotopy equivalences of
$X$
,
$A$
, or the pair
$(X, A)$
. This more or less on its own implies that homology cannot be compatible with quotients in general, since it
does
respect homotopy equivalences.

On the other hand, having a robust theory of subspaces and quotients available is clearly highly desirable, so one has to come up with some way to remedy this. This is the notion of
cofibration
: A cofibration is a pair
$(X, A)$
such that homotopies defined on
$A$
can be extended to homotopies defined on all of
$X$
. A bunch of technical nonsense then shows that this does give rise to a robust notion of quotient, and that for cofibrations like that you always get a long exact sequence in homology involving the quotient.

Luckily for us, cofibrations are abundant: For instance, the inclusion of any subcomplex of a CW-complex (or simplicial complex) is one. Also, any map
$f\colon X \to Y$
can be turned into a cofibration, up to replacing
$Y$
with something homotopy equivalent: The inclusion
$X \hookrightarrow M_f$
into the mapping cylinder happens to be a cofibration. And if you believe me that for a cofibration you get a long exact sequence, and observe that
$M_f / X = C_f$
, then we've recovered the usual sequence involving the homology of the pair.

In this context, would it be reasonable to make a similar generalisation to homotopy groups and homotopy sets in general?

In this context, would it be reasonable to make a similar generalisation to homotopy groups and homotopy sets in general?

Sure.

If so, why do cofibres work for relative homology while we require fibres for relative homotopy?

If so, why do cofibres work for relative homology while we require fibres for relative homotopy?

Addressing this in full would be too much for this answer, but this is often vaguely referred to as
Eckmann-Hilton duality
. The gist is that homotopy groups behave well with fiber sequences because
$\pi_n(X) = [S^n, X]_*$
and the Puppe sequence for fibrations gives you a long exact sequence in
$X$
. Homology, on the other hand, is largely the same as cohomology, and cohomology can be described as
$H^k(X) = [X, K]$
for a suitable space
$K$
. The description of homotopy groups is just the definition, but that of cohomology is a somewhat deep result (but still textbook fare, so you will get to it eventually).

More generally, I am getting the feeling that we do not have to stop here, and might wish to describe the homology/homotopy of "an arbitrary diagram D" of topological spaces. If this is correct, then what is the purpose of trying to describe such groups. How are they of value to us?

More generally, I am getting the feeling that we do not have to stop here, and might wish to describe the homology/homotopy of "an arbitrary diagram D" of topological spaces. If this is correct, then what is the purpose of trying to describe such groups. How are they of value to us?

Not of a diagram, but of its homotopy co/limit. For instance, the cofiber of a map
$f\colon X \to Y$
is the homotopy pushout
$\require{AMScd}$
\begin{CD}
X @> f >> Y \\
@VVV @VVV \\
* @>>> C_f
\end{CD}
where the "homotopy" just denotes some unspecified notion of pushout that is homotopy invariant. (You run into similar issues with ordinary pushouts and homotopies as described for quotients above. In fact, the ordinary pushout of this diagram is simply the quotient
$Y / X$
, so this gives another angle on how
$C_f$
is a "homotopy quotient.")

As for "how is knowing invariants of these guys useful," that questioned is answered by pointing out that (a) "knowing invariants of a space
$X$
is useful" is perhaps
the
basic tenet of algebraic topology, and every application of homology or homotopy groups justifies this, and (b) these constructions are ubiquitous and describe essentially all the things you want to do, ever (at least to the homotopy theorist). For instance, a CW-complex is sequential homotopy colimit over its skeleta, and each skeleton is obtained by attaching cells to the previous one, where "attaching cells" describes the process of taking a certain homotopy pushout.

Other examples include stuff like group actions (there is a homotopy quotient by a group action, and also a notion of homotopy fixed points, and there are tools that can compute, say, the homology of such a thing from the homology of the space being acted on and the action; one can compute
$H_*(\mathbb{R}\mathrm{P}^\infty)$
this way, for a toy example, since it is the homotopy quotient of the antipodal action on
$S^\infty$
).

One can also mix things up and try to figure out how to compute, say,
$H_*(F_f)$
; this was done in Serre's thesis in the early 50s, and this thesis remains probably the single most influential PhD thesis (or perhaps even paper ever, flat out) in algebraic topology. There are many wonderful things one can do that way, for instance compute homotopy groups of spheres, but that's a story for another time... :-)

question:
I am currently working through Spanier's text on Algebraic Topology.

From the beginning, Spanier emphasises the notion of the topological pair. He defines it as a tuple
$(X, A)$
consisting of a space
$X$
and a subspace
$A$
. I am trying to understand why this relative viewpoint is necessary.

Of course, when we compute the homology groups of spheres
$S^n$
, we look at the sphere as quotient of a closed disc
$\mathbb{D}^{n}$
along its boundary sphere of a lower dimension and the long exact sequence captures how "Information about homology of the disc" + "Information about the homology of the boundary sphere
$S^{n-1}$
being quotiented" can give information about the homology of
$S^n$
. I have over-simplified it, by skipping over the use of the long exact sequence and excision, but in essence I believe that we wanted a way to describe homologies of quotients using only information from about the space upstairs and the subspace being crushed. We might have already suspected that the information should come in this exact sequence recipe, but we were not quite right: The homology quotient does not always fit into this exact sequence recipe, but something very similar does, and that is the relative homology, and we are fortunate that in most nice cases, the two are the same. Is this a reasonable way of looking at it for homology and homotopy groups?

Now, I also saw in a course on homology that for any pair
$(X, A)$
, the relative homology
$H_{n}(X, A)$
is the same as
$\tilde{H}_{n}(C)$
where
$C$
is the (unbased) mapping cone of the inclusion
$i: A \rightarrow X$
. This led me to suspect that homology can be generalised to be seen as a property of a continuous map between spaces, rather than that of an individual space. Would it make sense to define the relative homology of an arbitrary map
$f: A \rightarrow X$
to be the reduced homology of the mapping cone
$C_{f}$
? If so, why do we not see this treatment in standard texts on algebraic topology?

In addition, after playing around with the Puppe fibre and cofibre sequences, we see that the relative homotopy group
$\pi_{n+1}(X, A)$
of a pair of pointed spaces is the absolute homotopy group
$\pi_{n}(F)$
where
$F$
is the mapping fibre of the inclusion
$i: A \rightarrow X$
. In this context, would it be reasonable to make a similar generalisation to homotopy groups and homotopy sets in general? If so, why do cofibres work for relative homology while we require fibres for relative homotopy? Is there a deeper reason behind this? More generally, I am getting the feeling that we do not have to stop here, and might wish to describe the homology/homotopy of "an arbitrary diagram
$D$
" of topological spaces. If this is correct, then what is the purpose of trying to describe such groups. How are they of value to us?
answer:
The answer here is a rather subtle one which is best understood from the
$\infty$
-categorical point of view. Let us restrict ourselves to working with “nice” topological spaces (eg compactly generated weakly Hausdorff spaces).

The homotopy cofiber of a map
$f : X \to Y$
has the following universal property. Consider an arbitrary space
$Z$
. Then there is a map of spaces
$Hom(cofib(f), Z) \to \{(g, h) \mid g : Y \to Z$
, and
$h$
is a homotopy between
$g \circ f$
and the constant map
$\}$
. The universal property of the homotopy cofiber is that this natural map is a homotopy equivalence for all
$Z$
. Observe that this universal property means that the data of the homotopy cofiber — the map
$Y \to cofib(f)$
, together with the homotopy
$(X \to Y \to cofib(f)) \sim const$
— are unique up to a contractible space of homotopy equivalence + data that exhibit that the homotopy equivalence preserves the data of the cofiber. This is the
$\infty$
-category theory generalization of the universal property of the cokernel, which defines the data of the cokernel uniquely up to unique isomorphism. Also, note that this universal property manifestly respects homotopy equivalence in
$X$
and
$Y$
and respects homotopies
$f \sim f’ : X \to Y$
.

A relative CW complex
$A \subseteq X$
has the property that the map
$X \to X / A$
, together with the universal homotopy
$(A \to X \to X / A) \sim const$
, form the homotopy cofiber of the inclusion
$A \to X$
. Remarkably, through the process of “cofibrant replacement”, we can always coherently replace any map
$W \to Y$
with a homotopy equivalent relative CW complex
$W \subseteq Y’$
by modifying the codomain of the map. This then allows us to compute the homotopy cofiber of the map
$W \to Y$
by taking
$Y’ / W$
.

In general, there is a theory of so-called “model categories” which, together with the theory of
$\infty$
-categories, fully explains the phenomenon. Fundamentally, we have two categories:
$Top$
, the one-category of topological spaces, and
$S$
, the
$\infty$
-category of topological spaces up to weak homotopy equivalence. You should think of an
$\infty$
-category as like a normal category, except that
$Hom(X, Y)$
forms a homotopy type instead of a set. We have a functor
$Top \to S$
.

The goal is to compute colimits (such as cofibers or, more generally, pushouts) in the
$\infty$
-category
$S$
. But doing explicit calculations in an
$\infty$
-category is extremely difficult. It turns out, however, that there are certain specific colimits in
$Top$
which we can show abstractly are preserved by the functor
$Top \to S$
. Thus, to compute a homotopy cofiber — that is, a cofiber in
$S$
— we first find a cofiber in
$Top$
that is automatically preserved and hence a colimit in
$S$
. This generally involves doing something called “cofibrant replacement” which, in our case, involves replacing an arbitrary map
$W \to Y$
with a good pair
$W \subseteq Y’$
.

question:
I have been recently looking at the Tarski's axioms of geometry, which led me to the following question about first order logic.

In first order logic, we have an axiom (schema), that, given the equality
$a = b$
, allows us to replace all occurences of a free variable
$a$
in any formula
$\phi$
by
$b$
(provided that
$b$
does not clash with any of the bound variables in
$\phi$
).

However, what do we do in a situation when we only want to replace some of the occurences of a variable. As a concrete example, suppose we want to prove
$b = c \land B a b c \vdash B a c b$
(where B is some relation between three points).
We start by replacing
$b$
by
$c$
and arriving at
$B a c c$
, but then we don't have any axiom to justify replacing only the second instance of
$c$
by
$b$
.

We could try to get around this by introducing an extra `placeholder' variable
$e$
, equal by definition to c (
$e := c$
) and, by transitivity and symmetry of equality, to
$b$
. We could then go:
$B a b c$
to
$B a b e$
to
$B a c e$
to
$B a c b$
.
However, I am not aware of there being any rule that allows us to simply introduce new variables in this way.
Moreover, this would not work if we wanted to prove
$b = c \land B a b b \vdash B a b c$
, where the assumption already contains a repeated variable.

What am I missing?
answer:
I shall give the main ideas, their formulations and details may differ with the writers' presentations.

We have two axioms for equality; the properties of symmetry and transitivity can be derived from these, but here, we shall simply assume them:

$\forall x(x = x)$
For each formula
$\phi$
$$\forall x\forall y((x = y)\rightarrow (\phi(x)\rightarrow\phi(y)))$$

Also, we can rename a variable
$x$

if
$x$
is free, uniformly within the formula,
if
$x$
is bound, uniformly within the scope of the bounding quantifier,

taking caution to avoid clash of variables or letting the variable be captured.

So, a dummy variable is not needed in logic as it is in computer programming; we arrange the formulas before the substitution. Hence, in the given example, we can carry out the proof as follows:

$$b=c\land B(a,b,c)\vdash B(a,c,b)$$

is what we want to derive.

Step 1: Use conjunction elimination on

$$b=c \land B(a,b,c)$$

and get:

$b=c$
$B(a,b,c)$

Step 2: Use substitutivity; arrange the formula in order to change the second argument

$$\phi(x)\equiv B(a,x,c)$$

Thus:

$\phi(b)\equiv B(a,b,c)$
$\phi(c)\equiv B(a,c,c)$

From
$b=c$
, we infer:

$$B(a,c,c)$$

Step 3: Use of substitutivity; arrange the formula in order to change the third argument

From
$b=c$
, we apply the property of symmetry to infer
$c=b$
.

$$\psi(x)\equiv B(a,c,x)$$

Thus:

$\psi(c)\equiv B(a,c,c)$
$\psi(b)\equiv B(a,c,b)$

From
$b=c$
, we infer:

$$B(a,c,b)$$

question:
I have been exploring high-weight integrals involving polylogarithms and inverse trigonometric functions, and I constructed the following integral which seems to perfectly resist all standard algorithmic and analytic approaches:

$$ I = \int_{0}^{\infty} \frac{\arctan^2(x) \operatorname{Li}_2\left(-x^2\right) \operatorname{Li}_3\left(-x^2\right)}{x^3} \, dx $$


Convergence:

The integral is well-behaved on
$(0, \infty)$
.
As
$x \to 0$
, the numerator behaves as
$(x^2)(-x^2)(-x^2) = x^6$
, so the integrand vanishes as
$x^3$
.
As
$x \to \infty$
,
$\arctan^2(x) \to \pi^2/4$
, and the polylogarithms grow logarithmically (
$\operatorname{Li}_2(-x^2) \sim -2\ln^2(x)$
and
$\operatorname{Li}_3(-x^2) \sim -\frac{4}{3}\ln^3(x)$
), leading to a numerator that grows as
$\ln^5(x)$
. Divided by
$x^3$
, it decays rapidly to
$0$
.


My attempts and thoughts so far:




Computer Algebra Systems:
Mathematica 13.0 completely hangs on this and eventually returns it unevaluated. Numerical integration evaluates fine, but the Inverse Symbolic Calculator yields no results, which is expected given the high transcendental weight.


Contour Integration:
Attempting a keyhole contour in the upper/lower half-planes seems nightmarish.
$\arctan^2(z)$
,
$\operatorname{Li}_2(-z^2)$
, and
$\operatorname{Li}_3(-z^2)$
all share overlapping branch cuts starting at exactly
$\pm i$
and running along the imaginary axis. Tracking the phase across these overlapping cuts requires evaluating integrals of the products of their imaginary parts, which seems just as difficult as the original problem.


Integration by Parts:
Because there are three distinct transcendental functions, applying IBP creates an explosion of terms. Differentiating any of them introduces rational functions involving
$1+x^2$
, and the
$x^3$
denominator guarantees the creation of several divergent or highly complex sub-integrals that don't telescope nicely.


Multiple Zeta Values (MZVs):
Given the transcendental weights (Weight 2 from arctan, Weight 2 from
$\operatorname{Li}_2$
, and Weight 3 from
$\operatorname{Li}_3$
), this integral should carry a total weight of 7. It stands to reason that expanding the integrand into Maclaurin series and utilizing Mellin transforms would map this to a combination of alternating Euler sums / generalized harmonic numbers.




I strongly suspect a closed form exists purely in the
$\mathbb{Q}$
-linear vector space of Weight 7 constants (e.g., combinations of
$\pi^7$
,
$\pi^5\zeta(2)$
,
$\pi^4\zeta(3)$
,
$\zeta(7)$
, and alternating colored polylogarithms).

Does anyone know of a feasible method to extract the exact closed form of this integral without drowning in hundreds of unmanageable Euler sums?
answer:
I don't think this integral has a nice, simple closed-form formula in known constants. I tried lots of methods, but this is what I got.

Let
$$I=\int_{0}^{\infty}\frac{\arctan^2 x\,\operatorname{Li}_2(-x^2)\,\operatorname{Li}_3(-x^2)}{x^3}\,d x.$$

Here the polylogarithms are taken on their principal branches.  Put

$$H_n^{(r)}=\sum_{j=1}^n \frac1{j^r},\qquad H_n=H_n^{(1)}.$$

The following exact forms hold.  With

$$L=\log(1-y),$$

let

$$U(y)=\operatorname{Li}_2(y)+\frac12L^2,$$

and

$$V(y)=\operatorname{Li}_3(y)+\operatorname{Li}_3(1-y)-\zeta(3)-\zeta(2)L+\frac12\log y\,L^2-\frac16L^3.$$

Then

$$\operatorname{Li}_2\!\left(-\frac{y}{1-y}\right)\operatorname{Li}_3\!\left(-\frac{y}{1-y}\right)=U(y)V(y),$$

and therefore

$$I=\frac12\int_0^1\frac{\arcsin^2\sqrt y}{y^2}\,U(y)V(y)\,d y.$$

The most useful Euler-sum form is

$$\boxed{\displaystyle I=\frac18\sum_{k,m,n\geq 1}\frac{4^k H_m\bigl(H_n^2+H_n^{(2)}\bigr)}{\binom{2k}{k}\,k^2mn\,(k+m+n-1)}.}$$

Also I got this different compressed one-outer-sum form

$$I=\frac14\sum_{N=1}^{\infty}A_NB_N,$$

where

$$A_N=\frac{\pi^2}{4N}\left(1-\frac{\binom{2N}{N}}{4^N}\right)-\frac{2}{N4^N}\sum_{r=0}^{\lfloor (N-1)/2\rfloor}\frac{\binom{2N}{N-2r-1}}{(2r+1)^2},$$

and

$$B_N=\sum_{m=1}^{N}\frac{H_m\left(H_{N+1-m}^2+H_{N+1-m}^{(2)}\right)}{m(N+1-m)}.$$

Proof.
Use the change of variable

$$y=\frac{x^2}{1+x^2}.$$

Then

$$x^2=\frac{y}{1-y},\qquad\arctan x=\arcsin\sqrt y,$$

and

$$\frac{\,d x}{x^3}=\frac{\,d y}{2y^2}.$$

Thus

$$I=\frac12\int_0^1\frac{\arcsin^2\sqrt y}{y^2}\operatorname{Li}_2\!\left(-\frac{y}{1-y}\right)\operatorname{Li}_3\!\left(-\frac{y}{1-y}\right)\,d y.$$

This substitution is the main simplification: the interval becomes finite, and the arctangent becomes an arcsine.

The required Landen formula for the dilogarithm is

$$\operatorname{Li}_2\!\left(-\frac{y}{1-y}\right)=-\operatorname{Li}_2(y)-\frac12\log^2(1-y).$$

It follows by differentiating both sides and checking the value at
$y=0$
.  Hence

$$\operatorname{Li}_2\!\left(-\frac{y}{1-y}\right)=-U(y).$$

For the trilogarithm, first note that

$$\frac{d}{dy}\operatorname{Li}_3\!\left(-\frac{y}{1-y}\right)=\frac{1}{y(1-y)}\operatorname{Li}_2\!\left(-\frac{y}{1-y}\right).$$

Using the previous identity and integrating from
$0$
to
$y$
, one obtains

$$\operatorname{Li}_3\!\left(-\frac{y}{1-y}\right)=-\operatorname{Li}_3(y)+L\operatorname{Li}_2(y)+\frac12J(y)+\frac16L^3,$$

where

$$J(y)=\int_0^y\frac{\log^2(1-t)}{t}\,d t.$$

The elementary identity

$$J(y)=\log y\,L^2+2L\operatorname{Li}_2(1-y)-2\operatorname{Li}_3(1-y)+2\zeta(3)$$

is again proved by differentiating and checking the value at
$y=0$
.  Euler's reflection formula

$$\operatorname{Li}_2(y)+\operatorname{Li}_2(1-y)=\zeta(2)-\log y\log(1-y)$$

then gives

$$\operatorname{Li}_3\!\left(-\frac{y}{1-y}\right)=-V(y).$$

Multiplying the two transformed polylogarithms gives the product identity

$$\operatorname{Li}_2\!\left(-\frac{y}{1-y}\right)\operatorname{Li}_3\!\left(-\frac{y}{1-y}\right)=U(y)V(y),$$

and the compact integral form follows.

We now expand the three factors.  The classical harmonic generating function

$$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}H_ny^n=-\frac{\log(1-y)}{1-y}$$

gives, after integration term by term,

$$U(y)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{H_n}{n}y^n.$$

Also

$$\sum_{j=1}^{n}\frac{H_j}{j}=\frac{H_n^2+H_n^{(2)}}{2}.$$

Since
$V'(y)=U(y)/(y(1-y))$
and
$V(0)=0$
, this gives

$$V(y)=\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{H_n^2+H_n^{(2)}}{2n}y^n.$$

Finally, the standard arcsine-square expansion is

$$\arcsin^2\sqrt y=\frac12\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{4^k}{k^2\binom{2k}{k}}y^k.$$

All coefficients in these three series are non-negative for
$0<y<1$
.  Therefore the term-by-term integration is justified by the monotone convergence theorem.  Substituting the three series into the compact integral gives

$$\begin{aligned}I&=\frac12\int_0^1\frac1{y^2}\left(\frac12\sum_{k=1}^{\infty}\frac{4^k}{k^2\binom{2k}{k}}y^k\right)\left(\sum_{m=1}^{\infty}\frac{H_m}{m}y^m\right) \\ &\hspace{4.6em}\times\left(\sum_{n=1}^{\infty}\frac{H_n^2+H_n^{(2)}}{2n}y^n\right)\,d y.\end{aligned}$$

Integrating
$y^{k+m+n-2}$
from
$0$
to
$1$
gives

$$I=\frac18\sum_{k,m,n\geq 1}\frac{4^k H_m\bigl(H_n^2+H_n^{(2)}\bigr)}{\binom{2k}{k}\,k^2mn\,(k+m+n-1)}.$$

This proves the boxed inverse-binomial Euler-sum representation.

It remains only to compress the triple sum.  Put

$$A_N=\int_0^1y^{N-1}\arcsin^2\sqrt y\,d y.$$

Grouping the terms with
$N=m+n-1$
gives

$$I=\frac14\sum_{N=1}^{\infty}A_NB_N,$$

with the stated expression for
$B_N$
.

To compute
$A_N$
, put
$y=\sin^2\theta$
.  Then

$$A_N=2\int_0^{\pi/2}\theta^2\sin^{2N-1}\theta\cos\theta\,d\theta.$$

Integration by parts gives

$$A_N=\frac{\pi^2}{4N}-\frac{2}{N}\int_0^{\pi/2}\theta\sin^{2N}\theta\,d\theta.$$

Use the finite Fourier expansion

$$\sin^{2N}\theta=\frac{\binom{2N}{N}}{4^N}+\frac{2}{4^N}\sum_{j=1}^{N}(-1)^j\binom{2N}{N-j}\cos(2j\theta).$$

Since

$$\int_0^{\pi/2}\theta\cos(2j\theta)\,d\theta=\frac{(-1)^j-1}{4j^2},$$

only odd
$j$
contribute.  Writing
$j=2r+1$
gives

$$A_N=\frac{\pi^2}{4N}\left(1-\frac{\binom{2N}{N}}{4^N}\right)-\frac{2}{N4^N}\sum_{r=0}^{\lfloor (N-1)/2\rfloor}\frac{\binom{2N}{N-2r-1}}{(2r+1)^2}.$$

The proof is complete.

Hope this is useful!

Improve this question

Let
$(X, \mathcal m, \mu)$
be a
$\sigma$
-finite measure space and
$p>1$
. Suppose
$\mu(X) = \infty$
. Is there a function
$ h \in L^p(\mu), h \notin L^1(\mu)$
?
answer:
Yes. Here is a (somewhat indirect) proof using functional analysis.

Assume that
$L^p(\mu)\subset L^1(\mu)$
. If a sequence
$(f_n)\in L^p(\mu)$
satisfies both
$f_n\to f$
in
$L^p$
and
$f_n\to g$
in
$L^1$
, then
$f=g$
almost everywhere. Thus the closed graph theorem asserts that the "identity map"
$L^p(\mu)\to L^1(\mu)$
is continuous; equivalently, there exists a constant
$C>0$
such that
$$\lVert f\rVert_1\leq C\lVert f\rVert_p $$
for all
$f\in L^p$
. In particular, for any set
$E$
of finite measure, plugging
$f=\chi_E$
in the above inequality gives
$\mu(E)\leq C\mu(E)^{1/p}$
, or
$$\mu(E)\leq C^{p/(p-1)} $$
for any set
$E$
of finite measure. Now since
$\mu$
is
$\sigma$
-finite, the total space
$X$
can be written as a union of increasing
$E_1\subset E_2\subset\cdots$
with
$\mu(E_n)<\infty$
, and by the above each
$E_n$
has measure at most
$C^{p/(p-1)}$
. By monotonicty of measures, we conclude that
$\mu(X)\leq C^{p/(p-1)}$
.

Remark.
After a little bit of modification, the proof still works even if we replace the
$\sigma$
-finiteness just by "semifiniteness", which means every set
$E$
with
$\mu(E)>0$
has a subset
$F$
such that
$0<\mu(F)<\infty$
. Indeed, the above argument shows that
$M:=\{\mu(E):\mu(E)<\infty\}$
is finite. Take
$E_1\subset E_2\subset\cdots$
such that
$\mu(E_n)\to M$
and let
$E=\bigcup E_n$
. Then
$\mu(E)=M$
by monotonicity, and by maximality of
$M$
we see that
$X\setminus E$
cannot have a subset of positive measure. By semifiniteness of
$\mu$
, this forces
$\mu(X\setminus E)=0$
and hence
$\mu(X)=\mu(E)=M<\infty$
.

Improve this question

Let
$(X, \mathcal m, \mu)$
be a
$\sigma$
-finite measure space and
$p>1$
. Suppose
$\mu(X) = \infty$
. Is there a function
$ h \in L^p(\mu), h \notin L^1(\mu)$
?
answer:
Answer to: Is there a function?

Yes, there is.

Take
$X=(0,\infty)$
, and
$\mu$
to be the Lebesgue measure. Then

$$\frac{1}{x+1}\notin L_1$$
but
$$\int_0^\infty\frac{1}{(x+1)^p}dx<\infty.$$

Simply because
$\sum \frac{1}{n^p}$
converges for
$p>1$
.

Answer to:
$L_p$
contained in
$L_1$
, then
$\mu$
is finite

Answered by @MintChocolate. But here is an argument by contradiction.

Assume
$\mu(X)=\infty$
. Since
$X$
is
$\sigma-$
finite, there exist disjoint countable collection of subsets of
$O_j$
with each greater than measure
$1$
. Now we construct

$$f(x)=\sum_j1_{O_j}(x)\frac{1}{\mu(O_j)(1+j)}$$

This is going to be in
$L_p$
but not in
$L_1$
.

Answer to:
$L_p$
contained in
$L_1$
, for finite
$\mu$

Does $L^1$ imply $L^p$ on finite measure spaces?

Original close reason(s) were not resolved

Improve this question

Let
$(X, \mathcal m, \mu)$
be a
$\sigma$
-finite measure space and
$p>1$
. Suppose
$\mu(X) = \infty$
. Is there a function
$ h \in L^p(\mu), h \notin L^1(\mu)$
?
answer:
Let
$1\leq p<q\leq \infty$
. Then we have
$L^p\not\subset L^q$
if and only if the ambient measure space has measurable subsets of arbitrarily small non-zero measure. We have
$L^q\not\subset L^p$
if and only if the measure space has measurable subsets of arbitrarily large finite measure. Now, in a
$\sigma$
-finite measure space of infinite measure this condition is always met so the answer to the question in the text is yes. The answer to your title question is actually no, for example if the measure space consists of a single atom with measure
$\infty$
.

question:
This point has a discontinuity of the first kind or of the second kind?

We consider the function

$
f(x)=
\begin{cases}
\arctan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{x}\right), & x>0 \\
a, & x=0
\end{cases}
$

We need to determine the value of
$a$
such that the function is continuous at
$x=0$
.

The continuity condition at
$x=0$
is:

$\lim_{x \to 0^+} f(x) = f(0) = a$

We compute the limit:

$\lim_{x \to 0^+} \arctan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{x}\right)$

As
$x \to 0^+$
,

$\dfrac{\pi}{x} \to +\infty$

and

$\lim_{t \to +\infty} \arctan t = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$

Therefore:

$\lim_{x \to 0^+} f(x) = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$

For continuity we must have:

$a = \dfrac{\pi}{2}$

Answer:

$\boxed
{a = \dfrac{\pi}{2}}$

First of all, the left-hand limit does not exist. According to the definition, a point
$x_0$
is a discontinuity of the second kind if at least one of the one-sided limits at
$x_0$
is infinite or does not exist.

However, taking the domain of definition into account, we still have that the left-hand limit does not exist, while

$f(0) = \lim_{x \to 0^+} f(x)$
, for
$a = \frac{\pi}{2}$
.

Hence, the discontinuity at
$x_0 = 0$
is of the first kind under this interpretation.
answer:
Discontinuities of the first and second kind are usually only defined for functions
$f : (a, b) \to \mathbb R$
(where
$a= -\infty$
and
$b = \infty$
are allowed). In fact, only in that case it makes sense to consider left and right limits of
$f$
at
$x_0 \in (a,b)$
.

Your function is defined on
$[0,\infty)$
, thus no left limit at
$x_0 = 0$
is defined and it does not make sense to speak about a discontinuity of the first or second kind.

You have correctly shown that
$f$
becomes continuous at
$x_0 = 0$
precisely for
$a = \pi /2$
.

However, we can consider
$f$
to be defined on
$\mathbb R$
via
$$ f(x)=
\begin{cases}
\arctan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{x}\right) & x \ne  0 \\
a & x=0
\end{cases}$$

Then again
$\lim_{x \to 0^+} f(x) = \pi/2$
. Moreover, since
$\pi/x \to -\infty$
as
$x \to 0^-$
, we get
$$\lim_{x \to 0^-} f(x) = \lim_{y \to - \infty} \arctan y = -\pi/2 .$$

This shows that no choice of
$a$
can make
$f$
continuous.
$f$
has a discontinuity of the first kind at
$x_0 = 0$
for each
$a$
.

However, for
$a = \pi/2$
it will be right continuous and for
$a = -\pi/2$
left continuous.

Alternatively we could define
$$ f(x)=
\begin{cases}
\arctan\left(\dfrac{\pi}{\lvert x \rvert}\right) & x \ne  0 \\
a & x=0
\end{cases}$$
In that case
$f$
becomes continuous at
$x_0 = 0$
precisely for
$a = \pi /2$
.

question:
So, I've been reading “Intrinsic geometry of convex surfaces.” (Aleksandrov, Aleksandr Danilovich et al. 1967). and struggle a lot with Aleksandrov's proofs.

One of them in particular, regarding the existence of tangent cones for each points of a convex surface (Ch. IV, p.142-143), is driving me mad because I do not understand the following sentence:

“Assume now that the [convex] surface
$F$
does not lie in any plane.

If we complement this surface so as to make it the boundary of a convex body
$H$
then the resulting body has interior points.
"

There is not much context information needed, aside that
$F$
is supposed convex and non flat (nor doubly covered).

How could the complement of
$F$
result in the boundary of a convex body
$H$
? What exactly is
$H$
if not simply the interior of
$F$
?
answer:
"Complement" here means: supply the rest of. So if we supply the rest of the boundary, we may choose to arrive at a surface bounding a convex body. A way that works for some surfaces,
$F$
, is to construct
$F \times [0,1]$
on one side or the other of the surface. (This is easy to see if the surface is part of a paraboloid or ellipsoid of revolution or is a spherical cap, where we thicken on the side that is inside the surface, a.k.a. the concave side, the inwardly curved side.) Here the complement of
$F$
is the surface
$(F \times \{1\}) \cup (\partial F \times [0,1])$
.

For more general surfaces, a way to proceed is to find one point,
$c$
, that can "see" every point of
$F$
(that is, there is a bijection between points of
$F$
and the cone of geodesic rays based at
$c$
that pass through
$F$
). The union of the segments of those rays from
$c$
to
$F$
is a convex surface -- or we need to pick
$c$
from the other side of the surface. We have found the complement of
$F$
in the boundary: the boundary component from the cone that contains
$c$
= the boundary component made from geodesic rays
$\vec{cf}$
for
$f \in \partial F$
.

question:
A bit of context: This question (which is notational in origin) came up in a theorem about the equivalence of the category of
$R$
-linear representations of a finite quiver
$Q$
and the category of
$RQ$
-modules, where
$RQ$
is the path algebra of
$Q$
over
$R$
. We define the functor
$F\colon \mathrm{Rep}_R(Q) \to \mathrm{Mod}_{RQ}$
via

$$
F(\underbrace{(N_i)_{i\in Q_0}, (N_a\colon N_{s(a)}\to N_{t(a)})_{a\in Q_1}}_{=:D}) := \bigoplus_{i\in Q_0}N_i
$$

where
$Q_0$
is the set of vertices of
$Q$
,
$Q_1$
the edges of
$Q$
,
$N_i$
are
$R$
-modules and
$N_a$
are
$R$
-linear maps. We need that
$F(D)$
is not just an
$R$
-module, which it is by definition, but also an
$RQ$
-module. The
$RQ$
-module structure on
$F(D)$
is established by defining
$R$
-linear maps
$e_j\colon F(D)\to F(D)$
where
$(n_i)_{i\in Q_0}\mapsto (\delta_{ij}n_i)_{i\in Q_0}$
for
$j\in Q_0$
, and
$a\colon F(D)\to F(D)$
via
$(n_i)_{i\in Q_0}\mapsto (\delta_{it(a)}N_an_{s(a)})_{i\in Q_0}$
for
$a\in Q_1$
, where
$\delta_{ij}$
is the usual Kronecker-delta.


The definition of
$a$
is where I think the notation is a bit sloppy, since technically speaking,
$a((n_i)_{i\in Q_0})$
as defined above is now an element of
$N_{t(a)}^{\oplus Q_0}$
.


After talking with my professor about this he agreed that to be precise, this should rather be written down in a piecewise definition, but he also said that he usually does not really differentiate between the zeros of
$R$
-modules. To a certain degree I see why this could be the case, since
$R$
-linear maps preserve 0, but it still seems strange to me.


So my question is: is it sensible to identify the zeros of different
$R$
-modules, and is there any other motivation to do this besides notational convenience?
answer:
Not only the definition of the homomorphism
$a$
, which should be denoted something like
$\tilde{a}$
to avoid confusion with the edge
$a$
, but already the definition of the homomorphism
$e_j$
is confusing, for the same reason you mentioned.

No, we do not identify zeros in different modules, and we don't need to either. Generally speaking, I recommend to
avoid silent identifications
: if we identify
$x$
with
$i(x)$
because
$i$
is a "canonical identification", it is better to just write
$i(x)$
. It will pay off in the long run. There have been many threads on MSE about identifications, so at this point I will not write more on this topic here.

Let me explain how to describe the functor from representations to modules without any of these identifications, and without Kronecker deltas as well.

Let
$D$
be a representation of
$Q$
. So we have an
$R$
-module
$N_i$
for every vertex
$i$
, and an
$R$
-linear map
$N_a : N_i \to N_j$
for every edge
$a : i \to j$
. Let
$F(D)$
be the direct sum of all
$N_i$
, where
$i$
is a vertex. Since
$Q$
is finite by assumption, this is also their direct product. Hence, we have inclusion maps
$$\iota_i : N_i \to F(D)$$
as well as projection maps
$$\pi_i : F(D) \to N_i$$
for all vertices
$i$
, and these maps satisfy the relations
$$\begin{align*}
\pi_i \circ \iota_i & = \mathrm{id} \\
\pi_j \circ \iota_i & = 0 \text{ when } i \neq j  \\
\textstyle \sum_i \iota_i \circ \pi_i & = \mathrm{id}.
\end{align*}$$
In short, we have a
biproduct
. In particular, the universal properties of a coproduct and a product are satisfied. This is essential when defining linear maps.

The relations imply immediately that
$$e_i := \iota_i \circ \pi_i : F(D) \to F(D)$$
is an (idempotent) endomorphism of
$F(D)$
. On elements, we have
$$(e_i(n))_i = n_i, \quad (e_i(n))_j = 0 \text{ for } j \neq i.$$

We could write this as
$e_i(n)_j = \delta_{ij} n_i$
for all
$j$
, but as you point out, this leads to unnecessary confusion if
$0 \in N_i$
is identified with
$0 \in N_j$
. No, it is not, and we don't need it. It is better to treat each individual module as its own separate object, and use explicit homomorphisms to communicate between these objects.

We could also redefine the Kronecker delta
$\delta_{ij} : N_i \to N_j$
as the identity homomorphism for
$i = j$
and the trivial homomorphism for
$i \neq j$
. Then, the equation
$e_i(n)_j = \delta_{ij}(n_i)$
holds for all
$i,j$
.

Next, for every edge
$a : i \to j$
, we want to define a linear map
$\tilde{a} : F(D) \to F(D)$
. By the universal properties of the product and coproduct, it suffices to define the linear maps
$$\pi_q \circ \tilde{a} \circ \iota_p : N_p \to N_q$$
for all vertices
$p,q$
in
$Q$
. We define them to be
$0$
unless
$p = i$
and
$q = j$
, in which case we define them to be
$N_a : N_i \to N_j$
.

In other words, we have
$$\textstyle \tilde{a} = \iota_j \circ N_a \circ \pi_i.$$
This is the composition
$$F(D) \xrightarrow{\pi_i} N_i \xrightarrow{N_a} N_j \xrightarrow{\iota_j} F(D).$$
On elements, this is just
$$\tilde{a}(n)_j = N_a(n_i), \quad \tilde{a}(n)_q = 0 \text{ for } q \neq j.$$
Again, we don't need any Kronecker delta here.

Here is an example: consider the basic quiver
$$Q = (i \xrightarrow{a} j).$$
A representation is just a homomorphism of modules
$$N_a : N_i \to N_j.$$
The associated module is
$N_i \oplus N_j$
. The two idempotent linear maps
$e_i,e_j$
are
$e_i(x,y) = (x,0)$
and
$e_j(x,y) = (0,y)$
. The linear map
$\tilde{a}$
is
$\tilde{a}(x,y) = (0,N_a(x))$
.

question:
My understanding of computability is probably not good enough. What I think I understood is that almost all reals are both undefinable and uncomputable.

The definition of a function
$f$
from
$A$
to
$B$
requires that for each
$x \in A$
, a unique
$y \in B$
exists such that the ordered pair
$(x,y)$
is in
$f$
.

The request for
$f$
to be computable is that a Turing machine exists such that
for each

$x \in A$
it is able to write
$f(x)$
.

How could a Touring machine write
$f(x)$
in correspondence with an infinite set of non-definable and non-computable inputs? Should the definition of a computable function be restricted to the computable subset of its domain? Or am I missing something fundamental?
answer:
Although it may not be obvious in some elementary computability textbooks, the fundamental concept of Turing computability is
relative computability
, also called
oracle computability
. This means: the Turing machine is given as an input the contents of an infinite input tape (the "oracle" tape) and also a natural number, and it computes a function which can depend on the values of the oracle tape and that number. The oracle tape does not need to hold a computable value - it can hold any value at all. That's the basic setup of Turing computability.

In that framework, the computability of functions from Baire space
$\mathbb{N}^\mathbb{N}$
to itself, or Cantor space
$\mathbb{2}^\mathbb{N}$
to itself, is straightforward. We use the notation
$\Phi^x(n)$
to denote the value computed by machine
$\Phi$
with input tape / oracle
$x$
and natural number input
$n$
. This gives a computable function
$f \colon \mathbb{N}^\mathbb{N} \to \mathbb{N}^\mathbb{N}$
defined by
$f(x)(n) = \Phi^x(n)$
.

If we look at elements of
$\mathbb{R}$
instead, there is a complication related to the difference in the topology of
$\mathbb{R}$
compared to
$\mathbb{N}^\mathbb{N}$
.

We need to choose some reasonable way to encode each real as an input tape for a Turing machine. We also need some way to decode the output of the machine as a real number. For example, we could represent a real
$r$
as the left Dedekind cut of
$r$
, or as a sequence of rationals that converges to
$r$
with a fixed modulus of convergence.

In that setting, in order for a function
$f$
from
$\mathbb{R}$
to
$\mathbb{R}$
to be computable, this means there is a Turing machine
$\Phi$
so that whenever
$r$
is a real number and
$x_r$
is a code for
$r$
, the result
$y = \Phi^{x_r}$
is a code for
$f(r)$
.

$$
\begin{array}{ccc}
\mathbb{R} & \xrightarrow{\quad f \quad} & \mathbb{R} \\
\downarrow \! \downarrow \! \downarrow & & \downarrow \! \downarrow \! \downarrow \\
\mathbb{N}^\mathbb{N} & \xrightarrow{\quad \Phi \quad} & \mathbb{N}^\mathbb{N}
\end{array}
$$
In particular, the function
$\Phi$
must be extensional in the sense that any two codes for
$r$
both map to codes for
$f(r)$
.

Clearly, the identity function on
$\mathbb{R}$
is computable regardless of what encoding system we use. If we use the coding system of Cauchy sequences with a fixed modulus of convergence, then the usual addition and multiplication operations are also computable, as well as many other functions defined by convergent series or other effective methods.

question:
My understanding of computability is probably not good enough. What I think I understood is that almost all reals are both undefinable and uncomputable.

The definition of a function
$f$
from
$A$
to
$B$
requires that for each
$x \in A$
, a unique
$y \in B$
exists such that the ordered pair
$(x,y)$
is in
$f$
.

The request for
$f$
to be computable is that a Turing machine exists such that
for each

$x \in A$
it is able to write
$f(x)$
.

How could a Touring machine write
$f(x)$
in correspondence with an infinite set of non-definable and non-computable inputs? Should the definition of a computable function be restricted to the computable subset of its domain? Or am I missing something fundamental?
answer:
I'm going to give a very different answer. I want to show, conceptually, exactly how a concrete computer program can represent a mathematical function on arbitrary reals within its domain. Whether or not those reals are themselves computable.

I don't know your background, so I'll start at the basics with the Cauchy sequence definition of arbitrary real numbers.

A sequence
$a_n$
is Cauchy if it "looks like" it is converging. Officially, for each
$\epsilon > 0$
there exists an
$N$
such that if
$N \le n$
and
$N \le m$
, then
$|a_n - a_m| < \epsilon$
.

We can construct the real numbers as equivalence classes of Cauchy sequences of rational numbers. Any Cauchy sequence of rationals represents a real number. Two Cauchy sequences
$a_n$
and
$b_n$
represent the same real number if they are converging to each other,
$\lim\limits_{n \to \infty} (a_n - b_n) = 0$
. So, famously,
$\frac{1}{1}, \frac{1}{1}, \frac{1}{1}, \dots$
and
$\frac{9}{110}, \frac{99}{100}, \frac{999}{1000}, \ldots$
are both Cauchy sequences of rationals, and both represent the same real number,
$1$
.

A real number is computable if the sequence can be produced by code. In other words we're doing to write a function
$f$
such that
$a_n = f_n$
. To be clear, I'll use Python functions that take in a natural number, and return a
fractions.Fraction
, which are Cauchy sequences.

from fractions import Fraction

# the usual 1
def one (n):
return Fraction(1, 1)

# 0.9999...
def point_9_repeat (n):
return Fraction(10**n - 1, 10**n)

# sqrt(2) by bisection.
def sqrt_2 (n)
lower = Fraction(0, 1)
upper = Fraction(2, 1)
for i in range(n):
mid = (upper + lower)/2
if 2 < mid*mid:
upper = mid
else:
lower = mid
return lower

# The Leibniz series, pi/4 = 1 - 1/3 + 1/5 - 1/7 + ...
def pi (n):
approx = Fraction(0, 1)
for i in range(n):
approx += Fraction((-1)**i, 2*i + 1)
return 4*approx

Now mathematicians may be satisfied with sequences that converge, some day, at some rate. Computers are not. To be useful for computation, we need one more condition. We need to know how
fast
it converges.

There are many ways to do that. I'll settle on,
$|f(n) - f(m)| \le \frac{1}{n} + \frac{1}{m}$
. Please verify that this condition implies Cauchy. All four of the functions above satisfy this though the equation for
$\pi$
only barely meets the criteria. See
computable real number
for some other, equivalent, definitions. Feel free to verify the equivalence.

And now let's look at computable functions of real numbers. There are several equivalent ways to do that, see
computable real function
. But I'll give the easiest to work with definition, that builds on the approach that I already took.

Let's call the Python functions that we're using to represent computable real numbers, computable Cauchy functions.

A Python function represents a computable real function if it takes in computable Cauchy functions, and returns another computable Cauchy function. And does so in such a way that two different representatives of the same inputs will return representatives of the same output.

I'm going to ignore the different representatives part of that. Here is a handwavy justification. Fundamentally, we can only do two kinds of things. We can discover the real number input to some finite precision. We can do operations on the fractions we get to get an output. To show that we have a computable Cauchy function, we'll need to show that if we got some other set of fractions, within the same precision, our answer will come out within the specified potential error. We need this for later terms of our own output sequence. But it will also apply to later terms of the output sequence we get from some other representative of our real number. And therefore the output sequences of equivalent inputs, will be equivalent outputs.

Back to programming. We're being passed in functions, and we're returning functions. That sounds scary, but it is easier than you might think. Here is an example:

def half (x):
# Remember, x is a function that maps n to a fraction.Fraction.
# We need to return the same kind of thing.
def result (n):
return x(n)/2

return result

That said, here is an important complication.

def double (x)
def result (n):
return 2*x(2*n)
return result

Why did we need to calculate
x(2*n)
? That's because
x(n)
is only guaranteed to be within
$\frac{1}{n}$
of the final answer, so
2*x(n)
could be up to
$\frac{2}{n}$
from the final answer. In order to get our final output to be within
$\frac{1}{n}$
, we need an approximation from
x
that is within
$\frac{1}{2n}$
of the final answer. Which
x(2*n)
does for us.

With that in mind:

def plus (x, y):
def result (n):
return x(2*n) + y(2*n)
return result

# abs for absolute values is built in.
# ceil for rounding the integer up needs to be imported.
from math import ceil
def times (x, y):
def result(n):
# We need to bound the absolute value of the reals, so that we know
# what precision we need to know each one to.
x_bound = 1 + ceil(abs(x(1)))
y_bound = 1 + ceil(abs(y(1)))

# conceptually we're getting:
# (x + delta_x) * (y + delta_y)
# = x*y + x*delta_y + y*delta_x + delta_x*delta_y
# So three sources of error. Let's bound each by 1/(3*n).
# That happens if delta_x < 1/(3*n*y_bound) and
# delta_y < 1/(3*n*x_bound). This makes the first two terms
# at most 1/(3*n). And the third term is bounded by
# 1/(3*n*y_bound * 3*n*x_bound) <= 1/(9*n*n).
return x(3*n*y_bound) * y(3*n*x_bound)

It is clear now that we can represent real polynomials. For infinite power series, such as
$e^x$
, we can first figure out how many terms of the power series we need to get to within a specified error, then calculate that to whatever accuracy is needed to get the rest of the error. And so we can add a variety of functions that are power series. And so on.

And now that we have a very explicit notion of how an actual computer program, representing a particular abstract computation, can take in a representation of a computable real number, and returns a representation of another computable real number.

And now, let's go to your original question. How can a computer program, that only actually operates on computable numbers, be a function on noncomputable reals?

Here is the answer. Take any Cauchy sequence
$a_n$
, converging to any real number. There is a subsequence
$b_n$
of it, converging to the same real, that satisfies the convergence criteria
$|b_n - b_m| \le \frac{1}{n} + \frac{1}{m}$
. Now let's go to our computational model and add a magic function, which we'll call an oracle, such that
oracle(n) = b_n
.
Even though
this is not a computable function,
oracle
is a perfectly good mathematical function. And we can mathematically figure out what
f(oracle)
returns - it will generally be another noncomputable oracle that is again a mathematical function. It will, be cause of what we proved about
f
, satisfy the rule that
abs(f(oracle)(n) - f(oracle)(m)) <= Fraction(1, n) + Fraction(1, m)
. (Sorry, I'm being persnickety about Python syntax there.) Which means that the sequence provided by
fb_n = f(oracle)(n)
is a Cauchy sequence, representing a real number.

In this way, a computer program can represent a mathematical function on noncomputable reals.

question:
Consider
$n$
nodes arranged on a circle. Initially, each node is connected to its next neighbor in the anti-clockwise direction, forming a directed cycle. Information can travel only along the direction of the edges.

The goal is to add additional directed paths in order to minimize the worst-case distance between any pair of nodes.

A valid path must satisfy the following conditions:

Every node has exactly
$2$
incident edges in that path.

All edges in a path have the same direction.

Each path forms a directed cycle over all
$n$
nodes.

With only the original cycle, the worst-case distance is exactly
$n−1$
.

Adding the reverse cycle immediately reduces the worst-case distance to approximately
$n/2$

The first
$2$
paths are therefore straightforward:

Clockwise cycle

Counter-clockwise cycle

I wasn't able to find the third path that will minimize the worst case.

A path can't depend on the number of total nodes. I.e if we add more nodes (I mean the previous connection still the same), the k-th path shouldn't change.
answer:
Suppose, as an example, that we have
$12$
nodes, as shown in the first diagram below. A first path is added in red, and a second path in blue, as you did in your diagram. A worst case scenario is illustrated between the points
$A$
and
$B$
and as you observed such paths minimised the worst case scenario on
$n$
nodes to be approximately
$n/2$
or, in this example,
$12/2=6$
; six hops along a red arrow or six hops along blue arrows take you from
$A$
to
$B$
.

The question asked how should additional paths be added to continue to minimise the worst case scenario.

By way of answer, the next two paths in the sequence are shown in gold and green. As Eric Towers claimed in his comment, on
$n$
nodes the worst case scenario is now approximately
$n/4$
or, in this example,
$12/4=3$
; ; three hops along a green arrow or three hops along gold arrows take you from
$A$
to
$B$
.

The first diagram below, with a little imagination, can be used further. Imagine that there is now an additional node between each of the
$12$
, so
$24$
nodes in total. The red and blue paths are now, not the "go by
$1$
s", but the "go by
$2$
s" and the green and gold the "go by
$4$
s" (to borrow Eric's expressions). The worst case scenario is now approximately
$n/8$
or, in this example
$24/8=3$
.

You could continue to visualise
$n$
increasing in this way.

However
, as Misha Lavrov points out in a comment, the assumption that the best second path is a blue reverse "go by
$1$
s" of the red cycle is false.

My second diagram illustrates this for a situation involving
$36$
nodes, which the previous scenario would give a path of length
$18$
steps as the best worst case. We can do better than
$18$
by adding a second blue reverse path of "go by
$6$
s" as shown. The worst case is now, for example, going from
$C$
to
$D$
; From
$C$
go
$5$
steps red, then
$5$
steps blue, then
$5$
steps red;
$15$
in total.

question:
Update
.

According to the comments, although
$\left(\frac{3^{12}}{2^{19}}\right)^{15/4} < 3^{15/317}$
,
but we can prove that: if
$\frac{256}{243} > \left(\frac{3^{12}}{2^{19}}\right)^{15/4}$
, then
$\frac{256}{243} > 3^{15/317}$
,
although the former looks 'weaker'.

Perhaps here is a similar example. If we know
$\mathrm{e}^x \ge 1 + x, \forall x\ge 0$
, then we can prove the 'stronger' inequality
$\mathrm{e}^x \ge 1 + x + \frac14x^2, \forall x\ge 0$
. That is to say,

$$\mathrm{e}^x \ge 1 + x, \forall x \ge 0 \implies \mathrm{e}^x \ge 1 + x + \frac14x^2, \forall x \ge 0.$$

(
Note
:
$\mathrm{e}^x = (\mathrm{e}^{x/2})^2 \ge (1 + x/2)^2 = 1 + x + \frac14x^2$
.)


We can rephrase the above example without
$\mathrm{e}^x$
as follows: Let
$f(x)$
be a function defined on
$[0, \infty)$
with the properties (i)
$f(x) > 0$
on
$[0, \infty)$
; (ii)
$f(x+y) = f(x)f(y)$
for all
$x, y\in [0, \infty)$
. Then we have

$$f(x) \ge 1 + x, \forall x \in [0, \infty) \implies f(x) \ge 1 + x + x^2/4, \forall x \in [0, \infty).$$

(
Note
.
$f(x) = [f(x/2)]^2 \ge (1 + x/2)^2 = 1 + x + x^2/4$
.)


A 'weaker' inequality implies a 'stronger' inequality?


$\phantom{2}$




In
this question
, something confuses me.


According to @French Man's answer, we have

$$2^{317} > 3^{200} \iff (2^{19})^{15} \cdot (2^8)^4 > (3^{12})^{15} \cdot (3^5)^4$$


$$\iff \left(\frac{2^8}{3^5}\right)^4 > \left(\frac{3^{12}}{2^{19}}\right)^{15}
\iff \frac{256}{243} > \left(\frac{3^{12}}{2^{19}}\right)^{15/4}.$$


On the other hand, we have

$$2^{317} > 3^{200}
\iff 2 > 3^{200/317} \iff 4 > 3^{400/317}$$


$$\iff \frac43 > 3^{83/317}
\iff (4/3)^4 > 3^{4\times 83/317}
\iff \frac{256}{243} > 3^{15/317}.$$


Thus, we have

$$\frac{256}{243} > 3^{15/317} \iff \frac{256}{243} > \left(\frac{3^{12}}{2^{19}}\right)^{15/4}.$$

Also, we have

$$\frac{256}{243} > \left(\frac{3^{12}}{2^{19}}\right)^{15/4} \implies \frac{256}{243} > 3^{15/317}.$$

However,

$$\left(\frac{3^{12}}{2^{19}}\right)^{15/4} < 3^{15/317}.$$

It looks like
$3 > 1 \implies 3 > 2$
.
What's wrong with my reasoning?
answer:
I am a high school student and we are yet to start calculus in a while so i could not quite understand the original linked question but i might have found a clue.
Let us switch to equality and not inequality

Let
$2x = 3y$
, let us try to find the value of 4.

Method 1:
$2=3y/x \implies 4=6y/x$

Method 2: 2=3y/x \implies 2^2 = (3y/x)^2 \implies 4= 9y^2/x^2

Now you might be thinking, what is the relation of this equality to the question?
To find it out, let's turn it into an inequality.

Let
$2x>3y$
.

Then
$4>6y/x$
and
$4>9y^2/x^2$
.

Let us take
$x=6$
,
$y=3$
.

Then
$4>3$
and
$4>9/4$
(respectively from the above inequality).
Now why does this happen?

In equalities there is a CERTAIN range of values for
$x$
and
$y$
,
i.e. in the first equation for
$y/x=2/3$
the equation gets satisfied AND these values are causing multiple methods to give a same result, right?

BUT, in inequalities there is no
$y/x=2/3$
in fact we have to follow
$y/x<2/3$
and only for values
$y/x=2/3$
these multiple methods can be equal, right? But in
$y/x< 2/3$
there is no case of
$y/x=2/3$
.

In conclusion, I think I can state that if follow multiple path where in each of the paths exponents are changed uniquely (i.e. in 1st method, getting
$3^x$
, in second method getting 3^2x and in 3rd method getting 3^7x as coefficients of a constant or another exponential value of number not having 3 as a factor) then the values in RHS of inequality will never be equal as long as
$>$
or
$<$
are used BUT if we introduce
$\ge$
or
$\le$
then there will be certain values for which RHS will be equal.
Thanks a lot reading.

BTW this is my first time ever contributing and please let me know if there is a much better explanation to it, I would absolutely love to hear it.

Edit:
I read your recent edit and I don't know the proof of that inequality but i can try to explain how I think this happened

e^(x/2) >= 1+x/2------------(ineq-01)

To get e^x >= 1+x+x²/4 we need to square up both of the side, in this case we are completely changing the exponents of the original inequality (e^(x/2) >= ....)

At least that is what i think is going on, because in the general inequality with x as exponent, we had done no exponential changes to it and in order achieve e^x on the (ineq-01) we had to perform exponential changes.

It feels like something is missing, my explanations might have holes in it so please let me know what I should change?

question:
It is known that the sum of
$n$
consecutive cubes starting from
$1$
is a square,

$$\sum_{k=1}^n k^3 = 1^3+2^3+3^3+\dots+n^3 = \left(\frac{n(n+1)}2\right)^2$$


where
$T_n = \dfrac{n(n+1)}2$
is a
triangular number
. In 1961, Sierpienski proved that
infinitely
many Pythagorean triples may have
two
terms as triangular numbers. The only known instance with all
three
terms is,


$$\left(\frac{132(132+1)}{2} \right)^2+\left (\frac{143(143+1)}{2} \right)^2 = \left(\frac{164(164+1)}{2} \right)^2$$


I don't have access to his paper "
Sur les nombres triangulaires carres
", so we will have to recreate some of Sierpienski's results from scratch.


$$x^2+\left(\frac{a(a+1)}2\right)^2=\left(\frac{b(b+1)}2\right)^2$$


This equation has infinitely many polynomial solutions
.
Proof
:


$$a = \frac{(2n^2-1)q-1}2,\quad b = \frac{(2n^2+1)q-1}2,\quad  x=\frac{npq}2$$


where
$(p,q)$
satisfy the
Pell equation
,


$$p^2-(1+4n^4)q^2=-1$$


Starting with polynomial solutions
$(p,q) = (2n^2,\,1)$
, we can find infinitely many
$(a,b,x)$
,


$$a = n^2-1,\quad b = n^2,\quad x = n^3$$


$$a= n^2(1-4n^2)^2-1,\quad b = n^2(1+4n^2)^2,\quad x=256n^{11}+64n^7+3n^3$$


and so on, with the first one implying the simple,


$$(n^3)^2 + (T_{n^2-1})^2 = (T_{n^2})^2$$




Question:
Are there other polynomial solutions that
don't
belong to this family, perhaps implied in Sierpienski's paper? Two examples are:


Ex. 1:
$\quad a = \frac12(2n^3-3n-1),\quad b = \frac12(2n^3-n-1),\quad x=\frac12(2n^5-3n^3+n)$


Ex. 2:
$\quad a = 2n^4-n^2-1,\qquad b = 2n^4+n^2-1,\qquad x=n^3(4n^4-1)$


Note
: For the first, terms are integers for odd
$n$
. So is there an Example 3, preferably where
$(a,b)$
are polynomials of small degree? (A computer search of the equation in the title yields
many
numerical solutions.)
answer:
Sierpinski looks at something a little different,
$T_r^2+T_s^2=x^2$
. That is, the two legs are triangular, not one leg and the hypotenuse. He establishes that
$T_r^2+T_{r+1}^2=x^2$
, where
$r=2u$
,
$x=(2u+1)v$
, and
$u^2+(u+1)^2=v^2$
. That last equation has infinitely many solutions in integers
$u,v$
by a simple argument from Pell equations, so there are infinitely many Pythagorean triples where the two legs are consecutive triangular numbers. He says one can prove that these formulas account for all Pythagorean triples where the two legs are consecutive triangular numbers, but doesn't provide a proof.

The article is available at
https://www.jstor.org/stable/43667114
– one needs to be able to login, but one can register there at no cost.

It is known that the sum of
$n$
consecutive cubes starting from
$1$
is a square,

$$\sum_{k=1}^n k^3 = 1^3+2^3+3^3+\dots+n^3 = \left(\frac{n(n+1)}2\right)^2$$


where
$T_n = \dfrac{n(n+1)}2$
is a

terms as triangular numbers. The only known instance with all

terms is,


$$\left(\frac{132(132+1)}{2} \right)^2+\left (\frac{143(143+1)}{2} \right)^2 = \left(\frac{164(164+1)}{2} \right)^2$$


I don't have access to his paper "
Sur les nombres triangulaires carres
", so we will have to recreate some of Sierpienski's results from scratch.


$$x^2+\left(\frac{a(a+1)}2\right)^2=\left(\frac{b(b+1)}2\right)^2$$


This equation has infinitely many polynomial solutions
.

:


$$a = \frac{(2n^2-1)q-1}2,\quad b = \frac{(2n^2+1)q-1}2,\quad  x=\frac{npq}2$$


where
$(p,q)$

Starting with polynomial solutions

,

Ex. 1:

Ex. 2:
$\quad a = 2n^4-n^2-1,\qquad b = 2n^4+n^2-1,\qquad x=n^3(4n^4-1)$

$n$
