answer:
Solutions for the equation break down into different forms. There may also be...
https://artofproblemsolving.com/community/c3046h1054874_triangular_numbers_for_pythagorean_triples

For this case...

$$x^2+(\frac{y(y+1)}{2})^2=(\frac{z(z+1)}{2})^2$$

It can also be represented by a lower degree of the Pell equation.
$p^2-5s^2=\pm1$

$$z=\pm{\frac{p(5s-2p)}{2}}$$

$$*******$$

$$z=\pm{\frac{5s(2s-p)}{2}}$$
$$y=\pm{s(5s-2p)}$$
$$x=\frac{3(p-2s)(5s-2p)ps}{8}$$

The general case record for
$n$
has the form.

For solutions of the Pell equation of the form
$p^2-2n(n^2+1)s^2=\pm1$

$$z=(n^2+1)(2ns-p)s$$
$$y=p(2ns-p)$$

$$*******$$

$$z=p((n^2+1)s-p)$$
$$y=2ns((n^2+1)s-p)$$
$$x=\frac{(n^2-1)(2ns-p)(p-(n^2+1)s)ps}{2}$$

question:
I currently write mathematics on a tablet, and have wondered whether I can use existing geometric tools on here to perform straightedge and compass constructions.

The straightedge is fine - I can draw a line passing through any two points using its built in line drawer.

For the compass, things are trickier. I can only draw a circle specified by the ends of a diameter, rather than with a given centre and radius.

I suspect this simply cannot replace a compass for geometrical constructions (even just the equilateral triangle construction can’t seemingly get off the ground), but I wonder whether there is a point I am missing here.

So my question: is there any way to replace a compass with a circle drawer in this way? The obvious choice would involve actually using a ruler to duplicate a line passing through two points, though I hope there may be a more elegant solution just given the tools described.
answer:
The classical compass and your 'new' compass are indeed equivalent but it is not an easy construction to give. I'll go by parts, proving each step is possible with a construction diagram and/or logic. Without loss of generality, suppose we have two points in the plane at
$(0,0)$
and
$(0,1)$
. Our goal is to create a point at
$(-1,0)$
because then we can make a circle of radius
$1$
with
$(0,0)$
as the center (which is equivalent to the classical compass) using only straight lines and the new compass.

Steps:

Create any perpindicular line to the
$x$
-axis
Create the perpindicular bisector at
$x=\frac12$
Create points at two very specific coordinates:
$(1/2,1/2)$
and
$(1/8,3/8)$
Create the point
$(-1,0)$

Step 1

To create any perpindicular line to the
$x$
-axis, first put a point on the upper semi-circle between points
$A$
and
$B$
in the picture (labeled
$D$
). Then draw a circle using
$B$
and
$D$
as the endpoints of the diameter and mark the intersection
$G$
of this new circle and the line segment
$AB$
. Then draw the line from
$D$
to
$G$
and this line is perpindicular to
$AB$
. For proof, let

$$D=\left(a,\sqrt{\frac{1}{4}-\left(a-\frac{1}{2}\right)^2}\right)$$

for some
$x$
and coordinate chase the
$x$
-coordinate of
$G$
. After working through the algebra you get
$G=(a,0)$
.

Step 2

Construct a perpindular line to
$AB$
and then construct two perpindicular lines to this line. In the diagram, these two are the lines which intersect the circle at
$J$
,
$I$
and
$G$
,
$H$
. Then connect with line segments
$JB$
,
$AI$
,
$AG$
, and
$HB$
. Mark the intersections of
$JB$
and
$AI$
as well as
$AG$
and
$HB$
. Draw a line connecting these intersections. This line is the perpendicular bisector of
$AB$
. For proof, we know
$J$
and
$I$
have the same
$y$
-coordinate (not that we know what that is) so the segments
$JB$
and
$AI$
mus intersect at
$x=\frac{1}{2}$
. Same for the portion below. Note that this construction means we can also mark the intersection of the perpendicular bisector and the circle so we can construct the point
$(1/2,1)$
using the new compass and lines.

Step 3

No diagram for this one. Since we can construct perpendicular bisectors, it is possible to construct any coordinate of the form

$$\left(\frac{r}{2^k},\frac{h}{2^k}\right)$$

with
$0< r,h<2^k$
. That is, we can always bisect a length to get a more refined measurement. Crucially, this means we can construct the points
$(1/2,1/2)$
and
$(1/8,3/8)$
.

Step 4

Connect these points with a line. This line intersects the line through
$A$
and
$B$
at
$(-1,0)$
(proof is obvious). Draw a circle using the points
$(-1,0)$
and
$(1,0)$
and we are done.

Constructions

In these constructions the blue points are free.
$A$
and
$B$
are the original points and in the first construction
$N$
is the point chosen arbitrarily on the circle. You can get away in the rest of the construction just using built in intersections.

Creating a perpendicular bisector

Creating the bigger circle

question:
I currently write mathematics on a tablet, and have wondered whether I can use existing geometric tools on here to perform straightedge and compass constructions.

The straightedge is fine - I can draw a line passing through any two points using its built in line drawer.

For the compass, things are trickier. I can only draw a circle specified by the ends of a diameter, rather than with a given centre and radius.

I suspect this simply cannot replace a compass for geometrical constructions (even just the equilateral triangle construction can’t seemingly get off the ground), but I wonder whether there is a point I am missing here.

So my question: is there any way to replace a compass with a circle drawer in this way? The obvious choice would involve actually using a ruler to duplicate a line passing through two points, though I hope there may be a more elegant solution just given the tools described.
answer:
Here's a simplification of QC_QAOA's construction (as an answer rather than a comment so I can embed diagrams):

Proposition 1.
(Adapted from QC_AQOA):
Given a line m and a point D not on the line, to construct the perpendicular from D towards m.

Choose a point B on m. Draw the circle with diameter DB. Let its second intersection with m be G. Then, by Thales's theorem, DG is perpendicular to m.

This is even easier than constructing perpendiculars with the usual Euclidean compass!

Proposition 2.
Given a line, to construct two congruent rectangles XYQP and YZRQ such that X, Y, Z lie on the given line.

Choose points P and G arbitrarily in general position outside the line. Draw perpendiculars from them to the line, intersecting it at X and Z. Draw the perpendicular PR from P towards ZG. Find the center of rectangle XZRP by intersecting the diagonals, and let YQ be the perpendicular from the center towards the line.

Proposition 3.
To double a given line segment AB.

Use the above proposition to construct rectangles XYQP and YZRQ with XYZ on the line through A and B. Let PA and QB intersect in F. Let C be the intersection of FR with AB. Then |BC| = |AB|, as desired.

The entire construction has taken just four uses of our "diametric compass". (Actually only 3, since the same circle on PZ can be used to construct both X and R).

question:
This question was asked in my assignment of abstract algebra and I am not able to make progress on these 2 parts.

Question: Consider the ring
$R$
={
$\sum_{n\in \mathbb{Z}} a_n X^n | a_n \in \mathbb{Z}$
and
$a_n\neq 0$
only for finitely many
$n \in \mathbb{Z}$
} where addition and multiplication are given by

$\sum_{n\in \mathbb{Z}} a_n X^n +\sum_{n\in \mathbb{Z}} b_n X^n =\sum_{n\in \mathbb{Z}}(a_n +b_n)X^n$

$(\sum_{n\in \mathbb{Z}} a_n X^n )(\sum_{m\in \mathbb{Z}} b_m X^m)$

$=\sum_{k \in \mathbb{Z}}\sum_{n+m=k}(a_n b_m)X^k$

Then which of the following are true:

(a)
$R$
is not commutative.

(b)The ideal
$(X-1) $
is a maximal ideal in
$R$

(c)The ideal
$(X-1,2)$
is a prime ideal in
$R$

(d)The ideal
$(X,5) $
is a maximal ideal in
$R$

Attempt: I have done a and c part

For part (b) , if
$R$
is maximal , then
$R/(X-1)$
must be a field but I am not sure how to prove or find a contradiction if
$R/(X-1)$

The
$R/(X,5)$
is isomorphic to
$ \mathbb{Z}_5 [X]$
but I am not sure if this ideal is a maximal ideal or not?

Can you please help me with part b and c?
answer:
The way one usually goes about showing that a quotient ring is isomorphic to some other ring is with the first isomorphism theorem: If you can construct a surjective homomorphism
$\phi: R\to S$
whose kernel is a given ideal
$I$
, then
$R/I\cong S$
with isomorphism given by the map between these two rings induced by
$\phi$
.

For (b), then, you should make a guess as to what the quotient is isomorphic to (and then your answer is based on whether is ring is a field) and then construct such a homomorphism (hint: for any polynomial in
$R$
, you can get a representative of its coset in
$R/(x - 1)$
by using the division algorithm).

For (d) (the part with
$R/(x,5)$
) you should actually just get
$\mathbb Z_5$
(intuitively,
$x$
being in the ideal means you are setting
$x$
to zero), and to verify that the rings are isomorphic you should construct a surjective homomorphism
$R\to \mathbb Z_5$
with kernel
$(x,5)$
(hint, also maybe useful for (b):
$R$
is generated by
$x$
and
$1$
, so figure out where to send those two).

question:
I face the problem of finding how many non-trivial ring or group homomorphisms there are from $\mathbb{Z}_m$ to $\mathbb{Z}_n$, where $m<n$. Is there any general formula?

At the moment, I want to know how many ring homomorphisms there are when $m=12,n=28$.

Please help.
answer:
A ring homomorphism $f:\mathbb Z_m\to \mathbb Z_n$ is uniquely determined by the conditions: $mf(1)=0$ and $f(1)^2=f(1)$.
In order to find out how many ring homomorphisms there are we have to count the number of elements of the set $\{e\in\mathbb Z_n:e^2=e,me=0\}$. It is not hard to see that this equals $2^{\omega(n)-\omega(n/(m,n))}$, where $\omega(a)$ is the number of distinct prime factors of $a$. (See Gallian and Van Buskirk,
The Number of Homomorphisms from $\mathbb Z_m$ to $\mathbb Z_n$
, AMM, 91(1984), 196-197.)

For particular cases we don't need the above result. For instance, if $m=12$ and $n=28$ we get $0=me=12e$ in $\Bbb Z_{28}$ iff $28\mid 12e$ iff $\,7\mid e,\,$ so $  f(1)\in\{0,7,14,21\}$ and only $0$ and $21$ are idempotent in $\Bbb Z_{28},$ so there are two ring homomorphisms from $\mathbb Z_{12}$ to $\mathbb Z_{28}$.

Edit.
If one asks $f(1)=1$ then the problem becomes trivial: there is a unitary ring homomorphism iff $n\mid m$.

question:
I face the problem of finding how many non-trivial ring or group homomorphisms there are from $\mathbb{Z}_m$ to $\mathbb{Z}_n$, where $m<n$. Is there any general formula?

At the moment, I want to know how many ring homomorphisms there are when $m=12,n=28$.

Please help.
answer:
Hint:
regardless of whether you're considering ring or group homomorphisms, where you send $1$ determines where you must send everything else, because for any $n$,
$$f(n)=f(\underbrace{1+\cdots+1}_{n\text{ times}})=\underbrace{f(1)+\cdots+f(1)}_{n\text{ times}}$$

But where can you send $1$?
Remember, the resulting function must be a homomorphism:
$$f(a+b)=f(a)+f(b)\qquad f(ab)=f(a)f(b)$$
Try to figure out what conditions this imposes on your choice of $f(1)$. See user26857's answer if you are stuck.

Note that the answer will depend on whether you require that a ring homomorphism $f:R\to S$ must preserve multiplicative identities, i.e. $f(1_R)=1_S$.

question:
While studying a physics problem in a wedge-shaped geometry, I encountered a recurrence relation for the unknown function
$f(\nu)$
, where
$\nu \ge 0$
is the radial wavenumber arising from the
Kontorovich-Lebedev transform
. Specifically

$$
f(i\nu+1) - f(i\nu-1) =
\frac{1}{\cos(2\alpha)+\cosh(2\alpha\nu)}
\left( \frac{1}{\rho}\, \sin(2\alpha) - \frac{1}{\nu}\, \sinh(2\alpha\nu)\,\frac{\partial}{\partial \rho}
\right) K_{i\nu}(\rho) \, ,
$$

where
$\alpha \in [0,\pi/2]$
and
$\rho \ge 0$
are parameters.


For a positive argument, the modified Bessel function of the second kind
$K_{i\nu}(\rho)$
is real:

$$
K_{i\nu}(\rho) = \int_0^\infty
e^{-\rho\cosh t} \cos(\nu t) \, \mathrm{d}t \, .
$$


I have tried several trial functions to see whether this recurrence relation can be satisfied, but without success. I was wondering whether there is a systematic method that could help determine the unknown function
$f(\nu)$
. Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated. Thank you.
answer:
Too long for a comment (I do not claim this is a final answer or solution):

One systematic way of looking at the equation may to regard it as a difference equation in one complex variable. I describe it because it also shows why the recurrence doesn't determine
$f$
uniquely without an additional condition.

Set
$z=iv-1$
, then

$$i\nu+1=z+2, \qquad i\nu-1=z, \qquad i\nu=z+1, \qquad \nu=-i(z+1)$$

So the equation has the form
$f(z+2)-f(z)=G(z)$
, where
$G(z)$
is the right-hand side written in terms of
$z$
. The recurrence has now become a first-order difference equation with step
$2$
.

Using
$\sinh(2\alpha\nu)=-i\sin(2\alpha(z+1))$
and
$\cosh(2\alpha\nu)=\cos(2\alpha(z+1))$
and therefore

$$
\frac{\sinh(2\alpha\nu)}{\nu}=\frac{\sin(2\alpha(z+1))}{z+1},
$$

the right-hand side, expressed in
$z$
, becomes the following fractional term:

$$
G(z)=\frac{\dfrac{\sin(2\alpha)}{\rho}K_{z+1}(\rho)-\dfrac{\sin(2\alpha(z+1))}{z+1}K'_{z+1}(\rho)}{\cos(2\alpha)+\cos(2\alpha(z+1))}
$$

Now use the standard identities
$\frac{K_{z+1}(\rho)}{\rho}=\frac{K_{z+2}(\rho)-K_z(\rho)}{2(z+1)}$
and
$K'_{z+1}(\rho)=-\frac{K_{z+2}(\rho)+K_z(\rho)}{2}$
and after substitution and collection of the
$K_{z+2}$
and
$K_z$
terms, this gives

$$
G(z)=\frac{1}{2(z+1)}\frac{\left[\sin(2\alpha)+\sin(2\alpha(z+1))\right]K_{z+2}(\rho)+\left[-\sin(2\alpha)+\sin(2\alpha(z+1))\right]K_z(\rho)}{\cos(2\alpha)+\cos(2\alpha(z+1))}
$$

The trigonometric ratios simplify nicely:

$\sin(2\alpha)+\sin(2\alpha(z+1))=2\sin(\alpha(z+2))\cos(\alpha z)$
,
$-\sin(2\alpha)+\sin(2\alpha(z+1))=2\cos(\alpha(z+2))\sin(\alpha z)$
,
$\cos(2\alpha)+\cos(2\alpha(z+1))=2\cos(\alpha(z+2))\cos(\alpha z)$

Then we obtain a "cleaner" form of the recurrence:

$$
f(z+2)-f(z)=\frac{\tan(\alpha(z+2))K_{z+2}(\rho)+\tan(\alpha z)K_z(\rho)}{2(z+1)}
$$

It is tempting to assign the first summand to
$f(z+2)$
and the second one to
$-f(z)$
. This gives the kind of split formula that appeared in the comments. But that split by itself does not yet define one global function
$f$
, because the two expressions must still be compatible when the argument is shifted by
$2$
. In general, the equation
$f(z+2)-f(z)=G(z)$
seems to not having a unique solution. On each lattice
$z_0+2\mathbb Z$
, one may choose an initial value freely and then define
$f(z_0+2n)=f(z_0)+\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}G(z_0+2k)$
for positive integers
$n$
, with the analogous backward sum for negative
$n$
. Equivalently, any particular solution may be modified by adding an arbitrary
$2$
-periodic function
$p(z)$
, since
$p(z+2)-p(z)=0$
. The recurrence determines only the difference of
$f$
between points separated by
$2$
. To select a unique
$f$
, we need an additional condition from the physical problem like an analyticity in a strip, a decay in some direction, a normalization or a boundary condition for example.

A numerical check of the simplified right-hand side does not prove uniqueness, but it may be useful for testing candidate closed forms against the recurrence:

from mpmath import mp, mpc, mpf, besselk, sin, cos, sinh, cosh, tan, fabs

mp.dps = 30
i = mpc(0, 1)

def K(mu, rho):
return besselk(mu, rho)

def Kp(mu, rho):
return -(K(mu - 1, rho) + K(mu + 1, rho)) / 2

def RHS_original(nu, rho, alpha):
return (sin(2*alpha)/rho * K(i*nu, rho) - sinh(2*alpha*nu)/nu * Kp(i*nu, rho))
/ (cos(2*alpha) + cosh(2*alpha*nu))

def RHS_clean(nu, rho, alpha):
z = i*nu - 1
return (tan(alpha*(z+2)) * K(z+2, rho) + tan(alpha*z) * K(z, rho))
/ (2*(z+1))

tests = [
(mpf('1.5'), mpf('2.0'), mpf('0.6')),
(mpf('0.05'), mpf('10.0'), mpf('1.5')),
(mpf('3.0'), mpf('1.2'), mpf('0.4')),
]

for nu, rho, alpha in tests:
err = fabs(RHS_original(nu, rho, alpha) - RHS_clean(nu, rho, alpha))
print(err)

The check above evaluates the original right-hand side and the simplified right-hand side at a few generic parameter values and prints the absolute difference. For the three test cases in the code above, the output is

1.23259516440783094595582588325e-32
6.16297582203915472977912941627e-33
0.0

Thus it only checks the algebraic simplification numerically. It is not used as a uniqueness argument, and the drafted idea here should be read as no more than an accompanying comment, not as an answer to the question.

question:
While studying a physics problem in a wedge-shaped geometry, I encountered a recurrence relation for the unknown function
$f(\nu)$
, where
$\nu \ge 0$
is the radial wavenumber arising from the
Kontorovich-Lebedev transform
. Specifically

$$
f(i\nu+1) - f(i\nu-1) =
\frac{1}{\cos(2\alpha)+\cosh(2\alpha\nu)}
\left( \frac{1}{\rho}\, \sin(2\alpha) - \frac{1}{\nu}\, \sinh(2\alpha\nu)\,\frac{\partial}{\partial \rho}
\right) K_{i\nu}(\rho) \, ,
$$

where
$\alpha \in [0,\pi/2]$
and
$\rho \ge 0$
are parameters.


For a positive argument, the modified Bessel function of the second kind
$K_{i\nu}(\rho)$
is real:

$$
K_{i\nu}(\rho) = \int_0^\infty
e^{-\rho\cosh t} \cos(\nu t) \, \mathrm{d}t \, .
$$


I have tried several trial functions to see whether this recurrence relation can be satisfied, but without success. I was wondering whether there is a systematic method that could help determine the unknown function
$f(\nu)$
. Any suggestions would be greatly appreciated. Thank you.
answer:
Here is an exact solution. Your recurrence relation can be recasted as follows (cf. Eldar Sultanow's answer) :
$$
f(\mu+1) - f(\mu-1) = g(\mu) = \frac{K_{\mu+1}\tan(\alpha(\mu+1)) + K_{\mu-1}\tan(\alpha(\mu-1))}{2\mu}
$$
Since it is linear, its solution is made of the general solution to the associated homogeneous problem and a particular solution to the above equation, i.e.
$f(\mu) = f_h(\mu) + f_p(\mu)$
.

The homogeneous part is given trivially by
$f_h(\mu) = \Theta_1(\mu) + e^{i\pi\mu}\,\Theta_2(\mu)$
, where
$\Theta_{1,2}$
are arbitrary 2-periodic functions. You may check that a particular solution is easily constructed through some telescoping series, namely
$$
f_p(\mu) = \sum_{n=0}^\infty g(\mu-(2n+1))
$$

question:
I would like to count subgroups of each order
(2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12) of $S_4$,
and, hopefully, convince others that I counted them correctly. In
order to do this by hand in the term exam, I need a clever way to do
this because there can be as many subgroups of a group of order 24 as
$2^{23}$.

Do you know how to do this?

(I would be most grateful if you could tell me what part of the answer to the old question answers my question before voting to close.)
answer:
By Lagrange's theorem, the order of a subgroup divides 24, so we are looking for subgroups of orders 1, 2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12, and 24. We go through the list, often using Sylow's theorems:

The subgroups of order 1 and 24 are obviously unique.

Subgroups of order 2 are in 1–1 correspondance with elements of order 2, so you get 4-choose-2 = 6 transpositions $\langle (i,j) \rangle$ and 4-choose-2-over-2 = 3 double transpositions $\langle (i,j)(k,l) \rangle$.

Nine subgroups of order 2, all cyclic, two conjugacy classes

By Sylow's theorem the subgroups of order 3 are all conjugate, so $\langle (1,2,3) \rangle$, $\langle (1,2,4) \rangle$, $\langle (1,3,4) \rangle$, and $\langle (2,3,4) \rangle$.

Four subgroups of order 3, all conjugate to the alternating group of degree 3

Size 4 is messy, so I delay it.

A subgroup of order 6 must have a normal Sylow 3-subgroup, so must live inside the normalizer (inside S4) of a Sylow 3-subgroup. The Sylow 3-subgroups are just the various alternating groups of degree 3, and their normalizers are various symmetric groups of degree 3, so are exactly the 4 subgroups of order 6.

four subgroups of order 6, $\langle (i,j), (i,j,k) \rangle$ parameterized by sets $\{i,j,k\} \subset \{1,2,3,4\}$ of size 3.

All subgroups of order 8 are conjugate by Sylow's theorem, so we just have $\langle (i,k), (i,j,k,l) \rangle$ which is dihedral.

three subgroups of order 8, all conjugate, all dihedral

A subgroup of order 4 is a subgroup of a Sylow 2-subgroup, so either cyclic $\langle (i,j,k,l) \rangle$ or one of the two kinds of Klein 4-subgroups $\langle (i,j), (k,l) \rangle$ (3 subgroups), or the true K4 $\langle (i,j)(k,l), (i,k)(j,l) \rangle$ (normal).

seven subgroups of order 4, three conjugacy classes

A subgroup of order 12 either has a normal Sylow 2-subgroup (and the only subgroups of order 4 with normalizers having elements of order 3 are K4 with normalizer A4) or a normal Sylow 3-subgroup, but in the latter case the normalizer of a Sylow 3-subgroup is only size 6, not 12.

one subgroup of order 12, the alternating group of degree 4

Those were all possible orders, and for each order we proved any subgroup of that order had a specific form, and then counted how many had that form.

question:
I would like to count subgroups of each order
(2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12) of $S_4$,
and, hopefully, convince others that I counted them correctly. In
order to do this by hand in the term exam, I need a clever way to do
this because there can be as many subgroups of a group of order 24 as
$2^{23}$.

Do you know how to do this?

(I would be most grateful if you could tell me what part of the answer to the old question answers my question before voting to close.)
answer:
Well, the first step would be to write out
$S_4$
:

$$
S_4=\{(1), (12), (13), (14), (23), (24), (34), (123), (132), (142), (124), (134), (143), (234), (243), (1234), (1243), (1324), (1342), (1423), (1432), (12)(34), (13)(24), (14)(23)\}
$$

That's one subgroup down (29 to go).

Next, consider subgroups isomorphic to
${\mathbb Z_2}$
. That's any element of order
$2$
(from the list below) and the identity (9 total):

$$
(12), (13), (14), (23), (24), (34), (12)(34), (13)(24), (14)(23)
$$

Move on to those isomorphic to
${\mathbb Z_3}$
. There are eight
$3$
-cycles in
$S_4$
and each unique subgroup must contain two of them, so there are a total of 4.

Now,
${\mathbb Z_4}$
. Consider
$\langle (1234) \rangle$
. We've got
$\{(1),(1234),(13)(24),(1432) \}$
and two more like this.

From here, just keep going through the list. Think about what elements you need to form a particular isomorphism type and then figure out how many ways you can form each type. For instance, if you have a subgroup isomorphic to
$D_8$
, you'll need a cyclic subgroup of order
$4$
(we have three of those) and 4 elements of order
$2$
.

Including what we've already come up with, there should be 4 subgroups isomorphic to the Klein 4-group (each contains 3 elements of order
$2$
), 4 to
$S_3$
(think of this one as choosing 3 elements out of
$\{1,2,3,4\}$
and considering the permutations on this new set of three symbols), 3 to
$D_8$
(again, we're limited by the cyclic "subgroup of rotations",
$A_4$
will be the only subgroup of order
$12$
, and don't forget
$\{(1)\}$
.

question:
I would like to count subgroups of each order
(2, 3, 4, 6, 8, 12) of $S_4$,
and, hopefully, convince others that I counted them correctly. In
order to do this by hand in the term exam, I need a clever way to do
this because there can be as many subgroups of a group of order 24 as
$2^{23}$.

Do you know how to do this?

(I would be most grateful if you could tell me what part of the answer to the old question answers my question before voting to close.)

Given
$f : \mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$
, set
$T > 0$
. If for every
$x \in \mathbb{R}$
,
$[f(x) = f(y) \ \forall \ y \in [x,x+T]]$
, show that
$f$
is a constant.

I can think of more than one way to solve this, and I am listing two of them below. However, what I am looking for is a proof that is simpler. A one-liner proof to be specific, as the problem
feels
a lot more trivial.

Note that
$f([x,x+T]) = c \in \mathbb{R}$
for an arbitrary
$x \in \mathbb{R}$
. Let
$z = \frac{1}{2}x + \frac{1}{2}(x+T)$
. Then
$f([z, z+T])$
must also be a constant equal to
$f(z) = f(x) = c$
. Similarly,
$f([z-T, z]) = c$
.

Overall, for an arbitrary
$x \in \mathbb{R}$
, we have
$f([x,x+T]) = c \implies f\left([x-\frac{1}{2}T, x+\frac{1}{2}T\right]) = c$
. Now for any
$y \in \mathbb{R}$
, we must have
$y \in [x-jT, x+(j+1)T]$
for some
$j \in \mathbb{N} \cup \{0\}$
. Apply the previous operation
$2j$
times to arrive at
$c = f([x-jT, x+(j+1)T]) = f(y)$
.

As the choice of
$y$
was arbitrary,
$f$
is constant over the entire domain.

Fix
$x > y$
such that
$f(x) \neq f(y)$
. Then there exists
$j\in \mathbb{N}\cup\{0\}$
such that
$x \in [y+jT,\,y+(j+1)T]$
. Applying the previous result repeatedly, we have
$f(x)=f(y+(k+1)T)=f(y+kT)=\cdots=f(y+T)=f(y)$
, a contradiction. Hence
$f$
is constant.
answer:
Starting from
$f(kT) = f(0)$
, for any
$k \in \mathbb{Z}$
, and noting that any
$x \in \mathbb{R}$
belongs some interval
$[k_0 T, (k_0+1)T]$
, we can conclude that
$f(x)=f(0)$
, for all
$x \in \mathbb{R}$
. But this is conceptually the same as you have presented.

question:
Given positive integers
$k \le d \le n$
and a finite field
$\mathbb{F}_q$
(
$q = p^m$
).

Let
$A = (a_1,...,a_d)$
be a
$d$
-tuple of linearly independent
$n$
-dimensional vectors on
$\mathbb{F}_q$
. In other words,
$A$
is a
$d$
-rank matrix of size
$d \times n$
on
$\mathbb{F}_q$
. When given a
$k$
-set of column indices
$I = \{i_1,...,i_k\}$
, let
$A_I := (a_{1,I},...,a_{d,I})$
be a
$d \times k$
matrix induced by a restriction of
$A$
on the column indices
$I$
. Define
$$N(A) := | \{ I : \text{ rank } {A_I} = k \} | \; \le \; \binom{n}{k} \; \; \; \forall A$$


Problem:
Tightly upper bound and lower bound
$N(A)$
.


I have a guess that
$\dfrac{N(A)}{\binom{n}{k}} \le \dfrac{P(d,k)}{q^{dk}}$
whereas
$P(d,k)$
is the number of
$k$
-tuples of linearly independent
$d$
-dimensional vectors on
$\mathbb{F}_q$
. Even if it is true, I am not sure whether it is tight.


Anyway, if there are some tight bounds, another problem naturally arises:


Subproblem:
How to choose / construct an
$A$
that maximizes or minimizes
$N(A)$
?
answer:
Stack the vectors as the rows of a
$d\times n$
matrix, and let the
columns be
$c_1,\ldots,c_n\in\mathbb F_q^d$
.

The minimum is exactly
$$
\min_A N(A)=\binom d k .
$$
Indeed, since
$\operatorname{rank}A=d$
, some
$d$
columns are linearly
independent, and every
$k$
of these columns is independent. Equality is
attained by
$$
A=[I_d\;0\;\cdots\;0].
$$

For the maximum it is better to pass to projective space. Let
$$
R=|\mathrm{PG}(d-1,q)|=\frac{q^d-1}{q-1}.
$$
For each projective point
$P$
, let
$m_P$
be the number of columns of
$A$
lying on the line
$P$
. Zero columns are irrelevant for maximizing. Then
$$
N(A)=
\sum_{\substack{S\subseteq \mathrm{PG}(d-1,q)\\ |S|=k,\ \operatorname{rk}S=k}}
\prod_{P\in S}m_P .
$$
Thus the exact maximum is the corresponding weighted extremal problem on
$\mathrm{PG}(d-1,q)$
.

This already shows that the proposed upper bound is false. If
$n$
is a
multiple of
$R$
, take exactly
$n/R$
copies of each projective point. Then
$$
N(A)=\frac{n^k}{k!}
\prod_{j=0}^{k-1}
\left(1-\frac{[j]_q}{[d]_q}\right),
\qquad
[j]_q=\frac{q^j-1}{q-1}.
$$
Asymptotically,
$$
\frac{N(A)}{\binom n k}\to
\prod_{j=0}^{k-1}
\left(1-\frac{[j]_q}{[d]_q}\right),
$$
which is strictly larger than
$$
\prod_{j=0}^{k-1}(1-q^{j-d})
=\frac{P(d,k)}{q^{dk}},
$$
since
$$
\frac{[j]_q}{[d]_q}<q^{j-d}
\qquad (1\le j<d).
$$

For example, over
$\mathbb F_2$
with
$d=k=3$
, take the seven nonzero
columns of
$\mathbb F_2^3$
, one from each projective point. Then the
dependent triples are exactly the seven lines of the Fano plane, so
$$
N(A)=\binom73-7=28,
$$
whereas the proposed bound gives
$$
\binom73\frac{(8-1)(8-2)(8-4)}{8^3}
=\binom73\frac{21}{64}<12.
$$
So the guessed upper bound fails.

question:
Let
$X=\mathbb R^2$
. A base for the
radial interval topology
on
$X$
consists of the open intervals disjoint from the origin on lines passing through the origin, along with sets of the form
$\bigcup\{I_\theta : 0 \leq \theta < \pi\}$
where
$I_\theta$
is an open interval about the origin on the line with slope
$\tan\theta$
. This is example 141 in
Counterexamples in topology
(Steen & Seebach).

A homeomorphic description is to take the disjoint union of continuum many copies of
$\mathbb R$
and identify the points
$0$
of all the copies to a single point.

Is
$X$
an
$\aleph$
-space? Is
$X$
a
$\sigma$
-space?

To explain the terminology, these two properties are part of set of four related ones for so-called "generalized metric spaces".

$X$
is an
$\aleph_0$
-space

$\iff$

$X$
is
$T_3$
and has a countable
$k$
-network.

$X$
is
cosmic

$\iff$

$X$
is
$T_3$
and has a countable network.

$X$
is an
$\aleph$
-space

$\iff$

$X$
is
$T_3$
and has a
$\sigma$
-locally finite
$k$
-network.

$X$
is a
$\sigma$
-space

$\iff$

$X$
is
$T_3$
and has a
$\sigma$
-locally finite network.

A family
$\mathcal N$
of subsets of
$X$
is called a
$k$
-network
if for every compact set
$K$
and open set
$U$
in
$X$
with
$K\subseteq U$
, there exists a finite
$\mathcal{N}^* \subseteq \mathcal{N}$
with
$K \subseteq \bigcup\mathcal{N}^* \subseteq U$
.

A family
$\mathcal N$
of subsets of
$X$
is called a
network
if for every open set
$U$
and point
$x\in U$
there is some
$A\in\mathcal N$
with
$x\in A\subseteq U$
.

Relations between these properties:

$$\aleph_0\text{-space }\implies\text{cosmic}\implies\sigma\text{-space}$$


$$\aleph_0\text{-space }\implies\aleph\text{-space}\implies\sigma\text{-space}$$


Furthermore, every separable metrizable space is
$\aleph_0$
and every metrizable space is
$\aleph$
.




Back to
$X$
= the radial interval topology on
$\mathbb R^2$
.
The information in pi-base shows that
$X$
is not cosmic (see
here
) and hence not an
$\aleph_0$
-space either.  For the two remaining properties, since
$X$
is
$T_3$
, the questions at the top are equivalent to asking:


Does
$X$
have a
$\sigma$
-locally finite network?
Does
$X$
have a
$\sigma$
-locally finite
$k$
-network?
answer:
Lemma 1
. Let
$\{A_i\}$
be a cover of
$X$
, and
$\mathcal N_i$
be a network for
$A_i$
, then
$\bigcup \mathcal N_i$
is a network for
$X$
.
Proof
. Let
$x \in U \subseteq X$
, take
$i$
such that
$x \in A_i$
. Take
$A \in \mathcal N_i$
such that
$x \in A \subseteq U \cap A_i \subseteq U$
.

Lemma 1
. Let
$\{A_i\}$
be a cover of
$X$
, and
$\mathcal N_i$
be a network for
$A_i$
, then
$\bigcup \mathcal N_i$
is a network for
$X$
.

Proof
. Let
$x \in U \subseteq X$
, take
$i$
such that
$x \in A_i$
. Take
$A \in \mathcal N_i$
such that
$x \in A \subseteq U \cap A_i \subseteq U$
.

Theorem 1
.
$X$
has a
$\sigma$
-locally finite network.
Proof
. Written
$X = \{0\} \cup \bigcup_{n \in \mathbb N^+} X_n$
, where
$X_n = \left\{ x \in X \mid \left\|x\right\|_2 \geq n^{-1}\right\}$
is closed in
$X$
.
Since each
$X_n$
is a disjoint union of rays, it has a
$\sigma$
-locally finite network.
Then combining these network together yields a network of
$X$
.
To preserving
$\sigma$
-locally finiteness, we only need to require each network of
$X_n$
is
$\sigma$
-locally finite at
every point in
$X$
, due to countability of this union.
Using the closedness of
$X_n$
, points outside
$X_n$
automatically has a neighborhood disjoint from all sets in its network.

Theorem 1
.
$X$
has a
$\sigma$
-locally finite network.

Proof
. Written
$X = \{0\} \cup \bigcup_{n \in \mathbb N^+} X_n$
, where
$X_n = \left\{ x \in X \mid \left\|x\right\|_2 \geq n^{-1}\right\}$
is closed in
$X$
.
Since each
$X_n$
is a disjoint union of rays, it has a
$\sigma$
-locally finite network.
Then combining these network together yields a network of
$X$
.

To preserving
$\sigma$
-locally finiteness, we only need to require each network of
$X_n$
is
$\sigma$
-locally finite at
every point in
$X$
, due to countability of this union.
Using the closedness of
$X_n$
, points outside
$X_n$
automatically has a neighborhood disjoint from all sets in its network.

Theorem 2
.
$X$
does not
have a
$\sigma$
-locally finite
$k$
-network.
Proof
. Since network and
$k$
-network directly pass to subspace topologies, these properties are hereditary. Particularly,
$X$
has a subspace homeomorphic to a “sequential fan with
$\omega_1$
-many spines”, denoted
$S_{\omega_1}$
.
The topology of
$S_{\omega_1}$
can be obtained from
$\omega_1$
many
Fort space
,
$\omega_1 \times (\omega \cup \{p\})$
, identifying all “limit” points
$(i, p)$
into single one
$p$
, and denote
$S_{\omega_1} = (\omega_1 \times \omega) \cup \{p\}$
. We only need to show
$S_{\omega_1}$
does not have a
$\sigma$
-locally finite
$k$
-network.
Let
$\mathcal N = \bigcup_{i \in \mathbb N} \mathcal N_i$
be a
$k$
-network of
$S_{\omega_1}$
, where each
$\mathcal N_i$
is locally finite.
Define
$\mathcal A_i = \left\{ A \in \mathcal N_i \mid p\in \operatorname{cl}(A) \right\}$
, then
$\mathcal A_i$
is finite by locally finiteness at
$p$
of
$\mathcal N_i$
.
Therefore,
$\mathcal A := \left\{ A \in \mathcal N \mid p \in \operatorname{cl}(A) \right\} = \bigcup_{i \in \mathbb N} \mathcal A_i$
is countable.
For any subset
$A \subset S_{\omega_1}$
, define
$$ \operatorname{supp}(A) = \left\{ m \in \omega_1 \mid \exists n \in \omega, (m, n) \in A \right\}. $$
Split
$\mathcal A$
into
$\mathcal F = \left\{ A \in \mathcal A \mid \left| \operatorname{supp}(A) \right| < \aleph_0 \right\}$
and
$\mathcal I = \left\{ A \in \mathcal A \mid \left| \operatorname{supp}(A) \right| \geq \aleph_0 \right\}$
, define
$V = \bigcup_{A \in \mathcal F} \operatorname{supp}(A) \subseteq \omega_1$
, which is countable, hence there exists
$v \in V^c$
.
Now list
$\mathcal I = \left\{ I_0, I_1, \dots \right\}$
, for each
$i$
, take
$(m_i, n_i) \in I_i$
. Since
$\left| \operatorname{supp}(I_i) \right| \geq \aleph_0$
, we can choose the
$m_i$
such that the
$m_i$
and
$v$
are all distinct, hence
$F = \left\{ (m_i, n_i) \mid i \in \mathbb N \right\}$
are closed.
Define
$K := (\{v\} \times \omega) \cup \{p\} \subseteq F^c =: U$
, suppose
$K \subseteq A_1 \cup A_2 \cup \dots \cup A_p \subseteq U$
.
If
$A_i = I_j$
for some
$j$
,
$(m_j, n_j) \in I_j = A_i \subseteq U = F^c$
, a contradiction.
If
$A_i \in \mathcal F$
, then
$A_i \cap K \subseteq \{p\}$
.
If
$A_i \notin \mathcal A$
, then
$p \notin \operatorname{cl}(A)$
, meaning that there exists
$n$
such that
$A_i \cap K \subseteq \left\{ v \right\} \times [0, n)$
.
Finitely many such sets cannot cover the compact set
$K$
, and we get a contradiction.

Theorem 2
.
$X$
does not
have a
$\sigma$
-locally finite
$k$
-network.

Proof
. Since network and
$k$
-network directly pass to subspace topologies, these properties are hereditary. Particularly,
$X$
has a subspace homeomorphic to a “sequential fan with

-many spines”, denoted

.

The topology of
$S_{\omega_1}$
can be obtained from
$\omega_1$
many
Fort space
,
$\omega_1 \times (\omega \cup \{p\})$
, identifying all “limit” points
$(i, p)$
into single one
$p$
, and denote

. We only need to show
$S_{\omega_1}$
does not have a
$\sigma$
-locally finite
$k$
-network.

Let
$\mathcal N = \bigcup_{i \in \mathbb N} \mathcal N_i$
be a
$k$
-network of
$S_{\omega_1}$
, where each
$\mathcal N_i$
is locally finite.

Define
$\mathcal A_i = \left\{ A \in \mathcal N_i \mid p\in \operatorname{cl}(A) \right\}$
, then
$\mathcal A_i$
is finite by locally finiteness at
$p$
of
$\mathcal N_i$
.

Therefore,
$\mathcal A := \left\{ A \in \mathcal N \mid p \in \operatorname{cl}(A) \right\} = \bigcup_{i \in \mathbb N} \mathcal A_i$
is countable.

For any subset
$A \subset S_{\omega_1}$
, define
$$ \operatorname{supp}(A) = \left\{ m \in \omega_1 \mid \exists n \in \omega, (m, n) \in A \right\}. $$

Split
$\mathcal A$
into
$\mathcal F = \left\{ A \in \mathcal A \mid \left| \operatorname{supp}(A) \right| < \aleph_0 \right\}$
and
$\mathcal I = \left\{ A \in \mathcal A \mid \left| \operatorname{supp}(A) \right| \geq \aleph_0 \right\}$
, define
$V = \bigcup_{A \in \mathcal F} \operatorname{supp}(A) \subseteq \omega_1$
, which is countable, hence there exists
$v \in V^c$
.

Now list
$\mathcal I = \left\{ I_0, I_1, \dots \right\}$
, for each
$i$
, take
$(m_i, n_i) \in I_i$
. Since
$\left| \operatorname{supp}(I_i) \right| \geq \aleph_0$
, we can choose the
$m_i$
such that the
$m_i$
and
$v$
are all distinct, hence
$F = \left\{ (m_i, n_i) \mid i \in \mathbb N \right\}$
are closed.

Define
$K := (\{v\} \times \omega) \cup \{p\} \subseteq F^c =: U$
, suppose
$K \subseteq A_1 \cup A_2 \cup \dots \cup A_p \subseteq U$
.

If
$A_i = I_j$
for some
$j$
,
$(m_j, n_j) \in I_j = A_i \subseteq U = F^c$
, a contradiction.
If
$A_i \in \mathcal F$
, then
$A_i \cap K \subseteq \{p\}$
.
If
$A_i \notin \mathcal A$
, then
$p \notin \operatorname{cl}(A)$
, meaning that there exists
$n$
such that
$A_i \cap K \subseteq \left\{ v \right\} \times [0, n)$
.

Finitely many such sets cannot cover the compact set
$K$
, and we get a contradiction.

The above argument can be generalized to any CW complex that is
not locally-countable
.

Improve this question

How to compute
$\int_a^b x^z dx$
where
$0 < a < b < 1$
and z is complex,
$z=\alpha + i\beta$
?

Thanks.
answer:
As long as
$z\not=-1$
, then the anti-derivative of
$x^z$
is

$$\frac{x^{z+1}}{z+1}$$

The assumption here is that the
$z$
you provided is a constant and not dependent on
$x$
. If
$z$
is a function of
$x$
, then obviously the anti-derivative is much harder.

I left the case for
$z=-1$
as an exercise.

Improve this question

How to compute
$\int_a^b x^z dx$
where
$0 < a < b < 1$
and z is complex,
$z=\alpha + i\beta$
?

Thanks.
answer:
We provide a proof without assuming the power rule applies to
$x^z$
.

Let
$$
x^z=e^{z \ln x}.
$$
Then, substitute
$t=\ln x$
. Then
$dx = e^t \ dt$
, so
$$
\int_a^b x^z \ dx = \int_{\ln a}^{\ln b} e^{zt} e^t \ dt = \int_{\ln a}^{\ln b} e^{(z+1)t} \ dt.
$$
If
$z\neq -1$
, then
$$
\int_a^b x^z \ dx = \int_{\ln a}^{\ln b} e^{(z+1)t} \ dt = \left[\frac{e^{(z+1)t}}{z+1}\right]_{\ln a}^{\ln b} = \frac{e^{(z+1)\ln b}-e^{(z+1)\ln a}}{z+1}.
$$
Since
$e^{(z+1)\ln k}=k^{z+1}$
, we get
$$
\boxed{\int_a^b x^z \ dx = \frac{b^{z+1}-a^{z+1}}{z+1}
}
\quad (z\neq -1).
$$

In the case of
$z=-1$
,
$$
\int_a^b x^{-1}\ dx = \left[\ln x\right]_a^b = \ln b - \ln a = \boxed{\ln\frac ba}.
$$

I am learning quadratic equations and got confused while solving this question:

$$x^2−5x+6=0$$

I tried splitting the middle term but could not understand how to find the correct numbers.

Can someone explain the step-by-step process of solving this using factorization? Also, how do we know when a quadratic equation can be factorized easily?
answer:
In order for a quadratic to factor,

$$ax^2 + bx + c$$

you need to find two numbers that multiple to same value as
$a\cdot c$
and add to the number in the middle
$b$
. This is mostly done by guessing and checking through the factors. It is very similar to finding a common denominator when adding two fractions. For the case of

$$x^2 - 5x + 6$$

we want two numbers that multiple to
$6$
and add to
$-5$
. The only pairs of numbers that multiple to
$6$
are
$(6,1)$
,
$(2,3)$
,
$(-2,-3)$
, and
$(-6,-1)$
. If none of those pairs work, then the quadratic does not factor. Which of these pairs adds to
$-5$
?

It is
$(-2,-3)$
. Now the steps to factor are guaranteed; split the middle by those two numbers and factor out the GCD of each pair of terms. You will always end up with the same factor left over.

$$x^2-5x +6$$

$$x^2-2x-3x+6$$

$$x\cdot (x-2)-3\cdot (x-2)$$

$$(x-2)\cdot (x-3)$$

The last step is the tricky one. You can see the same factor
$x-2$
showed up when we factored out the GCD from each pair of terms. No matter what, as long as you found the correct pair, those factors will end up the same. Hence, they too can also be factored out, completing the factorization of the problem.

$$x^2−5x+6=0$$

I tried splitting the middle term but could not understand how to find the correct numbers.

Can someone explain the step-by-step process of solving this using factorization? Also, how do we know when a quadratic equation can be factorized easily?
answer:
First of all,
Welcome to the site !

One way to do it is to start completing the square, writing
$$x^2−5x+6=\left(x-\frac{5}{2}\right)^2+a$$
Subtract both sides to obtain the value of
$a$
and then use
$A^2-B^2=(A+B)(A-B)$

Do it; it is very simple.

Improve this question

I am learning quadratic equations and got confused while solving this question:

$$x^2−5x+6=0$$

I tried splitting the middle term but could not understand how to find the correct numbers.

Can someone explain the step-by-step process of solving this using factorization? Also, how do we know when a quadratic equation can be factorized easily?
answer:
First, let's be clear on what we mean when we say "factorization". A
quadratic
is an expression of the form

$Ax^2 + Bx + C$

where
$A,B,C$
are
constants
and
$x$
is a
variable
. Typically, when we are first learning about these objects, all constants and variables are real numbers.

The idea of factorization starts with the observation that the product of two linear expressions is a quadratic, i.e.

$\begin{align*}(ax+b)(cx+d) &= (ax)(cx) + (ax)(d)+(b)(cx) + (b)(d) \\ &= (ac)x^2 + (ad+bc)x + (bd) \\ &= Ax^2 + Bx + C\end{align*}$

Factorization is like the opposite of multiplication, in that we start with the end result and ask which things multiplied to make it. So, the problem of factorization is equivalent to the problem of solving this system of equations

$$\begin{align*}A &= ac \\ B &= ad+bc \\ C &= bd\end{align*}$$

for
$a,b,c,d$
from the given
$A,B,C$
. This is three equations and four variables, so there's generally infinitely many ways to do this. To simplify, we usually start with the case where
$a=c=1$
. In this case,
$A=1$
is forced (we will need to modify this method to handle other cases for
$A$
), and the remaining system is

$$\begin{align*} B &= d+b \\ C &= bd\end{align*}$$

In other words, we seek a pair of numbers whose sum is one given value and whose product is another given value.

We make one more simplifying assumption at the start of all this, which is that all the constants are
integers
, i.e. positive or negative whole numbers (or zero). Again, this forces
$B$
and
$C$
to be integers, and we will need a modification to handle other cases.

Once we've made these simplifying assumptions, the situation becomes very "small". For any integer, there are only finitely many pairwise factorizations. For example,

$$\begin{align*}12 &= 1\times 12 \\ &= 2\times 6 \\ &= 3\times 4 \\\end{align*}$$

so if
$C=12$
, you simply need to sum each of those pairs to see which one matches
$B$
. If any do, then

$$x^2+Bx+C = (x+b)(x+d)$$

as claimed; if none do, then the quadratic cannot be factorized by this method. Keep in mind that e.g.
$(-2)\times (-6) = 2\times 6$
, so you may need to adjust the signs to get the sum to be
$B$
.

That covers factorization of quadratics, but to solve a quadratic
equation
by factorization requires one extra step. Once you've got

$$x^2+Bx+C = (x+b)(x+d) = 0$$

you simply notice that the product of two real numbers will be zero if and only if at least one of them is zero. So, you consider the cases where each factor is zero, and combine the possible solutions.

$$ x+b = 0 \implies x = -b\\ x+d = 0 \implies x = -d $$

so the full set of solutions is
$x\in\{-b,-d\}$
.

Taking your example, we have
$C = 6 = 1\times 6 = 2\times 3$
.

$$
\begin{align*}
1+6 &= 7 \neq -5 && \text{❌} \\
2+3 &= 5 \neq -5 && \text{❌} \\
(-1)+(-6) &= -7 \neq -5 && \text{❌} \\
(-2)+(-3) &= -5 = -5 && \text{✅}
\end{align*}
$$

Thus, we have

$$x^2 - 5x + 6 = (x-2)(x-3) = 0$$

Setting each of these factors equal to zero gives

$$x-2=0 \implies x = 2 \\ x-3=0\implies x=3$$

and so the solution is
$x\in\{2,3\}$
.

Improve this question

I am learning quadratic equations and got confused while solving this question:

$$x^2−5x+6=0$$

I tried splitting the middle term but could not understand how to find the correct numbers.

Can someone explain the step-by-step process of solving this using factorization? Also, how do we know when a quadratic equation can be factorized easily?
answer:
To factor a quadratic:

Let's say we have a quadratic
$x^2 + bx + c$
, where
$b, c$
represent coefficients. Then,
try finding divisors of
$c$
that add up to
$b$
.
Usually,
$a=1$
, which makes the job easier. These allow for factorization.

What this means: for your formula above, we can see that
$b=-5, c=6$
. We notice that
$c = 6 = -2 \times -3$
, and that
$-2 -3 = 5 = b$
. Now, for the factorization:
$$
x^2-5x+6 = (x-3)(x-2) = x(x-3)-2(x-3)=x^2-3x-2x+6.
$$
Notice that the last term is the product of
$-2 \times -3 = 6$
while the coefficient of the middle term is
$-2 -3 = -5$
.

Essentially, finding divisors of
$c$
that add up to
$b$
and adding those terms in
$(x \ \cdots\ )$
allows us to factorize a quadratic.

To be honest, a
concise Khan Academy video
and a lot of practice will be a lot more helpful than knowing multiple methods for factorization.

Note that later, you will encounter quadratics where the
$x^2$
has a coefficient, but for now, we can skip this.

To find out whether a quadratic equation can be factored easily:

First, just try a variety of factors with the above method.

If that doesn't work, you should attempt to use the quadratic formula to find the roots:
$$
x = \frac{-b \pm \sqrt{b^2 - 4ac}}{2a}.
$$

Hope this helps.

Improve this question

I am learning quadratic equations and got confused while solving this question:

$$x^2−5x+6=0$$

I tried splitting the middle term but could not understand how to find the correct numbers.

Can someone explain the step-by-step process of solving this using factorization? Also, how do we know when a quadratic equation can be factorized easily?
answer:
Background

Your final polynomial will be of the form
$$(x+p)(x+q).$$
Expanding this gives
$$x^2+(p+q)x+pq.$$
Your original polynomial is of the form
$x^2+bx+c$
.
So, in order to find
$p$
and
$q$
we need to find two numbers such that
$b=p+q$
and
$c=pq$
. In other words, we need to two numbers that add together to
$b$
and that multiply together to
$c$
.

Sign of
$p$
and
$q$

First, we get some hints from the signs. We have
$b=-5$
and
$c=6$
. Let's focus on
$c$
first. We need two numbers that together multiply to a positive number. So that means that
$p$
and
$q$
are either both positive or both negative. For example,
$10\times 3=+30$
or
$(-10)\times(-3)=+30$
. Looking at
$b$
we immediately see that both numbers must be negative, since their sum must be negative.

Factorising

Usually these numbers are nice and simple. This is not a fact of nature, but this is because your textbook usually picks nice numbers. So as a first step we can check into which numbers
$c$
factorises. We have
$6=(-6)\times(-1)$
and
$6=(-3)\times(-2)$
. The last one looks promising so let's check it. We have
$p+q=-5=b$
,  so these numbers have solved the problem! Our final answer is
$p=-3, q=-2$
:
$$(x-3)(x-2)$$
Final note: you cannot know upfront if the quadratic can be factored easily. You have to try and see. Luckily there is a formula that finds the roots for a general quadratic (the quadratic formula) in case factoring is too hard.

Improve this question

I am learning quadratic equations and got confused while solving this question:

$$x^2−5x+6=0$$

I tried splitting the middle term but could not understand how to find the correct numbers.

Can someone explain the step-by-step process of solving this using factorization? Also, how do we know when a quadratic equation can be factorized easily?
answer:
Suppose there is a factorization [1]:
$$\begin{align*}(ax+b)(cx+d)  \ &= (ac)x^2 + (ad+bc)x + (bd) \\ &= Ax^2 + Bx + C.\end{align*}$$
If we compute the discriminant
$B^2-4AC$
we find it is
$(ad-bc)^2.$
So defining
$E=ad-bc$
we have
$ad+bc=B,ad-bc=E$
from which
$ad=(B+E)/2$
and
$bc=(B-E)/2.$

To get at
$a,b,c,d$
individually, we assume
$\gcd(A,B,C)=1.$
This implies that each of the pairs
$a,b$
and
$c,d$
is coprime. Finally we assume
$a>0$
, so that, using
$\gcd(c,d)=1,$
we can recover
$a$
from
$ac=A$
and
$ad=(B+E)/2$
as the gcd of these. Now knowing
$a$
we next recover
$c,d$
using again the equations
$ac=A$
and
$ad=(B+E)/2.$
Finally
$d=C/b.$

question:
I am interested in the divisibility problem
$(n^2+1)\mid(2^n+n)$
for
$n>0$
.

Some immediate conditions: If
$n$
is odd, then
$n^2+1$
is even but
$2^n+n$
is odd, so
$n$
must be even. Moreover, if
$p\mid(n^2+1)$
is prime, then
$n^2\equiv -1\pmod p$
, hence
$p\equiv 1\pmod 4$
. Also, from
$(n^2+1)\mid(2^n+n)$
we get
$2^n\equiv -n\pmod p$
, so
$2^{2n}\equiv -1\pmod p$
and therefore
$2^{4n}\equiv 1\pmod p$
.

In order to get a family of solutions
. Is there a function such that if
$n$
is a solution implies
$f(n)$
is also a solution?

Is there a proof that no such
$n>0$
exists?

@Lhachimi found
$n=4 968 914 496=2^6\cdot 77639289$
and @JordanB proved
$16|n$
but
$32,64|n$
remains possible, to verify it run (maybe in Colab):

n = 4968914496
modulo = (n ** 2) + 1
r1 = pow(2, n, modulo)
r2 = modulo - n
if r1==r2:
print("Solution")
else:
print("No solution")
answer:
I've made a modest strengthening of the elementary obstruction. It does not yet settle the problem, but it shows that any solution must be divisible by
$16$
. I will continue to think about this but I post this answer in the hopes it pushes myself or others towards a full proof.

My claim: If
$n>0$
and
$(n^2+1)\mid(2^n+n)$
, then
$16\mid n$
.

Proof: Let
$M=n^2+1$
, and write
$n=2^a u$
, with
$u$
odd. As noted in the question,
$a\geq 1$
. Let
$p\mid M$
be prime, and put
$d_p=\operatorname{ord}_p(2)$
. Since:
$$
2^n\equiv -n\pmod p,
$$
and
$-n$
has order
$4$
modulo
$p$
, we have:
$$
\frac{d_p}{(d_p,n)}=4.
$$
Hence
$v_2(d_p)=a+2$
. In particular this means
$p\equiv 1\pmod 8$
, so
$2$
is a quadratic residue modulo
$p$
. Thus
$d_p\mid (p-1)/2$
, and as a result:
$$
p\equiv 1\pmod {2^{a+3}},
$$
for every prime
$p\mid M$
.

For
$a=1$
this gives
$M\equiv 1\pmod 8$
, whereas:
$$
M=4u^2+1\equiv 5\pmod 8.
$$
For
$a=2$
it gives
$M\equiv 1\pmod {32}$
, whereas:
$$
M=16u^2+1\equiv 17\pmod {32}.
$$
Thus
$a\geq 3$
.

It remains to exclude
$a=3$
. Set
$x=n+1$
. For
$p\mid M$
, we have:
$$
x^2=(n+1)^2\equiv 2n\pmod p,
$$
so:
$$
x^4\equiv -4\pmod p.
$$
Also
$2^{2n}\equiv -1\pmod p$
, and
$n$
is even, so:
$$
x^{4n}\equiv (-4)^n=2^{2n}\equiv -1\pmod p.
$$
Therefore
$\operatorname{ord}_p(x)$
divides
$8n$
but not
$4n$
. If
$a=3$
, this gives:
$$
v_2(\operatorname{ord}_p(x))=6.
$$
Since we already know that
$p\equiv 1\pmod {64}$
, it follows that
$x$
is a quadratic non-residue modulo
$p$
precisely when
$p\equiv 65\pmod {128}$
.

Now I want to compute the Jacobi symbol. Since
$M\equiv 1\pmod 4$
and
$M\equiv 2\pmod {n+1}$
:
$$
\left(\frac{x}{M}\right)
=
\left(\frac{n+1}{n^2+1}\right)
=
\left(\frac{n^2+1}{n+1}\right)
=
\left(\frac{2}{n+1}\right).
$$
For
$a=3$
we have
$n+1\equiv 1\pmod 8$
, so this Jacobi symbol is
$1$
.

Writing
$M=\prod p^{e_p}$
, the preceding work says that the number of primes
$p$
with
$e_p$
odd and
$p\equiv 65\pmod {128}$
is even. But every
$p\mid M$
is
$1$
more than a multiple of
$64$
, so
$p^{e_p}\equiv 65\pmod {128}$
exactly for those same primes. It follows that
$M\equiv 1\pmod {128}$
. This contradicts:
$$
M=n^2+1=64u^2+1\equiv 65\pmod {128}.
$$
Thus
$a\neq 3$
as well. Therefore
$v_2(n)\geq 4$
, i.e.
$16\mid n$
.

for

answer:
I post this answer in the hopes it pushes myself or others towards a full proof.

My claim: If
$n>0$
and
$n^2+1\mid2^n+n$
, then
$n$
is not a power of
$2$

let
$n=2^v\alpha$
where
$\alpha$
and
$N=n^2+1$
are odd integers

we have
$N\mid 2^n+n \Rightarrow N\mid 2^{n-v}+\alpha \Rightarrow 2^{n-v}\equiv-\alpha\pmod N$

so
$\text{ord}_N(-\alpha)=\text{ord}_N(2^{n-v})=\displaystyle \frac{\text{ord}_N(2)}{\gcd(\text{ord}_N(2),n-v)}$
and
$v_2(\text{ord}_N(2))=2+v$

note that
$v_2(n-v)=v_2(2^v\alpha-v)=v_2(v)\le \log_2(v)<v+2$

so
$\min(v_2(\text{ord}_N(2)),v_2(n-v))=v_2(v) \implies v_2(\text{ord}_N(-\alpha))=2+v-v_2(v)\ge 2+v-\log_2(v)$

thanks to @Jordan B,
$v\ge 4 \Rightarrow 2+v-\log_2(v)\ge 4 $
so
$\alpha>1$
because
$\text{ord}_N(-1)=2<4$

question:
Given a time-domain function
$f(t)$
representing an audio signal over a short time interval, I would like to find the coefficients
$a$
and
$b$
such that:

$$\tilde{f}(t) = \sum_{k=0}^{N} \left( a_k \sin(2^{k/n}\omega_0 t) + b_k \cos(2^{k/n}\omega_0 t) \right)$$


Minimizing the total squared error
$\int_{0}^{T} (\tilde{f}(t) - f(t))^2 ~dt$
over a time slice of length
$T$
.


Where
$n$
is the number of divisions per octave (defining an octave as a double of frequency),
$\omega_0$
is the angular frequency corresponding to
$k=0$
(e.g.,
$27.5$
cycles or
$55\pi$
radians per second for the lowest note on a standard grand piano), and
$N \approx 10n$
is the upper bound for the index
$k$
.


My question is motivated by musical signal analysis.  The standard Fourier series uses linearly spaced frequencies (
$k\omega_0 t$
), but I want a model that more closely reflects
the logarithmic nature of pitch perception
.


For example, with
$n=12$
, the basis frequencies correspond to the standard
twelve-tone equal-tempered scale
.  Using larger values of
$n$
allows more precise representation of microtonal or nonstandard-tuned (intentionally or not) music.

Does this type of trigonometric series have a name? And is there a formula for the coefficients?
answer:
You can easily do this using the theory of projecting onto the span of a collection of (not-necessarily-orthogonal) vectors. Fix
$T>0$
. Let
$V$
be the real vector space of functions
$g:[0,T]\rightarrow\mathbb{R}$
that satisfy
$$ \int_0^T g(t)^2dt<\infty$$
Define real inner product
$$\langle f, g\rangle = \int_0^Tf(t)g(t)dt \quad \forall f, g \in V$$
Let
$\{v_1, ...., v_m\}$
be any collection of functions in
$V$
(such as
$v_1=1$
,
$v_2=\sin(2^{1/n}\omega_0t)$
,
$v_2=\sin(2^{2/n}\omega_0t)$
and so on). Define the subspace
$$ S = Span\{v_1, v_2, ..., v_m\}$$
which is defined as the set of all linear combinations of the functions
$v_1, ..., v_m$
. Given a function
$f\in V$
, the function
$p \in S$
that minimizes
$$\langle p-f,p-f\rangle = \int_0^T(p(t)-f(t))^2dt$$
is the projection of
$f$
onto the subspace
$S$
. The answer is
$$ \boxed{p(t) = \sum_{i=1}^m a_i v_i(t)}$$
where the best coefficients
$a=(a_1, ..., a_m)$
solve the Gram equation
$$ \boxed{h=Ga}$$
where
$G=(g_{ij})$
is a
$m\times m$
symmetric matrix with
$$ g_{ij} = \langle v_i,v_j\rangle = \int_0^Tv_i(t)v_j(t)dt$$
and
$h=(h_1, ..., h_m) \in \mathbb{R}^m$
satisfies
$$ h_i = \langle f, v_i\rangle = \int_0^Tf(t)v_i(t)dt$$
The equation
$h=Ga$
always has at least one solution, and
$G$
is invertible if and only if
$\{v_1, ..., v_m\}$
are linearly independent (in which case
$a=G^{-1}h$
). (Fourier series is for the special case when
$\{v_1, ..., v_m\}$
are orthonormal, in which case
$G=I$
, but that does not look like your situation.) You can get some intuition about how your frequencies are related to each other by looking at the
$G$
matrix and seeing how the
$g_{ij}$
coefficients decay as
$|i-j|$
gets large.

See page 50 here:
https://ee.usc.edu/stochastic-nets/docs/projections.pdf

In your case, it looks like you will have
$m=1+2(N+1)$
(if we include the constant function
$1$
, plus
$N+1$
cosines, plus
$N+1$
sines). If you define
$$ v_1=1$$
$$v_2 = \sin(2^{1/n}\omega_0t)$$
$$v_3 = \sin(2^{2/n}\omega_0 t)$$
and so on, then, for example
$$ g_{11} = \int_0^T 1\cdot 1 dt = T$$
$$g_{12} = \int_0^T 1\cdot \sin(2^{1/n}\omega_0 t)dt $$
$$g_{23} = \int_0^T \sin(2^{1/n}\omega_0t) \sin(2^{2/n}\omega_0t)dt$$
and so on.

You can derive the same thing directly by defining your cost as
$$ \int_0^T\left(f(t) - \sum_{i=1}^m a_i v_i(t)\right)^2dt$$
and minimizing with respect to all
$a_i$
coefficients by setting
$\frac{\partial}{\partial a_i} = 0$
.

question:
Problem
. Prove that
$2^{317} > 81^{50}$
by AM-GM only (along with some easy calculations),
without calculus, logarithms, Bernoulli inequality,
$\mathrm{e}^{x} \ge 1 + x$
, binomial theorem, etc.

(
Note
:
$(1+x)^n \ge 1 + nx$
(
$n\in \mathbb{Z}_{>1}$
) can be seen as AM-GM:

$(1+ nx)^{1/n} \le \frac{(1+nx) + 1 \times (n-1)}{n} = 1 + x$
.)

More concretely, I want to convert
$2^{317} > 81^{50}$
to
$\frac{p}{q} > x^{m/n}$
(
$p, q, m, n\in \mathbb{Z}_{>1}$
) and the apply AM-GM:
$x^{m/n} \le \frac{x\times m + 1 \times (n-m)}{n}$
, something like that.

This question is a follow-up of
here
.

My attempt
. We have

$2^{317} > 81^{50} \iff 2\cdot (8/9)^5 > (9/8)^{15/17}$
. Then I use
AM-GM:

$$(9/8)^{15/17} \le \frac{\frac98\times 15 + 1 \times 2}{17}.$$

But it is not enough.


Motivation
. For number comparison
$a^b > c^d$
where
$a, b, c, d\in \mathbb{Z}_{>1}$
, I prefer to use AM-GM. Some examples (in MSE) are given as follows.


(i)
$5^{53} > 2^{123}$


We have

$$5^{53} > 2^{123} \iff (5/4)^{53} > 2^{17}
\iff (5/4)^{68} > 2^{17}\cdot (5/4)^{15}$$


$$\iff (5/4)^{4} > 2 \cdot (5/4)^{15/17}
\iff \frac{5^4}{2^9} > (5/4)^{15/17}$$


$$\impliedby \frac{5^4}{2^9} > \frac{83}{68}$$

where we use AM-GM:

$$(5/4)^{15/17} = \sqrt[17]{\left(\frac54\right)^{15}\times 1 \times 1}
\le \frac{\frac54 \times 15 + 1 + 1}{17} = \frac{83}{68}.$$


(ii)
$2^{2016} > 10^{605}$


We have

$$2^{2016} > 10^{605}
\iff 2^{1411} > 5^{605} \iff 2^{201} > (5/4)^{605}$$


$$\iff 2 > (5/4)^3 \cdot (5/4)^{2/201}$$


$$\impliedby 2 > (5/4)^3 \cdot \frac{403}{402}$$

where we use AM-GM:

$$(5/4)^{2/201} = \sqrt[201]{(5/4)^2 \times 1^{199}}
\le \frac{\frac54\times 2 + 199}{201} = \frac{403}{402}.$$


(iii)
$7^{31} > 8^{29}$


We can do it by AM-GM.


(iv)
$(6/5)^{\sqrt{3}} > (5/4)^{\sqrt2}$


Note
. After some manipulations, using
$\sqrt6 < \frac{49}{20}$
, it suffices to prove that
$(6/5)(24/25)^3(24/25)^{29/20} > 1$
.


We can use AM-GM to prove that
$(24/25)^{29/20} > 1 - \frac{29}{20}\cdot \frac{1}{25}$
.

I was wondering what are the homeomorphisms of the real line of order two, that is, continuous maps
$f:\mathbb{R} \to \mathbb{R}$
such that
$f^{-1}$
is continuous and
$f = f^{-1}$
. For example, if
$f$
is an homeomorphism of order
$2$
, and
$\varphi$
is an homeomorphism, then
$g = \varphi^{-1} f \varphi$
works (which makes sense, as elements of order
$2$
have conjugates of order
$2$
).

Is there a unique non-trivial equivalence class of order two homeomorphisms (under conjugation; it should be the class containing the homeomorphism
$f(x) = -x$
)?
answer:
There is only one such class.

Take any homeomorphism
$f$
satisfying the requirements of the question. Continuous injections either preserve or reverse the order on
$\mathbb{R}$
, so such a map
$f$
must be strictly decreasing.

I claim that the map
$f$
has a unique fixed point. Since
$f(f(0))=0$
, we have

$f(f(0))-f(0)=-f(0)$
$f(0)-0=f(0)$
,

so we can apply the Intermediate Value Theorem to the continuous function
$f(x)-x$
between
$0$
and
$f(0)$
to find a
$c$
satisfying
$f(c)-c=0$
, or in other words
$f(c) = c$
. Uniqueness of this fixed point follows since
$f$
is strictly decreasing.

Define the map
$$\varphi(x)=\begin{cases}c+x&x>0 \\ c&x=0\\ f(c-x)&x<0\end{cases}$$

which has inverse
$$\varphi^{-1}(x)=\begin{cases}x-c&x>c \\ 0&x=c\\ c-f(x)&x<c\end{cases}$$

Now, if
$x>0$
, then
$(\varphi^{-1} \circ f\circ \varphi)(x)= (\varphi^{-1} \circ f)(c+x)= \varphi^{-1}(f(c+x))=c-f(f(c+x))=c-(c+x)=-x$
.

For
$x<0$
the analogous calculation yields
$(\varphi^{-1} \circ f\circ \varphi)(x)=(\varphi^{-1} \circ f)(f(c-x))=\varphi^{-1}(f(f(c-x)))=\varphi^{-1}(c-x)=c-x-c=-x$
.

Since we started with an arbitrary
$f$
, the equivalence class of
$x\mapsto -x$
indeed contains every such
$f$
.

Improve this question

If you were asked to find a way to express the volume of an n dimensional hypercube with sides P1,P2,P3...Pn (such that they are all integers) as the sum of some integers, how would you go about it?
I thought about using determinants to express it while changing the hypercube into an n dimensional parallelotop then somehow working it out using volume squared but I am not sure if this can work
Can anyone suggest a method like maybe transforming the hypercube into another shape with the same volune where this is possible
There are no specific instructions on how to go about this and I apologize already if I missed something obvious here
answer:
Assume this is the n-dimensional box

$$
B=[0,P_1]\times\cdots\times[0,P_n],
$$

where each
$P_i$
is a positive integer. Divide each side into unit intervals. Then
$B$
is tiled by
unit n-cubes
indexed by

$$
(k_1,\ldots,k_n),\qquad 0\le k_i<P_i.
$$

Each unit n-cube has volume
$1$
, so

$$
\operatorname{Vol}(B)
=
\sum_{k_1=0}^{P_1-1}\cdots
\sum_{k_n=0}^{P_n-1}1
=
P_1P_2\cdots P_n.
$$
Now, let
$D=\operatorname{diag}(P_1,\ldots,P_n)$
, so the original box has volume

$V=|\det D|=P_1\cdots P_n$
.

Now choose any nontrivial integer matrix
$U\in SL_n(\mathbb Z)$
, meaning
$\det U=1$
, and set

$A=UD$
.

The columns of
$A$
give an integer parallelotope with the same volume, since

$|\det A|=|\det U||\det D|=P_1\cdots P_n$
.

Using the
Leibniz formula
,

$V=\det A=\sum_{\sigma\in S_n}\operatorname{sgn}(\sigma)\prod_{i=1}^n A_{i,\sigma(i)}$
.

Each term is an integer, so this expresses the volume as a signed sum of integers.

For example, in dimension
$2$
, take

$U=\begin{pmatrix}1&1\\1&2\end{pmatrix}$
.

Then

$A=\begin{pmatrix}P_1&P_2\\P_1&2P_2\end{pmatrix}$
,

so

$V=\det A=2P_1P_2-P_1P_2=P_1P_2$
.

ie shear the box by an integer volume-preserving transformation, then expand the determinant.

question:
The set
$S = \{1, 2, 3, \dots, 12\}$
is to be partitioned into three sets
$A$
,
$B$
,
$C$
of equal size. Such that
$A \cup B \cup C = S$
,
$A \cap B = B \cap C = A \cap C = \emptyset$
. Then find the number of ways to partition
$S$
.

My doubt is that, we begin with 12C4 since we have 12 objects and now we have to select four of them. Similarly out of 8 remaining objects later, and obviously the remaining 4. So, there are
$$12C4×8C4×4C4$$
ways. I agree this. But we don't know which is A, B and C. To permute this between, we need 3! of ways. But the term is absent from the answer. The answer is:
$$12!/(4!)^3.$$

I feel that, due to this reason, the answer should be
$$12!/3!(4!)^3.$$

I would like to know which's wrong – my method of solving, or the official answer. Websites and YouTube Channels had given dissatisfactory explanations that they didn't even consider about this permutation between A,B and C, and in fact, given a blind answer without a proper approach, that's why I asked this question here.

Note: I know that there's the same question been asked by someone in our site too, but answer still not correct, though the questioner had the same doubt as me (asked 11 years ago).
answer:
Your way calculates the number of ways to partition the elements of the set as successive ordered triplets of quadruplets, then dividing by the number of orderings of the resulting triplet of quadruplets. The rationale you used for this last step is because for each
unordered
triplet, there are
$3! = 6$
distinct orderings. So your calculation is
$$\frac{\binom{12}{4} \binom{8}{4} \binom{4}{4}}{3!} = 5775.$$

The same result can be obtained by considering the number of orderings of
$S$
, then inserting partitions between the
$4^{\rm th}$
and
$5^{\rm th}$
element, and betwen the
$8^{\rm th}$
and
$9^{\rm th}$
element.  Then we divide by
$(4!)^3$
to adjust for the overcounting in the number of ways to count unordered quadruplets, and finally, to divide by
$3!$
as before to account for the overcounting in the arrangement of triplets.  This gives

$$\frac{12!}{(4!)^3 \, 3!} = 5775.$$

But the problem says to partition
$S$
into
sets
$A$
,
$B$
,
$C$
.  Your interpretation of the problem does not identify which set is
$A$
,
$B$
, or
$C$
.  However, the problem is implying that, for instance, the partitions
$$A = \{1, 2, 3, 4\}, \quad B = \{5, 6, 7, 8\}, \quad C = \{9, 10, 11, 12\}$$
and
$$A = \{5, 6, 7, 8\}, \quad B =  \{9, 10, 11, 12\}, \quad C = \{1, 2, 3, 4\},$$
are
distinct
.  So you do
not
divide by
$3!$
in either case because the premise that the subsets are indistinguishable is incorrect.  Now, could the problem been worded more precisely to address this issue of interpretation?  Absolutely.  But in my mind, if the problem had wanted us to divide by
$3!$
, the wording would have taken care to not assign labels to the three subsets.

question:
I am working through Spanier's text on Algebraic Topology, particularly the chapter on homotopy theory.

In Spanier's text, when developing the Puppe cofibre sequence, we consider a map
$f:X \rightarrow Y$
of pointed spaces and consider the sequence
$X \rightarrow Y \rightarrow C_{f}$
and show that this is co-exact (where
$C_{f}$
is the based mapping cone of f).

Question: I suspect that if we have a sequence of maps
$X \rightarrow Y \rightarrow Z$
of pointed spaces given by
$f$
and
$g$
respectively, it is also true that the natural sequence of based cones:
$C_{f} \rightarrow C_{g \circ f} \rightarrow C_{g}$
is co-exact. I read that this is precisely the octahedral axiom for a triangulated category. I am not very familiar with triangulated categories and could not find any literature that discusses this phenomenon in the category
$\mathsf{Top_{*}}$
. If
$\phi: C_{f} \rightarrow C_{g \circ f}$
is the first map, I think I will have to show that
$C_{g}$
is naturally homotopy equivalent to
$C_{\phi}$
, and then use the standard Puppe Co-exact sequence for the latter. But is this true at all? I am unable to see why. If so, would I be correct in assuming that all of this holds analogously for the case of the Puppe fibre sequence?
answer:
Yes on all accounts. That is: Given maps
$X \xrightarrow{f} Y \xrightarrow{g} Z$
of pointed spaces, the sequence
$C_f \to C_{gf} \to C_g$
is a cofiber sequence. If
$\mathrm{hTop}_*$
admitted the structure of a triangulated category, where distinguished triangles were given by cofiber sequences, then this would just be the octahedral axiom, but it does not, so one has to give a different proof.

The modern, categorial perspective is straightforward: The commutative square
$\require{AMScd}$
\begin{CD}
X @> f >> Y \\
@| @VV g V \\
X @>>gf> Z
\end{CD}
induces a morphism of cofiber sequences
\begin{CD}
X @> f >> Y @>>> C_f \\
@| @VV g V @VVV\\
X @>>gf> Z @>>> C_{gf}
\end{CD}
Taking vertical cofibers then yields a (homotopy) commutative diagram
\begin{CD}
X @> f >> Y @>>> C_f \\
@| @VV g V @VVV \\
X @>>gf> Z @>>> C_{gf} \\
@VVV @VVV @VVV \\
* @>>> C_g @= C_g
\end{CD}
where each row and each column is a cofiber sequence, so the vertical maps at the very right give you the sequence you're looking for.

Whether you'll accept this as proof depends on how comfortable you are with the last step, i.e. that taking vertical cofibers in a diagram gives you another cofiber sequence. In principle this is something that can be verified just using a point-set definition of cofiber; a modern proof would deduce this by fiddling around with pushouts.

Either way, there is an elementary proof in the literature, which you can find under Lemma 1.2.5 in
May & Ponto's "More Concise Algebraic Topology"
(this link goes to the publisher's website, but a draft version of the book is available freely from the first author's site). The dual version for fibers also holds and is stated right afterwards.

This is the only reference I know for this from the top of my head, and taking a look may serve to motivate why I gave the proof sketch above: The statement in More Concise is rather less concise and more convoluted. The argument above is rather simpler and (in my opinion, anyhow) more enlightening (the comparison makes a good case for the modern approach to homotopy theory, if you ask me :-).

question:
John L. Kelley proved in his 1950 article "
The Tychonoff Product Theorem Implies the Axiom of Choice
" what its title says.

The given indexed family of nonempty sets is denoted there by
$(X_a)_{a\in A}$
. The first step is to adjoin, for each
$a\in A$
, a single point denoted by
$\Lambda$
by Kelley to
$X_a$
to obtain
$Y_a=X_a\cup\{\Lambda\}$
.

Then, quoting Kelley: "We assign a topology for
$Y_a$
by defining the void set and complements of finite sets to be open." It seems to me that he was taking the cofinite topology on
$Y_a$
, which is indeed compact.

Question

But in cofinite topology, the closed subsets are the finite sets and the whole space
$Y_a$
itself. Thus if
$X_a$
is infinite, then
$X_a$
is not closed in
$Y_a$
. But the proof requires that
$X_a$
is closed in
$Y_a$
, so that
$\{\pi_a^{-1}(X_a)\}_{a\in A}$
(where
$\pi_a$
is the canonical projection) is a family of closed subsets on which one can talk about the finite intersection property.

So, what am I missing here?
answer:
You are right, Kelley's proof does not work.
$X_\alpha$
is closed in
$Y_\alpha$
iff
$\{\Lambda\}$
is open
$Y_\alpha$
, and for infinite
$X_\alpha$
this is not true with the cofinite topology on
$Y_\alpha$
.

To correct the proof, you can enlarge the topology by appending all sets of the form
$\{\Lambda\} \cup U$
with
$U$
in the cofinite topology. With this finer topology everything works nicely.

However, it is much easier to take
$\{\emptyset, \{\Lambda\}, Y_\alpha\}$
as the topology on
$Y_\alpha$
.

Remark.

For his proof Kelley has to take a point
$\Lambda$
not contained in
$\bigcup_{\alpha \in A} X_\alpha$
. Alternatively one could take an arbitrary
$\Lambda$
and give
$Y_\alpha = X_\alpha \cup \{\Lambda\}$
the trivial topology if
$\Lambda \in X_\alpha$
and the topology
$\{\emptyset, \{\Lambda\}, Y_\alpha\}$
if
$\Lambda \notin X_\alpha$
.

question:
Consider the scalar conservation law

$$
u_t + f(u)_x = 0, \qquad (x,t) \in \mathbb{R} \times (0,\infty),
$$


with a strictly convex flux
$f \in C^2(\mathbb{R})$
,
$f'' > 0$
, and Riemann initial data


$$
u(x,0) =
\begin{cases}
u_l, & x < 0,\\
u_r, & x > 0.
\end{cases}
$$


It is well known that:




If
$u_l < u_r$
, the entropy solution is a
rarefaction wave
.


If
$u_l > u_r$
, the entropy solution is a
shock wave
(satisfying the Lax entropy condition
$f'(u_l) > s > f'(u_r)$
).




This problem models where a tube is divided by a diaphragm at
$x=0$
; the left side has high pressure/density, the right side low pressure/density. After rupture, a rarefaction moves left and a shock moves right. Now imagine observing the same experiment from the opposite end of the tube. It seems that physically nothing changes and we just flip our coordinate system. But mathematically, if we simply swap the labels "left" and "right", the initial discontinuity now has
$u_l < u_r$
(since the high state is now on the right). For a convex flux, this would be classified as a rarefaction, yet the original experiment had a shock on the right. So flipping the tube seems to turn a shock into a rarefaction? How does the theory resolve this?
answer:
You'll have to set
$U(t,X)=u(t,x)$
where
$X=-x$
. This way,
$u_t = U_t$
, and
$$f(u)_x = f'(u) u_x = -f'(U) U_X = -f(U)_X .
$$
In other words, we need to swap the initial data,
$(U_l, U_r) = (u_r, u_l)$
, and replace
$f'(u)$
by
$-f'(U)$
in the resolution of the Riemann problem. Then it appears that the Lax entropy condition is written
$$
-f'(U_l) > S > -f'(U_r) , \quad S=-s,
$$
in the reflected coordinate system, which is exactly equivalent to the Lax entropy condition in the initial coordinate system,
$f'(u_l) > s > f'(u_r)$
. A shock is still obtained under the same condition if we look at the Riemann IVP from the right to the left (instead of from the left to the right)!

Improve this question

A block slides on top of a rough surface such that its coefficient of kinetic friction is
$\mu$
. If initially the block has kinetic energy
$K$
and it has traveled a distance
$x$
before it stops, what is the mass of the object?

So I'm trying to check if my solution was correct or not.

We know that the work is just the change of the kinetic energy of the block. That is
$$W = \Delta K$$


Thus we conclude that
$W = -K$
. Now we also know that
$W = F_{x} \Delta x = (F - f_{k})(x-0)$


Where
$F$
is whatever the force that is causing the the block to move/accelerate. Then we wee that,


$$F-mg\mu = -\frac{E}{x} \implies m = \frac{E}{g\mu x}+\frac{F}{g \mu}$$


This is correct right? Or am I missing something...also can the
$F$
be written as
$F =ma$
instead?  Where
$a$
is the acceleration of the block?
answer:
You noted that
$W=\Delta K=-K$
. The forces acting on the object are gravity
$mg$
acting downwards, the normal to the surface
$N$
, acting upwards, and the friction, opposing the motion direction. The normal force and gravity cancel each other, and the friction force is proportional to the normal force.
$$N=mg\\F_f=\mu N=\mu mg\\W=\vec F_f\Delta \vec x$$
Note that
$F_f$
and
$\Delta x$
are opposite directions, so
$$K=\mu mg x$$

question:
Let
$R$
be a commutative ring (may assumed to be a domain if needed) and
$I$
be any ideal in
$R$
. It is well-known that if
$R$
is a Bézout domain (finitely generated ideals are principal), then the natural group morphism
$\mathrm{SL}(2,R)\to \mathrm{SL}(2,R/I)$
is surjective for all
$I$
. We know that
$R$
being simply a UFD is not enough for surjectivity. So it is motivated to ask is the converse of the statement true: Suppose
$\mathrm{SL}(2,R)\to \mathrm{SL}(2,R/I)$
is surjective for all
$I$
, is
$R$
necessarily a Bézout domain? If true, could you give a reference of the result? Thanks in advance.
answer:
Edit (13/05).
In order to make this answer more readable, I have rewritten it completely. The contents remain the same (i've included the edits of May 12th, and added clarifications for Dedekind domains)

Nope.
To see why, we need few lemmas:

Lemma 1.
If
$S$
is a commutative semi-local ring, then for all
$a,b\in S$
satisfying
$(a,b)=S$
, there exists
$s\in S$
such that
$a+sb\in S^\times$
.

Proof.
This follows from the following version of prime avoidance lemma, applied to the finitely many maximal ideals of
$S$
: assume that
$a\in S$
and
$I$
is an ideal of
$S$
such that
$aS+I\not\subset \bigcup_{k=1}^n P_i$
, where the
$P_i's$
are prime ideals. Then
$a+c\not\subset \bigcup_{k=1}^n P_i$
for some
$c\in I$

Lemma 2.
If
$S$
is semi local,
$SL_2(S)$
is generated by transvections.

Proof.
Since elements of rows/columns of an element of
$SL_2(S)$
satisfy the condition of the lemma, it follows easily that row/columns operations allow us to reduce an element of
$SL_2(S)$
to a diagonal matrix with invertible entries and determinant
$1$
, then to the identity matrix by a standard procedure, meaning that
$SL_2(S)$
is generated by transvections.

Lemma 3.
Let
$R_1\to R_2$
be a surjective ring homorphism. If
$SL_2(R_2)$
is generated by transvections, then the induced group morphism
$SL_2(R_1)\to SL_2(R_2)$
is surjective.

Proof.
This is clear, since surjectivity allows us to lift a transvection of
$SL_2(R_2)$
to a transvection of
$SL_2(R_1)$
.

Putting things together, we have the following proposition.

Proposition.
Let
$R$
be a ring such that
$R/J$
is semi local for every non zero ideal
$J$
. Then
$SL_2(R)\to Sl_2(R/J)$
is surjective for every non zero ideal
$J$
.

We may now construct several families of rings which are not Bézout, and for which the surjectivity properties holds.

Examples.
The proposition applies in the following situations:

$R$
is semi-local.

Indeed,
$R/J$
is then semi-local for every
$J$
.

In particular, this applies if
$R$
is finite, or Artinian (an Artinian ring is a finite product of local rings, hence semi-local)

$R$
is a Dedekind domain.

Indeed, a nonzero ideal
$J$
is a finite product of powers of maximal ideals. The CRT then yields that
$R/J$
is a product of local rings, hence semi-local.

Remark.
Examples 1) and 2) already proves that you have no hope classifying rings for which the surjectivity property holds.

Indeed, loads of Dedekind domains are not semi-local or Bézout (eg the ring of integers of a number field), and you have plenty of semi-local rings which are not Dedekind or Bézout.

To have a family of explicit examples, recall that
the ring of integers of some number field
$K$
is a Noetherian domain, hence is Bézout if and only if it is a PID.

All in all, since a ring of integers is Dedekind and Noetherian with infinitely many maximal ideals, non principal rings of integers yield examples of doùains which are not Bézout neither semi local, and for which the map
$SL_2(R)\to SL_2(R/J)$
is surjective for every nonzero ideal
$J$
.

You get infinitely many examples of such rings by taking
$R=\mathbb{Z}[\sqrt{-d}],$
where is
$d>0$
, square free, and congruent to
$1$
mod
$4$
.

question:
Let
$A,B,C,D,E,F\in \mathbb{C}$
be the side vectors of a closed polygon, so

$$
A+B+C+D+E+F=0.
$$


In my surveying class, I was given the area formula

$$
\text{Area}
=
\left|
\Re\left(
\frac{i}{2}
\left(
A\overline{B}
+(A+B)\overline{C}
+(A+B+C)\overline{D}
\right)
\right)
\right|.
$$

This pattern can be generalized to more than six vertices, or fewer, as in the case for a 4-sided polygon with edge-vectors
$A,B,C,F$
:

$$
\text{Area}
= \frac{i}{2}\left(
F\overline{A}
+ (F+A)\overline{B}
\right)
$$


I'm already familiar with something called (in my surveying class) the "shoelace" formula for the area of a polygon in terms of the coordinates
$(a_1, b_1)$
,
$(a_2, b_2), \ldots$
of its vertices, namely

$$
\text{Area} = \frac12 \left( a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1 + \ldots + a_n b_1 - a_1 b_n \right).
$$


I would like to understand why the complex-number-based formula works, and whether there's some geometric intuition for it. In particular, I don't see how the conjugates mean anything geometrically.


Central to my confusion is that this formula uses what surveyors call "relative coordinates" (i.e., the edge vectors of the polygon), while the shoelace formula uses absolute coordinates, and I don't see how to get from one to the other.


In short: How do I go from the shoelace formula to the complex number formula, and is there a geometric intuition for the terms of the complex-number formula?

&= \mathrm{N}\ 40^\circ 3' 7.71''\ \mathrm{W}.
\end{aligned}
$$
