answer:
$$
\newcommand{\ii}{{\mathbf{i}}}
\newcommand{\conj}[1]{{\overline{#1}}}
$$

At the risk of boring you, I'm going to start from basics, including a bunch of stuff you probably already know, but written in slightly different notation. The first half of what I write will all be in what you'd call "absolute coordinates"; I'll get to the relative coordinates later.

Let's consider a sequence of points
$U_j = (a_j, b_j)$
,
$j = 1, \ldots, n$
in the plane, points that describe the boundary of a polygonal area by traversing that boundary in such a way that the area is always to the left as you walk along the path. I'm going to show a couple of things:

The shoelace formula, translated to complex numbers, lets you compute that area in a way similar to the one shown by your teacher, and
A
similar
formula (the one shown by your teacher) lets you compute that area using "relative" coordinates.

The shoelace formula, translated to complex numbers, lets you compute that area in a way similar to the one shown by your teacher, and

A
similar
formula (the one shown by your teacher) lets you compute that area using "relative" coordinates.

Side notes:

If your sequence of points describes a path that crosses itself, or for which the enclosed area is to your
right
as you traverse the path, then the comments that follow no longer hold. I'm assuming that in a surveying task, this "counterclockwise and non-crossing" assumption is absolutely standard, which is why you didn't mention it.
To keep the notation simple, I'm going to write
$U_{n+1}$
sometimes to denote
$U_1$
(and similarly for
$a_{n+1}$
and
$b_{n+1}$
). That lets me write things like
$a_j b_{j+1}$
without worrying about whether
$j$
is less than
$n$
or equal to
$n$
. I'll do this for other symbols as well (i.e., assume "wrap around" indexing).

If your sequence of points describes a path that crosses itself, or for which the enclosed area is to your
right
as you traverse the path, then the comments that follow no longer hold. I'm assuming that in a surveying task, this "counterclockwise and non-crossing" assumption is absolutely standard, which is why you didn't mention it.

To keep the notation simple, I'm going to write
$U_{n+1}$
sometimes to denote
$U_1$
(and similarly for
$a_{n+1}$
and
$b_{n+1}$
). That lets me write things like
$a_j b_{j+1}$
without worrying about whether
$j$
is less than
$n$
or equal to
$n$
. I'll do this for other symbols as well (i.e., assume "wrap around" indexing).

You already know the "shoelace" formula, i.e., that the area of the polygon is
$$
A = \frac{1}{2}\left( a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1 + \ldots + a_n b_{n+1} - a_{n+1}b_n \right)
$$

which makes sense because the
$a_j$
and
$b_j$
numbers are "absolute coordinates".

Before delving into complex numbers, I want to limit myself to a particularly simple case where
$n = 3$
(i.e. we're talking about a triangle) and that the origin (orange dot) lies within the triangle.

We'll get rid of these "simplifying assumptions" in a little while. The
$n = 3$
condition won't come up for a while, so I'm just going to keep using indexes like
$a_j$
and
$b_j$
for the most part.

Now I'm going to write
$Z_j$
for the complex number
$b_j + \ii a_j$
, and observe that the number
$a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1$
appears as part of
$Z_1 \conj{Z_2}$
. Let's just expand out terms:
\begin{align}
Z_1 \conj{Z_2}
&= (b_1 + \ii a_1) (b_2 - \ii a_2) \\
&= (b_1 b_2 + \ii a_1 b_2 - b_1 \ii a_2 - \ii a_1 \ii a_2) \\
&= (b_1 b_2 + \ii (a_1 b_2 - b_1 a_2) - \ii^2 a_1 a_2) \\
&= (b_1 b_2 + \ii (a_1 b_2 - b_1 a_2) - (-1) a_1 a_2) \\
&= (b_1 b_2 + \ii (a_1 b_2 - b_1 a_2) + a_1 a_2) \\
&= (b_1 b_2 + a_1 a_2 + \ii (a_1 b_2 - b_1 a_2).
\end{align}
If we multiply both sides by
$\ii$
, we get

\begin{align}
\ii Z_1 \conj{Z_2}
&= \ii (b_1 b_2 + a_1 a_2 + \ii^2 (a_1 b_2 - b_1 a_2) \\
&= -(a_1 b_2 - b_1 a_2) + \ii (b_1 b_2 + a_1 a_2
\end{align}

Finally, if we compute the real part of both sides, we get
$$
\Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{Z_2}) = -(a_1 b_2 - b_1 a_2).
$$
and so
$$
|\Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{Z_2})| = (a_1 b_2 - b_1 a_2).
$$
where here I'm using the simplifying assumption: in that particular situation, it takes a little geometry and algebra to show that
$a_1 b_2 - b_1 a_2$
is in fact nonnegative, so that we need to remove the "-" sign to compute the absolute value. (Side note: for complex numbers, mathematicians use the word "modulus" instead of "absolute value".)

Another side note: if only your teacher had used
$Z_i = a_j + \ii b_j$
, there would have been no negative sign to get rid of!

Is there any geometric intuition to be had here?
Multiplication of complex numbers has various geometric interpretations, but in this case, we're doing the multiplication and then
ignoring
one of the resulting terms (the imaginary part). There may be some geometric intuition to be had here, but I think that in this case, the simplest understanding is that this is a cheap way to do some algebra: if you have a way to do complex products (like "built into your calculator"), you can easily extract a bit that happens to do the algebra you need for the shoelace formula. I know that's not very satisfying, and I'm sorry.
End of discussion of intuition.

If we now apply this same analysis to the points
$U_2$
and
$U_3$
, or
$U_3$
and
$U_4$
(which, because of our simplifying assumption that we're working with a triangle, really means
$U_1$
), we see that
$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \left(
|\Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{Z_2}) +
\Re(\ii Z_2 \conj{Z_3}) +
\Re(\ii Z_3 \conj{Z_{1}})|
\right).
$$
which is more or less the shoelace formula using complex numbers, at least for a triangle that contains the origin.

Now let's get rid of the simplifying assumption that the origin is within the triangle: suppose that we add
$p$
to each
$x$
-coordinate, as in this picture:

Then we'll have moved our whole triangle to the right by the amount
$p$
, where
$p$
is an arbitrary number. By picking
$p$
large enough, we can move the triangle so far that it no longer surrounds the origin, right? (Imagine the sides of the triangle are 100 yards long, more or less, and we move them all 6 miles to the right!). What happens to our formula? We previously computed
$$
\begin{align}
A_1 &= \frac{1}{2} \left(
|\Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{Z_2}) +
\Re(\ii Z_2 \conj{Z_3}) +
\Re(\ii Z_3 \conj{Z_{1}})|
\right)\\
&= \frac{1}{2}\left(
(a_1 b_2 - a_2 b_1) +
(a_2 b_3 - a_3 b_2) +
(a_3 b_1 - a_1 b_3)
\right)
\end{align}
$$
but if we apply this to the shifted triangle, we get
$$
\begin{align}
A_2
&= \frac{1}{2}\left(
((a_1+p) b_2 - (a_2+p) b_1) +
((a_2+p) b_3 - (a_3+p) b_2) +
((a_3+p) b_1 - (a_1+p) b_3)
\right)\\
&= \frac{1}{2}\left(
(a_1 b_2 + pb_2 - (a_2 b_1 + pb_1)) +
(a_2 b_3 + p b_3- (a_3b_2 + pb_2)) +
(a_3 b_1 + p b_1 - (a_1b_3 +p b_3))
\right)\\
&= \frac{1}{2}\left(
(a_1 b_2  - a_2 b_1) + (pb_2 - p b_1) +
(a_2 b_3  - a_3 b_2) + (pb_3 - p b_2) +
(a_3 b_1  - a_1 b_3) + (pb_1 - p b_3)\right) \\
&= A_1
\end{align}
$$
where the last simplification occurs because each
$pb_j$
appears once with a plus-sign and once with a minus-sign. So the same formula, applied to the shifted triangle, yields the same result, even though the shifted triangle no longer contains the origin. The "contains the origin" assumption wasn't really needed!

Whew! So the complex version of the shoelace formula now applies for
any
counterclockwise triangle, whether it surrounds the origin or not.

Now let's generalize by adding a fourth point:

We know the left area is
$$
A_L = \frac{1}{2} \left(
|\Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{Z_2}) +
\Re(\ii Z_2 \conj{Z_3}) +
\Re(\ii Z_3 \conj{Z_{1}})|
\right).
$$
The right area is similar, but we have to traverse the triangle in counterclockwise order so as to keep our "contained area" to our left, i.e., we have to go from
$Z_3$
to
$Z_2$
to
$Z_4$
and back to
$Z_3$
, and we get
$$
A_R = \frac{1}{2} \left(
|\Re(\ii Z_3 \conj{Z_2}) +
\Re(\ii Z_2 \conj{Z_4}) +
\Re(\ii Z_4 \conj{Z_{3}})|
\right).
$$

You can see that
$\Re(\ii Z_3 \conj{Z_2})$
appears in
$A_R$
and
$\Re(\ii Z_2 \conj{Z_3})$
appears in
$A_L$
, and if you write these out in terms of the
$a$
s and
$b$
s, you'll find that they cancel, so that
$A_L + A_R$
now turns out to be
\begin{align}
A_L + A_R
&= \frac{1}{2} \left(
|\Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{Z_2}) +
\Re(\ii Z_2 \conj{Z_3}) +
\Re(\ii Z_3 \conj{Z_4}) +
\Re(\ii Z_4 \conj{Z_{1}})|
\right).
\end{align}
A similar approach lets us add more and more triangles.

This removes the other simplifying assumption, so we now have a general formula for the area of an
$n$
-vertex polygon, expressed using complex numbers:

$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \left(
|\Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{Z_2}) +
\Re(\ii Z_2 \conj{Z_3}) +
\ldots +
\Re(\ii Z_n \conj{Z_{n+1}})|
\right).
$$
where
$Z_{n+1}$
is just another name for
$Z_1$
.

That is the complex-number version of the shoelace formula.

On to relative coordinates

I've now finished up my lead-up remarks about the shoelace formula, and given the shoelace formula in a way that works for absolute coordinates represented by complex numbers. Aside from the use of complex numbers, there should really be nothing new or surprising above.

But your real question was, "What's going on with the relative coordinates? How can this still work?", so let's get to that.

I want to return to the triangle case, because everything beyond that is just done by 'gluing up triangles' as in the last step of the introductory material above.

So we start with a triangle with vertices in absolute coordinates
$U_1, U_2, U_3$
, and "complex" coordinates
$Z_1, Z_2, Z_3$
.

The "relative" coordinates for this triangle will be
$$
V_1  = U_2 - U_1,
$$
which represents the displacement needed to get from
$U_1$
to
$U_2$
, and
$$
V_2  = U_3 - U_2,\\
V_3  = U_1 - U_3,\\
$$
which represent similar displacements for the other two edges. Assuming that
$U_1$
has some coordinates
$(a_1, b_1)$
, we have

\begin{align}
U_2 &= U_1 + V_1 \\
U_3 &= U_2 + V_2 \\
U_1 &= U_3 + V_3
\end{align}
which we can expand to write
\begin{align}
U_2 &= U_1 + V_1 \\
U_3 &= U_1 + V_1 + V_2 \\
U_1 &= U_1 + V_1 + V_2 + V_3.
\end{align}

That last formula tells us that
$V_1 + V_2 + V_3 = 0$
, which is good, because the sum of the relative coordinates around a closed loop should be zero!

Now just as we turned each
$U_j$
into a corresponding complex number
$Z_j$
, let's do the same thing for the
$V_j$
coordinates, turning each one into a complex number
$W_j$
. When we do this we find that just as
$U_2 = U_1 + V_1 $
, we also have
$Z_2 = Z_1 + W_1$
,
$Z_3 = Z_2 + W_2 = Z_1 + W_1 + W_2$
, and so on.

Let's look at the area formula in the triangle case:
$$
A = \frac{1}{2} \left(
|\Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{Z_2}) +
\Re(\ii Z_2 \conj{Z_3}) +
\Re(\ii Z_3 \conj{Z_{1}})|
\right)
$$
and convert that into a statement about the
$W$
s, i.e., convert from a statement about absolute coordinates to a statement about relative coordinates. Replacing each
$Z_j$
by a sum starting from
$Z_1$
, i.e., by
\begin{align}
Z_2 &= Z_1 + W_1 \\
Z_3 &= Z_1 + W_1 + W_2
\end{align}
(and similar things when we work with something more complex than triangles), we get
\begin{align}
A
&= \frac{1}{2} \left(
|\Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{Z_2}) +
\Re(\ii Z_2 \conj{Z_3}) +
\Re(\ii Z_3 \conj{Z_{1}})|
\right)\\
&= \frac{1}{2} \left(
|\Re(\ii Z_1 (\conj{Z_1 + W_1})) +\\
\Re(\ii (Z_1 + W_1) (\conj{Z_1 + W_1 + W_2})) +\\
\Re(\ii (Z_1 + W_1 + W_2 + W_3) (\conj{Z_{1} + W_1 + W_2 + W_3}))|
\right)
\end{align}

Now I need to use a couple of basic facts about complex numbers. The first is that
$\conj{(s + t)} = \conj{s} + \conj{t}$
. The second is that
$s \conj{s}$
is always a
real
number -- no imaginary part. That means that
$\ii s \conj{s}$
is pure imaginary, so its real part is zero. And the third is that
$\Re{(s + t)} = \Re{(s)} + \Re{(t)}.$

I'm going to simplify the insides of that first term
$\Re(\ii Z_1 (\conj{Z_1 + W_1}))$
:
\begin{align}
\Re(\ii Z_1 (\conj{Z_1 + W_1}))
&=  \Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{Z_1} + \ii Z_1 \conj{W_1}))\\
&=  \Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{Z_1}) + \Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{W_1}))\\
&=  0 + \Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{W_1}))\\
\end{align}
where the zero appears because
$Z_1 \conj{Z_1}$
is real, so
$\ii Z_1 \conj{Z_1}$
is pure imaginary, so its real part is zero.

Similarly, the insides of the second term become
\begin{align}
\Re(\ii (Z_1 + W_1) (\conj{Z_1 + W_1 + W_2}))
&=  \Re(\ii (Z_1 + W_1) \conj{(Z_1 + W_1)} + \ii (Z_1 + W_1) \conj{W_2}))\\
&=  0 + \Re(\ii (Z_1+W_1) \conj{W_2}))
\end{align}
and something similar happens to the third term, so we end up with
\begin{align}
2A
&= \left|
\Re(\ii Z_1 (\conj{W_1})) +\\
\Re(\ii (Z_1 + W_1) \conj{W_2}) +\\
\Re(\ii (Z_1 + W_1 + W_2) \conj{W_3})
\right|\\
&= \left|
\Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{W_1}) +\\
\Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{W_2}) + \Re(\ii W_1 \conj{W_2})\\
\Re(\ii Z_1 \conj{W_3} + \Re(\ii (W_1 + W_2) \conj{W_3}
)
\right| \\
&= \left|
\Re(\ii Z_1 (\conj{W_1 + W_2 + W_3})) +\\
\Re(\ii W_1 \conj{W_2})\\
\Re(
\ii (W_1 + W_2) \conj{W_3}
)
\right| \\
&= \left|
\Re(\ii W_1 \conj{W_2})\\
\Re(
\ii (W_1 + W_2) \conj{W_3}
)
\right| \\
\end{align}

where the last simplification comes from the fact that
$W_1 + W_2 + W_3 = 0$
.

Notice that in the final formula here, the absolute coordinate
$Z_1$
has entirely disappeared, and the entire formula is in terms of relative coordinates.

So the secret sauce, in deriving your teacher's formula, comes in two pieces:

$q\conj{q}$
is a real number, so
$\Re(\ii q \conj{q})$
is always zero, and
The sum of the relative coordinates along a path is always zero, which makes the last absolute coordinate in the formula end up being multiplied by zero, which makes it disappear.

The generalization from triangles to more general polygons involves nothing interesting, just formulas with more terms that cancel in the same ways as the ones for the triangle, so I'm going to stop here.

answer:
First, we note, that your formula is
not
correct as stated. Namely, given the vertices
$A,B,C,D$
as complex numbers for the sides, is not the case that the polygon has area

$$\text{Area}(A,B,C,D)=\left|\text{Re}\left(\left(\frac{i}{2}\right)(A\bar{B}+(A+B)\bar{C}+(A+B+C)\bar{D})\right)\right|$$

A counterexample using the right, left, top, and bottom of a
$1$
x
$1$
square,

$$\text{Area}(i,-i,-1,1) = 0\ \ \ \text{(should be 1)}$$

What are you missing from the formula? There
must
be a central point chosen in your polygon (the line from vertex to the center must be within the polygon) from which all other vertices are listed in order as you rotate about that central point. The vertices must be shifted so that the central point is at the origin.
Then
your vertices can be considered as vectors or as complex numbers and the formula for the area works. Namely,

$$\text{Area}(v_0,\ldots,v_n)=\frac{1}{2} \sum_{k=0}^{n} ||v_i \times v_{i+1} ||\ \ \ \text{(note that $v_{n+1}=v_0$)}$$

which is equivalent to the form you gave with complex numbers. This is known as the shoelace formula or Gauss' area formula. This formula is simply the generalization of how you find the "area" spanned between two vectors; the
$1/2$
cuts that parallelogram in half, forming 1 edge of the polygon as you rotate about the origin.

The introduction of the complex numbers is nothing more than a fancy way of working with the vectors of the vertices. The formula is not different in it's application or usage. The main difference, is that you are replacing
$v-u$
with a complex number for each edge as you rotate about the polygon, which creates the formula you have.

Let

be the side vectors of a closed polygon, so

$$
A+B+C+D+E+F=0.
$$


In my surveying class, I was given the area formula

$$
\text{Area}
=
\left|
\Re\left(
\frac{i}{2}
\left(
A\overline{B}
+(A+B)\overline{C}
+(A+B+C)\overline{D}
\right)

This pattern can be generalized to more than six vertices, or fewer, as in the case for a 4-sided polygon with edge-vectors
