question: In a paper, I would like to include a statement to the effect that no general technique is known for enumerating elements of finitely generated submonoids of a product of free monoids, and that this is a highly nontrivial problem that has to be tackled on a case-by-case basis. As far as I can tell from my research, this certainly seems to be the case; however, is there any specific reference I can cite to back up this statement?
answer:
Not an answer, as your question is a bit unclear, but too long for a comment.

I suppose you are referring to the
Post correspondence problem
. This undecidable decision problem asks whether, given a finite subset
$F$
of the product
$A^* \times A^*$
, say
$$
F = \bigl\{(u_1, v_1), \ldots, (u_n, v_n)\bigr\}
$$
there exists a sequence of indices
$(i_k)_{1 \leqslant k \leqslant K}$
with
$K \geqslant 1$
and
$(u_{i_k}, v_{i_k}) \in F$
for all
$k$
such that
$$
u_{i_1} \dotsm u_{i_K} = v_{i_1} \dotsm v_{i_K}
$$
Let
$
D = \{(u, u) \mid u \in A^*\}
$
be the diagonal of
$A^* \times A^*$
.
When you want to enumerate the elements of the submonoid
$F^* \cap D$
of
$A^* \times A^*$
, you end up with this type of problem.

question:
Let
$M$
be a smooth manifold and
$TM:=\bigsqcup_{p\in M}T_pM$
its tangent bundle. Suppose
$(U, \varphi)$
is a chart of
$M$
, where the homeomorphism
$\varphi: U\to \varphi(U)\subset \mathbb{R}^n$
takes coordinates form
$\varphi(p):=\left(x_1(p), \cdots, x_n(p)\right)\in \mathbb{R}^n$
. Then, we have a basis of the tangent space
$T_pM$
, and that is
$\left(\frac{\partial}{\partial x_1}, \cdots, \frac{\partial}{\partial x_n}\right)\big|_p$
. Usually, a chart for the bundle is induced by

$$\overline{\varphi}: TU\ni v=\sum_1^n c_i \frac{\partial}{\partial x_i}\big|_p\mapsto \left(x_1(p), \cdots, x_n(p), c_1, \cdots, c_n\right)\in\mathbb{R}^{2n}.$$

Later compatibility of different charts of such kind is proved, and this proof justifies
$TM$
becoming a smooth manifold in its own right.




Question.
It seems the above map
$\overline{\varphi}$
is dependent on what basis I choose for the target space
$\mathbb{R}^{2n}$
. Why is this not an issue? Isn't everything in differential geometry supposed to be "basis-free"?

Thanks in advance.
answer:
If you choose a different basis for the target Euclidean space, you'll get a different chart, say
$\varphi'$
. That chart and
$\overline{\varphi}$
will, however, be part of the same atlas (i.e., roughly speaking, they'll differ by a diffeomorphism of Euclidean space), which shows that the choice doesn't really matter.

Working out the details of this is a great exercise.

BTW, your instincts in asking this question are excellent. The end result here is that the choice of atlas is basis-independent, and because all you need to make
$TM$
a manifold is an atlas, you've got what you need without basis-dependence.

question:
Main question

Here's the relevant extract from Rudin's PMA :

Now let
$T$
be 1-1 mapping of [the
$n$
-simplex]
$Q^n$
into
$\mathbb{R}^n$
, of class
$C^2$
, whose Jacobian is positive (at least in the interior of
$Q^n$
). Let
$E=T(Q^n)$
. By the inverse function theorem,
$E$
is the closure of an open subset of
$\mathbb{R}^n$
. We define the positively oriented boundary of the set
$E$
to be the
$(n-1)$
-chain
$$\partial T = T(\partial\sigma_0)$$
and we may denote this
$(n-1)$
chain by
$\partial E$
[
$\sigma_0$
is the identity mapping on
$Q^n$
].


An obvious question occurs here: If
$E=T_1(Q^n)=T_2(Q^n)$
, and if both
$T_1$
and
$T_2$
have positive Jacobians, is it true that
$\partial T_1 = \partial T_2$
? That is to say, does the equality
$$\int_{\partial T_1}\omega=\int_{\partial T_2}\omega$$
hold for every
$(n-1)$
-form
$\omega$
? The answer is yes, but we shall omit the proof.




How is this last statement proved?


Attempt


This is inspired by a deleted answer to the now unanswered question
here
. See also that link for definitions or Rudin's book (I will refer to equations and theorems in the 3rd edition).


I think it helps to state some facts first. I will use
$n=k$
and
$\sigma$
will be the identity map on
$Q^k$
.




The oriented affine
$k$
-simplex
$\sigma=[0,e_1,\ldots,e_k]$
is a
$k$
-surface in
$\mathbb{R}^k$
with parameter domain
$Q^k$
. It is the identity on
$Q^k$
.


The oriented affine
$(k-1)$
-simplexes
$\sigma_j=(-1)^j[0,\ldots,e_{j-1},e_{j+1},\ldots,e_k]$
for
$0\leq j\leq k$
are
$(k-1)$
-surfaces in
$\mathbb{R}^{k}$
with parameter domain
$Q^{k-1}$
. Note
$\sigma_j(u)\in Q^k$
for
$u\in Q^{k-1}$
.


$T_i:Q^k\to\mathbb{R}^k$
are 1-1
$C^2$
maps. Using the convention at the beginning of the section (see page 253), this means there are
$C^2$
maps
$S_i:W_i\to\mathbb{R}^k$
with open
$W_i\supset Q^k$
such that
$S_i(x)=T_i(x)$
for
$x\in Q^k$
.


We have
$\partial\sigma=\sum_{j=0}^{k} \sigma_j$
and this is an affine
$(k-1)$
-chain in
$\mathbb{R}^k$
. Also define
$T_i(\partial \sigma):=S_i(\partial \sigma)$
(this makes sense in view of 10.30 and also
$S_i\circ\sigma=T_i\circ\sigma$
).




Now, let
$\omega$
be a
$(k-1)$
-form in
$\mathbb{R}^k$
and let
$\omega_{S_i}$
be
$(k-1)$
-forms in
$W_i$
. Then
$$
\int_{\partial T_i}\omega=\int_{S_i(\partial \sigma)}\omega=\sum_{j=0}^k\int_{S_i\sigma_j}\omega=\sum_{j=0}^k\int_{\sigma_j}\omega_{S_i}=\int_{\partial\sigma}\omega_{S_i}.
$$

First equality is definition, second is (87) and (88), third is Theorem 10.25 and the last one is (82). We thus need to prove
$$\int_{\partial \sigma}\omega_{S_1}=\int_{\partial \sigma}\omega_{S_2}.$$
By (38), we need only consider the case
$\omega=a(y)\,dy_{i_1}\land\cdots\land dy_{i_{k-1}}$
for
$y\in\mathbb{R}^k$
, so that
$\omega_{S_1}=a(S_1(x))\,ds_{i_1}\land\cdots\land ds_{i_{k-1}}$
for
$x\in W_1$
(this is just (68)). We already know from above
$$\int_{\partial\sigma}\omega_{S_1}=\sum_{j=0}^k\int_{\sigma_j}\omega_{S_1}.$$
Let's look at a single term in this sum. By (35),
$$\int_{\sigma_j}\omega_{S_1}=\int_{Q^{k-1}} a(S_1(\sigma_j(u)))\frac{\partial(s_{i_1},\ldots,s_{i_{k-1}})}{\partial(u_1,\ldots,u_{k-1})}\,du.$$
The Jacobian
$J(u)$
here is the one determined by
$$(u_1,\ldots,u_{k-1})\mapsto(s_{i_1}(\sigma_j(u)),\ldots,s_{i_{k-1}}(\sigma_j(u)))$$
or simply
$\frac{\partial(t_{i_1},\ldots,t_{i_{k-1}})}{\partial(u_1,\ldots,u_{k-1})}$
, where
$t_{i_l}$
is a component of
$T_1$
.


This where my attempt ends, because I don't know how to continue, and where I'm heading. The
$C^2$
assumption of
$T_1$
implies that its derivative has a symmetric matrix representation, and the Jacobian is positive. I don't know how to use this though.


Closing remarks


Yes, a couple of related questions are out there apart from the one I first linked to above;




Unanswered
.


Answered
; like the OP in the question I first linked to, I don't understand the answer here in view of Rudin's exposition.


Answered
; the OP asks among other things about the significance of Rudin's statement "By the inverse function theorem,
$E$
is the closure of an open subset of
$\mathbb{R}^n$
." Despite also reading the comments, it's still not clear to me why he made that statement.

Appreciate any help on this!
answer:
I think Rudin's statement

By the inverse function theorem,
$E$
is the closure of an open subset of
$\mathbb{R}^n$
.

By the inverse function theorem,
$E$
is the closure of an open subset of
$\mathbb{R}^n$
.

tries to give intuition for his definition of "positively oriented boundary" making sense. Note that in 10.30, he has defined the boundary of a
$k$
-simplex
$\Phi=T\circ \sigma$
by
$$\partial \Phi=T(\partial\sigma),\quad(\sigma\text{ being an arbitrary oriented affine }k\text{-simplex}).$$
This is (89). The
$n$
-simplex we are considering in 10.31 is simply the 1-1
$C^2$
map
$T$
with (parameter) domain
$Q^n$
and whose Jacobian is positive in the interior of
$Q^n$
. Thus it makes sense to write
$$\partial T=T(\partial\sigma),\quad (\sigma\text{ being the identity on } Q^n).$$
As for the intuition,
$T$
is a (global)
$C^2$
diffeomorphism in the interior of
$Q^n$
. By e.g. Theorem 9.25,
$T$
restricted to the interior of
$Q^n$
is an open map; it can only map an interior point of
$Q^n$
to an interior point of
$E$
. In this sense, his definition makes sense.

As for the main question and my attempt, first read
this answer
and the associated question. It defines what the "pullback"
$\omega_T$
means if
$T$
is a
$C^1$
(or
$C^2$
) map from a compact set
$D\subset\mathbb{R}^k$
(here
$D$
is of course a
$k$
-cell or the standard
$k$
-simplex). In view of that answer, we can write
\begin{align*}
\int_{\partial T_i}\omega&=\int_{T_i(\partial \sigma)}\omega=\sum_{j=0}^k\int_{T_i\sigma_j}\omega \\
&=\sum_{j=0}^k\int_{\sigma_j}\omega_{T_i}=\int_{\partial\sigma}\omega_{T_i}.
\end{align*}
and we want to prove
$$\int_{\partial\sigma}\omega_{T_1}=\int_{\partial\sigma}\omega_{T_2}.$$
If we define¹
$\phi=T^{-1}_2\circ T_1$
, another (global)
$C^2$
diffeomorphism in the interior of
$Q^k$
(with positive Jacobian in the interior by the chain rule), then applying Rudin's definition of
$\partial Q^k$
, this is the affine
$(k-1)$
-chain
$$\partial\sigma=:\partial Q^k:=\partial\phi=\phi(\partial\sigma).$$
Also,
$T_2\circ\phi=T_1$
, so
$$\int_{\partial\sigma}\omega_{T_1}=\int_{\partial\sigma}(\omega_{T_2})_\phi=\int_{\phi(\partial\sigma)}\omega_{T_2}=\int_{\partial\sigma}\omega_{T_2}.$$
This completes the proof.

¹ We want
$T$
to be 1-1 in Rudin's definition of positively oriented boundary in order to define this function.

question:
I am currently learning about finite fields and have encountered several problems that I do not know where to begin.

Let
$\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$
be the finite extension field of
$\mathbb{F}_q$
of degree
$n$
, and let

$\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q}:\mathbb{F}_{q^n}\to\mathbb{F}_q$

denote the field trace map.

For a basis

$B=\{\beta_1,\beta_2,\ldots,\beta_n\}$

of
$\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$
over
$\mathbb{F}_q$
, it is called
self-dual
if

$$
\operatorname{Tr}_{\mathbb{F}_{q^n}/\mathbb{F}_q}
(\beta_i\beta_j)
=
\delta_{ij},
\qquad
1\le i,j\le n.
$$


Prove that
$\mathbb{F}_{q^n}$
admits a self-dual basis over
$\mathbb{F}_q$
if and only if either
$q$
is even or both
$q$
and
$n$
are odd.


For this particular problem, I am completely stuck. I do not see how one might construct a self-dual basis, nor can I understand the connection between its existence and the size of the field (or the values of
$q$
and
$n$
).

Could anyone offer some ideas or hints? I would greatly appreciate any help.
answer:
This result is proven in the following paper:

G. Seroussi and A. Lempel. Factorization of symmetric matrices and trace-orthogonal bases in finite fields. SIAM J. Comput. 9 (1980), no. 4, 758-767.

https://doi.org/10.1137/0209059

Reading this paper should answer your question.

Another (shorter) proof is in the following paper:

D. Jungnickel, A. J. Menezes, and S. A. Vanstone. On the number of self-dual bases of GF(qm) over GF(q). Proc. Amer. Math. Soc. 109 (1990), no. 1, 23-29.

https://doi.org/10.2307/2048357

question:
See
the same problem but for T-tetrominoes
.

Consider the infinite square grid whose squares are each colored black or white. What is the
minimum required density
of cells to be colored black such that there is no all-white rotation or translation of a V-pentomino?

It seems the answer is also 1/3. An upper bound is easy: coloring black every third diagonal (like the previous problem), or every third row.

I have proven 1/4 for the lower bound. Consider a 3x3 ring, which has 8 cells. At least two cells must be colored black to block all V's. Since this constraint applies identically at any position on the grid, then by a linearity of expectation-ish argument,
$\geq\frac{1}{4}$
holds globally as well.

I don't see an obvious way to extend either of the previous solutions (at the time of writing this) to this problem.
answer:
This construction comes from
Polyominoes on the Infinite Checkerboard (1980)
with density
$4/13$
. It's not hard to see this blocks all the V-Pentominoes. It was proven to be optimal in
Barring Rectangles from the Plane (1982)
by F. W. Barnes and J. B. Shearer (pg. 26)

question:
Let
$S^1$
denote the circle with its usual topology, and let
$Y$
be a compact metric space, such that there is a continuous, open surjection
$f:S^1\to Y$
. What can
$Y$
be?

Of course
$Y$
must be a connected space, and it is also locally connected: if
$y\in U\subset Y$
with
$U$
open, then
$f^{-1}(U)$
is an open subset of
$S^1$
that contains a point
$x$
with
$f(x)=y$
. Take a small open arc
$I$
around
$x$
with
$I\subset f^{-1}(U)$
, then
$f(I)$
is an open connected set in
$Y$
containing
$y$
and contained in
$U$
.

I am suspecting that
$Y$
must be either a point, a circle or a closed arc; clearly all three spaces occur: any constant map
$S^1\to Y$
, any map
$z\mapsto z^n$
(
$n\in\mathbb{Z}$
), and a projection map
$x+iy\mapsto x$
are such examples (
bonus question
: is there any known way to classify all continuous open surjections from
$S^1$
onto the circle and the closed interval?)

I couldn't prove this however, but I could also not come up with any other example; it is clear for instance that a wedge sum of two circles cannot appear as
$Y$
. Indeed, let
$p\in S^1\vee S^1$
be the wedge point and let
$x\in S^1$
be such that
$f(x)=p$
. Take a small open arc
$I$
around
$x$
; its image
$f(I)$
is an open set containing
$p$
(without loss of generality, its complement has at least 2 points...), so
$f(I)\setminus\{p\}$
has four connected components. The punctured arc
$I\setminus\{x\}$
is open, and it has precisely two connected components, say
$I\setminus\{x\}=A\sqcup B$
with
$A,B$
open, connected, and so
$f(I\setminus\{x\}) = f(A)\sqcup f(B)$
with
$f(A),f(B)$
open and connected. Now there are two cases: if
$p\not\in f(I\setminus\{x\})$
, then
$f(I\setminus\{x\}) = f(I)\setminus\{p\}$
, a contradiction, since the left set has two connected components while the right set has four. If
$p\in f(I\setminus\{x\})$
, then
$f(I\setminus\{x\})=f(I)$
, again a contradiction, since the left set has two connected components while the right set is connected.

If there is any relevant reference I would appreciate that as well!
answer:
Here's a positive answer if you don't mind working through exercises.

Exercise 9.46e of
Continuum theory
by Nadler says that if
$f:S^1\to Y$
is an open continuous surjective map, then
$Y$
is either a point, closed interval, or a circle. As a hint, Nadler says to recall proposition 9.5, if
$X$
is a non-degenerate continuum then
$\text{ord}(x, X)\leq 2$
for all
$x\in X$
iff
$X$
is a closed interval or a circle. For definition of
$\text{ord}(x, X)$
see definition 9.3.

In the derivation of that proposition, Nadler uses exercise 8.40b, which says that if
$X$
is a non-degenerate continuum which doesn't contain a simple triod (a
$T$
or
$Y$
shape), then
$X$
is either a closed interval or a circle.

Let
$S^1$
denote the circle with its usual topology, and let
$Y$
be a compact metric space, such that there is a continuous, open surjection

with
$U$

is an open subset of

that contains a point
$x$
with

. Take a small open arc
$I$
around
$x$
with
$I\subset f^{-1}(U)$
, then
$f(I)$
is an open connected set in
$Y$
containing
$y$
and contained in
$U$
.

$Y$
must be either a point, a circle or a closed arc; clearly all three spaces occur: any constant map
$S^1\to Y$
, any map
$z\mapsto z^n$
(

onto the circle and the closed interval?)

I couldn't prove this however, but I could also not come up with any other example; it is clear for instance that a wedge sum of two circles cannot appear as
$Y$
. Indeed, let
$p\in S^1\vee S^1$
be the wedge point and let
$x\in S^1$
be such that
$f(x)=p$
. Take a small open arc
$I$
around
$x$

with
$f(A),f(B)$
open and connected. Now there are two cases: if

, then

If there is any relevant reference I would appreciate that as well!
answer:
I can think of a non-Hausdorff counterexample. Consider the 4 point space
$X=\{E,N,W,S\}$
with topology {
$\{E,N,W\}, \{E,S,W\}, \{E,W\}, \{E\}, \{W\}, \{E,N,S,W\}, \emptyset$
}. Define a map
$f\colon S^1\to X$
by
$(x,y)\mapsto E$
if
$x>0$
,
$(x,y)\mapsto W$
if
$x<0$
,
$(0,1)\mapsto N$
and
$(0,-1)\mapsto S$
. This can easily be seen to be continuous since there are only finitely many open sets to compute the preimage of. To check that it is open, note that any open set containing the points
$(0,1)$
or
$(0,-1)$
would also meet the preimages of
$\{E,W\}$
. Hence if the image of a nonempty open set contains
$N$
or
$S$
is must also contain
$E,W$
, and hence be open in the image.

Now that I reread your question, you did specify metric space, so this doesn't quite work, but this might still be interesting for consideration. It's a bit too long for a comment.

question:
Is the below ring a UFD? Is it a PID?
$$\frac{\mathbb{Z}[x,y,z]}{(3x-1,z^3x-x^2y+x^3y)}$$




Since
$3x-1$
becomes
$0$
in the quotient, hence
$3$
becomes a unit, and I think the above ring is isomorphic to
$$\frac{\mathbb{Z}[1/3][y,z]}{(9z^3-2y)}.$$


I am not sure how to show whether this ring is a UFD or not. Since the quotienting gives
$9z^3=2y,$
it seems like we are getting two different factorizations, but
$y$
or
$z$
may not be irreducible in the quotient. Even if this particular instance does not violate unique factorisation, how to show, in general that it is a UFD, if at all it is? We would first have to check if it is a domain, which I think is true because
$9z^3-2y$
, being linear in
$y$
, is irreducible.


This (
About the localization of a UFD
), and the fact that a polynomial ring over a UFD is a UFD (Gauss's Lemma) tell us that
$\mathbb{Z}[1/3][y,z]$
is a UFD, maybe this could help.

Any help to finish the problem would be appreciated.
answer:
First, we classify this commutative ring:

$$\begin{align*}
& \phantom{{}\cong{}} \mathbb{Z}[x,y,z]/ \langle 3x-1,z^3 x-x^2 y+x^3 y \rangle \\
& \cong \mathbb{Z}[1/3][y,z]/ \bigl\langle \tfrac{1}{3} z^3  - \tfrac{1}{3^2} y+ \tfrac{1}{3^3} y \bigr\rangle \\
& = \mathbb{Z}[1/3][y,z]/ \langle 9 z^3 - 3 y+ y \rangle \\
& = \mathbb{Z}[1/3][y,z]/ \bigl\langle z^3 - \tfrac{2}{9} y \bigr\rangle  \qquad (u := y/9) \\
& \cong \mathbb{Z}[1/3][u,z]/ \langle z^3 - 2u \rangle \\
\end{align*}$$
We can write this as
$$A' = A[z]/\langle z^3 - 2u \rangle, \text{ where } A := \mathbb{Z}[1/3][u].$$
Notice that
$A$
is an UFD with
$A^{\times} = \{\pm 3^k : k \in \mathbb{Z} \}$
, and both
$2$
and
$u$
are prime in
$A$
. Every element of
$A'$
has a unique representation as
$[a+bz+cz^2]$
with
$a,b,c \in A$
, which I will just write as
$a + bz + cz^2$
, and we calculate with the rule
$z^3=2u$
. The ring
$A'$
is an integral domain (Eisenstein criterion), and the element
$2 \in A'$
is not a unit and not prime since
$$A' / \langle 2 \rangle \cong \mathbb{F}_2[u][z]/\langle z^3 \rangle$$
is a ring with non-zero nilpotents. I claim that
$2 \in A'$
is irreducible, which shows that
$A'$
is not an UFD. This is the hard part here.

Since
$A'$
is free of rank
$3$
over
$A$
(with basis
$1,z,z^2$
), we have a norm map
$$N : A' \to A, \quad x \mapsto \det(\lambda_x : A' \to A'),$$
where
$\lambda_x$
is the multiplication with
$x \in A'$
. It is multiplicative. If
$N(x)$
is a unit,
$\lambda_x$
is bijective, so that
$x$
is a unit. Let us calculate it in general:

$$N(a + bz + cz^2) = \det \begin{pmatrix} a & 2uc & 2ub \\ b & a & 2uc \\ c & b & a \end{pmatrix} = a^3 - 6 a b c u + 2 b^3 u + 4 c^3 u^2$$

Now, assume that
$2 \in A'$
is reducible, say
$2 = xy$
with non-units
$x,y \in A'$
. Then
$$2^3=N(2) = N(x) N(y)$$
with non-units
$N(x),N(y) \in A$
. Since
$2 \in A$
is prime, we therefore get an element
$x \in A'$
such that
$N(x)$
is
$2$
up to a unit, say
$N(x) = \pm 3^k \cdot 2$
. Thus, we have
$a,b,c \in A$
with
$$a^3 - 6 a b c u + 2 b^3 u + 4 c^3 u^2 = \pm 3^k \cdot 2.$$
Modulo
$u$
, i.e. in
$\mathbb{Z}[1/3]$
, this becomes
$$d^3 = \pm 3^k \cdot 2$$
for some
$d \in \mathbb{Z}[1/3]$
. This is of course not possible, in any UFD a prime cannot be associated to a cube.

question:
Is the below ring a UFD? Is it a PID?
$$\frac{\mathbb{Z}[x,y,z]}{(3x-1,z^3x-x^2y+x^3y)}$$




Since
$3x-1$
becomes
$0$
in the quotient, hence
$3$
becomes a unit, and I think the above ring is isomorphic to
$$\frac{\mathbb{Z}[1/3][y,z]}{(9z^3-2y)}.$$


I am not sure how to show whether this ring is a UFD or not. Since the quotienting gives
$9z^3=2y,$
it seems like we are getting two different factorizations, but
$y$
or
$z$
may not be irreducible in the quotient. Even if this particular instance does not violate unique factorisation, how to show, in general that it is a UFD, if at all it is? We would first have to check if it is a domain, which I think is true because
$9z^3-2y$
, being linear in
$y$
, is irreducible.


This (
About the localization of a UFD
), and the fact that a polynomial ring over a UFD is a UFD (Gauss's Lemma) tell us that
$\mathbb{Z}[1/3][y,z]$
is a UFD, maybe this could help.

Any help to finish the problem would be appreciated.
answer:
$R = \mathbb Z[x, y, z]/(3x - 1,\ z^3x - x^2y + x^3y)$
.

From
$3x-1=0$
we get
$x = 1/3$
, so
$R \cong \mathbb Z[1/3][y,z]/(9z^3 - 2y)$
.

Let
$A = \mathbb Z[1/3]$
. Then
$R \cong A[y,z]/(2y - 9z^3)$
.
Since
$9z^3 - 2y$
is linear in
$y$
and primitive in
$A[z][y]$
,
$R$
is a domain.

In
$R$
,
$2y = 9z^3$
with
$9$
a unit, so
$2 \mid z^3$
.

$2$
is irreducible in
$R$
:
$R/(2) \cong \mathbb F_2[y,z]/(z^3) \neq 0$
,
so
$2$
is not a unit; any factorization
$2 = uv$
in
$R$
lifts to
$A[y,z]$
where
$2$
is irreducible, so
$u$
or
$v$
is a unit.

But
$2$
is not prime in
$R$
:
$2 \mid z^3$
but
$2 \nmid z$
because
$z = 2w$
in
$R$
would imply
$z = 0$
in
$R/(2) \cong \mathbb F_2[y,z]/(z^3)$
,
where
$z \neq 0$
.

Hence
$R$
has an irreducible non‑prime element, so
$R$
is not a UFD.
A PID is always a UFD, so
$R$
is not a PID either.

$\boxed{\text{not a UFD, not a PID}}$

question:
I recently learnt the justification for the change of variables technique used widely (and recklessly!) in integral calculus. The exact statement of the theorem is as follows:

Let
$I, J$
be closed and bounded intervals. Let
$u:J\rightarrow \mathbb{R}$
. Let
$u(J)\subset I$
and
$f:I\rightarrow \mathbb{R}$
.
$f, u$
are continuous. Then
$f\circ u$
is continuous and we have

$$\int_a^bf(u(x))u'(x)\ dx = \int_{u(a)}^{u(b)} f(y)\ dy$$




I thought that I had understood it pretty well. It matched my past experience really well, so I thought I had got it. But then I tried solving the following problem:




Let
$f(x) = \int_{\sin x}^{\cos x} \sqrt{1-t^2}\ dt$
, for
$x\in [0, \pi/2]$
. Show that
$f(x) = \pi/4 - x$
.




Here's what I did using the theorem above (using the exact same symbols as used in the statement).

$$J = [0, \pi];\ I = [0, 1]$$


$$u:[0, \pi]\rightarrow [0,1]; \ u(t) = \sin(t)$$


$$f:[0,1]\rightarrow \mathbb{R};\ f(x) = \sqrt{1-x^2}$$


$$u(a) = \sin x;\ u(b) = \cos x$$

Now here's where I think I am missing something.
$u$
is not one-to-one here. I write
$a = x$
and I can write
$\cos x = \sin(x + \pi/2)$
, so (I think) I can write
$b = x + \pi/2$
. Since
$x\in [0, \pi/2]$
,
$a$
and
$b$
are in domain of
$u$
, i.e.
$[0, \pi]$
. Now, by using the above theorem, I get the following integral:
$$\int_{\sin x}^{\cos x} \sqrt{1-t^2}\ dt = \int_x^{x+\pi/2} \cos^2t\ dt$$
Solving this does not yield the desired result.


But when I write
$\cos x = \sin(x-\pi/2)$
, I get the desired result by simple integration. What is going on? As far as I have checked, the hypothesis of the theorem does not require
$u$
to be one-to-one, and I don't see anything else be violated.

I am not interested in getting to the answer, rather finding where the application of the aforemention theorem is failing here.
answer:
The problem is that
$\sqrt{1 - \sin^2(t)} = \cos(t)$
only holds for values of
$t$
such that
$\cos{t} \geq 0$
.
For other values, the relation is
$\sqrt{1 - \sin^2(t)} = -\cos(t)$
.
When you use
$\cos(x) =\sin (\frac{\pi}{2}-x)$
, (with
$x \in [0, \pi/2]$
) you ensure the bounds for the final integral are in the range
$[-\pi/2, \pi/2]$
, in which cosine is positive.

question:
While working on a textbook, I stumbled upon the following task : “Prove that the category of smooth manifolds admits countable coproducts”.

The first thing that came to mind was : something’s wrong, because if the dimensions of the manifolds are not the same, then their disjoint union can’t be a smooth manifold as when you move along the disjoint union (with its canonical topology), the dimension changes (which is not allowed in the textbook’s definition of manifolds).

So I guessed it was only a typo and that the dimension of the manifolds had to be the same for it to work.

But then I wondered… was it really a typo ?

Being given countably many manifolds, maybe there exists some bizarre manifold, that as a set would not be the disjoint union of these manifolds, but that would nonetheless be a coproduct in the category of smooth manifolds. I wasn’t able to find an easy argument to prevent this from happening and my proofs for that are rather unnatural and/or lengthy.

How would you proceed ?
answer:
As noted in the comments, if we allow different components to have different dimensions in the definition of a manifold, then this result is true and not hard. Let us, however, as you desire, only consider manifolds where all components have the same dimension and smooth maps between them. I'll denote this category
$\mathbf{Man}$
. Then, the only coproducts that exist in
$\mathbf{Man}$
are the "obvious" ones:

Proposition:
The coproduct of a collection of smooth manifolds exists in
$\mathbf{Man}$
if and only if there are only countably many non-empty smooth manifolds in this collection and these all have the same dimension.

Proof:
Let
$M_i,\,i\in I$
be a collection of smooth manifolds. Note that we may remove all copies of the empty manifold from this collection and the old collection admits a coproduct if and only if this new one does (because
$\emptyset$
is initial), so we may assume these are all non-empty and show that the collection admits a coproduct if and only if they all have the same dimension. Sufficiency is easy to prove, so we prove necessity. To this end, assume that there is a smooth manifold
$M$
and smooth maps
$M_i\rightarrow M,\,i\in I$
that exhibit
$M$
as a coproduct of the
$M_i,\,i\in I$
,

For any
$i\in I$
, there is a map
$M\rightarrow\{0,1\}$
given by mapping
$M_i$
to
$0$
and
$M_j$
to
$1$
for
$j\neq i$
. This implies the canonical maps
$M_i\rightarrow M,\,i\in I$
take values in pairwise distinct connected components of
$M$
. Since every
$M_i$
is non-empty and smooth manifolds have only countably many components, we see that
$I$
is at most countable. Now, let
$M_i'$
be the union of all the components of
$M$
that meet the image of
$M_i$
, so that
$M_i'$
is a smooth manifold and
$M_i\rightarrow M$
factors through a smooth map
$M_i\rightarrow M_i'$
. Let also
$M'$
denote the union of all components of
$M$
that do not meet any of these images. Then, using the universal property of
$M$
and the Yoneda lemma(*), we see that each of the maps
$M_i\rightarrow M_i'$
for
$i\in I$
as well as
$\emptyset\rightarrow M'$
are diffeomorphisms. Thus, all
$M_i$
have the same dimension (the dimension of
$M$
) and
$M$
is their disjoint union, as one would expect.

(*) To elaborate, let
$h_N=\mathrm{Hom}_{\mathbf{Man}}(N,-)$
. Then, we have natural transformations
$$h_M\Rightarrow h_{M'}\times\prod_{i\in I}h_{M_i'}\Rightarrow\prod_{i\in I}h_{M_i}.$$
The first is a natural isomorphism since
$M$
is the disjoint union of
$M'$
and the
$M_i',\,i\in I$
, the composite is a natural isomorphism by assumption that
$M$
is a coproduct of the
$M_i,\,i\in I$
, hence the second natural transformation is a natural isomorphism. The non-emptiness of the
$M_i,\,i\in I$
implies that each component
$h_{M'}\Rightarrow h_{\emptyset}=\{\ast\}$
resp.
$h_{M_i'}\Rightarrow h_{M_i},\,i\in I$
is a retract of this natural isomorphism (use constant maps to construct a section of the component projection), hence a natural isomorphism itself. Then, the Yoneda lemma implies that
$\emptyset\rightarrow M'$
resp.
$M_i\rightarrow M_i',\,i\in I$
are isomorphisms in
$\mathbf{Man}$
, i.e. diffeomorphisms.

question:
While working on a textbook, I stumbled upon the following task : “Prove that the category of smooth manifolds admits countable coproducts”.

The first thing that came to mind was : something’s wrong, because if the dimensions of the manifolds are not the same, then their disjoint union can’t be a smooth manifold as when you move along the disjoint union (with its canonical topology), the dimension changes (which is not allowed in the textbook’s definition of manifolds).

So I guessed it was only a typo and that the dimension of the manifolds had to be the same for it to work.

But then I wondered… was it really a typo ?

Being given countably many manifolds, maybe there exists some bizarre manifold, that as a set would not be the disjoint union of these manifolds, but that would nonetheless be a coproduct in the category of smooth manifolds. I wasn’t able to find an easy argument to prevent this from happening and my proofs for that are rather unnatural and/or lengthy.

How would you proceed ?
answer:
Another reason why
Man
does not have coproducts, in fact not all copowers, is the following result, which is essentially due to Freyd:

If a category has copowers and is essentially small, then it is thin.

If a category has copowers and is essentially small, then it is thin.

For the proof: Let
$M$
be the set of all morphisms, which is w.l.o.g. small. Assume that
$f,g : A \rightrightarrows B$
are different morphisms. If the copower
$M \otimes A$
exists (the
$M$
-indexed coproduct of copies of
$A$
), then
$$M \supseteq \hom(M \otimes A,B) \cong \hom(A,B)^M \supseteq \{f,g\}^M,$$
which contradicts Cantor's Theorem from set theory.
$\checkmark$

Now, the category
Man
is essentially small. You can either deduce this from Whitney's embedding theorem, or prove it like this: Since a manifold is second-countable, it is Lindelöf (
proof
). In particular, there is a countable atlas. It is then completely determined by countable many open subsets of Euclidean spaces and the transition maps.

This proof that
Man
does not have copowers has been generated automatically by CatDat, using its other properties.

question:
Here's a problem I just came up with:

I considered a triangle
$\triangle ABC$
such
$|AB|=11$
and
$|AC|=5$
.

I asked myself the following question:

What must angle A be when the incircle of ABC has a maximum radius?

I performed several tests with GeoGebra which led me to determine:
$A=80^\circ$
. So, I'm wondering if this is correct and if the exact answer can be found through calculation?
answer:
We recall that
$$|\triangle ABC| = rs,$$
where
$r$
is the inradius and
$s = (a+b+c)/2$
the semiperimeter.  Also recall that
$$|\triangle ABC| = \frac{1}{2}bc \sin \alpha,$$
where
$\alpha = \angle BAC$
.  Thus
$$r = \frac{bc \sin \alpha}{2s} = \frac{bc \sin \alpha}{b + c + \sqrt{b^2 + c^2 - 2bc \cos \alpha}}.$$
Since
$c = 11$
and
$b = 5$
are fixed, we have as a function of
$\alpha$
$$r(\alpha) = \frac{55 \sin \alpha}{16 + \sqrt{146 - 110 \cos \alpha}}.$$
All that remains is to compute a derivative and locate the critical point; however, this is computationally tedious and leads to the cubic
$$\cos^3 \alpha + 3 \cos^2 \alpha - \frac{347}{55} \cos \alpha + 1 = 0,$$
with numeric solution (in radians)
$$\alpha \approx 1.3962369082289012941855528838 \ldots,$$
which in degrees is approximately
$$\alpha^\circ \approx 79.99848204191088333239913491 \ldots.$$
The value of the inradius for this angle is attained at the unique positive real root of
$$r^6 + 677r^4 + 4211r^2 - 27225 = 0,$$
with approximate value
$$r \approx 1.9865967673502241946 \ldots.$$

question:
Here's a problem I just came up with:

I considered a triangle
$\triangle ABC$
such
$|AB|=11$
and
$|AC|=5$
.

I asked myself the following question:

What must angle A be when the incircle of ABC has a maximum radius?

I performed several tests with GeoGebra which led me to determine:
$A=80^\circ$
. So, I'm wondering if this is correct and if the exact answer can be found through calculation?
answer:
I arrive at the same conclusion as @heropup, with a different path (I use trigonometry for the very last computation).

Notations :
$S$
for the area of triangle
$ABC$
;
$s=\frac12(a+b+c)$
for its semi-perimeter, and
$b:=AC=5$
and
$c:=AB=11$
.

I use relationship
$$S=rs \ \iff \ r^2=\frac{S^2}{s^2}\tag{1}$$
(see
here
)

Using Heron's formula :

$$S^2=s(s-a)(s-b)(s-c),\tag{2}$$

we have to maximize
$$r^2=\underbrace{\frac{1}{s}(s-a)(s-b)(s-c)}_{f(a)}=\frac{2(a^2 -36)(16 - a)}{16 + a} \ \text{for} \ a \in [c-b,c+b]=[6,16]$$

Fig. 1 : Graphical representation of function
$f$
showing that
$r^2$
is maximal for a unique value
$a=a_0$
.

Differentiation gives :

$$f'(a)=\frac{-a^3 - 16a^2 + 256a + 576}{2(16 + a)^2}$$

Equation
$f'(a)=0$
has three real roots. Only one of them is positive

$$a_0 \approx 11.264804945818$$

which is therefore the  extremal value of
$f$
(see fig. 1).

We know now
$s=\frac12(a_0+b+c) \approx 13.632402472909$

By definition of
$f$
, we have
$$S=s \sqrt{f(a_0)}\approx 27.082086683899.$$

The final touch : using formula
$S=\frac12 bc \sin(\hat{A})$
, we get

$$\hat{A}=\arcsin\left(\frac{2S}{bc}\right)\frac{180}{\pi} \approx 79.998482041911°$$

(decimal degrees) : not exactly
$80°$
but so near !

Remark :
For any triangle with given sides
$a$
and
$b$
, this optimal angle giving a maximal inradius cannot be less than :
$$2 \arcsin(\Phi-1) \ \text{radians}$$

(where
$\Phi$
is golden ratio)

$\approx 76.3454153°$
, this value being reached for isosceles triangles (
$b=c$
).

question:
Here's a problem I just came up with:

I considered a triangle
$\triangle ABC$
such
$|AB|=11$
and
$|AC|=5$
.

I asked myself the following question:

What must angle A be when the incircle of ABC has a maximum radius?

I performed several tests with GeoGebra which led me to determine:
$A=80^\circ$
. So, I'm wondering if this is correct and if the exact answer can be found through calculation?
answer:
Write the formula for inradius as
$$r=\frac{Area}{s}$$
where
$Area$
is the area of the triangle and
$s$
is the semiperimeter. In general case, you know
$b$
and
$c$
, and you want to write all these is terms of angle
$A$
.
$$Area=\frac12bc\sin A$$
$$s=\frac12\left(b+c+\sqrt{b^2+c^2-2bc\cos A}\right)$$
You can then plot
$r(A)$
. This is what I get from google:
The maximum value is indeed around
$80^\circ$
.

If you want the exact minimum, take the derivative, and set it to
$0$
.

question:
This question already has answers here
:

Proving a limit using the $\epsilon$ - $\delta$ definition of limit.

(4 answers)

Closed
last month
.

Prove using means of
$(\epsilon, \delta)$
that if
$\lim_{x\to a}f(x) = \infty$
and
$\lim_{x\to a}g(x) = c$
where c is a real number. Prove that
$\lim_{x\to a}[f(x)+g(x)] = \infty$

Stewart's Calculus
page 81 ex 44a

I tried setting it up for a normal epsilon-delta proof so for every real number
$M$
there exists a
$\delta$
s.t. if
$0<|x-a|<\delta$
then
$(f(x)+g(x))>M$

So I noticed that
$f(x) > M-g(x)$
so if I bound
$g(x)$
then I just have to prove that
$f(x)$
is greater than some real number. I'm stuck on how to bound
$g(x)$
and overall how to proceed from there.

All help is appreciated!
answer:
I've actually been surprised to see that this question has not been answered before in this forum.

To your question, you were actually very closed.
$g(x)$
is indeed bounded.

HINT: use the definition for a limit of a function, for a constant
$\varepsilon = 1$
(or practically any other constant, for convenience I chose
$1$
).

If you're struggling, here is the standard solution:
You know that
$g(x)$
is bounded in some
$\delta_1$
-neighborhood of
$a$
(in other words, exists some
$\delta_1$
such that
$\forall 0 < |x - a| < \delta_1 \colon\, 0 \le |g(x) - c| < \varepsilon)$
. In particular
$g(x) > c - \epsilon = c - 1$
. It is common to use that tactic in calc1.

Now continue the proof from where you stopped (you will probably be able to complete that part): there is
$\delta_2$
such that
$f(x) > M - c + 1$
for each
$x \in (a - \delta_2, a + \delta_2) \setminus \{a\}$
, therefore by taking
$\delta = \min\{\delta_1, \delta_2\}$
we obtain that for each
$0 < x < \delta$
:
$$f(x) + g(x) > M -c + 1 + g(x) > M -c + 1 + c - 1 = M$$
as intended.

question:
How can I evaluate the following integral?

$$ \int_{0}^{1} x \left( \int_{0}^{1} x \left( \int_{0}^{1} x \left(\cdots \infty \right) {\rm d} x\right) {\rm d} x\right) {\rm d} x $$


I assumed that let


$$ I = \int_{0}^{1} x \left( \int_{0}^{1} x \left( \int_{0}^{1} x \left( \cdots \infty \right) {\rm d} x \right) {\rm d} x \right) {\rm d} x \implies I = \int (xI) {\rm d} x $$


Applying integration by parts,


$$\implies I=x\int I {\rm d}x-\int\left(\int I {\rm d} x\right) {\rm d} x$$

However, I am unable to understand further. Please help me with this integral. Because I can't apply integration by parts properly for the above integral.
answer:
The notation in the original query conflates definite integrals with antiderivatives. If interpreted strictly as a definite integral evaluating to a constant
$I$
, we have
$I = \int_0^1 x I \, \mathrm{d}x = I/2$
, which implies
$I = 0$
.

Alternatively, interpreting the nested structure as a sequence of functions defined by repeated integration yields a well-posed formulation. Let
$f_0(x) = 1$
and define recursively
$$f_n(x) = \int_0^x t f_{n-1}(t) \, \mathrm{d}t, \quad n \ge 1.$$

Notice that this is a weighted variant of Cauchy's formula for repeated integration. By Fubini's theorem, changing the order of integration over the domain reduces the
$n$
-fold integral to a single integral with a kernel. Specifically, for a weight function
$g(t) = t$
, we have
$$f_n(x) = \frac{1}{(n-1)!} \int_0^x t \left( \int_t^x s \, \mathrm{d}s \right)^{n-1} \, \mathrm{d}t.$$

Evaluating the inner integral yields
$\int_t^x s \, \mathrm{d}s = \frac{x^2 - t^2}{2}$
. Thus,
$$f_n(x) = \frac{1}{(n-1)!} \int_0^x t \left( \frac{x^2 - t^2}{2} \right)^{n-1} \, \mathrm{d}t.$$

Using the substitution
$u = \frac{x^2 - t^2}{2}$
, it follows that
$$f_n(x) = \frac{1}{(n-1)!} \int_0^{x^2/2} u^{n-1} \, \mathrm{d}u = \frac{1}{n!} \left( \frac{x^2}{2} \right)^n.$$

Taking the limit as
$n \to \infty$
,
$$\lim_{n \to \infty} f_n(x) = \lim_{n \to \infty} \frac{x^{2n}}{2^n n!} = 0 \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}.$$

Evaluating at the specified upper bound
$x=1$
yields
$0$
. Integration by parts is unnecessary and leads to circular reasoning due to the initial misinterpretation of the integration bounds.

question:
How can I evaluate the following integral?

$$ \int_{0}^{1} x \left( \int_{0}^{1} x \left( \int_{0}^{1} x \left(\cdots \infty \right) {\rm d} x\right) {\rm d} x\right) {\rm d} x $$


I assumed that let


$$ I = \int_{0}^{1} x \left( \int_{0}^{1} x \left( \int_{0}^{1} x \left( \cdots \infty \right) {\rm d} x \right) {\rm d} x \right) {\rm d} x \implies I = \int (xI) {\rm d} x $$


Applying integration by parts,


$$\implies I=x\int I {\rm d}x-\int\left(\int I {\rm d} x\right) {\rm d} x$$

However, I am unable to understand further. Please help me with this integral. Because I can't apply integration by parts properly for the above integral.
answer:
Since these are definite integrals you can integrate from the inside out.

Let
$I_0 = \int_{0}^{1} xI \, dx$
so then
$I_n = \int_{0}^{1} x I_{n-1} \, dx$
, and the limit as
$n \to \infty$
is equal to
$I$
.

Since
$I_{n-1}$
is a constant you can move it outside of the integral.

$$
I_n = I_{n-1} \int_{0}^{1} x \, dx
$$

Integrating gives

$$
\int_{0}^{1} x \, dx = \frac{1}{2}.
$$

So

$$
I_n = \frac{1}{2} I_{n-1}.
$$

Using induction we get that

$$
I_n = \frac{1}{2^{n+1}}.
$$

Taking the limit as
$n \to \infty$
gives

$$
I = 0.
$$

question:
I attempted solving question 17P(1) in
Willard's book
; consider some
$X, Y$
compact Hausdorff spaces, and a continues surjective
$f \colon X \to Y$
. Then prove the existence of a compact
$X_0 \subset X$
s.t.
$f(X_0) = Y$
, but
$f$
maps no proper closed subsets of
$X_0$
onto
$Y$
. Later on, by other sources, I found that such function
$f|_{X_0}$
is called "irreducible" (which makes sense -- I can't "reduce"/restrict
$f|_{X_0}$
to be "more injective" than that).

The author instructs the reader to use Zorn's Lemma. I am not really experienced with applying Zorn's Lemma to solve exercises.

I attempted to consider the family of closed (and in particular compact) subsets of
$X' \subset X$
with the relation
$\subset$
s.t.
$f(X') = Y$
, but was not able to find any lower bound to each chain, and then use Zorn to get
$X_0$
minimal in the
$X'$
-s.

I will appreciate if anyone could point me in the right direction on how to apply Zorn's Lemma. I assume I will be able to continue from there.
answer:
You were on the right track. Let
$\mathcal K$
denote the set of closed subsets
$K \subset X$
such that
$f(K) = Y$
, ordered by
$K_1  \le K_2$
iff
$K_1 \supset K_2$
.

Consider a chain
$\mathcal C$
in
$\mathcal K$
. If we can prove that
$K^* = \bigcap_{K \in \mathcal C} K \in \mathcal K$
, we have found an upper bound for
$\mathcal C$
and Zorn's lemma applies.

We have to show that each
$y \in Y$
is in
$f(K^*)$
. Assume to the contrary that there exists
$y \in Y$
with
$y \notin f(K^*)$
. Then
$K^* \cap f^{-1}(y) = \emptyset$
which means
$$\bigcap_{K \in \mathcal C} (K \cap f^{-1}(y)) = \left(\bigcap_{K \in \mathcal C} K\right) \cap f^{-1}(y) = \emptyset .$$

All
$K \cap f^{-1}(y)$
are closed in
$X$
, thus the finite intersection property says that there exists a finite subset
$\mathcal C'$
of
$\mathcal C$
such that
$\bigcap_{K \in \mathcal C'} (K \cap f^{-1}(y)) = \left(\bigcap_{K \in \mathcal C'} K\right) \cap f^{-1}(y) = \emptyset$
. Since
$\mathcal C$
is a chain, the finite subset
$\mathcal C'$
has a biggest element
$K'$
so that
$\bigcap_{K \in \mathcal C'} K = K'$
. Hence
$K' \cap f^{-1}(y) = \emptyset$
which is a contradiction.

question:
I attempted solving question 17P(1) in
Willard's book
; consider some
$X, Y$
compact Hausdorff spaces, and a continues surjective
$f \colon X \to Y$
. Then prove the existence of a compact
$X_0 \subset X$
s.t.
$f(X_0) = Y$
, but
$f$
maps no proper closed subsets of
$X_0$
onto
$Y$
. Later on, by other sources, I found that such function
$f|_{X_0}$
is called "irreducible" (which makes sense -- I can't "reduce"/restrict
$f|_{X_0}$
to be "more injective" than that).

The author instructs the reader to use Zorn's Lemma. I am not really experienced with applying Zorn's Lemma to solve exercises.

I attempted to consider the family of closed (and in particular compact) subsets of
$X' \subset X$
with the relation
$\subset$
s.t.
$f(X') = Y$
, but was not able to find any lower bound to each chain, and then use Zorn to get
$X_0$
minimal in the
$X'$
-s.

I will appreciate if anyone could point me in the right direction on how to apply Zorn's Lemma. I assume I will be able to continue from there.
answer:
If you look at the pre-images of each element of
$Y$
, then all the combinations in which you take precisely one element of each pre-image will function as an upper bound, because at least one of such sets must be contained in the subspace
$X'$
, to get a surjective map.

question:
I’m putting together a paper on the Mordell curve
$$Y^2=X^3+2000651625,$$
demonstrating how to find all integer points by elementary means.


After two steps, my method has found
$20$
integer points (corresponding to
$10$
positive-
$y$
solutions). At that point in the paper — which is aimed at “beginners” (amateurs, high-school students, etc.) — I want to introduce the concept of rank and generators. By non-elementary means (Sage, LMFDB,
etc.
), we know that it’s a rank
$5$
curve with
$46$
integer points (i.e.,
$23$
positive-
$y$
solutions), so we know
a priori
that only two of the five generators have been revealed so far.


My questions are this:




What’s the most elementary and simple/elegant way to show that the known points only correspond to two generators?




and




What’s the most elementary and simple/elegant way to find how many other generators there are (a.k.a. determine the rank)?




I don’t mind
referencing
non-elementary concepts and methods, and using known results to prove that the complete set of (
$46$
) integer points is eventually found by my method… but if possible I’d love for all the
actual math
in this paper to be completely elementary. (
n.b.
I’m even avoiding using UFDs, Gaussian factorization, and other “borderline elementary” tools. The most advanced math I’m incorporating is the fact that numbers of the form
$a^2+3b^2$
have restricted prime factorizations.)

In case it helps: After two steps, my method has revealed the points

\begin{align*}
(x,y) \in \bigl\{&(-1260,\pm 525),\, (-1134, \pm 23289),\, (-210, \pm 44625),\, (490, \pm 46025),\\
&\hspace{1em} (840, \pm 50925),\, (996, \pm 54669),\, (1446, \pm 70881),\\
&\hspace{2em} (3319, \pm 196372),\, (3234, \pm 189273), (20577816, \pm 93346689261)\bigr\}
\end{align*}

and the first two [only] are generators. It takes five more steps of my method to reveal the remaining solutions.
answer:
The OP framework is giving ten pairs of points
$(x_k, \pm y_k)$
on the curve, and assumes some hidden preliminary work.
With only this information i am trying to address the two questions by some disjoint approaches as follows, and this would
my choice
of a didactically
oriented warm-up contact with the arithmetics of elliptic curves.

My point of view is that sooner or later the computers should come into the play. (At least for giving an answer for a question stated from a stronger position.)
But there is a difference between the ways to proceed computer-aided with the target of getting all integral points, the rank and the generators.
We can of course ask for them and get them.
Inside the tunnel of these computations there is a high level industry of involved theoretical results and implemented algorithms.
My way below is using computer aids only for "elementary" computations or for insight that can be verified at elementary level.
(It is however not a simple matter to take the intersection of amateurs / beginners / high-school students with people addressing diophantine questions
related to elliptic curves, their ranks and computing a full system of generators - then insist and further intersect with the set of those that are not using
computers for such purposes.)

So i do use high level tools (e.g. height pairing) in the sequel,
obtain results with them (dependencies),
then check these with low level operations (check dependencies by adding points on the curve).
The made experience would at least be a motivation for learning about advanced techniques and tools.
I have no idea how to show independence in an other manner, thus address the second question.

Using some hope-to-work, possibly non-systematic strategies, we exhibit as many integral points as possible on the curve.
We are using only elementary computations, and use sage for addition and
$\Bbb Z$
-scalar multiplication on the curve to accelerate the searching process.
(Adding points (solving systems of two ecuations, a cubic and a linear one,)
must be a part of the allowed elementary bridges in the process. )
One strategy will be to take linear combinations of already found points, so that the first question is covered as a corollary.
For the second question, we stop by finding that the rank is at least five, and need the height pairing (as a reference).

The answer is given rather in order to provide pictures, many pictures, that may be possibly useful in the presentation.

As a final note, the given elliptic curve has not only one minimal system of generators. Starting with one, e.g. the one delivered by sage,
we may use a (non-trivial) GL-matrix of determinant
$\pm 1$
to pass to an other one. It turns out that we may pick (first) four out of the
ten (modulo
$\pm1$
-action) given points, add one more (e.g. the
$126$
-
$x$
-lift) and get such a good one.

Let
$B = 2000651625= 3^{2} \cdot 5^{3} \cdot 7^{2} \cdot 36293$
and
$E$
$$
E\ :\qquad Y^2 = X^3+B\ .$$

Searching for integral points
$(x,y)\in E(\Bbb Q)$
with
$x<0$
:

The assumption
$x<0$
is fair (to start with). It finds pairs
$(x,\pm y)$
with
$y\le \lfloor \sqrt B\rfloor=44728$
.
Brute search, we list only
$P=(x,y)$
with
$y>0$
for the pair
$\pm P=(x,\pm y)$

B = 2000651625
V = ZZ(floor(sqrt(B)))    # sqrt(B) ~ 44728.644345653, so V is 44728

for y in [0..V]:
x = -(B - y^2)^(1/3)
if x in ZZ:
print(f"{x} & {y}\\\\\\hline")

$$
\small
\begin{array}{|r|r|}
\hline
x & y\\\hline
-1260 & 525\\\hline
-1134 & 23289\\\hline
-656 & 41453\\\hline
-594 & 42321\\\hline
-276 & 44493\\\hline
-210 & 44625\\\hline
\end{array}
\qquad\text{ and let }\qquad
\begin{aligned}
Q_1 &= (-1260, 525)\\
Q_2 &= (-1134, 23289)\\
Q_3 &= (-656, 41453)\\
Q_4 &= (-594, 42321)\\
Q_5 &= (-276, 44493)\\
Q_6 &= (-210, 44625)
\end{aligned}
$$

Note (on the fairness of the first approach):
The value of
$x$
is in all the above cases negative and in modulus
$|x|\le B^{1/3}=1260.05786771265\dots$
(and by chance the minimal integral value is also reached.)
In such cases
my fair choice
is to search for integral points
$(x,y)$
with
$|x|\le B^{1/3}$
allowing for
$x$
both signs
.
This would find some further integral (for the present discussion) important points, and there is little to be said
structurally below. The simple code
[x for x in [-1260..1260] if (x^3 + 2000651625).is_square()]
finds the
$x$
-values:
$-1260, -1134, -656, -594, -276, -210, 126, 490, 840, 996$
. (Sage generators are covered.)

To have some terms to compare, i will mention some Mordell elliptic curves
from my drawer that may fit in the pattern (moderate rank, "
many
" integral points, integral generators).

The elliptic curve
$E_1$
defined by
$y^2 = x^3 + 105633$
, with
$105633 = 3^2 \cdot 11^2 \cdot 97$
, rank
four
, generators
$P_1=(-44, 143)$
,
$P_2=(-33, 264)$
,
$P_3=(2, 325)$
,
$P_4=(22, 341)$
. (Sage gives
$(66, 627)$
instead of
$P_3$
.) There are
$19$
(pairs of) integral points on it:
$$
\small\color{blue}{(-44, 143)}\ \color{blue}{(-33, 264)}\ \color{blue}{(-24, 303)}\ \color{blue}{(-2, 325)}\ \color{blue}{(22, 341)}\ \color{blue}{(27, 354)}\ (31, 368)\\
\small\color{blue}{(48, 465)}\ \color{blue}{(66, 627)}\ \color{blue}{(132, 1551)}\ \color{blue}{(198, 2805)}\ \color{blue}{(396, 7887)}\ (412, 8369)\ \color{blue}{(891, 26598)}\\
\small(1446, 54987)\ (2322, 111891)\ (4026, 255453)\ (19272, 2675409)\ (1031448, 1047540945)\ .
$$
The generators are well covered by the
$x$
-range from
$-47$
to
$47$
. The blue values correspond to points
$(x,y)$
with
$x$
dividing the discriminant, "DD".
The rank
four
elliptic curve
$E_2$
defined by
$y^2 = x^3 + 9428625$
with
$9428625 = 3^2 \cdot 5^3 \cdot 17^2 \cdot 29$
and generators
$(-204, 969)$
,
$(-170, 2125)$
,
$(-120, 2775)$
,
$P_4=(-36,3063)$
. (Sage gives
$(255, 5100)$
instead of
$P_4$
as generator.) There are
$23$
(pairs of) integral points on it,
$$\small\color{blue}{(-204, 969)}\ \color{blue}{(-170, 2125)}\ (-149, 2474)\ \color{blue}{(-120, 2775)}\ \color{blue}{(-36, 3063)}\ \color{blue}{(30, 3075)}\ \color{blue}{(34, 3077)}\ (64, 3113)\ (94, 3203)\\
\small\color{blue}{(204, 4233)}\ \color{blue}{(255, 5100)}\ \color{blue}{(306, 6171)}\ \color{blue}{(459, 10302)}\ \color{blue}{(816, 23511)}\ \color{blue}{(1080, 35625)}\ \color{blue}{(1530, 59925)}\ (1611, 64734)\ (2586, 131541)\\

The chosen generators are in the range between

An example of rank
five

given by

with
$77806025 = 5^2 \cdot 11^2 \cdot 17^2 \cdot 89$
. The
$41$
(pairs of) integral points are
$$
\tiny\color{blue}{(-425, 1020)}\ (-409, 3064)\ \color{blue}{(-374, 5049)}\ \color{blue}{(-340, 6205)}\ \color{blue}{(-220, 8195)}\ \color{blue}{(-200, 8355)}\ \color{blue}{(-110, 8745)}\ (-106, 8753)
\ \color{blue}{(34, 8823)}\ \color{blue}{(170, 9095)}\ \color{blue}{(220, 9405)}\ (355, 11070)\ \color{blue}{(374, 11407)}\\
\tiny\color{blue}{(484, 13827)}\ (544, 15453)\ \color{blue}{(680, 19805)}\ \color{blue}{(1870, 81345)}\ (2036, 92291)\ (2915, 157630)\ (4486, 300591)\ \color{blue}{(4675, 319770)}\ (7040, 590755)\ (7990, 714255)\ \color{blue}{(9350, 904145)}\\
\tiny(11356, 1210179)\ (13904, 1639517)\ \color{blue}{(14960, 1829795)}\ (24046, 3728769)\ (33430, 6112305)\
(36410, 6947545)\ (110275, 36619770)\ (136510, 50436705)\\
\tiny(237116, 115462589)\ (1090210, 1138322295)\ \color{blue}{(1398760, 1654302045)}\ (2345575, 3592311480)\ .$$
Sage gives the five generators:
$(-374, 5049),\ (-110, 8745),\ (170, 9095),\ (935, 29920),\ (1870, 81345)$
. Three of them are in my "fair" range between
$-426$
and
$426$
. But we have plenty of choices for five generators in
$[-426,\ 426]$
, for instance by replacing the last two sage generators by the integral points with minimal absolute value for
$x$
, which are
$(-106, 8753)$
and
$(34,8823)$
.

A reason for DD points:
In all the cases
$y^2=x^3+k$
above, there were "some DD points"
$(x,y)$
(with
$x$
integral dividing the discriminant).
This is not always the case, but sometimes it is the case. The reason for this behaviour is as follows (and may be mentioned at an elementary level).
In
$\Bbb Z$
we have a "natural arithmetic setting" and one main characteristic is the unique factorization. For short,
$\Bbb Z$
is a UFD.
Let us look at the smallest example among the above ones, the elliptic curve
$E$
of (affine) equation
$y^2 = x^3+B$
,
$B= 105633=3^2 \cdot 11^2 \cdot 97$
.
We may consider the number
$w=\sqrt {97}$
and work in the field
$L=\Bbb Q(w)$
. By chosen chance, its class number is one.
The units in the ring of integers in
$L$
are generated by
$u = (5604+569w)$
, which can be obtained as usual by solving the Pell equation
$a^2-97b^2=1$
. To motivate at elementary level how to obtain it, note that
$a,b$
are "relatively big" (in this case) so that we expect that
$a^2-97b^2\approx 0$
, so
$a/b\approx\sqrt {97}$
. It is natural to consider the continued fraction of
$\sqrt {97}$
,
and its convergents are rational numbers that approximate step for step better and better this irrational value.  We have
$$
\sqrt{97} = 9
+ \frac1{1
+ \frac1{5
+ \frac1{1
+ \frac1{1
+ \frac1{1
+ \frac1{1
+ \frac1{1
+ \frac1{1
+ \frac1{5
+ \frac1{\ddots}}}}}}}}}}
=
[9,\overline{(1, 5, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 1, 5, 1, 18)}]
$$
with an overline over the period. Taking the list of convergents,
$9$
,
$10$
,
$\frac{59}{6}$
,
$\frac{69}{7}$
,
$\frac{128}{13}$
,
$\frac{197}{20}$
,
$\frac{325}{33}$
,
$\frac{522}{53}$
,
$\frac{847}{86}$
,
$\frac{4757}{483}$
,
$\color{maroon}{\frac{5604}{569}}$
,
$\frac{105629}{10725}$
, ...
it turns out that for
$\color{maroon}{5604}$
and
$\color{maroon}{569}$
we have
$-1 = 5604^2-97\cdot 569^2$
.

Now let
$P=(x,y)$
be an integral
point on
$E$
. Then
$$
x^3 = y^2 - B = (y-33w)(y+33w)\ .
$$
For the integral points
$(x,y)$
on
$E$
we have the following sample relations inside (the integers of)
$L$
. For the point
$P=(-2,325)$
with smallest
$|x|$
we have:
$$
\small
\begin{aligned}
325 + 33w &= u      \left(\frac{69 -7w}2\right)^2 \left(\frac{69 +7w}2\right)\\
325 - 33w &= u^{-1} \left(\frac{69 -7w}2\right)   \left(\frac{69 +7w}2\right)^2\\
2 &= \left(\frac{69 -7w}2\right)   \left(\frac{69 +7w}2\right)
\end{aligned}
$$
Taking the difference of the first two lines we have
$2\cdot 33w$
equals a multiple of
$\left(\frac{69 -7w}2\right)\left(\frac{69 +7w}2\right)$
,
which is
$x$
.
Also:
$$
\small
\begin{aligned}
143 + 33w &= -u^{-2}\left(\frac{69 -7w}2\right)\left(\frac{69 +7w}2\right)^5 (59+6w)^2 (59-6w)\ ,\\
143 - 33w &= -u^{2}\left(\frac{69 -7w}2\right)^5\left(\frac{69 +7w}2\right) (59+6w) (59-6w)^2\ ,\\
-44 &= \left(\frac{69 -7w}2\right)^2\left(\frac{69 +7w}2\right)^2(59+6w) (59-6w)\ .
\end{aligned}
$$
(Taking the difference...)

In general, there is an extension of
$\Bbb Q(\sqrt[3]B, \zeta)$
,
$\zeta=\zeta_3$
, with UF, and in this UFD framework
$$y^2=x^3 + B = (x-e_1)(x-e_2)(x-e_3)=(x-e)(x-e\zeta)(x-e\zeta^2)$$
has factors
$(x-e)$
,
$(x-e\zeta)$
,
$(x-e\zeta)^2$
with matching square free parts.
For instance, if
$(x-e_1)=(x-e)=p_1p_2\dots p_s\; (\dots)^2$
then the primes
$p_1,p_2,\dots,p_s$
also appear
once more
in the
decomposition of either
$(x-e_2)$
or
$(x-e_3)$
. So also in the difference
$(e_1-e_2)$
or
$(e_1-e_3)$
, i.e. in the discriminant. If there is no part
$(\dots)^2$
, which makes the problems,
then we have a "DD" case. And there is a good chance to sometimes miss this
$(\dots)^2$
part for the "small"
$x$
-values.

In our case, the situation is more complicated,
$B$
has the squarefree part
$181465=5 \cdot 36293$
, the field
$L=\Bbb Q(w)=\Bbb Q(\sqrt{181465})$
has class number two,
its Hilbert class field is
$\Bbb Q(\sqrt 5, \sqrt{36293})$
. The continued fraction of
$w$
is "long'', (try
sage: L.<w> = QuadraticField(181465); continued_fraction(w)
,)
so the corresponding generating unit. Nevertheless, we may still
hope
for integral points during a DD search.

DD search:
It is easy now to search for integral points
$P=(x,y)$
with
$x$
dividing the discriminant:

sage: B = 2000651625
sage: E = EllipticCurve([0, B])
sage: x_values = [x*sig for x in ZZ(E.discriminant()).divisors() for sig in [-1, 1] if (sig*x^3 + 2000651625).is_square()]
sage: x_values
[126, -210, 490, 840, -1134, -1260]
sage: [G.xy() for G in E.gens()]
[(-1260, 525), (-210, 44625), (126, 44751), (490, 46025), (840, 50925)]

And now we have now enough points!

A plot so far:

We are trying to work in an elementary manner, so let us draw the points
$\pm Q_k$
,
$1\le k\le 6$
, and imagine how lines through two of them, including tangents, look like.

Note:
The
$y$
-axis is rescaled, it has for the points
$\pm Q_6$
the height
$\pm 44625$
, so the points
$\pm Q_1$
(close to the left most point on
$E(\Bbb R)$
) with heights
$\pm 525$
cannot be distinguished in this picture. The sage code used for it follows.

B = 2000651625
E = EllipticCurve([0, B])

Qs = []
k = 0
offset = 8000
xmin, xmax = -1600, 4000
graph = E.plot(xmin=xmin, xmax=xmax,
color='blue', thickness=1, adaptive_recursion=100,
title="
$y^2 = x^3 + 2000651625$
")

for y in [0..ZZ(floor(sqrt(B)))]:
x = -(B - y^2)^(1/3)
if x not in ZZ:    continue
k += 1
x = ZZ(x)
Qs.append(E(x, y))
graph += point((x, y), color='red', size=30, zorder=5)
graph += point((x,-y), color='red', size=30, zorder=5)
graph += text(f"
$Q_{k}$
",  (x,  y + offset), color='black', fontsize=8)
graph += text(f"
$Q'_{k}$
", (x, -y - offset), color='black', fontsize=8)


graph.show(xmin=xmin, xmax=xmax, figsize=[16, 9],
aspect_ratio='automatic',
ticks=[[-1600, 0, 2000, 4000], []],
tick_formatter=[None, None])

The six points
$Q_1,Q_2,\dots, Q_6$
can be now introduced in code from their list
Qs
as:

Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4, Q5, Q6 = Qs

Some lines:

Consider some pair
$(S,T)$
of points among the plotted red points. Draw the line
$ST$
through them. It intersects the curve in a third point
$R$
which is by definition of the operation of adding points on
$E(\Bbb Q)$
the point
$R=-(S+T)$
. So we obtain one more point. Repeat the process. The list of integral points get thus larger and larger, and it is a fact that we may stop adding (the same point) when "denominators show up", because then the integral points are not "coming back". We have many pairs, so we obtain many points. Some of them are integral. Let us see, by computing the list of all points
$\pm S\pm T$
with
$S,T$
among
$Q_1,Q_2,\dots,Q_6$
, and keeping only those which are integral,
with positive
$y$
-component. This is a first wave of generating points.

def is_integral(Q):
"""Check if Q (is not the infinity point and) has components in ZZ"""
return Q and Q[0] in ZZ and Q[1] in ZZ

points = [s*S + t*T
for s in [-1, 1] for t in [-1, 1]
for S in Qs      for T in Qs
if is_integral(s*S + t*T)
and (s*S + t*T)[1] > 0]    # keep only new points with y > 0
points = list(set(points))           # remove possible repetitions
points.sort()
Q7, Q8, Q9, Q10, Q11, Q12, Q13, Q14, Q15, Q16 = points

We obtain some new ten integral points, and if we slightly enlarge the code above to also remember how the new points were obtained, e.g. by replacing the first occurrence of
s*S + t*T
by the tuple
(s*S + t*T, s, S, t, T)
, we have:

$$
\small
\begin{aligned}
Q_{ 7} &= (490, 46025)    &&= -(Q_5+Q_6)\\
Q_{ 8} &= (840, 50925)    &&= -(Q_4+Q_6)\\
Q_{ 9} &= (996, 54669)    &&= -(Q_3+Q_5)\\
Q_{10} &= (1446, 70881)   &&= -(Q_3+Q_4)\\
Q_{11} &= (3234, 189273)  &&= -(Q_1+Q_6) = -(Q_2+Q_3)\\
Q_{12} &= (3319, 196372)  &&= -(Q_6-Q_1)\\
Q_{13} &= (7651, 670726)  &&= -(Q_5-Q_2)\\
Q_{14} &= (38115, 7441350) &&= -(Q_2-Q_1)=-(Q_6-Q_3)\\
Q_{15} &= (75399, 20703768) &&= -(Q_5-Q_4)\\
Q_{16} &= (20577816, 93346689261) &&= -(Q_1+Q_1)
\end{aligned}
$$

Each line can be checked, for instance typing
Q16 == -2*Q1
delivers
True
. Same for
Q11 + Q1 + Q6 == 0
and
Q11 + Q2 + Q3 == 0
. We have in particular a relation among the first six
$Q$
-points,
$Q_1+Q_6 = Q_2+Q_3$
, which is in the picture:

import random
random.seed('MSE question 5131546')    # to fix randomness

E = EllipticCurve([0, 2000651625])
graph = E.plot(xmin=-1400, xmax=3400,
color='blue', thickness=1, adaptive_recursion=100,
title="
$y^2 = x^3 + 2000651625$
")
for P, k in zip(Qs + points[:6] + [-Q1], [1..12] + [-1]):
x, y = P.xy()
graph += point((x, y), color='red', size=30, zorder=5)
graph += text(f"
$Q_{{{k}}}$
",  (x,  y + offset*sgn(k)), color='black', fontsize=8)

for S, T in ([Q5, Q7], [Q4, Q8], [Q3, Q9],
[Q3, Q10], [Q1, Q11], [Q2, Q11], [-Q1, Q12]):
color = [random.uniform(0.6, 0.9) for choice in 'rgb']
graph += line([S.xy(), T.xy()], color=color, thickness=1, zorder=4)

graph.show(xmin=-1400, xmax=3400, figsize=[16, 9],
aspect_ratio='automatic',
ticks=[[-1400, 0, 1000, 2000, 3000], []],
tick_formatter=[None, None])

(Using the
line
command we are drawing segments, so the list of "lines" for
S, T
was manually chosen for the two extremal points among the three points on it in the
$Q$
-list.) The points
$Q_1$
and
$Q_{-1}=-Q_1$
are very close to each other and to the "turning point"
$(0,\sqrt B)$
, relative to the scale of the picture. The point
$Q_{16}=-2Q_1$
is "far away", to imagine where it lives consider the tangent in
$Q_1$
to the real curve
$E(\Bbb R)$
displayed in the picture, then its third intersection point...

The process can be repeated. No new integral points show up.

Dependent points:
This section addresses the first OP question. In our notations, the ten given and known
integral
points on
$E(\Bbb Q)$
(after the first steps) are in the present notations
$Q_k$
for
$k$
among:
$$
1,\ 2,\ 6;\qquad 7,\ 8,\ 9,\ 10,\ 11,\ 12,\ 16\ .
$$
I need a working definition of "rank" for the group
$\langle \mathcal Q\rangle$
generated by some set
$\mathcal Q$
of points. By chance, our curve
$E$
has no torsion points. This is a special case. For this case we know that
$E(\Bbb Q)$
is a (torsion-free) finitely generated free abelian group, the theorem of invariant factors gives the structure
$$E(\Bbb Q)\cong \Bbb Z^r\ ,$$
where
$r\ge 0$
is the rank, an integer. In particular
$\langle \mathcal Q\rangle$
is a (free) subgroup of rank
$\le r$
.
We may map injectively
$E(\Bbb Q)$
into
$\Bbb R^r\cong \Bbb Z^r\otimes_{\Bbb Z} \Bbb R$
.

The working definition i am using at an "elementary level" is:

Ad-hoc definition:
Let
$\mathcal Q$
be a set of points on
$E$
.
Let
$A$
be the group generated by the set
$\mathcal Q$
inside
$E(\Bbb Q)$
.
There is a map from
$A$
to some
$\Bbb R^r$
, where
$r=r(A)=r(\mathcal Q)$
is the number of "degrees of freedom" of the chosen set
$\mathcal Q$
.
Let
$\iota$
be this map. Then a
$\Bbb Z$
-linear relation in
$A$
, say
$s_1Q_1+s_2Q2+\dots$
with scalars
$s_1,s_2,\dots\in \Bbb Z$
and points
$Q_1,Q_2,\dots \in \mathcal Q$
is mapped into
a
$\Bbb Z$
-linear relation of
vectors
in
$\Bbb R^r$
.

This map
$\iota$
is a black box. We use it to obtain relations, when they are present.

Let us see how this works for our points
$Q_1,Q_2,Q_3,Q_4,Q_5,Q_6$
.
The "black box" is "indirectly" implemented in sage,
we have the scalar products
$(\iota(Q_j),\iota(Q_k))$
,
$1\le j,k\le 6$
.
We just call this height pairing matrix. We use
E
and
the points
Qs = [Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4, Q5, Q6]
introduced in advance.

sage: E.height_pairing_matrix(Qs)
[  3.58699551869278  0.920580254776707   2.03861260144042  0.950169743934381   1.22601583579364 -0.627802662475656]
[ 0.920580254776707   5.18374551924690  -3.25922614554427   1.25105208408066   1.92481519731440   1.00393911892592]
[  2.03861260144042  -3.25922614554427   7.16977951019958  -3.47591748290245  -3.52719018907160   1.87194076321488]
[ 0.950169743934381   1.25105208408066  -3.47591748290245   6.43254752483924   1.16857484475376  -3.17503514275617]
[  1.22601583579364   1.92481519731440  -3.52719018907160   1.16857484475376   6.41302200766230  -2.82839082755083]
[-0.627802662475656   1.00393911892592   1.87194076321488  -3.17503514275617  -2.82839082755083   3.50368254461646]
sage: E.height_pairing_matrix(Qs, precision=250).det()
-2.3182538441796383660823345384672746958547279867236193765331494118234883376e-69

To "guess" a
$\Bbb Z$
linear relation among the
$Q's$
, we "guess" one for the row (or column) vectors in
$\Bbb R^6$
.
The numerical computation of the determinant of the matrix is "close to zero", so we suppose there is such a linear relation.
In fact, we already have one such relation,
$Q_1+Q_6=-Q_{11}=Q_2+Q_3$
.
Let us still "check" this numerically for the corresponding row vectors:

sage: M = E.height_pairing_matrix(Qs)
sage: M1, M2, M3, M4, M5, M6 = M.rows()
sage: sage: M1 + M6 - M2 - M3
(-4.44089209850063e-16, 0.000000000000000, 0.000000000000000, -8.88178419700125e-16, 0.000000000000000, 4.44089209850063e-16)
sage: Q1 + Q6 - Q2 - Q3 == 0
True

We drop
$Q_6$
and ask for a
$\Bbb Z$
linear relation for
$\mathcal Q$
given by
$Q_1,Q_2,Q_3,Q_4,Q_5$
. We have a new matrix
$M$
, which is the corresponding
$5\times 5$
minor of the above height pairing matrix. We build the block matrix
$BM=[10^{30}M\ I_5]$
(with
$I_5$
being the
$5\times 5$
identity matrix), apply the LLL-algorithm on its version with rounded integer coefficients, and obtain the wanted relation. For short:

sage: M = E.height_pairing_matrix([Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4, Q5], precision=300)
sage: BM = block_matrix(1, 2, [10^30*M, ID])
sage: BM = matrix(5, 10, BM.list()).map_coefficients(lambda x: ZZ(round(x)), new_base_ring=ZZ)
sage: BM.LLL()[0]
(-1, 0, 0, 0, 1, -2, 1, 2, 1, 1)
sage: BM.LLL()[0][5:]
(-2, 1, 2, 1, 1)

We forget now everything about the height pairing matrix, we have obtained the (numeric, approximate) relation among the
$M$
-rows, and can check the corresponding relation for the points:

M = E.height_pairing_matrix([Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4, Q5])
M1, M2, M3, M4, M5 = M.rows()
-2*M1 + M2 + 2*M3 + M4 + M5
-2*Q1 + Q2 + 2*Q3 + Q4 + Q5

Explicitly:

sage: -2*M1 + M2 + 2*M3 + M4 + M5
(-4.44089209850063e-16, -4.44089209850063e-16, 0.000000000000000, 8.88178419700125e-16, 0.000000000000000)
sage: -2*Q1 + Q2 + 2*Q3 + Q4 + Q5 == 0
True

We keep thus only
Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4
, and the corresponding determinant is numerically far away from zero,

sage: E.height_pairing_matrix([Q1, Q2, Q3, Q4]).det()
143.841058564073

So we are no longer searching for a linear relation. Unfortunately, i have no idea how to show that this system is linear independent in the abelian group
$E(\Bbb Q)$
in an
elementary
manner.

This closes my comments to the first stated question.

Note:
We still have no clue if the subgroup
$\langle
Q_1,Q_2,Q_3,Q_4\rangle$
(equivalently the corresponding lattice in
$\Bbb R^4$
)
admits a proper subgroup inside
$E(\Bbb Q)$
(respectively a corresponding sublattice in
$\Bbb R^4$
in the image of
$\iota$
). It may happen (speaking at elementary level) that there is some
$R$
with
$2R\overset ?=Q_1$
or
$3R\overset ?=Q_1+Q_2+Q_3+Q_4$
or
$17R\overset?=3Q_1+7Q_2-5Q_3+144Q_4$
or something "like this". Why not? (Would for instance
$R$
in such a case be also integral?)

(In such a case we should replace one of
$Q_1,Q_2,Q_3,Q_4$
by
$R$
when searching for
generators
of
$E(\Bbb Q)$
.)

Plot for the OP points found after two steps:

Here we are using the OP points initialized as follows:

Rs = [E.point((x, ZZ(sqrt(x^3 + B))))
for x in [-1260, -1134, -210, 490, 840, 996, 1446, 3234, 3319, 20577816]]
R1, R2, R3, R4, R5, R6, R7, R8, R9, R10 = Rs

then plot them using the code from the
some lines
section, using the
zip(Rs[:9], [1..9])
(and not hoping to see more), and the
$R$
-labels.
Before we plot, we are searching for lines supporting three points among them. For instance,
are the points
R1, R3, R8
linearly dependent?

system = [R1, R3, R8]
M = E.height_pairing_matrix(system)
M.det()

and we obtain a "small value",
7.10542735760100e-15
, suggesting that there may be some dependency relation. To obtain it:

system = [R1, R3, R8]
n = len(system)    # 3, since the system contains three elements
M = E.height_pairing_matrix(system, precision=300)
ID = identity_matrix(n)
BM = block_matrix(1, 2, [10^30*M, ID])    # and the next lines rounds it and insures entries are in ZZ
BM = matrix(n, 2*n, BM.list()).map_coefficients(lambda x: ZZ(round(x)), new_base_ring=ZZ)

# now call the LLL algorithm, and pick the last n entries in the first (pythonically 0.th) line
BM.LLL()[0][n:]

This gives
(1, 1, 1)
so we expect $R_1+R_3+R_8=0$, and this is the case indeed:

sage: R1 + R3 + R8 == 0
True

The plot above was generated via:

")

Generators:
From the OP
Rs
above i will pick
R1, R2, R3, R4
, and add the point
S
below, the/one
$x$
-lift of
$126$
. Then the system
using these points has a height matrix determinant equal to the one for the sage generators, so they are generators as well.
Together with the sage data above we have:

S = E.lift_x(126)
gens = E.gens()
system = [R1, R2, R3, R4, S]
E.height_pairing_matrix(gens, precision=300).det()
E.height_pairing_matrix(system, precision=300).det()
