Are there known results or standard criteria for products of this form?

Is this example related to any known special functions or classical infinite products?

Let
$G$
be a directed graph that allows loops but not multiple edges. When is the spectral radius
$\rho$
(defined to be the maximum modulus across all eigenvalues of the adjacency matrix) greater than 1?

This stems from some work on the growth of monomial algebras and an old result of Ufnarovski suggests that
$\rho >1$
iff
$G$
contains two distinct cycles sharing a common vertex, but I can’t find any results in the literature.
answer:
If
$A$
is the adjacency matrix, with eigenvalues
$\lambda_1, \dots, \lambda_n$
, then
$\operatorname{tr}(A^k) = \lambda_1^k + \lambda_2^k + \dots + \lambda_n^k.$
If
$\rho \le 1$
, then this will remain bounded by
$n$
as
$k \to \infty$
. If
$\rho > 1$
, then it will grow exponentially.

(Fine print: if
$\rho>1$
and there are multiple eigenvalues with absolute value
$\rho$
, then their powers may sometimes cancel, so all we can say is that
$\operatorname{tr}(A^k)$
is exponentially large for infinitely many values of
$k$
. For more details, see the end of this answer.)

In the directed graph,
$\operatorname{tr}(A^k)$
counts the number of closed walks of length
$k$
, which is how we relate its growth back to graph theory.

Suppose that there is a vertex
$x$
contained in two cycles: one of length
$a$
and one of length
$b$
; without loss of generality,
$b\ge a$
. For any
$t$
, we can find
$2^t$
different closed walks starting and ending at
$x$
by walking all the way around one of these cycles,
$t$
times. These closed walks have length between
$at$
and
$bt$
, so by pigeonhole, there is some
$k \in [at, bt]$
such that at least
$\frac{2^t}{bt - at + 1}$
of these closed walks have length
$k$
; therefore
$\operatorname{tr}(A^k) \ge \frac{2^t}{bt - at + 1}$
. This grows exponentially, so
$\rho > 1$
.

Suppose each vertex of the graph is contained in at most one cycle. Then every closed walk is just following one of the cycles around multiple times: if a closed walk were to leave a cycle
$C$
and come back, it would give us a second cycle that intersects
$C$
. So for every vertex
$x$
and every length
$k$
, there is either
$0$
or
$1$
closed walk of length
$k$
starting at
$x$
(depending on whether
$x$
lies on a cycle whose length divides
$k$
). It follows that
$\operatorname{tr}(A^k) \le n$
, and so
$\rho \le 1$
.

For any future readers, I should emphasize that the graph is directed here, and this matters. The condition is different when the graph is undirected: in that case,
$\rho > 1$
if and only if there is a vertex of degree at least
$2$
. (This follows from the result here, since we can think of each undirected edge as a pair of directed edges in opposite directions. This pair of edges forms a directed
$2$
-cycle, so a vertex of degree
$\ge 2$
in the undirected graph lies on two cycles in the directed version.)

Finally, let me add an argument for why
$\operatorname{tr}(A^k)$
really should grow exponentially when
$\rho > 1$
.

For this, we consider the generating function
$$\operatorname{tr}(A) + \operatorname{tr}(A^2)x + \operatorname{tr}(A^3)x^2 + \dots = \sum_{i=1}^n \frac{\lambda_i}{1 - \lambda_i x}.$$
If we combine all these fractions into one term, it will be equal to
$-\frac{q'(x)}{q(x)}$
, where
$q(x) = (1 - \lambda_1 x)(1 - \lambda_2 x) \cdots (1 - \lambda_n x)$
. (It is more common to go the other way:
$q'(x)/q(x)$
is the
logarithmic derivative
of
$q(x)$
, the logarithmic derivative of a product is just the sum of the logarithmic derivatives of each factor, and
$\frac{(1 - \lambda_i x)'}{1 - \lambda_i x} = - \frac{\lambda_i}{1 - \lambda_i x}$
.)

The rational function
$-q'(x)/q(x)$
has a pole at
$1/\lambda_i$
for each nonzero eigenvalue
$\lambda_i$
. (This is true even if there is cancellation between
$q'(x)$
and
$q(x)$
: a root of
$q(x)$
with multiplicity
$m$
has multiplicity
$m-1$
in
$q'(x)$
, so it cannot entirely cancel.) Therefore its radius of convergence as a power series is
$1/\rho$
. Therefore the coefficients
$\operatorname{tr}(A^k)$
cannot always be bounded by a multiple of
$C^k$
for any
$C < \rho$
.

question:
Let
$G$
be a directed graph that allows loops but not multiple edges. When is the spectral radius
$\rho$
(defined to be the maximum modulus across all eigenvalues of the adjacency matrix) greater than 1?

This stems from some work on the growth of monomial algebras and an old result of Ufnarovski suggests that
$\rho >1$
iff
$G$
contains two distinct cycles sharing a common vertex, but I can’t find any results in the literature.
answer:
Let
$A$
be the adjacency matrix of a finite directed graph
$G$
(loops allowed, no multiple edges). Then
$\rho(A)>1$
if and only if
$G$
contains two distinct directed cycles sharing a vertex. Equivalently,
$\rho(A)\le 1$
if and only if every strongly connected component of
$G$
is either a single vertex with no loop, or a directed cycle.

Proof:
Reorder the vertices by strongly connected components. Then
$A$
becomes block upper triangular, with diagonal blocks
$A_1,\dots,A_r$
equal to the adjacency matrices of the strongly connected components of
$G$
. Hence
$\rho(A)=\max_i \rho(A_i)$
.
So it is enough to prove the claim for one strongly connected component at a time.

First assume a strongly connected component
$C$
contains no two distinct directed cycles sharing a vertex. I claim that every vertex of
$C$
has outdegree
$1$
inside
$C$
. Indeed, if some vertex
$v$
had two distinct outgoing edges
$v\to u$
and
$v\to w$
, then since
$C$
is strongly connected there is a directed path from
$u$
back to
$v$
and also a directed path from
$w$
back to
$v$
. These produce two distinct directed cycles through
$v$
, contradiction. Thus every vertex has outdegree
$1$
.

Now a finite directed graph in which every vertex has outdegree
$1$
is a disjoint union of directed cycles with rooted in-trees feeding into them. If such a graph is strongly connected, no in-tree can occur, so the whole component must be a single directed cycle. Therefore every strongly connected component with no shared-vertex pair of cycles is either a directed cycle, or a single vertex with no loop. Its adjacency matrix then has spectral radius
$1$
in the first case and
$0$
in the second. Hence if
$G$
has no two distinct directed cycles sharing a vertex, then
$\rho(A)\le 1$
.

Conversely, suppose
$G$
contains two distinct directed cycles sharing a vertex
$v$
. Let their lengths be
$r$
and
$s$
. Define two closed walks based at
$v$
:
$W_1:=C_1^s$
and
$W_2:=C_2^r$
.
Then
$W_1$
and
$W_2$
are distinct closed walks from
$v$
to
$v$
, both of the same length
$L:=rs$
.

For each binary word
$\varepsilon_1\cdots\varepsilon_m\in\{1,2\}^m$
, concatenate the corresponding blocks
$W_{\varepsilon_1}\cdots W_{\varepsilon_m}$
.
This gives a closed walk from
$v$
to
$v$
of length
$mL$
. Different binary words give different closed walks, because the walks differ on the first block where the words differ. Therefore
$(A^{mL})_{vv}\ge 2^m$
for every
$m\ge 1$
.

Now use the matrix norm
$\|\cdot\|_\infty$
(maximum row sum). Since
$A^{mL}$
is nonnegative:
$$(A^{mL})_{vv}\le \|A^{mL}\|_\infty$$
By Gelfand's formula,
$\rho(A)=\lim_{n\to\infty}\|A^n\|_\infty^{1/n}$
.
Hence:
$$\rho(A)\ge \limsup_{m\to\infty} \bigl((A^{mL})_{vv}\bigr)^{1/(mL)}
\ge \lim_{m\to\infty} (2^m)^{1/(mL)}
=2^{1/L}>1$$

Therefore
$\rho(A)>1$
.

question:
The concept of atom is quite standard in measure theory. Recall that if
$(\Omega, \Sigma)$
is a measurable space and
$\mu: \Sigma \to [0,+\infty]$
is a positive measure defined in said space, then a set
$A \in\Sigma$
is called an
atom
if
$\mu(A) > 0$
and for every measurable
$B \subseteq A$
, either
$\mu(B)=0$
or
$\mu(B)=\mu(A)$
.

Currently, I'm working on vector measures for my undergradute thesis and a question on this matter has surged to me. If you have a vector measure
$\mu: \Sigma \to X$
(that is,
$X$
is a Banach space and
$\mu$
satisfies that
$\mu(\emptyset)=0$
and it is countably additive), then can you give an analogous definition for atom in this case? That is, say
$A\in\Sigma$
is an atom for
$\mu$
if
$\mu(A)\neq 0$
(where
$0$
denotes the zero vector in the Banach space
$X$
) and for every measurable
$B \subseteq A$
either
$\mu(B) = 0$
or
$\mu(B)=\mu(A)$
?

At first glance, it feels pretty natural to do so, but, sometimes in Mathematics, what seems natural may not be the correct way to do something, so I want to solidly understand why this may or may not be the case. I haven't been able to find any definition for atom of a vector measure anywhere, so I'm not sure whether this definition is totally correct, or if it's even useful at all.

I would really appreciate it if someone could give some light on this matter, explaining how this definition may extend the one we use for positive measures or why some further definitions and results may not hold up if we define an atom for vector measures this way (this is just a hypothetical, it may be that the definition is purely correct and that this is the way to proceed. I, personally, have no idea).

Thank you very much.
answer:
Vector measures is a demanding topic for an undergraduate thesis. I would suggest you look into a standard book on the subject like Diestel,Uhl: Vector measures. It all depends on where you want to go.

Your definition is the right one. With this definition, the most celebrated result is a theorem of Lyapunov [1940]: Every non-atomic countably additive vector measure taking its values in a finite-dimensional Banach space has a convex compact range. This is no longer true for vector measures taking values in infinite-dimensional Banach spaces. It fact, it fails in every infinite-dimensional Banach space. Subtle things happen depending on the geometry of the infinite-dimensional Banach space. Much is connected with the so called Radon-Nikodym property.

So, to make a long story short, the definition is the right one but much of measure and integration theory depends on the Banach space in which the vector measure takes its values.

question:
Consider Pascal's triangle, without the
$1$
's, with
$n$
rows, with each number in a regular hexagonal cell.

Here is an example with
$n=4$
:

image source

For which values of
$n$
can we colour every cell either blue or yellow, so that the blue sum equals the yellow sum, and the blue and yellow regions are each connected?

$n=1,2,3$
are not possible, by (easy) exhaustive search.

$n=4$
is possible:

For
$n=5$
, the following attempt fails, because the blue region is not connected.

But
$n=5$
is possible (thanks to a comment by @anankElpis):

(Either
$10$
can be coloured blue, so the solution is not unique up to reflection.)

Remarks

The sum of the first
$n$
rows is
$S(n)=2^{n+2}-2n-4$
.

We exclude the
$1$
's because the sum of the first
$n$
rows with the
$1$
's is
$2^{n+1}-1$
, an odd number, which cannot be halved.

Expressions for the partial sums of a row of Pascal's triangle are
not simple
.

This question is a follow-up to my previous
question
. (The two questions are different: the previous question was a yes/no question, this question asks for a set of
$n$
-values.)
answer:
For all
$n \ge 5$
solutions exist which satisfy an additional condition: the bottom left number is blue and the bottom right number is yellow.

Once such a solution is found for any odd number of rows, it can be extended one row further by just coloring the first half of the last row blue, and the second half yellow.

But the next row after that is tricky because it cannot be split evenly: it has a central binomial coefficient
$\binom{2k}{k}$
in the middle.

Color that central cell blue along with everything to its left, and color everything else in that row yellow. This has given blue an unfair advantage, but we can compensate for it exactly by changing the color of
$\binom{2k-1}{k-1}$
from blue to yellow.

Here is an illustration of the coloring with
$12$
rows. You can see that in the rows beginning
$8$
,
$10$
, and
$12$
, the left half and the middle entry are blue, while the rest are yellow. In the rows beginning
$7$
,
$9$
, and
$11$
, not quite the entire left half is blue: we've recolored the last cell of the left half yellow to compensate. In the last row (beginning
$13$
) the colors are evenly split down the middle for now, but when we add the next row, we will change the blue
$1716$
to be yellow.

We can also see from the picture why both colors are connected. Ignoring the first
$4$
rows (which we can check manually) from each blue cell, you can get to the bottom left corner along other blue cells by going left and then diagonally down. From each yellow cell, you can get to the bottom left corner along other yellow cells by going right and then diagonally down.

question:
So given a short exact sequence of vector spaces $$0\longrightarrow U\longrightarrow V \longrightarrow W\longrightarrow 0$$  With linear transformations $S$ and $T$ from left to right in the non-trivial places.


I want to show that the corresponding sequence of duals is also exact, namely that $$0\longleftarrow U^*\longleftarrow V^* \longleftarrow W^*\longleftarrow 0$$


with functions $\circ S$ and $\circ T$ again from left to right in the non-trivial spots. So I'm a bit lost here. Namely, I'm not chasing with particular effectiveness. Certainly this "circle" notation is pretty suggestive, and I suspect that this is a generalization of the ordinary transpose, but I'm not entirely sure there either.

Any hints and tips are much appreciated.
answer:
The exactness of the first sequence means that $S$ is injective, $T$ surjective, and the range of $S$ meets the kernel of $T$ just the right way in $V$.

Okay, so to show that the second sequence is exact, we'll start by showing $\circ T$ is injective. Let $g,g'$ be elements of $W^{*}$. Suppose that $g(T) = g'(T)$. Since $T$ is surjective, for any $w \in W$ there is some $v \in V$ so that $T(v) = w$. Then $g(T(v)) = g'(T(v))$ so that $g(w) = g'(w)$, so that $g$ and $g'$ are the same on all elements of $W$, and hence are the same element of $W^*$.

Next, we'll show that $\circ S$ is surjective. Let $h$ be an arbitrary element of $U^*$. We want to produce an element $f \in V^*$ such that $f(S) = h$. We can define $f$ on the range of $S$, knowing that it can be extended to a linear functional on all of $V$. On the range of $S$, define $f$ to be $h(S^{-1})$. This makes sense, since $S$ is injective. Then $f(S) = h(S^{-1}(S)) = h$, proving surjectivity of $\circ S$.

I'll leave it to you to verify that $V^*$ splits as the range of $\circ T$ and the kernel of $\circ S$, using the techniques outlined in the prior steps.

question:
So given a short exact sequence of vector spaces $$0\longrightarrow U\longrightarrow V \longrightarrow W\longrightarrow 0$$  With linear transformations $S$ and $T$ from left to right in the non-trivial places.


I want to show that the corresponding sequence of duals is also exact, namely that $$0\longleftarrow U^*\longleftarrow V^* \longleftarrow W^*\longleftarrow 0$$


with functions $\circ S$ and $\circ T$ again from left to right in the non-trivial spots. So I'm a bit lost here. Namely, I'm not chasing with particular effectiveness. Certainly this "circle" notation is pretty suggestive, and I suspect that this is a generalization of the ordinary transpose, but I'm not entirely sure there either.

Any hints and tips are much appreciated.
answer:
You can actually prove a more general result: that if you have a sequence $U \xrightarrow{S} V \xrightarrow{T} W$ with the property that $\mathrm{Ker}(T) = \mathrm{Im}(S)$, i.e. the sequence is exact at $V$, then the dual sequence $W^* \xrightarrow{T^*} V^* \xrightarrow{S^*} U^*$ is also exact at $V^*$; that is, $\mathrm{Ker}(S^*) = \mathrm{Im}(T^*)$.

Indeed, if $f \in W^*$ and $u \in U$, then $$(S^* \circ T^*)(f)(u) = T^*(f)(S(u)) = f((T \circ S)(u)) = 0$$ because $T \circ S = 0$ by exactness of $V$. So $\mathrm{Im}(T^*) \subseteq \mathrm{Ker}(S^*)$.

On the other hand, suppose $f \in \mathrm{Ker}(S^*)$. This means for every $u \in U$, $S^*f(u) = f(S(u)) = 0$. We want a $g \in W^*$ so that $T^* g = f$. So define $g \in \mathrm{Im}(T)^*$ by $g(T(v)) = f(v)$. This is well-defined on $\mathrm{Im}(T) \subseteq W$, because if $T(v) = T(v')$ for $v, v' \in V$, then $T(v-v') = 0$, so $v-v' \in \mathrm{Ker}(T) = \mathrm{Im}(S)$, so there is a $u \in U$ so that $S(u) = v - v'$; but since $f \in \mathrm{Ker}(S^*)$, this means $g(T(v-v')) = f(v-v') = f(S(u)) = 0$, so $g(T(v)) = g(T(v'))$. Let $\tilde W$ be any subspace of $W$ so that $W = \mathrm{Im}(T) \oplus \tilde W$, and declare $g|_{\tilde W} = 0$. Then $g \in W^*$ satisfies $T^*g(v) = g(T(v)) = f(v)$ for all $v \in V$, so $T^* g = f$, so $\mathrm{Ker}(S^*) \subseteq \mathrm{Im}(T^*)$.

question:
So given a short exact sequence of vector spaces $$0\longrightarrow U\longrightarrow V \longrightarrow W\longrightarrow 0$$  With linear transformations $S$ and $T$ from left to right in the non-trivial places.


I want to show that the corresponding sequence of duals is also exact, namely that $$0\longleftarrow U^*\longleftarrow V^* \longleftarrow W^*\longleftarrow 0$$


with functions $\circ S$ and $\circ T$ again from left to right in the non-trivial spots. So I'm a bit lost here. Namely, I'm not chasing with particular effectiveness. Certainly this "circle" notation is pretty suggestive, and I suspect that this is a generalization of the ordinary transpose, but I'm not entirely sure there either.

Any hints and tips are much appreciated.
answer:
Suppose your vector spaces are all over some field $\Bbb{F}$. Now the functor $\textrm{Hom}(-,\Bbb{F})$ in general is only right exact. However because $\Bbb{F}$ as a module over itself is injective, the functor $\textrm{Hom}(-,\Bbb{F})$
is
exact and so we get the exact sequence

$$0 \longrightarrow \textrm{Hom}(W,\Bbb{F}) \longrightarrow \textrm{Hom}(V,\Bbb{F}) \longrightarrow \textrm{Hom}(U,\Bbb{F}) \longrightarrow 0.$$

Or you can notice that in the long exact sequence of Ext groups, the boundary map

$$\partial : \textrm{Hom}(U,\Bbb{F}) \rightarrow \textrm{Ext}^1_{\Bbb{F}}(W,\Bbb{F})$$

is actually the zero map because $\textrm{Ext}^1_{\Bbb{F}}(W,\Bbb{F}) = 0$. Hence $\textrm{Hom}(-,\Bbb{F})$ is an exact functor which completes the problem.

question:
If
$f(x) = x^m - a \in \mathbb{F}_p[x]$
, where
$\gcd(p,m) = 1$
then if
$b$
is any root of
$f(x)$
, then can we expect
$[\mathbb{F}_p(b):\mathbb{F}_p] \mid m$
? I mean, can we prove this, or is there a counterexample?

I was thinking like: If we assume
$\mathrm{ord}(a) = \tau$
and
$[\mathbb{F}_p(b):\mathbb{F}_p] = \ell$
. Now as
$p \equiv 1 \bmod{p-1}$
, then
$p^m \equiv 1 \bmod{m(p-1)}$
, now as
$\tau \mid p-1$
, so we have
$p^m \equiv 1 \bmod{m\tau},$
which implies
$\mathrm{ord}(p) \mid m$
in
$(\frac{\mathbb{Z}}{m\tau\mathbb{Z}})^{*}$
, that is
$\ell \mid m$
. But the problem is
$p^m \equiv 1 \bmod{m(p-1)}$
doesn't have any proof. Is it wrong, or is this the wrong way to approach it?

Any kind of help is appreciated. I am sorry if this question has already been answered. In such a case, it would be a great help if somebody could give the exact reference. Thank you in advance.

Edit: If b is any root of
$f(x)$
but
$b \notin \mathbb{F}_p$
. Then, what can we say?
answer:
No.

Possibly the simplest example is when
$p=5$
,
$m=3$
,
$a=2$
. The polynomial
$f(x)=x^3-2$
has a single root
$b_1=3$
in the prime field. This gives us
$$
f(x)=(x-3)(x^2+3x-1)
$$
as a factorization into irreducibles in the ring
$\Bbb{F}_5[x]$
.

Either of the roots
$b_2,b_3$
of the quadratic factor generate the quadratic extension, and
$2\nmid3$
.

In this case
$a$
has order
$\tau=4$
so
$m\tau=12$
. We see that
$p^m\not\equiv1\pmod{m\tau}$
. Indeed
$p^2=25\equiv1\pmod{12}$
, all in line with the original binomial having a degree two irreducible factor.

question:
The point
$P$
lies on the internal bisector of
$\angle BAC$
. The point
$D$
is the midpoint of
$BC$
and
$PD$
meets the external bisector of
$\angle BAC$
at
$E$
.

Prove that if
$F$
is the point such that
$PAEF$
is a rectangle then
$PF$
bisects (internally or externally)
$\angle BFC$
.

Because we are given that
$PAEF$
is a rectangle, we know its opposite sides are parallel, meaning
$PF$
is parallel to the external bisector
$AE$
, and
$EF$
is parallel to the internal bisector
$AP$
.
If we extend the line segment
$PF$
so that it intersects the lines
$AB$
and
$AC$
, this parallel relationship with the external bisector guarantees that the newly formed triangle with sides lying on
$AB$
and
$AC$
is isosceles. To complete the proof, I'd say apply Menelaus's Theorem or evaluate the cross-ratios of the collinear points
$P$
,
$D$
, and
$E$
to establish a specific geometric ratio. By using this ratio with the parallel lines created by the rectangular condition, we could directly demonstrate that the point
$F$
is equidistant from the sides of
$\angle BFC$
, which proves that the line
$PF$
perfectly bisects the angle.

Would this approach work, and if so how would I continue? And if not, is there a better way forward?

Thanks in advance.
answer:
It is good but since it is harsh to go further, I recommand this way:

Analytic Geometry (Set a coordinate system)

The problem has perpendicular lines, a rectangle, and a midpoint, the first idea comes from my mind is to set a coordinate system. By using coordinates, you can translate your parallel and perpendicular relationships directly into elegant algebraic equations, perfectly avoiding auxiliary lines(which is too complex). I'll given a try on this way, if there is anything wrong, please give me a remind in the comment.

So here is the proof using analytic geometry:

Let point
$A$
be the origin
$(0,0)$
of the system.

Set the internal bisector
$AP$
as the
$x$
-axis, and the external bisector
$AE$
as the
$y$
-axis. (Matches the right-angle of the rectangle
$PAEF$
, which was a convenience for us).

Let
$\angle BAC = 2\theta$
. Then the lines
$AB$
and
$AC$
are symmetric with respect to the
$x$
-axis, equations are:

Line
$AB$
:
$y = -x \tan \theta$
and Line
$AC$
:
$y = x \tan \theta$

Then let's determine the coordinates of the points

Let point
$P$
$P(p, 0)$
and point
$E$
$E(0, e)$
.(As they are on the axis)

Simply,
$F(p, e)$
.

Let point
$B$
lie on line
$AB$
, with coordinates
$(c_1, -c_1 \tan \theta)$
.

Similarly, let point
$C$
lie on line
$AC$
, with coordinates
$(c_2, c_2 \tan \theta)$
.

Given that
$D$
is the midpoint of
$BC$
, we find
$D$
:
$$D \left( \frac{c_1+c_2}{2}, \frac{c_2-c_1}{2} \tan \theta \right)$$

Since line
$PE$
has an
$x$
-intercept of
$p$
and a
$y$
-intercept of
$e$
, the equation of line
$PE$
is:

$$\frac{x}{p} + \frac{y}{e} = 1 \implies ex + py = pe$$

Since point
$D$
lies on line
$PE$
, substitute its coordinates into the equation:

$$e\left(\frac{c_1+c_2}{2}\right) + p\left(\frac{c_2-c_1}{2}\right) \tan \theta = pe$$

Multiply both sides by 2 to get the core condition equation:

$$e(c_1+c_2) + p(c_2-c_1) \tan \theta = 2pe \quad \text{--- (1)}$$

Notice that point
$P(p,0)$
and point
$F(p,e)$
share the same
$x$
-coordinate, which means line
$PF$
is a vertical line (equation is
$x=p$
).

To prove that a vertical line bisects
$\angle BFC$
, it is equivalent to proving that the slopes of lines
$FB$
and
$FC$
are additive inverses of each other, meaning
$m_{FB} + m_{FC} = 0$
.

Let's calculate the slopes of these two lines:

$$m_{FB} = \frac{-c_1 \tan \theta - e}{c_1 - p} = \frac{e + c_1 \tan \theta}{p - c_1}$$

$$m_{FC} = \frac{c_2 \tan \theta - e}{c_2 - p} = \frac{e - c_2 \tan \theta}{p - c_2}$$

Add the two slopes and set the sum to
$0$
:
$$\frac{e + c_1 \tan \theta}{p - c_1} + \frac{e - c_2 \tan \theta}{p - c_2} = 0$$

$$(e + c_1 \tan \theta)(p - c_2) + (e - c_2 \tan \theta)(p - c_1) = 0$$

Expand the brackets:
$$ep - ec_2 + p c_1 \tan \theta - c_1 c_2 \tan \theta + ep - ec_1 - p c_2 \tan \theta + c_1 c_2 \tan \theta = 0$$

Combine like terms:
$$2ep - e(c_1 + c_2) + p(c_1 - c_2) \tan \theta = 0$$

$$e(c_1 + c_2) + p(c_2 - c_1) \tan \theta = 2ep$$

This is identical to (1).

question:
The point
$P$
lies on the internal bisector of
$\angle BAC$
. The point
$D$
is the midpoint of
$BC$
and
$PD$
meets the external bisector of
$\angle BAC$
at
$E$
.

Prove that if
$F$
is the point such that
$PAEF$
is a rectangle then
$PF$
bisects (internally or externally)
$\angle BFC$
.

Because we are given that
$PAEF$
is a rectangle, we know its opposite sides are parallel, meaning
$PF$
is parallel to the external bisector
$AE$
, and
$EF$
is parallel to the internal bisector
$AP$
.
If we extend the line segment
$PF$
so that it intersects the lines
$AB$
and
$AC$
, this parallel relationship with the external bisector guarantees that the newly formed triangle with sides lying on
$AB$
and
$AC$
is isosceles. To complete the proof, I'd say apply Menelaus's Theorem or evaluate the cross-ratios of the collinear points
$P$
,
$D$
, and
$E$
to establish a specific geometric ratio. By using this ratio with the parallel lines created by the rectangular condition, we could directly demonstrate that the point
$F$
is equidistant from the sides of
$\angle BFC$
, which proves that the line
$PF$
perfectly bisects the angle.

Would this approach work, and if so how would I continue? And if not, is there a better way forward?

Thanks in advance.
answer:
This is a classic problem where
harmonic bundles
and
harmonic pencils
will come in handy. It also helps us make the proof more rigorous, as we don't have to divide the problems into many possible geometric configurations. However, to make the solution easier to follow, I will not explicitly use the properties of the harmonics. And for brevity, I will only prove for one such geometric case.

Let
$X$
and
$Y$
be the intersections of
$BC$
and the line
$FP$
and
$FE$
respectively. Let
$Z$
and
$T$
be the intersections of
$BC$
and the line
$AP$
and
$AE$
also.

Because
$AZ$
and
$AT$
are the interal and external bisectors of
$\angle BAC$
, we have the following proportions:
$$\frac{ZB}{ZC} = \frac{TB}{TC} \left(=\frac{AB}{AC}\right).$$

It's easy to see that if we let
$a = BC$
,
$BD = DC = \frac{a}{2}.$
Applying algebraic manipulation to the proportion:
$$
\begin{align*}
ZB\cdot TC &= TB\cdot ZC \\
\left(\frac{a}{2}-ZD\right)\cdot (a+TB)&=TB\cdot \left(\frac{a}{2} + ZD\right) \\
\frac{a^2}{2} - ZD\cdot a-ZD\cdot TB&=TB\cdot ZD \\
\frac{a^2}{4} &= ZD\left(\frac{a}{2} + TB\right) \\
DB^2 &= DZ \cdot DT.
\end{align*}
$$

Because
$PAEF$
is a rectangle,
$AP$
is parallel to
$EF$
and
$AE$
is parallel to
$PF$
. Therefore,
$$
\frac{DX}{DT} = \frac{DZ}{DY} \left(=\frac{DP}{DE}\right) \implies DZ \cdot DT = DX \cdot DY.
$$

Combining the two findings to get
$DC^2 = DX\cdot DY$
. By using a procedure similar to the reversal of the previous algebraic manipulation, we can get
$\frac{YC}{YB} = \frac{XC}{XB}$
.

From
$C$
, draw a line parallel to
$FP$
which intersects
$FE$
at
$M$
and
$FX$
at
$N$
. The new parallel line gives us the following ratios:
$$
\begin{cases}
\frac{YC}{YB} = \frac{MC}{FB} \\
\frac{XC}{XB} = \frac{NC}{FB}
\end{cases}.
$$

We already have
$\frac{YC}{YB} = \frac{XC}{XB}$
, which means
$C$
is the midpoint of
$MN$
.
$\angle MFN$
is a right angle (from the given rectangle) helps us deduce that
$CM = CF = CN$
. Since,
$\triangle MCF$
is isosceles,
$\angle CMF = \angle CFM$
. Alternate interiors of the parallel lines also gives
$\angle CMF = \angle MFB$
. Hence,
$FE$
is a bisector of
$\angle BFC$
. This will easily conclude that
$FP$
is the other one.

answer:
In the picture a a particular case is shown where P on the circumcircle of triangle BCF is the point where the bisector of angle BAC meets the bisector of BC, this means that PD is perpendicular bisector of BC.Now in triangle BCF E is the point where the perpendicular bisector of BC meets EF ,this means that EF must be the bisector of the angle BC F, since
$EF\bot PF$
, it deduces that PF must be the exterior bisector of angle BFC.

In picture b other case is shown where P is not on the
$c_1$
which is the circumcircle of triangle BCF but PF intersects
$c_1$
at H.In this case since
$HF\bot LF$
then LH is a diameter of
$c_1$
O. On the other hand the diameter LH passes through the midpoint D of the chord BC, this means is triangle BCFwe have that LH is the perpendicular bisector of BC which meets FL at L on the circumcircle
$c_1$
hence EF is the bisector of the angle BFC, so is PF which is perpendicular to it.

Similar reasoning is valid for the case P is inside the triangle, this case is shown in the picture c.

,

question:
The point
$P$
lies on the internal bisector of
$\angle BAC$
. The point
$D$
is the midpoint of
$BC$
and
$PD$
meets the external bisector of
$\angle BAC$
at
$E$
.

Prove that if
$F$
is the point such that
$PAEF$
is a rectangle then
$PF$
bisects (internally or externally)
$\angle BFC$
.

Because we are given that
$PAEF$
is a rectangle, we know its opposite sides are parallel, meaning
$PF$
is parallel to the external bisector
$AE$
, and
$EF$
is parallel to the internal bisector
$AP$
.
If we extend the line segment
$PF$
so that it intersects the lines
$AB$
and
$AC$
, this parallel relationship with the external bisector guarantees that the newly formed triangle with sides lying on
$AB$
and
$AC$
is isosceles. To complete the proof, I'd say apply Menelaus's Theorem or evaluate the cross-ratios of the collinear points
$P$
,
$D$
, and
$E$
to establish a specific geometric ratio. By using this ratio with the parallel lines created by the rectangular condition, we could directly demonstrate that the point
$F$
is equidistant from the sides of
$\angle BFC$
, which proves that the line
$PF$
perfectly bisects the angle.

Would this approach work, and if so how would I continue? And if not, is there a better way forward?

Thanks in advance.
answer:
Let
$\omega$
be the circumcircle of
$APFE$
. Let
$\omega$
intersect
$AB$
and
$AC$
at
$K\neq A$
and
$L\neq A$
, respectively. Let
$M$
be the center of
$\omega$
. Note that
$M$
is the midpoint of
$AF$
and
$EP$
. Let
$N$
be the reflection of
$F$
in
$D$
.

Since
$AP$
and
$AE$
are the internal bisector and external bisector of
$BAC$
, we have
$PK=PL$
and
$EK=EL$
. This shows that
$PE$
is the perpendicular bisector of
$KL$
. The dilation at
$F$
with scale
$2$
maps
$M$
to
$A$
and
$D$
to
$N$
. Thus
$AN\parallel DM$
. The line
$DM$
coincides with
$EP$
, hence
$AN\parallel EP$
and therefore
$AN \perp KL$
. Since
$AN$
contains the
$A$
-altitude of
$AKL$
and
$M$
is the circumcenter of
$AKL$
, we have that the lines
$AN$
and
$AM$
are isogonal in angle
$KAL$
. In other words,
$\angle BAN = \angle FAL$
.

Now, note that
$BFCN$
is a parallelogram, as
$D$
is the midpoint of both
$BC$
and
$FN$
. Let
$O$
be such that
$\overrightarrow{AO}=\overrightarrow{BF}=\overrightarrow{NC}$
. Then clearly
$\angle BAN = \angle FOC$
. Since
$\angle BAN = \angle FAC$
, we obtain
$\angle FAC = \angle FOC$
, thus
$A,F,C,O$
are concyclic. Therefore
$\angle ACF = \angle AOF$
. But
$AOFB$
is a parallelogram, hence
$\angle AOF = \angle FBA$
.

Now, a simple angle chasing finishes the proof: both
$\angle CFE$
and
$\angle EFB$
turn out to be equal to
$\pi-\angle BAP - \angle FBA = \pi - \angle PAC = \angle ACF$
. This follows easily from the fact that
$AP \parallel EF$
.

question:
I'm reading Achim Klenke's Probability Theory, Section 5.5.

The section starts by formalizing some properties the Poisson process should satisfy - namely, a family of
$\mathbb{N}_0$
(i.e.
$\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$
)-valued random variables
$(N_I, I \in \mathcal{I})$
(where
$\mathcal{I} := \{(a,b]: a,b \in [0,\infty), a \leq b\}$
) that satisfies properties (P1)-(P5). Specifically, (P1) is finite additivity:

(P1)
$N_{I \cup J} = N_I +N_J$
if
$I \cap J = \emptyset$
and
$I \cup J \in \mathcal{I}$
.

(P2) The distribution of
$N_I$
depends only on the length of
$I$
:
$\mathbf{P}_{N_I} = \mathbf{P}_{N_J}$
for all
$I,J \in \mathcal{I}$
with
$\ell(I) = \ell(J)$
.

(P3) If
$\mathcal{J} \subseteq \mathcal{I}$
with
$I \cap J = \emptyset$
forall
$I,J ∈ \mathcal{J}$
with
$I \neq J$
, then
$(N_J, J \in \mathcal{J})$
is an independent family.

(P4) For any
$I \in \mathcal{I}$
, wehave
$\mathbf{E}[N_I] < \infty$
.

(P5)
$\limsup_{\epsilon \downarrow 0} \epsilon^{-1} \mathbf{P}[N_\epsilon \geq 2]=0$
.

Later, the term "Poisson process" is defined. Specifically, we require that the map
$t \mapsto N_t$
is right continuous almost surely:

Definition 5.33 (Poisson process) A family
$(N_t, t \geq 0)$
of

$\mathbb{N}_0$
-valued random variables is called a
Poisson process

with intensity
$\alpha \geq 0$
if
$N_0 = 0$
and if:

(i) For any
$n \in \mathbb{N}$
and any choice of
$n + 1$
numbers
$0 =
> t_0 < t_1 < ... < t_n$
, the family
$(N_{t_i}-N_{t_i-1}, i = 1, ...,
> n)$
is independent.

(ii) For
$t > s \geq 0$
, the difference
$N_t - N_s$
is
Poisson-distributed with parameter
$\alpha(t −s)$
; that is,

$$\mathbf{P}[N_t −N_s = k] =e^{−\alpha(t−s)}\frac{(\alpha(t
> −s))^k}{k!}$$
for all
$k \in \mathbb{N}_0$
.


(iii) Almost surely the map
$t \mapsto N_t$
is monotone increasing and

right continuous.




Note that (iii) is added in
errata
.


Later a theorem states that a family is a Poisson process if and only if it satisfies (P1)-(P5). However, in the proof, (iii) is not explicit used/proved in either direction. The monotone part is easy - in one direction it's used to ensure the random variables are indeed
$\mathbb{N}_0$
-valued (i.e. never negative), and in the other direction it follows from finite additivity and non-negativity.


However, I struggle to see how right continuity comes into play. On one hand, I don't see why it's needed for proving (P1)-(P5), and on the other hand, I don't see how I can prove it from (P1)-(P5). But if (P1) is replaced with countable additivity, then both directions make sense. Essentially for each realization there is a Lebesgue–Stieltjes measure
$\nu$
such that
$\nu(I) = N_I$
. So should (P1) really be countable additivity?


Also, in (iii) the monotone condition is satisfied almost surely. Then random variables are
$\mathbb{N}_0$
-valued only almost surely. So are we really talking about a modification that satisfies the five properties?
answer:
I think the right continuity comes from the fact that
$\mathcal{I}$
consists of half-open intervals
$(a,b]$
that are closed on the right. The right closed interval
$(a,b]$
includes the jump at
$b$
while the complementary interval on the right (say
$(b,c]$
) does not contain that jump. Hence a jump at
$b$
gives rise to a left discontinuity but the sample path is right continuous. If on the other hand, we had chosen half open intervals
$[a,b)$
that are closed on the left, we would have got left continuity with the jump causing a right discontinuity. Note that non negativity or monotonicity ensures that any discontinuity is a simple jump.

This is perhaps easier to see if we define the Poisson process in terms of the arrival times
$T_k$
with exponential inter arrival times
$T_k-T_{k-1}$
. In this set up, if we define
$N_t$
as
$\sup \{k : T_k \le t\}$
, we would get jump discontinuity on the left and continuity on the right. The reverse would happen if we replace the inequality
$\le$
with a strict inequality
$\lt$
.

question:
I'm reading Achim Klenke's Probability Theory, Section 5.5.

The section starts by formalizing some properties the Poisson process should satisfy - namely, a family of
$\mathbb{N}_0$
(i.e.
$\mathbb{Z}_{\geq 0}$
)-valued random variables

(where

if

for all

with

with

of

-valued random variables is called a
Poisson process

with intensity
$\alpha \geq 0$
if
$N_0 = 0$
and if:

(i) For any
$n \in \mathbb{N}$
and any choice of
$n + 1$
numbers
$0 =
> t_0 < t_1 < ... < t_n$
, the family
$(N_{t_i}-N_{t_i-1}, i = 1, ...,
> n)$
is independent.

(ii) For

is
Poisson-distributed with parameter

; that is,

-valued (i.e. never negative), and in the other direction it follows from finite additivity and non-negativity.

But if (P1) is replaced with countable additivity, then both directions make sense. Essentially for each realization there is a Lebesgue–Stieltjes measure
$\nu$
such that
$\nu(I) = N_I$
. So should (P1) really be countable additivity?


Also, in (iii) the monotone condition is satisfied almost surely. Then random variables are
$\mathbb{N}_0$
-valued only almost surely. So are we really talking about a modification that satisfies the five properties?
answer:
Good observation. I think you are right. Here I recommend the following change that I shall call "change C," which I think is the change you were suggesting:

Change C:
Change P1 to "countably additive." Also, change property (iii) in the second definition to "monotone increasing and right-continuous surely" (instead of "almost surely").

[Additionally, we should also change the set
$\mathcal{I}$
to
$\mathcal{I}=\{(a,b]: 0\leq a<b\}$
, instead of
$a\leq b$
, since
$(a,a]$
does not make sense.]

[We may also need to formally allow
$[0,0]$
into the set
$\mathcal{I}$
and define
$N_{[0,0]}=0$
, but I will ignore the pesky case when
$t=0$
.]

Here is a justification of change C:

Existence:
Starting from time
$0$
, consider a sequence of (surely positive) inter-arrival times that are i.i.d. Exp-
$\lambda$
. Let
$N_t$
count the number of arrivals during
$[0,t]$
. Then
$N_t$
is countably additive and surely right-continuous. So Change C does not hinder existence or impact our usual understanding of a Poisson process.
Countable additivity implies (surely) right-continuous:
Suppose we have P1 modified to countable additivity. Fix
$t>0$
. We want to show that
$$\lim_{\delta\searrow 0} N_{(0,t+\delta]}=N_{(0,t]}$$
We know
$N_I$
is a nonnegative integer for all intervals
$I$
. We know
$$ (0,t+1] = (0,t] \cup \left(\cup_{i=1}^{\infty}\left(t+\frac{1}{i+1}, t+\frac{1}{i}\right]\right)$$
so by countable additivity we have
$$ \infty>N_{(0,t+1]} = N_{(0,t]} + \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_{(t+\frac{1}{i+1}, t+\frac{1}{i}]}  $$
It follows that
$N_{(t+\frac{1}{i+1},t+\frac{1}{i}]}$
is nonzero for at most finitely many
$i \in \{1, 2, 3, ...\}$
. Let
$i^*$
be a positive integer such that
$N_{(t+\frac{1}{i+1},t+\frac{1}{i}]}=0$
for all
$i\geq i^*$
. Then, again by countable additivity
$$N_{(0,t+\frac{1}{i^*}]}=N_{(0,t]}+\sum_{i=i^*}^{\infty}N_{(t+\frac{1}{i+1},t+\frac{1}{i}]}=N_{(0,t]}$$
Since
$N_u$
is nondecreasing in
$u$
, it follows that
$N_{t+\delta}=N_t$
for all sufficiently small
$\delta>0$
.
Finite additivity does not imply right-continuous:
The example you already gave in the comment to the other answer works: Consider the iid process of (surely positive) inter-arrival times that are iid Exp-
$\lambda$
. Define
$N_{(a,b]}$
as the number that arrive in the interval
$[a,b)$
. [This is intentionally mismatched to ensure
$N_{(0,t]}$
is not right-continuous.] We now show
$N_{(a,b]}$
satisfies the old properties P1-P5. Since it is clear that nothing changes about the distribution, it is only P1 that should be verified: Consider
$0<a<b<c$
.  Then
$$N_{(a,c]} = \mbox{Number of arrivals in $[a,c)$}$$
$$N_{(a,b]} = \mbox{Number of arrivals in $[a,b)$}$$
$$N_{(b,c]} =  \mbox{Number of arrivals in $[b,c)$}$$
and indeed the number of arrivals in
$[a,c)$
is equal to that of
$[a,b)$
plus that of
$[b,c)$
. On the other hand, this definition fails countable additivity (*see footnote below).

Existence:
Starting from time
$0$
, consider a sequence of (surely positive) inter-arrival times that are i.i.d. Exp-
$\lambda$
. Let
$N_t$
count the number of arrivals during
$[0,t]$
. Then
$N_t$
is countably additive and surely right-continuous. So Change C does not hinder existence or impact our usual understanding of a Poisson process.

Countable additivity implies (surely) right-continuous:
Suppose we have P1 modified to countable additivity. Fix
$t>0$
. We want to show that
$$\lim_{\delta\searrow 0} N_{(0,t+\delta]}=N_{(0,t]}$$
We know
$N_I$
is a nonnegative integer for all intervals
$I$
. We know
$$ (0,t+1] = (0,t] \cup \left(\cup_{i=1}^{\infty}\left(t+\frac{1}{i+1}, t+\frac{1}{i}\right]\right)$$
so by countable additivity we have
$$ \infty>N_{(0,t+1]} = N_{(0,t]} + \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_{(t+\frac{1}{i+1}, t+\frac{1}{i}]}  $$
It follows that
$N_{(t+\frac{1}{i+1},t+\frac{1}{i}]}$
is nonzero for at most finitely many
$i \in \{1, 2, 3, ...\}$
. Let
$i^*$
be a positive integer such that
$N_{(t+\frac{1}{i+1},t+\frac{1}{i}]}=0$
for all
$i\geq i^*$
. Then, again by countable additivity
$$N_{(0,t+\frac{1}{i^*}]}=N_{(0,t]}+\sum_{i=i^*}^{\infty}N_{(t+\frac{1}{i+1},t+\frac{1}{i}]}=N_{(0,t]}$$
Since
$N_u$
is nondecreasing in
$u$
, it follows that
$N_{t+\delta}=N_t$
for all sufficiently small
$\delta>0$
.

Finite additivity does not imply right-continuous:
The example you already gave in the comment to the other answer works: Consider the iid process of (surely positive) inter-arrival times that are iid Exp-
$\lambda$
. Define
$N_{(a,b]}$
as the number that arrive in the interval
$[a,b)$
. [This is intentionally mismatched to ensure
$N_{(0,t]}$
is not right-continuous.] We now show
$N_{(a,b]}$
satisfies the old properties P1-P5. Since it is clear that nothing changes about the distribution, it is only P1 that should be verified: Consider
$0<a<b<c$
.  Then
$$N_{(a,c]} = \mbox{Number of arrivals in $[a,c)$}$$
$$N_{(a,b]} = \mbox{Number of arrivals in $[a,b)$}$$
$$N_{(b,c]} =  \mbox{Number of arrivals in $[b,c)$}$$
and indeed the number of arrivals in
$[a,c)$
is equal to that of
$[a,b)$
plus that of
$[b,c)$
. On the other hand, this definition fails countable additivity (*see footnote below).

*This definition fails countable additivity because, if the first arrival comes at time 1 and the next at time 100 then
$$N_{(0,2]}= \mbox{Number of arrivals in [0, 2)}=1$$
$$N_{(0,1]} = \mbox{Number of arrivals in [0, 1)}=0$$
$$ \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} \underbrace{N_{(1+\frac{1}{i+1},1+\frac{1}{i}]}}_{0}=0$$
So
$$N_{(0,2]} =1\neq 0 = N_{(0,1]} + \sum_{i=1}^{\infty} N_{(1+\frac{1}{i+1},1+\frac{1}{i}]}$$

question:
The Riesz Lemma states that if
$X$
is a Banach space,
$Y$
a proper closed subspace, and
$1> \epsilon > 0$
, then there exists some unit vector
$v \in X$
so that
$d(v,Y)=inf_{y \in Y} ||v-y|| \geq 1-\epsilon$
.

Can we in fact take
$\epsilon=0$
and select a unit vector so that
$d(v,Y) = 1$
? Here has been my thinking:

Since
$Y$
is not dense in
$X$
,
$Y$
is not dense in the unit sphere of
$X$
. Therefore, we may find a unit vector
$v \in X \setminus Y$
with
$d(v,Y) > 0$
. Notice that since
$d(v,0)=1$
,
$d(v,Y)\leq 1$
.

Now,
$\langle v \rangle + Y$
is also a closed subspace of
$X$
. We may define a linear functional
$\phi: \langle v \rangle + Y \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$
via
$\phi(v) = 1, Y = ker \phi$
. Observe that
$||\phi ||_{op}=1$
[Edit: This is incorrect, and was the source of my error].

Since
$|\phi(x)| \leq ||x||$
for any
$x \in dom(\phi)$
, The Hahn Banach theorem then allows us to find a continuous extension
$\tilde \phi : X \rightarrow \mathbb{R}$
so that
$||\tilde \phi||_{op}=1$
. Then we may compute,

$$
d(v,Y) = \inf_{y \in Y} ||v-y||\\
= \inf_{y\in Y}||\tilde \phi||_{op}||v-y|| \\
\geq inf_{y \in Y}|\phi(v-y)|\\
= |\tilde \phi(v)| = 1
$$


This seems odd, however, since it seems to imply
$d(v,Y)=1$
for any unit
$v \notin Y$
, which I find unlikely.

If I have made an evident mistake, I would greatly appreciate it being pointed out.
answer:
You seem to have spotted an error in your own argument by yourself, so I will focus on answering the question and not pointing the mistake out. As you suspect, in general the answer is no. There are a number of useful perspectives on this question.

If
$Q: X \to X/Y$
denotes the quotient map, note that your Riesz lemma is one way of seeing that the operator norm of
$Q$
is exactly
$1$
(when maybe all that falls right out of the definitions is that
$\|Q\| \leq 1$
). Note also that for any
$v \in X$
one has
$$
d(v,Y) = \|Qv\|
$$
more or less by the definitions of all of these things.  So one way of rephrasing your question is "must the operator
$Q$
attain its norm" (i.e., must there be a unit
$v$
with
$\|Qv\| = \|Q\|$
).

The reason this might be a helpful rephrasing is that if you have studied Banach space theory for any length of time, you may already be aware that generally speaking
there are bounded operators on Banach spaces that do not attain their norms
and this might lead you to think that the answer to your question might be no (or at least to think that if the answer is yes, it will something particular to do with the fact that
$Q$
is a quotient map, or something particular to do with the space
$X$
and/or the subspace
$Y$
, and will not just fall out of the abstract theory of arbitrary bounded maps on Banach spaces).

Another helpful instructive set of examples would be the case where
$Y$
is a proper closed subspace of
$X$
of codimension
$1$
, or
equivalently
in the case where
$Y = \ker f$
for a bounded linear functional
$f$
on
$X$
.  In this case it is easy to see that
$$
d(v,Y) = \frac{|f(v)|}{\|f\|}, \qquad v \in X,
$$
(i.e., the quotient map
$Q$
onto
$\ker f$
has a nice expression in terms of
$f$
itself) and so that your question, specific to this type of
$X$
and
$Y$
, becomes the question of whether there must exist unit
$v$
with
$|f(v)| = \|f\|$
, i.e. the question of whether the linear functional
$f$
must attain its norm (or, if you ask this question for all such subspaces
$Y$
of
$X$
, whether an arbitrary linear functional on
$X$
must attain its norm).

This second perspective may be of interest for example because if you have studied Hilbert space theory, you may know (perhaps even from
another theorem often named after Riesz
) that bounded linear functionals on Hilbert space
do
attain their norms, so that provides a class of examples to your question where the answer would be yes.  But this perspective also points to a fruitful place to find counterexamples, and indeed it is not too hard to find examples of Banach spaces with bounded linear functionals on them that do not attain their norms.

This MSE answer
has one example for
$X$
the space of bounded and convergent sequences of complex numbers with the supremum norm (see
this MSE answer
for a discussion of why that space is a Banach space).

As another example for a linear functional that does not attain its norm, in the case
$X = \ell^1$
of summable complex sequences (with the
$\ell^1$
norm), consider
$f$
given by
$$
f(x) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{n}{n+1} x_n, \qquad x = (x_n)_{n=1}^{\infty} \in \ell^1.
$$
It is not hard to check that
$\|f\| = 1$
(if you know the usual identification of the
dual of
$\ell^1$
this follows immediately from the fact that
$\sup_{n \geq 1} \frac{n}{n+1} = 1$
) while
$f$
does not attain its norm.  Indeed if
$\|x\|_1 = 1$
we have
$$
|f(x)| \leq \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \frac{n}{n+1} |x_n| < \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} |x_n| = \|x\| = 1,
$$
where the strict inequality holds because
$\|x\| = 1$
implies that at least one of the terms
$|x_n|$
is nonzero (and for that term we have
$\frac{n}{n+1} |x_n| < |x_n|$
simply because
$\frac{n}{n+1} < 1$
).

As another example of a linear functional that does not attain its norm, take the case
$X = c_0$
of complex sequences with limit
$0$
, and the supremum norm, and
$f$
given by
$$
f(x) = \sum_{n=1}^{\infty} 2^{-n} x_n, \qquad x = (x_n)_{n=1}^{\infty} \in c_0.
$$
Once again it is easy to see that
$f$
has norm
$1$
but that no unit vector attains the norm of
$f$
(the argument is very similar to the one just given, e.g. if
$\|x\| = 1$
, then to have
$|f(x)| = 1$
one would need
$|x_n| = 1$
for all
$n$
which cannot happen for
$x \in c_0$
).

So we have exhibited at least three Banach spaces
$X$
the answer to this question is negative.

Far more deeply one has
James's theorem
which tells you that if
$X$
is a Banach space, then "every linear functional on
$X$
attains its norm" is equivalent to
$X$
being
reflexive
in the usual sense of Banach spaces. (As commenter user8675309 points out, that reflexivity implies the norm-attaining property for bounded linear functionals is an easy consequence of the Hahn-Banach theorem; the content of what people refer to as "James's theorem" is the other, and much harder, direction of the equivalence.) So if you know that Hilbert space is reflexive and that the other sequence spaces mentioned above are not, then James's theorem provides a very high-level "explanation" of why we were able to find such examples in these other spaces; indeed, the hard direction of James's theorem assures us that we can find examples of such functionals on
any
non-reflexive Banach space.

question:
From an interview question,

You play a game with a fair
$10$
-sided die labeled from
$0$
-
$9$
. Every time you roll, you add the number you rolled to your payoff. However, if you roll a
$0$
, the game ends and your payoff is
$0$
. You can roll however many times you want, and can choose to stop at anytime. What is the optimal strategy and expected payoff?

I would like to know how to answer this in that setting.

Here's what I have so far:

The strategy should be in the form of I keep rolling until my sum is some threshold, after which I stop rolling and take my payoff. If we let
$S$
be my current sum, the threshold occurs when I am indifferent to stopping the game now or rolling again. This means
$$S = \frac{9}{10}(S+5) + \frac{1}{10}(0)\implies S=45.$$


To calculate the expected payoff, we can estimate that you will need to survive
$9$
rounds to reach
$45$
(since each roll that is not
$0$
is on average
$5$
), so we either get to
$45$
or lose before
$45$
. The probability of surviving 9 rounds is
$$ 0.9^9 \approx 0.81^4 \cdot 0.9 \approx 0.65^2 \cdot 0.9 \approx 0.43 \cdot 0.9 \approx 0.4. $$
Thus, the expected payoff is roughly
$0.4 \cdot 45=18$
. However, this is not the solution the interviewer was looking for (kept asking if there was another way to calculate this), so I wonder if anyone has another way to quickly calculate the EV or any idea of what the interviewer may be looking for.


Followup questions:




If you are allowed to roll
$0$
twice before the game ends (you get another "life"), how would your threshold change?




Let
$d_i$
be the difference in EV between having
$i$
lives (the game ends the
$i^{th}$
time you roll a
$0$
) and
$i-1$
lives. Is
$d_3$
or
$d_2$
greater?






For followup 1., we can either roll our first
$0$
before reaching
$45$
, or roll it after. If we roll our first
$0$
before
$45$
, then we should follow the same strategy as the original question and stop once we reach
$45$
. If we don't roll a
$0$
by the time we reach
$45$
, we continue rolling until our first
$0$
, then we stop. So I guess the threshold doesn't change?


For followup 2., my intuition is that
$d_3$
is greater than
$d_2$
since the probability of extreme outcomes (you roll a long sequence of numbers without hitting a
$0$
) with
$3$
lives is relatively large compared to having
$2$
lives or
$1$
life, so those outliers will pull the EV of the
$3$
life case higher than the increase when jumping from
$1$
life to
$2$
.

I don't really have a logically sound argument for the second followup question (maybe it has something to do with the "correct" way to calculate EV in the original question), so wondering if anyone has any ideas how to tackle this (not looking for super rigorous or long answers that require advanced computation with DP, more of what could be done in an interview).
answer:
For the EV calculation I think your approach is reasonable. My calculator gives
$0.9^9 \approx 0.387$
and an EV your way a little closer to
$17$
. A spreadsheet could compute the chance of hitting
$45$
easily, but I believe that is out of bounds. One effect you are missing is that you can overshoot
$45$
as you can roll a large number on the last roll, which will improve the EV a bit. It can't be much but maybe you should mention it.

For followup 1 I think you have the right idea. I would state it as the threshold is 45 and have rolled one 0, which is a change from just 45.

I agree with your claim that
$d_3 \gt d_2$
. An informal way to see it is to say that for large
$i$
, when you are "sure" to get to
$45$
before rolling
$i-1\ 0'$
s,
$d_i-d_{i-1}$
is the expected total before a zero, so it is
$E=\frac 1{10}\cdot 0+\frac 9{10}(E+5)$
or
$45$
. It is approaching this from below, as runs that have
$i\ 0'$
s before
$45$
will return
$0$
, and these runs are becoming less and less probable.

You play a game with a fair
$10$
-sided die labeled from
$0$
-
$9$
. Every time you roll, you add the number you rolled to your payoff. However, if you roll a
$0$
, the game ends and your payoff is
$0$
. You can roll however many times you want, and can choose to stop at anytime. What is the optimal strategy and expected payoff?

I would like to know how to answer this in that setting.

$S$
be my current sum, the threshold occurs when I am indifferent to stopping the game now or rolling again. This means
$$S = \frac{9}{10}(S+5) + \frac{1}{10}(0)\implies S=45.$$

$9$
rounds to reach
$45$
(since each roll that is not
$0$
is on average
$5$

$45$

If you are allowed to roll
$0$
twice before the game ends (you get another "life"), how would your threshold change?




Let
$d_i$
be the difference in EV between having
$i$
lives (the game ends the
$i^{th}$
time you roll a
$0$
) and
$i-1$
lives. Is
$d_3$
or
$d_2$
greater?






For followup 1., we can either roll our first
$0$
before reaching
$45$
, or roll it after. If we roll our first
$0$
before
$45$
, then we should follow the same strategy as the original question and stop once we reach
$45$
. If we don't roll a
$0$
by the time we reach
$45$
, we continue rolling until our first
$0$
, then we stop. So I guess the threshold doesn't change?


For followup 2., my intuition is that
$d_3$
is greater than
$d_2$
since the probability of extreme outcomes (you roll a long sequence of numbers without hitting a
$0$
) with
$3$
lives is relatively large compared to having
$2$
lives or
$1$
life, so those outliers will pull the EV of the
$3$
life case higher than the increase when jumping from
$1$
life to
$2$
.

I don't really have a logically sound argument for the second followup question (maybe it has something to do with the "correct" way to calculate EV in the original question), so wondering if anyone has any ideas how to tackle this (not looking for super rigorous or long answers that require advanced computation with DP, more of what could be done in an interview).
answer:
A) The Interview Problem:

Your threshold argument is fine, and the estimate
$18$
is a fair answer in an interview imo. My guess is that the interviewer was not objecting to the idea, but was instead looking for a more exact way to justify it. Let
$V(s)$
denote the optimal expected payoff when the current sum is
$s$
. But first, there is a quick way to see why
$45$
is the
indifference point
.

Suppose we ignore stopping for a moment and
just keep rolling until ruin
starting from current sum
$s$
. Each nonzero roll contributes an average of
$$\frac{1+2+\cdots+9}{10}=\frac{45}{10}=4.5$$
and each round survives with probability
$0.9$
. So the expected
future
gain from continuing is

$$4.5+0.9(4.5)+0.9^2(4.5)+\cdots =\frac{4.5}{1-0.9} =45.$$

So if my current sum is
$s$
, then
continue forever until hitting
$0$
has expected value
$45$
more than where I am now, except that if I stop immediately I lock in
$s$
. So the threshold should occur at
$s=45$
: below
$45$
there is still positive expected gain in continuing, and above
$45$
there is not. That is the heuristic. To make it exact, plug this into Bellman's recurrence:
$$V(s)=\max\left\{\,s,\ \frac{1}{10}\sum_{k=1}^9 V(s+k)\right\}.$$

If
$s\ge 45$
, then one checks that stopping is optimal, so
$V(s)=s$
. For
$s<45$
, continuing is optimal, so
$V(s)=\frac{1}{10}\sum_{k=1}^9 V(s+k).$
Solving the recurrence backward (i.e.,
$V(45) = 45$
,
$V(44) = \frac{45+46+\dots+53}{10} = 44.1$
, etc.),

$$V(0) \approx 17.68$$

That'll be your optimal expected payoff.

B) Follow-up 1:

Intuitively, there is no indifference point in the usual sense: once we hit the first
$0$
, the game just reduces to the original problem with stopping threshold
$45$
, so we may as well keep rolling until then.

question:
This question already has answers here
:

$\mathbb{Z}_m \times \mathbb Z_n$ is cyclic if and only if $\gcd(m,n)=1$ [closed]

(1 answer)

When are the product of cyclic groups also cyclic? [duplicate]

(2 answers)

The product of finitely many cyclic groups is cyclic [closed]

(6 answers)

Cyclic Groups and Direct Product

(2 answers)

Product of two cyclic groups is cyclic iff their orders are co-prime

(4 answers)

Closed
last month
.

I am trying to prove that
$\mathbb{Z}_{ab} \cong \mathbb{Z}_{a} \times \mathbb{Z}_{b}$
when
$\gcd(a,b) = 1$
. Mainly, I was trying to prove that every
$\mathbb{Z}_n$
when
$n$
is not a prime power can be decomposed into a direct product of cyclic groups of the prime powers that are factors of
$n$
(I think these statements are the same). I was trying to represent the group
$\mathbb{Z}_{a} \times \mathbb{Z}_{b}$
as a row of two numbers
$(p,q)$
such that it can generate the entire group
$\mathbb{Z}_{a} \times \mathbb{Z}_{b}$
. Hence, the product is cyclic. Is this the right approach? Does proving this require something from number theory? I only know the basics of group theory.
answer:
Yes, these statements are essentially the same, and yes, your idea is on the right track. The cleanest proof uses the map
$\phi:\mathbb Z_{ab}\to \mathbb Z_a\times \mathbb Z_b$
given by
$\phi([x]_{ab})=([x]_a,[x]_b)$
.
This is a group homomorphism. To prove it is an isomorphism when
$\gcd(a,b)=1$
, it is enough to show it is injective and surjective.

For injectivity, suppose
$\phi([x]{ab})=(0,0)$
. Then
$x\equiv 0\pmod a$
and
$x\equiv 0\pmod b$
, so
$a\mid x$
and
$b\mid x$
. Since
$\gcd(a,b)=1$
, it follows that
$ab\mid x$
. Hence
$[x]{ab}=0$
in
$\mathbb Z_{ab}$
. So
$\ker\phi={0}$
, and
$\phi$
is injective.

For surjectivity, let
$([r]_a,[s]b)\in \mathbb Z_a\times \mathbb Z_b$
. Since
$\gcd(a,b)=1$
, Bézout gives integers
$u,v$
such that
$au+bv=1$
. Define
$x=sau+rbv$
.
Then modulo
$a$
we have:
$$x\equiv rbv\equiv r(1-au)\equiv r\pmod a$$
and modulo
$b$
we have:
$$x\equiv sau\equiv s(1-bv)\equiv s\pmod b$$
Thus
$\phi([x]{ab})=([r]_a,[s]_b)$
. So
$\phi$
is surjective. Therefore
$\phi$
is an isomorphism, so
$\mathbb Z_{ab}\cong \mathbb Z_a\times \mathbb Z_b$
whenever
$\gcd(a,b)=1$
.

This is exactly the simplest case of the Chinese remainder theorem. It does use a little number theory, but only Bézout’s identity: if
$\gcd(a,b)=1$
, then there exist integers
$u,v$
with
$au+bv=1$
.

Your generator idea also works. In
$\mathbb Z_a\times \mathbb Z_b$
, consider
$(1,1)$
. Its order is
$\operatorname{lcm}(a,b)$
, because the order of
$(1,1)$
is the least positive
$n$
such that
$n\equiv 0\pmod a$
and
$n\equiv 0\pmod b$
. Since
$\gcd(a,b)=1$
, we get
$\operatorname{lcm}(a,b)=ab$
. So
$(1,1)$
has order
$ab$
. But
$\mathbb Z_a\times \mathbb Z_b$
has exactly
$ab$
elements, hence it is cyclic, generated by
$(1,1)$
. Any cyclic group of order
$ab$
is isomorphic to
$\mathbb Z_{ab}$
. So this is also a correct proof.

From this you get the more general decomposition. If
$n=p_1^{e_1}\cdots p_k^{e_k}$
with distinct primes
$p_i$
, then repeated use of the above gives
$\mathbb Z_n\cong \mathbb Z_{p_1^{e_1}}\times\cdots\times \mathbb Z_{p_k^{e_k}}$
.
This works because the prime powers
$p_i^{e_i}$
are pairwise coprime. So yes, your intuition is correct: when
$n$
is not a prime power,
$\mathbb Z_n$
splits as a direct product of cyclic groups corresponding to the prime power factors of
$n$
.

(1 answer)

When are the product of cyclic groups also cyclic? [duplicate]

(2 answers)

(6 answers)

Cyclic Groups and Direct Product

(2 answers)
