\end{CD}$$
the morphisms
$f,g,h$
are isomorphisms, the upper row is an exact triangle if and only if the lower row is an exact triangle;


T3.
any morphism
$X\to Y$
can be completed to a diagram of the form
$X\to Y\to Z\to \Sigma X$
that is an exact triangle;


T4.
any diagram of the form
$X\xrightarrow {u} Y\xrightarrow {v}Z\xrightarrow {w}\Sigma X$
is an exact triangle if and only if the diagram
$$Y\xrightarrow {-v}Z\xrightarrow {-w}\Sigma X\xrightarrow {-\Sigma f}\Sigma Y$$
is an exact triangle;


T5.
any commutative diagram of the form
$$\require{AMScd}\begin{CD}
X @>u>> Y @>v>> Z @>w>>\Sigma X\\
@VVfV @VVgV @. @.\\
Y' @>-v'>> Z' @>-w'>> \Sigma X' @>-\Sigma u'>>\Sigma Y'
\end{CD},$$
where the rows are exact triangles, can be completed to a commutative diagram
$$\require{AMScd}\begin{CD}
X @>u>> Y @>v>> Z @>w>>\Sigma X\\
@VVfV @VVgV @VVhV @VV\Sigma fV\\
Y' @>-v'>> Z' @>-w'>> \Sigma X' @>-\Sigma u'>>\Sigma Y'
\end{CD},$$
choosing the morphism
$h$
in such a way that the diagram
$$X\oplus X'\xrightarrow {\begin{bmatrix} u & 0\\ f & u' \end{bmatrix}} Y\oplus Y'\xrightarrow{\begin{bmatrix} v & 0\\ g & v' \end{bmatrix}} Z\oplus Z'\xrightarrow{\begin{bmatrix} w & 0\\ h & w' \end{bmatrix}} \Sigma X\oplus \Sigma X'$$
is an exact triangle.


By the way, it seems to me that the axiom
T5
can be equivalently written as follows:


T5'.
any commutative diagram of the form
$$\require{AMScd}\begin{CD}
X @>u>> Y @>v>> Z @>w>>\Sigma X\\
@VVfV @VVgV @. @.\\
X' @>u'>> Y' @>v'>> Z' @>w'>>\Sigma X'
\end{CD},$$
where the rows are exact triangles, can be completed to a commutative diagram
$$\require{AMScd}\begin{CD}
X @>u>> Y @>v>> Z @>w>>\Sigma X\\
@VVfV @VVgV @VVhV @VV\Sigma fV\\
X' @>u'>> Y' @>v'>> Z' @>w'>>\Sigma X'
\end{CD},$$
choosing the morphism
$h$
in such a way that the diagram
$$X\oplus \Sigma^{-1}Z'\xrightarrow {\begin{bmatrix} u & 0\\ f & -\Sigma^{-1}w' \end{bmatrix}} Y\oplus X'\xrightarrow{\begin{bmatrix} v & 0\\ g & -u' \end{bmatrix}} Z\oplus Y'\xrightarrow{\begin{bmatrix} w & 0\\ h & -v' \end{bmatrix}} \Sigma X\oplus Z'$$
is an exact triangle.


Question.


My issue is with this sentence that I read on the course slides: given composable morphisms
$X\xrightarrow {u} Y\xrightarrow {g} Y'$
in a triangulated category
$\cal T$
, any diagram of the form
$$(*)\ \require{AMScd}\begin{CD}
X @>u>> Y @>v>> Z @>w>>\Sigma X\\
@VV1_XV @VVgV @. @.\\
X @>gu>> Y' @>v'>> Z' @>w'>>\Sigma X
\end{CD},$$
where the rows are exact triangles, can be completed to a commutative diagram
$$\require{AMScd}\begin{CD}
X @>u>> Y @>v>> Z @>w>>\Sigma X\\
@VV1_XV @VVgV @VVhV @VV1_{\Sigma X}V\\
X @>gu>> Y' @>v'>> Z' @>w'>>\Sigma X
\end{CD},$$
choosing the morphism
$h$
in such a way that the diagram
$$Y\oplus X\xrightarrow{\begin{bmatrix} -v & 0\\ g & gu \end{bmatrix}} Z\oplus Y'\xrightarrow{\begin{bmatrix} -w & 0\\ h & v' \end{bmatrix}} \Sigma X\oplus Z'\xrightarrow {\begin{bmatrix} -\Sigma u & 0\\ 1_{\Sigma X} & w' \end{bmatrix}}\Sigma Y\oplus \Sigma X$$
is an exact triangle.


More precisely, my issue is about the signs of
$g,h,1_{\Sigma X}$
in the latter exact triangle. In fact I would say that such exact triangle is obtained applying
T5'
to the diagram
$(*)$
, yielding a morphism
$h$
such that the diagram
$$X\oplus \Sigma ^{-1}Z'\xrightarrow {\begin{bmatrix} u & 0\\ 1_X & -\Sigma^{-1}w' \end{bmatrix}} Y\oplus X\xrightarrow{\begin{bmatrix} v & 0\\ g & -gu \end{bmatrix}} Z\oplus Y'\xrightarrow{\begin{bmatrix} w & 0\\ h & -v' \end{bmatrix}} \Sigma X\oplus Z'$$
is an exact triangle. Then one can apply
T4
to obtain the exact triangle
$$Y\oplus X\xrightarrow{\begin{bmatrix} -v & 0\\ -g & gu \end{bmatrix}} Z\oplus Y'\xrightarrow{\begin{bmatrix} -w & 0\\ -h & v' \end{bmatrix}} \Sigma X\oplus Z'\xrightarrow {\begin{bmatrix} -\Sigma u & 0\\ -1_{\Sigma X}& w' \end{bmatrix}}\Sigma Y\oplus \Sigma X.$$
However here the signs of
$g,h,1_{\Sigma X}$
are opposite to the signs obtained above by the teacher, and I do not see an isomorphism of diagrams between my triangle and the teacher's one.

Thanks in advance to anyone that will have the patience to read all this machinery, any suggestion is appreciated.
answer:
I think that the triple
$$\left[\begin{pmatrix}1_Y&0\\0&-1_X\end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix}1_Z&0\\0&-1_{Y'}\end{pmatrix}, \begin{pmatrix}1_{\Sigma X}&0\\0&-1_{Z'}\end{pmatrix}\right]$$
yields an isomorphism of triangles.

question:
Does a sequence meeting these 4 criteria exist?

$k_j \rightarrow + \infty$

$\sum_{j \in \mathbb {N}}{2^{-k_j}} \rightarrow + \infty$

$k_j - \log_2\left(\sum_{i<j} {k_i}\right) \rightarrow  + \infty$

$k_j$
is increasing

If you like some context this question is related to a variation of my earlier Math.SE question
"Encoding prefixes of infinite sequences"
where the size of the description of
$m$
is not only
$|m|$
but
$|m| + \mbox{log}_2(|m|)$
(taking into account the description of the lenght of
$m$
in its description) and trying to adapt the approach of Aurora.

edited: i added
$k_j$
increasing, not the most difficult condition but a necessary one for the related problem.
answer:
Note that
$$\sum_{k=1}^n \log k=n(\log n+O(1))$$
$$\sum_{k=3}^n \log \log k=n(\log n \log n+O(1))$$
$$\sum_{k= [e^3]+1}^n \log \log \log k=n(\log n \log n \log n+O(1))$$

Let
$c=\frac{1}{\log 2}$
and take
$$k_n=\log_2 n+\log_2 \log n+ \log_2 (\log \log n), \qquad n \ge [e^3]+1=21$$
and any initial segment that is positive increasing and less than
$k_{21}$

So
$$k_n=c (\log n+\log \log n+\log \log \log n), \qquad n \ge 21$$

We have
$k_n$
increasing to infinity (conditions
$1,4$
),
$$2^{k_n}=n (\log n)(\log \log n)$$
so condition
$2$
is satisfied

Now
$$K_n=\sum_{n=1}^nk_j=cn (\log n+\log \log n +\log \log \log n + O(1)) $$

So
$$\frac{2^{k_n}}{K_n}=\frac{n (\log n)(\log \log n)}{cn (\log n+\log \log n +\log \log \log n + O(1))}=\frac{\log \log n}{c(1+o(1))} \to \infty$$

Hence
$k_n - \log_2 K_n \to \infty$
and that is (slightly stronger than) condition
$3$

question:
This question already has answers here
:

Question on adjoint functors

(3 answers)

Closed
last month
.

Say we have two categories with an adjunction between them
$\mathcal{C}\overset{F}{\underset{G}{\leftrightarrows}}\mathcal{D}$
and
$F\dashv G$
. I want to show that if
$A\overset{f}{\to}B$
, in
$\mathcal{C}$
, is monic, then so is
$Gf$
. So I assume that
$U\overset{a}{\underset{b}{\rightrightarrows}}GA\overset{Gf}{\to}GB$
and make the following diagram of hom-sets:

Since
$f$
is monic, composition with
$f$
is injective on hom-sets. So, by the isomorphism, composition with
$Gf$
is injective on hom-sets. So
$Gf$
is monic.

This is my attempt at a proof, but I'm really unsure about whether the assumption 'injective on homsets
$\iff$
monic' holds. And the proof was so straightforward that I'm doubting whether I've missed something... is my proof correct? Could somebody provide a proof of my assumption? And is there a nicer/more standard proof that monics are preserved under right adjoints?
answer:
As mentioned in the comments, this proof is correct and personally I find it quite elegant.

Your "assumption" that postcomposition with
$f: A \to B$
is injective on hom-sets if and only if
$f$
is a monomorphism is also correct. It is just the definition of being a monomorphism: both mean precisely that given
$g,h: X \to A$
we have that
$fh = fg$
implies
$h = g$
.

Finally, you asked about other proofs of this fact. You could use that right adjoints preserve limits and the fact that an arrow
$f$
is a monomorphism if and only if the square below is a pullback square (exercise).
$$
\require{AMScd}
\begin{CD}
A @>{id_A}>> A\\
@V{id_A}VV @VV{f}V \\
A @>>{f}> B
\end{CD}
$$
However, this is less elementary than your proof and I am not sure I would call it 'nicer'.

question:
I know how to reduce
$I_2 $
to
$I_1 $
specifically, as given below

\begin{align}
&\int\frac{p^2-1}{(1+p\cos{\theta})^2} d\theta\\
=&\ \int d\left(\frac{p\sin\theta}{1+p\cos{\theta}}\right)-\int\frac{1}{1+p\cos{\theta}} d\theta \\
=&\ \frac{p\sin x}{1+p\cos{x}}-\int\frac{1}{1+p\cos{\theta}} d\theta
\end{align}

But, how to find the reduction formula for

$$I_n =\int\frac{1}{(1+p\cos{\theta})^n} d\theta$$
answer:
Define a helper integral:
$$V_n = \int \frac{\sin^2\theta}{(1+p\cos\theta)^n} d\theta$$
Let
$u = 1+p\cos\theta$
. The integral becomes:
$$V_n = \int \left( \frac{p^2-1}{p^2 u^n} + \frac{2}{p^2 u^{n-1}} - \frac{1}{p^2 u^{n-2}} \right) d\theta$$
So,
$$V_n = \frac{p^2-1}{p^2}I_n + \frac{2}{p^2}I_{n-1} - \frac{1}{p^2}I_{n-2}$$
Now we can apply integration by parts on
$V_n$
and then use the same trick again.
$$V_n = \frac{\sin\theta}{p(n-1)(1+p\cos\theta)^{n-1}} - \int \frac{\cos\theta}{p(n-1)(1+p\cos\theta)^{n-1}} d\theta$$
Let
$u = 1+p\cos\theta$
again. The remaining integral becomes:
$$\frac{1}{p^2(n-1)} \int \left( \frac{1}{u^{n-2}} - \frac{1}{u^{n-1}} \right) d\theta=\frac{1}{p^2(n-1)} (I_{n-2} - I_{n-1})$$
Thus,
$$V_n = \frac{\sin\theta}{p(n-1)(1+p\cos\theta)^{n-1}} - \frac{1}{p^2(n-1)}I_{n-2} + \frac{1}{p^2(n-1)}I_{n-1}$$
Set the two equations for
$V_n$
equal to each other, and simplify to find:
$$(p^2-1)(n-1)I_n = \frac{p\sin\theta}{(1+p\cos\theta)^{n-1}} - (2n-3)I_{n-1} + (n-2)I_{n-2}$$

question:
I am trying to analyze the asymptotic behaviour of the improper integral in the limit
$r \to 0$
:

$$
f(r;\rho,\alpha) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{\rho r}}\int_0^\infty
(1-\tanh(\pi \nu)\tanh(\alpha \nu) )\, P_{i \nu - \frac{1}{2}} \left( c \right) \mathrm{d}\nu \, ,
$$

where

$$
c = \frac{r^2 + \rho^2}{2 \rho r} \, .
$$

Here,
$r\ge 0$
, and the parameters
$\rho$
and
$\alpha$
are defined in the ranges
$\rho\in[0,\infty)$
and
$\alpha\in(0,\pi/2]$
.


To make analytical progress, I use the integral representation of the Legendre function

$$
P_{i \nu - \frac{1}{2}} \left( c \right) = \frac{\sqrt{2}}{\pi} \, \coth(\pi\nu)
\int_{\operatorname{arccosh}c}^\infty \frac{\sin(\nu s)}{\sqrt{\cosh s - c}} \, \mathrm{d}s \, .
$$


Accordingly, the function of interest can be expressed in terms of a double improper integral of the form

$$
f(r;\rho,\alpha)= \frac{2}{\pi}\int_0^\infty \int_{\operatorname{arccosh}c}^\infty
\frac{\sin(s \nu) \bigl(\coth(\pi \nu) - \tanh(\alpha \nu)\bigr)}{\sqrt{2 \rho r \cosh s  - \rho^2 - r^2}} \, \mathrm{d}s\, \mathrm{d}\nu \, .
$$


By expanding the expression in a series around
$r=0$
, the last equation becomes purely imaginary. I am unsure how to proceed from here. Any suggestions would be highly appreciated. Thank you!
answer:
I would substitute
$s$
with a new variable
$u$
such that
$$\cosh(s) = c \cdot u$$
$$\mathrm{d}s = \frac{c}{\sqrt{c^2 u^2 - 1}} \, \mathrm{d}u$$
The inner integral is now:
$$
\int_1^\infty \frac{\sin(\nu \operatorname{arccosh}(cu))}{\sqrt{r^2 + \rho^2}\sqrt{u - 1}} \frac{c}{\sqrt{c^2 u^2 - 1}} \, \mathrm{d}u
$$
As
$r \to 0$
and
$c \to \infty$
,
$$\frac{c}{\sqrt{c^2 u^2 - 1}}\to \frac{1}{u}$$
$$\operatorname{arccosh}(cu) \to \ln(2c) + \ln(u)$$

Substituting back:
$$\int_1^\infty \frac{\sin\bigl(\nu \ln(2c) + \nu \ln u\bigr)}{u \sqrt{u - 1}} \, \mathrm{d}u$$
$$= \operatorname{Im} \left[ e^{i\nu \ln(2c)} \int_1^\infty \frac{u^{i\nu}}{u \sqrt{u - 1}} \, \mathrm{d}u \right]$$
This integral is a standard form of the Beta function and can be simplified to:
$$=\operatorname{Im} \left[ e^{i\nu \ln(2c)}\sqrt{\pi} \frac{\Gamma\left(\frac{1}{2} - i\nu\right)}{\Gamma(1 - i\nu)} \right]$$
Back to the original function:
$$\frac{2}{\pi \rho} \, \text{Im} \int_0^\infty \bigl(\coth(\pi \nu) - \tanh(\alpha \nu)\bigr) \frac{\sqrt{\pi} \, \Gamma\left(i\nu + \frac{1}{2}\right)}{\Gamma(i\nu + 1)} e^{i\nu \ln(2c)} \, \mathrm{d}\nu$$
Notice the
$e^{i\nu\ln(2c)}$
term is highly oscillatory. According to the Riemann-Lebesgue lemma, the dominant contribution to this integral comes entirely from the singularities of the non-oscillatory part of the integrand. The only singularity on the real axis is a simple pole at
$\nu = 0$
. As
$\nu \to 0$
,
$$\coth(\pi \nu) - \tanh(\alpha \nu) \to \frac{1}{\pi \nu}$$
$$\frac{\sqrt{\pi} \, \Gamma\left(i\nu + \frac{1}{2}\right)}{\Gamma(i\nu + 1)} \to\pi$$
And we're left with
$$\frac{2}{\pi \rho} \, \int_0^\infty  \frac{\sin(\nu \ln(2c))}{\nu} \, \mathrm{d}\nu = 1/\rho$$
This is the leading term and it continues
$$f(r, \rho, \alpha, \theta) \sim \frac{1}{\rho} - \frac{2 \sqrt{\pi}\Gamma\left(\frac{1}{2} + \frac{\pi}{2\alpha}\right)}{\rho \alpha  \Gamma\left(1 + \frac{\pi}{2\alpha}\right)} \left(\frac{r}{\rho}\right)^{\frac{\pi}{2\alpha}} + \dots \quad \text{as } r \to 0$$
The next term would require evaluating the next pole in the complex plane.

question:
Around two weeks ago, someone posted (since closed and deleted, so I can't find it to reference it properly) asking about the iterative sequence:
$a_{n+1}=|a_n-\text{rot}(a_n)|$
, when the function
$\text{rot}(x)$
is a function that rotates the digits of
$x$
in base 10, while omitting leading zeroes. For example,
$\text{rot}(12345)=23451$
. The poster noticed two behaviors, either reaching
$0$
(and then entering a trivial loop) or forming a nontrivial loop, for example
$142857, 285714, 142857, ...$
.

The poster asked both if there were other possible behaviors and if there was a rule to predict which behavior the sequence beginning with
$a_0$
will have. One of the commenters pointed out that
$a_{n+1}$
cannot have more digits than
$a_n$
, and therefore after
$~10^k$
numbers in the sequence (where
$k$
is the number of digits in
$a_0$
), the sequence must either reach
$0$
or form a loop. The question was closed and deleted before anyone addressed the question regarding predicting which numbers result in a loop.

In this vein, I want to present a generalized version of the rot function
$f(x;b,d)$
. To calculate this, first write
$x$
in base
$b$
. Then, if
$d$
is positive, rotate the first digit of
$x$
to the end of
$x$

$d$
times, and if
$d$
is negative, rotate the last digit of
$x$
to the front of
$x$

$-d$
times. For example, to calculate
$f(19;3,2)$
, first write 19 in base 3:
$19=201_3$
. Then rotate the first digit twice:
$201\rightarrow012=12\rightarrow21$
. Therefore,
$f(19,3,2)=21_3=7$
. Using this function, we define the sequence
$z_{n+1}=|z_n-f(z_n;b,d)|$
.

This leads to the general question: What patterns can be observed based on the parameters
$b,d$
and the initial seeding value
$z_0$
?

First, as in the case from the original question, regardless of
$b,d$
, all
$z_0$
must clearly either loop or converge to
$0$
, because
$z_{n+1}$
cannot have more digits in base
$b$
than
$z_n$
. Second, because the function
$f$
does not change the digit sum in base
$b$
,
$z_{n>0}$
must be a multiple of
$b-1$
. Trivially, if
$d=0$
, then
$z_1=0$
regardless of
$b,z_0$
.

There are three possible cases of
$f(z_n;b,d)$
:
$f(z_n;b,d)$
has fewer digits in base
$b$
than
$z_n$
,
$f(z_n;b,d)$
has the same number of digits in base
$b$
as
$z_n$
with the same leading digit, or
$f(z_n;b,d)$
has the same number of digits in base
$b$
as
$z_n$
with a different leading digit. In each of the first two cases,
$z_{n+1}<z_n$
, which means that for
$b=2$
, in which the third case is impossible, all
$z_0$
must converge to
$0$
regardless of
$d$
.

In order to form a loop, this third case must occur, but this is not a sufficient condition. In order to have a loop, at least once in the loop,
$z_{n+1}\geq z_n$
. Because
$z_{n+1}=|z_n-f(z_n;b,d)|$
, having
$z_{z+1}\geq z_n$
requires
$f(z_n;b,d)\geq2z_n$
. Although I have not been able to prove it, it appears this cannot occur for
$b=3$
.

Specific questions:

Can anyone prove that the loop cannot occur for
$b=3$
?

What is the smallest
$b$
for which a nontrivial loop is possible?

Is the answer for 2 different for positive and negative
$d$
?
answer:
Consider
$x = 123_4 = 27$
with
$d = -1$
. Then we rotate the digits of
$x$
right one place to get
$312_4 = 54$
. The absolute difference is
$123_4 = 27$
which is where we started. This is a non-trivial loop for
$b = 4$
. Since all the digits are non-zero, I could equally have used
$d = 2$
.

For
$b = 3$
, consider
$1012_3 = 32$
with
$d = -1$
. Then we rotate the digits of
$x$
right one place to get
$2101_3 = 64$
. The absolute difference is
$1012_3 = 32$
which is where we started. This is a non-trivial loop for
$b = 3$
. In this case however, it doesn't equate to a loop with positive
$d$
because the zero digit causes truncation.

I don't know yet if there's a loop for
$b = 3$
and
$d > 0$
.

question:
I've checked that the equality
$\text{tr}\left(ad_{x}ad_{y}ad_{z}\right)=-\text{tr}\left(ad_{x}ad_{z}ad_{y}\right)$
holds for Lie algebras up to dimension 4; I also proved that it holds for semisimple Lie algebras, as with
$K:L\to L^{*}$
the invertible linear map corresponding to the killing form, we have
$$\text{tr}\left(ad_{x}ad_{z}ad_{y}\right)=\text{tr}\left(K^{-1}Kad_{x}K^{-1}Kad_{z}K^{-1}Kad_{y}\right)=\text{tr}\left(Kad_{z}K^{-1}Kad_{y}K^{-1}Kad_{x}K^{-1}\right)=\text{tr}\left(\tilde{ad}_{z}\tilde{ad}_{y}\tilde{ad}_{x}\right)=-\text{tr}\left(ad_{x}ad_{y}ad_{z}\right),$$
where
$\tilde{ad}$
is the dual of the adjoint rep,
$\tilde{ad}_{x}=-\left(ad_{x}\right)^{T}$
, using
$Kad_{x}=\tilde{ad}_{x}K$
. I am doubtful this fact holds for any Lie algebra, so I was hoping someone could find a counterexample (ideally finite dimensional, over
$\mathbb{C}$
, etc.).
answer:
Take any Lie algebra where there is a vector
$v$
such that
$ad(v)^3$
has nonzero trace. Then take
$x=y=z=v$
. For example, let
$L$
be the Lie algebra of dimension
$5$
, given by
$$
[e_1,e_2]=-e_3+e_5, [e_1,e_4]=e_1-e_2, [e_2,e_4]=e_1+e_2, [e_3,e_4]=2e_3-2e_5.
$$
Then
$ad(e_4)^3$
has trace
$-4$
, which is different from
$4$
, so we are done.

Actually, the
$2$
-dimensional nonabelian Lie algebra is already a counterexample, with
$[x,y]=x$
, because
$ad(y)^3$
has nonzero trace.

question:
Closed.
This question does not meet
Mathematics Stack Exchange guidelines
. It is not currently accepting answers.

Please
provide additional context
, which ideally explains why the question is relevant to you and our community. Some forms of context include: background and motivation, relevant definitions, source, possible strategies, your current progress, why the question is interesting or important, etc.

Closed
last month
.

Improve this question

Let
$g: L \to M$
be a linear mapping. Assume
$\dim L, \dim M <\infty$
. Does there even exist any canonical isomorphism from
$\ker g^* \to \text{coker} g$
? In my proof, I was trying to first define the function from
$M^* \to M/\text{im} g$
, but I couldn't find any natural definition for this.

Edit: There was a typo coker instead of coim?
$\text{coker} g= M/\text{im} g$
.
answer:
There is no canonoical isomorphism beteween
$\ker(g^*)$
and
$\operatorname{coker}g$
.

There is, however, a canonical isomorphism between
$\ker(g^*)$
and
$(\operatorname{coker}g)^*$
. This map can be extended to a non-canonical isomorphism between
$\ker(g^*)$
and
$\operatorname{coker}g$
using the isomorphism between
$\operatorname{coker}g$
and its own dual.

We can understand the isomorphism between
$\ker(g^*)$
and
$(\operatorname{coker}g)^*$
as follows: to begin, note that
$\ker(g^*)$
is the annihilator of
$\text{im}(g)$
. That is,
$$
\ker(g^*) = (\text{im}(g))^0 = \{\varphi \in M^*: \varphi (y) = 0 \text{ for all } y \in \text{im}(g)\}.
$$
Now, let
$\pi : M \to M/\text{im}(g)$
denote the canonical projection map, noting that
$M/\text{im}(g) = \text{coker}(g)$
. Just as
$\pi$
is surjective, so is its dual
$\pi^*:\text{coker}(g)^* \to M^*$
injective.

Intuitively,
$\pi^*$
takes a functional over
$M/\text{im}(g)$
and "extends" it to a functional on
$M$
that is zero over
$\text{im}(g)$
.

The key from there is to show that
$\text{im}(\pi^*) = \ker(g^*)$
, so that
$$
(\text{coker}(g))^* \cong \text{im}(\pi^*) = (\text{im}(g))^0 = \ker(g^*)
$$
follows from injectivity.

It's easy to see that
$\text{im}(\pi^*) \subseteq (\text{im}(g))^0$
: for any
$\alpha \in \text{coker}(g)$
and
$y \in \text{im}(g)$
,
$$
[\pi^*(\alpha)](y)  = \alpha (\pi(y)) = \alpha(y + \text{im}(g)) = \alpha(0 + \text{im}(g)) = 0.
$$
For
$\text{im}(\pi^*) \supseteq (\text{im}(g))^0$
, the universal property of quotients means that for each
$\varphi \in (\text{im}(g))^0$
, there is a unique map
$\tilde \varphi: M/\text{im}(g) \to \Bbb F$
such that
$\varphi = \tilde \varphi \circ p$
. That is, there is a unique
$\tilde \varphi \in \text{coker}(g)$
such that
$p^*(\tilde \varphi) = \varphi$
.

question:
This is from Problem
$8$
, Chapter II of
Rudin's
Real and Complex Analysis
.

The problem asks for a Borel set
$M \subset \mathbb{R}$
such that for any interval
$I \subset \mathbb{R}$
, the intersection
$M \cap I$
satisfies

$$
0 < m(M \cap I) < m(I),
$$


where
$m$
denotes Lebesgue measure.


I was thinking of taking the Cantor set approach: write
$\mathbb{R}$
as the union of intervals
$[a,b]$
with
$a$
and
$b$
rational, and for each such
$[a,b]$
, construct a Cantor-like set inside it. During the construction of each Cantor set, to ensure it has positive measure, we remove smaller and smaller open intervals at each step—namely, the total length removed becomes small and the remaining set is dense near its endpoints.


As a result, these Cantor sets are extremely “dense” near the ends of their respective intervals. If, for a given interval
$I$
, it intersects such a Cantor set contained in some
$[a,b]$
with
$b - a \gg m(I)$
, we expect that
$m(M \cap I)$
may be very close to
$m(I)$
, and hence we lose control over the upper bound in the inequality above.

Is there any way to fix this approach, or should I consider a different construction altogether?

Thank you.
answer:
Instead of trying to understand your approach (which sounds complicated), let me tell you how I'd do it. I guess you know how to construct a closed nowhere dense set of positive measure inside a given interval, right? Enumerate all the rational intervals in a sequence $I_1,I_2,I_3,\dots$. Now construct an infinite sequence $M_1,N_1,M_2,N_2,M_3,N_3,\dots$ of pairwise disjoint closed nowhere dense sets of positive measure, with $M_k,N_k\subset I_k$. [1] The $F_\sigma$-set $M=M_1\cup M_2\cup M_3\cup\cdots$ does what you want. [2]

[1] Note that, at each step of the construction, you have constructed a finite number of closed nowhere dense sets, whose union is therefore a closed nowhere dense set. Hence the interval $I_k$ you are currently working in will contain a subinterval which is disjoint from that nowhere dense set. Construct the next closed nowhere dense set inside that subinterval.

[2] Any interval $I$ contains some rational interval $I_k$. Since $N_k\subseteq I_k\subseteq I$ and $N_k\cap M=\emptyset$, it follows that $M\cap I\subseteq I\setminus N_k$ and $m(M\cap I)\le m(I\setminus N_k)=m(I)-m(N_k)\lt m(I)$.

question:
First, the relevant definition. If
$G$
is a finite group, a subgroup
$M < G$
is
strongly
$p$
-embedded
if
$p$
divides
$|M|$
and, for all
$g \in G \setminus M$
,
$M \cap M^g$
is a
$p'$
-group. It is known that, in fact,
$M$
contains a Sylow
$p$
-subgroup of
$G$
in this case. Also, there exists a characterization stating that
$M < G$
is strongly
$p$
-embedded iff
$p$
divides
$|M|$
and, for all
$p$
-subgroups
$1 \neq Q \leq M$
,
$\mathbf{N}_G(Q) \leq M$
.

I came across this result as (one direction of) Lemma 7.54 of Professor David Craven's book on fusion systems, but there are a couple steps in his proof that I don't quite follow. It goes like this (
$G$
is a finite group,
$H \unlhd G$
and
$P$
is a Sylow
$p$
-subgroup of
$G$
contained in
$H$
):

Let
$M$
be a strongly
$p$
-embedded subgroup of
$H$
containing
$P$
. Then
$\mathbf{N}_G(M)$
contains
$P$
and, since
$\mathbf{N}_H(M) = M < H$
, it is a proper subgroup of
$G$
. We claim that
$\mathbf{N}_G(M)$
is strongly
$p$
-embedded in
$G$
. Let
$g \in G \setminus \mathbf{N}_G(M)$
and write
$L = \mathbf{N}_G(M) \cap \mathbf{N}_G(M)^g$
. Since
$L$
normalizes
$M$
,
$LM/M \cong L/(L \cap M)$
.
Clearly,
$L \cap M = L$
; hence
$M = LM$
, so that
$L \leq M$
. Similarly,
$L \leq M^g$
, and hence
$L \leq M \cap M^g$
,
a
$p'$
-group
, as required.

My two issues are the parts in italics:

Why is
$L \cap M = L$
? I can't seem to see why this holds...

Why is
$M \cap M^g$
a
$p'$
-group? This would certainly hold if
$g \in H$
, by definition, but why can't there be a
$G$
-conjugate of
$M$
with which this intersection has nontrivial
$p$
-part?

I suspect at least one of the two questions above must involve the second isomorphism theorem, since it is not really involved in the book's proof despite being mentioned (after all, if
$L \cap M = L$
, then we already have
$L \leq M$
...).

I have been trying multiple kinds of arguments using both equivalent definitions, but it always seems to boil down to the second point of contention above. For example, I was able to show that, if
$M \cap M^g$
is divisible by
$p$
, then it contains a full Sylow
$p$
-subgroup of
$G$
and
$g \in \mathbf{N}_G(P) M$
.

I also cannot find any other references to this direction of the result...

Any help is greatly appreciated!
answer:
I think the argument as provided in the book is wrong, and
$L \cap M = L$
is false.

Firstly, I believe the author tried to use Frattini's argument here but didn't say so. Since
$H \unlhd G$
and
$P$
is a Sylow-
$p$
subgroup of
$H$
, Frattini's argument tells us
$G = H N_G(P)$
.

Since
$P \leq M$
, and
$n \in N_G(P)$
, then conjugation by
$n$
gives
$M^n \leq H^n = H$
. Since automorphisms preserve embedding,
$M^n$
is another strongly
$p$
-embedded subgroup of
$H$
containing
$P^n = P$
. But such embeddings are unique -
$M$
is the unique strongly
$p$
-embedded subgroup of
$H$
containing
$P$
. Thus,
$M^n = M$
, so
$n \in N_G(M)$
. Therefore,
$N_G(P) \leq N_G(M)$
.

Thus,
$G = HN_G(M)$
.

Is
$L \cap M = L$
?

No, this is false. Consider
$G = H \times K$
, where
$H = A_5$
and
$K = C_2$
, and let
$p = 5$
. Let
$P \in \operatorname{Syl}_5(A_5)$
, which makes
$M = N_{A_5}(P) \cong D_{10}$
a strongly
$5$
-embedded subgroup of
$A_5$
.
Here,
$N_G(M) = M \times K$
. For
$g \in H \setminus M$
,
$N_G(M)^g = M^g \times K$
. Therefore,
$$L = N_G(M) \cap N_G(M)^g = \left (M \cap M^g\right ) \times K = C_2 \times C_2 \not \leq M$$
Hence,
$L \not \leq M$
, and
$L \cap M = L$
is false.

$G = H \times K$
, where
$H = A_5$
and
$K = C_2$
, and let
$p = 5$
. Let
$P \in \operatorname{Syl}_5(A_5)$
, which makes
$M = N_{A_5}(P) \cong D_{10}$
a strongly
$5$
-embedded subgroup of
$A_5$
.

Here,
$N_G(M) = M \times K$
. For
$g \in H \setminus M$
,
$N_G(M)^g = M^g \times K$
. Therefore,
$$L = N_G(M) \cap N_G(M)^g = \left (M \cap M^g\right ) \times K = C_2 \times C_2 \not \leq M$$
Hence,
$L \not \leq M$
, and
$L \cap M = L$
is false.

Why is
$M \cap M^g$
a
$p'$
-group?

Since
$G = HN_G(M)$
, we can factor out element
$g \in G \setminus N_G(M)$
as
$g = hn$
, where
$h \in H$
and
$n \in N_G(M)$
. Since
$n \in N_G(M)$
,
$$M \cap M^g = M \cap M^{hn} = M^n \cap M^{hn} = \left (M \cap M^h\right )^n$$
Since
$g \notin N_G(M)$
and
$n \in N_G(M)$
,
$h \notin N_G(M)$
, so
$h \notin N_G(M) \cap H = N_H(M)$
.
If there exists an
$x \in N_H(M) \setminus M$
, then
$M \cap M^x = M$
, contradinting the
$p'$
-intersection property. Hence
$M = N_H(M)$
. Thus
$h \in H \setminus M$
. Since
$M$
is strongly
$p$
-embedded in
$H$
,
$M \cap M^h$
is a
$p'$
-group.
Hence
$\left (M \cap M^h\right )^n$
is a
$p'$
-group, so we conclude
$M \cap M^g$
is a
$p'$
-group.

Since
$G = HN_G(M)$
, we can factor out element
$g \in G \setminus N_G(M)$
as
$g = hn$
, where
$h \in H$
and
$n \in N_G(M)$
. Since
$n \in N_G(M)$
,
$$M \cap M^g = M \cap M^{hn} = M^n \cap M^{hn} = \left (M \cap M^h\right )^n$$
Since
$g \notin N_G(M)$
and
$n \in N_G(M)$
,
$h \notin N_G(M)$
, so
$h \notin N_G(M) \cap H = N_H(M)$
.

If there exists an
$x \in N_H(M) \setminus M$
, then
$M \cap M^x = M$
, contradinting the
$p'$
-intersection property. Hence
$M = N_H(M)$
. Thus
$h \in H \setminus M$
. Since
$M$
is strongly
$p$
-embedded in
$H$
,
$M \cap M^h$
is a
$p'$
-group.

Hence
$\left (M \cap M^h\right )^n$
is a
$p'$
-group, so we conclude
$M \cap M^g$
is a
$p'$
-group.

Fixing using Second Isomorphism Theorem

To prove that
$N_G(M)$
is strongly
$p$
-embedded in
$G$
, we only need to prove that
$L = N_G(M) \cap N_G(M)^g$
is a
$p'$
-group. To do this, it suffices to take an arbitrary
$p$
-subgroup
$Q \leq L$
, and prove
$Q = 1$
.
Given that we have
$G = HN_G(M)$
, by the Second Isomorphism Theorem, we have
$$N_G(M) / (N_G(M) \cap H) \cong HN_G(M)/H \implies N_G(M) / M \cong G / H$$
Since
$P \leq H$
is a Sylow
$p$
-subgroup of
$G$
, the index
$[G : H]$
is co-prime to
$p$
, so
$G / H$
is a
$p'$
-group. Thus,
$N_G(M) / M$
is a
$p'$
-group, so it cannot contain any non-trivial
$p$
-elements. Therefore,
$M$
contains every
$p$
-subgroup of
$N_G(M)$
.
Since
$Q \leq L \leq N_G(M)$
, and
$Q$
is a
$p$
-subgroup of
$N_G(M)$
, we must have
$Q \leq M$
. This is probably what the author's proof was supposed to prove.
Since
$Q \leq L \leq N_G(M)^g$
,
$Q^{g^{-1}} \leq N_G(M)$
. Since
$Q^{g^{-1}}$
is also a
$p$
-group in
$N_G(M)$
, we must have
$Q^{g^{-1}} \leq M$
, and so
$Q \leq M^g$
.
Thus,
$Q \leq M \cap M^g$
, but we already previously showed that
$M \cap M^g$
is a
$p'$
-group - any such
$p$
-group living inside a
$p'$
-group must be trivial. We conclude
$Q = 1$
.
Therefore, any
$p$
-group inside
$L$
is trivial, so
$L$
is a
$p'$
-group.

To prove that
$N_G(M)$
is strongly
$p$
-embedded in
$G$
, we only need to prove that

Since
$Q \leq L \leq N_G(M)^g$
,

$Q^{g^{-1}}$

$p$
-group in

Thus,
$Q \leq M \cap M^g$

$M \cap M^g$
is a
$p'$

$p$
-group living inside a

question:
Prove or disprove that for each
$k \geq 1$
,

\begin{equation*}
\sum_{m=0}^k \binom{k}{m} (-1)^m \left( \frac{1 - q}{1-q^{m+1}} \right) = \sum_{r=0}^{\infty} \left[ (q^r - q^{r+1})(1 - q^r)^k \right]
\end{equation*}

is monotonically increasing for
$q \in (0, 1)$
.

A friend gave me this question; I don't know if it's true or false.

The equality in the question can be justified by a swap of summations. Note that for
$|q|<1$
, we have

$$
\frac{1-q}{1-q^{m+1}} = (1-q) \sum_{r=0}^{\infty} q^{r(m+1)} = \sum_{r=0}^{\infty} q^r (1-q) (q^m)^r.
$$

So,

\begin{align*}
\sum_{m=0}^k \binom{k}{m} (-1)^m \frac{1-q}{1-q^{m+1}}
&= \sum_{m=0}^k \binom{k}{m} (-1)^m \sum_{r=0}^{\infty} q^r (1-q) (q^m)^r \\
&= \sum_{r=0}^{\infty} q^r (1-q) \sum_{m=0}^k \binom{k}{m} (-1)^m (q^r)^m \\
&= \sum_{r=0}^{\infty} q^r (1-q) (1 - q^r)^k.
\end{align*}

Probably the expression on the right hand side is more useful. Differentiating the right hand side term by term gives (steps skipped):

$$
\sum_{r=0}^{\infty} q^{r-1}(1-q^r)^{k-1}
\left[
(1-q^r)(r-rq-q)
-
(q-q^2)krq^r
\right]
$$

While the first two terms are positive, I don't think one can say anything about the third term. I think differentiating term wise is probably not the way to go.

I played around with the number of nonzero digits of nonnegative integers and their sums. I did not find a counterexample nor a proof to the question, whether
$f(m+n) \le f(m)+f(n)$
for all nonnegative integers
$m,n$
, where
$m+n$
is usual integer addition and
$f(n)$
is the number of nonzero digits in decimal expansion. If true, it should be in the literature somewhere. The case of binary addition without carry (i.e. XOR-operation in bitstrings) is as easy as it is important.
answer:
Let me correct my previous (wrong) answer: Set
$s = m + n$
, let and
$s_i$
denote the
$i$
-th decimal digit of
$s$
. Then
$s_i \neq 0$
is only possible if
$(m_i \neq 0 \lor n_i \neq 0)$
or if there exists a ''chain of carries''
$j < i$
with (
$m_j \neq 0 \land n_j \neq 0$
) and (
$m_k \neq 0 \lor n_k \neq0$
) for
$j<k<i$
(consider for example, 99+1). Note that each pair
$(m_j,n_j)$
with both entries being nonzero generates at most one chain. Therefore
\begin{align*}
f(s)
&= \#\{i \colon s_i \neq 0\}
\leq \#\{i \colon m_i \neq 0 \lor n_i \neq 0\}
+ \#\{j \colon m_j \neq 0 \land n_j \neq 0\}
\\ &= \#\{i \colon m_i \neq 0\}
+ \#\{i \colon n_i \neq 0\}
= f(m) + f(n).
\end{align*}

question:
Let us have a simple connected undirected graph
$G_n$
.
Name two vertices
"associated"
if they have at least one common neighbor
(its adjacency lists intersected)
regardless of whether they are directly connected
.

First question, is there a special name for this relationship in graph theory?

Next, a simple procedure is followed: create a new graph of the same order
$G'_n$
.

Between its two vertices there will be an edge if they were associated in the source graph.

We will conduct this procedure iteratively.

It firstly appears that sooner or later we will get a complete graph
$K_n$
.

Indeed, experiments with random graphs first showed that graphs of
$20-100$
degree would form a complete graph after
$3 - 4$
iterations. I was about to ask how to estimate this number of steps, but suddenly found that
grid graphs
of any order after the first step turn into two disconnected components and none of the components becomes a complete graph.
Then I found other examples of different behavior.

So the question now is:

Is it possible for a given graph to predict its behavior as a result of such iterations?

And if it turns into a complete graph, how many steps does it take?
answer:
In general, any
bipartite graph
will become disconnected after one step of this procedure.

In bipartite graphs, the vertex set can be split into two sides (call them side
$A$
and side
$B$
) such that every edge has one endpoint in
$A$
and one endpoint in
$B$
. As a result, two vertices can only have a common neighbor if they are on the same side. After passing from
$G$
to its common-neighbor graph
$G'$
, there will be no edges from
$A$
to
$B$
at all.

This is the only exception. For this, we'll need to know a second property of bipartite graphs: a graph is bipartite if and only if it has no odd-length cycles. If a graph
$G$
is connected and not bipartite, then:

For any two vertices
$x$
and
$y$
, we can find an
$x-y$
walk in
$G$
of even length. (Walk from
$x$
to the nearest odd cycle, and from there to
$y$
. If that walk happens to be odd, then insert one loop around the odd cycle.
An even-length walk in
$G$
corresponds to a walk in
$G'$
of half the length: just skip every other step. So there is an
$x-y$
walk in
$G'$
, and since
$x$
and
$y$
were arbitrary,
$G'$
is still connected.
An odd cycle in
$G$
gives us an odd cycle in
$G'$
: if
$(x_0, x_1, x_2, \dots, x_{2k}, x_0)$
represents an odd cycle in
$G$
, then in
$G'$
, we have
$$(x_0, x_2, x_4, \dots, x_{2k}, x_1, x_3, \dots, x_{2k-1}, x_0).$$
So
$G'$
is not bipartite, either.

Since
$G'$
is connected and not bipartite, we know that the next iteration
$G''$
will be connected and not bipartite, and then everything will work out great.

This does not always mean that we will eventually arrive at a complete graph. Another example to consider: if we start with an odd cycle
$C_{2k+1}$
, then after one iteration we will have a graph isomorphic to
$C_{2k+1}$
, and so nothing will ever change.

However, if the graph contains an odd cycle
and
something else connected to it, we can prove that it stabilizes at a complete graph in around
$\log_2 n$
iterations (give or take a small constant).

In such a case, there must be three vertices
$x,y,z$
with a common neighbor. After one iteration, those vertices form a triangle, and that triangle will persist forever from there on out.

Meanwhile, for every other vertex, since the graph is connected, every other vertex
$w$
will have a path to the triangle. With every iteration, the length of such a path gets cut in half: if the distance from
$w$
to the triangle is
$k$
, it will be
$\lceil k/2\rceil$
in the next iteration. Since the initial distance is less than
$n$
, it takes at most around
$\log_2 n$
steps for this distance to hit
$1$
, meaning
$w$
is adjacent to one of
$x$
,
$y$
, or
$z$
. In the next step,
$w$
is adjacent to at least two of them; in the step after that, it is adjacent to all three, and it stabilizes there.

Since
$w$
is arbitrary, this will be happening simultaneously for
all
the vertices other than
$x,y,z$
. After all vertices are adjacent to all three of
$x$
,
$y$
, and
$z$
, the graph will become complete in the next iteration, if it isn't there already.

The worst case appears to be a
$(2k+2)$
-vertex graph that consists of an odd cycle
$C_{2k+1}$
and an extra vertex with one neighbor on the odd cycle. It takes
$2 + \lceil \log_2 k \rceil$
step to turn this into a complete graph.

question:
In Pascal's triangle with
$n$
rows (the
$1$
at the top is row
$0$
), we will choose two random numbers as follows.

Random number
$A$

Choose a random row (each row has equal probability of being chosen).

In that row, choose a random number (in the chosen row, each number has equal probability of being chosen).

Call this number
$A$
.

Random number
$B$

The diagram shows Pascal's triangle with diagonals in red.

image source

Choose a random diagonal (each diagonal has equal probability of being chosen).

In that diagonal, choose a random number (in the chosen diagonal, each number has equal probability of being chosen).

Call this number
$B$
.

What is
$L=\lim\limits_{n\to\infty}P(A<B)?$
(closed form or approximation)

Experimental results

For various values of
$n$
, I used Excel to run
$10^6$
trials each and got the following results.

Context

I sometimes ask questions about Pascal's triangle and probability or expectation (
example1
,
example2
,
example3
).
answer:
We want to map the binomial coefficients to continuous variables. Using Stirling's approximation,
$\frac{1}{n} \ln \binom{r}{k}$
converges to
$Z = x H(y)$
, where
$x = r/n$
is the relative row,
$y = k/r$
is the relative column, and
$H(y) = -y \ln(y) - (1-y) \ln(1-y)$
is the
binary entropy function
.

For the number
$A$
, the row
$x_A$
and relative position
$y_A$
are chosen uniformly and independently, meaning
$x_A, y_A \sim U(0,1)$
with a joint PDF of
$f_A(x,y) = 1$
. Because they are independent, we can separate their expectations:
$E[Z_A] = E[x_A]E[H(y_A)]$
. Since
$E[x_A] = 0.5$
, the expected exponent is
$E[Z_A] = \frac{1}{2} \int_0^1 H(y) \,dy = 0.25$
.

For the number
$B$
, the choices are dependent. Choosing a diagonal uniformly means its starting position is
$\alpha \sim U(0,1)$
. Choosing an element on that diagonal means the relative row
$x_B$
is distributed uniformly on
$(\alpha, 1)$
, giving a joint density of
$f(\alpha, x_B) = \frac{1}{1-\alpha}$
. To map this to our
$(x,y)$
coordinate system, we use the transformation
$\alpha = xy$
to obtain the joint PDF
$f_B(x, y) = \frac{x}{1-xy}$
. Thus,
$E[Z_B] = \int_0^1 \int_0^1 x H(y) \frac{x}{1-xy} \,dx\,dy \approx 0.44288$
. Because
$B$
stochastically dominates
$A$
in the asymptotic limit, the exact probability
$L = P(Z_A < Z_B)$
is the integral of the cumulative distribution function of
$A$
over the probability density function of
$B$
:
$$L = \int_0^1 \int_0^1 \int_0^1 \min\left(1, \frac{x H(y)}{H(w)}\right) \frac{x}{1-xy} \,dx\,dy\,dw \approx 0.6121$$

question:
My initial task was to find the radius of curvature of the curve
$y^2 = 4ax$
for
$a>0$
at the origin
$O(0,0)$
.

My solution is:

Let the circle tangent to the curve at the origin be
$(x-h)^2 +y^2 = h^2$
. Here,
$h$
would be the radius of the required circle. Solving this equation with
$y^2 =4ax$
and then equating the discriminant of the quadratic obtained to
$0$
, I am getting the correct answer of
$h=2a$
. But why did I not get negative values for
$h$
eventhough all the circles having their centres on the negative
$x$
-axis and which touch the
$y$
-axis at origin also touch the parabola at only one point?
answer:
Now that I understand the question better (see discussion in the comments), here is an explanation of what is going on here.

Let
$h \in \mathbb{R}$
. We have two curves, a parabola
$P$
given by
$\{ (x,y) \mid y^2=4ax, x\geq 0\}$
and a circle
$C_h$
given by
$(x-h)^2 +y^2 =h^2$
. We wish to find the set
$S= \{h \in \mathbb{R} \mid P \cap C_h = (0,0)\}$
.

In order to do so, we subsitute
$y^2=4ax$
in the equation of the circle and obtain:
$$ x^2 +(4a-2h)x =0$$
Equivalently,
$$x(x+4a-2h)=0$$

So
$x=0$
and
$x=-4a+2h$
are the (possibly non-distinct) solutions to the quadratic equation.

Note now that we want the
intersection
of
$P$
and
$C_h$
to be singleton. It is perfectly acceptable for the other solution to be different from
$x=0$
, so long as it is not on the parabola
$P$
. Clearly negative roots cannot be on the parabola, and are in fact allowed!

This is why solving for the discriminant of the quadratic being zero does not get us the full solution space, hope this helps.

question:
My initial task was to find the radius of curvature of the curve
$y^2 = 4ax$
for
$a>0$
at the origin
$O(0,0)$
.

My solution is:

Let the circle tangent to the curve at the origin be
$(x-h)^2 +y^2 = h^2$
. Here,
$h$
would be the radius of the required circle. Solving this equation with
$y^2 =4ax$
and then equating the discriminant of the quadratic obtained to
$0$
, I am getting the correct answer of
$h=2a$
. But why did I not get negative values for
$h$
eventhough all the circles having their centres on the negative
$x$
-axis and which touch the
$y$
-axis at origin also touch the parabola at only one point?
answer:
You are looking for the
osculating circle
to the parabola at the origin, not merely any circle that will be tangent to the parabola there. The curvature of the parabola at the point is the reciprocal of the radius of the osculating circle (which is also called the radius of curvature). You can find further details in my
lecture notes on introductory differential geometry
.

question:
Let $f \in C[a,b]$ be a function whose derivative exists on $(a,b)$. Suppose $f$ is to be evaluated at $x_0$
in $(a,b)$, but instead of computing the actual value $f(x_0)$, the approximate value,
$\hat f(x_0)$, is the actual
value of $f$ at $x_0 + \epsilon $, that is, $\hat f^~(x_0 ) = f(x_0 + \epsilon)$.

Use the Mean Value Theorem to estimate the absolute error $|f(x_0 )−\hat f(x_0 )|$ and the relative
error $|f(x_0 )−\hat f(x_0 )|/|f(x_0)|$, assuming $f(x_0 ) \neq 0$.

Here's what I tried:

Absolute error:
$|f(x_0 )−\hat f(x_0 )|=|f(x_0)-f(x_0+\epsilon)|=|f'(c)|\,\epsilon\,$ where $c \in (x_0, x_0 + \epsilon)$.

And relative error:$$\frac{|f'(c)|\epsilon}{|f(x_0)|}$$
where $c \in (x_0, x_0 + \epsilon)$.
I'm not sure if this is right, and if it is how do I find $c$.
answer:
You pretty much have the right idea. As you pointed out,
$$\left|f(x_0)-f(x_0+\epsilon)\right|=\left|f^\prime(c)\right| \epsilon$$
for some $c$ in the interval $I = (x_0, x_0+\epsilon)$. Therefore,
$$\left|f(x_0)-f(x_0+\epsilon)\right|\leq \epsilon \cdot \sup_{c\in I}\left|f^\prime(c)\right|.$$
Divide both sides by $|f(x_0)|$ to get a bound on the relative error.

question:
I am trying to compute the limit

$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(\frac{2n - 1}{3n + 1}\right)^n,
$

and I would appreciate some guidance.

My first attempt was to apply the theorem stating that if
$a_n \to L > 0$
and
$b_n \to \infty$
, then

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n^{b_n} = \exp\left(\lim_{n \to \infty} b_n (a_n - 1)\right)
$$

provided the limit in the exponent exists. However, in this case, the corresponding limit in the exponent does not exist.


I also tried using the ratio approach, but the expressions quickly became quite messy and difficult to handle.


Another idea I explored was to consider the series defined by

$
\sum \left(\frac{2n - 1}{3n + 1}\right)^n.
$

Using the root test, the limit becomes easy to compute, and it follows that the series converges. However, this applies to series, not directly to the sequence itself, so it does not immediately give the value of the limit I am looking for.

This made me wonder whether there exists a criterion analogous to the root test, but for sequences, that could help evaluate limits of this form.

Any hints or alternative approaches would be greatly appreciated.
answer: $$\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(\frac{2n-1}{3n+1}\right)^n=\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(\frac{2-\frac1n}{3+\frac1n}\right)^n \leqslant\lim_{n\to\infty}\left(\frac23\right)^n=0$$

question:
I am trying to compute the limit

$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(\frac{2n - 1}{3n + 1}\right)^n,
$

and I would appreciate some guidance.

My first attempt was to apply the theorem stating that if
$a_n \to L > 0$
and
$b_n \to \infty$
, then

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n^{b_n} = \exp\left(\lim_{n \to \infty} b_n (a_n - 1)\right)
$$

provided the limit in the exponent exists. However, in this case, the corresponding limit in the exponent does not exist.


I also tried using the ratio approach, but the expressions quickly became quite messy and difficult to handle.


Another idea I explored was to consider the series defined by

$
\sum \left(\frac{2n - 1}{3n + 1}\right)^n.
$

Using the root test, the limit becomes easy to compute, and it follows that the series converges. However, this applies to series, not directly to the sequence itself, so it does not immediately give the value of the limit I am looking for.

This made me wonder whether there exists a criterion analogous to the root test, but for sequences, that could help evaluate limits of this form.

Any hints or alternative approaches would be greatly appreciated.
answer:
Recall that if
$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} a_n$
converges then
$a_n\to 0$
. The result now follows from the convergence of
$\sum_{n=1}^{\infty} \left(\dfrac{2n-1}{3n+1} \right)^n$

For completeness, notice that if
$\sum_{k=1}^{\infty} a_k$
converges then
$$\lim\limits_{n\to\infty} \sum_{k=1}^{n}a_k=\lim\limits_{n\to\infty}\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}a_k $$
Also, notice that

$$a_n=\sum_{k=1}^{n}a_k-\sum_{k=1}^{n-1}a_k$$
and if
$n\to \infty$
, by te previous equality,
$a_n\to 0$
.

question:
I am trying to compute the limit

$
\lim_{n \to \infty} \left(\frac{2n - 1}{3n + 1}\right)^n,
$

and I would appreciate some guidance.

My first attempt was to apply the theorem stating that if
$a_n \to L > 0$
and
$b_n \to \infty$
, then

$$
\lim_{n \to \infty} a_n^{b_n} = \exp\left(\lim_{n \to \infty} b_n (a_n - 1)\right)
$$

provided the limit in the exponent exists. However, in this case, the corresponding limit in the exponent does not exist.


I also tried using the ratio approach, but the expressions quickly became quite messy and difficult to handle.


Another idea I explored was to consider the series defined by

$
\sum \left(\frac{2n - 1}{3n + 1}\right)^n.
$

Using the root test, the limit becomes easy to compute, and it follows that the series converges. However, this applies to series, not directly to the sequence itself, so it does not immediately give the value of the limit I am looking for.

This made me wonder whether there exists a criterion analogous to the root test, but for sequences, that could help evaluate limits of this form.

Any hints or alternative approaches would be greatly appreciated.
answer:
When your power also changes with
$n$
you should be a little more carefule with getting common factors out and probably use Euler's limit:

$$\lim_{n\to \infty}{\Big(1+\frac{a}{n}\Big)^{n}}=e^a$$

Now of course your case is not the basic form of this formula, but we can start from there. First we add and subtract things to create the 1.

$$\lim_{n\to \infty}{\Big(\frac{2n-1}{3n+1}\Big)^{n}} = \lim_{n\to \infty}{\Big(\frac{2n-1 + n - n + 1 - 1}{3n+1}\Big)^{n}}= \lim_{n\to \infty}{\Big(\frac{(3n+1)+(-n-2)}{3n+1}\Big)^{n}}= \lim_{n\to \infty}{\Big(1+\frac{-n-2}{3n+1}\Big)^{n}}$$

Now, we need the power of the outside to match the same order of the denominator, so we can multiply and divide, and use power rules:

$$\lim_{n\to \infty}{\Big(1+\frac{-n-2}{3n+1}\Big)^{n\times\frac{3n+1}{3n+1}}} = \lim_{n\to \infty}{\Big(1+\frac{-n-2}{3n+1}\Big)^{3n+1\times\frac{n}{3n+1}}} = \lim_{n\to \infty}{\Big[\Big(1+\frac{-n-2}{3n+1}\Big)^{3n+1}\Big]^{\frac{n}{3n+1}}}$$

The inside has a limit from Euler's formula, which is exponent to the power of the numerator, so from that we get:

$$= \lim_{n\to \infty}{\Big[\Big(1+\frac{-n-2}{3n+1}\Big)^{3n+1}\Big]^{\frac{n}{3n+1}}}= \lim_{n\to \infty}{\Big[e^{-n-2}\Big]^{\frac{n}{3n+1}}} = \lim_{n\to \infty}{e^{\frac{n(-n-2)}{3n+1}}}$$

The exponent is a continuous function, and it preseves limit, so we can allow ourselves to "move" the limit calculation to the power, therefore:

$$= \lim_{n\to \infty}{e^{\frac{n(-n-2)}{3n+1}}} = e^{\lim_{n \to \infty}{\frac{-n^2-2n}{3n+1}}}$$

Now:
$$\lim_{n \to \infty}{\frac{-n^2-2n}{3n+1}} = -\infty$$

So we end up with

$$e^{-\infty} = 0$$

question:
In triangle
$\triangle ABC$
, the points
$L, M$
are the midpoints of
$BC$
and
$CA$
, respectively, and
$CF$
is the altitude from
$C.$
The circle through
$A$
and
$M$
which
$AL$
is tangent to at
$A$
meets the extension of
$BA$
at
$X$
.

Find the minimum value of
$\frac{BX}{FC}$
and specify the triangles
$ABC$
for which it is obtained

The first thing to do might be to find the length of
$AX$
most likely with Tangent-Chord Theorem and the Law of Sines/Ratio Lemma.

$AX \cdot AB = \frac{1}{2}AC^2$
.

By the Tangent-Chord theorem on the circle passing through
$A, M, X$
with tangent
$AL$
, the angle between tangent
$AL$
and chord
$AM$
equals the inscribed angle. Thus,
$\angle LAM = \angle AXM$
. Since
$M$
is on
$AC$
, this means
$\angle LAC = \angle AXM$
. After finding more angles we could apply sin rule in
$\triangle AMX$
, but I can't find more useful angle chases.

Could anyone help me with a hint or solution for this problem?

Regards.
answer:
Let the lengths of
$BC$
,
$CA$
,
$AB$
be
$a$
,
$b$
,
$c$
respectively, as usual.

Using your finding that
$AX\cdot AB=\frac{1}{2}AC^2$
, we have
$AX=\frac{b^2}{2c}$
, and hence
$$BX=BA+AX = c + \frac{b^2}{2c} = \frac{2c^2+b^2}{2c}.$$

Basic trigonometry gives us
$$FC=b\cdot \sin{(\angle FAC)}.$$

Therefore,
$$\frac{BX}{FC} = \frac{\frac{2c^2+b^2}{2c}}{b\cdot \sin{(\angle FAC)}}=\frac{b^2+2c^2}{2bc\cdot \sin{(\angle FAC)}}.$$

It's time for some inequalities:
$$
\begin{cases}
b^2+2c^2 \geq 2\sqrt{2}bc\qquad \text{(AM-GM inequality)} \\
\sin{(\angle FAC)} \leq 1
\end{cases}.
$$

And hence
$\frac{BX}{FC}\geq\sqrt{2}$
. Equality holds if and only if
$$
\begin{cases}
b = \sqrt 2 c \\
\angle FAC = 90^\circ
\end{cases}
$$
which can only be satified when
$\triangle ABC$
is a right triangle at
$A$
and
$AC = \sqrt{2}AB$
.

Note:
Here's the proof why
$AX = \frac{b^2}{2c}$
to make the solution complete. Applying the law of Sines in
$\triangle AMX$
:
$$\frac{AX}{\sin{(\angle AMX)}} = \frac{AM}{\sin{(\angle AXM)}}.$$

The tangent-chord theorem provides us:
$$
\begin{cases}
\angle AMX = \angle LAB\\
\angle AXM = \angle LAC
\end{cases}.
$$

Combining our findings,
$AX = AM \cdot \frac{\sin{(\angle LAB)}}{\sin{(\angle LAC)}}$
. Moreover, notice that
$\triangle ABL$
and
$\triangle ACL$
have the same areas,
$$\frac{c\cdot AL\cdot \sin{(\angle LAB)}}{2} = \frac{b\cdot AL\cdot \sin{(\angle LAC)}}{2} $$
which is equivalent to
$\frac{\sin{(\angle LAB)}}{\sin{(\angle LAC)}} = \frac{b}{c}$
.

It's easy to see that
$AM = \frac{b}{2}$
, meaning that
$AX = \frac{b^2}{2c}$
.

question:
While contributing to OEIS, I noticed that entries display a “status” field such as “proposed”, followed by numbers like “+12 −2”.

For example, an entry may show:
“Status: proposed +12 −2”

I understand that the textual part (“proposed”, “approved”, etc.) refers to the editorial status of the entry, as described in the OEIS Wiki. However, I could not find any official documentation explaining the meaning of the numbers that appear next to it.

What do these numbers represent? Are they related to community voting, editorial feedback, or something else?

Is there any official source describing this notation?
example (screenshot)
answer:
https://oeis.org/wiki/OEIS_Help_Page

Question 2: What is the significance of the "+" and "-" numbers that appear next to a sequence and under the Status heading? For example, if a proposed sequence has a +5 -3 beside it, what does this signify?

Answer 2: Lines added/lines removed. If you correct a comment and add a formula the sequence should have "+2 -1". Charles R Greathouse IV 18:33, 29 May 2013 (UTC)

question:
I have faced the following problem:

Let
$M$
be a nonzero Noetherian module over a Noetherian ring
$R$
. Prove that there exists
such a sequence of submodules
$0 = M_k ⊂ M_{k−1} ⊂ ... ⊂ M_0 = M$
, where factor module

$M_{i−1} /M_i$
are isomorphic to
$R/I_i$
for some prime ideals
$I_i$
.

My idea was to take maximal submodule of
$M$
, say
$N$
and then
$M/N$
is isomorphic to
$R/m$
for some maximal ideal
$m$
and the repeat it for
$N$
and so on. But I can' find an argument for this descending sequence to stabilize.
Can you help me with some ideas of maybe a different solution?
answer:
Q:
"My idea was to take maximal submodule of M, say N and then M/N is isomorphic to R/m for some maximal ideal m and the repeat it for N and so on. But I can' find an argument for this descending sequence to stabilize. Can you help me with some ideas of maybe a different solution?"

A:
This is proved in the case of
$A$
a noetherian ring and
$M\neq 0$
a finite
$A$
-module in Matsumura, Thm. 6.4. There is also a version for graded modules on graded rings in Hartshorne, Prop. I.7.4:

If
$S$
is a noetherian graded ring and
$0 \neq M$
a finitely generated graded
$S$
-module there is a finite filtration
$0=M^0 \subsetneq M^1 \subsetneq \cdots \subsetneq M^r=M$
with
$M^i/M^{i-1} \cong (S/\mathfrak{p}_i)(l_i)$
where
$\mathfrak{p}_i$
is a homogeneous prime ideal and
$l_i \in \mathbb{Z}$
.

question:
Let
$X$
be a smooth projective curve over an algebraically closed field
$k$
. A vector bundle
$\mathcal{E}$
on
$X$
is semistable if
$\mu(\mathcal{F})\leq \mu(\mathcal{E})$
for any subbundle
$\mathcal{F}$
, where
$\mu(-)$
is the slope.

From the definition, it is not guaranteed that a subbundle
$\mathcal{F}$
of a semistable bundle
$\mathcal{E}$
is again semistable. My question is: is there any concrete example showing
$\mathcal{F}$
may not be semistable?
answer:
For a rather simple example, consider
$\Bbb P^1$
and the rank
$3$
bundle
$\mathcal{O}_{\Bbb P^1}^{\oplus 3}$
. The Euler sequence twisted by
$\mathcal{O}_{\Bbb P^1}(-1)$
is given by
$${\displaystyle 0\longrightarrow {\mathcal {O}_{\Bbb P^1}(-1)}\longrightarrow {\mathcal {O}_{\Bbb P^1}^{\oplus 2}}\longrightarrow {\mathcal {O}_{\Bbb P^1}(1)}\longrightarrow 0}.$$
Adding a copy of
$\mathcal{O}_{\Bbb P^1}$
entails
$${\displaystyle 0\longrightarrow {\mathcal {O}_{\Bbb P^1}\oplus\mathcal {O}_{\Bbb P^1}(-1)}\longrightarrow {\mathcal {O}_{\Bbb P^1}^{\oplus 3}}\longrightarrow {\mathcal {O}_{\Bbb P^1}(1)}\longrightarrow 0}.$$
So
$\mathcal {O}_{\Bbb P^1}\oplus\mathcal {O}_{\Bbb P^1}(-1)$
is a subbundle of
$\mathcal {O}_{\Bbb P^1}^{\oplus 3}$
, but it is
not
semistable. Indeed,
$$\mu(\mathcal{O}_{\Bbb P^1}\oplus\mathcal {O}_{\Bbb P^1}(-1)) = \frac{0-1}{2} = -\frac{1}{2},$$
but the subbundle
$\mathcal{O}_{\Bbb P^1} \hookrightarrow\mathcal{O}_{\Bbb P^1}\oplus\mathcal {O}_{\Bbb P^1}(-1)$
is destabilizing:
$$\mu(\mathcal{O}_{\Bbb P^1}) = 0 > \mu(\mathcal{O}_{\Bbb P^1}\oplus\mathcal {O}_{\Bbb P^1}(-1)).$$

question:
Informally, I would like to find an infinite product of rational numbers that evaluates to a nonzero rational number such that the multiplicity of each prime in the numerator is finite, while on the denominator there are an infinite number of primes with unbounded multiplicity. I will make this more precise below.

I would like to determine a pair of infinite integer sequences
$a_n$
and
$b_n$
such that

$$\prod_{n=1}^\infty \frac{a_n}{b_n} = r,$$


where
$r\in\Bbb Q, r\not=0$
.


Let


$$A_N = \prod_{n=1}^N a_n,$$


and let


$$B_N = \prod_{n=1}^N b_n.$$


Set
$v_p(n)$
as the p-adic valuation of n, namely, the multiplicity of a prime number p in the prime factorization of n. The constraints on
$A_N$
and
$B_N$
are as follows:


For all prime numbers
$p$
,
$\lim\limits_{N \to \infty} v_p(A_N)$
is finite.


For an infinite number of primes
$p$
,
$\lim\limits_{N \to \infty} v_p(B_N)=\infty$
.


My motivation for question is that I would like a non obvious infinite product of rational numbers for a rational number. These constraints avoid the product from telescoping.


Some thoughts on the problem:
Part of the reason I was curious about this question is that I noticed that Euler's product formula for
$\zeta(k), k\in\Bbb Z, k>1$
satisfied these constraints except for rationality of r. That is
$a_n=p_n^k$
and
$b_n=p_n^k - 1$
where
$p_n$
is the nth prime number. In fact using Dirichlet's theorem on arithmetic progressions and the fact that
$p_n-1$
divides
$p_n^k-1$
, one can show that for every prime
$p$
,
$\lim\limits_{N \to \infty} v_p(B_N)=\infty$
since for every prime p, infinitely many primes are of the form
$q=pn+1, n\in\Bbb Z^+$
.


Another idea that partially satisfies the conditions is:


$$r = \prod_{n=1}^\infty \frac{2^{2^n}+1}{2^{2^n}} = \frac{4}{3}\in \Bbb Q,$$


which has
$a_n = 2^{2^n}+1$
and
$b_n = 2^{2^n}$
. For all prime numbers
$p$
,
$\lim\limits_{N \to \infty} v_p(A_N)$
is finite since if
$p$
divides
$a_n$
, then
$a_m\equiv 2\pmod p$
for
$m>n$
. The only constraint that fails is:
$\lim\limits_{N \to \infty} v_p(B_N)=\infty$
which only holds for p=2.


This whole question relies on the assumption that this specific infinite product form is possible. If it is impossible, then this would imply the irrationality of
$\zeta(k), k\in\Bbb Z, k>1$
. So, if it is impossible, it would be very hard to prove it!

Thanks in advance.
answer:
Any rational
$r$
has a representation as in the OP: for simplicity, I will give a construction for
$1/2$
but it can be adapted easily. The construction is inductive elementary, the only nontrivial part being the fact that if one fixes an
$a>1$
then there is
$N_a$
st
$[Y, aY]$
contains a prime for
$Y \ge N_a$
.

Choose
$A_1=a_1=1, B_1=b_1=2$
and fix
$e(n)=1/n$
and write the odd primes in triangular blocks
$3,3,5,3,5,7,3,5,7,11,...$
and call
$s_n$
the above sequence - note that each odd prime appears infinitely many times.

Now pick
$m_2 \ge 2$
large enough (eg
$m_2=2$
works) so with
$b_2={s_2}^{m_2}=3^{m_2}, Y_2=\frac{B_1b_2}{2A_1}=b_2=3^{m_2}$
we have
$Y_2>2$
and the interval
$[Y_2, (1+1/2)Y_2]$
contains a prime
$a_2$
and then noting that
$a_2/b_2-1 \le b_2/(2b_2)=1/2, a_1/b_1=1/2$
$$0=a_1/b_1-1/2 \le A_2/B_2-1/2=\frac{a_2a_1}{b_2b_1}-1/2 \le a_1/(2b_1) \le 1/4$$

Assume now we chose
$a_n, b_n$
st
$b_n=s_n^{m_n}, Y_n=\frac{B_{n-1}b_n}{2A_{n-1}}, a_n$
is a prime in an interval
$[Y_n, (1+e(n))Y_n]$
where
$Y_n >2Y_{n-1}$
st with
$A_n=a_1..a_n, B_n=b_1...b_n$
we have
$$0 \le A_n/B_n -1/2 \le e(n)/2<1$$
we continue as follows.

We pick
$b_{n+1}=s_{n+1}^{m_{n+1}}$
where
$m_{n+1}$
is large enough so with
$Y_{n+1}=B_nb_{n+1}/(2A_n) \ge b_{n+1}/2$
(so going to infinity if
$m_{n+1}$
does so and the fact in the introduction above about the existence of a prime in a selected interval depending on
$Y_{n+1}$
applies) and we have
$Y_{n+1}>2Y_n$
while there is a prime
$a_{n+1} \in [Y_{n+1}, (1+e(n+1))Y_{n+1}]$
. Note that
$2A_n/B_n=b_{n+1}/Y_{N+1}$

Then
$$A_{n+1}/B_{n+1}=(a_{n+1}/b_{n+1})(A_n/B_n)=a_{n+1}/(2Y_{n+1}) >1/2$$
while
$$A_{n+1}/B_{n+1}-1/2=a_{n+1}/(2Y_{n+1})-1/2 \le (e(n+1))Y_{n+1}/(2Y_{n+1})=e(n+1)/2$$
so the induction works and
$A_n/B_n \to 1/2$

By construction,
$A_n$
adds only one large prime so
$\nu_p(A_n)=0$
or
$\nu_p(A_n)=1, n \ge n_p$
while
$B_n$
adds a factor
$s_n^{m_n}$
and
$s_n$
cycles infinitely through all odd primes so
$\nu_p(B_n) \to \infty$
for all odd primes
$p$

question:
I'm studying complex analysis via Conway's Functions of one Complex Variable. My teacher is following Stein's Complex Analysis instead so I'm learning things a bit out of order. I was given an exercise which essentially entails proving that the winding number makes sense.

The exercise isn't precise on what is meant by "closed curve", so I'm working with a continuous
$\gamma : [0,1] \to \mathbb{C}$
that is holomorphic except at a finite amount of points and doesn't have
$0$
derivative anywhere (tell me if this doesn't make sense). I found the following non-iluminating proof in Conway's book

Defining
$g$
like that makes some sense, but why would I ever look at the derivative of
$e^{-g}(\gamma - a)$
?

My questions are preciselly the following:

I'm aware the fundamental group of
$\mathbb{C}\setminus \{a\}$
is isomorphic to
$\mathbb{Z}$
and I have seen the winding number defined using lifts.
Can I prove the integral of a holomorphic function over a (add your hypothesis here, I'm interested in knowing the weakest) curve depends only on its homotopy class
?

Is there a more illuminating way of seeing Conway's argument
?

Thanks in advance
answer:
Per @J.G's comment... Consider the function
$$g(t) = \int_0^t \frac{\gamma'(s)}{\gamma(s)-a} ds$$

If these were real variables and
$f(t)$
was a real function, you would immediately recognize this integral using substitution (
$u = f(s)$
) and obtain
$$\int \frac{f'(s)}{f(s)-a} ds = \ln(f(t)-a) + C$$

So, intuition tells us that
$g(t)$
should be something like
$\ln(\gamma(t)-a) - \ln(\gamma(0)-a)$
. However, in the complex plane, the logarithm
$\ln(z)$
is a multi-valued function (nasty stuff). To make it a well-defined, single-valued function, you have to choose a branch cut (a line extending from the origin to infinity where the function is discontinuous). Because
$\gamma$
is a closed curve that might wind completely around
$a$
, it is guaranteed to cross any branch cut you draw. Therefore, you cannot globally define
$g(t)$
using the complex logarithm without running into discontinuities

To solve this, you can consider the exponential function, which is single-valued, continuous, and entirely well-behaved everywhere in
$\mathbb{C}$
('entire'). If intuition dictates that
$g(t) = \ln(\gamma(t)-a) + C$
, then exponentiating both sides gives
$$e^{g(t)} = c(\gamma(t)-a)$$

This motivates Conway's argument hopefully :)

The weakest definition of the curve for which the integral is defined would be that
$\gamma$
is a rectifiable curve (continuous with finite arc length), where the integral
$\int_\gamma f(z) dz$
may be defined using a Riemann-Stieltjes integral.

question:
I'm studying complex analysis via Conway's Functions of one Complex Variable. My teacher is following Stein's Complex Analysis instead so I'm learning things a bit out of order. I was given an exercise which essentially entails proving that the winding number makes sense.

The exercise isn't precise on what is meant by "closed curve", so I'm working with a continuous
$\gamma : [0,1] \to \mathbb{C}$
that is holomorphic except at a finite amount of points and doesn't have
$0$
derivative anywhere (tell me if this doesn't make sense). I found the following non-iluminating proof in Conway's book

Defining
$g$
like that makes some sense, but why would I ever look at the derivative of
$e^{-g}(\gamma - a)$
?

My questions are preciselly the following:

I'm aware the fundamental group of
$\mathbb{C}\setminus \{a\}$
is isomorphic to
$\mathbb{Z}$
and I have seen the winding number defined using lifts.
Can I prove the integral of a holomorphic function over a (add your hypothesis here, I'm interested in knowing the weakest) curve depends only on its homotopy class
?

Is there a more illuminating way of seeing Conway's argument
?

Thanks in advance
answer:
Usually complex curve integrals
$\int_\gamma f(z)dz$
are defined for continuously differentiable
$\gamma :[c, d] \to U$
and holomorphic (or more generally continuous)
$f : U \to \mathbb C$
by
$$\int_\gamma f(z)dz = \int_c^d f(\gamma(t))\gamma'(t)dt .$$
This can easily be generalized to piecewise continuously differentiable curves.

An alternative approach is the work with
rectifiable curves
and define
$\int_\gamma f(z)dz$
as a Riemann-Stieltjes-integral as Conway has done in his book. One must then prove that both definitions agree for piecewise continuously differentiable curves (see Conway's Theorem 1.9).

Can I prove the integral of a holomorphic function over a (add your hypothesis here, I'm interested in knowing the weakest) curve depends only on its homotopy class?

Can I prove the integral of a holomorphic function over a (add your hypothesis here, I'm interested in knowing the weakest) curve depends only on its homotopy class?

An
explicit formula
for
$\int_\gamma f(z)dz$
is available only for rectifiable curves. However, one can prove that curve integrals of holomorphic functions
$f$
only depend the homotopy class of curves.
This allows to define
$\int_\gamma f(z)dz$
for all continuous curves.
See
Is Stein & Shakarchi's "Complex Analysis" book too loose about homotopic curves?

Note, however, that such general  integrals
$\int_\gamma f(z)dz$
are not explicitly computable by a formula only in terms of
$f$
and
$\gamma$
.

Is there a more illuminating way of seeing Conway's argument?

Is there a more illuminating way of seeing Conway's argument?

This was already addressed in Lucien Jaccon's answer. The
obvious approach would be to observe that with
$\bar \gamma(t) = \gamma(t) - a$
we get
$$\frac{\gamma'(t)}{\gamma(t)-a} = (\ln \circ \bar \gamma)'(s)$$
for curves
$\gamma$
with image in a simply connected domain (e.g. a sliced plain)
$\Omega$
not containing
$0$
, where
$\ln$
is any branch of the complex logarithm. In that case
$$\int_\gamma \frac{\gamma'(t)}{\gamma(t)-a}dt = \ln(\bar \gamma(d)) - \ln(\bar \gamma(c))  .$$
What if the image of
$\gamma$
is not contained in a simply connected domain? In that case we can take a partition
$c = t_0 < t_1 < \ldots < t_n = d$
such that each
$\gamma([t_{i-1]}, t_i])$
is contained in a simply connected domain
$\Omega_i$
with a branch
$\ln_i : \Omega_i \to \mathbb C$
of the complex logarithm. Then
$$\int_\gamma \frac{\gamma'(t)}{\gamma(t)-a}dt = \sum_{i=1}^n \ln_i(\hat \gamma(t_i)) - \ln_i(\hat \gamma(t_{i-1}) \\ = \ln_n(\bar \gamma(d)) - \ln_1(\bar \gamma(c)) + \sum_{i=1}^{n-1} (\ln_{i+1}(\bar \gamma(t_i)) - \ln_i(\bar \gamma(t_i))).$$
For a closed curve
$\gamma$
we have
$\bar \gamma(c) = \bar \gamma(d)$
. Since any two branches of the logarithm differ by a integral multiple of
$2\pi i$
on the intersection of their domains, we see that
$$\int_\gamma \frac{\gamma'(t)}{\gamma(t)-a}dt = 2k \pi i$$
for some
$k \in \mathbb Z$
.

To avoid to use partitions of
$[c,d]$
, Conway uses a "trick" as explained in Lucien Jaccon's answer. This gives a very elegant proof, at the price of not being really intuitive.

question:
Problem proposed for JBMO practice in symmetrical inequalities (Chebyshev, rearrangement):

For every positive real numbers
$a, b, c$
, for which
$a + b + c = 3$
we have:

$$\sum_{cyc} \frac{1}{a^6 + b^6 + 3c^3 + 4} \leq \frac{3}{3 + 2(\sqrt{ab} + \sqrt{bc} + \sqrt{ca})}$$




My attempt:


Step 1


AM-GM states that
$$a^6 + b^6 \geq 2a^3b^3$$


Therefore,
$$ LHS \leq \sum_{cyc} \frac{1}{2a^3b^3 + 2c^3 + 2 + c^3 + 2}$$


Step 2


AM-GM states that
$2a^3b^3 + 2c^3 + 2 \geq 6abc$
and that
$c^3 + 1 + 1 \geq 3c$


This means that
$$ LHS \leq \sum_{cyc} \frac{1}{6abc + 3a} = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{cyc} \frac{1}{2abc + a}$$


Step 3


Rearrangement inequality states that
$$\sqrt{ab} + \sqrt{bc} + \sqrt{ca} \leq a + b + c = 3$$

This means:
$$ RHS \geq \frac{1}{3}$$


Remaining of the proof and further ideas


We should prove
$$ \sum_{cyc} \frac{1}{2abc + a} \leq 1$$

I should probably apply Chebyshev but I could get no good result after applying its variants...

Thanks in advance!
answer:
By AM-GM, we have
$\sqrt{ab} + \sqrt{bc} + \sqrt{ca} \le a + b + c = 3$
. Thus,
$\mathrm{RHS} \ge \frac{1}{3}$
.

By Chebyshev's sum inequality, we have
\begin{align}
a^3 + b^3 + c^3 - (a+b+c) &= (a-1)(a^2 + a) + (b-1)(b^2+b) + (c-1)(c^2+c)\\
&\ge \frac{1}{3}(a-1 + b-1 + c-1)(a^2+a + b^2+b + c^2 + c)\\
& = 0.
\end{align}
Thus, we have
$a^3 + b^3 + c^3 \ge a + b + c = 3$
. Thus, we have
\begin{align}
a^6 + b^6 + 3c^3 + 4 &= (a^6 + 1) + (b^6 + 1) + 3c^3 + 2\\
&\ge 2a^3 + 2b^3 + 3c^3 + 2\\
&= 2(a^3 + b^3 + c^3) + c^3 + 2\\
&\ge 8 + c^3.
\end{align}
Thus,
$\mathrm{LHS} \le \sum_{\mathrm{cyc}} \frac{1}{8 + c^3}$
.

It suffices to prove that
$$\sum_{\mathrm{cyc}} \frac{1}{8 + c^3} \le \frac{1}{3}$$
or (tangent line method)
$$\sum_{\mathrm{cyc}} \left(\frac{1}{8 + c^3} - \frac{1}{9} + \frac{1}{27}(c-1) \right) \le 0$$
or
$$\sum_{\mathrm{cyc}} \frac{(c^2-2c-5)(c-1)^2}{27(8 + c^3)} \le 0$$
which is true since
$x^2-2x-5 < 0$
for all
$0 \le x \le 3$
.

Problem proposed for JBMO practice in symmetrical inequalities (Chebyshev, rearrangement):

For every positive real numbers
$a, b, c$
, for which
$a + b + c = 3$
we have:

$$\sum_{cyc} \frac{1}{a^6 + b^6 + 3c^3 + 4} \leq \frac{3}{3 + 2(\sqrt{ab} + \sqrt{bc} + \sqrt{ca})}$$




My attempt:


Step 1


AM-GM states that
$$a^6 + b^6 \geq 2a^3b^3$$


Therefore,
$$ LHS \leq \sum_{cyc} \frac{1}{2a^3b^3 + 2c^3 + 2 + c^3 + 2}$$


Step 2


AM-GM states that
$2a^3b^3 + 2c^3 + 2 \geq 6abc$
and that
$c^3 + 1 + 1 \geq 3c$


This means that
$$ LHS \leq \sum_{cyc} \frac{1}{6abc + 3a} = \frac{1}{3} \sum_{cyc} \frac{1}{2abc + a}$$


Step 3


Rearrangement inequality states that
$$\sqrt{ab} + \sqrt{bc} + \sqrt{ca} \leq a + b + c = 3$$

This means:
$$ RHS \geq \frac{1}{3}$$


Remaining of the proof and further ideas


We should prove
$$ \sum_{cyc} \frac{1}{2abc + a} \leq 1$$

I should probably apply Chebyshev but I could get no good result after applying its variants...

Thanks in advance!
answer:
$$
\sum_{cyc}\frac{1}{a^6+b^6+3c^3+4}\overset{\text{AM-GM}}{\leq}\sum_{cyc}\frac{1}{(6a-5)+(6b-5)+3(3c-2)+4}=\sum_{cyc}\frac{1}{3(c+2)}
$$

$$
=\frac{1}{6}\sum_{cyc}\left(1-\frac{c}{c+2}\right)=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{6}\sum_{cyc}\frac{c}{c+2}\overset{\text{C-S}}{\leq}\frac{1}{2}-\frac{1}{6}\frac{\left(\sum\limits_{cyc}\sqrt{a} \right)^2}{9}=\frac{1}{2}-\frac{3+2\sum\limits_{cyc}\sqrt{ab}}{54}=\frac{12-X}{27}\overset{?}{\leq}\frac{3}{3+2X}.
$$
Where
$X=\sum\limits_{cyc}\sqrt{ab}\overset{\text{AM-GM}}{\leq}\sum\limits_{cyc}a=3$
, thus it's enough to show that

$$
(3-X)(15-2X)\geq 0.
$$

Which is obviously true.

Improve this question

While exploring the area of a regular
$n$
-gon inscribed in a unit circle (
$r=1$
), I arrived at the equation:

$$\frac{n}{2}\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{n}\right) = 1$$


or equivalently:


$$n\sin\left(\frac{2\pi}{n}\right) = 2$$


The numerical solution is approximately
$n \approx 2.309\dots$


My questions:


$1$
.  Is this solution a transcendental number?


$2$
.  Does it have a known relationship with
$e$
or
$\pi$
?


$3$
. Is this constant already known in mathematical literature?
answer:
Let
$\alpha = 2/n$
.
Substitute to get
$$\sin(\pi\alpha) = \alpha$$
If
$\alpha$
is rational, this contradicts
Niven's Theorem
which states that for a rational number
$\alpha$
, the only possible rational values for
$\sin(\pi\alpha)$
are
$\{0, \pm 1/2, \pm 1\}$
.

If
$\alpha$
is irrational and algebraic,
$e^{i\pi\alpha}$
is transcendental by the
Gelfond-Schneider Theorem
.  However, using
Euler's formula
, we can rewrite the equation as
$$e^{i\pi\alpha} = i\alpha \pm \sqrt{1 - \alpha^2}$$
Which implies
$e^{i\pi\alpha}$
is algebraic. This is a contradiction so
$\alpha$
must be transcendental, and then so is
$n$
.

question:
Let
$M$
be an abelian group, and consider a “projective resolution”

$$
0 \to M \to P_0 \to P_1 \to \cdots \to P_n \to 0.
$$

Note that this is not a projective resolution in the usual sense since it's going the wrong way! But I claim that there must exist a projective resolution
$P_\bullet \to M$
going the right way. The argument is as follows. The exactness of the above sequence exhibits a quasi-isomorphism
$M \to P_\bullet$
. This arrow must be invertible in the derived category, and since
$P_\bullet$
is “cofibrant” (here I am using the boundedness of
$P_\bullet$
), there must exist a map
$P_\bullet \to M$
realizing this inverse. Thus,
$P_\bullet$
is a projective resolution of
$M$
in the usual sense.

Is this correct? I'm mainly asking as a sanity check since this situation seems so strange to me.
answer:
What you claim is correct, but not particularly interesting, since the only
$M$
that have a "projective resolution" in the sense you describe are the projective ones (the map
$P_{n-1}\to P_n$
splits, and now work by induction), so the "projective resolution" is of the form
$$0\to M\to M\oplus Q_0\to Q_0\oplus Q_1\to\dots\to Q_{n-1}\oplus Q_n\to Q_n\to 0.$$

question:
We consider infinite binary sequences
$x \in[0,1]$
via their binary expansion.

$O: \{0,1\}^* \rightarrow  \{0,1\}^*$
maps finite binary strings to finite binary strings.

for a string
$m$
we define:

$$
s_O(x,m) = \left\{
\begin{array}{ll}
0 \mbox{    if O(m) is not a prefix of x }\\
|O(m)|- |m| \mbox{ otherwise}
\end{array}
\right.
$$


The question: is there such an
$O$
such that:
$\mathbb{P}_{x \mbox{ uniform in [0,1]}} (\mbox{sup}_m (s_O(m,x)) = + \infty) > 0$




I ve a ton of questions on
$\mbox{sup}_m (s_O(m,x))$
, this one has to come first.


edited:
$|O(m)|- \mbox{ln}_2(m) \rightarrow |O(m)|- \mbox{ln}_2(|m|) \rightarrow |O(m)|- \mbox |m|$
sorry

Thank you Brian for the parantheses editing.
answer:
Yes, such an
$O$
exists. In fact, one can construct
$O$
so that
$\mathbb{P}\!\left(\sup_m s_O(x,m)=+\infty\right)=1$
.

Here is one explicit construction.

For each integer
$j\ge 1$
, define
$k_j:=\lceil \log_2 j\rceil$
.
Then
$k_j\to\infty$
as
$j\to\infty$
, but it grows slowly enough that
$\sum_{j=1}^\infty 2^{-k_j}=\infty$
. Next choose integers
$n_j$
so that the blocks of coordinates we use are disjoint. For example, set
$n_1:=0$
and
$n_{j+1}:=n_j+k_j+1$
.Then the intervals
$$I_j:=\{n_j+1,\dots,n_j+k_j\}$$
are pairwise disjoint. Now define the map
$O:{0,1}^*\to{0,1}^*$
as follows. If a finite binary string
$m$
has length
$|m|=n_j$
for some
$j$
, then let
$O(m):=m\,0^{k_j}$
,
that is, append
$k_j$
zeros to the end of
$m$
. For all other strings
$m$
, define
$O(m):=m$
.

We now show that this choice works. Let
$x=x_1x_2x_3\cdots$
be a random infinite binary sequence, with independent fair bits. For each
$j$
, consider the event
$E_j:=\{x_{n_j+1}=\cdots=x_{n_j+k_j}=0\}$
.
This is the event that the block of bits of
$x$
on the interval
$I_j$
consists entirely of zeros. Since the intervals
$I_j$
are pairwise disjoint, the events
$E_j$
are independent. Also, because each bit is
$0$
with probability
$1/2$
, we have
$\mathbb{P}(E_j)=2^{-k_j}$
.

Now suppose that
$E_j$
occurs. Let
$m$
be the prefix of
$x$
of length
$n_j$
, namely
$m=x_1x_2\cdots x_{n_j}$
.
By construction,
$O(m)=m\,0^{k_j}$
.
Because
$E_j$
says that the next
$k_j$
bits of
$x$
are all zero, it follows that
$O(m)$
is a prefix of
$x$
.

Therefore, from the definition of
$s_O(x,m)$
, we get:
$$s_O(x,m)=|O(m)|-|m|=(n_j+k_j)-n_j=k_j$$
So whenever
$E_j$
occurs, there exists some
$m$
such that
$s_O(x,m)=k_j$
.
Hence
$$\sup_m s_O(x,m)\ge k_j$$
on the event
$E_j$
. Now
$\sum_{j=1}^\infty \mathbb{P}(E_j)=\sum_{j=1}^\infty 2^{-k_j}=\infty$
,
and the events
$E_j$
are independent. Therefore, by the second Borel--Cantelli lemma, with probability
$1$
, infinitely many of the events
$E_j$
occur. But whenever
$E_j$
occurs, we have
$\sup_m s_O(x,m)\ge k_j$
,
and since
$k_j\to\infty$
, these lower bounds become arbitrarily large. Therefore, with probability
$1$
,
$\sup_m s_O(x,m)=+\infty$
. So the answer to your question is yes, and in fact one can achieve the stronger statement
$$\boxed{\mathbb{P}\!\left(\sup_m s_O(x,m)=+\infty\right)=1.}$$

EDIT:
Only one part needs to be corrected. The construction of
$O$
stays the same, and the Borel--Cantelli argument stays the same. The only point that needs to be replaced is the step where one justifies that the modified score still goes to
$+\infty$
after subtracting the penalty term
$\ln|m|$
. The correct statement to insert is the following. On the event
$E_j$
, if
$m$
is the prefix of
$x$
of length
$n_j$
, then:
$$s_O(x,m)=k_j-\ln n_j$$

Therefore it is enough to prove that
$k_j-\ln n_j\to+\infty$
. Now
$n_j=1+\sum_{i=1}^{j-1}(k_i+1)$
,
and since
$k_i=\lceil \log_2 i\rceil\le \log_2 i+1$
,
we get
$n_j\le 1+\sum_{i=1}^{j-1}(\log_2 i+2)$
. In particular, there exists a constant
$C>0$
such that for all sufficiently large
$j$
,
$n_j\le C_j\log j$
. Taking natural logarithms gives
$\ln n_j\le \ln C+\ln j+\ln\ln j$
. On the other hand,
$k_j=\lceil \log_2 j\rceil\ge \log_2 j=\dfrac{\ln j}{\ln 2}$
. Hence:
$$k_j-\ln n_j
\ge
\dfrac{\ln j}{\ln 2}-(\ln C+\ln j+\ln\ln j)$$
Equivalently,
$$k_j-\ln n_j\ge\left(\dfrac{1}{\ln 2}-1\right)\ln j-\ln\ln j-\ln C$$
Since
$\dfrac{1}{\ln 2}-1>0$
and
$\ln\ln j=o(\ln j)$
, the right-hand side tends to
$+\infty$
. Therefore
$k_j-\ln n_j\to+\infty$
.
