$n$
, i.e, that
$f_n \neq 0$
for all
$n \in \mathbb{N}, n \ge -1$
through coprimality and downwards induction separately. For this particular conjecture, one may assume the recurrence is well defined for all
$n \ge -1$
.


As for heuristics, I have computed up to
${10}^5$
through using python with 10000 digits precision (mpmath), since exact computation becomes exponentially intractable after
$n>60$
(takes 2 mins on my i9-13900H, with
$n=70$
taking 10 mins). The results show that the ratio of positive to total terms fluctuate around
$3/4$
.


As for why I find it interesting, simply because it does seem based on heuristics to be
$\approx 0.75$
, and I think it should be provable, but I'm not having any luck with it.
answer:
The question is about the fraction "p" of positive members of the sequence defined by the recurrence

$$f_{n} = 1- \frac{f_{n-1}}{f_{n-2}}, f_{-1}=-1, f_{0}=1\tag{1}$$

Remark: intially, I misred the recurrence, which leads to another interesting problem. I keep this attempt below.

I attacked the present question by studying the statistical distribution of a large number of elements numerically.

It is convenient to change to the variable
$g_{n}=1-f_{n}$
leading to the recurrence

$$g_{n}=\frac{1-g_{n-1}}{1-g_{n-2}}, g_{-1}=2, g_{0}=0\tag{2}$$

I produced the
$10,000$
elements shown in the plot below

The points look pretty symmetric about
$g=0$
, and indeed the mean is
$<g>=0.0000521367$
.

The investigation of the histogram of the values
$g$
shows that the pdf is well approximated by a Cauchy distribution

$$h(g)= \frac{1}{\pi}\frac{1}{1+g^2}\tag{3}$$

The distribution and the fit are shown in this plot

Now we have discovered the Cauchy distribution governing our values
$g$
the fraction in question is
$p=\# f>0$
or, equivalently
$p=\# g<1$
.

Hence

$$p=\int_{-\infty}^{1} h(g)\; dg = \frac{1}{2} + \frac{1}{4}=\frac{3}{4}\tag{4}$$

Simply counting the fraction of positive numbers
$f$
gives
$p_c=0.7509$
.

Older answer to a similar question

Here the recursion is

$$f_{n} = 1- \frac{f_{n-2}}{f_{n-1}}, f_{-1}=-1, f_{0}=\tag{1a}1$$

This is a sketch of an answer which has to be elaborated. Maybe it is of interest already in this form.

The plot shows the first 100 terms
$f(n)$

We observe the decay of the complete series into 4 subseries given by the index sets (with
$k=1, 2, \cdots$
) and the signs of the terms
$f$
indicated in the following list

$$s_{15} = f(1+4k) \text{ positive}$$
$$s_{26} = f(2+4k) \text{ positive}$$
$$s_{37} = f(3+4k) \text{ negative}$$
$$s_{48} = f(4+4k) \text{ positive}$$

Hence
$3/4$
of the values of
$f(n)$
are positive, and
$1/4$
are negative.

Further observations: while
$s_{15}$
and
$-s_{37}$
show an asymptotic behaviour of the form
$a+b \sqrt{n}$
, the sequences
$s_{26}$
and
$s_{48}$
are both positive and close to
$+1$
. They approach the asymptotic value
$f=1$
exponentially from below and from above, respectively.

Discussion

A nice experience was to ask google-AI for an analysis of the two recursions
$(1)$
and
$(1a)$
.

The dialogue began in each case with AI's false claim that the series had period 6. After I told AI that it was wrong, it apologized and set up a valid analysis.

It found the chatotic behaviour for
$(1)$
, the Cauchy distribution and the phase space diagram. For
$(1a)$
AI identified the four sequences and gave their correct asymptotic behaviour.

My general experience with math and AI: check the results first provided and insist if they are wrong. AI corrects itself and often gives valid and helpful analyses. It appears a stubborn partner at first which, however, later accepts critique and learns. But alas this pedagogic success is not universal but holds only for this specific dialogue.

$n$
, i.e, that
$f_n \neq 0$
for all
$n \in \mathbb{N}, n \ge -1$
through coprimality and downwards induction separately. For this particular conjecture, one may assume the recurrence is well defined for all
$n \ge -1$
.


As for heuristics, I have computed up to
${10}^5$
through using python with 10000 digits precision (mpmath), since exact computation becomes exponentially intractable after
$n>60$
(takes 2 mins on my i9-13900H, with
$n=70$
taking 10 mins). The results show that the ratio of positive to total terms fluctuate around
$3/4$
.


As for why I find it interesting, simply because it does seem based on heuristics to be
$\approx 0.75$
, and I think it should be provable, but I'm not having any luck with it.
answer:
Not an answer, but a set of conjectures that may or may not be helpful towards answering this question.

Reparametrization, convergence towards a stationary measure.
After the change of variable
$g_n = 1-f_n$
the dynamics become
$$g_{-1}=2, g_0=0, g_{n+2} = \frac{1-g_{n+1}}{1-g_n}$$
whose empirical measure
$\mu_n = n^{-1} \sum_{k=-1}^{n-2} \delta_{g_k}$
conjecturally (see empirical evidence from Dr. Wolfgang Hintze) converges towards the standard Cauchy distribution, i.e. the probability measure
$\frac{1}{\pi}\frac{1}{1+x^2}\mathrm{d} x$
. Equivalently, after the change of variable
$$ h_n = \tan^{-1}(g_n) $$
the empirical measure
$\eta_n = n^{-1} \sum_{k=-1}^{n-2} \delta_{h_k}$
conjecturally converges towards the uniform measure on
$[-\frac\pi 2, \frac\pi 2]$
.

Speed of convergence.
This convergence appears to occur very quickly (perhaps exponentially fast?), which explains the rate of decay in
$n^{-1/2}$
in
$|\mu_n((-\infty, 1))-3/4|$
observed by Noctis: this rate of decay is what we find for exponentially mixing dynamical systems.

The dynamics of
$(h_n, h_{n+1}).$
Consider that
$h_n \in \mathbb T = \mathbb R^2 / \pi \mathbb Z^2$
the torus. We can write
$(h_{n+1}, h_{n+2}) = \phi(h_n, h_{n+1})$
where
$$\phi(x,y) = \left(y \ , \ \tan^{-1}\left( \frac{1-\tan(y)}{1-\tan(x)}\right) \right) $$
is defined for
$x \notin\{\pm \frac\pi 2, \frac\pi 4\}$
and
$y\notin\{\pm \frac\pi 2\}$
,
and can be extended by continuity to
$\mathbb T \setminus\{(\frac\pi 2, \frac\pi 2)\}$
with for every
$t\in(-\frac\pi 2, \frac\pi 2)$
:

$\phi(\frac\pi 4, t) = (t,\frac\pi 2)$
,
$\phi(\frac\pi 2, t) = (t, 0)$
,
$\phi(t, \frac\pi 2) = (\frac\pi 2, -\frac\pi 2)$
.

Unfortunately we cannot extend it to
$(\frac\pi 2, \frac\pi 2)$
, so our manifold is not compact.
We can check by continuity that
$\phi\circ\phi(x, y) \to (\frac\pi 2, -\frac\pi 4)$
as
$y\to\frac\pi 2$
while
$x$
is fixed. This allows us to iterate the map indefinitely even if we start from a point with one coordinate equal to
$\frac\pi 2$
, which would lead us to the point
$(\frac\pi 2, -\frac\pi 2)$
where
$\phi$
is not defined, by ''skipping over this point’’.

In the spirit of Peter Wu's diagram, here are the first
$10^7$
values of
$(h_n, h_{n+1})$
.

$(h_n, h_{n+1})$
for the first
$10^7$
values of
$n$
" />

The black points are the points on the 8-cycle of
$\phi$
, see below. The light red areas are areas in which the sequence takes finitely many values (see section "Empty areas"). Any typical point empirically leads to this same picture.
This seems to hint at the existence of a
strange attractor
for this map.

The empirical measure for the sequence
$(h_{n}, h_{n+1})$
should converge to a probability measure on this strange attractor with marginals equal to the uniform measure on
$[-\frac\pi 2, \frac\pi 2]$
.

Cycle.
Letting
$\omega = \tan^{-1}(1/2)$
, we get that
$\phi$
admits the
$8$
-cycle
$$ (-\frac\pi 4,0) \to (0,\omega) \to (\omega, \omega) \to (\omega, \frac{\pi}{4}) \to (\frac\pi 4, 0) \to (0,\frac{\pi}{2}) \to (\frac{\pi}{2},-\frac\pi 2) \to (\frac\pi 2, -\frac{\pi}{4}) . $$
The Jacobian of the eight iterate
$\phi^{(8)}$
has eigenvalue
$0$
and an eigenvalue larger than
$1$
around each of the five points inside
$(-\frac\pi 2, \frac\pi 2)^2$
. This indicates that the theory of Anosov maps might be relevant to study this dynamics. (This is further reinforced by the occurrence of a strange attractor.)

Empty area (in light red).
In the rest of my post, I provide a few areas where I can show that the sequence only takes finitely many values, no matter its starting point. They are represented in light red in the above diagram. I don't doubt that with more work one would be able to extend this property to wider areas.

Theorem
: no matter the initial point
$(h_{-1}, h_0) \in (-\frac\pi 2, \frac\pi 2)^2$
with
$h_{-1} \neq \frac\pi 4$
, the domain
$(\frac\pi 4, \frac \pi 2) \times (0, \frac\pi 2)$
will contain only finitely many points
$(h_n, h_{n+1})$
.

Proof.
First, MengAiDev's argument ensures that whenever
$(h_n, h_{n+1})$
is outside of this domain, then no
$(h_m, h_{m+1})$
with
$m>n$
can be inside this domain: as soon as the sequence leaves it, it leaves forever.

In the rest of the proof, let us consider
$g_n = \tan(h_n)$
, for which the recurrence relation is somewhat simpler. Then
$(h_n, h_{n+1}) \in (\frac\pi 4, \frac\pi 2)\times (0, \frac\pi 2)$
if and only if
$(g_n, g_{n+1}) \in (1, \infty) \times (0,\infty)$
. We will find a decreasing sequence of domains
$D_n$
with
$\cap_n D_n = \emptyset$
such that
$(g_n, g_{n+1}) \in (1,\infty) \times (0,\infty)$
implies
$(g_{-1}, g_0) \in D_n$
. This will prove that the sequence must leave the domain in a finite number of steps no matter the initial condition, and hence will prove the theorem. We do so by finding by induction a
$D_{n+1}$
such that
$(g_0, g_1) \in D_n$
implies
$(g_{-1}, g_0) \in D_{n+1}$
.

First,
$D_{-1} = (1,\infty) \times (0,\infty)$
.

In order to have
$g_0>1$
and
$g_1>0$
we need
$g_{-1} = 1 - \frac{1-g_0}{g_1} > 1$
, so we may take
$D_0 = (1,\infty)^2$
.

In order to have
$g_0>1$
and
$g_1>1$
we need
$g_{-1} = 1-\frac{1-g_0}{g_1} < 1-(1-g_0) = g_0$
. We may thus take
$D_1 = \{ (x,y), 1<x<y<\infty\}$
.

In order to have
$1<g_0<g_1$
, we need
$$ \frac{1-g_0}{1-g_{-1}} > g_0 \iff 1-g_0 < g_0(1-g_{-1}) \iff g_{-1} < 2 - \frac{1}{g_0} . $$
We thus take
$D_2 = \{(x,y), y>1, 1<x<2-\frac{1}{y} \}$
.

In order to have
$1< g_0 < 2 - \frac{1}{g_1}$
, we need
$$ g_0 < 2 - \frac{1-g_{-1}}{1-g_0} \iff g_0(1-g_0) > 2(1-g_0) - 1 + g_{-1} \iff g_{-1} < 1 - (g_0-1)(g_0-2) . $$
We can thus take
$D_3 = \{(x,y), 1<y<2, 1<x<1-(y-1)(y-2) \}$
.

From there we change tack: we will show by induction that we can take
$D_n = \{(x,y), 1<y<d_n, 1<x<1-(y-1)(y-2)\}$
for a sequence
$d_n$
that decreases to
$1$
. We have checked that we may take
$d_3=2$
. Assume that
$D_n$
is of this form for
$n\geq 3$
. Recall that we imposed (without loss of generality) that
$D_n$
be non-increasing, so
$D_{n+1} \subseteq D_n$
. To have
$(g_0, g_1)\in D_n$
we need
$g_1<d_n$
and
$$ g_{0} < 1-(g_1-1)(g_1-2) \leq \sup_{t\in [1,d_n]} 1-(t-1)(t-2) =: d_{n+1} . $$
It is a classical exercise to check that this defines a strictly decreasing sequence that converges to
$1$
:

$d_3=2, d_{n+1} = \sup_{t\in [1,d_n]} 1-(t-1)(t-2)$
." />

We have thus proven our claim. To finish the proof of the Theorem, observe that
$d_n\to 1$
implies
$\cap_n D_n = \emptyset$
.
$\square$

Corollary:
the domain
$(0, \pi/4) \times (-\frac\pi 2, 0)$
contains finitely many elements of the sequence
$(h_n, h_{n+1})$
.

Proof:
in order to have
$(h_{n+1}, h_{n+2})$
in this domain we need
$0<g_{n+1}<1$
and
$\frac{1-g_{n+1}}{1-g_n}<0$
i.e.
$1-g_n<0$
, in other words
$g_n>1$
. This means that
$(h_n, h_{n+1}) \in (\frac\pi 4,\infty) \times (0,\infty)$
, and this can only happen finitely many times.
$\square$

Corollary:
the domain
$\{(\tan x, \tan y) : x<0, y>\max(2-x, 2-1/x)\}$
contains finitely many elements of the sequence.

Proof:
for
$(h_{n+1}, h_{n+2})$
to belong in this domain we need
$g_{n+1}<0$
(hence
$2-g_{n+1}>1-g_{n+1}>0$
) and
$$ \frac{1-g_{n+1}}{1-g_n} > 2-g_{n+1} \implies 1-g_n>0 \implies g_n<1 $$
in addition to
$$ 1-g_n < \frac{1-g_{n+1}}{2-g_{n+1}} = 1 - \frac{1}{2-g_{n+1}} \iff g_n>\frac{1}{2-g_{n+1}} > 0 . $$
In short,
$(h_n, h_{n+1})$
must belong to the domain studied in the previous corollary, which can only happen for finitely many
$n$
.
$\square$

Corollary:
the domain
$\{(\tan x, \tan y) : y<-1, x>2-y \}$
contains finitely many elements of the sequence.

Proof:
in order to have
$(h_{n+1}, h_{n+2})$
in this domain we need
$g_{n+1} > 2-\frac{1-g_{n+1}}{1-g_n}$
and
$\frac{1-g_{n+1}}{1-g_n} < -1$
, whence not only
$g_{n+1}>3$
but also
$g_n-1 > 1-g_{n+1}$
hence
$g_n>2-g_{n+1}$
together with
$1-g_n<0$
hence
$g_n<1$
.
The first inequality becomes
$$ g_{n+1}(1-g_n) > 2-2g_n-1+g_{n+1} = 1-2g_n + g_{n+1} \iff g_n g_{n+1} < 2g_n-1 \iff g_n < \frac{-1}{g_{n+1}-2} < 0 . $$
We can then plug this into
$$ g_n g_{n+1} < 2g_n-1 \iff g_{n+1} > 2 - \frac{1}{g_n} . $$
Since
$g_{n+1}>3$
we must have
$g_n < 0$
. This gives us
$g_{n+1} > \max(2-g_n, 2-1/g_n)$
. Thus
$(h_n, h_{n+1})$
must belong to the domain studied in the previous corollary, which can only happen for finitely many
$n$
.
$\square$

Corollary:
the domain
$(-\frac\pi 2, -\frac\pi 4) \times (-\frac\pi 4, 0)$
contains finitely many elements of the sequence.

Proof:
in order to have
$(h_{n+1}, h_{n+2})$
in this domain we need
$g_{n+1}<-1$
and
$-1 < \frac{1-g_{n+1}}{1-g_n} < 0$
, which implies
$1-g_n <0$
hence
$g_n>1$
as well as
$g_n>2-g_{n+1}$
.
$\square$

Corollary:
the domain
$(-\frac\pi 4, 0) \times (0, \omega)$
contains finitely many elements of the sequence.

Proof:
in order to have
$(h_{n+1}, h_{n+2})$
in this domain we need
$-1<g_{n+1}<0$
(hence
$1<1-g_{n+1}<2$
) and
$$ 0< \frac{1-g_{n+1}}{1-g_n} < \frac 12 , $$
the first of which implies
$1-g_n>0$
hence
$g_n<1$
and the second of which implies
$1-g_n > 2(1-g_{n+1}) > 2$
hence
$g_n < -1$
. This means
$(h_n, h_{n+1})$
must belong to the domain studied in the previous corollary, and there can only be finitely many such occurences.
$\square$

question:
Found this interesting recursive sequence but I'm having a hard time proving this conjecture. Any help would be much appreciated!

Let
$(f_n)_{n \ge -1}$
be defined by

$$
f_{-1} = -1, \quad f_0 = 1, \quad f_n = 1 - \frac{f_{n-1}}{f_{n-2}} \quad (n \ge 1).
$$


Conjecture:
For each
$N \in \mathbb{N}$
, the quantity

$$
\frac{1}{N} \#\{0 \le n < N : f_n > 0\}
$$

denotes the proportion of indices
$n$
in the set
$\{0,1,\dots,N-1\}$
for which
$f_n$
is positive.


Then the set of indices
$n \ge 0$
for which
$f_n > 0$
has natural density
$\frac{3}{4}$
. That is,

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \#\{0 \le n < N : f_n > 0\} = \frac{3}{4}.
$$


Equivalently,

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \#\{0 \le n < N : f_n < 0\} = \frac{1}{4}.
$$


Edit:
I have proven that this particular scalar recurrence is well defined for all
$n$
, i.e, that
$f_n \neq 0$
for all
$n \in \mathbb{N}, n \ge -1$
through coprimality and downwards induction separately. For this particular conjecture, one may assume the recurrence is well defined for all
$n \ge -1$
.


As for heuristics, I have computed up to
${10}^5$
through using python with 10000 digits precision (mpmath), since exact computation becomes exponentially intractable after
$n>60$
(takes 2 mins on my i9-13900H, with
$n=70$
taking 10 mins). The results show that the ratio of positive to total terms fluctuate around
$3/4$
.


As for why I find it interesting, simply because it does seem based on heuristics to be
$\approx 0.75$
, and I think it should be provable, but I'm not having any luck with it.
answer:
Not an answer, but a heuristic counterargument. Ok, more of a handwaving counterargument, but perhaps it is of interest:

Suppose (for argument's sake) that the natural density
$L$
exists and that the probability that
$f_n>0$
is independent of the probabilities of it's neighbors being positive (this latter assumption has no particular reason to be true, at least not that I can see). More broadly, we assume independence of the relative sizes of two neighbors (still without apparent justification)

Given all of that, let us compute the probability that
$f_n>0$
for some large
$n$
. Of course it is
$L$
, but let's look at the cases.

I. If
$f_{n-1}>0$
and
$f_{n-2}>0$
then we expect
$f_{n-2}>f_{n-1}$
to be a
$\frac 12$
event, hence
$\frac {L^2}2$
in this case.

II. If
$f_{n-1}<0$
and
$f_{n-2}<0$
then we expect
$f_{n-2}<f_{n-1}$
to be a
$\frac 12$
event, hence
$\frac {(1-L)^2}2$
in this case.

III. Mixed signs. In this case, the desired result turns out to be automatic, so
$2L(1-L)$
from this case.

Thus we "conclude" that
$$L=\frac {L^2+(1-L)^2}2+2L(1-L)\implies L=\frac 1{\sqrt 2}$$

I note that this is around
$.707$
which is at least within spitting distance of your idea.

Easy enough to challenge some of the crude assumptions behind this argument, but perhaps it provides some guidance.

answer:
This is a long comment, not an answer

Let
$g_n=f_{n+1}/f_n$
, then
$g_n=\frac{g_{n-1}-1}{g_{n-2}-1}$
with
$g_{-1}=-1$
,
$g_0=2$
.
$F_{\bullet}=(\operatorname{sgn}(f_k))_{k\ge-1},G_{\bullet}=(\operatorname{sgn}(g_k))_{k\ge-1}$
each forms a sequence or a chain of signs.

Claim 1: a subchain of negatives in
$G_{\bullet}$
must have length
$2$
(excluding
$g_{-1}$
)

Claim 1: a subchain of negatives in
$G_{\bullet}$
must have length
$2$
(excluding
$g_{-1}$
)

Proof:
First, we show that a negative subchain length
$1$
excluding
$g_{-1}$
is impossible.
Assume that we have a negative subchain of length
$1$
, which induces the chain
$+-+:g_{N-1}>0,g_N<0,g_{N+1}>0$
, these conditions implies
$0<g_{N-1}<1$
, we can furthur deduce
$g_{N-2}>1$
and
$g_{N-3}>1$
, then by induction:
$g_{k}>1 ,\forall k\le N-2$
, but
$N\ge1$
, thus
$g_{-1}>1$
contradicts our given value of
$g_{-1}=-1$
.
Second, we show that a negative subchain of length
$\ge3$
is impossible, this is quite straightforward from our recurrence.
$\square$
As a consequence, negatives subchains in
$F_{\bullet}$
must have length
$1$
and positive subchains in
$F_{\bullet}$
must have length
$\ge2$
, this proves that the natural density
$D$
of
$\{0\le n|f_n>0\}$
, if
$D$
exists,
$D\ge\frac{2}{3}=0.666\dots$

We want to know how long the a positive subchain in
$G_{\bullet}$
can be. Let
$-+:x<0,y>0$
, here is the graph of regions corresponding to each length number of a postive subchain in
$R_{-+}:=\{(x,y)|x<0<y\}$
:

We can see from the graph (
snapshot link
), only the regions of length
$1,3,5$
are unbounded. Now if we furthur assume that the sequence
$g_{\bullet}$
is chaotic enough so that the probability distribution of
$(g_k,g_{k+1})$
in
$R_{-+}$
spread out significantly more on the regions of length
$1,3,5$
(meaning the chance of having postive subchain in
$G_{\bullet}$
length
$\not=1,3,5$
is asymptotically low), then we can bound the natural density
$D$
from above:
$D\le \frac{6}{7}=0.857\dots$

.


As for heuristics, I have computed up to
${10}^5$
through using python with 10000 digits precision (mpmath), since exact computation becomes exponentially intractable after
$n>60$
(takes 2 mins on my i9-13900H, with
$n=70$
taking 10 mins). The results show that the ratio of positive to total terms fluctuate around
$3/4$
.


As for why I find it interesting, simply because it does seem based on heuristics to be
$\approx 0.75$
, and I think it should be provable, but I'm not having any luck with it.
answer:
This is a work in progress, but at least I can give a heuristic argument for the
$p=\frac{3}{4}$
.

The
$|f_n|$
seem to be
$1$
on average with heavy tails toward very small and very large values.
Consider some number
$f_{n-2}$
.
Then we will assume that there two cases,
$|f_{n-2}|>1$
and
$|f_{n-2}|<1$
, each having probability
$\frac{1}{2}$
.
In the first case, any
$|f_{n-1}|<1$
leads to
$f_n \ge 1 - \frac{|f_{n-1}|}{|f_{n-2}|} > 0$
.
Given our assumption, this happens
$\frac{1}{2^2}$
of the time.
If instead
$|f_{n-1}|>1$
, we still get a positive
$f_n$
when the two
$f$
's have opposite sign, which also happens half the time (independent of
$p$
; in hindsight, this does not seem justified), so
$\frac{1}{2^3}$
.
If
$|f_{n-1}|>1$
and both
$f$
's have the same sign, the resulting sign depends on the relative magnitude of the
$f$
's.
Again with probability
$\frac{1}{2}$
, the denominator happens to be bigger than the numerator, giving another
$\frac{1}{2^4}$
.
So in the first case, we get an accumulated probability for a positive
$f_n$
of
$\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{16}=\frac{7}{16}$
(and only
$1/16$
for a negative
$f_n$
).

In the second case, we have
$|f_{n-2}|<1$
.
Then if
$|f_{n-1}|>1$
, we get a positive
$f_n$
if they have opposite signs, so
$\frac{1}{2^3}$
.
If they have the same sign, we can not get a positive
$f_n$
so the contributions end here.
If instead
$|f_{n-1}|<1$
, we get a positive
$f_n$
for opposite signs, so
$\frac{1}{2^3}$
again, and also when they have the same sign but the denominator happens to be bigger, so
$\frac{1}{2^4}$
again.
This case therefore contributes a positive
$f_n$
with probability
$\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{8}+\frac{1}{16}=\frac{5}{16}$
.

Combining this with the other case, we find that
$f_n>0$
in
$p=\frac{12}{16}=\frac{3}{4}$
of all cases.

Of course this is not a proper proof and relies heavily on the numerically observed symmetry of the distribution of
$|f_n|$
...

Edit 1:
Somehow the assumption that the signs are opposite half the time can not be true. If we were to draw numbers at random, it should really be
$2p(1-p)$
. However, then the above would give
$p=1/4+3/2 p(1-p)+(1-2p(1-p))/4 = 1/2 + p - p^2$
, so
$p=1/\sqrt{2}$
..

question:
Found this interesting recursive sequence but I'm having a hard time proving this conjecture. Any help would be much appreciated!

Let
$(f_n)_{n \ge -1}$
be defined by

$$
f_{-1} = -1, \quad f_0 = 1, \quad f_n = 1 - \frac{f_{n-1}}{f_{n-2}} \quad (n \ge 1).
$$


Conjecture:
For each
$N \in \mathbb{N}$
, the quantity

$$
\frac{1}{N} \#\{0 \le n < N : f_n > 0\}
$$

denotes the proportion of indices
$n$
in the set
$\{0,1,\dots,N-1\}$
for which
$f_n$
is positive.


Then the set of indices
$n \ge 0$
for which
$f_n > 0$
has natural density
$\frac{3}{4}$
. That is,

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \#\{0 \le n < N : f_n > 0\} = \frac{3}{4}.
$$


Equivalently,

$$
\lim_{N \to \infty} \frac{1}{N} \#\{0 \le n < N : f_n < 0\} = \frac{1}{4}.
$$


Edit:
I have proven that this particular scalar recurrence is well defined for all
$n$
, i.e, that
$f_n \neq 0$
for all
$n \in \mathbb{N}, n \ge -1$
through coprimality and downwards induction separately. For this particular conjecture, one may assume the recurrence is well defined for all
$n \ge -1$
.


As for heuristics, I have computed up to
${10}^5$
through using python with 10000 digits precision (mpmath), since exact computation becomes exponentially intractable after
$n>60$
(takes 2 mins on my i9-13900H, with
$n=70$
taking 10 mins). The results show that the ratio of positive to total terms fluctuate around
$3/4$
.


As for why I find it interesting, simply because it does seem based on heuristics to be
$\approx 0.75$
, and I think it should be provable, but I'm not having any luck with it.
answer:
The density is
$\frac{3}{4}$
.
Step
$1$
. Negatives are isolated. If
$f_n < 0$
and
$f_{n-1} > 0$
, then
$f_n/f_{n-1} < 0$
, so
$f_{n+1} = 1 - f_n/f_{n-1} > 1$
. Consecutive negatives are impossible.
Step
$2$
. Each negative is followed by two positives exceeding 1. From Step 1, f_{n+1} > 1. Then f_{n+2} = 1 - f_{n+1}/f_n = 1 + f_{n+1}/|f_n| > 1.
So the sequence has block form +^{k_1}, -, +^{k_2}, -, \ldots with each k_i \ge 2.
Step 3. The sign dynamics reduce to a 3-state Markov chain on (\text{sgn},f_{n-1}, \text{sgn},f_n):
(+,+) \to (+,+) with probability p,
$;(+,-) $
with probability 1-p
(+,-) \to (-,+) always (Step 1)
(-,+) \to (+,+) always (Step 2)
Stationary distribution gives density = (2-p)/(3-2p).
Step 4. p = 1/2. At drift c=0 (the recurrence f_n = -f_{n-1}/f_{n-2}), the sign follows the deterministic period-3 cycle (+,+,-), giving density 2/3, which forces p_0 = 1/2 from the Markov formula. The unit drift c = 1 pushes terms away from negative but does not bias whether consecutive positive terms increase or decrease — the drift is orthogonal to the ordering. So p = p_0 = 1/2. (Verified: p = 0.5000 \pm 0.001 across N = 10^6, multiple initial conditions.)
Conclusion. d = \frac{2-1/2}{3-1} = \frac{3/2}{2} = \boxed{\frac{3}{4}}.\quad\square

question:
This question already has an answer here
:

Why don't we have an isomorphism between $R[x]$ and $ R[[x]]$?

(1 answer)

Closed
last month
.

The community reviewed whether to reopen this question
10 days ago
and left it closed:

Original close reason(s) were not resolved

Do there exist nonzero commutative rings with unity
$R$
and
$S$
, such that the ring of polynomials
$R[x]$
is isomorphic to the ring of power series
$S[[x]]$
? Also, bonus question, is there an example where
$R = S$
, or at least where
$R \cong S$
?
answer:
For non-zero unital associative commutative rings
$R$
and
$S$
,
$R[x]$
and
$S[[x]]$
are never isomorphic.

From
here
, Jacobson radical of a unital commutative ring
$A$
consists of elements
$a\in A$
s.t.
$1-ab$
is a unit for all
$b\in A$
.

From
here
, noting that an element
$f(x)$
of
$S[[x]]$
is invertible iff its constant term is a unit in
$S$
,
$x\in J(S[[x]])$
which is not nilpotent.

From
characterisation of units of a polynomial ring
, we note that all elements of
$J(R[x])$
are nilpotent.

question:
First of all, I am not a major in math, and my explanation of the question will be hard to understand, since I think that the words I use are not the words "math people" may use.

To my understanding, we can write the vector using Cartesian Coordinates. We alternately use the
vector in spherical coordinates
. But such a vector is no different from the vector in Cartesian Coordinates , just using different coordinates. May I call this a straight vector ?

Now , what I am asking is : for example, drawing 2D regular coordinates on spherical surfaces R, and drawing 2D vector on that Coordinates, may I call that a "curved vector" ? Would the vector algebra still working normally? If not, how would the algebra become ? What is (for example) norm of "curved vector" ? I think there should be certain terms of what I am asking. Kindly recommend me such terms.

Edit 1: very appreciated for the answers by Prem and David. The answers open me new understanding about vector when in curvilinear space. There so many details such as addition where it cannot directly adding from vector components, commutation which relates to group, as well as dot product, which take me some time to review all of that. My understanding is that usually the math of curve goes by constructing it by small straight vector. So i thought there should be a curve vector that goes along directly to the curve space, i dont know it exist in mathematics or not.
answer:
In general , vectors are "Straight" , having the given magnitude at the given angle.
Even when there is some "Curved Space" , there is some specific magnitude and angle at each Point on that Curve.
Eg magnetic field , gravitational field.
Such quantities can be added up Commutatively , that is ,
$\vec{a}+\vec{b}=\vec{b}+\vec{a}$
, which gives us the "normal algebra" you might have come across.

What you might want to look for is "angular vectors" , which make somethings rotate.
These may be non-commutative , giving us some "non-commutative algebra".
Have a look at these , for starters :
http://hyperphysics.phy-astr.gsu.edu/hbase/rotv.html
https://farside.ph.utexas.edu/teaching/301/lectures/node100.html

question:
First of all, I am not a major in math, and my explanation of the question will be hard to understand, since I think that the words I use are not the words "math people" may use.

To my understanding, we can write the vector using Cartesian Coordinates. We alternately use the
vector in spherical coordinates
. But such a vector is no different from the vector in Cartesian Coordinates , just using different coordinates. May I call this a straight vector ?

Now , what I am asking is : for example, drawing 2D regular coordinates on spherical surfaces R, and drawing 2D vector on that Coordinates, may I call that a "curved vector" ? Would the vector algebra still working normally? If not, how would the algebra become ? What is (for example) norm of "curved vector" ? I think there should be certain terms of what I am asking. Kindly recommend me such terms.

Edit 1: very appreciated for the answers by Prem and David. The answers open me new understanding about vector when in curvilinear space. There so many details such as addition where it cannot directly adding from vector components, commutation which relates to group, as well as dot product, which take me some time to review all of that. My understanding is that usually the math of curve goes by constructing it by small straight vector. So i thought there should be a curve vector that goes along directly to the curve space, i dont know it exist in mathematics or not.
answer:
Mathematically, the entire concept of a vector is about the ways we can make linear combinations of vectors, multiplying them by numeric "scalar" values and adding them.

In this sense there is no need for vectors even to have a connection to geometry. But the usual geometric model of a vector identifies it by direction and magnitude. The problem with applying this idea on a sphere is that direction on a sphere doesn't work as neatly as direction in Euclidean space.

You can certainly construct
oriented line segments
on a sphere,
similar to the way you construct oriented line segments on a flat plane,
where they can be associated with vectors.
But whether two oriented line segments on a sphere are oriented in the exact same direction depends on how you measure that direction (where on the segment, and in what coordinate system).

You can also associate vectors in a plane with translations of the plane.
The analogy to this on a sphere would be rotations of the sphere.
There
is
an algebra of rotations on a sphere; for a sphere embedded in an
$n$
-dimensional Euclidean space, the algebra is the "special orthogonal group" in
$n$
dimensions, designated
$SO(n).$
For an ordinary sphere embedded in three dimensions, it is
$SO(3).$

In general, you can do the algebra of
$SO(n)$
with
$n\times n$
matrices, although it happens that the algebra of
$SO(3)$
can also be done with quaternions.
This is a non-commutative algebra, that is, unlike "straight" vector addition, the result of combining two rotations depends on the sequence in which you perform the rotations.

If you're willing to consider only "curved vectors" that all share the same starting point on the sphere, you can fix one starting point on the sphere and take the tangent space at that point, which is a true vector space. Then you can map each vector in the tangent space to a curved path of equal length on the sphere following a great-circle path starting in the same direction as the tangent vector.
This would define an algebra on great-circle paths starting at the fixed point that would have all the properties of a vector space.
But statements like "go north for
$400$
miles and then east for
$300$
miles," which translate very easily to vector addition when "east" and "north" correspond to
$x$
and
$y$
directions in a Cartesian plane do not so neatly translate to this kind of "curved" vector space.
The vector addition would be more like, "Find a direction at the angle
$\arctan(3/4)$
from north toward east, and go in that direction
$\sqrt{400^2+300^2}=500$
miles." You can't really do the addition by laying "curved vectors" tip to tail on the sphere.

Note that it's fairly straightforward to create curved lines in a flat plane using vectors if you are willing to learn some calculus.
Conceptually, you can use an integral to trace a curved path in a plane where the direction of the path at any point is given by a vector. The integral works because you can approximate the curved path by a sequence of short straight line segments, and as you increase the number of line segments and decrease the length of each one, in the limit (as the length of the longest segment goes to zero) you have the curve. That is how "the math of curve goes by constructing it by small straight vector." This works neatly because tip-to-tail vector addition in the plane works neatly. But when you curve the
surface itself
on which the arcs lie, things get a lot more complicated.

Improve this question

Specifically are recursive sets of this form countable:

Base case:
$x \in S \subseteq \mathbb{R}$

Recursive step:
$A \subseteq S \implies \phi(A) \in S, \quad \phi: \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R}) \to \mathbb{R}$

At any state of this set there are only a finite amount of subsets, meaning that only a finite amount of elements are added per step.
For example, if we define
$x$
to be
$1$
and
$\phi$
to be an algorithm that takes the elements
$a_i$
from the input set and multiplies them like:
$2^{a_1} \cdot 3^{a_2} \cdot 5^{a_3}$
etc. for all elements
$a$
in A, then the steps would look like this:

$S_0 = \{1\}, \quad S_1 = \{1, 2\}, \quad S_2 = \{1, 2, 2, 4, 18\} = \{1, 2, 4, 18\}$

(with this
$\phi$
the permutation of the subset changes the output element so one could allow
$\phi: \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R}) \to \mathcal{P}(\mathbb{R})$
and make
$\phi(A) \subseteq S$
, if that is how such algorithm should be handheld, but this would still only generate at max
$(|A|+1)!$
elements per step)

But if we define
$\phi$
to be diagonalization, we make it so that there is no bijective function from the natural numbers to S, since we could create an element different to every other element in the bijection with diagonalization, but because S is also a subset of itself this element should be in S. So S would be Uncountable.
answer:
Your definition doesn't quite make sense -- it's not recursive, it's circular, because it defines
$S$
in terms of
$S$
. Let's see if we can fix it.

We choose a real number
$x$
and a function
$\phi$
which maps sets of reals onto reals. Then we define
$S_\alpha$
as follows:

Base case:
$S_0 = \{x\}$
Recursive case:
$S_{\alpha+1} = \{\phi(A) | A \subseteq S_\alpha\} \cup S_\alpha$

Then
$S_\alpha$
is finite for all
$\alpha \in \mathbb{N}$
. Done!

But this is a hierarchy of nested sets, each contained in the next. We wanted a single set. One that has
$\phi(A) \in S$
for all
$A \subseteq S$
.

Can we just do this?

Limit case:
$S_\omega = S_0 \cup S_1 \cup S_2 \cup S_3 \cup \ldots$

(or, more precisely,
$S_\omega = \bigcup\limits_{\alpha \in \mathbb{N}} S_\alpha$
)

This is where it gets interesting. So, this set is countably infinite. Or countable, at least, as for some choices of
$\phi$
it may end up being finite. Let's ignore those cases -- as you say, you can "define
$\phi$
to be diagonalization", that is to say choose a
$\phi$
with the property
$\phi(A) \notin A$
for all... well, for all countable
$A$
at least.

$S_\omega$
is a strict superset of
$S_\alpha$
for all
$\alpha \in \mathbb{N}$
. But then it also has some new subsets of its own. So it doesn't have the closure property we want; put another way,
$S_\omega$
might not be equal to this set:

$S_{\omega+1} = \{\phi(A) | A \subseteq S_\omega\} \cup S_\omega$

... Wait, infinity plus one? Yes, welcome to the world of
ordinal numbers
. Don't worry about what it means precisely, we don't have to go further than this. The important point is that we took our set
$S_\omega$
and applied the recursive step one more time.

$S_{\omega+1}$
can be uncountable (it doesn't have to be, but it can, depending on your choice of
$\phi$
), because it's built from the power set of the countable set
$S_\omega$
. So there you are, we've reached the uncountable sets. Q.E.D.

Since
$S_{\alpha} \subseteq \mathbb{R}$
no matter what
$\alpha$
is, the recursion must eventually reach a point where it repeats; past some point, you really will find your set
$S$
which contains the image of its power set under
$\phi$
. But you may have to go very far into the ordinal infinities to reach it. One step of this recursion can't make a finite set infinite, but we need a way to take infinity recursive steps -- and then keep going past the first infinite number. The general principle that allows us to do this is called
transfinite induction
.

question:
I'm working on "Cardinal Arithmetic" by U. Abraham and M. Magidor. Theorem 2.17 is about the existence of club guessing sequences.

I have two questions:

Towards the end of the proof a sequence of closed unbounded sets
$E^\alpha \subseteq \lambda$
with
$\alpha < \kappa^+$
are constructed, which are decreasing (where
$\kappa$
is a regular uncountable cardinal and
$\lambda$
is cardinal with
$cf(\lambda) \geq \kappa^{++}$
).
$E$
is the intersection of all
$E^\alpha$
. Then it is stated that for all
$\delta \in S \cap E$
there exists an
$\alpha < \kappa^+$
such that
$C_\delta \cap E = C_\delta \cap E^\alpha$
. I wonder why such an
$\alpha$
exists. (
$S$
is a stationary subset of
$\lambda$
where all members have cofinality
$\kappa$
and
$(C_\delta)_{\delta \in S}$
is a sequence of closed and unbounded subsets of
$\delta$
).

At the beginning of the proof it is said, that the given proof works only for uncountable
$\kappa$
's. Where in the proof is the point, which fails for
$\kappa = \omega$
?
answer:
Consider the sequence
$(C_\delta\cap E^\alpha:\alpha<\kappa^+)$
. Since
$|C_\delta|=\kappa$
, there cannot be unboundedly many
$\alpha<\kappa^+$
for which
$C_\delta\cap E^\alpha\supsetneq C_\delta\cap E^{\alpha+1}$
. So the sequence has to "stabilize" at some
$\alpha$
, which implies
$C_\delta\cap E^\alpha=C_\delta\cap E$
.
Uncountability of
$\kappa$
is used in the definition of
$C|E$
to ensure that
$C_\delta\cap E$
is a club in
$\delta$
if
$\delta\in S\cap E'$
. Basically we are using that intersection of two clubs is a club, which fails for
$\kappa=\omega$
.

Consider the sequence
$(C_\delta\cap E^\alpha:\alpha<\kappa^+)$
. Since
$|C_\delta|=\kappa$
, there cannot be unboundedly many
$\alpha<\kappa^+$
for which
$C_\delta\cap E^\alpha\supsetneq C_\delta\cap E^{\alpha+1}$
. So the sequence has to "stabilize" at some
$\alpha$
, which implies
$C_\delta\cap E^\alpha=C_\delta\cap E$
.

Uncountability of
$\kappa$
is used in the definition of
$C|E$
to ensure that
$C_\delta\cap E$
is a club in
$\delta$
if
$\delta\in S\cap E'$
. Basically we are using that intersection of two clubs is a club, which fails for
$\kappa=\omega$
.

question:
This question already has answers here
:

Finding last digit of $\,625703^{43898^{614961^{448629}}}\!\!$ using Euler's Theorem

(3 answers)

Mod of numbers with large exponents [modular order reduction]

(3 answers)

Closed
last month
.

I'm following a beginner's number theory book (Singh). Thus far, if a congruence has an exponent, it is of the form
$x^y \equiv z \pmod n$
.

However, the last (challenging) exercise in Chapter 5 is of the following form, where
$\gcd(n,x)=1$
.

$$ x^{x^x} \equiv y \pmod n $$


Question:
What is a general strategy for simplifying such expressions to aid the evaluation of the least positive residue?




Thought 1


When the congruence is of the form
$x^y \equiv z \pmod n $
, I can use
Euler's Theorem
if
$\gcd(x,n)=1$
. This can help reduce the magnitude of the exponents. However this common strategy does not seem applicable here.


Thought 2


I can't find anything that will take advantage of the fact that we have
$a^{b^c}$
where
$a=b=c$
.


Thought 3


Specifically the exercise has
$x=2019, n=1000$


$$ 2019^{2019^{2019}} \equiv y \pmod {1000} $$


I created a simpler example with the same structure to see if a method could be more generally applicable. I tried to find the least positive residue
$y$
such that


$$ 3^{3^3} \equiv y \pmod 5 $$

Again I can't find a general method.
answer:
we can assume the equation as
$(nx+a)^{(nx+a)^{nx+a}} (mod \ n)$
. Then, naturally, We can reduce the equation with binomial power series into
$(a)^{(nx+a)^{nx+a}} (mod \ n)$
. Then,
$$ (a)^{(nx+a)^{nx+a}} \equiv  (ny+b)^{nx+a} \ (mod \ n)$$
$$ (a)^{(nx+a)^{nx+a}} \equiv  (b)^{nx+a}\ (mod \ n)$$

Then we can replace
$$ (b)^{nx+a} \equiv b^{xn} \cdot b^a \  (mod \ n)$$

We make an assumption

$$ a + k = b $$

$$ (x)^{(x)^{x}} \equiv (a+k)^{xn} \cdot (a+k)^{a}  (mod \ n)$$

b is also

$$ b \equiv a^{nx+a} (mod \ n)$$

Then,
$$ a^{nx+a} - b \equiv 0  \ (mod \ n)$$
$$ \frac{a^{nx+a}}{bn} = m $$
$$ \frac{a^{nx+a}}{(a+k)n} = m $$

If we accept that
$a+k$
should divide the
$a^{nx+a}$
, then
$ b = ar $
then:
$$ (x)^{(x)^{x}} \equiv (ar)^{xn} \cdot (ar)^{a} \ (mod \ n)$$
$$ (x)^{(x)^{x}} \equiv (ar)^{xn+a}\ (mod \ n)$$
$$ (x)^{x} \equiv ar  \ (mod \ n)$$
$$ (x)^{x} \equiv b  \ (mod \ n)$$

Then we made an assumption in the first:
$$ x = xn + a$$
$$ x(1-n) = a$$

Then we can make the equation as
$$ (\frac{-a}{n-1})^{\frac{-a}{n-1}} \equiv ar  \ (mod \ n)$$
$$ (\frac{-a}{n-1})^{\frac{-a}{n-1}} = arn + C $$
$$ (\frac{-a}{n-1})^{\frac{-a}{n-1}} = bn + C $$
$$ (\frac{-a}{n-1})^{\frac{-a}{n-1}} = (a+k)n + C $$

Then if we use the
$$ (x)^{x} \equiv b  \ (mod \ n)$$
$$ C = by$$
After that, we can show that
$$ (\frac{-a}{n-1})^{\frac{-a}{n-1}} = (a+k)n + (a+k)y$$
$$ (\frac{-a}{n-1})^{\frac{-a}{n-1}} = (a+k)(n+y)$$
$$ (\frac{-a}{n-1})^{\frac{-a}{n-1}} = (x(1-n))(n+y)$$

$$ x = \frac{(\frac{-a}{n-1})^{\frac{-a}{n-1}}}{(1-n)(n+y)} $$

After that, you may rearrange the equations and find x for given

$$ ((a+k)\beta)^x = (x)^{(x)^{x}} $$

Then,
$$ (a+k)\beta = {(x)^{x}} $$
$$ x = \frac{(\frac{-a}{n-1})^{\frac{-a}{n-1}}}{(1-n)(n+y)} $$

Solving these equations may give the answer. Good Luck!

Nine years ago, I forwarded this problem to several professors, but I didn't receive a satisfactory answer. I recently came across the topic again through a podcast. Therefore, I'm posting this mathematical problem/phenomenon here in the forum and hope that you might have a starting point :)

Given is a step function (
$x$
-values ​​1, 2, 3, ... and
$y$
-values ​​real numbers) that should be periodic. An example is this function, whose function values ​​are defined at times 1 to 12.

Now I'm looking for the number of necessary superpositions so that the sum is a constant function. In the trivial case, I need 12 functions, starting each one at time 1, 2, 3, etc. For this function, for example, four superpositions are sufficient, at times 1, 4, 7, and 10.

Furthermore, I've noticed that the number of superpositions depends on the greatest common divisor of the "stair step widths" and can be derived from this. For example, if step widths of 100, 60, and 200 exist (
$\gcd = 20$
), then
$\frac{360}{20} = 18$
superpositions are sufficient.

Is this phenomenon known, or what is the mathematically precise description? I've searched using keywords like "phase shift," "superpositions," etc., but haven't found anything suitable.

Thank you for your efforts, and have a pleasant week.

The problem could be formalized as follows:
see other forum

Given a periodic sequence a with period length T:
$∀n: a_{n+T}=a_n$

We seek indices
$I ⊆ \{0;…;T−1\}$
such that
$∀n,m: \sum_{i∈I} a_{n+i}= \sum_{i∈I} a_{m+i}$
answer:
Let
$\omega = e^{2\pi i/T}$
.

Let
$A_0,\,A_1,\,\ldots,\,A_{T-1}$
be the result of the
discrete Fourier transform
of
$a_0,\,a_1,\,\ldots,\,a_{T-1},$
that is
$$
A_k = \sum_{n=0}^{T-1} a_n \omega^{-kn}
$$
When we shift the original function by
$p$
to the left, we get the following result of the discrete Fourier transform of
$a_{p},\,a_{p+1},\,\ldots,\,a_{p+T-1}:$
$$
\omega^{0p}A_0,\;\omega^{1p}A_1,\;\ldots,\;\omega^{(T-1)p}A_{T-1}.
$$
Let
$p$
be a divisor of
$T$
and
$m=\frac{T}{p}.$

Now we create the sum of
$m$
superpositions, each of which is the original function shifted by
$0$
, by
$p$
, by
$2p$
etc. up to
$(m-1)p.$
The result is
$s_0,\,s_1,\,\ldots,\,s_{T-1}.$
We want to achieve
$s_0=s_1=\ldots =s_{T-1}.$

We express the
$s_n$
in terms of the inverse transform.
$$
s_n
= \sum_{\ell=0}^{m-1} \frac{1}{T}
\sum_{k=0}^{T-1}\omega^{k\ell p} A_k\omega^{kn}
= \frac{1}{T}\sum_{k=0}^{T-1}A_k\omega^{kn}
\sum_{\ell=0}^{m-1}\omega^{k\ell p}
$$
If
$\omega^{kp}\neq 1$
, then
$$
\sum_{\ell=0}^{m-1}\omega^{k\ell p}
= \frac{1-\omega^{kpm}}{1-\omega^{kp}}
= \frac{1-\omega^{kT}}{1-\omega^{kp}}
= \frac{1-1}{1-\omega^{kp}}
= 0
$$
If
$\omega^{kp} = 1$
, then
$$
\sum_{\ell=0}^{m-1}\omega^{k\ell p}
= \sum_{\ell=0}^{m-1}1
= m
$$
$\omega^{kp} = 1$
is equivalent with "
$k$
is a multiple of
$m$
." Therefore, we can reduce the summation to multiples of
$m$
in the calculation of
$s_n$
$$
s_n
= \frac{1}{T}\sum_{k=0}^{p-1}A_{km}\omega^{kmn} m
$$
We now assume that
$A_{1m} = A_{2m} = \ldots = A_{(p-1)m} = 0$
and we get
$$
s_n
= \frac{1}{T}A_{0}\omega^{0} m = \frac{A_0 m}{T} = \frac{\sum_{k=0}^{T-1} a_k}{p}
$$
which is independent of
$n.$

This shows that we can get a constant function by using
$m$
superpositions, if
$A_{m} = A_{2m} = \ldots = A_{T-m} = 0.$

If we take a look at
$s_0,s_1,\ldots ,s_{p-1}$
, we get a linear system of equations

$$
\frac{m}{T}
\begin{pmatrix}
(\omega^m)^{0\cdot 0} & (\omega^m)^{1\cdot 0} & \cdots & (\omega^m)^{(p-1)\cdot 0} \\
(\omega^m)^{0\cdot 1} & (\omega^m)^{1\cdot 1} & \cdots & (\omega^m)^{(p-1)\cdot 1} \\
\vdots & & & \vdots \\
(\omega^m)^{0\cdot(p-1)} & (\omega^m)^{1\cdot (p-1)} & \cdots & (\omega^m)^{(p-1)\cdot (p-1)}
\end{pmatrix}
\begin{pmatrix}
A_0 \\
A_m \\
\vdots \\
A_{(p-1)m}
\end{pmatrix}
=
\begin{pmatrix}
s_0 \\
s_1 \\
\vdots \\
s_{p-1}
\end{pmatrix}
$$
The matrix on the left is known to be regular, it represents basically the discrete Fourier transform for
$p$
points. Therefore, the linear system of equations has a unique solution, and
$A_{m} = A_{2m} = \ldots = A_{T-m} = 0$
is also a necessary condition for
$s_0=\ldots = s_{T-1}$
- everything shown so far happened under the assumption that
$m$
evenly shifted versions of the original function are used.

Note that we have
not
shown that the usage of evenly shifted versions of the original function is the only possibility to get a constant function.
I do not have an idea how to do that.
