Improve this question

Let
$(\Omega,\mathcal A,P)$
be a probability space, and let
$T:(\Omega,\mathcal A)\to (\Omega',\mathcal A')$
be measurable. Also, let
$X,Y$
be real random variables on
$(\Omega',\mathcal A')$
, with
$X$
being
$T(P)$
integrable. Then,

$$E_{T(P)}[X|Y=y]=E_P[X\circ T|Y\circ T=y],$$


$P_Y$
almost surely.

How can I prove this?
answer:
Let
$g(y)=E_{T(P)}[X|Y=y]$
. It suffices to show that
$g(Y\circ T)$
is a version of
$E[X\circ T|Y\circ T]$
. Let
$A\in\sigma(Y\circ T)$
. Then
$A=T^{-1}(B)$
for some
$B\in \sigma(Y)$
. We compute:

$$E_P[g(Y\circ T)1_A]=E_{T(P)}[g(Y)1_B]=E_{T(P)}[X1_B]=E_P[(X\circ T)1_A]$$
where the second equality is because
$g(Y)$
is a version of
$E_{T(P)}[X|Y]$
. Since
$A\in\sigma(Y\circ T)$
is arbitrary the conclusion follows.

question:
Let
$\mathcal{A}$
and
$\mathcal{B}$
be
$C^*$
-algebras.
For each
$n \geq 1$
, let
$M_n(\mathcal{A})$
denote the algebra of all
$n \times n$
matrices
$(a_{i,j})$
with entries in
$\mathcal{A}$
. For a linear map
$\Phi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$
, define
$\Phi_n : M_n(\mathcal{A}) \to M_n(\mathcal{B})$
by

$$
\Phi_n((a_{i,j})) = (\Phi(a_{i,j})).
$$


Definition.
A linear map
$\Phi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$
is completely positive if
$\Phi_n : M_n(A) \to M_n(B)$
is positive for all
$n \geq 1$
.


I want to prove the following characterization.


Proposition.
A linear map
$\Phi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$
is completely positive if and only if for any two finite collections
$a_1, \dots, a_n \in \mathcal{A}$
and
$b_1, \dots, b_n \in \mathcal{B}$
, the element

$$
\sum_{i,j=1}^n b_i^* \, \Phi(a_i^* a_j)\, b_j \in \mathcal{B}
$$

is positive.


The "only if" direction is easy, but I cannot prove the "if" part of the statement. I know that we can write any positive matrix
$A \in M_n (\mathcal{A})$
as a sum of
$n$
matrices of the form
$[a_i ^* a_j ]_{i, j}$
, so it is enough to prove
$n$
-positivity of
$\Phi$
matrices of these kind. However, I could not prove that for the matrix
$A \in M_n (\mathcal{B})$
that if
$$\begin{bmatrix} b_1 ^* & \cdots & b_n ^* \end{bmatrix} A \begin{bmatrix} b_1  \\ \cdots \\ b_n \end{bmatrix} \in \mathcal{B} _+$$
was positive for all
$b_1, \dots, b_n \in \mathcal{B}$
, then
$A$
is a positive matrix. My attempt was to use some faithful nondegenerate representation
$\pi: \mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{B} (\mathcal{H})$
, but that did not work out. I would appreciate a proof or a reference, that is as elementary as possible.
answer:
$\def\cB{\mathcal B}\def\abajo{\\[0.2cm]}$
Here's an argument. Not sure if the simplest, but it is decently intuitive.

Let
$\tilde\pi:M_n(\cB)\to \cB(H)$
be a faithful representation. We may assume that
$\cB$
is unital by working in the unitization if needed. Given
$\xi\in H$
, we have
\begin{align}
\langle\tilde\pi(A)\xi,\xi\rangle
&=\sum_{k,j}\langle\tilde\pi(a_{kj}\otimes E_{kj})\xi,\xi\rangle
=\sum_{k,j}\langle\tilde\pi(I\otimes E_{k1})\tilde\pi(a_{kj}\otimes E_{11})\,\tilde\pi(I\otimes E_{1j})\xi,\xi\rangle\abajo
&=\sum_{k,j}\langle\tilde\pi(a_{kj}\otimes E_{11})\,\tilde\pi(I\otimes E_{1j})\xi,\tilde\pi(I\otimes E_{1k})\xi\rangle\abajo
&=\sum_{k,j}\langle\pi(a_{kj})\eta_j,\eta_k\rangle,
\end{align}
where
$\pi(a)=\tilde\pi(a\otimes E_{11})$
and
$\eta_j=\tilde\pi(I\otimes E_{1j})\xi$
. If we show that the last sum is
$\geq0$
for any
$\eta_1,\ldots,\eta_n$
, it will follow that
$\tilde\pi(A)\geq0$
and hence
$A\geq0$
. The representation
$\pi:\cB\to\cB(\tilde\pi(I\otimes E_{11})H)$
is (as is any representation) a direct sum of cyclic representations. So we may assume without loss of generality that
$\pi$
is cyclic. It is then a GNS representation for a state
$\psi$
. For such representation we have
\begin{align}
\sum_{k,j}\langle\pi_\psi(a_{kj})\eta_j,\eta_k\rangle
&=\lim_m\sum_{k,j}\langle\pi_\psi(a_{kj})b_{j}^{(m)}\Omega_\psi,b_k^{(m)}\Omega_\psi\rangle\abajo
&=\lim_m\sum_{k,j}\psi((b_k^{(m)})^*a_{kj}b_j^{(m)})\abajo
&=\lim_m\psi\Big(\sum_{k,j}(b_k^{(m)})^*a_{kj}b_j^{(m)}\Big)\geq0.
\end{align}

question:
Let
$\mathcal{A}$
and
$\mathcal{B}$
be
$C^*$
-algebras.
For each
$n \geq 1$
, let
$M_n(\mathcal{A})$
denote the algebra of all
$n \times n$
matrices
$(a_{i,j})$
with entries in
$\mathcal{A}$
. For a linear map
$\Phi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$
, define
$\Phi_n : M_n(\mathcal{A}) \to M_n(\mathcal{B})$
by

$$
\Phi_n((a_{i,j})) = (\Phi(a_{i,j})).
$$


Definition.
A linear map
$\Phi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$
is completely positive if
$\Phi_n : M_n(A) \to M_n(B)$
is positive for all
$n \geq 1$
.


I want to prove the following characterization.


Proposition.
A linear map
$\Phi : \mathcal{A} \to \mathcal{B}$
is completely positive if and only if for any two finite collections
$a_1, \dots, a_n \in \mathcal{A}$
and
$b_1, \dots, b_n \in \mathcal{B}$
, the element

$$
\sum_{i,j=1}^n b_i^* \, \Phi(a_i^* a_j)\, b_j \in \mathcal{B}
$$

is positive.


The "only if" direction is easy, but I cannot prove the "if" part of the statement. I know that we can write any positive matrix
$A \in M_n (\mathcal{A})$
as a sum of
$n$
matrices of the form
$[a_i ^* a_j ]_{i, j}$
, so it is enough to prove
$n$
-positivity of
$\Phi$
matrices of these kind. However, I could not prove that for the matrix
$A \in M_n (\mathcal{B})$
that if
$$\begin{bmatrix} b_1 ^* & \cdots & b_n ^* \end{bmatrix} A \begin{bmatrix} b_1  \\ \cdots \\ b_n \end{bmatrix} \in \mathcal{B} _+$$
was positive for all
$b_1, \dots, b_n \in \mathcal{B}$
, then
$A$
is a positive matrix. My attempt was to use some faithful nondegenerate representation
$\pi: \mathcal{B} \to \mathcal{B} (\mathcal{H})$
, but that did not work out. I would appreciate a proof or a reference, that is as elementary as possible.
answer:
I came up with a very stupid way to prove this: I would appreciate if anyone would provide a more clever way.

Lemma.
If
$A = [a_{ij}]_{i, j} \in M_n (\mathcal{B})$
such that
$$\begin{bmatrix} b_1 ^* & \cdots & b_n ^* \end{bmatrix} A \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ \vdots \\ b_n  \end{bmatrix} = 0$$
for all
$b_1, \dots, b_n \in \mathcal{B}$
, then
$B = 0$
.

Proof.
We prove this componentwise. First, by taking
$b_i = 1$
and zero otherwise, we get
$$a_{ii} = 0, \quad \forall i,$$
so the diagonal elements are zero. For nondiagonal elements, take the indices
$i \neq j$
. If we take
$b_i := b$
,
$b_i := c$
and the rest zero, we get
$$b ^* a_{ii} b + b^* a_{ij} c + c^* a_{ji} b + c^* a_{jj} c = 0.$$
Since the diagonal elements are zero, this implies
$$b^* a_{ij} c + c^* a_{ji} b = 0.$$
Substituting
$b = c = 1$
yields
$$a_{ij} + a_{ji} = 0,$$
while substituting
$b = 1$
and
$c = i$
gives
$$i a_{ij} - i a_{ji} = 0.$$
Therefore,
$$a_{ij} = a_{ji} = 0$$
and
$A = 0$
.

Corollary.
Suppose that
$$\begin{bmatrix} b_1 ^* & \cdots & b_n ^* \end{bmatrix} A \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ \vdots \\ b_n  \end{bmatrix} \in \mathcal{B}$$
is self-adjoint for all
$b_1, \dots, b_n \in \mathcal{B}$
. Then
$A$
is self-adjoint.

Proof.
By assumption, we have that
$$\sum_{i, j} ^n b_i ^* a_{ij} b_j$$
is self adjoint for any chosen
$b_i$
's, hence it is equal to
$$\left( \sum_{i, j} ^n b_i ^* a_{ij} b_j \right)^* = \sum_{i, j} ^n b_j ^* a_{ij}^* b_i = \sum_{i, j} ^n b_i ^* a_{ji}^* b_i$$
and so
$$\sum_{i, j} ^n b_i ^* (a_{ij} - a_{ji}^*) b_i = 0.$$
By the previous lemma, we obtain
$A - A^* = 0$
.

Proposition.
Suppose that
$$\begin{bmatrix} b_1 ^* & \cdots & b_n ^* \end{bmatrix} A \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ \vdots \\ b_n  \end{bmatrix} $$
is positive for any
$b_1, \dots, b_n \in \mathcal{B}$
. Then
$A$
is positive.

Proof.
By the previous corollary,
$A$
is self-adjoint, so we can write by functional calculus
$$A = A_+ - A_-,$$
where
$A_+, A_-$
are both positive and
$A_+ A_- = A_- A_+ = 0$
. By assumption, we have that
$$\begin{bmatrix} b_1 ^* & \cdots & b_n ^* \end{bmatrix} A_- A A_-\begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ \vdots \\ b_n  \end{bmatrix}  \geq 0,$$
however the term on the right simplifies to
$$- \begin{bmatrix} b_1 ^* & \cdots & b_n ^* \end{bmatrix} A_- ^3 \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ \vdots \\ b_n  \end{bmatrix} \leq 0.$$
This implies that
$$\begin{bmatrix} b_1 ^* & \cdots & b_n ^* \end{bmatrix} A_- ^3 \begin{bmatrix} b_1 \\ \vdots \\ b_n  \end{bmatrix} = 0$$
for all
$b_1, \dots, b_n \in \mathcal{B}$
, which implies
$A_- ^3 = 0$
by the above lemma. By continuous functional calculus, we get
$A_- = 0$
and
$A = A_+$
is positive.

question:
In
Stewart's Calculus
on page 81 question 34 it says the following:

Verify, by means of a
geometric
argument, that the largest possible choice of
$\delta$
for showing that
$\lim_{x\to 3}x^2=9$
is
$\delta = \sqrt{9+\epsilon}-3$
.

If I attempt a regular epsilon delta proof here is what happens.

$\lim_{x\to 3}x^2=9 \Leftrightarrow \forall\epsilon>0, \exists\delta > 0 \text{ } s.t. \text{} 0<|x-3|<\delta \longrightarrow |x^2-9|<\epsilon$

and
$|x^2-9| = |x-3||x+3|$
and then from this you can make an
algebraic
argument like in this book, but I am unable to get a
geometric
proof for
$\delta = \sqrt{9+\epsilon}-3$

Now, I don't know how to answer this question (hence, why I'm asking it here) but I did notice that
$\delta = \sqrt{9+\epsilon}-3$
and
$\lim_{x\to 3}x^2=9$
seems like a special case of
$\lim_{x\to a}x^2=a^2$
and
$\delta = \sqrt{a^2+\epsilon}-a$
so maybe that's useful?

All help is appreciated!
answer:
When the goal is to use a geometric argument, it is useful to actually sketch a diagram. As such, I encourage you to do so by not furnishing one here.

The idea is to first sketch the curve
$y = x^2$
, and visualize the fact that when
$x = 3$
, we have
$x^2 = 9$
by drawing a vertical line segment from
$(3,0)$
to
$(3,9)$
, and then a horizontal segment from
$(3,9)$
to
$(0,9)$
.

Next, the idea that whenever
$0 < |x - 3| < \delta$
implies
$|x^2 - 9| < \epsilon$
is reflected by choosing some distance
$\epsilon > 0$
on the vertical
$y$
-axis at which we place additional horizontal lines above and below
$y = 9$
: i.e., draw the horizontal lines
$$y = 9 - \epsilon, \quad y = 9 + \epsilon,$$
and where these intersect the parabola, we draw a vertical line down to the
$x$
-axis.

Where do these vertical lines meet the
$x$
-axis?  They meet at
$$x = \sqrt{9 - \epsilon}, \quad x = \sqrt{9 + \epsilon}.$$
How far are these points from
$x = 3$
?  The distances are
$$|\sqrt{9 - \epsilon} - 3|, \quad |\sqrt{9 + \epsilon} - 3|.$$
Since
$\epsilon > 0$
, we may remove the absolute values and rewrite the above as
$$3 - \sqrt{9 - \epsilon}, \quad \sqrt{9 + \epsilon} - 3.$$
Of these two choices, which one is
smaller
?  Why must we pick the smaller of the two?  Study the picture.  What do these quantities have to do with
$\delta$
?

question:
The taxicab numbers are those integers of the form

$$N=a^3+b^3=c^3+d^3$$


where
$a, b, c, d \in \mathbb{N}$
and
$\{ a, b \} \neq \{c, d \}$
. Famously, the first taxicab number is
$1729 = 1^3+12^3 = 9^3 + 10^3$
.


Let us consider the
list of taxicab numbers
in ascending order and keep track of what proportion of them are even. After the first
$30,000$
terms, it appears that a bias towards evenness is present:




This seems surprising to me. After
$30,000$
terms, we have about
$\sim 63\%$
of taxicab numbers being even, quite substantially more than half. Why does this appear to be the case?


This apparent bias seems to come from the fact that the numbers are sums of cubes in two different ways. Indeed, the weaker property of a number merely being the sum of two cubes results an even split between even and odd numbers - intuitively this is to be expected, with formal argument can be made along the lines of observing that the sums of cubes modulo
$2$
are
$0^3+0^3, 0^3+1^3,1^3+0^3$
and
$1^3+1^3$
, two of which are
$0$
and two of which are
$1 \mod 2$
.


It's possible that this trend does not continue and after some extensive enumeration of the taxicabs, perhaps the proportion drops back down again (
similar surprising things can happen
). But the consistency of the trend is so consistent
$^1$
and remarkable that I thought a question about it is of note regardless, even if it is merely a coincidence.




$^1$
indeed, the only times that we ever have more odd than even taxicabs enumerated in my data is after enumerating the first
$1, 35$
and
$37$
taxicab numbers.
answer:
Here's a heuristic argument. Let's look at pairs
$(a, b)$
and the resulting parity of
$a^3 + b^3$
$\pmod 8$

Even cubes,
$(2k)^3 \equiv 0$

Odd cubes,
$(2k+1)^3 \equiv 2k+1$
. So they're evenly distributed between
${1,3,5,7}$

An odd sum of cubes comes from the sum of an even and odd cube, so they're evenly distributed among
$1,3,5,7$
. (representing
$1/2$
of integers).

An even sum of cubes comes from either two evens or two odds. The (even + even) pairs are equivalent to
$0$
(
$1/8$
of integers). The (odd + odd) pairs are evenly distributed among the remainders
$0, 2, 4$
, and
$6$
(representing
$1/2$
of the integers).

Now we can calculate the expected proportions of even and odd taxicab numbers.

Odd Taxicabs (Sums
$\equiv 1, 3, 5, 7 \pmod 8$
).
$1/2$
of all pairs land here, distributed evenly across
$1/2$
of the integers.
$$\text{Odd Taxicabs} \propto \frac{(1/2)^2}{1/2} =\frac{1}{2}$$
Even Taxicabs (Sums
$\equiv 0 \pmod 8$
). This specific remainder makes up
$1/8$
of the integers. The pairs that land here are the (even + even) pairs (
$1/4$
of total pairs), plus
$1/4$
of the (odd + odd) pairs.
$$(\text{Even Taxicabs}\equiv 0) \propto \frac{(1/4+(1/4)(1/4))^2}{1/8} = \frac{25}{32}$$
Even Taxicabs (Sums
$\equiv 2, 4, 6 \pmod 8$
). Together, these three remainders make up
$3/8$
of the integers. The pairs that land here are the remaining (odd + odd) pairs.
$$(\text{Even Taxicabs}\equiv 2, 4, 6) \propto \frac{((3/4)(1/4))^2}{3/8} = \frac{3}{32}$$

Odd Taxicabs (Sums
$\equiv 1, 3, 5, 7 \pmod 8$
).
$1/2$
of all pairs land here, distributed evenly across
$1/2$
of the integers.
$$\text{Odd Taxicabs} \propto \frac{(1/2)^2}{1/2} =\frac{1}{2}$$

Even Taxicabs (Sums
$\equiv 0 \pmod 8$
). This specific remainder makes up
$1/8$
of the integers. The pairs that land here are the (even + even) pairs (
$1/4$
of total pairs), plus
$1/4$
of the (odd + odd) pairs.
$$(\text{Even Taxicabs}\equiv 0) \propto \frac{(1/4+(1/4)(1/4))^2}{1/8} = \frac{25}{32}$$

Even Taxicabs (Sums
$\equiv 2, 4, 6 \pmod 8$
). Together, these three remainders make up
$3/8$
of the integers. The pairs that land here are the remaining (odd + odd) pairs.
$$(\text{Even Taxicabs}\equiv 2, 4, 6) \propto \frac{((3/4)(1/4))^2}{3/8} = \frac{3}{32}$$

So, the expected proportion of Even Taxicabs is:
$$\frac{\text{Even}}{\text{Even} + \text{Odd}} = \frac{25/32+3/32}{25/32+3/32 + 16/32} = \frac{7}{11}\approx63.6\%$$

question:
It is well known that

$$H^*(\mathrm{SU}(n))\cong\Lambda[x_3,\ldots,x_{2n-1}]$$

and

$$H^*(\mathrm{Sp}(n))\cong\Lambda[x_3,\ldots,x_{4n-1}].$$

I was wondering how do the induced morphisms by complex and quaternionic conjugation look like. This is, the maps

$$\mathrm{SU}(n)\to\mathrm{SU}(n)\quad\text{and}\quad \mathrm{Sp}(n)\to\mathrm{Sp}(n)$$

sending each matrix to its complex (resp. quaternionic) conjugate, i.e. taking the complex (resp. quaternionic) conjugate of each entry. Any reference is appreciated.
answer:
Note:
The question was edited shortly before this answer was published to also ask about
$Sp(n)$
, which I do not consider, but which I think can be treated in essentially the same way.

I don't have a direct reference, but this is easy enough to put together using standard techniques.

Claim:
Under the isomorphism
$H^*(U(n)) \cong \Lambda_{\mathbb{Z}}(x_1, \ldots, x_{2n - 1})$
the induced map of the conjugation map
$c\colon U(n) \to U(n)$
on cohomology rings takes the form
$x_{2k + 1} \mapsto (-1)^{k + 1} x_{2k + 1}$
.

Since
$H^*(SU(n))$
sits inside
$H^*(U(n))$
as the subring
$\Lambda_{\mathbb{Z}}(x_3, \ldots, x_{2n - 1}) \subseteq \Lambda_{\mathbb{Z}}(x_1, \ldots, x_{2n - 1})$
, this answers your question. There is no good technical reason to work with
$U(n)$
over
$SU(n)$
here (apart from the fact that the result is ever so slightly more general); I do so simply because the
$U(n)$
are a much more common object of study.

Proof of claim:
Since
$c$
is a group homomorphism, it can be delooped to a map
$Bc\colon BU(n) \to BU(n)$
. This map is well understood; it classifies taking the complex conjugate of a rank
$n$
complex vector bundle. Pulling back the universal complex
$n$
-plane bundle
$\gamma_n$
over
$BU(n)$
along
$Bc$
yields a bundle
$\overline{\gamma_n}$
with total Chern class
$$
c(\overline{\gamma_n}) = 1 - c_1 + c_2 - \ldots + (-1)^n c_n
$$
where
$c_i := c_i(\gamma_n)$
is the
$i$
th Chern class of
$\gamma_n$
, see Lemma 14.9 in Milnor and Stasheff's
Characteristic Classes
. Since
$H^*(BU(n))$
is the polynomial ring
$\mathbb{Z}[c_1, \ldots, c_n]$
, this implies that
$Bc^*\colon H^*(BU(n)) \to H^*(BU(n))$
is given by sending
$c_i$
to
$(-1)^i c_i$
.

Consider now the fiber sequence
$$
SU(n) \to * \to BU(n)
$$
and its associated Serre spectral sequence with signature
$$
E_2^{*, *} \cong \mathbb{Z}[c_1, \ldots, c_n] \otimes \Lambda_{\mathbb{Z}}(x_1, \ldots, x_{2n - 1}) \Rightarrow H^*(*)
$$
I claim that in this spectral sequence
$x_{2k + 1}$
transgresses to
$c_{k + 1}$
(a priori up to sign, which we however fix to be
$+1$
by replacing
$x_{2k + 1}$
with
$-x_{2k + 1}$
when necessary). Indeed this must be true for
$x_1$
for degree reasons, and if it is true for
$x_1, \ldots, x_{2k - 1}$
, then all differentials involving these classes is fully determined in view of the structure of the
$E_2$
-page, and none of them hit
$c_{2k}$
, so
$x_{2k + 1}$
must transgress to it or it becomes a permanent cycle.

Now
$c$
and
$Bc$
induce an endomorphism of this fiber sequence, and consequently of the spectral sequence. We thus have
$$
d_{2k}(c^* x_{2k + 1}) = (Bc)^* d_{2k}(x_{2k + 1}) = (Bc^*) c_{k + 1} = (-1)^{k + 1} c_{k + 1}
$$
and therefore
$c^* x_{2k + 1} = (-1)^{k + 1} x_{2k + 1}$
as desired.
$\Box$

question: I am trying to locate a lecture by David Gillman titled “The best picture of Poincaré's homology sphere.” However, I have not been able to find a video recording or lecture notes. Does anyone know where I can find this lecture (if a video is available)? Thanks
answer:
After digging into it, I managed to find the audio file and slides. Specifically, while the talk is no longer featured on the present PIMS site (Pacific Institute for the Mathematical Sciences), it is still present on the Wayback Machine:

The best picture of Poincare's homology sphere

This includes the slides, starting
here
, and a downloadable
MP3
for the audio file. A video file is also linked but seems to require a connection to the PIMS site which no longer functions. That's far enough back (pre-Youtube) that if it still exists you may need to contact PIMS staff.

Improve this question

How to find the solution of this differential equation?
\begin{equation}
\left(2xy + 2y^3 - 6y + 1\right)\,dx \;+\; \left(2x^2 + 2xy^2 - 6x + y\right)\,dy \;=\; 0
\end{equation}

I've tried searching for an integrating factor, but it doesn't depend solely on
$x$
or
$y$
. It also doesn't depend on basic combinations like
$x^a  y ^ b$
. Please assist me.
answer:
The reason your standard integrating factors aren't appearing is that the problem almost certainly contains a small typo that breaks an otherwise elegant symmetry. When we test the components for exactness, we find:

$$\frac{\partial M}{\partial y} = 2x + 6y^2 - 6, \quad \frac{\partial N}{\partial x} = 4x + 2y^2 - 6$$

Since these aren't equal, the equation isn't exact as written. However, if we look at the structure and group terms that share the common factor
$(x + y^2 - 3)$
, the intended path becomes visible:

$$2(x + y^2 - 3)(y \, dx + x \, dy) + dx + y \, dy = 0$$

We can immediately recognize the first part as containing the differential of the product
$xy$
, which simplifies the expression to:

$$2(x + y^2 - 3) \, d(xy) + dx + y \, dy = 0$$

This is exactly where the typo reveals itself. For this to be solvable via inspection or simple substitution, that trailing
$y \, dy$
term really needs a coefficient of
$2$
. If the original equation had
$2y$
instead of
$y$
in the
$N$
term, the last two components would form the exact differential
$d(x + y^2)$
. Assuming this correction, the equation transforms into a separable form involving the grouping
$w = x + y^2 - 3$
:

$$2w \, d(xy) + dw = 0$$

Dividing through by
$w$
—effectively using
$\frac{1}{x + y^2 - 3}$
as your integrating factor—gives us an equation that is trivial to integrate:

$$2 \, d(xy) + \frac{dw}{w} = 0$$

Integrating both sides leads directly to the implicit solution:

$$2xy + \ln|x + y^2 - 3| = C$$

I’d bet a fair amount that the
$y$
was meant to be
$2y$
.

Improve this question

How to find the solution of this differential equation?
\begin{equation}
\left(2xy + 2y^3 - 6y + 1\right)\,dx \;+\; \left(2x^2 + 2xy^2 - 6x + y\right)\,dy \;=\; 0
\end{equation}

I've tried searching for an integrating factor, but it doesn't depend solely on
$x$
or
$y$
. It also doesn't depend on basic combinations like
$x^a  y ^ b$
. Please assist me.
answer:
$$ (2xy + 2y^3 - 6y + 1) \, dx + (2x^2 + 2xy^2 - 6x + y) \, dy = 0. $$

$$ \frac{dx}{dy} = -\frac{2x^2 + 2xy^2 - 6x + y}{2xy + 2y^3 - 6y + 1}. $$

substitute,

$$ u = x + y^2 - 3. $$

Then

$$ x = u - y^2 + 3, \quad \frac{dx}{dy} = \frac{du}{dy} - 2y. $$

Also,

$$ 2xy + 2y^3 - 6y + 1 = 2y(x + y^2 - 3) + 1 = 2yu + 1, $$

and

$$ 2x^2 + 2xy^2 - 6x + y = 2xu + y = 2(u - y^2 + 3)u + y = 2u^2 + 6u - 2y^2u + y. $$

Substitute into the differential equation:

$$ (2yu + 1) \left( \frac{du}{dy} \right) - 2y(2yu + 1) + 2u^2 + 6u - 2y^2u + y = 0. $$

$$ (2yu + 1) \frac{du}{dy} + 2u^2 + 6u - 6y^2u - y = 0, $$

$$ \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{6y^2u + y - 2u^2 - 6u}{2yu + 1}. $$

substitute

$$ v = 2yu + 1. $$

Then

$$ u = \frac{v - 1}{2y}, \quad \frac{du}{dy} = \frac{y v' - (v - 1)}{2y^2}. $$

Substitute into the equation

$$ y v \frac{dv}{dy} = v(6y^3 - 6y + 1) - (4y^3 - 6y + 1). $$

$$ \frac{dv}{dy} = \frac{6y^3 - 6y + 1}{y} - \frac{4y^3 - 6y + 1}{y} \frac{1}{v}. $$

let

$$ w = \frac{1}{v}. $$

Then
$w' = -v' / v^2$
,

$$ \frac{dw}{dy} = \frac{4y^3 - 6y + 1}{y} w^3 - \frac{6y^3 - 6y + 1}{y} w^2. $$

So the explicit Abel equation is

$$ \frac{dw}{dy} = \frac{4y^3 - 6y + 1}{y} w^3 - \frac{6y^3 - 6y + 1}{y} w^2 \quad (y \neq 0). $$

This is an Abel equation of the first kind, with the
$w$
and constant terms absent.

I need help to find the analytical solution (if it exists) of the following Stochastic differential equation:

$$dR_{t} = \frac{D}{R_t}\,dt  +  \sqrt{2D}\, dW_t $$


Here,




D is a positive constant.


$W_t$
is a standard wiener process


Assume the initial condition
$R_0 > 0$

Can someone kindly help me with this and it would be very helpful for me if the steps are shown.
answer:
$$ dR_t = \frac{D}{R_t} dt + \sqrt{2D} dW_t, \quad R_0 > 0, D > 0. $$

Set

$$ X_t := \frac{R_t}{\sqrt{2D}}. $$

$$R_t = \sqrt{2D} X_t$$

$$ \sqrt{2D} dX_t = \frac{D}{\sqrt{2D} X_t} dt + \sqrt{2D} dW_t, $$

hence

$$ dX_t = \frac{1}{2X_t} dt + dW_t, \quad X_0 = \frac{R_0}{\sqrt{2D}}. $$

This is exactly the Bessel SDE of dimension 2:

$$ dX_t = \frac{\delta - 1}{2X_t} dt + dW_t \quad \text{with } \delta = 2. $$

So
$R_t$
is just a scaled
$BES(2)$
process.

Edit

For the diffusion in the disk
$\Omega = \{x \in \mathbb{R}^2 : |x| < R\}$
with reflecting boundary, the density
$u(t, x \mid x_0)$
satisfies

$$\partial_t u = D \Delta u, \quad \partial_n u|_{\partial \Omega} = 0.$$

If the molecule is released at the center
$x_0 = 0$
, then
$u$
is radial and has the Neumann eigenfunction expansion

$$u(t, r \mid 0) = \frac{1}{\pi R^2} + \frac{1}{\pi R^2} \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} \frac{J_0(\beta_m r / R)}{J_0(\beta_m)^2} e^{-D \beta_m^2 t / R^2},$$

where
$\beta_m$
are the positive zeros of
$J_1$
(equivalently
$J_0'(\beta_m) = 0$
).

If you want the radial marginal density

$$p(r, t) = 2 \pi r u(t, r \mid 0),$$

then

$$p(r, t) = \frac{2r}{R^2} \left[ 1 + \sum_{m=1}^{\infty} \frac{J_0(\beta_m r / R)}{J_0(\beta_m)^2} e^{-D \beta_m^2 t / R^2} \right].$$

The long-time limit is uniform in the disk:

$$u(t, r \mid 0) \to \frac{1}{\pi R^2}, \quad p(r, t) \to \frac{2r}{R^2}.$$

question:
In Jacobs'
Categorical Logic and Type Theory
a theorem for fibred adjunctions goes like this:

Let
$p:\mathcal E\to\mathcal B,q:\mathcal D\to\mathcal B$
be fibrations, and
$H: \mathcal E\to\mathcal D$
be a fibred functor. Then
$H$
admits a fibred left adjoint iff:

the functor
$H$
admits left adjoints when restricted to each fibre, say
$K(I)\dashv H_I$
for
$I\in\mathcal B$
.

the Beck-Chevalley condition holds: for each map
$u: I\to J$
in the base category, the canonical natural transformation
$K(I)u^\#\Rightarrow u^* K(J)$
is iso, where
$u^\#,u^*$
denotes the corresponding reindexing functors.

Now an excercise states that the same holds if the BC condition is replaced by that
$(u^\#,u^*)$
forms a pair of pseudo map of adjunctions, as follows:

Let
$F:\mathcal C\leftrightarrows\mathcal D:G$
and
$F':\mathcal C'\leftrightarrows\mathcal D':G'$
be adjunctions with
$F\dashv G, F'\dashv G'$
, with units and counits
$\eta, \eta', \epsilon, \epsilon'$
respectively. a pair of functor
$(K,\mathcal{C\to C'}, L:\mathcal{D\to D'})$
is called a pseudo map of adjunctions between them if there are moreover natrual iso
$\varphi: F'K\Rightarrow LF$
and
$\psi: G'L\Rightarrow KG$
such that
$\psi F\circ G'\varphi\circ \eta' K=K\eta$
and
$L\epsilon\circ \varphi G\circ F'\psi=\epsilon' L$
.

I understand that in the first proposition, the BC condition is stating that the
mate
of an iso (the fibred functor preserves Cartesian lifting) is iso. So I expect the 'pesudo map of adjunctions' to be an equivalent way of saying the two given natural isomorphisms are mate of each other.

However, I failed to prove this, the two equations are hard to interprete for me. Could anyone show me how the two are equivalent? Thanks in advance!
answer:
We are going to show that, given an isomorphism
$\varphi$
as above, we obtain a pseudo map of adjunctions
$(K, L, \varphi, \psi)$
if and only if the canonical map
$\psi$
is also isomorphism, from which the conclusion follows.

We construct the mate of
$\varphi$
, that is precisely
$$
\begin{align}
\chi =& \overline{F'KG\xrightarrow{\varphi G}LFG\xrightarrow{L\epsilon}L}\\
=&KG\xrightarrow{\eta'KG}G'F'KG\xrightarrow{G'(L\epsilon\circ\varphi G)}G'L.
\end{align}
$$

First, suppose that we have a pseudo map of adjunction
$(K, L, \varphi, \psi)$
. We need to show that
$\chi=\psi^{-1}$
. Indeed,

$$
\begin{align}
\chi\circ\psi =& G'(L\epsilon\circ\varphi G)\circ\eta' KG\circ\psi\\
=& G'L\epsilon\circ G'\varphi G\circ\eta' KG\circ\psi\\
=& G'L\epsilon\circ G'\varphi G\circ G'F'\psi\circ \eta'G'L\quad\text{by interchange}\\
=& G'(\epsilon' L)\circ \eta' G'L\\
=& 1\quad\text{by triangular identity}
\end{align}
$$

Conversely, suppose the canonical morphism
$\chi$
is an isomorphism, we show that
$(K,L,\varphi,\chi^{-1})$
is a pseudo map of adjunctions. That is, we check that the two equations hold. Take the first one for example, we need
$\chi F\circ K\eta=G'\varphi\circ \eta'K$
. The transpose of RHS under
$F'\dashv G'$
is precisely
$\varphi$
, while the transpose of LHS is

$$
\begin{align}
\overline{\chi F}\circ F'K\eta=&L\epsilon F\circ \varphi GF\circ F'K\eta\\
=&L\epsilon F\circ LF\eta\circ\varphi\quad\text{by interchange}\\
=&\varphi\quad\text{by triangular identity}
\end{align}
$$

The same calculation hold for the other equation.

question:
I am trying to formalise a structure where each system is represented by a rooted tree of possible future states. I would like to define a distance between two such trees based on the minimal “cost” of transforming one tree into the other via operations such as adding, deleting, or modifying branches.

Is there an existing mathematical framework that studies distances between such branching structures, or standard ways to define such a metric?
answer:
Yes, this is known as
Tree Edit Distance
. You typically assign a cost function to each edit operation (insertion, deletion, or modification). The distance
$d(T_1, T_2)$
between two trees is the minimum sum of costs over all possible sequences of operations
$S = (s_1, s_2, \dots, s_k)$
that transform
$T_1$
into
$T_2$
:
$$d(T_1, T_2) = \min_{S} \sum_{i=1}^{|S|} c(s_i)$$

If your trees are ordered, the Zhang-Shasha algorithm computes the distance in polynomial time. If the trees are unordered, computing the exact distance is NP-hard, though efficient approximations exist.

question:
The Centroid Theorem says that when the medians of a triangle intersect at the triangle's centroid (its point of concurrency), each median is split into two subsegments which are
$\frac{1}{3}$
and
$\frac{2}{3}$
the length of the median.

Commandino's theorem proves a different ratio, but the same principle, for tetrahedra, as explained below.

How were these theorems/formulas proven? It seems like it should be relatively simple but no obvious approach stands out to me.
answer:
Let the vertices of the triangle be vectors
$A$
,
$B$
, and
$C$
. By definition, the centroid
$G$
is the average of all three vertices:
$$G = \frac{A + B + C}{3}$$
A median is a line from a vertex (let's use
$A$
) to the midpoint of the opposite side. The midpoint of the side connecting
$B$
and
$C$
is simply their average:
$$M = \frac{B + C}{2}$$
So
$$G = \frac{1}{3}A + \frac{2}{3}M$$
The same logic applies to any
$n-$
dimensional simplex (which has
$n+1$
vertices):
$$G = \frac{1}{n+1}A + \frac{n}{n+1}M$$

question:
The Centroid Theorem says that when the medians of a triangle intersect at the triangle's centroid (its point of concurrency), each median is split into two subsegments which are
$\frac{1}{3}$
and
$\frac{2}{3}$
the length of the median.

Commandino's theorem proves a different ratio, but the same principle, for tetrahedra, as explained below.

How were these theorems/formulas proven? It seems like it should be relatively simple but no obvious approach stands out to me.
answer:
For the triangle, you just need the
midpoint theorem
.

Let
$P$
,
$Q$
be the midpoints of
$AE$
and
$BE$
respectively in triangle
$ABC$
. Then
$PQ$
is parallel and congruent to
$M_{AC}M_{CB}$
, because both segments are parallel to
$AB$
and congruent to its half.

It follows that
$PQM_{CB}M_{AC}$
is a parallelogram and its diagonals meet at their midpoint. Hence
$M_{CB}E\cong EP\cong PA$
and
$M_{AC}E\cong EQ\cong QB$
, as it was to be proved. For the third median you can repeat the same argument.

For a tetrahedron, you can argue in a similar way. If
$M$
,
$N$
are the midpoints of edges
$AB$
,
$BC$
(see figure below) and
$E$
,
$F$
are the cemtroids of faces
$ABD$
,
$BCD$
, then we know that
$EF$
is parallel to
$MN$
and
$EF={2\over3}MN$
. But
$MN$
is parallel to
$AC$
and
$MN={1\over2}AC$
. It follows that
$EF$
is parallel to
$AC$
and
$EF={1\over3}AC$
. By similarity then we also get
$$
{GF\over AG}={GE\over CG}={1\over3}.
$$

question:
I derived the following formula which extends the zeta function
to all integers:

$$\zeta(s) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(s)(2^s - 2)} \sum_{n=1}^{\infty}
\frac{\Gamma(n+s)}{2^n \Gamma(n+1)} \zeta(n+s)$$


I also derived the following for the Hurwitz zeta function:


$$\zeta(s,x) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(s)} \sum_{n=0}^{\infty}
\frac{(-1)^n \zeta(n+s)\Gamma(n+s)}{\Gamma(n+1)}(x-1)^n,
\quad 0 < x < 2$$

The derivation relies on a fractional derivative approach,
and the choice of definition significantly affects the result.

The first formula behaves well at integers but breaks down for
non-integer and complex inputs, predicting an infinite number of
poles in certain regions of the complex plane.

My main question is:

Does the choice of fractional derivative definition
fundamentally affect the validity of this extension?

I have written formal documentation of this derivation. I am 17
and pre-college, and would genuinely appreciate expert feedback
— both to verify the result and to determine whether the fractional
derivative framework used is the most appropriate one.
answer:
Looking at your formulation, I suppose you have used Riemann-Liouville’s right? If this were the case, notice that the singular kernel of such operator plays a key role for the derivation of your series expansion. If, for example, you had something like
$(x^2-t^2)^{\alpha-1}$
in the argument of the involved fractional integral, the result of your identity would be different. So yes, the choice of your fractional operator affects the extension/identity you obtain indeed.

Improve this question

I don't really think it is possible to evaluate this, but I tried to, and my rough estimate was that the probability should be
$<0.5$
as there are relatively more irrationals than rationals, but I might be wrong and also I'd like to see if there is a way to evaluate this.
answer:
A proof from first principles would go as follows. First, we show that the rationals in the unit interval
$[0,1]$
are countable by enumerating them as follows:

First, we enumerate the rationals with denominator
$1$
namely
$0/1=0$
and
$1/1=1$
.
Next we enumerate the rationals with denominator
$2$
not already enumerated namely
$1/2$
(note that
$0/2=0$
and
$2/2=1$
have already been enumerated).
Next we enumerate the rationals with denominator
$3$
not already enumerated namely
$1/3$
and
$2/3$
.

Proceeding in this manner, we enumerate all rationals in a sequence
$r_1=0, r_2=1, r_3=1/2, r_4=1/3, \dots$
.

Now, we fix a small real number
$\epsilon>0$
and cover each rational number
$r_k$
with an interval of length
$\frac \epsilon {2^k}$
. For example,
$r_3=\frac 1 2$
would be covered by the interval
$(\frac 1 2 - \frac \epsilon {16}, \frac 1 2 + \frac \epsilon {16})$
of length
$\frac \epsilon {2^3} = \frac \epsilon 8$
. Every rational number lies in at least one of these intervals, but each of these intervals contains many irrational numbers as well.

The probability that a random number in
$[0,1]$
is rational is
less than
the probability that it lies in one of these intervals. The latter probability is
less than
the sum of the lengths of all these (overlapping) intervals. Summing the geometric series, we get

$$\sum_{k=1}^\infty \frac \epsilon {2^k} = \epsilon$$

We have established that the probability that a random number in
$[0,1]$
is rational is
less than
$\epsilon$
for every
$\epsilon>0$
. The probability must therefore be
$0$
.

Improve this question

I don't really think it is possible to evaluate this, but I tried to, and my rough estimate was that the probability should be
$<0.5$
as there are relatively more irrationals than rationals, but I might be wrong and also I'd like to see if there is a way to evaluate this.
answer:
The set of rational numbers in
$[0,1]$
is countable, whereas the irrationals are uncountable, However the relevant concept here is not just cardinality, but measure.

Any countable subset of
$\mathbb{R}$
has Lebesgue measure zero. Since the uniform distribution on
$[0,1]$
is defined via Lebesgue measure, it assigns probability zero to the rationals. Therefore,

$$ \mathbb{P}(\text{Rational}) = 0, \mathbb{P}(\text{Irrational}) = 1.$$

question:
Given three positive numbers
$a,b,c$
such that
$c\ge a+b$
.

For three positive numbers
$x,y,z$

constrained by the triangle inequalities for each set of sides
$(x,z,a)$
,
$(x,y,b)$
, and
$(y,z,c)$
:

$|x - z| < a < x + z$
,

$|x - y| < b < x + y$
,

$|y - z| < c < y + z$
,

the angles
$\alpha,\beta,\gamma\in[0,\pi]$
are determined by the Law of Cosines on their respective triangles:

$$ \alpha = \arccos\frac{x^2+z^2-a^2}{2xz}, \quad \beta = \arccos\frac{x^2+y^2-b^2}{2xy}, \quad \gamma = \arccos\frac{y^2+z^2-c^2}{2yz} $$


I want to minimize the function

$$ f(x, y, z) = \frac{\gamma^2 - \alpha^2 - \beta^2}{2\alpha\beta} $$


Question: How to prove


$$f(x,y,z)\ge\frac{c^2-a^2-b^2}{2ab}$$


(Equality only occurs when
$c=a+b$
alongside
$z=x/\cosα$
and
$y=x/\cosβ$
).




Empirical evidence


Consider the configuration where
$x = y = z = R$
where
$R \to \infty$
.


Notice that for any constants
$a,b,c$
, if
$R$
is sufficiently large, the triangle inequalities (e.g.,
$|x-z| = 0 \le a \le 2R = x+z$
) are trivially satisfied. Thus, this symmetric expansion is completely valid within the feasible region.


For large
$R$
, the angles
$\alpha, \beta, \gamma$
become small. We can apply the Puiseux series expansion
$\arccos(1-u)=\sqrt{2} \sqrt{u}+\frac{u^{3 / 2}}{6 \sqrt{2}}+\mathcal O\left(u^{5 / 2}\right)$
to approximate them:

$$ \cos \alpha = \frac{2R^2-a^2}{2R^2} = 1 - \frac{a^2}{2R^2} \implies \alpha = \frac{a}{R}\left(1 + \frac{a^2}{24R^2}\right)+ \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{R^5}\right)$$

Substitute these leading-order approximations into the objective function
$f(x,y,z)$
:

$$ f(R, R, R) = \frac{c^2-a^2-b^2}{2ab} + \frac{1}{24R^2ab} \left[ c^4 - a^4 - b^4 - \frac{c^2(a^2+b^2)}{2} + \frac{(a^2+b^2)^2}{2} \right] + \mathcal{O}\left(\frac{1}{R^4}\right) $$

Because we are given the condition
$c \ge a + b$
, we know
$c^2 \ge (a+b)^2 > a^2+b^2$
. Consequently, the coefficient dominating the
$1/R^2$
term evaluates to a positive number.


This indicates that for large
$R$
, the value of the objective function is
greater
than the limit.
answer:
Prove
$\alpha + \beta < \pi$

Assume for the sake of contradiction that
$\alpha + \beta \ge \pi$
.
The condition
$\alpha + \beta \ge \pi$
is equivalent to
$\alpha \ge \pi - \beta$
. Because the cosine function is strictly decreasing on the interval
$(0, \pi)$
, this implies:
$$\cos \alpha \le \cos(\pi - \beta) = -\cos \beta \implies \cos \alpha + \cos \beta \le 0$$
Consider
$a$
and
$b$
as functions of
$x$
:
$$a(x) = \sqrt{x^2 + z^2 - 2xz\cos \alpha}$$
$$b(x) = \sqrt{x^2 + y^2 - 2xy\cos \beta}$$
Define the sum function
$h(x) = a(x) + b(x)$
. We know that at
$x = 0$
, the distances simplify to:
$$h(0) = \sqrt{z^2} + \sqrt{y^2} = z + y$$
The first derivative
$h'(x)$
at
$x=0$
is:
$$h'(0) = \left. \frac{x - z\cos \alpha}{a(x)} + \frac{x - y\cos \beta}{b(x)} \right|_{x=0} = \frac{-z\cos \alpha}{z} + \frac{-y\cos \beta}{y} = -(\cos \alpha + \cos \beta)$$
From our assumption, we know
$\cos \alpha + \cos \beta \le 0$
, so
$h'(0) \ge 0$
.

Furthermore,
$h(x)$
is the sum of two strictly convex functions (their second derivatives are
$\frac{z^2\sin^2\alpha}{a^3} > 0$
and
$\frac{y^2\sin^2\beta}{b^3} > 0$
). A strictly convex function starting with a non-negative derivative at
$x=0$
must be strictly increasing for all
$x > 0$
. Therefore:
$$h(x) > h(0) \implies a + b > z + y$$
conflicting with the given conditions:
$a + b \le c < y + z$
(since
$c \ge a+b$
and
$c < y+z$
). The sum
$a+b$
cannot be both strictly greater than and strictly less than
$y+z$
. Thus, our assumption that
$\alpha + \beta \ge \pi$
must be false. Conclusion:
$\alpha + \beta < \pi$
.

Prove
$\alpha + \beta \le \gamma$

Suppose we construct two planar triangles
$\triangle XOZ$
and
$\triangle XOY$
sharing the side
$OX = x$
, with
$XZ = a$
and
$XY = b$
. Then
$$ YZ = \sqrt{y^2 + z^2 - 2yz \cos(\alpha+\beta)} \le XY + XZ = a + b $$
Since we are given that
$c\ge a + b$
, we have
$c\ge YZ$
.
Substituting
$c$
into the Law of Cosines for the third triangle
$\triangle YOZ$
, we get:
$$ c^2 = y^2 + z^2 - 2yz \cos \gamma > y^2 + z^2 - 2yz \cos(\alpha+\beta) $$
$$ -2yz \cos \gamma \ge -2yz \cos(\alpha+\beta) \implies \cos \gamma \le \cos(\alpha + \beta) $$
Let
$M = \frac{\cos\alpha\cos\beta - \cos\gamma}{\sin\alpha\sin\beta}$
. Then
$M\ge1$
.

Earlier we proved that
$\alpha + \beta < \pi$
. For angles strictly within
$(0, \pi)$
,
$\cos \gamma \le \cos(\alpha + \beta)$
implies:
$$ \gamma \ge \alpha + \beta $$

Bound the RHS
$\frac{c^2-a^2-b^2}{2ab}=\frac{(y^2+z^2-2yz\cos\gamma)-(x^2+z^2-2xz\cos\alpha)-(x^2+y^2-2xy\cos\beta)}{2\sqrt{x^2+z^2-2xz\cos\alpha}\sqrt{x^2+y^2-2xy\cos\beta}}$

Divide the numerator and denominator of the RHS by
$x^2$
and use substitutions
$u = z/x$
and
$v = y/x$
. This transforms the expression purely into terms of
$u, v$
and angles
$\alpha, \beta, \gamma$
:
$$ \text{RHS} = \frac{u\cos\alpha + v\cos\beta - uv\cos\gamma - 1}{\sqrt{1+u^2-2u\cos\alpha}\sqrt{1+v^2-2v\cos\beta}} $$

We can express
$\cos\gamma = \cos\alpha\cos\beta - M\sin\alpha\sin\beta$
. Plugging this into the numerator of the RHS, we obtain:
$$ \text{RHS} = \frac{M (u\sin\alpha)(v\sin\beta) - (1-u\cos\alpha)(1-v\cos\beta)}{\sqrt{(u\sin\alpha)^2 + (1-u\cos\alpha)^2} \sqrt{(v\sin\beta)^2 + (1-v\cos\beta)^2}} $$
Notice that the terms inside the square roots are norms of vectors. We can define angles
$\theta_1$
and
$\theta_2$
such that:
$$ \cos\theta_1 = \frac{u\sin\alpha}{\sqrt{(u\sin\alpha)^2 + (1-u\cos\alpha)^2}}, \quad \sin\theta_1 = \frac{1-u\cos\alpha}{\sqrt{(u\sin\alpha)^2 + (1-u\cos\alpha)^2}} $$
And similarly for
$\theta_2$
using
$v$
and
$\beta$
.

This lets us rewrite the RHS compactly as:
$$ \text{RHS} = M \cos\theta_1 \cos\theta_2 - \sin\theta_1 \sin\theta_2 $$

Using the product-to-sum trigonometric identities, we can split this into:
$$ \text{RHS} = \frac{M-1}{2} \cos(\theta_1 - \theta_2) + \frac{M+1}{2} \cos(\theta_1 + \theta_2) $$
Since earlier we proved
$M \ge 1$
, the coefficient
$\frac{M-1}{2}$
is strictly positive. The cosine bounds
$\cos(\theta_1 \pm \theta_2) \le 1$
allow us to globally maximize the RHS:
$$ \text{RHS} \le \frac{M-1}{2}(1) + \frac{M+1}{2}(1) = M = \frac{\cos\alpha\cos\beta - \cos\gamma}{\sin\alpha\sin\beta} $$

This maximum is actually attainable (making the bound sharp). This occurs when
$\theta_1 = \theta_2 = 0$
, which corresponds to the equality conditions
$z = x/\cos \alpha$
and
$y = x/\cos \beta$
.

Prove
$f(x, y, z) \ge M$

Let
$F(\gamma) = f(x, y, z) = \frac{\gamma^2 - \alpha^2 - \beta^2}{2\alpha\beta}$
and
$G(\gamma) = \frac{\cos\alpha\cos\beta - \cos\gamma}{\sin\alpha\sin\beta}$
. We must show
$F(\gamma) \ge G(\gamma)$
on the required interval
$\gamma \in [\alpha+\beta, \pi]$
.

Check the boundary where
$\gamma = \alpha + \beta$
:
$$ F(\alpha+\beta) = \frac{(\alpha+\beta)^2 - \alpha^2 - \beta^2}{2\alpha\beta} = 1, \quad G(\alpha+\beta) = 1 $$

Take the derivative of their difference with respect to
$\gamma$
:
$$ \frac{d}{d\gamma}[F(\gamma) - G(\gamma)] = \frac{\gamma}{\alpha\beta} - \frac{\sin\gamma}{\sin\alpha\sin\beta} = \frac{\sin\gamma}{\alpha\beta} \left( \frac{\gamma}{\sin\gamma} - \frac{\alpha\beta}{\sin\alpha\sin\beta} \right) $$

For this to be strictly positive, we need to show
$\frac{\gamma}{\sin \gamma} > \frac{\alpha\beta}{\sin \alpha \sin \beta}$
.

$\frac{\gamma}{\sin \gamma} \ge \frac{\alpha + \beta}{\sin(\alpha + \beta)}$
(because
$t/\sin t$
is increasing and
$\gamma \ge \alpha + \beta$
).
Also, we can prove
$\frac{\alpha+\beta}{\sin(\alpha+\beta)} > \frac{\alpha\beta}{\sin\alpha\sin\beta}$
by cross multiplying to get
$\frac{\sin\alpha\sin\beta}{\sin(\alpha+\beta)} = \frac{1}{\cot\alpha + \cot\beta}$
. Since
$x \cot x < 1$
for all
$x \in (0, \pi)$
, we have
$\cot\alpha + \cot\beta < \frac{1}{\alpha} + \frac{1}{\beta} = \frac{\alpha+\beta}{\alpha\beta}$
.

Thus,
$\frac{d}{d\gamma}[F(\gamma) - G(\gamma)] > 0$
.

$F(\gamma) - G(\gamma)$
is strictly increasing for
$\gamma > \alpha + \beta$
.

Because both start at
$1$
when
$\gamma = \alpha+\beta$
and the difference strictly increases, it implies
$F(\gamma) \ge G(\gamma)$
with strictness holding for
$\gamma > \alpha + \beta$
.

Finally, linking the bounds together:
$$ f(x,y,z) = F(\gamma) \ge G(\gamma) = M \ge \text{RHS} = \frac{c^2 - a^2 - b^2}{2ab} $$

question:
Trying to understand a portfolio of geometric Brownian variables through approximating the LogSumExp function.

Intro

This is a continuation of this
deleted question
: there I explored the 2 variables scenario, were I was able to split the
LogSumExp
function into the mean of the assets plus a "pseudo-quadratic-alike" non-linear term, that behaves as a parabola near the origin as then becomes linear in each assets, as expected for a
smooth maximum
.

Then trying to generalize the problem into many assets, I tried the 2nd order Taylor expansion which also leads to an average plus a quadratic term, and as I did for the deleted question, I tried to improve the approximation by introducing cosine functions, since I found they also fit and I can calculate too their expected values (as done
here
).

For a
Geometric Brownian Motion
asset
$S_i(t)$
and weights
$w_i$
such
$0\leq w_i \leq 1$
with
$\sum_i w_i = 1$
, the portfolio will be built as
$\Pi(t) = \sum_i w_i S_i(t)$
where
$S_i(t) = S_{0i}e^{(\mu_i-\sigma_i^2/2)t+\sigma_i W_{it}}$
with
$W_{it}$
a correlated
Wiener processes
with constant correlations
$\rho_{ij}$
, drifts
$\mu_i$
, initial values
$S_{0i}$
, and volatility
$\sigma_i$
. Since I can write
$w_i = e^{\ln(w_i)}$
the portfolio could be described just as a sum of variables
$x_i$
defined as
$e^{x_i} = e^{\ln(w_i)+\ln(S_{0i})+(\mu_i-\sigma_i^2/2)t+\sigma_i W_{it}}$
, and I am interested in understand the behavior of a variable
$Z_t$
such
$e^{Z_t} = \Pi(t)$
, which would lead to the description
$Z_t = \ln\left(\sum_i e^{x_i}\right)$
which is the LogSumExp function
$\operatorname{LSE}(x)$
.

So, the
$\operatorname{LSE}(x)$
and its 2nd order Taylor's approximation series near the origin
$\operatorname{LSET}(x)$
are given by:

$$\operatorname{LSE}(\vec{x})=\ln\left(\sum\limits_{k=1}^n e^{x_k}\right) \tag{Eq. 1}\label{Eq. 1}$$


$$\operatorname{LSET}(\vec{x})=\ln(n)+\bar{x}+\frac{1}{2}V_x + O(3) \tag{Eq. 2}\label{Eq. 2}$$


where the average is
$\bar{x} = \frac{1}{n}\sum\limits_{k=1}^n x_k$
and the variance is
$V_x = \frac{1}{n}\sum\limits_{k=1}^n (x_k-\bar{x})^2$
.


Questions


Question 1:


Differently from the 2 variables' scenario, using cosines functions I were able to built 2 different approximations now for the function
$\operatorname{LSE}(x)$
with the help of ChatGPT (
and after arguing many hallucinations and mistakes, but also good insights, like the bounds - suggestions are welcomed anyway
):

$$\operatorname{LSE}(\vec{x}) \approx \operatorname{LSEC}(\vec{x}) := \begin{cases} \max\limits_{k}\{x_k\}, \quad \max\limits_{k} |x_k-\bar{x}| \geq \sqrt{6} \\
\ln(n)+\bar{x}+ 1 -\dfrac{1}{n} \sum\limits_{k=1}^n \cos(x_i-\bar{x}),\quad \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \tag{Eq. 3}\label{Eq. 3}$$


and


$$\operatorname{LSE}(\vec{x}) \approx \operatorname{LSECO}(\vec{x}) := \begin{cases} \max\limits_{k}\{x_k\}, \quad \max\limits_{k} |x_k-\bar{x}| \geq \sqrt{2} \\
\ln(n)+\bar{x}+ \frac12\left(1 -\cos\left(\sqrt{2V_x}\right)\right),\quad \text{otherwise} \end{cases} \tag{Eq. 4}\label{Eq. 4}$$


Here is a
Desmos3D link
comparing these approximations, where I would like to use the
$\operatorname{LSEC}(x)$
option since
stays
for longer approximating the
$\operatorname{LSE}(x)$
function (but sacrificing a little accuracy) and they even met at a point that could work as threshold, which would be beneficial given the random nature of the variables
$x_i$
(I will require less interventions to the portfolio), but here is were the question comes, they look fine in 2 dimensions, and given the Taylor series is quadratic at least give some hope about the representativity, but I am aware that very weird counter-intuitive things happen with geometry in higher dimensions (this
3b1b video
as example), so my first question is:


Are these approximations really representing accurately the LogSumExp function in higher dimensions (finite)?
To be somehow specific about what I mean with
accurately
, I am thinking if they approximates the function at least as close as the 2 variable scenario does.


Question 2


I want to use these approximation to study the variable
$Z_t$
and take conclusions, but I am afraid that everything I could have been doing so far could be mistaken: since I will have to update the weights periodically such no assets takes the full representation of the porfolio, thinking in the
$x_i$
classically tells me I would be able to keep the portfolio within bounds, but unfortunately for my analysis these assets belong to the
Stochastic calculus
and don't behave as the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus dictates. The formal way to see the problem is by applying
Itô's lemma
to the
$\operatorname{LSE}(x)$
function of \eqref{Eq. 1} and there see how it will behave, which I tried and I got many
Softmax functions
involved within the stochastic terms, but since I am new into stochastic calculus I got lost, which leads to the second question:


Could you deliver the proper formulation of Itô's lemma for the \eqref{Eq. 1} explaining how it compares with the approximations? Would they hold? Is there any threshold that will tell when the approximations would be useful or not?
(I don't know, maybe some "while
$\max{\sigma_k}<\text{something}$
the intuition of the approximations will hold", as hypothetical example).
answer:
[I will provide just the full Ito formula application to
$Z_t$
, and a possible further lead]. Let
$S=(S^{(1)},...,S^{(n)})$
be of the form
$dS^{(k)}_t=S_t^{(k)}(\mu^{(k)}(t,S_t)+\sigma^{(k)}(t,S_t)\cdot dW_t)$
where
$W$
is a
$\mathbb{R}^n$
-Brownian motion and
$\mu^{(k)}:[0,\infty)\times \mathbb{R}^n\to \mathbb{R},\sigma^{(k)}:[0,\infty)\times \mathbb{R}^n\to \mathbb{R}^n$
be s.t. a (possibly strong) solution for
$S$
exists. We define
$Z_t=\ln(w^{(1)}S^{(1)}_t+...+w^{(n)}S^{(n)}_t)=f(S_t)$
. Hence for the function
$f(s)=f(s_1,...,s_n)$
we get
$$
\partial_{s_k}f(s)=\frac{w^{(k)}}{{w^{(1)}s_1}+...+w^{(n)}s_n},\,\partial_{s_ks_\ell}f(s)=-\frac{w^{(k)}w^{(\ell)}}{({w^{(1)}s_1}+...+w^{(n)}s_n)^2}
$$
So by Ito's formula
$$
\begin{aligned}
dZ_t&=\sum_{1\leq k \leq n}\partial_{s_k}f(S_t)dS_t^{(k)}+\frac{1}{2}\sum_{1\leq k,\ell \leq n}\partial_{s_ks_\ell}f(S_t)S_t^{(k)}S_t^{(\ell)}(\sigma^{(\ell)}(t,S_t)\cdot \sigma^{(k)}(t,S_t))dt\\
&=\bigg(\sum_{1\leq k \leq n}\frac{w^{(k)}S_t^{(k)}}{e^{Z_t}}\mu^{(k)}(t,S_t)-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{1\leq k,\ell \leq n}\frac{w^{(k)}S_t^{(k)}w^{(\ell)}S_t^{(\ell)}}{e^{2Z_t}}(\sigma^{(\ell)}(t,S_t)\cdot \sigma^{(k)}(t,S_t))\bigg)dt\\
&+\sum_{1\leq k \leq n}\frac{w^{(k)}S_t^{(k)}}{e^{Z_t}}\sigma^{(k)}(t,S_t)\cdot dW_t
\end{aligned}$$
If we define
$a_t^{(k)}:=w^{(k)}S_t^{(k)}/e^{Z_t}$
(the relative weights) we can cleanly rewrite the differential as
$$
\begin{aligned}
dZ_t&=\bigg(\sum_{1\leq k \leq n}a_t^{(k)}\mu^{(k)}(t,S_t)-\frac{1}{2}\sum_{1\leq k,\ell \leq n}a_t^{(k)}a_t^{(\ell)}(\sigma^{(\ell)}(t,S_t)\cdot \sigma^{(k)}(t,S_t))\bigg)dt\\
&+\sum_{1\leq k \leq n}a_t^{(k)}\sigma^{(k)}(t,S_t)\cdot dW_t
\end{aligned}$$
If we further define
$X=(X^{(1)},...,X^{(n)})$
where
$X_t^{(k)}:=\ln(w^{(k)}S_t^{(k)})$
we get
$$dX_t^{(k)}=\bigg(\mu^{(k)}(t,S_t)-\frac{1}{2}\|\sigma^{(k)}(t,S_t)\|^2\bigg)dt+\sigma^{(k)}(t,S_t)\cdot dW_t$$
and by applying Ito to
$Z_t=\ln(e^{X^{(1)}_t}+...+e^{X^{(n)}_t})=g(X_t)$
where
$g(x)=\ln(e^{x_1}+...+e^{x_n})$
we obtain the same result, noting that
$a_t^{(k)}=e^{X_t^{(k)}}/e^{Z_t}$
. At this point, if
$Y_t=h(X_t)$
is another process (the approximation), you may want to see what
$dZ_t-dY_t$
looks like by applying Ito to
$Y_t$
and seeing what happens to
$dZ_t-dY_t$
.

question:
Given a positive
$x$
we want to prove:

$$\cos\left(\frac{1}{3}\arccos\left(\sqrt{\frac{27x}{2(2x+3)^3}}\right)-120^\circ\right)+\sqrt{\frac{6x}{(2x+3)(4x+3)^2}}>0$$

The plot of the left hand side is given below and we see it is positive.
I am interested in using any calculus or algebra to prove it.
Below is my efforts so far:
Let:

$$f=\sqrt{\frac{27x}{2(2x+3)^3}}$$


$$g=\sqrt{\frac{6x}{(2x+3)(4x+3)^2}}$$


Therefore, we need to prove:


$$\cos{\left(\frac{\arccos(f)-360^\circ}{3}\right)}>-g$$

Using the differential calculus we can prove (taking the derivatives to find the local maxima)

$$0<f\le \frac{1}{3}\qquad(1)$$


$$0<g\le 0.1734\qquad(2)$$

Since
$g$
is within
$-1$
and
$1$
we can take the
$\arccos$
of it. Using the fact that the
$\arccos$
is descending we need to prove:

$$\frac{-\arccos(f)+360^\circ}{3}<\arccos(-g)=180^\circ-\arccos(g)$$

or:

$$-\arccos(f)+3\arccos(g)<180^\circ\qquad(3)$$

From (
$1$
) & (
$2$
) we get:

$$70.53^\circ=\arccos\left(\frac{1}{3}\right)\le\arccos(f)<90^\circ$$


$$90^\circ<\frac{-\arccos(f)+360^\circ}{3}\le96.49^\circ$$


$$80.01^\circ=\arccos(0.1734)\le\arccos(g)<90^\circ$$


$$240.03^\circ\le3\arccos(g)<270^\circ$$

Therefore we have:

$$-\arccos(f)+3\arccos(g)<270^\circ-70.53^\circ=199.47^\circ$$

Which is close to what I want to prove but not
$180^\circ$
as below:

$$-\arccos(f)+3\arccos(g)<180^\circ\qquad(3)$$
answer:
It suffices to prove that
$$\sqrt{\frac{6x}{(2x+3)(4x+3)^2}}
\ge \cos \left(\frac13 \arccos \sqrt{\frac{27x}{2(2x+3)^3}} + \frac{\pi}{3}\right),$$
or
$$\arccos \sqrt{\frac{6x}{(2x+3)(4x+3)^2}}
\le \frac13 \arccos \sqrt{\frac{27x}{2(2x+3)^3}} + \frac{\pi}{3},$$
or
$$3\arccos \sqrt{\frac{6x}{(2x+3)(4x+3)^2}}
\le \arccos \sqrt{\frac{27x}{2(2x+3)^3}} + \pi,$$
or
$$3\arccos \sqrt{\frac{6x}{(2x+3)(4x+3)^2}} - \pi
\le \arccos \sqrt{\frac{27x}{2(2x+3)^3}},$$
or
$$\cos\left(3\arccos \sqrt{\frac{6x}{(2x+3)(4x+3)^2}} - \pi\right)
\ge \sqrt{\frac{27x}{2(2x+3)^3}},$$
or (using
$\cos (3u-\pi) = -4\cos^3 u + 3\cos u$
)
$$-4 \left(\sqrt{\frac{6x}{(2x+3)(4x+3)^2}}\right)^3 + 3\sqrt{\frac{6x}{(2x+3)(4x+3)^2}} \ge \sqrt{\frac{27x}{2(2x+3)^3}}. \tag{1}$$

Let
$y := \sqrt{\frac{6x}{2x+3}}$
(so
$x = \frac{3y^2}{2(3-y^2)}$
). (1) is written as
$$-4 \left(\frac{y(3-y^2)}{3(y^2+3)}\right)^3 + 3\left(\frac{y(3-y^2)}{3(y^2+3)}\right) \ge \frac16 y(3-y^2), $$
or
$$\frac{y(17y^4 + 30y^2 + 81)(3-y^2)^2}{54(3+y^2)^3}\ge 0$$
which is true.

We are done.

Improve this question

Let
$n \in \mathbb{N}$
and let
$A, B \in M_n(\mathbb{C})$
such that
$AB - BA$
is invertible and

$(A - B)^2 = I_n$
.
Prove that
$\operatorname{Tr}(A) = \operatorname{Tr}(B)$
and that
$n$
is even. -problem written by Mihai Opincariu, "Gazeta Matematica Seria B".

I have been practicing some olympiad problems and I have struggled with this one. I currently have no idea how to approach it. Could you help me?
answer:
Observe that
$M=\frac{I+A-B}{2}$
is idempotent and that
$MA-AM=\tfrac{1}{2}(AB-BA)$
which is invertible.

In this
quora answer
, I had shown that:

Proposition
: Let
$\mathbb F$
be a field and
$n$
be a positive integer
$\geq 2$
. Suppose
$A\in M_n(\mathbb F)$
is idempotent and there is a
$B\in M_n(\mathbb F)$
such that
$AB-BA$
is invertible then
$n$
has to be even and
$\text{rank}(A)=\frac n2$
.

Proposition
: Let
$\mathbb F$
be a field and
$n$
be a positive integer
$\geq 2$
. Suppose
$A\in M_n(\mathbb F)$
is idempotent and there is a
$B\in M_n(\mathbb F)$
such that
$AB-BA$
is invertible then
$n$
has to be even and
$\text{rank}(A)=\frac n2$
.

Proof
:
$\DeclareMathOperator{\rk}{rk} \DeclareMathOperator{\tr}{tr}$

Case 1
: Suppose
$\rk(A)$
is strictly less than
$\frac n2$
then
$\rk(AB)$
and
$\rk(BA)$
are strictly less than
$\frac n2$
as well. Since
$\rk(AB-BA)$
is no more than sum of
$\rk(AB)$
and
$\rk(BA)$
, we see that
$\rk(AB-BA)<n$
. Thus,
$AB-BA$
is not invertible. (contradition!)
Case 2
: Now, suppose that
$\rk(A)>\frac n2$
. Since
$A$
is idempotent, we know that
$\rk(A)$
and
$\rk(I-A)$
add to
$n$
. Thus,
$\rk(I-A)<\frac n2$
. Observe that
$BA-AB=(I-A)B-B(I-A)$
. Repeat the same argument as in case 1 to arrive at a contradiction.
Thus,
$\rk(A)=\frac n2$
is the only possibility since the other two contradict the fact that
$AB-BA$
is invertible. Since
$\rk(A)$
is a positive integer,
$n$
has to be even.
$\blacksquare$

Case 1
: Suppose
$\rk(A)$
is strictly less than
$\frac n2$
then
$\rk(AB)$
and
$\rk(BA)$
are strictly less than
$\frac n2$
as well. Since
$\rk(AB-BA)$
is no more than sum of
$\rk(AB)$
and
$\rk(BA)$
, we see that
$\rk(AB-BA)<n$
. Thus,
$AB-BA$
is not invertible. (contradition!)

Case 2
: Now, suppose that
$\rk(A)>\frac n2$
. Since
$A$
is idempotent, we know that
$\rk(A)$
and
$\rk(I-A)$
add to
$n$
. Thus,
$\rk(I-A)<\frac n2$
. Observe that
$BA-AB=(I-A)B-B(I-A)$
. Repeat the same argument as in case 1 to arrive at a contradiction.

Thus,
$\rk(A)=\frac n2$
is the only possibility since the other two contradict the fact that
$AB-BA$
is invertible. Since
$\rk(A)$
is a positive integer,
$n$
has to be even.
$\blacksquare$

Using this proposition, you get that
$n$
must be even. Also that
$\rk(M)=\tfrac n2$
.
Over a field of characteristic zero, rank of an idempotent matrix is equal to its trace
so, you have
$\rk(M)=\tr(M)$
. Using linearity of trace,
$\tr(M) = \tfrac{n+\tr(A)-\tr(B)}2$
i.e.,
$\tfrac n2 = \tfrac{n+\tr(A)-\tr(B)}2$
so we get
$\tr(A)=\tr(B)$
.

Improve this question

Let
$n \in \mathbb{N}$
and let
$A, B \in M_n(\mathbb{C})$
such that
$AB - BA$
is invertible and

$(A - B)^2 = I_n$
.
Prove that
$\operatorname{Tr}(A) = \operatorname{Tr}(B)$
and that
$n$
is even. -problem written by Mihai Opincariu, "Gazeta Matematica Seria B".

I have been practicing some olympiad problems and I have struggled with this one. I currently have no idea how to approach it. Could you help me?
answer:
Let
$C = A - B,D = AB - BA,B = A - C$
, then
$$
D = A(A - C) - (A - C)A
= A^2 - AC - A^2 + CA
= CA - AC
$$

$$CD = C(CA - AC) = C^2 A - CAC$$

$(A - B)^2 = I_n \Rightarrow C^2 = I_n$
. Then
$$CD = C(CA - AC) = A - CAC,DC = (CA - AC)C = CAC - AC^2 = CAC - A$$

So
$CD + DC = 0 ,C = -DCD^{-1}$
.
It implies that
$C$
is similar to
$-C$
. Then they have the same trace, so:
$Tr(C) = Tr(-C) =-Tr(C),Tr(C) =0$
. Thus
$Tr(A)=Tr(B)$
.

Also
$C^2 = I_n$
. Therefore, the eigenvalues of
$C$
can only be
$1$
or
$-1$
.
Let
$k$
be the multiplicity of the eigenvalue
$1$
and set equation, it is easy to show
$n$
must be an even number.

Improve this question

In this remark in Weierstrass Preparation theorem, he claims
$b_i$
mod
$m^{r+1}$
are uniquely determined because all
$b_i\  =0\ (\mbox{mod}\ m\ )$
.
But I don't understand how
$b_i$
mod
$m^{r+1}$
are determined directly from all
$b_i$
mod
$m^{r}$
and
$b_i\  =0\ (\mbox{mod}\ m\ )$
. What am I missing?
answer:
Suppose we have solved all equations
$\bmod {\frak m}^k$
and we now want to solve them
$\bmod {\frak m}^{k+1}$
. Since
$c_0=1$
, the first equation is easily solved as
$b_0$
mod
${\frak m}^{k+1} = a_0$
mod
${\frak m}^{k+1}$
.

In addition,
$b_0 = 0 \bmod{}{\frak m}$
and therefore

$$b_0=b'_{0,k}+ m_{0b,1} + m_{0b,k} \quad m_{0b,1} \in {\frak m} \quad m_{0b,k} \in {\frak m}^k$$

From the solution mod
${\frak m}^{k}$
in the previous round, we write

$$c_1=c_{1,k} + m_{1c,k} \quad m_{1c,k} \in {\frak m}^k$$

Now turn to the second equation. The first term
$b_0c_1$
can be written as

$$b_0c_1 =(b'_{0,k}+ m_{0b,1} + m_{0b,k}) (c_{1,k} + m_{1c,k}) \\
= (b'_{0,k}+ m_{0b,1} + m_{0b,k}) c_{1,k} + b'_{0,k} m_{1c,k} + (m_{0b,1} + m_{0b,k})m_{1c,k}
$$

The last term
$(m_{0b,1} + m_{0b,k})m_{1c,k} \in {\frak m}^{k+1}$
, and so we have computed
$b_0c_1 \bmod {\frak m}^{k+1}$
though we know
$c_1$
only
$\bmod {\frak m}^k$
and this happens because
$b_0 = 0 \bmod{}{\frak m}$
. Therefore the equation

$$b_0c_1 + b_1 c_0 = a_1 \quad \bmod {\frak m}^{k+1}$$

can be solved for
$b_1 \bmod {\frak m}^{k+1}$
. What appears to be one equation in two variables (
$b_1$
and
$c_1$
) turns out to be one equation in
$b_1$
.

Proceeding in like manner, the first set of equations determine
$b_j$
and the second set of equations determine
$c_j$
.

Suppose that
$f$
and
$fg$
are both differentiable at
$a$
with
$(fg)'(a) \neq 0$
. Must
$g$
be differentiable at
$a$
?

Suppose that
$g\circ f$
is differentiable at
$a$
and
$g$
is differentiable at
$f(a)$
, with
$(g \circ f)'(a)\neq0$
. Must
$f$
be differentiable at
$a$
?

The counterexamples I have come up with have
$g$
discontinuous. Is it possible to find counterexamples where all functions are continuous?
answer:
Product case:
Define
$$h(x) = \left\{\begin{array}{cc} x\sin(1/x) & \mbox{if $x\neq 0$} \\ 0 & \mbox{ if $x=0$} \end{array}\right.$$
Then
$h$
is continuous but not differentiable. Now define
$$f(x) = x\quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$$
$$ g(x) = 1+h(x) \quad \forall x \in \mathbb{R}$$
and consider
$f$
and
$fg$
.

question:
I am investigating the existence conditions (realizability in
$\mathbb{R}^3$
) for a tetrahedron
$ABCD$
possessing
$C_2$
symmetry. Specifically, I am looking at the case where two pairs of opposite edges are equal:

$AC = BD = p$

$AD = BC = q$

Let the lengths of the remaining opposite edges be
$AB$
and
$CD$
. I have derived an expression for the length of the bimedian
$h$
(the segment
$EF$
connecting the midpoints of
$AB$
and
$CD$
) using the following steps:

Median of the face triangle: In
$\triangle ABC$
, the median
$CE$
to the side
$AB$
is found via Apollonius's Theorem:
$$CE^2 = \frac{AC^2 + BC^2}{2} - \left(\frac{AB}{2}\right)^2 = \frac{p^2 + q^2}{2} - \frac{AB^2}{4}$$


Bimedian length: By symmetry,
$DE = CE$
, making
$\triangle CED$
an isosceles triangle. The bimedian
$EF$
(with length
$h$
) is the altitude from
$E$
to the base
$CD$
. Therefore:
$$h^2 = CE^2 - CF^2 = \left( \frac{p^2 + q^2}{2} - \frac{AB^2}{4} \right) - \left( \frac{CD}{2} \right)^2$$
$$h^2 = \frac{1}{4}(2p^2 + 2q^2 - AB^2 - CD^2)$$








For any tetrahedron
$ABCD$
, we can establish a general vector identity relating the dot product of opposite edges to the squares of the remaining four edges. Let
$\vec{A}, \vec{B}, \vec{C}, \vec{D}$
be the position vectors of the vertices. Then:

\begin{align}&\vec{CA}^2-\vec{AD}^2+\vec{DB}^2-\vec{BC}^2\\
&=(\vec{A}-\vec{C})^2-(\vec{D}-\vec{A})^2+(\vec{B}-\vec{D})^2-(\vec{C}-\vec{B})^2\\
&=2(-\vec{A}\cdot\vec{C}+\vec{D}\cdot\vec{A}-\vec{B}\cdot\vec{D}+\vec{C}\cdot\vec{B})\\
&=2(\vec{B}-\vec{A})\cdot(\vec{C}-\vec{D})\\&=2\vec{AB}\cdot\vec{DC}
\end{align}




Substitute the edge lengths into the identity
$\vec{AB}\cdot\vec{DC}=\frac{1}{2}(CA^2-AD^2+DB^2-BC^2)$
,

$$\vec{AB}\cdot\vec{DC}=p^2-q^2$$

Since
$|\vec{AB}\cdot\vec{DC}| \le AB\cdot CD$
, we must have:
$$|p^2 - q^2| \le AB \cdot CD$$




My Question: While it is clear that we must have
$\begin{cases}AB^2 + CD^2 \le 2p^2 + 2q^2\\AB \cdot CD \ge |p^2 - q^2|\end{cases}$
, why are two conditions sufficient for the realizability of the tetrahedron?




These two conditions are independent. One does not imply the other, as shown by these cases:




(1) holds, (2) fails: If
$p=5, q=1, AB=2, CD=2$
, then
$AB^2+CD^2=8 \le 52$
, but
$AB \cdot CD=4 < 24$
. Geometrically, the edges are "short enough" to exist, but the symmetry forces an impossible rotation angle.


(2) holds, (1) fails: If
$p=1, q=1, AB=3, CD=3$
, then
$AB \cdot CD=9 \ge 0$
, but
$AB^2+CD^2=18 > 4$
. Geometrically, the rotation is valid, but the edges
$AB$
and
$CD$
are too far apart to be joined by edges of length
$p$
and
$q$
.






Proof that Conditions (1) and (2) imply the Triangle Inequalities


We wish to show that the conditions




$AB^2 + CD^2 \le 2p^2 + 2q^2$


$AB \cdot CD \ge |p^2 - q^2|$




imply the face triangle inequalities
$|p
- q| \le AB \le p + q$
and
$|p - q| \le CD \le p + q$
.


From condition (2), we have
$CD \ge \frac{|p^2 - q^2|}{AB}$
. Substituting this into condition (1):
$$AB^2 + \frac{(p^2 - q^2)^2}{AB^2} \le 2(p^2 + q^2)$$
Let
$x = AB^2$
. Multiplying by
$x$
(which is positive) and rearranging into a quadratic form:
$$x^2 - 2(p^2 + q^2)x + (p^2 - q^2)^2 \le 0$$
To find the interval where this quadratic is non-positive, we find the roots using the quadratic formula:
$$x = \frac{2(p^2 + q^2) \pm \sqrt{4(p^2 + q^2)^2 - 4(p^2 - q^2)^2}}{2}$$
$$x = (p^2 + q^2) \pm \sqrt{(p^2 + q^2)^2 - (p^2 - q^2)^2}$$
The term under the square root simplifies as follows:
$$(p^2 + q^2)^2 - (p^2 - q^2)^2 = (p^4 + 2p^2q^2 + q^4) - (p^4 - 2p^2q^2 + q^4) = 4p^2q^2$$
Thus, the roots are:
$$x = p^2 + q^2 \pm 2pq = (p \pm q)^2$$
Since the leading coefficient of the quadratic
$x^2 + \dots$
is positive, the inequality
$f(x) \le 0$
holds only between the roots:
$$(p - q)^2 \le x \le (p + q)^2$$
Taking the square root (noting
$AB > 0$
):
$$|p - q| \le AB \le p + q$$
By the symmetry of the initial conditions in
$AB$
and
$CD$
, the same derivation holds for
$CD$
. Thus, the face triangle inequalities are necessarily satisfied whenever conditions (1) and (2) hold.
answer:
Here's how I would think about it. I take the ABC and ABD faces and unfold them along AB until they are flat, say in the
$xy$
plane:

Then ACBD is always a parallelogram, and for fixed
$p,q$
there is one degree of freedom in choosing the parallelogram: we can vary
$\theta$
or equivalently we can vary the length
$AB$
. For a given choice, consider folding along AB, keeping AB fixed in the
$xy$
plane and moving C and D closer together in such a way that they are always the same height above the
$xy$
plane until the CD length is correct, and the tetrahedron is regenerated. This motion has one degree of freedom, described by either angle CED or the CD distance. If E is the origin, then the midpoint of CD is directly above the origin and the
$z$
axis is a
$C_{2}$
rotation axis.

These two degrees of freedom mean there are two conditions to specify a tetrahedron of the required type; these can be specifying
$\theta$
and the angle CED.

question:
Is every associative algebra isomorphic to a matrix algebra or a subset of a matrix algebra ?

We know matrix multiplication is associative.

We also know functional composition is associative.

With Carleman matrices we can get functional composition of taylor series in matrix form.

Note I do consider infinite matrices as matrices here too.

Rotation and linear operators are also matrices.

So I can not think of a way that an associative algebra is not matrix algebra.

By the above I think I can say that movement as a functional composition on a surface or other simple geometric structure is a matrix algebra.

Nilpotent elements can also be given by matrices.

Now maybe you think :

What about nonunital associative algebras ?

An example of a non-unital associative algebra is given by the set of all functions f : R → R whose limit as x goes to infinity is zero.

This can be given by Taylor series and Carleman matrices so it seems like just another matrix algebra.

And extending the reals to complex or such does not seem to influence it.

Isomorphic also seems like a weak equivalence like for instance any bijective mapping that preserves properties will do.

I think even associative movement on surfaces with holes in them is still a matrix algebra.

It seems even non-distributive algebra can have a matrix representation.

And matrices also appear in multivariable calculus.

Even tropical geometry and similar optimization stuff has a matrix representation.

However I have no formal proof of this simple statement :

Conjecture :

Every associative algebra isomorphic to a matrix algebra or a subset of a matrix algebra

I find this fascinating.

And it implies somewhat that studying associative algebras is studying matrices and vice versa in a way.

Some possible ways to resolve it maybe :

The vector space of continuous periodic functions, together with the convolution product.

But I think this also has a matrix representation ?

Some people have told me about tensors but I think associative tensors still belong to matrices in a way ?

Lie algebra's or quantum ??

Ring theory and minimal polynomials might help ?

Any help is appreciated.

ps : Most ideas here are about square matrices.
I almost wanted to write square matrix algebra.
answer:
No. Indeed the following famous example of Bergman gets the job done:

For any prime
$p$
let
$R$
the algebra of endomorphisms of the abelian group
$\mathbb{Z}/(p) \oplus \mathbb{Z}/(p^2)$
. Then
$R$
does not embed into any algebra of matrices over any commutative ring!!

See the original paper
Some Examples in PI Ring Theory
by Bergman for a proof.

I hope this helps ^_^

Lie algebra's or quantum ??

Ring theory and minimal polynomials might help ?
