Mathematics Stack Exchange guidelines
. It is not currently accepting answers.

This question is not about mathematics, within the scope defined in the
help center
.

Closed
last month
.

Improve this question

The book chapter on Gaussian channels that I am reading says this is a constraint for "The average power of a transmission of length
$T$
" without any further explanation, where
$x(t)$
is the transmission input:

$$\int_0^T \frac{dt[x(t)]^2}{T} \le P$$

Struggling to understand where exactly the intuition is coming from. Would appreciate a hint.
answer:
Let
$x(t)$
denote the time-dependent received voltage on an
$R$
resistor. Then the received and dissipated instantaneous power is exactly
$p(t)=\frac{x^2(t)}{R}$
whose time average over
$T\rm{sec}$
(and we let
$T \to \infty$
) when for
$R=1\Omega$
is
$$\langle p\rangle_T=\frac{1}{T}\int_0^T p(t)\rm dt = \frac{1}{T}\int_0^T x^2(t)\rm dt.$$

An RF communication channel is, first of all, peak power limited because of its output power amplifier preceding the antenna will blow up if the peak is exceeded, in other words if the transmit voltage of the amp is
$y(t)$
then there is a
$max |y(t)|^2=P_{max}$
. Given that peak power limitation, which leads to a very difficult problem to analyze mathematically, the channel itself is randomly varying for several reasons: the distance between the transmitter and receiver is random, the channel itself attenuates randomly, and may include other random signal interferences (jamming), the channel itself is time-varying, etc. For these the natural prior limit is the mean square average power and in some very important cases that average power limitation can be handled analytically.

question:
Let
$A$
be a commutative ring and let
$n$
be a natural number.
If we want
$\mathbb{P}^n (A)$
to be the set of lines (through the origin) in
$\mathbb{A}^{n+1} (A)$
, then we have to use the following definition:

A
line
(through the origin) in
$\mathbb{A}^{n+1} (A)$
is an
$A$
-submodule
$L \subseteq A^{\oplus (n+1)}$
that is a direct summand (hence, a fortiori, locally free and finitely generated) of rank 1.

So, for example, when
$n = 0$
, there is only one line in
$\mathbb{A}^1 (A)$
, consistent with
$\mathbb{P}^0 (A)$
being a single point.
On the other hand, cases such as
$2 \mathbb{Z} \subsetneqq \mathbb{Z}$
(a free submodule of rank 1 that is not a direct summand!), or more generally any non-trivial principal ideal in a principal ideal domain, indicate
some
extra condition is needed on top of being a locally free submodule of rank 1.

Question.

Is there some intuitive (and preferably geometric) condition that can be added to submodule to obtain the correct definition of "line"?

For illustration, consider the condition that, for every prime ideal
$\mathfrak{p} \subset A$
,
$L_\mathfrak{p}$
be (freely) generated by
$u \in A_\mathfrak{p}^{\oplus (n+1)}$
such that at least one
$u_i$
is a unit in
$A_\mathfrak{p}$
(or, equivalently,
$\textrm{Frac} (A / \mathfrak{p})$
).
This suffices to complete the definition of "line", but (in my opinion) is impossible to explain intuitively without first going through the whole Grothendieck yoga of affine schemes.
(On the other hand, if we take scheme theory for granted, then this condition is easy to explain: it just means that
$L$
is locally free of rank 1 and the embedding of
$L$
does not vanish anywhere.)

I would also accept an argument showing that the direct summand condition follows from some natural (preferably geometric) considerations.
Why should it be that a line in an affine space must admit a projection onto it?
answer:
Firstly,the space
$\mathbb{P}^n(A)$
should parameterise "line subbundles" (rather than lines) in a rank
$n+1$
trivial vector bundle over
$\mathrm{Spec}(A)$
. Now a line bundle is indeed a locally free rank 1 module as you noted.

The key word here is "subbundle": this means that not only is the map of sheaves
$L \rightarrow O_X^{n+1}$
an inclusion, it has to be an inclusion on every fibre.

For example, instead of
$\mathbb{Z}$
, if we use the more geometric example of the affine line
$\mathbb{A}^1 = \mathrm{Spec}(k[t])$
, then the inclusion
$tk[t] \subseteq k[t]$
, which we can rewrite as
$k[t] \xrightarrow{\times t} k[t]$
, corresponds to the map of trivial line bundles, which at a point
$t\in \mathbb{A}^1$
is given by multiplication by
$t$
. But at the origin, this map is not injective. Thus this map cannot reasonably be considered the inclusion of a subbundle.

Luckily there is a very neat way to enforce the inclusion to be a subbundle: simply require that the quotient is also locally free (equivalently flat). Indeed, if
$0 \rightarrow L \rightarrow A^{n+1} \rightarrow Q \rightarrow 0$
is the corresponding short exact sequence and
$Q$
is flat, then if we restrict to any point of
$A$
, corresponding to
$A \rightarrow k$
(or more generally any
$A$
-algebra), then
$\mathrm{Tor}_1^A(k, Q) = 0$
, so by the long exact it follows
$L \otimes k \rightarrow k^{n+1}$
is still injective.

So a line subbundle of a finite free module is simply a locally free rank 1 submodule with a locally free quotient.

question:
Let
$A$
be a commutative ring and let
$n$
be a natural number.
If we want
$\mathbb{P}^n (A)$
to be the set of lines (through the origin) in
$\mathbb{A}^{n+1} (A)$
, then we have to use the following definition:

A
line
(through the origin) in
$\mathbb{A}^{n+1} (A)$
is an
$A$
-submodule
$L \subseteq A^{\oplus (n+1)}$
that is a direct summand (hence, a fortiori, locally free and finitely generated) of rank 1.

So, for example, when
$n = 0$
, there is only one line in
$\mathbb{A}^1 (A)$
, consistent with
$\mathbb{P}^0 (A)$
being a single point.
On the other hand, cases such as
$2 \mathbb{Z} \subsetneqq \mathbb{Z}$
(a free submodule of rank 1 that is not a direct summand!), or more generally any non-trivial principal ideal in a principal ideal domain, indicate
some
extra condition is needed on top of being a locally free submodule of rank 1.

Question.

Is there some intuitive (and preferably geometric) condition that can be added to submodule to obtain the correct definition of "line"?

For illustration, consider the condition that, for every prime ideal
$\mathfrak{p} \subset A$
,
$L_\mathfrak{p}$
be (freely) generated by
$u \in A_\mathfrak{p}^{\oplus (n+1)}$
such that at least one
$u_i$
is a unit in
$A_\mathfrak{p}$
(or, equivalently,
$\textrm{Frac} (A / \mathfrak{p})$
).
This suffices to complete the definition of "line", but (in my opinion) is impossible to explain intuitively without first going through the whole Grothendieck yoga of affine schemes.
(On the other hand, if we take scheme theory for granted, then this condition is easy to explain: it just means that
$L$
is locally free of rank 1 and the embedding of
$L$
does not vanish anywhere.)

I would also accept an argument showing that the direct summand condition follows from some natural (preferably geometric) considerations.
Why should it be that a line in an affine space must admit a projection onto it?
answer:
Consider the map of rank-1 trivial bundles over
$\mathbb{A}^1$
given by

$$T: \mathbb{A^1} \times k \to \mathbb{A^1} \times k$$
$$(c, v) \mapsto (c, cv).$$

Would you consider this an inclusion of a line subbundle in another line bundle? I would not.

However, the induced map of modules of sections is injective: if
$s_1, s_2: \mathbb{A}^1 \to \mathbb{A}^1 \times k$
are sections and
$T \circ s_1 = T \circ s_2$
, then
$s_1 = s_2$
.

The issue is that a map of modules being injective describes the second thing I’ve described, not the first. It is in fact equivalent to a map of bundles that is injective on a dense open set of fibers. This is weaker than being injective on every fiber (which is my definition of inclusion of a subbundle).

Edit: just to add that my answer is basically the same as @Calculus101’s answer, just in slightly different words.

question:
Recall that a homeomorphism is a continuous, bijective function
$f:X\to Y$
between two topological spaces that has a continuous inverse
$f^{-1}:Y\to X$
.

Let
$\eta:[0,+\infty)\to [0,+\infty)$
be a homeomorphism. It must be strictly increasing, isn't it?

The problem is the point
$0.$
I think it is true. But I don't know how to prove it. Any comments will be appreciated.
answer:
Assume there exists
$x< y$
in
$[0,\infty)$
such that
$f(y)<f(x)$
. Note that
$f$
restricted to
$[0,y]$
must have a maximum by the Extreme Value Theorem, call it
$M$
. Since
$f$
is bijective there exists
$z\in [0,\infty)$
such that
$f(z)=M+1$
. We have
$y<z$
by definition of
$M$
. By the Intermediate Value theorem on the intervals
$(x,y)$
and
$(y,z)$
we have
$c_1\in (x,y)$
,
$c_2\in (y,z)$
such that
$$f(c_1)=\frac{f(x)+f(y)}{2}, f(c_2)=\frac{f(x)+f(y)}{2}$$
since
$f(y)<\frac{f(x)+f(y)}{2}<f(x)<M+1=f(z)$
by the definition of
$M$
. Thus
$f$
is not injective, contradiction.

question:
Recall that a homeomorphism is a continuous, bijective function
$f:X\to Y$
between two topological spaces that has a continuous inverse
$f^{-1}:Y\to X$
.

Let
$\eta:[0,+\infty)\to [0,+\infty)$
be a homeomorphism. It must be strictly increasing, isn't it?

The problem is the point
$0.$
I think it is true. But I don't know how to prove it. Any comments will be appreciated.
answer:
Because
$0$
is the only point with connected complement, and continuous maps preserve connectedness (bijections complements...),
$f(0)=0$
. Thus, any connected open neighbourhood of
$0$
$B:= [0, x)$
must be mapped on such an
$0-$
neighbourhood
$\tilde B =[0, y_x)$
and by a similar argument:
$f(x)=y_x$
.
So, if
$x\leq z$
also
$[0, x)\subseteq [0,z)$
and therefore
$[0, f(x)) \subseteq [0, f(z))$
, i.e.
$f(x)\leq f(z)$
, where injectivity implies the strictness.

So yes:
$0$
is sort of the reason (the half-closedness of the interval to be more precise). For example for the space
$(0, \infty)$
also exist monotone decreasing homeomorphisms.

question:
We need to evaluate,

$$I = \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\text{Li}_2(q) - \text{Li}_2(-qt^2)}{1 + t^2} dt $$

This integral came up in solving this
question

I am not able to find a closed form for this integral. Any help would be much appreciated.
answer:
Here's what I've come up with.

On the principal branches, for
$|q|<1$
, let
$$I(q)=\int_0^1 \frac{\operatorname{Li}_2(q)-\operatorname{Li}_2(-qt^2)}{1+t^2}\,dt, \qquad a=\sqrt q, \qquad \lambda=\frac{1-a}{1+a}.$$
Then
$$I(q)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{q^n}{n^2}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{(-1)^k}{2k+1}$$
and also
$$I(q)=\frac{\pi}{4}\operatorname{Li}_2(q)+\frac14\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^n q^n}{n^2}\left(\psi\!\left(\frac n2+\frac14\right)-\psi\!\left(\frac n2+\frac34\right)\right)$$
and also
$$I(q)= i\Bigl(\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(a,-i)-\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(a,i)-\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(-a,i)+\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(-a,-i)\Bigr),$$
where
$$\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(x,y):=\sum_{m>n\ge1}\frac{x^m y^n}{m^2 n}.$$
Moreover,
$$qI'(q)=\operatorname{Ti}_2(\lambda)-G-\frac{\pi}{4}\log\lambda, \qquad I(q)=4\int_{\lambda}^{1}\frac{\operatorname{Ti}_2(x)-G-\frac{\pi}{4}\log x}{1-x^2}\,dx,$$
where
$$\operatorname{Ti}_2(x):=\int_0^x \frac{\arctan u}{u}\,du=\sum_{m=0}^\infty \frac{(-1)^m x^{2m+1}}{(2m+1)^2}, \qquad G=\operatorname{Ti}_2(1).$$
All identities extend by principal-branch analytic continuation.

To prove these

We first assume
$|q|<1$
and
$\Re a>0$
. After that the final formulas extend by analytic continuation.

We can observe from the power series
$$\operatorname{Li}_2(z)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{z^n}{n^2}$$
that
$$\operatorname{Li}_2(q)-\operatorname{Li}_2(-qt^2)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{q^n\bigl(1-(-1)^n t^{2n}\bigr)}{n^2}.$$
Also,
$$\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{|q|^n(1+t^{2n})}{n^2}\le 2\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{|q|^n}{n^2}$$
for
$0\le t\le 1$
. Hence the Weierstrass
$M$
-test allows termwise integration, and therefore
$$I(q)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{q^n}{n^2}\int_0^1 \frac{1-(-1)^n t^{2n}}{1+t^2}\,dt.$$
We can see that
$$\frac{1-(-1)^n t^{2n}}{1+t^2}=\frac{1-(-t^2)^n}{1-(-t^2)}=\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}(-1)^k t^{2k}.$$
Hence
$$I(q)=\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{q^n}{n^2}\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{(-1)^k}{2k+1}.$$
This is the first closed form.

For the second closed form, we write the finite sum in terms of the digamma function. We can observe that
$$\sum_{k=0}^{2m-1}\frac{(-1)^k}{2k+1}=\sum_{j=0}^{m-1}\left(\frac{1}{4j+1}-\frac{1}{4j+3}\right)$$
and
$$\sum_{k=0}^{2m}\frac{(-1)^k}{2k+1}=\sum_{j=0}^{m}\frac{1}{4j+1}-\sum_{j=0}^{m-1}\frac{1}{4j+3}.$$
After that we use
$$\psi(z+N)-\psi(z)=\sum_{j=0}^{N-1}\frac{1}{z+j} \qquad\text{and}\qquad \psi(1-z)-\psi(z)=\pi\cot(\pi z).$$
Hence
$$\sum_{k=0}^{2m-1}\frac{(-1)^k}{2k+1}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac14\left(\psi\!\left(m+\frac14\right)-\psi\!\left(m+\frac34\right)\right)$$
and
$$\sum_{k=0}^{2m}\frac{(-1)^k}{2k+1}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac14\left(\psi\!\left(m+\frac54\right)-\psi\!\left(m+\frac34\right)\right).$$
Therefore the two cases combine into
$$\sum_{k=0}^{n-1}\frac{(-1)^k}{2k+1}=\frac{\pi}{4}+\frac{(-1)^n}{4}\left(\psi\!\left(\frac n2+\frac14\right)-\psi\!\left(\frac n2+\frac34\right)\right).$$
Hence substitution gives
$$I(q)=\frac{\pi}{4}\operatorname{Li}_2(q)+\frac14\sum_{n=1}^\infty \frac{(-1)^n q^n}{n^2}\left(\psi\!\left(\frac n2+\frac14\right)-\psi\!\left(\frac n2+\frac34\right)\right).$$

For the third closed form, we start from the definition
$$\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(x,y)=\sum_{m>n\ge1}\frac{x^m y^n}{m^2 n}.$$
Hence
$$i\Bigl(\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(a,-i)-\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(a,i)-\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(-a,i)+\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(-a,-i)\Bigr)=i\sum_{m>n\ge1}\frac{(a^m+(-a)^m)\bigl((-i)^n-i^n\bigr)}{m^2 n}.$$
We can observe that only even
$m$
and odd
$n$
remain. Therefore we write
$m=2r$
and
$n=2k+1$
. After that the condition
$m>n$
becomes
$2r>2k+1$
, so we can see that
$0\le k\le r-1$
. Therefore
$$i(\cdots)=\sum_{r=1}^\infty \frac{a^{2r}}{r^2}\sum_{k=0}^{r-1}\frac{(-1)^k}{2k+1}.$$
Since
$a^2=q$
, this is exactly the first series for
$I(q)$
. Hence
$$I(q)= i\Bigl(\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(a,-i)-\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(a,i)-\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(-a,i)+\operatorname{Li}_{2,1}(-a,-i)\Bigr).$$

We now derive the differential form. We can differentiate under the integral sign by dominated convergence. Since
$$\operatorname{Li}_2'(z)=-\frac{\log(1-z)}{z},$$
we get
$$I'(q)=\frac1q\left(J(q)-\frac{\pi}{4}\log(1-q)\right), \qquad J(q):=\int_0^1 \frac{\log(1+qt^2)}{1+t^2}\,dt.$$
We now put
$t=\tan\theta$
. After that we put
$\phi=2\theta$
. Hence
$$J(q)=\frac12\int_0^{\pi/2}\log\!\left(\frac{1+a^2+(1-a^2)\cos\phi}{1+\cos\phi}\right)\,d\phi.$$
We can see that
$$1+a^2+(1-a^2)\cos\phi=\frac{(1+a)^2}{2}\,\bigl|1+\lambda e^{i\phi}\bigr|^2$$
and
$$1+\cos\phi=\frac12\,\bigl|1+e^{i\phi}\bigr|^2.$$
Therefore
$$J(q)=\frac{\pi}{2}\log(1+a)+\int_0^{\pi/2}\log|1+\lambda e^{i\phi}|\,d\phi-\int_0^{\pi/2}\log|1+e^{i\phi}|\,d\phi.$$
Now let
$|\rho|<1$
. We can use the logarithmic Fourier series
$$\log(1+\rho e^{i\phi})=\sum_{n=1}^\infty (-1)^{n+1}\frac{\rho^n e^{in\phi}}{n}.$$
Taking real parts and integrating term by term, we get
$$\int_0^{\pi/2}\log|1+\rho e^{i\phi}|\,d\phi=\sum_{n=1}^\infty (-1)^{n+1}\frac{\rho^n\sin(n\pi/2)}{n^2}=\operatorname{Ti}_2(\rho).$$
Therefore, with
$\rho=\lambda$
and then
$\rho\to1^-$
, we obtain
$$J(q)=\frac{\pi}{2}\log(1+a)+\operatorname{Ti}_2(\lambda)-G.$$
Also,
$$1-q=(1-a)(1+a)=\lambda(1+a)^2.$$
Hence
$$qI'(q)=\operatorname{Ti}_2(\lambda)-G-\frac{\pi}{4}\log\lambda.$$

Finally, we can observe that
$I(0)=0$
and
$$q=\left(\frac{1-\lambda}{1+\lambda}\right)^2, \qquad \frac{dq}{q}=-\frac{4\,d\lambda}{1-\lambda^2}.$$
Therefore
$$I(q)=\int_0^q I'(u)\,du=4\int_{\lambda}^{1}\frac{\operatorname{Ti}_2(x)-G-\frac{\pi}{4}\log x}{1-x^2}\,dx.$$
Hence all the stated closed forms follow.

question:
We need to evaluate,

$$I = \int_{0}^{1} \frac{\text{Li}_2(q) - \text{Li}_2(-qt^2)}{1 + t^2} dt $$

This integral came up in solving this
question

I am not able to find a closed form for this integral. Any help would be much appreciated.
answer:
This is not an answer.

This integral resisted to all CAS I was able to use.

You could at least obtain a decent approximation using the
$[2n,2n]$
Padé approximant
$P_n$
of
$$\text{Li}_2\left(-q \,t^2\right)$$
such as
$$P_1=-\frac{4 q t^2}{q t^2+4}$$
which would give for the integral
$$I \sim \frac{\pi  }{4}\text{Li}_2(q)+\frac{\pi  q-8 \sqrt{q}\, \cot
^{-1}\left(\frac{2}{\sqrt{q}}\right)}{q-4}$$
A bit more complicated but doable would be
$$P_2=-\frac{9 q t^2 \left(13 q t^2+28\right)}{17 q^2 t^4+180 q t^2+252}$$
which gives an ugly (but workable) formula.

Just a few numbers
$$\left(
\begin{array}{cccc}
q & \text{using }P_1& \text{using }P_2 & \text{using }P_3& \text{numerical integration} \\
-1.0 & -0.89654 & -0.90692 & -0.90851  & -0.90918 \\

+0.2 &  +0.20749 & +0.20752 & +0.20752& +0.20752 \\
+0.4 &  +0.43426 &  +0.43445 & +0.43445 & +0.43445 \\
+0.6 &  +0.69051 &  +0.69108 &+0.69109 & +0.69109 \\
+0.8 &  +0.99909 &  +1.00030 &+1.00032  &+1.00032 \\
+1.0 &  +1.48112 &  +1.48326 &+1.48330  & +1.48330 \\
\end{array}
\right)$$

question:
Being challenged by the
Round 2 Problem 4

$$
I=\int \frac{d x}{x^4+5 x^3+10 x^2+9 x+3}
$$

I need to resolve the integrand into partial fractions.
Let
$f(x)$
be the denominator, then
$f(-1)=f’(-1)=0$
implies that
$f(x)$
is divisible by
$(x+1)^2$
and hence
$f(x)=(x+1)^2(x^2+3x+3)$
. Let

$$ \frac{1}{x^4+5 x^3+10 x^2+9 x+3}\equiv\frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B}{(x+1)^2}+\frac{P(x)}{x^2+3 x+3}
$$

for some constants
$A,B $
and linear function
$P(x)$
.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 1\equiv A(x+1)\left(x^2+3 x+3\right)+B\left(x^2+3 x+3\right)+(x+1)^2 P(x)
\end{aligned}\tag*{(*)}
$$


Putting
$x=-1$
yields
$B=1.$


Differentiating both sides w.r.t.
$x$
at
$x=-1$
yields
$A=-1$
.


Rearranging
$(*)$
yields

$$
\begin{gathered}
(x+1)^2 f(x)=1+(x+1)\left(x^2+3x+3\right)-( x^2+3 x+3) \Rightarrow  P(x)=x+1
\end{gathered}
$$


$$
\begin{aligned}
I & =\int\left[\frac{x+1}{x^2+3 x+3}-\frac{1}{x+1}+\frac{1}{(x+1)^2}\right] d x \\
& =\int \frac{\frac{1}{2}(2 x+3)-\frac{1}{2}}{x^2+3 x+3} d x-\ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{x+1} \\
& =\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(x^2+3 x+3\right)-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^2+\frac{3}{4}} -\ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{x+1} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \ln \left(x^2+3 x+3\right)-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{3}}\right) -\ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{x+1}+C
\end{aligned}
$$

Are there any other methods for the MIT Integration Bee 2026 Semifinal round 2 Problem 4?
answer:
To avoid partial fractions, substitute
$x=\frac{1-t}{1+t}$
to simplify the integrand

$$
\int \frac{1}{x^4+5 x^3+10 x^2+9 x+3}dx=-\frac12\int \frac{(t+1)^2}{t^2+4t+7}dt
$$

question:
Being challenged by the
Round 2 Problem 4

$$
I=\int \frac{d x}{x^4+5 x^3+10 x^2+9 x+3}
$$

I need to resolve the integrand into partial fractions.
Let
$f(x)$
be the denominator, then
$f(-1)=f’(-1)=0$
implies that
$f(x)$
is divisible by
$(x+1)^2$
and hence
$f(x)=(x+1)^2(x^2+3x+3)$
. Let

$$ \frac{1}{x^4+5 x^3+10 x^2+9 x+3}\equiv\frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B}{(x+1)^2}+\frac{P(x)}{x^2+3 x+3}
$$

for some constants
$A,B $
and linear function
$P(x)$
.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 1\equiv A(x+1)\left(x^2+3 x+3\right)+B\left(x^2+3 x+3\right)+(x+1)^2 P(x)
\end{aligned}\tag*{(*)}
$$


Putting
$x=-1$
yields
$B=1.$


Differentiating both sides w.r.t.
$x$
at
$x=-1$
yields
$A=-1$
.


Rearranging
$(*)$
yields

$$
\begin{gathered}
(x+1)^2 f(x)=1+(x+1)\left(x^2+3x+3\right)-( x^2+3 x+3) \Rightarrow  P(x)=x+1
\end{gathered}
$$


$$
\begin{aligned}
I & =\int\left[\frac{x+1}{x^2+3 x+3}-\frac{1}{x+1}+\frac{1}{(x+1)^2}\right] d x \\
& =\int \frac{\frac{1}{2}(2 x+3)-\frac{1}{2}}{x^2+3 x+3} d x-\ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{x+1} \\
& =\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(x^2+3 x+3\right)-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^2+\frac{3}{4}} -\ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{x+1} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \ln \left(x^2+3 x+3\right)-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{3}}\right) -\ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{x+1}+C
\end{aligned}
$$

Are there any other methods for the MIT Integration Bee 2026 Semifinal round 2 Problem 4?
answer:
$$ I=\int \frac{d x}{x^4+5 x^3+10 x^2+9 x+3} $$

$$ = \int \frac{dx}{(x + 1)^2(x^2 + 3x + 3)} $$

Let

$$ y = x + 1, \quad dx = dy. $$

Then

$$ x^2 + 3x + 3 = (y - 1)^2 + 3(y - 1) + 3 = y^2 + y + 1, $$

hence

$$ I = \int \frac{dy}{y^2(y^2 + y + 1)}. $$

Let,

$$ u = \frac{1}{y}, \quad dy = -\frac{du}{u^2}. $$

$$ y^2(y^2 + y + 1) = \frac{1}{u^2}\left(\frac{1}{u^2} + \frac{1}{u} + 1\right) = \frac{u^2 + u + 1}{u^4}, $$

The integral is,

$$ I = -\int \frac{u^2}{u^2 + u + 1} \, du. $$

$$ I = -\int du + \int \frac{u + 1}{u^2 + u + 1} \, du. $$

For the second term, write

$$ u + 1 = \frac{1}{2}(2u + 1) + \frac{1}{2}, $$

hence

$$ \int \frac{u + 1}{u^2 + u + 1} \, du = \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{2u + 1}{u^2 + u + 1} \, du + \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{du}{u^2 + u + 1}. $$

Therefore

$$ = \frac{1}{2} \ln(u^2 + u + 1) + \frac{1}{2} \int \frac{du}{(u + \frac{1}{2})^2 + \frac{3}{4}}. $$

So

$$ \int \frac{du}{u^2 + u + 1} = \frac{2}{\sqrt{3}} \arctan\left(\frac{2u + 1}{\sqrt{3}}\right). $$

Thus

$$ I = -u + \frac{1}{2}\ln(u^2 + u + 1) + \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \arctan\left(\frac{2u + 1}{\sqrt{3}}\right) + C. $$

Now substitute back
$u = \frac{1}{x+1}$
:

$$ I = -\frac{1}{x + 1} + \frac{1}{2} \ln\left(\frac{x^2 + 3x + 3}{(x + 1)^2}\right) + \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \arctan\left(\frac{x + 3}{\sqrt{3}(x + 1)}\right) + C $$

question:
Being challenged by the
Round 2 Problem 4

$$
I=\int \frac{d x}{x^4+5 x^3+10 x^2+9 x+3}
$$

I need to resolve the integrand into partial fractions.
Let
$f(x)$
be the denominator, then
$f(-1)=f’(-1)=0$
implies that
$f(x)$
is divisible by
$(x+1)^2$
and hence
$f(x)=(x+1)^2(x^2+3x+3)$
. Let

$$ \frac{1}{x^4+5 x^3+10 x^2+9 x+3}\equiv\frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B}{(x+1)^2}+\frac{P(x)}{x^2+3 x+3}
$$

for some constants
$A,B $
and linear function
$P(x)$
.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 1\equiv A(x+1)\left(x^2+3 x+3\right)+B\left(x^2+3 x+3\right)+(x+1)^2 P(x)
\end{aligned}\tag*{(*)}
$$


Putting
$x=-1$
yields
$B=1.$


Differentiating both sides w.r.t.
$x$
at
$x=-1$
yields
$A=-1$
.


Rearranging
$(*)$
yields

$$
\begin{gathered}
(x+1)^2 f(x)=1+(x+1)\left(x^2+3x+3\right)-( x^2+3 x+3) \Rightarrow  P(x)=x+1
\end{gathered}
$$


$$
\begin{aligned}
I & =\int\left[\frac{x+1}{x^2+3 x+3}-\frac{1}{x+1}+\frac{1}{(x+1)^2}\right] d x \\
& =\int \frac{\frac{1}{2}(2 x+3)-\frac{1}{2}}{x^2+3 x+3} d x-\ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{x+1} \\
& =\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(x^2+3 x+3\right)-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^2+\frac{3}{4}} -\ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{x+1} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \ln \left(x^2+3 x+3\right)-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{3}}\right) -\ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{x+1}+C
\end{aligned}
$$

Are there any other methods for the MIT Integration Bee 2026 Semifinal round 2 Problem 4?
answer:
First, factorize the polynomial in the denominator:
$$x^4+5x^3+10x^2+9x+3 = (x+1)^2(x^2+3x+3)$$
Thus, the integral can be rewritten as:
$$I = \int \frac{\mathrm{d}x}{(x+1)^2(x^2+3x+3)}$$

Observe the following algebraic identity:
$$4(x^2+3x+3) = (x+3)^2 + 3(x+1)^2$$
Dividing both sides by
$(x+1)^2$
yields:
$$\frac{4(x^2+3x+3)}{(x+1)^2} = \left(\frac{x+3}{x+1}\right)^2 + 3$$

Introduce variables
$p$
and
$q$
as functions of
$x$
:
$$
\begin{cases}
p = \frac{x+3}{x+1} \\
q = \frac{2\sqrt{x^2+3x+3}}{|x+1|}
\end{cases}
$$
It follows immediately that:
$$q^2 - p^2 = 3 \implies q\,\mathrm{d}q = p\,\mathrm{d}p$$

Differentiating
$p$
with respect to
$x$
:
$$p = 1 + \frac{2}{x+1} \implies \mathrm{d}p = -\frac{2}{(x+1)^2} \, \mathrm{d}x \implies \frac{\mathrm{d}x}{(x+1)^2} = -\frac{1}{2} \, \mathrm{d}p$$
Additionally, from
$p - 1 = \frac{2}{x+1}$
, squaring both sides gives:
$$\frac{4}{(x+1)^2} = (p-1)^2$$

Now, transform the integral
$I$
entirely into the
$(p, q)$
domain:
$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= \int \frac{1}{\frac{4(x^2+3x+3)}{(x+1)^2}} \cdot \frac{4}{(x+1)^2} \cdot \frac{\mathrm{d}x}{(x+1)^2} \\
&= \int \frac{1}{q^2} \cdot (p-1)^2 \cdot \left(-\frac{1}{2}\right) \mathrm{d}p \\
&= -\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{p^2 - 2p + 1}{q^2} \, \mathrm{d}p
\end{aligned}
$$

Utilizing the relation
$p^2 = q^2 - 3$
, expand the numerator:
$$
\begin{aligned}
I &= -\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{(q^2 - 3) - 2p + 1}{q^2} \, \mathrm{d}p \\
&= -\frac{1}{2} \int \left( 1 - \frac{2p}{q^2} - \frac{2}{q^2} \right) \mathrm{d}p \\
&= -\frac{1}{2} \int \mathrm{d}p + \int \frac{p}{q^2} \, \mathrm{d}p + \int \frac{1}{p^2+3} \, \mathrm{d}p
\end{aligned}
$$

Evaluate each term independently. For the second term, apply
$p\,\mathrm{d}p = q\,\mathrm{d}q$
:

$\int \mathrm{d}p = p$
$\int \frac{p\,\mathrm{d}p}{q^2} = \int \frac{q\,\mathrm{d}q}{q^2} = \int \frac{\mathrm{d}q}{q} = \ln|q|$
$\int \frac{\mathrm{d}p}{p^2+3} = \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\arctan\left(\frac{p}{\sqrt{3}}\right)$

Combining the evaluated terms:
$$I = -\frac{1}{2}p + \ln|q| + \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}}\arctan\left(\frac{p}{\sqrt{3}}\right) + C$$

Finally, substitute
$p$
and
$q$
back in terms of
$x$
. Absorbing the constant
$\ln 2$
into the arbitrary constant
$C$
, the result is:
$$I = -\frac{x+3}{2(x+1)} + \frac{1}{2}\ln\left( \frac{x^2+3x+3}{(x+1)^2} \right) + \frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \arctan\left(\frac{x+3}{\sqrt{3}(x+1)}\right) + C$$

question:
Being challenged by the
Round 2 Problem 4

$$
I=\int \frac{d x}{x^4+5 x^3+10 x^2+9 x+3}
$$

I need to resolve the integrand into partial fractions.
Let
$f(x)$
be the denominator, then
$f(-1)=f’(-1)=0$
implies that
$f(x)$
is divisible by
$(x+1)^2$
and hence
$f(x)=(x+1)^2(x^2+3x+3)$
. Let

$$ \frac{1}{x^4+5 x^3+10 x^2+9 x+3}\equiv\frac{A}{x+1}+\frac{B}{(x+1)^2}+\frac{P(x)}{x^2+3 x+3}
$$

for some constants
$A,B $
and linear function
$P(x)$
.

$$
\begin{aligned}
& 1\equiv A(x+1)\left(x^2+3 x+3\right)+B\left(x^2+3 x+3\right)+(x+1)^2 P(x)
\end{aligned}\tag*{(*)}
$$


Putting
$x=-1$
yields
$B=1.$


Differentiating both sides w.r.t.
$x$
at
$x=-1$
yields
$A=-1$
.


Rearranging
$(*)$
yields

$$
\begin{gathered}
(x+1)^2 f(x)=1+(x+1)\left(x^2+3x+3\right)-( x^2+3 x+3) \Rightarrow  P(x)=x+1
\end{gathered}
$$


$$
\begin{aligned}
I & =\int\left[\frac{x+1}{x^2+3 x+3}-\frac{1}{x+1}+\frac{1}{(x+1)^2}\right] d x \\
& =\int \frac{\frac{1}{2}(2 x+3)-\frac{1}{2}}{x^2+3 x+3} d x-\ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{x+1} \\
& =\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(x^2+3 x+3\right)-\frac{1}{2} \int \frac{d x}{\left(x+\frac{3}{2}\right)^2+\frac{3}{4}} -\ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{x+1} \\
&= \frac{1}{2} \ln \left(x^2+3 x+3\right)-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{3}}\right) -\ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{x+1}+C
\end{aligned}
$$

Are there any other methods for the MIT Integration Bee 2026 Semifinal round 2 Problem 4?
answer:
As wisely suggested by @Shreyash Pachore, let
$y=x+1$
, then
$$
\begin{aligned}
I & =\int \frac{d y}{y^2\left(y^2+y+1\right)} \\
& =\int\left(\frac{y}{y^2+y+1}-\frac{y-1}{y^ 2}\right) d y \\
& =\int\left(\frac{\frac{1}{2}(2 y+1)-\frac{1}{2}}{y^2+y+1} d y-\frac{1}{y}+\frac{1}{y^2}\right) d y \\
& =\frac{1}{2} \ln \int \frac{d\left(y^2+y+1\right)}{y^2+y+1}-\int \frac{2 d y}{(2 y+1)^2+3}-\ln |y|-\frac{1}{y} \\
& =\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(y^2+y+1\right)-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1}\left(\frac{2 y+1}{\sqrt{3}}\right)-\ln |y|-\frac{1}{y}+C \\
& =\frac{1}{2} \ln \left(x^2+3 x+3\right)-\frac{1}{\sqrt{3}} \tan ^{-1} \frac{2 x+3}{\sqrt{3}}-\ln |x+1|-\frac{1}{x+1}+C
\end{aligned}
$$

question:
As shown, on pg 259 of Ramanujan's 2nd notebook he states a few identities:

I am easily able to decipher the second half, which just consists of some class equations for the theory of alternative bases for
$q_3$
.

However, other than the first, which is seemingly just a trig identity, I can't seem to figure out what the first half is supposed to mean since he never defines the variables. My only guess is that they have something to do with modular equations, since this part of his notebook is mostly dedicated to them.

Could someone explain what these are supposed to mean, and maybe prove the identities on this page?

(Also, I didn't really know what category to put this in, so feel free to change it.)
answer:
I can give you the general writing on what's written on the page for better understanding.

$$ PQ^2 = 2 BP \cdot QC $$

$$ (a + b - \sqrt{a^2 + b^2})^2 = 2(\sqrt{a^2 + b^2} - a)(\sqrt{a^2 + b^2} - b) $$

$$ \{ \sqrt[3]{(a+b)^2} - \sqrt[3]{a^2 - ab + b^2} \}^3 = 3(\sqrt[3]{a^3 + b^3} - a)(\sqrt[3]{a^3 + b^3} - b) $$

$$ \sqrt{A + B \sqrt[3]{p}} = \sqrt{\frac{B}{p + k^3}} \left( \frac{k^2}{2} + k \sqrt[3]{p} - \sqrt[3]{p^2} \right) $$

where
$Bk^4 - 4Ak^3 - 8Bkp - 4Ap = 0$
.

$F \frac{1 - \sqrt{1 - t^{24}}}{2} = e^{-\pi \sqrt{29}}$
. then

$$ t^{24} + 9t^{20} + 5t^{16} - 2t^{12} - 5t^8 + 9t^4 - 1 = 0 $$

$$ \frac{t^6 + t^2}{1 - t^4} = \sqrt{\frac{\sqrt{29} - 5}{2}} $$

$$ \frac{t^3 + t \sqrt{\sqrt{29} - 2}}{1 + t^2 \sqrt{\sqrt{29} + 2}} = \sqrt[4]{\frac{\sqrt{29} - 5}{2}} $$

if
$\sqrt[4]{1 - t^8} = t(1 + u^2)$
, then
$u^3 + u = \sqrt{2}$
.

$F \cdot \frac{1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{64} t^{24}}}{2} = e^{-\pi \sqrt{79}}$
. then

$$ t^5 - t^4 + t^3 - 2t^2 + 3t - 1 = 0 $$

$F \cdot \frac{1 - \sqrt{1 - \frac{1}{64} t^{24}}}{2} = e^{-\pi \sqrt{47}}$
, then

$$ t^5 + 2t^4 + 2t^3 + t^2 - 1 = 0 $$

Edit

The substitution is a reverse-engineered parametrization of the relation

$$PQ^2 = 2 BP \cdot QC.$$

Let

$$s = \sqrt{a^2 + b^2}.$$

Then the proposed choice is

$$BP = s - a, \quad QC = s - b, \quad PQ = a + b - s.$$

Now check what happens:

$$PQ^2 = (a + b - s)^2$$

and

$$2 BP \cdot QC = 2(s - a)(s - b).$$

Expanding both sides gives

$$(a + b - s)^2 = a^2 + b^2 + 2ab + s^2 - 2s(a + b),$$
$$2(s - a)(s - b) = 2(s^2 - s(a + b) + ab).$$

These are equal exactly because
$s^2 = a^2 + b^2$
. So the identity is built in.

The idea is to write the three lengths from the same quantity
$s$
:

$$s - a, \quad s - b, \quad a + b - s.$$

That is a symmetric way to make a quadratic relation like

$$x^2 = 2yz$$

question:
The most common formula for generating Pythagorean triples is Euclid's, shown here as

$$A=m^2-k^2\qquad B=2mk\qquad C=m^2+k^2$$


It generates all primitive triples. It generates trivial triples, e.g.

$F(1,1)=(0,2,2)$
. It generates imprimitives, e.g.
$(6,8,10)$
that are doubles, odd square multiples, even square multiples, or double of square multiples, and infinitely many of primitives or type imprimitives. Assuming infinities of the same size, we might think that with
$1$
type of primitive,
$1$
type of trivial, and
$5$
types of imprimitives, that primitives make up only
$\dfrac{1}{7}$
of all triples.


Not knowing any formula for generating triples, I created this one in 2009:


\begin{equation*}
F(n,k)=
\begin{cases}
A=(2n-1)^2+2(2n-1)k\\
B =\phantom{(2n-1)^2+{}} 2(2n-1)k+2k^2\\
C=(2n-1)^2+2(2n-1)k+2k^2
\end{cases}
\end{equation*}


and discovered later that it is the same as replacing
$m$
in Euclid's formula with
$2n-1+k$
:


$$A=(2n-1+k)^2-k^2\qquad B=2(2n-1+k)k\qquad C=(2n-1+k)^2+k^2$$


This formula generates no trivial triples and the only imprimitives are odd square multiples of primitives. Again, assuming infinitives of equal size, this suggests that only 50% of those generated are imprimitives.


Still, dealing with infinities is a tricky business so my question is, can we calculate the ratios of primitives to imprimitives for either of these formulas?


Take the case of the second formula where the only imprimitives are odd square multiples of primitives. It obvously generates fewer imprimitives than Euclid's formula but there are still an infinite number of them for every Pythagorean triple, i.e.

$$S_1=\{(27,36,45),\, (75,100,125),\, (147,196,245),\,\cdots\}$$


I believe these infinities are all countable
$\,(\aleph_0)\,$
so none is larger than any other. This means there are as many primitives as there are imprimitives but there is no way to express this such as
$50\%$
.
answer:
Perhaps surprisingly, in the limit, 0% of Pythagorean triples are primitive!

Let
$R(x)$
be the number of primitive Pythagorean triples with the hypotenuse
$C\le x$
. A variation of the method of
this answer
shows that
$R(x) \sim \frac1\pi x$
as
$x\to\infty$
.

Let
$S(x)$
be the number of all Pythagorean triples with
$C\le x$
. Note that if
$(A,B,C)$
is a primitive Pythagorean triple, then
$d(A,B,C)$
is a Pythagorean triple for any positive integer
$d$
. Therefore
$S(x)$
disjointly counts, for any positive integer
$N$
:

all primitive
$(A,B,C)$
with
$C\le x$
;
$2(A,B,C)$
for all primitive
$(A,B,C)$
with
$C\le \frac12x$
;
$3(A,B,C)$
for all primitive
$(A,B,C)$
with
$C\le \frac13x$
;
and so on up to
$N(A,B,C)$
for all primitive
$(A,B,C)$
with
$C\le \frac1Nx$
.

These counts (which form a lower bound for
$S(x)$
) total
$\sim \frac1\pi\bigl( x+\frac12x+\frac13x+\cdots+\frac1Nx \bigr) \sim H_N R(x)$
when
$x$
is large compared to
$N$
. And since
$H_N$
can be made arbitrarily large, we conclude that
$R(x)/S(x) \to 0$
as
$x\to\infty$
.

question:
I saw this game on YouTube Shorts, thought I am good enough to solve it and it is already two days and I am giving up.

Two players each secretly choose a number from (1) to (K).
Then they take turns guessing the other person’s number. (This is different from Price is right because both players have different hidden number)

If you guess correctly, you win immediately.

If you guess wrong, the other person truthfully says
higher
or
lower
.

Then it becomes the other player’s turn.

So both players are basically trying to “binary search” each other’s hidden number, but only one guess per turn. There is benefit to doing non binary search when you are obviously losing to hope for a faster win if you get lucky.

I tried to solve even the (K=3) case, it got surprisingly complicated. My incorrect solution lost to binary search bot in simulations. (100K equal number of starter and responder roles).

What I want to know is:

What is the
Nash equilibrium
of this game?

For small cases like (K=3), what is the exact optimal hiding / guessing strategy?

Does the optimal hiding distribution depend on whether you go
first or second
?

Is there any known general solution for arbitrary (K)?
answer:
There won't be a Nash equilibrium for the numbers they choose. If there was, then they could calculate what their opponent's optimal numbers are, and win instantly. The second player does better with a mixed strategy - literally any mixed strategy gives them a nonzero probability of having a chance to guess. Likewise, the first player needs a mixed strategy if they want to survive player 2's first guess.

For
$K=2$
, there's no difference between choosing
$1$
or
$2$
, the optimal strategy for both players will be to choose their number randomly (with both choices equally likely), and then to guess randomly.

For
$K=3$
, binary search bot is not playing optimally. You know the bot will always guess
$2$
first, so you pick
$1$
or
$3$
with equal probability and guarantee the bot needs two guesses. But a smarter bot will know you've done this, and choose
$1$
or
$3$
randomly, and get your number in (on average) 1.5 guesses. So the optimal choice of secret number follows a mixed strategy, but so does the optimal choice of first guess.

And this is not a simple game theory game, since players gain information as the game progresses. But we can try:

We could model a player's strategy (for
$K=3$
) as

deciding the probabilities of choosing
$1$
,
$2$
or
$3$
as the secret number:
for player 1: deciding the probability of guessing
$1$
,
$2$
or
$3$
for player 2: deciding the probabilities of guessing
$1$
,
$2$
or
$3$
, which will depend on what player 1 has chosen and (for larger
$K$
at least) what their secret number actually is
for player 1: deciding probabilities for their second guess - though this will be a guaranteed win or a choice between two numbers, so it's probably solveable
for player 2: deciding the probabilities for their second guess, which will also be solveable.
For large K, the player needs a separate set of probabilities. Note that deterministic strategies can be countered: the player isn't just trying to guess in as few turns as possible, they are doing so in the knowledge that the opponent has taken their plans into account, so they need a strategy with some randomness thrown in which will have them playing suboptimally sometimes at almost every stage.

Algebraically, this becomes a right proper mess. I attempted to do work through the details for
$K=3$
, ended up with 5th degree polynomials to solve, and then in the end realised I must have made a mistake when some probabilities came out negative.

Algorithmically, it might be feasible though: model the strategy as a list of real numbers (representing probabilities). For a given strategy (and counterstrategy), you can find the player's chance of winning: at any stage, a certain player is choosing what to guess. You can recursively evaluate their chance of winning, because each possible guess simplifies the problem for the next recursive steps. Then you can optimise somehow to find the best list of numbers.

Improve this question

Given a strongly-connected directed graph
$G = (V, E)$
, find a smallest-size subset of edges
$S \subset E$
such that
$G' = (V, E - S) $
is no longer strongly connected. Note, it can still have cycles.

It seems a pretty basic problem, but I could not find any references to it. Thanks for any information!
answer:
If you fix two vertices
$s,t \in V$
such that
$G'$
is specifically required to have no directed
$s-t$
path, then you are looking for a smallest
$s-t$
edge cut, which can be solved by standard network flow techniques.

To solve the original problem, it's enough to repeat this for every ordered pair
$(s,t)$
where
$s\ne t$
.

(As user14111 points out in the comments, just
$|V|$
repetitions suffice. If
$V = \{s_1, s_2, \dots, s_n\}$
, just look for an
$s_i - s_{i+1}$
edge cut for each
$i=1,\dots,n$
(taking
$s_{n+1} = s_1$
) and pick the best one you find. To see that this works, suppose for contradiction that the desired solution
$S$
is
not
an
$s_i - s_{i+1}$
edge cut for any
$i$
. Then
$G'$
contains a directed
$s_i - s_{i+1}$
path for all
$i$
, and concatenating all these paths gives us a walk through all
$n$
vertices. But that makes
$G'$
strongly connected, which is the contradiction.)

Improve this question

I'm trying to self-learn how to write proofs in a more standard textbook way, though I know there is no one way to write a proof. I was using an abbreviated form of the rules of predicate calculus to show that given statements are equivalent, a somewhat slow and tedious process. But when I asked AI (Chat GPT) to prove something in order to see if it did it in a similar way that I did, it was completely different.

Is AI's proof a good way to prove the statement as opposed to mine?

AI's proof:

To prove that
$X \cup Y \subseteq Z$
given
$X \cup Z $
and
$ Y \subseteq Z$
, you use the element-based approach.

The Proof

1.Pick an arbitrary element:Let a be any element such that
$a \in (X \cup Y)$

Apply the definition of union: by definition, if a
$\in (X \cup Y)$
, then
$a \in X$
or
$a \in Y$

Analyze Case 1
$(a \in X)$
: Since we are given that
$(X \subseteq Z)$
, every element in X must also be in Z. Therefore,
$a \in Z$
.

Analyze Case 2
$(a \in Y)$
: Since we are given that
$(Y \subseteq Z)$
, every element in Y must also be in Z. Therefore,
$a \in Z$
.

Conclusion: In either case, a is an element of Z. Since every element of
$(X \cup Y)$
is also an element of Z, it follows that
$(X \cup Y) \subseteq Z$
.

My proof:

Prove:
$[(X \subseteq Z) \land (Y \subseteq Z)] \Rightarrow (X \cup Y \subseteq Z)$

We are trying to prove that if we assume
$(X \subseteq Z) \land (Y \subseteq Z)$
we obtain
$X \cup Y \subseteq Z$
.

The former statement is equivalent to
$(a \in X \Rightarrow a \in X) \land (a \in Y \Rightarrow a \in Z)$
by the definition of subsets, this becomes
$(a \in Z\lor \lnot a \in X) \land (a \in Z \lor \lnot a \in Y)$
which then becomes
$a \in Z \lor (\lnot a \in X \land  \lnot a \in Y)$
, this then is
$\lnot (a \in X \lor a \in Y) \lor a \in Z$
and finnaly this becomes
$(a \in X \lor a \in Y) \Rightarrow a \in Z$
, which is equivalent to
$X \cup Y \subseteq Z$
by the definition of unions and subsets.

So now we have
$[(X \subseteq Z) \land (Y \subseteq Z)] \Rightarrow (X \cup Y \subseteq Z)$
answer:
This question will probably be closed because it does not have a mathematical answer (even after the nonsense parts are cleaned up) but I will venture an answer as if the question were "what makes a good proof?", hoping to save you and your instructors grief.

A mathematical proof should communicate meaning between the writer and the reader. To that end, words are better than formal symbolic logical statements. Given that, I consider the first proof in your question better than the second. once you correct the fact that your AI garbled what it was trying to prove. Yours is one long run-on sentence.

There is no such thing as "the standard text-book way of writing" a proof. The best proofs (in books, articles and homework) are the ones that are easy to understand.

question:
Is there is a way to go from

$$(xy + xz)(y - z) + (xy + yz)(z - x) + (xz + yz)(x - y)  $$

to


$$(z - x)(y - z)(x - y)?$$

I am referring here to a well known Mathematics theorem or rule, e.g. Distributive property of multiplication over addition.

Allowing me to justify the final factored form, because just staring at the expression without knowing anything else is hard to come up (I mean, one can try by brute force for several hours) with the idea of multiplying the differences.

Thanks!
answer:
Suppose
$y=z$
. Then the first term vanishes, and the rest of it becomes
$(xz+z^2)(z-x)+(xz+z^2)(x-z)$
, which is obviously zero. So,
$y-z$
must be a factor.

By similar arguments (or by symmetry),
$z-x$
and
$x-y$
must be factors. So
$(y-z)(z-x)(x-y)$
must be a factor.

Now, that's a polynomial of degree three, and so is the one you're trying to factor, so there can't be any more nonconstant factors. It only remains to check for constant factors, which you can do by, say, substituting
$x=1$
,
$y=0$
,
$z=-1$
.

question:

$$(xy + xz)(y - z) + (xy + yz)(z - x) + (xz + yz)(x - y)  $$

to


$$(z - x)(y - z)(x - y)?$$

I am referring here to a well known Mathematics theorem or rule, e.g. Distributive property of multiplication over addition.

Allowing me to justify the final factored form, because just staring at the expression without knowing anything else is hard to come up (I mean, one can try by brute force for several hours) with the idea of multiplying the differences.

Thanks!
answer:
Is there is a way to go from
$$(xy + xz)(y - z) + (xy + yz)(z - x) + (xz + yz)(x - y)  $$
to
$$(z - x)(y - z)(x - y)?$$

Is there is a way to go from
$$(xy + xz)(y - z) + (xy + yz)(z - x) + (xz + yz)(x - y)  $$
to
$$(z - x)(y - z)(x - y)?$$

You can
meta-cheat
.  Assuming that the two expressions are equivalent,
$~(z-x)~$
must divide
the first expression.

Clearly,
$~(z-x)~$
divides the middle term of the first expression,
namely
$~(xy + yz)(z-x). \tag1$

So, the next step is to simplify the expressions of the first and third terms to demonstrate that
$~(z-x)~$
divides the sum of the first and third terms.

$$(xy + xz)(y - z) + (xz + yz)(x - y)$$

$$= \{ ~xz ~[ ~(y - z) + (x - y) ~] ~\} \tag2 $$

$$+ \{ ~[ ~xy ~(y - z) ~] + [ ~yz ~(x - y) ~] ~\}. \tag3 $$

The expression in (2) above can be re-expressed as

$$xz \times (x - z) = (-xz) \times (z-x). \tag4 $$

Similarly, the expression in (3) above can be re-expressed as

$$+ \{ ~[ ~xyz (-1 + 1) ~] + [ ~y^2 ~(x - z) ~] ~\} = (-y^2) \times (z - x). \tag5 $$

Therefore, combining the expressions in (1), (4), and (5), you have that the first expression can be re-expressed as

$$(z-x) \times [ ~(xy + yz) + (-xz) + (-y^2) ~]. \tag6 $$

Therefore, the entire problem is completed by simply noticing that

$$(y - z)(x - y) = xy - y^2 - xz + yz.$$

$\underline{\text{Addendum}}$

This addendum is added because I originally overlooked the possibility that
$~(z - x) = 0.~$
Under that assumption, using analysis similar to that used in Gerry Myerson's answer, you get that both expressions equal
$~0.~$

That is,

$$(z - x)(y - z)(x - y) = 0.$$

and substituting
$~x~$
for
$~z~$
:

$$(xy + xz)(y - z) + (xy + yz)(z - x) + (xz + yz)(x - y)$$

$$= x(y + x)(y - x) + 0 + x(x + y)(x - y) = 0.$$
